The elections loomed and Nigeria's literati rejoiced that, at last, they had a president who had their interest at heart. Authors were treated like celebrities, as the nation's number one citizen reminded the gathering that a literate populace was vital to his vision for the country.
This was only the much anticipated crescendo of the growing importance literary activities in recent years. Back in 2004, a howl of protest had greeted the decision by the Nigerian Liquefied Natural Gas Company to make its recently endowed literature prize exclusive to locally-based Nigerian writers.
The thinking was that the $20,000 prize would encourage a vibrant literary culture in a creativity-stifling environment like Nigeria.
Curiously, this apparent "ghetto" literary prize - even before it upped the ante to morph into $50,000 and recently to $100,000 - was the highest in the sector. When none of the 2004 contestants won the prize, scoffers didn't hesitate to conclude that it was a red-herring prize.
The organisers never really intended to award a $20,000 prize to a Nigerian writer, some literary activists even insinuated.
But they were, of course, wrong as the history of the prize later proved. There were not only joint winners but there were also hitherto uncelebrated individual winners. Among the latter, was the latest winner, Mai Nasara, who clinched the $100,000 prize!
Meanwhile, a cavalcade of Nigerian-born writers had continued to make waves on the international circuit.
Besides Chimamanda Adichie, who won the Orange Prize for Fiction in 2007, and Helon Habila, who had won the 2001 Caine Prize for African Writing, there were also the Belgian-based Chika Unigwe and two other Nigerian Caine Prize winners, Segun Afolabi (2005 winner) and E.C. Osondu (2009 winner), among a host of others.
Their phenomenal triumphs in the international scene buoyed the fervour of locally-based writers. A literary equivalent of Bolshevism seems to have recently crept into the scene. The Association of Nigerian Authors - better known by its acronym ANA - seems to have been relegated into virtual irrelevance.
The association, formed in 1968 by the old guard comprising Chinua Achebe, Wole Soyinka and John Pepper Clark, among others, may still uphold its fundamental principle of protecting the interest of the Nigerian author.
Yet, this has not stymied the emergence of several literary circles.
Besides, with more authors drawn to vanity publishing and self-promotion as well as the allure of online publishing, a literary ferment in the local scene was inevitable. In Abuja, the Abuja Literary Society and the Abuja Writers' Forum are complementing the efforts of ANA.
In Port Harcourt, the Rainbow Book Club sets the literary agenda with its monthly readings and its annual festival. In Lagos, literary activities have mushroomed in both likely and unlikely places.
Sporadic readings at venues like Jazzhole, Bookworm and Quintessence soon inspired a groundswell of others. Igoni Barrett, the scion of the leading poet Lindsay Barrett, initiated Bookjam@Silverbird Galleria, which held every last Saturday of the month.
Celebrity Read, the brainchild of Bede Okoro, offers another monthly reading opportunity for the literati. Just as it happens at the Rainbow Book Club monthly readings, celebrities are either invited to read from their favourite books or authors are called upon to read from their own book.
Among other literary gatherings are the Book N Guage, a pet project of PulpFaction online book club which holds at Debonair Bookshop in Yaba area of Lagos, the Committee for Relevant Art's Word Slam and the Friday meets of The Weavers, a writers' society formed last year at the University of Lagos campus.
Book tours, usually organised for specific authors by cultural centres like the British Council and publishing houses like Farafina and Cassava Republic, soon took a unique dimension when a group of nine writers led by the younger Barrett embarked on a reading tour of four cities.
The tour, tagged Nine Authors and Four Cities, took them from Lagos through Ibadan, Benin, Warri and back.
Workshops have also become literary hothouses for hitherto reticent writers. First, there are the workshops organised by the literary societies. Then the annual workshop initiated by Fidelity Bank has grown in stature to become a landmark event. Nigeria writers like Adichie and Habila have headlined the event alongside their foreign counterparts.
Literary festivals have evolved from these activities. The CORA-organised Lagos Book and Art Festival - now on its 13th edition - ends today at the Freedom Park in Lagos Island.
It kicked off on Friday. Just a few months ago, the Garden City Literary Festival, organised by the Rainbow Book Club in collaboration with the Rivers State Government, held in Port Harcourt.
The event has arguably become the biggest of its kind in sub-Saharan Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Top South Korea Officials Visit Nation
Headline: Tanzania: Top South Korea Officials Visit Nation
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — The top leadership of the South Korea's Saemael Undong or new village movement, has is in the country to assess development of their pilot projects they started in 2009.
The pilot projects sought to support people get out of abject poverty are at Mwambao and Njia Nne villages both in Mkuranga District, Coast Region. Speaking to journalists in Dar es Salaam over the weekend on behalf of the secretary general of Saemael Undong Korea, Mr Kwon Doo Hyun, Pastor Joshua Lee said in 2009, the Korean institution saw the need to popularise the system in Tanzania.
"It is a good method to train Tanzanians rid the country of poverty as their counterparts in South Korea did," he said, adding that the two villages in Mukuranga District were used as pilot projects.
Mr Lee said South Korea in the past was among the poorest nations on the earth, but in 1970 people opted to introduce Saemael Undong long-term plan which helped the growth as viewed today.
"Under Saemael Undong system, the country (Korea) attained a lot of development achievements, leaving behind many countries in social and economic development," he noted. "As I speak you (the press), the two villages implementing Saemael Undong plan have begun to construct roads, cultivating rice and cassava," Mr Lee said.
He said the Korean team is in Tanzania to see the development of people trained by the organisation and the introduced projects if were sustainable.
Earlier, the Member of Parliament for Mbinga East, Mr Gaudence Kayombo, who is also the leader of Saemael Undong Tanzania, said were set to benefit a lot under the Korean introduced programme.

Original_Search_Title: Life Style Implications On the Health of Tanzanians
Headline: Tanzania: Life Style Implications On the Health of Tanzanians When We Observe 50 Years of Independence
Body_Text:
Health education comprises consciously constructed opportunities for learning involving some form of communication designed to improve health literacy. It includes improving and developing life skills that are conducive to individual and community health.
Health promotion, public information and education for health should be developed at country level in the spirit of the Alma-Ata Declaration and First and Second International Conferences on health promotion, strategies for health promotion and education as essential elements of primary health care.
Tanzania as a country must strengthen national capabilities in all aspects of health promotion. There is need to adopt an evidence-based approach to health promotion policy and practice, using the full range of quantitative and qualitative methodologies, and give health promotion top priority in the Ministry of Health and Social Welfare.
Strategies, models and methods in health promotion are limited to neither a specific health issue nor a specific set of behaviours, but apply to a variety of population groups of all ages, risk factors, diseases and settings. Efforts put in improving education; community development, policy, legislation and regulations are valid for prevention of communicable diseases as they are for tackling the major risk for noncommunicable diseases (unhealthy diet, tobacco use, sedentary lifestyle and alcohol abuse) and for preventing injury, violence and mental illness.
The adoption of WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control, the work towards a global strategy on diet, physical activity and health, the move for health initiative are major global steps towards reducing these common risks. The challenges
Tanzanians need to walk the talk especially those in the health cum medical profession to embark on health promotion on diet starting with their own health before promoting other people's health. Mental health promotion constitutes an important component of overall health promotion.
In view of the stress and conflicts that individuals and communities face, greater efforts are needed to promote mental health. In the wake of globalization, poverty reduction strategies at country level and at individual levels, some of the health cum medical professionals has bought shares in the cigarette and breweries companies.
It is obvious there will be a conflict of interest and moral ethics, if those shareholders will utter words of health promotion to discourage the community from avoiding smoking and reducing or stopping alcohol consumption. This is a dilemma. Malentlema, defines alcohol: "Alcohol is not food for leisure as most people think.
It is dangerous in moderate or large amounts, if one wishes to remain healthy, just avoid it." Health promotion is important for attaining the health-related United Nations Millennium Development Goals (MDGs), contributing to the reduction in child mortality; improvement of maternal health, prevention and control of HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis and malaria, access to better sanitation and clean drinking water.
Achieving MDGs will need greater recognition of the inextricable links between health, development and poverty reduction and improved access to major health services; health promotion will be crucial for mobilizing society in this task through advocacy and suitable strategies.
Insufficient evidence on the effectiveness of health promotion contributes to limited allocation of resources, and consequently under funded interventions and less effective health promotion. Special efforts are needed to collect sound evidence, particularly in Tanzania where there are a lot of promotions on cigarette smoking and beer promotions with fewer warnings.
Cigarette warnings like "Smoking causes impotence", Smoking causes cancer", may deter people from smoking. The words must be legibly written to occupy 30% of the size of cigarette packet. Health promotion: The combination of education and environmental supports for action and conditions of living conducive to health.
The actions may be those of individuals, groups or communities, of policymakers, employers, teachers or others whose actions control or influence the determinants of health. The purpose of health promotion on diet is to enable people to gain greater control over the determinants of their own health.
In health promotion, health is seen as a resource for everyday life, not the objective of living. Health is a positive concept, emphasizing social and personal resources, as well as physical capabilities. Life style: a way of living based on identifiable patterns of behaviour which are determined by the interplay between an individual's personal characteristics, social interactions, and socioeconomic and environmental living conditions.
The term "life style" is often associated with rich upper-class people, who can choose freely how they spend their time and resources. But with the term "lifestyle" we are referring here to habits that people choose either voluntarily or involuntarily, which affect health-habits in areas such as food (eg use of sugars and salts), hygiene, coping mechanisms, alcoholism and cigarette smoking, etc.
Lifestyles of the poor have changed within a few generations -- thanks to urbanization, change in the availability of water, food and the changed spectrum of occupation. Food patterns have changed. The use of salt, sugars and fats has increased among many urban and rural populations.
Even the dominant staple foods have changed in several countries including Tanzania. We now have male chips (chips dume- the fried cassava) with potato fries with eggs and or chicken and chips, the so-called JUNK FOOD-FAST FOOD. Engagement in the following lifestyles: Exercise for at least 30 minutes 3 times a week regularly. Eat 7-10 portions of fruits and vegetables daily? Drink one glass of skim, or low fat soymilk. Avoid fatty meats.
Hypertension occurs widely among Africans, irrespective of their affluence. Hypertension is very common in Tanzania, it is seen in 44-48% of Executives. Overweight, obesity, lack of exercises, eating habits of more salt and sugar in foods and excessive consumption of alcohol contribute to hypertension. Another alarming development is the widespread tobacco use in Africa, Asia and Eastern Europe.
WHO predicts that smoking of tobacco will become the biggest single cause of death in the 21st century. The world health report (2002) indicates that mortality, morbidity and disability attributed to the major noncommunicable diseases currently account for about 60% of all deaths and 47% of the global burden of disease, which figures are to rise to 73% and 60%, respectively by 2020.
It should be seriously noted that 66% of the deaths attributed to non-communicable diseases occur in developing countries including Tanzania, where those affected are on average younger than in developed countries. There is in existence, a vast body of knowledge and public health potential, the need to reduce the level of exposure to the major risks resulting from unhealthy diet and physical inactivity, and the largely preventable nature of the consequent diseases:
The diseases we always fear due to our modern lifestyles are, just to mention a few: High blood pressure, Diabetes mellitus; Road Traffic Accidents; The ALCOHOL related diseases which are 60 in number; The TOBACCO related diseases which are 40 in number.
The risk factors: The factors that increase the chances of a person developing a disease are known as risk factors. One risk factor is more likely to lead to another.
For example: If one is obese (fat), he or she is more likely to develop high blood pressure and diabetes than somebody who is not obese. Similarly, if one is always nervous and tense she or he stands a greater chance of developing hyperacidity and or gastric ulcer.
Therefore, to save Tanzanians from developing the Non Communicable Diseases within the scope of Health Education and Health promotion, according "We may need to return to the old style of eating more fruits and vegetables, less fat, salt and less refined foods. We also have to involve ourselves in more physical activities and avoid tobacco and alcohol."
The way forward: The best way to prevent and manage non-diet and diet-related non-communicable diseases at community level by: Developing and disseminating guidelines on healthy eating and life styles, Training health workers and other service providers on dietary management of non-communicable diseases, and Raising public and community awareness on prevention and management of non-communicable disease through diet and physical activities.
Community involvement and participation in health promotion on diet and health education should be continued and sustained in all walks of life beginning at home, in schools, work places and wherever and whenever people meet. Places of worship like mosques, temples, synagogues and churches should also be used for health promotion and health education as long life investment to improve people's health.

Original_Search_Title: How I'll Transform Bayelsa, If Elected - Ben Murray-Bruce
Headline: Nigeria: How I'll Transform Bayelsa, If Elected - Ben Murray-Bruce
Body_Text:
If what Mr. Ben Murray Bruce, one of the governorship aspirants to the Bayelsa State Government House said is anything to go by, then Bayelsans are in for a great time if he is voted into power come 2012.
Addressing participants at the 4th biennial convention of the National Association of Ijaw Female Students (NAIS) at the National Theatre Iganmu, Lagos recently, Murray-Bruce, who was the chairman of the occasion, said Bayelsans had no business being poor as according to him, the state has a N2 trillion economy, one of the highest in Africa.
"In deciding to run for public office, I decided to study Bayelsa State, one of our Izon states. I wanted to understand what the problems were. Bayelsa has a N2 trillion economy, one of the richest states in Africa on paper.
Bayelsa has very high infant mortality rate, one of the highest in the world. It has one of the highest maternal mortality rates in the world. Bayelsa came last in WAEC last year. It has the highest unemployment rate in Nigeria and it has the highest misery index. But definitely, we are the richest state," he stated.
The Chairman of Silverbird Group and former NTA D_G said that education of Bayelsans was the key to the state realising its full potential. "In resettling our people, first of all, they must go to school. We have a very small number of people going to primary school in the state and only 18 per cent of that go on to secondary school.
In the Niger_Delta University, we have 4,000 students, 2,000 of them are not from the state. Nineteen years after freedom, we are developing an illiterate population but we have the richest economy. It makes no sense!"
Waxing philosophical, Murray-Bruce said: "You cannot be rich on one hand and so poor on the other hand. This is an inconsistency. As I look at you today, I see the future but what kind of future will it be for our people? We cannot have an 18th Century economy in the 21st Century, yet we have the resources.
Thirty per cent of all oil and gas comes from Bayelsa, yet we live in abject poverty. Who will you blame? Who is responsible? Do we blame ourselves? Do we blame the Federal Government? What is fundamentally wrong with our people?" he asked.
He regretted that many of the leaders are only interested in consuming the resources of the land without producing anything. "If we get in the position of leadership, why are we so concerned about consuming the resources of our land and producing nothing?
There are two kinds of people in this world. You are either a producer or you are a consumer. If you produce, it leads to freedom and if you consume, it leads to fatness. You must produce to be free. We have a responsibility to our people."
In order to transform the state, Murray-Bruce recognised the power of education to empower the people and to ensure that none will have any excuse for not getting an education, he promised Bayelsans free and compulsory education.
"As governor, education will be free and compulsory at every level. I am not doing you a favour, it is your money. We will provide one free meal a day in every school. We will provide buses to pick you up at designated points and take you back home every single day."
He continued: "I still have to worry about the parents as a governor so I will create a hundred million naira intervention fund where your parents can borrow money without collateral. Your existence is your collateral, the state will guarantee the loan and the banks will disburse the loans.
"For students, if you are smart/intelligent and get the best grade, I will send you to Harvard University, it is your money. Your universities will be equipped to take part in every single Olympic sport. If you produce cassava, the state will buy it from you so that you will record no loss. You will survive and your parents will survive."
He advised the students to always keep hope alive because they have the wealth that they produce, the resources and there is no reason for them to be poor.
He noted that as a private sector person, he is not a consumer but a producer and guaranteed that his administration will transform Bayelsa State. "The money is yours and should be used for you. There must be transparency and accountability. Nigerians are not less intelligent than the Japanese or Americans and our environment is more favourable. If you have no father or mother, I will be your father and mother," he said.
He congratulated Comrade Joseph Evah for keeping the Ijaw and Niger-Delta flag flying over the years.

Original_Search_Title: Dancehall Sensation Mavado Expected in December
Headline: Zimbabwe: Dancehall Sensation Mavado Expected in December
Body_Text:
In recent years Zimbabwe has witnessed an influx of international artistes flocking into the country. Cocoa Tea, Fantan Mojah, Elephant Man and Mr Vegas among others came, performed and fell in love with Zimbabwe.
The latest in the line is dancehall sensation David Constantine, popularly known as Mavado or "The Gully God", who is expected to come through to Zimbabwe this December.
C and A Entertainment said Mavado is scheduled for a one-off performance on December 16 at the Glamis Arena.
The promotions company is behind several shows such as that of Sean Kingston, Elephant Man and more recently Mr Vegas, who unfortunately did not live up to expectations.
Mavado is the man responsible for massive dancehall hits such as "Gal a mad ova", "Money Changer", "I'm so special" and "Step Up (Pepper)".
Dancehall enthusiasts will recall the singer for his eerie wails "Anyway!" and "Gangsta for life!" which dominate his tunes.
Born in the micro-ghetto community "Cassava Piece" within Kingston, Jamaica, the 30- year-old became an overnight sensation with his debut single on the Anger Management riddim - "Real McKoy" in 2004.
Before getting his break in the music business, Brooks worked as a barber. Mavado says his two main influences on the direction of his young life were his grandmother and the music of Bounty Killer.
"It was my grandmother that first really brought me in the studio, cause she brought me in the church to sing. She always told me to sing for her and that really gave me the courage to love music," said Mavado.
The sweet sounds of gospel provided the motivation, while it was the gruff voice of Bounty Killer that proved to be the more powerful influence.
With rap influences to his dancehall flow "The Gully God" has featured artistes such as Jay-Z, Wycliff, Chipmunk and DJ Khaled.
To date Mavado has two albums to his name - "Gangsta for Life: The Symphony of David Brooks" (2007) and "Mr Brooks . . . A Better Tomorrow" (2009).

Original_Search_Title: Letter From Kireka - Do Not Push Mzee Back to the 'Bush'
Headline: Uganda: Letter From Kireka - Do Not Push Mzee Back to the 'Bush'
Body_Text:
Dear Members of Parliament, we are writing this letter on behalf of law-abiding citizens of Kireka, who are very concerned about the direction you are sending this country.
About a fortnight ago, our beloved President addressed a press conference, where he said because of your intransigence, he was considering going back to the 'bush', where things could be done without much concern for red tape and unending debate on nearly each and every aspect of life.
To show how serious this matter is, His Excellency repeated similar sentiments a week ago in Kabale--and has asked voters to help him sort out some of you. The President is dead serious on the possibility of a second liberation! But must we go that far? As the crème de la crème of this country, when the gravity of this matter began taking shape, we quickly congregated at our kafunda in Kireka and decided to deliberate on the way forward.
Some of you MPs might not realise, but bush life can be very trying. Why do you want to push our President back into hunting for cassava tubers in peasants' gardens as he dodges bullets from UNLA (aha, sorry, will it be UPDF?) soldiers?
Do you MPs know what it means to go for days without a shower and wearing the same combat fatigues, at times until they wear out and leave one's buttocks exposed? Is that what you people want our decorated generals to go through again as they engage in the second liberation just because some of you noisy MPs won't shut up about oil deals and stuff like that?
Imagine Salim Saleh or Kahinda Otafiire back in the bushes of Nakasongola, AK-47s strapped on their backs with only bottles of swamp water to keep them going as they run rings around enemy forces! How can you MPs, like Cerinah Nebanda who was not even born as our Ssabalwanyis engaged the enemies or Muhammed Nsereko, who was in primary school as Elly Tumwine and Jim Muhwezi put their lives on line, be the ones pushing back our good generals into the bush?
In fact, we in Kireka, have learnt that some of you were hiding under your beds as the struggle gained momentum and yet are now making noise like you ever slept in the wild and sung songs like:
Vibonge vya mungu watoto wanakimbia musituni! Moto iliwaka Moroto, nawaka, moto nawaka moto nawaka moto! So, as deliberations on the way forward got stormier in the bar, Alfredo raised the following matter: "And who will fund this new bush war? Look, Gaddafi is gone, where shall we get guns to start the new struggle?"
Araali, our colleague from Tooro, was also concerned: "I am totally opposed to the idea of sending Mzee back to the bush. Won't he be committing treason? Is that not like overthrowing the Constitution? And seeing how Kale Kayihura is anxious to follow up treasonable offences, won't Mzee be bundled to Luzira?"
Rusaniya, our good waitress, got sucked in: "Won't Kayihura accompany Mzee back to the bush? Remember he is a trusted cadre. Mzee has previously commended him for revolutionarising the police. And by the way, do we still have bushes? Haven't they gone Mabira-style?"After hours of heated debate, punctuated with swallowing all tribes of brew, the crème de la crème of Kireka, agreed that we ask our MPs to go slow. We adopted a resolution that reads:
"We, residents of Kireka, cognisant of the fact that this country has a sole visionary, aware of the possibility of us degenerating into nothingness if this vision is tampered with, pray that our noisy MPs stop their blubbering and let our Leader rule unhindered."
dwanyama@ug.nationmedia.com

Original_Search_Title: Against All Odds - an Autobiography (2)
Headline: Nigeria: Against All Odds - an Autobiography (2)
Body_Text:
BOOK TITLE: AGAINST ALL ODDS: An Autobiography
Author: Adamu Baikie
Publisher: Tamaza Publishing Co. Zaria, 2011.
First, one could have titled this book, How One Flew over the Cuckoo's Nest. For, how did this great man escape the vagaries, vicissitudes and challenges of life? This autobiography is a clarion call to all those who have played a role in the life of this nation to step forward and tell their stories themselves. The tragedies afflicting leaders around the world, from the Arab dictators to our own local dictators here, show very clearly that if you do not try to tell your own story as you see your own life, you will reduce your future to speculation, innuendos, superstition, suspicion and misrepresentation. The world will see you not as you may want to be seen but sometimes as your worst enemies want you to be seen.
Writing this book has been no mean task because the author himself admits that in the course of writing: I was carried away by the imagination and hallucinations wondering whether I was myself indeed writing about myself, wondering how I survived those dangers or being wiped out of existence by an imposed adventure of which I did not bargain (pg xii). But clearly, the author demonstrates a deep and abiding faith in God, a faith that is the product of listening to his parents who counseled him not to be like the Joneses (pg. xii).
His story is that of a man who lived through hardships, but took them like a journey to Golgotha believing that those who trust in God will reap a handsome reward from Life the supreme teacher. It is the story of strict parenting by a father who had suffered but never became bitter. Despite all that his parents passed through, their life became for him a great lesson, a lesson that he and his wife have quite successfully passed on to their children.
There is a very interesting story of how his mother taught him a simple lesson about how not to develop langa throat even as a child. The author said one day, he saw his mother cooking and, as women are wont to do, she was constantly dipping the soup from the pot to the palm of her hand and licking it. He approached his mother and begged her to let him lick the soup too. She obliged him. She fetched some of the soup with the long spoon, let it cool off a little bit and then asked him to open his hand. He did and as he said: .... poured the hot soup in my hand and then, clasped my tiny fingers to cover the still simmering hot vegetable in my hand and held on tightly for a while (pg 35). Lesson? Do not poke your little nose in matters that do not concern you!
This book is a narrative of love. There is a deep spiritual streak that runs through the entire book. It is clear that the author's focus is on the Christian principles of Character, Trust, Love, Integrity, Faith, Perseverance, Fortitude, Courage, Humility, etc. It seems that these were also the qualities he looked for in the people that touched his own life. These themes run through the way he introduced many of his main characters, people who had influence in his life. I was quite struck by the kind of adjectives he used to describe many persons who touched his life. Let me use just a few examples to illustrate the point:
He referred to his friend, David Ogazuma a man of great influence and character who also shaped his life as,a very amiable gentleman, kind, gentle and not given to anger (p15). Mrs.Christiana Fearon (mother of Bishop Fearon), is referred to as, an angel, a devoted Christian and one who believed in the sanctity of family hood (p15). Any wonder her son is a Bishop today? He refers to the Bakos as being,special (p28). The late Baba Miller was very saintly and most considerate and humane person (p29), the Miller sisters, Jummai and Maude are of outstanding character and achievements, Brother Mali was blind, but was shown love and compassion and never in want of anything (pg 30). This amazing appreciation of other people is borne out of a deep sense of tolerance of other people. This catalogue of terms of endearment are however broken when the author narrates his experience in Lokoja. One person who gets the rough end of the tongue of the author is one Pa Makina who was supposed to be like a foster father.
They had been left behind to stay with this seemingly cantankerous old man who had become bedridden as a result of a nail injury which he had failed to treat. Pa Makina as he was called seemed to receive the most caustic assessment. He is described by the author as being sadistic. He made them work from 5.30pm to 6.30pm, feeding on roasted cassava or yam and water(pg.136). He held Pa Makina solely responsible for the loss of his beloved young brother, Nasiru who, one day, just suddenly disappeared. This was obviously one of the most painful phases of the life of the author as narrated in the book.
There are also issues in this book that tell us a lot about what one might call the moral impact of the Wusasa school. For example, the author, along with such friends like General Gowon, Professor Ishaya Audu among others stand out clearly as persons who placed premium on fear of God and character. All this runs through in how they passed through life, held positions of trust and influence and still came out with their integrity intact at the other end of the spectrum. I had heard so much of Professor Ishaya Audu. When I heard that he had taken up practice in Samaru after his time as Nigeria's Foreign Minister, I decided to trace his Clinic and visit him. I found the place and was shocked by the humility of the environment. I was glad I managed to be at his funeral. Today, with no written biography, the shadow of his legacy has been shortened in many respects.
It is impossible to talk about ABU now without reference to men of such sterling qualities as Professor Ishaya Audu and his controversial exit. It is interesting to note that after his tenure, most VCs, according to the author have had an average lifespan of just three years! After his exit, the author tries to tell a very important story on what might have been the early signs of the cancer of religious bigotry that would later threaten the foundation of the university. The author declined to say why he, Professor Onazi and Angulu were allegedly victims of what he referred to as a plot that fanned the embers of an unnecessary controversy which in his words had;... potential for putting an end to the lives of (they and their) innocent families (p416). Although the author decided to withhold further details as he stated on the same page, it is curious that in 1978, he topped the list of prospective candidates for the post of VC, but was not appointed. Their silence meant that over forty years later, the same ABU was to face the same challenge when a Christian from the Middle Belt in the name of Professor Andrew Nock, topped the list of candidates who were interviewed for the post, but was not given the job. Perhaps if they had stood up to this cancer when it reared its head, our stories would have been different today.
It is sad that today, the wonderful experiment of 1978 has collapsed on the altar of the gods of ethno-religious and regional warlords. Today, favourite sons of the soil who are determined by the temples they worship in or the content of their blood now superintend most federal Universities. This is a monumental tragedy for a nation that seeks to compete with the rest of the world.
We cannot end this review without drawing attention to the state of our academia and the teaching profession today. Sadly, the era of the likes of the author are fast disappearing with hardly any successors. His love for the teaching profession and its attendant honour rings through the book. You would think that one who has been a Professor in two Universities in Nigeria and one abroad would be a rich man, with houses and choice plots of land in Asokoro, Benin, Lagos, Gaborone among others. This gentleman, at 80, and after all he has traversed can only boast of property not even in the GRA of Zaria, but in middle level Hanwa estate, in Zaria!
The author speaks about how this property was acquired. To build his first house, the author says he had to take a loan of N45, 000: 00 (pg 693). Later on, when he wanted to further develop the land, he secured a mortgage of N90, 000, which enabled him build two modest bungalows in the same premises. Even after serving as VC in Benin for 8 years he returned to a humble office and a meager salary Zaria. He had to take a loan to be able to buy a N15, 000 Peugeot 504 station wagon. One can go on and on narrating this life of selflessness, contentment, integrity and faith.
When we consider the author, the likes of General Gowon and Professor Ishaya Audu, we would need to look nor further to find living saints who exhibit such virtues of modesty, selflessness, humility, faith in God and contentment. He may feel embarrassed but the public needs to know that despite starting the search for a new capital for Nigeria way back in 1974 as Head of State, it is barely a year ago that General Gowon can boast of a house (built for him and not by him) he can call his own in Abuja. All this after being a Head of State at the most lucrative period for any military General! It is easy to say that of Professor Baikie, that they don't make them like this any more. I pray I am wrong. There of course, others like General TY Danjuma who have combined faith, hard work and material prosperity. These qualities are not mutually exclusive and we must seek combine them.
Many people still believe that teachers deserve our sympathy. Most teachers have since re-adjusted by insisting that they want their reward here on earth. However, our papa, Professor Baikie has decided that he would rather have his reward in Heaven. The result is that in reality, he will not need to wait to get to heaven to have his reward. He already has it here and heaven will only affirm it. Let me end by commending his most wonderful wife who comes out as a splendid partner, the type that indeed enabled Adam to exclaim that this is bone of my bone and flesh of my flesh. Her exemplary qualities helped to stabilize her husband. It is of her that the Bible says;happy indeed is the husband of a good wife.
On behalf of my humble self, I with you, dear Professor Baikie a happy birthday, being one who has survived it all and proved that with God, we can all succeed against all odds. May you and your lovely wife enjoy many more years of peace.

Original_Search_Title: Vivien Nsenga, "When They Were Finished They Asked Me If I Wanted Them to Kill Me"
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Vivien Nsenga, "When They Were Finished They Asked Me If I Wanted Them to Kill Me"
Body_Text:
Goma — Vivien Nsenga (not her real name) does not know the year of her birth, but she knows the date of her gang-rape - 3 April 2010. A petite woman, she appears to be about 19 years old.
Early in the morning of 3 April she set out from the home she shares with her mother to weed her peanut fields, about three hours' walk from her village in the Massisi district of North Kivu Province, in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo.
"When I arrived at the field, all of a sudden three soldiers appeared from nowhere. They spoke Kinyarwanda [a language spoken in neighbouring Rwanda] and each man had a gun and two were also carrying pangas [machetes].
"They raped me for about two hours while one was holding my hands and the other my feet. When they were finished they asked me if I wanted them to kill me. I was so traumatised I could not even speak.
"The FDLR [Democratic Forces for the Liberation of Rwanda] are rulers there. They raped my grandmother also. But it would be a paradise [without the conflict] as there is lots of water and we can grow anything from tomatoes to rice and cassava.
"Some people found me a few hours later [after the rape] and took me back to my village. I was made pregnant by the rape and the baby was stillborn, but it caused fistula. My grandmother developed a fistula from being raped and came to Goma [the provincial capital] to have it repaired and that is why I am here.
"I had to walk for about 12 hours to catch a motorbike taxi, and then a taxi to get to Goma. I am staying with my uncle [here in Goma]. I would like to stay here [after my operation] because of the insecurity in Massisi."
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Abortion Remains Illegal in Country
Headline: Tanzania: Abortion Remains Illegal in Country
Body_Text:
UNDER the shade of a tin roof, amidst the household sounds of chickens, fussing babies, and chai (tea) being served, a group of 60 eastern African men and women took turns role playing the plight of women desperately seeking abortion.
They were participants in a "Training of Trainers" conference convened by a small NGO in rural Tanzania and the Dutch reproductive rights NGO Women on Waves.
Women on Waves employs often radical methods to increase global awareness and access to safe abortion, with a focus on medical abortion using the drug misoprostol. In 2007, misoprostol was registered in Tanzania as a drug to stop postpartum hemorrhage (PPH).
PPH is the leading cause of maternal mortality in Tanzania and across the globe, commonly due to lack of affordable and accessible medical care. Pharmaceutical companies and women's health groups across Tanzania are running awareness campaigns to equip Tanzanian pharmacies with misoprostol for PPH.
However, few people there know that the drug can safely induce abortion if taken off-label in the appropriate dosage.
At the conference, local activists and volunteers from around the world trained 60 Tanzanians, Congolese, Rwandans, and Burundians in how to find and keep misoprostol in pharmacies, how to use it safely for first-trimester abortion, what to do in case of complications, and how to train others with this information.
The idea is that the information will travel virally across the country, to parallel the awareness campaigns by pharmaceutical companies. The activists connected rural pharmacists and distributors in the capital, and discussed starting up new pharmacies to keep the drug available.
They spoke with women who were interested in following a microfinance model to become suppliers in areas outside of the reach of pharmacists, and with religious and activist leaders from refugee camps who wanted to strategize how to get the pills into the camps.
The training was held in a rural part of Tanzania near Congo and Burundi. Surrounded by such troubled nations, this area of northern Tanzania shelters a flood of refugees, and in their wake the inevitable NGOs.
The area also combines stunning poverty, a lack of access to contraception, rape as a war crime, and an AIDS epidemic that has infected one in four Tanzanians. Because of these conditions, the need for abortion overshadows the moral arguments that play out in the US.
Yet the law still imprisons women who abort, or anyone who helps them, so abortions are not available in hospitals or clinics. Instead the women use blue. Most people in the area know at least one woman who has died in attempt to abort.
Women often use sticks or a strong tincture of cassava root, but in conversation they also bring up blue, a mysterious reference I did not grasp until I was invited to visit a friend's soap factory.
There I was introduced to a brilliant deep cobalt blue powder that sticks under your fingernails and stains your hands, like henna. This was blue. The powder is mixed with water to produce a strong household liquid bleach used for disinfecting and cleaning.
This is what women drink to terminate unwanted pregnancies, often corroding their internal organs and ending their lives. One-third of maternal deaths in Tanzania are due to unsafe abortion practices like drinking blue, and WHO research demonstrates the Eastern African region to have the second highest rate of unsafe abortions in the world.
The dissemination of information has always been at the heart of Women on Waves' initiatives. Their work in Europe and Latin American focuses on increasing access to a drug that women already know about and desperately need. The difference in Tanzania is that access to the drug already exists.
The drug was available without a prescription in nearly every pharmacy the volunteers visited, and not once did a pharmacist express suspicion of illegal use. The cost is low enough for most of the participants, who ranged in demographic, nationality, and profession, to agree it was affordable.
Africa has the highest percentage of maternal deaths due to unsafe abortion in the world. In sub-Saharan Africa, an estimated 4.7 million abortions occur each year, and of these, about 98 percent are performed either by persons lacking the minimal skills, or in an environment lacking the minimal medical standards, or both.
According to data from the World Health Organization (WHO), in 2003, unsafe abortions accounted for 14 per cent of maternal deaths in Sub-Saharan Africa, the equivalent of 120 deaths per 100,000 live births.
A new study by the Guttmacher Institute has actually managed to quantify the direct costs of treating the complications that result from unsafe abortions on health systems in the global south.
Based on two different methods of calculation, -a World Health Organization model and a comparison of 20 empirical studies, 9 from sub-Saharan Africa-the authors calculate that on average, treating post-abortion complications costs an estimated USD83 per patient in Africa (based on 2006 reported costs).
When overhead and capital costs are included, these averages jump to USD114 for Africa. Assuming women continue to pay for their own abortions, if able to access safe, legal abortion services, the reduction in costs to healthcare systems in all African nations is undeniable.
These new estimates of the direct costs of treating abortion complications lend incredible weight to raising the profile of access to safe and legal abortion on the development agenda. We have seen the world community come together to discuss development on many occasions.
A working committee drawn from a range of UN bodies, special agencies and specialists, including the World Bank, the International Monetary Fund, UNICEF, the Population Fund and the World Health Organization, put together the 18 specific targets.
It also put together 48 indicators that formed the Millennium Development Goals, based on the Millennium Declaration of September 2000. Along with education and gender equality, health is a major focus of the MDGs and yet reproductive health seems to have received only cursory attention.
It was only in 2005 that a new target under MDG 5 (improved maternal health) was added: to ensure universal access to reproductive health by 2015. Quantitative measures now include contraceptive prevalence, adolescent pregnancies, antenatal care coverage and unmet need for family planning.
The issue of maternal mortality certainly draws global attention. However, even when advocates highlight the correlation between unsafe abortions and maternal mortality, some of the world's most restrictive abortion laws have remained in place.
So perhaps this new study is the impetus needed to advance the abortion agenda. It is indeed frustrating to need to put figures on the table. Ultimately, this is a woman's life, health and rights we are talking about and it is easy to ask why money should matter.
But when we have talked rights in the past, laws have not changed. The 2003 Protocol to the African Charter on Human and Peoples' rights on the Rights of Women in Africa, adopted by the African Union mandates countries to protect the reproductive rights of women by authorizing medical abortion.
The authorization, however, was deemed necessary only in cases of sexual assault, rape, incest, and where the continued pregnancy endangers the mental and physical health of the mother or the life of the mother or the fetus.
In the context of women's health programs and the high maternal mortality rates resulting from unsafe abortions, the Africa Health Strategy 2007-2015 recommends "safe termination of pregnancy and post-abortion services should be included as far as country's law allow".
However, few African governments have implemented the Protocol's recommendations. As of January of this year, for a host of African countries, abortion remains illegal in all circumstances or is permitted only to save a women's life.
These countries include Angola, Benin, Côte d'Ivoire, Kenya, Lesotho, Mali, Nigeria, Senegal, Somalia, Tanzania, Togo and Uganda. It is clear that change requires a fundamental shift in knowledge, attitudes and incentives.

Original_Search_Title: 20 Families Homeless After Heavy Rain
Headline: Uganda: 20 Families Homeless After Heavy Rain
Body_Text:
Twenty families in Namayingo District are homeless after a downpour destroyed their houses. The Tuesday night downpour caused the collapse of rooftops which injured seven family members, currently receiving treatment at Buyinja Health Centre IV.
The victims were identified as Hellen Nakaziba, 10, Perusi Nakilanda, 30, Christine Badebye, 5, Jude maganda, 6, Jenifer Nabirye, 35, Miria Sandra, 8 and Abbey Ngobi, 15.
The chairperson of Namayingo East village, Mr Asakasa Kirunda, said the four-hour downpour destroyed maize, cassava and ground-nut gardens, and other property worth Shs30 million. Other areas affected by the downpour are Bulamba Village and Namayingo Central. Mr Kirunda called upon the ministry of disaster preparedness to aid the affected families with relief items.
The Uganda Red Cross Society in a recent country assessment singled out floods and hailstorms as the most prevalent disasters in south eastern region. The far-eastern region of Mt. Elgon are prone to landslides due to destructive human activity on the Mountain slopes.

Original_Search_Title: Poisonous Substance Caused Tragedy
Headline: Namibia: Poisonous Substance Caused Tragedy
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Post-mortems carried out on the bodies of seven children who died at a village outside Rundu indicate that they died from a toxic substance.
It was reported last week that seven children from Andara died at Rundu State Hospital after eating maize meal porridge, while five others were in a critical condition.
The victims were from the same homestead and their ages ranged between two to 18 years. They died after eating maize meal porridge that was said to have been poisoned.
Nampa reported that post-mortem results indicated that their deaths were caused by toxic substances.
The children had the maize meal porridge for supper last Tuesday at the Kahanga village, some 180 kilometres east of Rundu in the Mukwe Constituency.
They are said to have shown signs of acute liver necrosis.
Necrosis is defined as the death of most or all of the cells in an organ or tissue due to disease, injury, or failure of the blood supply, reported Nampa.
The victims were named as: Thikambo Ndunda, 11; Basilius Moyo, 9; Murare Mbuve, 9; Tina Kunyima, 2; Tivi Marungu, 5; Mupaye Mukenge, 18 and Thikambo Kathongo aged 8.
Samples were taken from their livers, stomachs and blood for the post-mortems.
Health and Social Services Minister Dr Richard Kamwi told a press conference on Friday at Rundu hospital that symptoms included, amongst other things, abdominal pains and vomiting. Their bodies were also weak.
They also fainted and showed signs of yellow fever. Kamwi said that this could be related to acute liver toxins that resulted in hepatitis.
Health officials did not want to rule out the possibility that cassava leaves may have contributed to the children's deaths as literature shows that the leaves can also contribute to food poisoning.
Kamwi said his ministry would provide coffins for all seven children. He extended his condolences to the bereaved family on behalf of the government.
He instructed social workers to visit the bereaved family to provide them with counselling, saying losing seven children in one household was a huge loss.
Police Acting Regional Commander Willie Bampton said apart from the seven children who died, a five-year old was fighting for his life in Rundu hospital.
According to Bampton, an eight-month-old baby, who may have been affected by the poison through his mother's milk was discharged from the hospital on Sunday.
He told New Era that it was strange that only the children died when the whole family, including parents, ate from the same pot.
The Acting Regional Commander said it was also strange that the maize meal turned out to be poisonous when the family had been using the same bag before to collect maize.
"This is an isolated case. It's very strange," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Oil - MPs Threaten Speaker Kadaga With Censure Motion
Headline: Uganda: Oil - MPs Threaten Speaker Kadaga With Censure Motion
Body_Text:
MPs who signed the House recall oil petition have warned Speaker Rebecca Kadaga that she risks undisclosed action, including a possible censure, if she does not recall the House in 21 days from the day she received the application.
Counting from the September 20 date when she received the petition, the 21 days provided for in the Constitution elapse on October 18, seven days earlier than October 25 when Parliament reconvenes.
The warning came during the petitioners' general meeting yesterday after it became evident that the Speaker was not yielding to their demands. It is suspected that Ms Kadaga has come under substantial, though quiet, pressure to scuttle the petitioners' intentions as the government fights to keep the delicate topic of oil off the public realm.
Aware that the MPs were meeting in the Members' Lounge, Ms Kadaga sent a letter to the lead petitioners, saying; "Please note that the notice for the commencement of the second meeting of the first session has been issued for the 25/10/2011."
As Mr Abdu Katuntu (FDC, Bugweri) read the letter aloud, the MPs denounced her message, saying she is buying time for a clique of characters who want the secrecy on oil maintained to cover up a suspected scheme to rip off the country.
"Oil is not a business of selling cassava or pancake. Oil is an extremely sensitive issue and the Speaker should be protected from being used," said Youth MP for Western Uganda Gerald Karuhanga. "We shall do anything possible to ensure that she doesn't yield to external pressure, including that from the President, because Parliament must be independent," he added.
Kampala Central MP Muhammed Nsereko warned that Ms Kadaga is trying "to draw" the MPs into compromising their position.
The petitioners also accused the Speaker of conspiring to commit constitutional subversion. "Two months back we were celebrating the birth of an independent Parliament, but who is interfering with it?" asked Kampala Woman MP Nabila Sempala. "This letter is giving her an escape route and we should adjust this date so that we do not create a precedent where a Speaker can abrogate the Constitution and recall Parliament at will."
The number of MPs supporting the recall of Parliament to discuss the oil sharing agreements and government's persisting secrecy in the nascent sector has risen from 166 to 190, meaning that half of the 375-member House is now ranged against Ms Kadaga.
In a situation where the Legislature finds itself in irreconcilable conflict with itself, the Judiciary usually becomes the first arbiter. But when contacted for a comment on where Parliament might be headed, Chief Justice Benjamin Odoki said he could not comment because "he is out of the country" and has not been following the matter.
During yesterday's meeting, Mukono Woman MP Betty Namboze unsuccessfully moved under Article 3 of the Constitution to have the Speaker charged with treason.
The legislator said by refusing to recall Parliament as provided for by Article 95(5) of the Constitution, the Speaker has already abrogated the supreme law and should be "dealt with accordingly."

Original_Search_Title: Country's Inflation Hits 28.3 Percent
Headline: Uganda: Country's Inflation Hits 28.3 Percent
Body_Text:
ANNUAL headline inflation hit 28.3% at the end of September, the highest since January 1993, due to shortage in food supply and the continued depreciation of the shilling, the Uganda Bureau of statistics (UBOS), has said.
The inflation figure, which excludes education data, shows a 6.9% jump in the general price level, from 21.4% at the end of August, to increase the chances of a higher Central Bank Rate for the month of October.
"During September, food prices went up by 8.4% due to reduced supplies to the market. Non-food prices rose by 5.9% due to higher prices of charcoal, firewood, local brew (waragi and malwa) and scholastic materials," Chris Mukiza, the UBOS director for macro-economic statistics, said.
"Prices of some imported goods, especially second-hand and new clothing, household items and pharmaceutical products went up due to the continued depreciation of the shilling," he noted.
Mukiza said upcountry towns of Arua, Masaka, Jinja and Gulu are experiencing annual higher inflation rates of 38%, 32.2%, 31.8% and 31.7%, respectively.
Prices of matooke, sugar, irish potatoes, cassava, pineapples, avocado, tomatoes, cabbage, green pepper, meat, chicken, eggs, cooking oil, fish and bread went up in most areas in September.

Original_Search_Title: Elderly Welcome Govt Cash Grant
Headline: Uganda: Elderly Welcome Govt Cash Grant
Body_Text:
ELDERLY Ugandans who are eligible for a monthly stipend under a pilot social scheme say they will be able to meet their basic needs.
The Social Assistance Grants for Empowerment cash transfer scheme will provide sh23,000 per month to the elderly and other vulnerable groups such as child-headed families, single mothers and the disabled.
The scheme worth 40 million pound sterling is implemented by the Ministry of Gender, Labour and Social Development with support from UK Aid, Irish Aid and UNICEF.
It is being piloted for the next five years in 14 districts of Kiboga, Kyankwanzi, Mubende, Kyenjojo, Kyegegwa, Kaberamaido, Katakwi, Kole, Nebbi, Zombo, Nakapiripirit, Moroto, Amudat and Napak.
It will be launched on Saturday during the national celebrations for the International Day of Older Persons in Kyenjojo district. President Yoweri Museveni will be the chief guest.
Immaculate Emorut, 83, a widow and one of the 861 beneficiaries in Kaberamido, said: "With this money, I no longer sleep hungry. I buy fresh cassava and process it into flour."
Emorut, a resident of Obur village in Bululu sub-county, said she used to work as a labourer but is now free from the strainous job.
"I use sh5,000 to buy flour which I eat with greens and use the balance to buy soap, salt, sugar and paraffin. This lasts for a month."
Eriesa Emoru, 87, also from Kaberamaido, said he spends the money to buy drugs because the nearby health centre sometimes runs out of drugs.
In Kyenjojo district, Joseph Twijukye, 75, a resident of Kisenge parish, said he now affords meat for his household. He said he lacked money to buy food in the past.
Alice Kabagenyi, 69, a resident of Nyaburongo in Kyenjojo, said: "I will save the monthly grant to take care of my grandchildren and emergencies like sickness."
Kabagenyi said she can save up to sh10,000 monthly, which she will invest in a piggery and poultry project for her grandchildren.
In Kyenjojo, Jessica Kyobutungi of Bigando said some of the elderly did not register because they were suspicious of the exercise, especially the Bakiga immigrants who thought the district wanted to evict them.
Simon Omoding, the communications manager of the Expanding Social Protection Programme in the gender ministry, said the scheme would cover 600,000 individuals and 95,000 households in the pilot districts.
While the initial sum is sh23,000, beneficiaries will get a 5% increment annually. Cash is delivered monthly to the beneficiaries through a mobile money system established by MTN, a telecommunication company.
Reginald Kafeero, the MTN Uganda general manager for sales and customer services, said they would issue beneficiaries with cards onto which the money will be credited monthly.
Sulaiman Madada, the minister for disability and elderly, said of the 1.4 million elderly people in Uganda, only 7.1 % have worked in the formal sector and thereby access pension.

Original_Search_Title: Three Die in Kibaale House
Headline: Uganda: Three Die in Kibaale House
Body_Text:
THREE people were discovered dead in a house in Kisenyi village, Muhorro trading centre in Kibaale district on Saturday morning.
The district officer in charge of criminal investigations, Ben Mugisha, identified the deceased as Molly Nyangoma, 29, and her two sons Kizito Kababa, 5, and Emma Kizito aged one-and-half years, all of Kisenyi.
Fatuma Kemigisa a neighbour, who occupied the room next to the deceased, said she woke up after encountering difficulty in breathing. On switching on her torch, she saw smoke coming from the next room.
"We entered the room through the ceiling and found these people lying on the bed, dead," she said.
Rashid Akugizibwe, one of the neighbours, said after entering the room they opened the door but it was too late to save the three people.
Akugizibwe said one of the children was still breathing and was rushed to Muhorro Health Centre and later transferred to Kagadi Hospital where he is recuperating from.
One other son identified as Raphael Musoke survived the incident as he was away at his grandmother's home
"We found a charcoal stove with a pan containing cooking oil and cassava chips almost drying up and we suspect that they could have died from suffocation," said Akugizibwe.
Nyangoma is reported to have woken up at around 4:00am, lit the charcoal stove and went back to sleep, causing the tragedy, according to the neighbours.
She was preparing the cassava chips to sell on Saturday because it is a market day which is always busy.
The father of the children, also called Kizito Musoke was not at home at the time of the incident.

Original_Search_Title: Businesses Hurt As Charcoal Goes Up
Headline: Uganda: Businesses Hurt As Charcoal Goes Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — The after shocks are what restaurants, kiosks, roadside bakers and homes around the country are grappling with.
A year ago, a sack of charcoal in western Uganda, cost about Ush25000 ($8.9). In northern Uganda it went for Ush12000 ($4.3) and in the central region, it was any where between Ush25000 ($8.9) to Ush35000 ($12.5). While people living in rural areas are lucky to get a sack between Ush15000 ($5) and Ush28000 ($10), in Kampala, the price has been steadily rising.
Currently, in Kampala, a bag of this vital source of fuel now costs any where between Ush65,000 ( $23.3) to Ush100,000 ($35.7) depending on where your buying with Kampala city.
"This is the highest inprice charcoal in this country has ever been. Three months ago, a bag was at Ush35000 ($12.5), now, its twice as much. This business is getting harder to earn from. So far, no one has bought from me a whole sack since the price hit the Ush70, 000 ($25) mark," said Namawanda Afua a charcoal trader in Wandegeya market, a suburb in Kampala.
Given the rise in fuel prices, transport costs have since gone up and now traders pay almost double to have their charcoal transported, while others find alternative ways of avoiding the transport costs. A trader in Kasubi, a suburb in Kampala commonly known as Maama Nakawooya, resorted to having her husband buy her charcoal during his up country travels.
"My husband uses his boss's car to buy me the charcoal which saves me. At the end of the day, I earn about Ush28000 ($ 10) from a sack. I had completely given up on selling charcoal." says Maama Nakawooya.
The traders however affirm that sometimes, the quality of the charcoal they buy is not equal to the now high price.
"Because the sack is closed, you cannot tell what is inside.
Its one thing to pay for a sack at Ush60, 000 ($21.4) it's quite another when you've invested a million into new stock and half of them turn out to be mere ash," says Namawanda.
Restaurants and food vendors in the city have not escaped the brunt of the costs.
In Kikumi Kikumi, one of the cheapest places of ready made meals in Wandegeya, food vendors have stepped up their prices.
A customer that used to buy a plate of katogo (mixture of beans and cassava and or bananas) for breakfast at Ush1000 ($0.4), now adds an extra Ush1000 ($0.4), for you to appease the pangs.
"We cannot sell as cheaply as we used too, both the food and the fuel now cost more" says Namukwaya.
"Nothing come s cheap these days so no one should expect cheap food either," she added.
Their neighbors, Obama Chapati, a popular franchise that deals in food and snacks, used to sell a chapatti (local delicacy made of wheat) at Ush500 ($0.3) now, one forks out Ush1000 ($0.35) to buy the same size.
In homes, some have altogether given up using charcoal for now. Maama Nakawooya says, though she sells charcoal, she doesn't use it.
"I do not see the point of buying expensive charcoal yet I get wood cheaply," says Maama Kawooya.
"I need all the money I can get from charcoal, I cannot afford to take any home given its cost these days," she added
"I am about to abandon charcoal. It has become too expensive now. I am opting to buy either gas or these new stoves that use firewood," says Joseph Olanyo
A canteen owner Flora on Kanjokya street in Kamokya, a surburb in Kampala, used to sell a samosa at Ush300 ($0.1) now sells it at Ush500 ($0.3) and a chapatti that used to cost Ush200 is now at Ush300 ($0.1). For a proper breakfast of a boiled egg, samosa and or a chapatti, now one pays Us1200 ($0.4) instead of Ush700 ($0.25) a month ago.
Given the situation, the cheaper alternative would have been fire wood. However, fire wood is not easy to come by in the city. Unlike the rural areas, persons living in urban centers do not have the luxury of space to allow the use of fire wood.
Most landlords cannot stand having their walls blackened by soot and as if to push the point home, the largest percentages of rentals do not even have chimneys.
"We prefer tenants that use charcoal or gas not fire wood. Other wise they would have to do so out side but this too is untidy," says Herbert Sebunya a landlord in Kawaala in Kampala.
By the end of the year, if the conditions remain the same, Namawanda estimates that a sack of charcoal may well be about Ush100,000 ($35). For the first time in as long as any one can remember, the price of charcoal is threatening to beat gas, if it hasn't already.

Original_Search_Title: Street Theatre Boosts This Year's Bayimba Art Festival
Headline: East Africa: Street Theatre Boosts This Year's Bayimba Art Festival
Body_Text:
The most captivating feature at this year's Bayimba International Festival of the Arts was the street play, which took an abstract and witty form.
At the opening of the fourth annual festival at the National Theatre in Kampala on September 16, a troupe of 10 Bayimba artists relived the activities of a community's daily life -- without the spoken word, except for a few screams and laughter.
The one-hour play captured a day's events right from the moment people wake up in the morning to perform household chores, to happy occasions like weddings and wrestling contests as well as sad moments like mourning the dead.
In the wedding scene, the bride used a scrubbing brush as a tooth brush and baskets and sauce pans as pillows.
"In abstract art, we use images to imitate everyday life. It is a unique art form because the deaf and people who may not understand the language the play is presented in can follow and enjoy it," said Bisaso Albert Ssempeke, one of the street players.
Another actor, Otako Willian Mpaata added: "Street theatre relieves stress and puts a smile on the faces of the audience."
Street theatre, which makes its debut this year, is part of Bayimba's outreach programmes covering the districts of Jinja, Mbarara, Mbale, Arua, Gulu and Kampala. The Bayimba Cultural Foundation is using it as a publicity component for the regional festivals that were introduced last year.
Trainers from Holland
In February, the Bayimba Cultural Foundation invited trainers from Holland to teach local artists this art form, which they felt would raise the profile of the festival. They were right. The street theatre became an instant crowd puller at this year's event, with some spectators following the troupe to the opening of each regional festival.
Festival director Faisal Kiwewa, said: "We believe it will have a long term impact on the festival scene in the country."
He, however, lamented the government's failure to support the festival: "We continue to face the challenge of funding and would appreciate if the government came on board -- even if it supported us through kind."
The festival, which ran from September 16-18, attracted local and international artists in various genres -- music, poetry, fine art, comedy, dance, film, photography, fashion and drama.
Also featured was a photographic exhibition titled Mu Katanga, by 10 photographers focusing on the joy and sadness of life in the Katanga slum.The images produced on canvas sold for Ush150,000 ($57.6) each. The proceeds will go to the families that shared their stories.
Because of a disagreement between the central government and the Buganda Kingdom, Katanga, which is close to Makerere University, is no-man's land where people have created living spaces.
Impressed by the strength and the creativity of some of its inhabitants, Arthur Kisitu and Irene Sinou started to document Katanga. For its second edition, the Bayimba photographic workshop, under the supervision of Andrea Stultiens, portrayed the people's daily struggles.
Veronicah Akankunda, a photographer and television producer, captured the life of Sula Kadidi -- Katanga's garbage collector. Despite being despised by his community because of his profession, Kadidi enjoys his work, Akankunda observed.
Kadidi earns Ush2,000 ($0.7) per day with which he somehow takes care of his family of five.
"They live in abject poverty and take one meal per day. But goes on despite these extreme hardships," Akankunda said.
Art exhibition
Also on offer was an exhibition of paintings and drawings by young talented slum dwellers under the theme, Our Portraits our People. Organised by the Butterfly Project based in Kireka, Wakiso District in central Uganda, the exhibition had contributions from children from Namakora village in Kitgum District, Acholi quarters in Kireka and the Kisenyi slum in Kampala.
"We are promoting the children's passion for art and development by mentoring them so they can become agents of change in societies they live in. We target individual talent like fine art, public speaking, leadership, music, dance and sports," Butterfly Project assistant director Alex Obeki said.
For the fourth year running, the live music stage attracted revellers in their droves with both local and international artists churning out popular hits. Among them were Qwela Band, Ndagire Josephine Nabitaka, the Uneven Band, Maraka Band, Kaz Kasozi, Fred Sebata and The Cassava Republic Band. Also present were Tanzania's Mzungu Kichaa, Malawi's Body Mind and Soul as well as Donna Michelle from Canada.
This year, Bayimba introduced a hip-hop stage to cater for the teenagers. Established artists like Kafulu Xenson and upcoming rappers performed.
The festival is preceded by a series of workshops for artists, aimed at stimulating creativity and collaboration.
In 2009, the foundation also initiated a successful series of debates on the role of culture and art in society.
Last year, the first annual conference on arts and culture was held. This year, the foundation started training arts and cultural managers as well as journalists.

Original_Search_Title: Lira RDC Warns On Food Export
Headline: Uganda: Lira RDC Warns On Food Export
Body_Text:
LIRA Resident district commissioner Ibrahim Abiriga has warned traders against exporting unprocessed food to South Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo.
Abiriga, while addressing journalists at his Arua office on Tuesday, urged the people to process food in order to get better returns.
He said he was alarmed by the truckloads of cassava flour and cattle exported to Congo through West Nile. Abiriga also condemned the hoarding of sugar by speculators, which he said was responsible for the unstable price of the sweetener.

Original_Search_Title: It's Cheap to Say African Writers Idolise the West
Headline: Kenya: It's Cheap to Say African Writers Idolise the West
Body_Text:
On July 28, on this page appeared an article about how Kenyan writers and readers allegedly idolise the West.
The article started by quoting extensively, a review by the Economist on Binyavanga Wainaina's One Day I Will Write About This Place.
What was laughable was the attendant irony linking a Western magazine's scathing attack on an African writer and accusing African writers and readers of idolising the West all in one breath.
The net effect was an article laden with intellectual conmanship.
Quoting the Economist's negative review merely because the book doesn't subscribe to the Western prescription of an African book, and accusing African writers and readers of idolising the West is the height of insincerity.
I read the manuscript of One Day I Will Write About this Place sometime last year and it was incredibly fresh.
Its language is cassava chips crispy, its events real and closer to home than anything I have read by a contemporary Kenyan writer.
Outside the foreign observer Michela Wrong in It's Our Time to Eat, no recent literary work captures modern Kenya as Binyavanga's memoir.
The Bookforum review says this of the book: "He does not present one mythical continent, but rather a fractured, complex, and ever-shifting collection of experiences."
In essence a fresh new examination of Africa in a way the world doesn't see it.
This Africa the world doesn't see is what the Economist's reviewer doesn't like.
He would rather the usual African pornography of poverty, war, disease and death.
Well, death is in One Day, but it's dignified. The writer's mother dies. Apparently that death isn't gritty enough to meet the Economist's approval. Africans should not die with dignity, but from war or disease or poverty!
The reviewer criticises the repetitive contexts of the book, the frequent plot returns to Nakuru, for example. What he fails to appreciate is that the book is a memoir.
The memoir opens in Nakuru and pans around the world to South Africa and Nairobi's Kirinyanga road and Uganda but always returns to Nakuru.
It's Binyavanga's hometown, at the floor of the Rift Valley, the home of flamingos and, apparently, has everything "West" that Binyavanga idolises that the article's writer finds it adequate to quote the review.
The same review criticises Binyavanga for writing a memoir! Now, magazine reviewers choose form for writers! Really!
And that is the review to quote and attack African writers for idolising the West!
The New York Times reviewed One Day recently. Nobody has reported that fact and nobody has quoted the major positive reviews.
It is true that African writers get fame and money from the West, whatever "the West" means in this global village.
But they don't go chasing for the fame and money, they are resultant perks from their creative toil. Perks from the West and East and from Africa.
The thread of thought on the West is so '60s, a bygone era that only washes on the idealistic which, unfortunately, remains just that - ideal.
The reality is that Chimamanda Adichie is Genius Award winner - MacArthur fellowship - and it enables her to continue writing.
Writing is not easy financially and, for ages, writers have survived on the gratitude of a benefactor, a writing advance or a secondary job, say teaching.
I see no African benefactor for our writers and few of our universities offer creative writing courses.
So give our writers a break if their fame and money comes from the 'West' of our global village.
I agree that literary prizes are donor-funded and, yes, they have an agenda. Take the Caine Prize; the winning stories not about Africa's porn are countable.
In Monica Arac de Nyeko, the 2007 winner, Africans can be same sex loving, and in Binyavanga's Discovering Home he travels to a happy family gathering.
The rest, Africans are butchering each other in Rwanda (Yvonne Odhiambo); in a refugee camp they wait to exhale in a donor T-shirts (E.C Osondu), then fighting with sticks in Sierra Leone (Olufemi Terry) and this year from Paradise we hit leafy Budapest, an imaginary Harare/Bulawayo suburb to scavenge guavas (NoViolet Bulawayo).
Not typical
Those who have read Binyavanga agree he is not your typical African writer. Not typical because he doesn't write the expected African porn.
He doesn't carry creaking dry bones stories, observes with satellite precision and renders with intelligence. Meet him and encounter his geo-politics and ideology, which is radical and cutting edge.
To quote the Economist review on his work, African writers and readers idolising the West is akin to calling the toilet seat a plate.
African writers are publishing and not cowed by the "publishing is expensive" mantra.
Traditional publishing is slowly regressing into alternative publishing.
The game is playing online with Kenya's Storymoja, Ivor Hartmann's Storytime Africa blog, AfricanWriter and Black Magazine to name a few.
The unwarranted pigeonholing of African writers for being 'tantalised' by the west is cheap, easy and escapist at best.

Original_Search_Title: Enugu Partners Vietnamese Team On Ailing Industries
Headline: Nigeria: Enugu Partners Vietnamese Team On Ailing Industries
Body_Text:
A team of Vietnamese Investors has arrived Enugu state on the invitation of the state government to explore ways of assisting the state to reactivate/revive some of her ailing industries and make them viable.
While in the state, the team will also collaborate with the state government to improve on its rice and cassava cultivation, production and processing.
Speaking when he received the industrialists at the Government House Enugu, the state Governor Sullivan Chime described their visit as a welcome development and a step further by his administration to reactivate her ailing industries and create jobs for the unemployed.
Governor Chime described the Vietnamese trade delegation as real partners in the states' match towards industrialization, adding that their proposals to assist the state in the areas of rice and cassava production in tandem with the administration's goals and aim.
The state chief executive therefore assured them that government was disposed to give them all the needed corporation and assistances to help them succeed.
He noted that full reactivation of government owned Sunrise Flour Mills and other ailing industrial establishment in the state would help revive the economy of the state and create jobs for the youths.
Earlier, the leader of the delegation and the Vietnamese commercial councilor in Nigeria, Mr. T. A. Hun Thinh told the Governor about their readiness to assist and collaborate with the state in its industrialization programmes.
Mr. Thinh who represented the country's Ambassador in Nigeria, said they are interested in reviving the state Sunrise Flour Mills, the vegetable oil refinery, rice and cassava production and processing.
He noted that Enugu State has very rich soil for cassava and rice production adding that if their experiment in reviving the Flour Mills work out they would venture into more areas that would boost the economy of the state a some of the proposed areas will include animal feed production, cake and biscuits among others.
He thanked the governor for his vision to revamp the economy of the state and assured him of their corporation if encouraged.

Original_Search_Title: Couple Marries Off Daughter for Sack of Cassava Flour
Headline: Uganda: Couple Marries Off Daughter for Sack of Cassava Flour
Body_Text:
Jinja — When Mufumbo realised he had impregnanted Naisiko, he sought to meet her parents to inform them that he was the man responsible.
At the meeting with the girl's parents, Namyandu and Tenywa, Mufumbo suggested that he gives the couple a sack of cassava flour so that he takes their daughter.
Tenywa quickly welcomed the idea, saying: "Back in our village in Budondo, we can afford only one meal a day.
Our family is big, so the flour will come in handy."
"We have survived hunger," Namyandu said.
Naisiko, looking content with the exchange, pledged to be loyal to her husband.
She becomes the sixth wife in Mufumbo's home in Wanyange.

Original_Search_Title: Tshila Comes of Age
Headline: Uganda: Tshila Comes of Age
Body_Text:
It was a small crowd and a good number of them were certainly friends. But then it had not been aggressively marketed; no ubiquitous posters all over the city. And even if it had been publicised, Tshila's music-world - fusion played with live instruments - is not a big sell in Kampala.
