I am not bitter about the process. I am a reporter of the process. I am only concerned because many Nigerians don't know the repercussion of indiscipline at all. God did not create slave. He created free men. It is human beings that created slaves. What happened in the North, the people don't even see reaction to the indiscretion of the elites? They destroyed Buhari's jeep because as far as they are concerned whoever rides a big car is a big man. Instead of looking deep into the cause of the problem, they are saying the post-election violence was orchestrated by the CPC. Is it CPC that went to church to burn cars and put on the T-Shirt of the party? Before the election, we alerted INEC and security agencies that some people were producing CPC caps and T-shirts abroad in order to cause problem in the North because of their agenda. If after the election that happened and the people who ought to monitor were not at work to get those who were fingered, it is unfortunate. CPC doesn't even have money to produce any T-shirt. Is it not laughable that some people produced CPC's T-shirts and went about destroying churches and mosques, and then started chanting 'Sai Buhari' and the PDP said we as a party were behind that? A reasonable person would know for sure that there was an agenda behind that. We have problem in this country that we are not looking at, at all. Late President Musa Yar'Adua started something called amnesty programme for the militants that were abandoned. A serious government would have something in the North or anywhere in Nigeria where, as a result of deprivation, many people that are jobless could be taking care of in the system. I mean the area boys and the Almajeris.
Assuming CPC is in charge of government, what alternative do you have for the masses?
The first thing on our agenda is political restructuring. Nigeria cannot work without political restructuring. When Buhari was asked during the presidential debate that he attended with ANPP and ACN presidential candidates that so much money is spent on political office holders particularly the legislature, that what programme did he have if elected he said he would persuade them to take less. I don't see any wrong in part time legislature. Nigeria is not a federation; we run a unitary constitution. The federating units in Nigeria are not strong enough. The other time the President gave power to the state governor in the North to declare state of emergency and they rigged the election. That was their understanding of federating power. If we have 93 areas we called exclusive and concurrent list and the President has the power to veto in these areas, there is no governor that has even veto laws that cover up to 23 areas. My position is that if we really want Nigeria to work we should look at what we can do to have cooperation to grow the country in international affairs, military, currency, immigration and several areas.
But when you have mere things like cassava, road, housing and many petty things that state and local government can handle, and the Federal Government is still in charge, definitely we have to repackage the economy to give powers to the other tiers of government for things to grow. Zik, Awolowo and Sardauna asked for just 10 units each, but now what do we have? We have had four zones, 10,12, 19 states and now we have 36 states. What have we done? We are even asking for more states. What all those who claimed to have knowledge about governance have done was to duplicate offices. The Mid West out of which Edo State was created had 18 permanent secretaries; it included the current Delta State and one would have thought that when Edo State was created, the permanent secretaries would reduce but the state alone has 32 permanent secretaries today. That is not healthy at all. The same money the permanent secretary in Bayelsa earns is what that of Abuja earns; do we have unified civil service? It is not based on how much you generate as a state. All the payments rest on oil revenue. If the oil get dried up what happens? In the developed countries, you buy the volume of food you can chew and digest but here we just put the whole loaf of bread in our mount. That is not reasonable in my own thinking.
In the overall interest of the country, why is your party refusing to participate in the Government of National Unity (GNU) being proposed by President Goodluck Jonathan?
That arrangement is not in the interest of Nigeria at all. It has been to the disadvantage of political development in the country each time we talk about unity government based on fraud and compromises. In 1959, there was a compromise, in 1964, the same thing happened. That year, there was compromise on the part of some parties to form the national government and it divided the South West. The Action Group led by Obafemi Awolowo, exploded when S. L. Akintola decided to join the unity government and it did not augur well for the system. Nigeria Peoples Party (NPP) was undermined after 1979 election because of compromise. In 1999, All Peoples Party (APP) compromised because of unity government. The party that had about 10 governors after the election, immediately it went into the unity government arrangement with PDP shrank, because of comprise.
The chief compromiser, Ume-Ezeoke, the former national chairman of ANPP even withdrew the case filed by his party to challenge the fraud perpetrated by the PDP during the presidential election. He had a son in the government of late Yar'Adua. Today, ANPP that was such a viable opposition party could not even record up to one million votes for its presidential candidate during the general elections. What always happens is that when you join this so-called unity government, the junior partner loses out always at the end of the day. When Ogbonanya Onu took over as the national chairman of ANPP, he asked all the members of the party to pull out of the government of PDP. Did they listen to him? I wish the man had been in charge since, maybe Buhari would not have left ANPP. ACN is said to be waiting to take five ministerial slots under Jonathan government, but I know Bola Ahmed Tinubu, the national leader of the party is too sophisticated to be dragged into that kind of arrangement. I can assure that if the ACN accepts the offer the party would lose all their five states in the South West during the next election. ACN, CPC, APGA, LP and ANPP belong to another class of political ideology with element of opposition that can rescue this country. It would be suicidal to agree to fuse into PDP that is corrupt.
Many Nigerians are still disappointed that with all the talks that went on among the opposition parties before the election, your people could not agree to form alliance with ACN to challenge PDP, why?
Where are the parties? The PDP and Obasanjo registered 85 per cent of the 64 political parties. Today the CPC is the most talked about opposition party. We did not have any seat before the election, but we now have members in the national and state Assemblies and even a governor.
Why did the alliance between the CPC and ACN fail?
You think the alliance was simple just like that. You think the PDP would go to sleep to allow the alliance to succeed. The party would be a fool to allow that to scale through. If I were to be in PDP you think I would allow it to work? I would scatter it. Why is it that when the CPC and ACN were talking about alliance simultaneously the PDP and the ACN were also talking about alliance? Suddenly, the ACN dumped its presidential candidate for Jonathan and it turned around to tell Nigerians that the South West voted for Jonathan and not PDP. Was Jonathan's name written on the ballot paper? It was that arrangement that blew off the alliance between the CPC and ACN. Time would tell the deal struck by the leaders of ACN with Jonathan and the PDP on the last presidential election. Well, I congratulate the PDP for working to destroy the alliance. No matter what happens, circumstance would bring all the opposition parties together, particularly the CPC and ACN.
One was even surprised that despite your influence, CPC could not even win in Edo State where you come from
No, we were not even looking for votes there. I was surprised that in my own state the CPC beat ANPP. There was no doubt there was agreement that South South and South East have meeting with all the traditional rulers to deliver the two zones to PDP during the presidential election. Did they have to harass the voters and stop them from voting the right candidate and party they loved? Let us stop deluding ourselves; the election was rigged. The opposition must come together to confront the PDP. The country would soon erupt in protest because there is no money to run the nation. In the next two or three months, labour would confront the government on the implementation of minimum wage. In most PDP states, they cannot pay the minimum wage because their party has eaten up the reserve. Except you reduce the cost of maintaining the legislative arm of government, and cut down other expenses of government, Jonathan would not have the money to cope. Why is it that many of us are not following the path laid by Awolowo, Zik, and Sardauna? We are all thieves.
Including your humble self?
Yes, anybody in government who is not giving service but taking is a thief. If we take from where we are supposed to give, then we are thieves. Until we decongest the political space to accommodate only what we need and not excesses, we would remain thieves. Today, the thieves we are talking about are 17,500 in numbers they are the ones who take all the resources that should better the lots of many Nigerians.
If CPC fails at the tribunal, are you likely to represent Buhari as presidential candidate in 2015?
Is the party about one man? There is no doubt that Buhari is the soul of the party, but he has said after this he is not going to contest any political office again but that does not mean that he would retire from politics. When 2015 comes we will analyse the situation and know who to present, even you, if you are the most suitable we will adopt you by then.

Original_Search_Title: Averting the Bakers' Strike
Headline: Nigeria: Averting the Bakers' Strike
Body_Text:
Three years ago almost to the day, Nigerians could not procure bread for the breakfast table for one week because of a strike action by the Association of Master Bakers and Caterers of Nigeria (MBCN ) over the Federal Government's decision to increase tariff on imported flour by a hundred percent. However, after consultation and promises by government to look into their complaints, the bakers returned to work but not without increasing the price of a loaf by 20 percent nationwide.
Today, the situation has reared its head again as the bakers on yesterday commenced a one-week 'warning strike' to press home their demand for government intervention over the rising cost of flour and other baking materials in the country. The association claimed that over 40 percent of its members have closed shop, and that the price of flour has increased by over 15 percent since December last year as a 50kg bag now goes for N6000 as against N5, 250. Same goes for sugar and other items. The prospect of another round of higher price for bread which is today considered as one of the most pocket-friendly foods on the street should be of serious concern to the government.
With the existing high cost of bread and other confectionaries in the country, it would not augur well for government not to listen to the plight of the bakers and act on it. As things stand, Nigerians are today going through enough stress in their daily lives to afford another problem. Coming on the heels of preparation for a new government on May 29, the demand by the bakers should be tackled forthwith.
It is unfortunate that when in November last year, the Federal Government lifted the ban on importation of items such as furniture, toothpicks, textile fabrics, lace and embroidered fabric among others with an import duty rate of 10 per cent, and a 10 per cent levy on health and energy drinks that had hitherto been excluded from the import prohibition list, it omitted any mention of any ingredients used in baking, such as flour and sugar. Therefore, it wouldn't be too late for Government to have a rethink because it cannot afford to trifle with something as essential as bread which today serves as one of the most affordable foods for vast majority of people.
This is necessary because history shows that increases in food prices, especially bread, have sparked violent revolutions in other countries. For instance what sparked off the French revolution in 1789 and the consequent storming of the Bastille, a prison and symbol of ancient tyranny, was an increase in the price of bread. Similarly, an increase in price of bread and other foodstuff recently led to mass protests in Kenya and Algeria. Also, the ongoing food crisis in Uganda has so far led to several deaths and the situation is still simmering.
The president should not hesitate to immediately intervene to avert the imminent bakers' strike. While we see the need for government to reduce the import duty on wheat, sugar and other ingredients for confectionaries, it is also high time to consider long-term solutions by encouraging mass wheat production in the country. Similarly, government should revisit the use of alternative raw materials for bread such as cassava and corn composites, a project initiated by the Obasanjo administration then abandoned.
As it is today, Nigeria is the largest single importer of wheat from the United States, according to a report by grain industry consultant David McKee, who described the trend as a clear departure from 1987, when Nigeria imposed a complete ban on wheat imports. At that time, one result of the ban was a flood of smuggled flour through neighbouring countries of Togo, Benin and Cameroon, the beneficiaries of subsidized wheat from the US. The ban was lifted in 1992 and since then, wheat imports have been rising, and currently stand at over 4 million tonnes a year. This is unhealthy and should be re-examined.

Original_Search_Title: Jalingo - Storm Renders Family of 23 Homeless
Headline: Nigeria: Jalingo - Storm Renders Family of 23 Homeless
Body_Text:
Jalingo — Over a hundred persons were displaced by a storm which occurred in Jalingo recently. Our reporter met one of the affected families in Jalingo.
The sight of a bare-chested woman cooking dinner in the rubble and children scampering around the debris of what used to be their house, tells the story of the Hassan Baba family, a driver with two wives and 20 children, who live at Mile Six, a fringe settlement of Jalingo town.
"We are homeless. All our furniture and electronics have been shattered to pieces by the rainstorm, and if not for kind neighbours, we would all have been sleeping outside," Veronica Hassan said, in an emotion laden voice.
Gazing around the smashed zinc sheets, the splintered wood, crushed cement blocks, and the broken pieces of furniture and electronics, it would be unimaginable that all 23 people came out of the ruined house unscathed.
But Ben Hassan, the eldest son of the family, said only five children sustained minor injuries, and the bruises have since been dressed.
He said after a long season of extremely hot weather with scorching sun and dry land, when dark clouds began to form in the blue sky last Saturday evening, he was overwhelmed with joy. His younger ones too were happy, rollicking around, arms outstretched and singing a welcome song for the rain. But when the rain came, it actually came with a tempestuous wind that blew away their roof, soaked their bags of maize and cassava flour, and destroyed every precious thing that they ever owned.
"The whole family was inside the house before the rain started. We were expecting light showers that will just cool the hot weather, but then we heard the skies rumble, and blustery wind blew across the horizon. Before we knew what was happening, sand particles and all sorts of objects began hitting us. The walls shook and the roof rattled. We then realized we were in danger and our father led us outside. It was when we were outside that we watched helplessly as the storm blew away our only shelter and crushed part of the six-room building," Hassan said.
When our correspondent visited the house, some family members huddled around the rubble wearing forlorn facades, while they await their dinner. Mrs. Hassan who suspended her cooking and spared a moment to express her grief, said she felt so sad that the rainstorm not only damaged their roof, it also damaged their pieces of furniture and electronics and food stuff.
"At least we should have been spared our chairs, Television and Video set, picture frames and our food. We are now like beggars. We live off the goodwill of our neighbours," she lamented.
Asked how such a large family manages to sleep now that their shelter has been blown away by the rainstorm, she answered: "Our neighbours assist us with accommodation at night, but when it is day time, we sit here and watch the destruction that the Almighty visited on us."
Almost a week after the disaster, the Hassan family members are still hoping for help to come their way, so that they can rebuild their house. Where and when it will come, they do not know.
Churches too were not spared the fury of the thunderstorm. Even the prayers of the pastor and some members of the congregation of Kingdom of God Gospel Church, could not calm the tempestuous wind that reduced the church to rubbles. James Sardauna is a member of the church, and he was seen working in the rubble of the knocked down church. He said the rainstorm found the pastor and some church members praying, and they had to run to safety in the pastor's house.
Mr. Anthony Danu too lost his roof and other properties in the rainstorm that affected a good number of houses in Mile Six. He told Daily Trust that he was inside the house with his wife and five children when the turbulent wind started shaking the roof, and he decided to take his family outside to safety.
"Immediately, we came out and were heading to our neighbour's house, we heard an explosive sound and when we turned to look, we saw the whole roof falling on the ground. I then heaved a big sigh of relief after imagining what would have happened if we had remained in the house," he said.
It was another pathetic sight having the family sit under a mango tree with some sympathizers, watching over their devastated house. The roof of the three-bedroom flat was blown off, the ceiling caved in and ceiling fans blown metres away from the building.
But it is the bags of rice, maize, millet and cassava flour that were soaked with rain and sand that pained Danu most. "I cannot believe that all our food stuff got soaked with water and sand. It is so painful, especially that I have small children to feed. For our electronics, chairs and beds, I don't care much. At least, when I woke up Sunday morning, I was able to search under the debris of the ruin and recover some things that were not seriously damaged," he said.
Mr. Danu works with Taraba State House of Assembly Service Commission, but he has not received assistance from anybody. However, his elder brother is taking care of him at Nukkai, another fringe settlement in Jalingo, about three kilometers away from Mile Six. He too is hoping that, at least, the State Emergency Management Agency (SEMA) would render assistance that could enable him put shelter over his family's head once again.
But the head of SEMA said the agency is cash strapped and has not been able to respond to disasters for the past few months. Adamu Sani who said he has gone to Mile Six to inspect the damage caused by the rainstorm, lamented the inactivity of the agency, saying under normal circumstances, the agency would have responded to the disaster within 72 hours.
"Our warehouses are empty and we do not have cash at hand, so there is nothing we can do for now. But if the deputy governor, who is the chairman of our governing board comes back and decides that we do something, then we will reach out to the victims," Sani said.
Unofficial figures put the number of those rendered homeless to over a hundred, but they all have to wallow in their despair for now.

Original_Search_Title: Nebbi Accident Claims 17 Lives
Headline: Uganda: Nebbi Accident Claims 17 Lives
Body_Text:
At least 17 people have been confirmed dead after they were involved in an accident when a lorry they were travelling on overturned at Nyaravur near Nebbi town on Monday night.
Five people died on the same road on Sunday in a similar accident.
According to a witness, who requested not to be named, the lorry, which was carrying mostly traders from Koboko District, rolled back after failing to climb a hill and crashed into a trench.
"I think the driver failed to engage the gear because I saw the vehicle reversing and shortly crushed into the trenches," the witness said.
"I counted 17 bodies as we retrieved them from the wreckage. The scene is nasty because there are broken bones and blood everywhere on the road and in the trenches," the witness said.
Survivor's account
Mr Muhammed Aliga, one of the survivors, said at Nebbi hospital: "It seems there was brake and gear failure because the driver wanted to engage the gear but it failed. So I decided to jump off when I heard a strange noise while the gear was being engaged and shortly I saw the truck overturn and people started wailing in pain."
The traders were returning to Koboko from Panyimur market in Nebbi District. The incident happened at Nyaravur, near Nebbi town. The truck was loaded with sacks of cassava, beans and fish.
According to medical statistics, out of the 17 who died, 13 are women, two men and two children. The driver of the truck is on the run.
The northwestern region police spokesperson, Ms Josephine Angucia, said the accident was caused by reckless driving and using a vehicle in dangerous mechanical condition.
At least 15 people are nursing injuries at Angal and Nebbi hospitals.

Original_Search_Title: Measles Epidemic Infects 3,000
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Measles Claims Lives as Public, Private Resources Stretched Thin
Body_Text:
Kinshasa — More than 3,000 cases of measles have been recorded in the past three months in two districts of Maniema Province, in the eastern part of the Democratic Republic of Congo.
Provincial statistics seen by IPS for the districts of Kibombo and Kindu, show that since mid-April, a measles epidemic has caused more than 15 deaths at health facilities, and three or four times as many have died at home, in cases where families did not take stricken children to medical centres.
"The villages further upstream along the Congo River are the worst affected by measles," says Dr Théo Katako, interim head of the Provincial Inspectorate of the Ministry of Public Health. "Meanwhile, the province was only able to organise a vaccination campaign against polio, for lack of resources to take on these two epidemics at once."
"The total number dying of measles at home in April, could have been more than 60; for lack of a way for these families to bring these patients to the hospital because of the distance to health facilities," says Germain Musombo, a member of civil society in Maniema.
"The fear is even greater because the measles vaccination campaign, planned for last week, did not take place for reasons no one has explained," says Julie Bibi Bin Kito, a small trader and mother of a six-year-old who died of measles last week. "We who don't have money would have benefited from these vaccinations to protect the lives of our children."
She told IPS it would take more than four hours to walk to the nearest hospital to her home. She has no access to a car, and that hospital requires patients to pay to open a medical file before they can receive care. Neither she nor her husband had any money on the evening their child died.
Dying of poverty
"The question of poverty is critical throughout the province of Maniema," says Musombo. "A large majority of the population lives on rice and cassava leaves grown in small gardens near the family home. There is also the fact that the province is landlocked and doesn't benefit from commerce with the rest of the country."
The province is generally underdeveloped, with few people enjoying access to clean drinking water and just 52,000 of the provinces' estimated population of two million having access to electricity.
"With the population living under these conditions, there is cause for concern," says Martin Bila Omari, who is responsible for epidemiology at the provincial inspectorate of the health ministry.
"The provincial authorities have outlined a plan to end the epidemic and prevent new cases," says Bila Omari, "which will cost 180,000 dollars. But there is an acute shortage of financial resources to carry it out. The modest provincial budget will not allow for an urgent response."
For the moment, the authorities are trying to raise awareness. "Several weeks ago, the government launched and awareness campaign for the population, and families in particular, on the necessity of vaccinations for children between six and 59 months and urging them to bring children in from time to time for their routine vaccinations," Dr Benoît Kebela Ilunga, director of the campaign against measles at the national health ministry, told IPS.
He said the government has also strengthened monitoring of new cases with a view to arresting the present outbreak of measles.
"Happily the province is benefiting from the support of UNICEF [the United Nations Children's Fund] and other international humanitarian organisations like Médecins Sans Frontières - France," says Bila Omari, adding that a measles vaccination campaign could be organised almost immediately as these partners already have the necessary vaccines in hand.
While the province of Maniema struggles with epidemics of measles and polio, non-governmental organisations warn of 80 cases of measles in the territory of Bukama, in Katanga province, in the southeast of the DRC.

Original_Search_Title: Priest, Nun Implicated in LRA Operations
Headline: Uganda: Priest, Nun Implicated in LRA Operations
Body_Text:
5th August 2004 SITREP
Kony, Otti, Lakati, Abuderna, Odyambo, Ocan Bunia, Okulu, Michael, Lotiba, Okuti, Labongo, and Angola were all on air.
Kony;
-Directed Angola not to cross from the right side of a location he described as where they killed all the cows when UPDF started Operation Iron Fist, but he later described it as icy.
-He told him that he has no hoes apart from 01 that he charged from EDF.
-Hinted that UPDF attacked his squad that was forced to withdraw. He instructed Lotiba to move and join Acelam Caesar in a sickbay.
-Commended the idea of receiving the important items from their important coordinator in Anaka area and added that it would be good if LRA could establish links with anti-government elements in Diaspora .
Otti;
-Revealed that he rang their important coordinator who has contact with anti-government outside the country and talked at length. That the coordinator expressed desire to talk to Otti, Otto or Kony because he has very important issues to discuss but fears government could kill him.
-That the coordinator wants to meet another lady coordinator to discuss what to do.
-Said that the coordinator has two important items he wants to send to LRA through a certain point on river Nile which can be received in areas of Anaka.
-That he told the coordinator he wrote only one letter of peace talks through Modesto when he asked whether he has ever written a letter requesting for peace talks with government. This was after the coordinator told Otti that peace talks with government is quite useless to LRA.
-Lakati denied losing five soldiers, that instead he lost one from a squad that had to loot cassava. This was before Kony told Lakati to accept his losses because this is war. He then said that 04 women escaped yesterday. He promised to inform Acelam later whether his wife was among the four.
-Abudema told Kony that LRA is spread 'all over so he cannot tell which group attacked a vehicle on a tarmac road. He asked for permission to kill Acire Said for failing to move and join him because he suspects him to have plans to escape. But Kony refused and advised him to be open to him that he knows his plans, disarm him and leave him with Okuti as he is coming to Sudan.
-Angola said that it was Kamduli who was attacked but did not elaborate. This was after Kony expressed worry that Angola could be off air because he was attacked. He described his location as where one Alice showed her power. He told Kony that he brought him 01 pair of civilian shoes and 01 for combat. He added that some of his 05 soldiers went back to Uganda with some of the items. This was after Kony asked what he brought for him.
-Lotiba reported that 13 people have escaped from his group.
-Among them is Omony, Okello, Okumu, Oyena, Oneka, Remu, Kalacel, Opoka, Odek. Onen Apyeto, Opoka Iron Fist, Kidega Iron Fist, a soldier belonging to Labalpiny and Okot Odek. That he had sent them on food mission. He added that he had looted enough maize.
-Labongo told Abudema not to be worried because he sent out a squad. He said that he hit UPDF that was following him.
-Okuti told Abudema that he sent the remaining soldiers of Anywar to him on Monday but Abudema said that he has not received them. That one Odongo escaped while on food mission.

Original_Search_Title: Arrow Boys Get Aid From Yambio Chiefs
Headline: Sudan: Arrow Boys Get Aid From Yambio Chiefs
Body_Text:
Yambio — The paramount chief of Yambio county who is also the chairperson of traditional authorities in Western Equatoria's ministry of local government Chief Wilson Peni disclosed that, 25 April he received donations from chiefs of Yambio County as humanitarian aid support.
Chiefs in Yambio county have resorted to taking contributions to support the home guards commonly known as the arrow boys or village vigilantes to boost their operations against the Lord's Resistance Army (LRA).
The LRA is a militia originally from northern Uganda. They have been the scourge of the region for many years, mutilating, raping, murdering and abducting citizens.
Arrow boys are local militias who have taken up arms to defend their communities from the LRA.
Peni said that "chiefs of Yambio have so far collected over 21,000 Sudanese pounds [US$7,860] from communities of Yambio county, 89 buckets of maize corn, 108 buckets of dura [sorghum], 150 buckets of cassava flour, 129 buckets of groundnuts [peanuts], 19 bottles of termite oil, 10 packets of table salt, and 18 bottles of honey."
Village chiefs conducted the exercise of small donations over a three week period.
He added that "the contribution comes as a result of the failure of the Government of Southern Sudan [GOSS] to release the 5 million Sudanese pounds which was approved by the South Sudan legislative assembly to the arrow boys."
The chief mentioned that as it is time for cultivation, the home guards have vowed to provide protections to their communities to enable them cultivate this year to bolster food supply.
"The small contribution will assist increase the morale of the home guards to comb the bush in new tactics that they have devised against the LRA" he said.
A former LRA captive being receiving after his escape, 2009 (Richard Ruati)
He stated that the scarcity of attacks by the LRA suggests that the arrow boys have almost finished the cruel fighters, hence those remaining are only "looking for food and survival, therefore we cannot give them chance to feed on our food any longer."
Peni pointed out that the utilisation of the money will be decided by a committee which was already formed, so that the cash is used appropriately and will foster their operations and the food items will be distributed to the home guards at their various camps.
The chief also said that he will tell all the chiefs around Yambio "to contribute with manpower nine youth members and hunters who are environmental-friendly with the dense forest of WES."
"As a new tactic I will also call upon the nearby counties, like Nzara, Ibba, and Ezo to send more hunters so that we join together and comb the bush once and for all," stressed Peni.
He disclosed that "Yambio County has been attacked over 18 times this year," adding that "it seems the target of the LRA is now the state headquarters; therefore the focal point is Yambio."
Most women and girls from Western Equatoria state who have been abducted and retained by the LRA are taken to serve as "wives" for the LRA commanders.
According to Gloria Isani a former captive "those who do not comply with the sexual demands made on them are beaten or killed."
Members of arrow boys display their welded weapons in Ezo, 2009 (Richard Ruati)
Eyewitness tell harrowing stories of the babies which have been born in the LRA camps, and for those young women who would otherwise try to escape and return to their families, the social stigma of having become pregnant under such circumstances adds to their reluctance and fear to return home.
"It is the first step that we have taken, therefore it is now the responsibility of every son and daughter of the state in their various capacities and places worldwide to contribute for the welfare of citizens of the state" he appealed.
Peni urged the communities of Yambio county who have not contributed "to at least give something that will let the state solve the LRA issue forever more specially this year."
He also called upon the GoSS to release the money which was approved by the regional assembly to foster military action in the fight against the insurgencies of the LRA rebels.
During the Equatoria conference 2011, in Juba, one of the resolutions was the follow up of the SDG5 million, which was approved by the regional assembly by the three governors of Equatoria.
The vice president of GoSS, Riek Machar Teny, in his closing remarks at the Equatoria conference in Juba on the 16 April 2011 disclosed that "the former Joint Integrated Units (JIUs) will be deployed in Western Equatoria to beef up security of the state.
However, there has been criticism against government from citizens of Western Equatoria, many of whom still remember the 2007 incident when three senior police officers were shot dead in Yambio. Members of the JIU were blamed for the killings but no one has ever been tried.
Together with their allies, the Ugandan army have been pursuing the LRA fighters since destroying their basis in Garamba on 14 December 2008.
The military actions have failed to defeat the LRA rebels, however, allowing them to rampage across the regions of Southern Sudan, DRC and CAR for the past three years, leaving thousands Congolese and South Sudanese civilians displaced, abducted and dead.
Despite the system of terror that reigns within the camps and the deadly punishment for showing disloyalty, children do escape from the LRA and make their way to freedom.
The African Union has also taken step to deploy joint operations against the LRA by the members of the three affected countries, according to their reports.
The six-year reign of violence by LRA rebels, mainly in Western Equatoria has scattered thousands of WES citizens. Since the LRA were dislodged from their Garamba park assembly point they stepped up attacks and reprisal killings, burning, looting and abducting many children and women in Western Equatoria.

Original_Search_Title: We Are Taming the Thieves in Health - Dr Atwine
Headline: Uganda: We Are Taming the Thieves in Health - Dr Atwine
Body_Text:
In 2009 President Museveni set up the Medicines and Health Services Delivery Monitoring Unit to rid the health sector of corruption. The unit has caused arrests of many health officials for alleged corruption. The Independent's Mubatsi Asinja Habati spoke to the unit Director Dr Diana Atwine and below are excerpts.
You have been unearthing corruption in health facilities in the country. What do you do after arresting the culprits?
Once a culprit has been arrested, the usual course of the law is followed. When we follow the line of prosecution all our files are sanctioned by the Director of Public Prosecutions' office. They are then sent to courts of law. But cases are not the same, so we treat culprits on a case-by-case basis. For example in some instances administrative measures usually led by district heads have been recommended and used to bring about the desired positive change among errant workers.
In your 2010 report some health workers would ask pregnant mothers or patients to bring them foodstuffs like cassava, eggs, millet etc as "appreciation". What does this say about the code of ethics and corruption in the health system?
It means the code of conduct is either nonexistent or not taken seriously - both of which are absurd. It implies that corruption gets worse by the day.
Other than exposing the corrupt, what else are you doing about corruption?
We are engaging stakeholders at both health worker training and workplace levels to take the code of conduct in healthcare delivery seriously. We are advocating a number of changes including within the curriculum itself and also on-the-job assessment as far as proper conduct is concerned. We are also engaging the public as receivers of this service and letting them know their rights. If they become aware that they should be at the heart of healthcare delivery and what to expect when they go to health facilities, then patients will realise that health workers are not doing them a 'favour' by treating them. Rather it is their basic right and no one should ask for anything in return. With these and other measures we shall see the return of proper code of conduct among health workers.
In Kagadi Hospital a hernia patient died after his relatives failed to raise 150,000 the doctor demanded an operation. Have you encountered such cases?
Many of them. For example in Itoojo Hospital parents of a young girl who had been defiled were turned away by a doctor who wanted money to give a medical report which the police required in order to work on the defilement case. The young girl later died. Such cases are leading us to the medico-legal territory. You see such an issue is multi-faceted and requires looking at it from at least three different angles - the medical, the legal and the law enforcement side. Most of these cases are very complex. The capacity in our nation in terms of medico-legal experts to handle some of these cases is still limited. But we are beginning to engage clinicians, the judiciary, police and other stakeholders to act. Those found guilty of charges such as neglect of duty or extortion in government hospitals are being dealt with.
In the same report, Mulago Hospital was transferring government medicines from the hospital's public pharmacy to the hospital's Private Wing pharmacy for sale. Yet in April 2010 a decision was reached by the hospital administration to stop this. What are you doing about it?
There has been change of guard at Mulago Hospital and a new management is in place. We are working with the new management to ensure that past mistakes are not repeated. On our part we shall continue monitoring how things take shape at Mulago.
Are local leaders supportive of your efforts to improve the health system?
We have found majority of local leaders very supportive of our initiatives. In places where we have gone we have called most of them to witness what's going on in their backyards. This has served a dual purpose - making the leaders more responsible and knowledgeable as local monitors but also making health workers more accountable to their local leaders who are more accessible to them than us. Most of the local leaders thought that monitoring health requires very complex and technical skills so they shy away from asking about anything. Wherever the Monitoring Unit has gone it has tried to demystify health monitoring and show people how they can be involved. And this has yielded results. But monitoring service delivery is everyone's responsibility. If all people in their different roles contributed to improving healthcare delivery it would be more effective.

Original_Search_Title: Usaid Assures Support to Forest Sector
Headline: Liberia: Usaid Assures Support to Forest Sector
Body_Text:
The Mission Director of USAID-Liberia, Patricia Rader, and a team of USAID officials paid a one day courtesy visit to Nimba on Tuesday 19 April 2011, where she was officially introduced as the new head of USAID in Liberia to the local authority of Nimba and leaders of communities where the USAID-supported Land Rights and Community Forestry Program (LRCFP) is piloting Community Forestry (CF).
Ms. Rader expressed satisfaction over the level of accomplishments made in community forestry (CF) in pilot communities of the LRCFP in Nimba.
Speaking at an elaborate ceremony marking her first official visit to Nimba, one of two counties in Liberia where LRCFP is piloting community forestry, she said her organization will continue to support the work of the project in Liberia.
Speaking in Zolowee Town at an interactive dialogue with representatives of community institutions, Miss Rader said she was impressed with the level of understanding demonstrated by community people on forest management and hoped that communities benefitting from the project's intervention will utilize the opportunity to improve their lot.
During an acquaintance meeting and lunch in Sanniquellie, the County Inspector of Nimba, Thomas Suah, spoke on behalf of the County Superintendent.
Mr. Suah underscored the significance of LRCFP intervention in the county and called on the new USAID Mission Director to continue support to the work of the project in the county. He said: "We want to thank you very much for the support you are giving to the partners that are working with our local people, and we promise that we will provide the political will to support the project in every way".
In separate remarks, the District Commissioners of Sanniquellie Mah Gabriel Gbanlon, Gbelay-Geh, John Quato, the Paramount Chiefs of Sehyi Willie Tokpa and Yarmein, Cooper Walaka, as well as the Mayor of Sanniquellie, Mary Gonlepah highlighted the collaboration between the local authority and the LRCFP and called for USAID to extend the duration of the project period so that community people can be fully capacitated.
As part of the visit, the USAID boss and delegation toured several project sites including a cassava processing mill, a nursery for the propagation of non-timber forest products (NTFPs) and a palm oil milling site where community groups are engaged in income generating activities.
LRCFP is a USAID project assisting the Forestry Development Authority (FDA) and forest-based communities to jointly manage Liberia's forest lands.
Recent policy and legal reforms in Liberia's forestry sector mandate FDA to empower and assist Liberian communities to manage their forest resources sustainably. LRCFP works with FDA and other stakeholders to develop this capacity at the national, county, and community levels. The LRCFP works with Zor and Gba communities, north of Sanniquellie in Nimba County, and with Nitrian and Nimopoh communities, east of Greenville, in Sinoe County.

Original_Search_Title: New Cassava Republic Store Opening Soon
Headline: Nigeria: New Cassava Republic Store Opening Soon
Body_Text:
Lagos — By May, a new outlet of Cassava Republic will be opened at Abuja, around Arts and Crafts Village, opposite Sheraton Hotel, next to the
Yar adua Centre in the Central Business District. The aim of the publishing outfit is for visitors to be able to get copies of all their titles as well as books by other African writers without stress. It was also declared that each month, there will be a new exhibition by a photographer available for purchase.
In a press statement issued by the outfit recently, all book purchase through the organisation will be donated to Reading And Writing (RAW) Book Drive Initiative.
RAW is an NGO supporting literacy development and creative writing programmes in children and their families in the Abuja environ. It is also seeking volunteers to go into communities on Saturdays to read to children. It also welcomes donations of creative books, games and toys.
Ankara Press, a fresh new voice publishing romantic fiction for the African market has also been recently introduced by Cassava Republic to readers. "We believe that today's African woman deserves a romance that reflects the full richness of her life. We want to showcase the modern African woman in all her strength and complexity while giving her the tools to shape her own destiny. In our stories, independent, capable women meet handsome, charming men who will respect their choices. We are seeking strong, original voices who can tell fast-paced and engaging stories. We want scenarios that discard dangerous notions of male dominance, control and manipulation. Above all, we want writers who will allow African women to see the best version of themselves in print." Meanwhile, writers that meet these requirements have been asked to reach the organisers through AnkaraSubmissions@gmail.com for submission guidelines.

Original_Search_Title: What is the Walk-to-Work Campaign About?
Headline: Uganda: What is the Walk-to-Work Campaign About?
Body_Text:
OFWONO OPONDO - NRM
DEFEATED, dejected and frustrated, the opposition led by Dr Kizza Besigye, and Norbert Mao on Monday tried a disguised effort dubbed "walk-to-work" protest allegedly against the rising cost of living, especially the rise in fuel and foodstuffs.
It is strange that they think mere walking to work and not hard work can cause economic miracles in Uganda.
And why did Besigye sit in the middle of the road, disrupting other traffic if his intention was to walk to work, or perhaps politicking is his occupation!
Opposition leaders, particularly those from the Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) have made a song claiming Uganda is about to explode due to an alleged severe socio-economic situation caused by President Yoweri Museveni. Having seen it not coming, they are trying to incite it.
This is the reason Besigye presented a flowery but fictitious election manifesto tinged with a political diatribe which the voters roundly rejected.
Their "walk-to-work" protests found me, like most Ugandans, putting our best efforts in private investments and so did not attract much of my attention, except for the usual gullible media that holds some opposition leaders as some sort of heroes.
Elections are over and those with genuine claims can go to courts of law instead of inciting violence against the Government, and to do so they should attract legitimate, prompt, effective and decisive police response.
Although it is called a "peaceful protest", the main objective of the walk was to hoodwink the Police, move from different locations, assemble at Christ the King Church and launch a massive civil disobedience campaign which the opposition leaders have been calling for against the NRM government and President Yoweri Museveni.
The opposition leaders with the support of some fifth columnists in the Church, and naïve western European missions had planned the assembly point as the begging of a massive protest similar to what happened in Tunis, Cairo and Benghazi, which would be difficult, if not impossible, for the security services to end without bloodshed.
These groups had planned that once successful at Christ the King, exploiting its sanctity, the mob would then descend of the Constitutional Square to declare the so-called "Freedom Day" and with the support of western governments Museveni would have been hard-pressed not to grant concessions to the opposition.
Fortunately, so far so good, because both intelligence and the Police have been so vigilant in collection, analysis and operational matters that they are on top of the opposition plans to destabilise the country.
Further more, the public has ignored the opposition's tantrums as cases of bad losers in the just-concluded general elections in which Besigye and Mao got 26% and 2% respectively.
The half-clever way in which they are trying to hype and exploit the escalating cost of living is a hollow strategy that the public won't buy into because they know that neither NRM nor Museveni is direct cause of these hardships.
Mao is so embarrassed by the dismal performance at the elections in which he had sung so loud like Aggrey Awori previously, that he (Mao) must try to find an explanation however unreasonable. And by what he does now, Mao risks going into oblivion like Michael Kaggwa of the DP Mobilisers' Group, and one Wasswa Ziritwaula of Tap Dialogue, another defunct DP faction. In the alternative, Mao should read from the now 'dead' Young Democrats.
With these numerous public protests planned ahead to try and thwart Museveni's inauguration, Besigye is trying to be relevant, which those jostling for positions within FDC like Abdu Katuntu have to join in albeit belatedly.
WAFULA OGUTTU - FDC
The Sunday Vision editor wants us this week to answer the following question: 'What is this walk-to-work, walk-to-pray all about'?
After Thursday's actions, I would like to believe that many people now understand what this new form of struggle is all about.
On Thursday, the struggle spread, on its own, to several other towns of Uganda. On their own, ordinary Ugandans are heeding the call by Action for Change (A4C) to walk to work as a protest against the escalating prices of fuel and other basic goods.
The A4C message is non-partisan and resonates well with all those who are increasingly finding it hard to put a decent meal on their family tables.
Prices of basic needs like staple food-stuffs, salt, soap, sugar and transport have more than doubled in the past four months.
Citizens were, therefore, praying and hoping that the Government would see sense and do something about those escalating prices.
They were wrong. The first response, by Minister for Information and National Guidance, Kabakumba Matsiko was insensitive and provocative.
In a very casual and arrogant manner she said the Government would do nothing about the prices.
That "Uganda is not a welfare state" that gives out food handouts.
Matsiko's arrogance is the typical arrogance of most National Resistance Movement leaders that Ugandans have suffered for years.
They will not listen to those who question their actions and seek to hold them accountable.
To them, those are enemies of the state, full of envy (nugu) against the great achievements of the NRM, including developing the national economy at over 7% per year for over 20 years and reducing poverty levels from 58% to 25% in 25 years.
The second response was by Third Deputy Prime Minister Kirunda Kivejinja who dismissed people's demands for the Government to do something about the rising prices, saying it was merely a ploy by the opposition to topple the Government.
Opposition leaders are not mad.
They do not love Uganda less.
They do not find fun in going to the streets and suffering tear gas, beating, humiliation and being grabbed and thrown onto police pick-up trucks like bags of cassava as they did to me on April 11, on the orders of Jinja Road DPC Denis Kamugisha for walking to work.
The Government, which claims now to enjoy about 70% popularity in the country, continues to close all avenues for dialogue with the citizens and the opposition including in Parliament where they are outnumbered, outgunned, ever booed and ridiculed.
The issues of people's welfare, national priorities and interests including minority interests, the Government's financial discipline and accountability should be bipartisan issues in Parliament.
But this is not so to NRM and its security, army, and Police cadres who see the opposition as a nuisance that has nothing else to offer apart from paying taxes for the rulers to enjoy.
Whenever we go for elections, the NRM treats the opposition like it has accompanied them to a cultural introduction ceremony (kwanjula). No say after the bride is home.
After February 18, that is now a closed avenue.
Therefore, it is only logical that the opposition will try to find other peaceful ways of expression like 'walk-to-work' to protect and further the interests of the people they represent.

Original_Search_Title: Solving the Cement Price Hike(II)
Headline: Nigeria: Solving the Cement Price Hike(II)
Body_Text:
China dominates global cement consumption at 1,105kg per capita, representing about 50 per cent of global cement consumption.
While Nigeria's low consumption reflects the historically weak investment in infrastructure for socio-economic and political reasons, it also lends support to the long-term growth potential of the industry.
However, the Federal Government's current commitment to revamping the nation's infrastructure would spur cement consumption in many ways.
In 2008, the Nigerian cement industry had an estimated market size of N361 billion (US$2.4 billion), or in aggregate consumption terms, 13.4 million tonnes, of which 46 per cent (6.2 million tonnes) was produced in Nigeria. Driven by the acute infrastructure deficit and significant demand for housing, domestic production volumes have grown at 25 per cent (CAGR) over the last four years.
Given the strong correlation between GDP growth and cement consumption, cement production growth has also been helped by Nigeria's strong economic performance in recent years. The Central Bank of Nigeria in 2008 said Nigeria needs US$510 billion in investments in infrastructure over the next 11 years if the nation was to achieve its vision of being one of the top-20 economies in the world by 2020.
The increasing demand for good quality housing, which is estimated at around 16 million housing units, is also expected to be a key catalyst for industry sales growth. Consequently, the CBN anticipated that demand will remain strong, with industry growth averaging between 12% and 15% in 2009, 10%-12% in 2010, and 15% to 20% thereafter.
Lead Capital Investments Lagos recently prepared a report on the cement industry in Nigeria wherein it provided an analysis of the industry explaining the challenges and opportunities within the industry. Some of the highlights include:
*There is the problem of demand versus supply inequality. Supply has not been able to meet the ever growing demand.
*Local production has remained at 50% of installed capacity with an annual growth rate of 3%.
*A major challenge facing the industry is the lack of sufficient funding to carry out operations on a large scale.
*The industry has the potential to contribute to the larger economy as it can be a major employer of skilled and unskilled labour, a vehicle for foreign direct investments, and a tool for infrastructural development, and
*Nigeria's cement consumption is one of the lowest in Africa.
As stated earlier, the yawning gap between demand and supply of cement will continue to exist until and unless government policy of 'favouritism' is removed.
Although the Federal Government is aware of the problem in the industry, rather than finding lasting solution, it has resorted to using the advice of just one individual to determine the fate of millions of Nigerians.
It is common knowledge that since 2004, Dangote cement has boasted that it would bring the cost of cement down to N500 per bag. But that has remained unattainable. We all know the antics. Alhaji Aliko Dangote wants to first, push other producers out of the market and thereafter use monopoly to increase cement price to his satisfaction. He did same in the sugar industry which today sells between N200 and N300 per packet as against N60 per packet before he came in.
It is trite to compare the production of cement to grinding of corn or cassava. There are many issues germane to the manufacturing of cement and which if not well handled would make the achievement of projections impossible.
Suffice it to say that Nigeria is so big a country for cement industry policy to be written around an individual. Industry operators have noted series of misleading press reports and comments being orchestrated by Dangote for the purpose of negating the very compelling reasons for which the Federal Government initiated the backward integration policy in 2001.
These press reports mostly trade on sophistry and guile, in order to obfuscate the truth and confuse the very same Nigerians for whose benefit the policy was drawn up. Quite obviously this calumnious press campaign is being orchestrated by the same oligopolistic cartel - CMAN- who claims to have the capacity to meet the entire cement needs of Nigerians.
Obinna Nwachukwu, a commentator on national issues,writes from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Besigye and Others Arrested for 'Illegal Walking' - a Road is Not a Road
Headline: Uganda: Besigye and Others Arrested for 'Illegal Walking' - a Road is Not a Road
Body_Text:
I knew the news from Kampala was a tragic-comedy when the SMS and emails couldn't stop coming. Following the arrest of opposition Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) leader Dr Kizza, Democratic Party (DP) chief Norbert Mao, and other opposition leaders in Kampala on Monday as they walked to work to protest skyrocketing prices of fuel, Twitter, Facebook and all manner of instant messaging services were full of giggles about how "walking to work was now illegal" in Uganda.
A TV station in Kenya had a caption reading "Uganda opposition leader arrested for walking illegally". You can bet that the video of Besigye being confronted on the road by soldiers and police and being told he had been banned from walking to work; him sitting on the road to protest his harassment; being bundled on the back of a police pick-up truck; and being hauled off it like a sack of potatoes is going to be big on YouTube.
You might say it's unbelievable that a government can react in such an absurd fashion to a few opposition leaders footing it to town with rucksacks on their backs. However, if you know the history of strongman regimes in Africa, this is one of their signatures. In Nigeria, for example, during the rule of the military tyrant Sani Abacha, the state would send security officials to arrest editors, then two days later would come back and arrest their wives, their babies, their gardeners, cooks, and pet dogs and cats!
There is something bigger. The road has been the sharp edge of the clash between the despotic state in Uganda, and civil society and opposition leaders since, particularly, Idi Amin's time. In many ways, the turning point for Amin's regime was 1977 when, as we now know, he had murdered Archbishop of the Church of Uganda, Janan Luwum, at the infamous State Research dungeons in Nakasero. Amin, however, still felt that that was not complete. So he had Luwum's body put in a car, and he stage-managed an accident, in which his regime claimed was where Luwum died.
Besigye was arrested smack in the centre of the road at Busega on November 14, 2005, three weeks after he returned from exile in South Africa. Now, again, they have done the same thing. There is striking similarity with the arrest of FDC official and Bukhooli Central MP-elect Wafula Oguttu, also being carried off the road.
In 2005, then Speaker Francis Ayume was killed in a road accident. Some of his supporters claim to this day that it was foul play.
In the lead-up to the last February elections, at least two presidential candidates - Bidandi Ssali and Norbert Mao - had close calls in car accidents. Of course, presidents too, from Amin to President Museveni have been involved in car accidents. And here is where we begin to understand the importance of the road. When our presidents, and sometimes their wives, are on the road their convoys spread across and force all other motorists off.
There are security reasons for this, but by travelling beyond the speed and hogging the road, the big men project power. That is because in our culture, and that of many other sources, we have this strong belief that the one thing that belongs to us all is the road. We accept it when we are chased off the lake, or when access to the airport is restricted. But the right to use the road, to walk it, even to dry cassava on its edges as the people do along Tirinyi Road, we consider that an inalienable right.
The presidential convoy pushes humble motorists because the president is projecting his power. Drivers of lorries and Land Cruisers intimidate drivers of smaller cars because they are bigger. Motorists splash pedestrians with mud water because they have more mechanical power. If a government takes away your possibility to walk on the road, it is a major blow to your citizenship rights and an act of humiliation. A Besigye insisting on walking the road is a claim on his wider rights.
And if the state can prevent you from using the road, or injure you in a car accident while you are on it and get away with it, then it has nearly taken everything away. That is why, for those who remember the Amin days, the ultimate show of power by his security goons was when they parked their cars in the middle of the street and chatted away as other motorists queued up behind them, terrified. If they honked, they would be killed. If they turned and drove away, it would be considered a protest, and they would be killed.
However, we might have arrived at this view of the road rather late. In 1965 Nobel laureate, Nigerian author Wole Soyinka, published a little but telling play called "The Road". I won't spoil it for you. If you haven't read it, or watched it being performed, please do. Soyinka's take 46 years ago was that a road, is not a road, is not a road. Besigye and his FDC fellows have now learnt that too.

Original_Search_Title: Ivoirian Refugees Hard to Reach
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Ivoirian Refugees Hard to Reach
Body_Text:
Dakar — Ivoirians who have fled to eastern and southeastern Liberia are choosing to settle in villages rather than camps and transit centres, making them harder to help, say NGO workers.
Most of the 130,000 Ivoirians who have fled into in Liberia since December 2010 are scattered across 90 villages in Nimba and Grand Geddeh counties, according to UN Refugee Agency (UNHCR) spokesperson Suleiman Momodu.
Ivoirians feel safest staying with host communities just across the border from their homes, as they may have relatives in these villages or share the same ethnic background, said Anika Krstic, spokesperson with the Danish Refugee Council (DRC) in Liberia's capital Monrovia.
As a result, a refugee camp in Bahn in Nimba County, 50km from the border, is sheltering some 2,500 refugees, despite being built to house up to 15,000.
Many Ivoirians return to their villages by day to keep up their livelihoods, re-crossing into Liberia at night, said Krstic. "With population movements continually shifting, it's hard to figure out who has already been registered and who is being registered for the first time," she added.
Poor roads impede access to many host villages said DRC, which is helping provide water and sanitation in transit centres, where refugees are temporarily housed before finding longer-term shelter.
Refugees who stay near the border, and continually cross back into Côte d'Ivoire put themselves at risk of attack, says DRC. Members of militias allegedly infiltrated border villages housing refugees in the southeast but were subsequently detained by Liberian police.
On 2 April, Valerie Amos, UN under-secretary-general for humanitarian affairs, warned of the potential for instability to spread across the border. "After years of war, Liberians are finally seeing the benefits of investment in peace, security and stability. We need to maintain that and ensure the country gets the help it needs, even as it welcomes so many refugees."
Host communities have largely welcomed the refugees thus far, as many of them were displaced during Liberia's 14-year civil war. One couple in Puuto, Nimba County, has taken in 75 Ivoirian refugees, giving them all their rice, most of their cassava supply, and whatever clothes they could find, according to David Waines, country director of NGO Equip. The couple just helped a refugee deliver her baby, and is helping another very sick refugee to recover.
However, many villages are becoming strained as new arrivals have caused their populations to double.
The local authorities have also been very responsive to the refugees' needs, according to Waines. The Liberian government's Refugee Repatriation and Resettlement Commission is leading the refugee aid response, working alongside UNHCR, the World Food Programme, the UN Children's Fund, and NGOs, including Equip, DRC, the Norwegian Refugee Council, Save the Children, and Oxfam.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Celebrating Ghariokwu as Goethe Institut Reopens
Headline: Nigeria: Celebrating Ghariokwu as Goethe Institut Reopens
Body_Text:
Lagos — Cultural exchange with other countries is a very important means of deepening understanding and promoting international friendship and goodwill worldwide. With interdependence among nations on the increase, it has become more and more important to promote mutual understanding of such social premises as language, custom, and cultural traditions and to strengthen heart-to-heart contacts through cultural exchange.
In promoting cultural exchange, it is necessary to make efforts to understand other countries' cultures as well as to promote their understanding through introducing the language, customs and cultural traditions to people in these nations. And when the German cultural centre, the Goethe Insitut, went off the radar in 2009, the art community felt its absence.
However, it was a gathering of the who-is-who in the Nigerian art world amidst fanfare, music and dance, the cultural centre was reopened. With the Lagos City Hall as its new domicile, director of the centre, McAndre Schmatel, said that with the new location, publicity would no longer be an issue.
Declaring the centre open to activities, Schmatel said that the centre was cited at the Lagos City Hall for strategic reason, in the sense that not only is it a good location for Nigerians, it is also central with good parking space.
"When I came to Nigeria in October last year, the programme for 2011 was already set up, presuming that the centre will take off very soon. Luckily enough, this came to reality for us and now that we are celebrating the opening of the centre we hope to start our regular programme. I can assure you this will be done very shortly," the director enthused.
Expressing delight at the new home of the centre, Schmatel said that every location has its advantages and disadvantages. "The disadvantage of this place is that in City Hall, we cannot do everything that we used to do in our former location. We have to look for partners in Lagos."
However, he said, the centre intends to use that to its advantage as the situation provides the possibility to go towards its objectives. "This is very important to me. This time around people will not only come to Goethe, Goethe will have to go to the people," he said.
The regional head of Sub-Saharan Africa, of the Goethe Institut, Katrina von Ruckteschell-Kate, described Lagos, host state of the centre, as the most vibrant city in sub-Saharan Africa.
"I am really happy that Goethe Institut has a shelter again. We are not roofless anymore. In the sub-Saharan region, Nigeria plays such a huge role in bringing together artists, filmmakers, directors, musicians, writers and artistes. This is happening here in a very special way," she opined.
One of the major roles of the centre, she said, is to bring together artist groups. "I think this is what can happen here now that the centre is bigger. I know you have started here already with a lot of energy, I hope that this will go on," she said.
At the opening, Consul General of the German Consulate in Lagos, Walter von den Dreisch, said that Goethe Institut worldwide acts as the cultural centre of the Federal Republic of Germany. Being in Nigeria since 1962, he said, makes it almost the twin of Nigerian independence.
"In the course of those nearly 50 years of its existence, the institut has built a very good reputation among Nigerians mainly by its successful effort to provide space for young artists; presenting them to a wider public. Many of the Nigerian artists, men and women now known or famous have performed in Goethe Institut, be it in photography, dramatic arts or dance," he said.
The effort of the cultural team, according to Dreisch, continues to be praised. "The exchange aspect of Goethe Institut is most important. International cultural cooperation which not only boosts the exchange between Germany and Nigeria but increasingly promotes the exchange between Nigerian and other African countries has become an important aspect of the centre's activities," he added.
Part of its activities, as explained by the Consular General includes also providing a comprehensive picture of Germany by giving information about the cultural, social and political life in Germany. "In this respect, Goethe Institut's library is here in the interest of providing for various information. We are glad that the institut is now domiciled in City Hall in the centre of the heart of old Lagos. Although not optimal, it permits however continuous cultural work and language teaching in a decent environment, both creating conditions for Goethe Institut worldwide existence. Culture is an important part of our exchange," Dreisch said.
As the centre was declared open, images of the late Afrobeat sage, Fela Kuti, a cultural icon in his own rights, came alive in Lemi Ghariokwu's art pieces dotting the exhibition hall. A self-taught album cover designer, Lemi, uses his illustrations to convey the messages in music albums.
A fine artist, graphic designer, illustrator and songwriter, he is well known for his captivating and complex record sleeves. He received global acclaim by creating the cover artwork for many of Fela Kuti's records. He has also designed the cover of Cassava Republic's republication of Fela: This Bitch of a Life, the authorised biography of Fela Kuti.
Using vibrant colours and unique typefaces of his own design, a style he has come to be known for, Lemi's display woke up memories of the late Afrobeat sage in the minds of guests. Three conspicuous works, Yellow Fever, I no get eye for Back, and Fela: The Best of the Black President adorned the new exhibition hall of the centre.
As an artist, Lemi has designed more than 2,000 album covers in the last 36 years, including covers for Bob Marley, E. T. Mensah, Osita Osadebe, Mandators, Orits Williki, Gilles Peterson, Sony Okosuns, Oliver De Coque, Miriam Makeba, Lucky Dube, Antibalas, Akoya Afrobeat, Dele Sosimi, Tony Tetuila, Eedris Abdulkareem, Tuface Idibia and a host of others.
The cover images on display tries to echo the life and times of the late and still celebrated Fela Kuti. Yellow fever is a deadly ailment known to have taken lots lives in Africa. In Nigeria, the term became used to refer to traffic wardens because of the their troublesome nature in the past and the orange colour of their outfit.
Fela, however, made the term even more popular with his songs, which addresses the issue of ladies changing the colour of their skins. In fusing his mind with that of the singer, Ghariokwu's portrayal of Yellow Fever reflects the sensuous nature of Fela's performances on stage. His relationship with Fela Kuti was known to be very cordial. Ghariokwu's work has attracted much attention in the West and is the subject of various exhibitions. In June 2010, Lemi was commissioned and successfully branded Fela-Bus, a marketing painting on wheels, for the producers of the hit Broadway musical "Fela!" in New York.
Spiced with music and dance by Orlando Julius, Ade Bantu and German-based Chinasa, the event was well attended by the greats in the art community like the legendary Bruce Onobrakpeya.

Original_Search_Title: Campaign Posters Come, Go With Costs
Headline: Uganda: Campaign Posters Come, Go With Costs
Body_Text:
MODERN elections are an expensive operation requiring several ways of communicating with the voters, the most popular being posters and billboards. But they don't come cheap or go away cheap either, as Esther Namugoji writes
As the country puts the hectic general elections behind it, reminders remain all over the place. The noisy trucks carrying ribald dancers may have gone silent, but one prominent medium of the campaigns will take some time to disappear.
This election has seen a lot of investment in different media to promote various aspirants' candidature, but posters have remained a time-tested favourite. For some candidates, posters were the only affordable choice, so they unleashed a flurry of paper on the public.
Wall fences were besieged; lampposts and other sign stems were covered in patchwork, as if in the beginning stages of a papier-marche project. Street dustbins that are usually ignored have become prime advertising space. Even tree trunks are not spared the forced adornment.
Now that the elections are over, the country must return to normal, but who will take charge of the clean-up and restoration of the ruined walls and artwork on signposts and at what cost?
Outdoor advertising companies are among those who will be picking up the tab for damage caused by posters. According to Jerome Hall, the client services manager of Adman Source, the company has billboards in different parts of the country and is still in the early stages of assessing the amount of damage and what needs to be done to rectify it. He noted, though, that the most damage seems to be in Wakiso, Kampala, Mukono and Entebbe.
Poster monster
One innocent-looking poster can do a lot of damage. This depends on its size and coverage of the adhesive element used, as well as the type of adhesive. Some people use a solution of cassava flour to stick their posters while others apply different types of glue which affect the advertiser's sticker on the signpost.
Layers of half-stripped posters add a fuzzy white coat where it was not intended. Over-enthusiastic application of glue leaves walls pock-marked and street signs unrecognisable.
"First, you cannot wash off the glue and secondly it has an effect on the sticker, which stretches around the glue and appears burnt. In most cases we have to replace everything," says Hall.
Hall puts an estimated figure of sh38m as losses incurred following the 2006 campaigns, while noting that at that time, not as much money was pumped into the elections as in the February 2011 elections. Sometimes there may be a fortunate coincidence that the client is due to change the content of the advert and will pay for new artwork. Otherwise, since they pay to rent the signpost for a specific period, the ad company has to cover the replacement of the advert.
"Thankfully most of our clients have been understanding because they know it was election time. We have to bear the cost of removing the posters and replacing the advert stickers," Hall says.
"It's expensive; because we use vehicles and staff to go out to different suburbs to remove those posters. We just left them (to put the posters), but it is tiresome," says a source from Atom Outdoor Ltd.
It costs between sh800,000 and sh1.6m to have an advertisement maintained for a month on an outdoor sign, depending on the size. The outdoor advertising firms have to pay some money to the town authority to allow them put up a billboard. In Kampala City Council, the biggest sign is charged sh1.3m a year, while the smallest billboard costs sh191,000 annually. Those who place their posters on top of an advertisement gain visibility at another company's expense.
The Gulu Municipality Town Clerk, Augustine Bujara, says candidates are supposed to pay advertising fees, inclusive of VAT, for the posters and billboards put up during the elections period. Many of them did not.
Simon Muhumuza, the Kampala City Council spokesperson, says there is a policy against the placement of posters without permission from the property owner.
"We don't like (posters on the streets) and we have guidelines on where they can be placed. But during election periods it is difficult to enforce, so we just let it go."
Who will clean up?
The teams that put up posters illegally do it under the cover of darkness, which makes it hard to hold someone responsible. The Gulu Town Clerk has no kind words for individuals who place their posters without regard to others. Bujara is of the view that the candidates should be held liable for the acts of their agents because they benefited from the promotion.
However, Bujara says the town authority has no choice but to clean up the mess, though the individuals should be held liable for restoration of property or monuments to their original state. He says there are no immediate plans to clean up yet, because he has no permission from the Electoral Commission declaring that it is no longer a crime to deface campaign posters.
"Since posters are not an environmental hazard, we are not in a rush," Bujara states, adding jokingly that the rain will take care of some posters. For now, the Peace Monument at the centre of Gulu town remains covered in bits and pieces of paper until the cleaning crew grudgingly comes round.
In Kampala, the clean-up is being done in the divisions using the same workers employed by the council. Muhumuza says sometimes a few extra tools may be procured to scrape some of the residues, "but I don't think the extra cost is more than sh200,000."
In some local governments, posters caused a storm when opposition politicians complained that the incumbent and ruling party candidates' posters were pasted all over public premises like sub-county headquarters or schools. Only in a few cases was this unfair advantage addressed or rectified.
But Lyantonde MP James Kakooza explains that this is an urban problem as the situation in rural areas is different. Up-country, the posters are mostly pinned at people's homes and with their permission. Besides, he explains, most people take their favourite candidate's poster to paste on their own property voluntarily. They then remove them at will.
The Electoral Commission, however, has only a rule making it illegal to vandalise, remove or otherwise desecrate a candidate's election poster. The EC public relations officer, Willy Ochola, says: "The Electoral Commission's position is clear that candidates must obtain permission from owners of property before they put posters there. They discuss the terms and conditions for using the space and when the campaigns are over, they are supposed to remove their posters."
Ochola says the only crime is when someone defaces another candidate's poster or places his/her poster on top of another's. The Police arrested some culprits for this during the recent elections.
Even outside the campaign period, major towns were already groaning under the ugly weight of posters advertising a hodgepodge of services like visa and job opportunities, body modification and match-making services, cleaning and coaching services. These have continued to flourish, advertising ever new fads.
Come 2016 or any other election before then, not much is likely to change. Posters will simply continue their dominance of the landscape, conquering every available vantage space, leaving others to pay for the free visibility and the clean-up costs afterwards.

Original_Search_Title: An African Solution to the Crisis?
