Headline: Africa: An African Solution to the Crisis?
Body_Text:
Bloggers across the continent are dissatisfied, dismayed and disappointed by the Africa Union's handling of the crises in Libya and in Côte d'Ivoire, writes Dibussi Tande.
On the Up Station Mountain Club collective blog, Lloney Monono writes to Jean Ping, Chairperson of the Africa Union Commission, to expresses his dissatisfaction with the AU's (mis)handling of the Libyan crisis:
'It is clear that there could be no misunderstanding as to what a No Fly Zone entailed, since in a widely reported address to a US Congressional Committee, on March 1st 2011, the US Defence Secretary warned that a No Fly Zone would "require first destroying Libya's air defence forces" and went on to call it a "big operation in a big country."
'Therefore there was no ambiguity. The UN Security Council Resolution 1973 spelt military action against Libya, a sovereign state and member of the African Union...
'By choosing instead to put out a press statement on the 17th of March 2011 about an "Ad hoc High Level Committee" which had been agreed a week earlier on 10th March 2011 in the Peace and Security Council's 265th meeting whilst the UN Security Council deliberated and voted in Resolution 1973, the AU demonstrated that it had been dazed into impotence by the crisis. It fiddled at a crucial moment while Libya burned...
'As the Libyan defences are degraded with each bomb dropped we are witnessing the destruction of a sovereign state with the risk that it becomes a haven to anarchists and terrorists as did happen in Somalia, Iraq and Afghanistan. An eventuality which will further destabilise Libya, bring more insecurity to its citizens likewise worsen the peace and stability of the region, infringing further on the AU's objectives of Article 3 in the Constitutive Act.
'In the light of this view and other points highlighted herein, I conclude that the AU, with respect to the Libyan situation, has completely failed in managing the crisis; it has failed to prevail in both its diplomacy with Libya and the world beyond Africa's shores. Lastly, and on the point which churns my insides, the African Union has like the defunct Organisation of African Unity demonstrated a complete inability to take ownership of and resolve its problems. It has simply failed to step up to the plate and demonstrate African self-reliance, as mandated by Article 4 subsection (k) of the Constitutive Act, when was most needed. Quite simply and sadly, your excellency it is my view history would record that at this point in time, the African Union dismally failed to provide leadership.'
Jimmy Kainka echoes the same sentiments by expressing disappointment at the AU's handling of the Ivoirian and Libyan crises:
'If we move on to the north of Africa and take a look at Libya, we have unbelievable nightmare happening right in front of our eyes. Where is the AU? So many presidents from different nations have spoken on the events in Libya, Ban Ki Moon (UN Secretary General) has made several media appearances to state the stance of the UN on the issue. So what about the AU? If the AU is not in a position to solve African problems, then who will? Libya has moved from having its leader (...and yes, the former AU chairman) killing his own people to having Western troops in the name of saving Libyans - more Libyans may now die in the hands of foreign forces.
'Where are the Leaders of Africa? Who in Africa has ever gone to kill people in France or USA or UK? Where is the African Union? I heard of the many meetings that happened before the "no fly zone" was imposed on Libya, there was even a consultation with the Arab Leaders. Does anyone know if AU was consulted and what they voted for? While you (AU) are mulling over your existence, why not also consider pulling out of the UN en masse as the UN is really a Western Union. By pulling out AU would stop providing the UN with cover.
'My biggest question is, if the AU made numerous visits to Ivory Coast, to "try" and resolve the situation there, why are they not helping the people of Libya? I have watched children being killed and everyone keeps saying "there are always casualties when there is war". Why should a child be sacrificed for democracy or oil or chocolate or whatever it is that is going on there in Libya and Ivory Coast? AU, where are you? Libya needs you, Ivory Coast has not been sorted out, what are you doing? If events like these are too big for you, then why do you exist?'
On the other end of the spectrum, Rosebell believes that the much vaunted 'African solution' to the current crises propounded by the likes of Yoweri Museveni is a mirage:
'The West may be wrong in the way they conduct the intervention in Libya but President Museveni together with his group of mostly African dictators cannot be trusted to bring a solution fast enough.
'When Gaddafi was declaring genocide on his own people saying he would 'cleanse Libya house by house', no one stood up to him. When we heard stories about Gaddafi ferrying young Africans into Libya to work as mercenaries, which escalated racist attacks on African immigrants, no African president came out to call for investigations. So many Africans stuck in Libya including Ugandans have been at the mercy of aid groups and some few government rescue missions.
'Let's now forget for the last four months, this group of men who rule the continent have failed to resolve the situation in Ivory Coast which we may as well say has slipped back into a civil war. So far more than 400 people have died in Ivory Coast and all they do is hop on planes meet in Addis Ababa.
'No wonder we have heard no calls on the AU from Libya's opposition...
'Africans want an African solution but current leaders like President Museveni who stifle freedoms in their own borders will not deliver us the much needed African solution. And that's what North Africa has realized and therefore moved to rid their countries of these leaders. Like Desmond Tutu has stated, Libya wouldn't be seeing these strikes if African leaders were answerable to their peers and populace. But which of Gaddafi's peers would have kept him in check? Museveni, Biya or Mugabe?'
The Chia Report responds to those who are against intervention in Libya on the grounds that this would most likely create a chaotic power vacuum in Libya:
'I have been asking myself why many oppressors across the world, including miscreants like Gaddafi and Paul Biya of Cameroon, invariably get sympathies from the very ones they oppress. This concern is more often than not encapsulated in the following question: who will fill the vacuum (after Biya/Gaddafi...)? My all time favorite is "what next"?
'It is pitiful to look at the almighty Gaddafi ranting that "my people all love me". There are certainly many Libyans who would die for Colonel Gaddafi. But can Gaddafi and the Biyas of the world understand that it is not about love? Better still, can they grasp that love is not a one way street? Can they grasp that walking away from power and having others give a shot at leading is the greatest love there is to share with their kids and other fellow citizens?...
'It is a false premise to think and act as if every democratic nation on earth was born on the same day. But there is the expectation that younger democratic nations, even as they forge their own democratic cultures, will not slide even further back in excuse and mockery of time-tested universal values of freedom, liberty, and respect of human rights. The biggest democracy of our time (USA) was stretched thin as the Supreme Court stepped in to decide whether it was Al Gore or George Bush. Budding democracies will go through chaotic patches. It is essential that a people go through these times together. Lasting bonds are formed from common struggles. Better yet, from these common struggles, a people emerge with a common vision and purpose. African countries seem to have only one purpose - producing consumers.
'We are better than that. After the coalition bombs the hell out of Gaddafi's Libya, (and hopefully take him out) a leader will emerge. It is bound to happen.'
Bantu Politics points out that observers and international journalists are increasingly comparing the situation in Côte d'Ivoire to the 'Pre-Genocide' atmosphere of Rwanda in 1994, and explains ethnicity, religion and hate speech are fueling the flames of the political crisis:
'The question of religious identity resurfaced on December 3, 2010, when the Ivorian Constitutional Council, in opposition to Electoral Commission's results, declared Laurent Gbagbo president by invalidating the votes of some 600,000 people in the northern, mostly Muslim, regions of the country.
'Laurent Gbagbo and his wife, Simone, are both declared born-again Christians who are not shy about sharing their faith in a political context. On Simone Gbagbo's website, religion is said to have an important place in the former first lady's life and political commitment...
'One Ivorian native netizen posted a message on American politician Sarah Palin's Facebook page in December 2010, in which he urged American Christians not to let US President Obama standby without supporting the Gbagbos: "Will Americans idly sit on the side and watch their President humiliate Laurent Gbagbo and Simone Ehivet Gbagbo, a Born-again Evangelical Christian couple and throw them out of office or watch some Muslim rebels invade their palace and kill them with the conspiracy of the international community?'
Sacha Project, a blogger who resides mainly in Abidjan, wrote a post... referring to French daily Le Monde's editorial of the same day entitled, 'The future of Africa is playing out in Côte d'Ivoire', in which the author compares Côte d'Ivoire to the Spain of 1936 under Franco. Sacha Project believes that the comparison has a lot of merit: 'Like in the Spain of 1936, a state is threatened by an armed minority which refuses a lawfully elected president (...). Gbagbo is not Franco, Gbagbo is not a fascist. Yet the ideas of the Ivorian Patriotic Front (FPI) are. Franco didn't consider himself fascist, but 'nationalist'. Gbagbo considers himself a 'patriot'. Yet the excessive xenophobic nationalism, Ivorian identity and its eulogists, media manipulation, and Simone Gbagbo's religious speech which considers her husband's power as quasi-divine, these are elements of an Ivorian style fascism.'
In a guest post on the Cassava Republic blog, historian Max Siollun challenges Nigerian writers to make Nigerian history more interesting to readers:
'I was literally heartbroken when not too long ago, a Nigerian acquaintance of mine (born and raised in Nigeria) told me that she thought Herbert Macaulay was a white American. She could recite (in chronological order) most of the post-World War II American Presidents, but she had no idea that Herbert Macaulay was a Nigerian. She was shocked when I told her that Macaulay was to Nigeria, what George Washington was to the United States of America.
'Why do so many Nigerians know so little about their own country's history? The federal government must take much responsibility for this. Nigerian history is not intensively taught in schools largely because after the civil war, the federal government tried to brush the country's past under the carpet in order to foster reconciliation....But the government is not entirely to blame... We writers must also share the responsibility....We writers must present Nigerian history as something more than a mechanical rendering of dates and facts...
'Dry, ponderous academic style renditions of Nigerian history will not do. In my writing, I have tried to dramatise the historic events I write about and bring the characters to life, so as to capture the reader's imagination. The reader momentarily suspends the belief that what they are reading is in fact....fact! We must. To interest readers in Nigerian history, we must turn our national characters into "stars" and, in the popular vernacular of the Iraq war, 'sex up' Nigerian history. That is the challenge for me and other writers...'
* Dibussi Tande blogs at Scribbles from the Den.

Original_Search_Title: Health-Damaging Radiation Imminent in Middle-Belt - Inyang
Headline: Nigeria: Health-Damaging Radiation Imminent in Middle-Belt - Inyang
Body_Text:
Prof. Hilary I. Inyang is a globally renowned Nigerian-born U.S environmental scientist and the first African immigrant to chair a permanent U.S. Congress-chartered Science Committee and also the first black person to be endowed as a distinguished Professor of Environmental Engineer by any university in the United States. He is a professor of Environmental Engineering and Science, University of North Carolina, Charlotte, USA, and Protem Chancellor and Board Chair, African Continental University System (ACUS) Initiative, Abuja, Nigeria. In this interview with correspondent Godwin Egba in Port Harcourt, he speaks on environmental quality and the health of Nigerians among other issues. Excerpts:
Among mining regions visited by you are the Gerais Region of Brazil, the Jiangsu Province of China and Slovenia. As Nigeria embarks on intensification of mining projects to diversify its economy, what advice do you have on human health and environmental issues?
Mining is an economic sector in which there is always the potential to put base-level workers and host communities to significant health, environmental and safety risks. What needs to be done now in Nigeria to minimise future problems is to draw up desirable guidelines on corporate social responsibility in the sector. Besides, codes of standards for planning, construction and operation of the mines should be developed at the appropriate level of detail. For example, the Middle Belt of Nigeria will have a very high concentration of the mines. Unfortunately, the area has many radon-bearing rocks and other geomaterials that can generate health-damaging gamma radiation. Years ago, I brought an equipment and performed measurements that indicated very high levels of radiation in Abuja. Exposure of workers and residents of the region that spans the Middle Belt northward to Abuja, to radiation can lead to blood diseases, liver cancer and other ailments, as experienced in other countries. Mining science and engineering post-graduate programmes should be established in many Nigerian universities, to provide research support and trained personnel for its developing industry.
Are environmental problems more severe in the technologically advanced countries than in developing countries like Nigeria?
The environmental burden of each country should realistically be scaled as the magnitude of the problem relative to the existing capacity to address them. The technologically advanced countries have higher environmental burden because thousands of chemicals permeate their commerce and lifestyle. However, their capacity to address those problems is also high. The situation is different in most developing countries. So on balance, the scaled environmental burden is higher in the developing countries than in the technologically advanced countries.
Some states have touted waste-to-wealth programmes that they believe would improve the environmental quality of their states while increasing their revenue and new jobs. Do you think they can meet their targets?
The various states that have declared their intentions to implement waste-to-wealth projects should be encouraged to go beyond slogans. Waste can not go to wealth unless effective waste collection systems, transfer stations, sorting/recycling stations, composting schemes, incinerators and landfills are developed and operated with engagement of the private sector. In some states, waste generation rates are not presently high enough for contractors to reach economy of scale in their operations. To support this sector, codes and standards, technical guidance, training and monitoring support systems need to be implemented. The Federal Ministry of Environment is trying to do this and should be applauded.
You served for some time as the first President (Vice-Chancellor) of the African University of Science and Technology (AUST) in Abuja, Nigeria. Do you plan to continue to serve Africa in some roles within Africa?
I served in the role that you mentioned for a short time, on leave-of-absence from my regular position at the University of North Carolina in the United States. I resigned from AUST in February, 2010 to continue with our long-standing international initiatives, some of which involve activities in Africa in general, and Nigeria in particular.
An Internet search reveals that you are currently the President of the Alliance for Disaster Reduction (GADR) and the International Society for Environmental Geotechnology (ISEG), and that your research results published in hundreds of papers, span work in virtually every continent. What are your current research targets?
My work has ceased to be exclusively focused on research. For the past 15 years, my research has been complimented by engagement in public policy formulation and support internationally, and sustainable development capacity-building in the roles that you have mentioned in your question. I have served in those roles now for about 12 years. We are now leading fast-growing networks to establish and operate high-class institutes in Africa, in ways that will respect internal governance in the tradition of exemplary institutes worldwide. Such institutions neither serve the ego of their chief proponents nor operate on informal gossip. They must "show the beef" that matches their claims of excellence with confirmation in continental and global rankings.As regards research, my current focus is on the theoretical basis for use of processed polymers from natural materials, including cassava, to stabilize soils in the tropics against dust generation.
We are also developing mathematical models that describe the transport of radiation from buried wastes to assess health hazards to people above-ground. These targets add to our continuing work on physico-chemical containment of contaminants, including oil spills, in the subsurface. I am aided by many research assistants, associates and colleagues in several countries. Some of my collaborators are Nigerians, and will hopefully return to Africa to provide technical support to national sustainable development initiatives in the future.

Original_Search_Title: Women Gardeners in Tanji and Tujereng Villages
Headline: Gambia: "We Do Gardening to Sustain Our Families, But It is Getting Tougher" Says the Tanji And Tujereng Women Gardeners
Body_Text:
Some of the women gardeners in Tanji and Tujereng villages in Kombo South have told this reporter that the reason why they are engaged in gardening is to support the livelihood of their families but that the situation is getting tougher by the day.
"With the rising prices of basic food commodities and the educational and health needs of the children, sustaining a family with the meager income derived from selling limited garden produce is becoming difficult for us as parents", said Kaddy Kanyi, one of the women gardeners in Tanji.
Kaddy said the burden of supporting her family together with her husband is becoming enormous and problematic. She said her husband, as a civil servant, has been earning nothing more than eight hundred dalasi as monthly salary. Though small, she said, this amount has in the past been able to address certain basic needs of the household but that this is no longer possible now given the escalating prices commodities and the high education fees. Her husband, she said, now earns close to three thousand dalasi as monthly salary, but that this amount is not even sufficient to meet the educational cost of one of their children.
Kaddy said she had eight issues with her husband and six of whom are presently going to school. Among those going to school, she added, two of them are asking for three thousand five hundred dalasi each for their school fees, whilst another two who are in upper basic school asked for one thousand five hundred dalasi each for their school fees and book bills.
Apart from the issue of survival, she explained, one of the major problems confronting them as parents is the ability to meet the educational cost of some of their children who are going to school. This, she said, is an area where they need help most.
She explained the difficulties they face in their garden work terms of acquiring adequate inputs and the low yields in their produce as well as lack of market access.
Kaddy Sanyang, another woman gardener who spoke to this reporter, reiterated the difficulties that women face in the maintenance of their children and households vis a vis their earnings from the gardens. Mrs. Sanyang stressed that the suffering that the women are encountering now is impacting negatively on the family, especially the children.
She acknowledged that their husbands are equally suffering because of low wages or income.
She said "If life is not easy with our husbands, it certain that it would also not be easy for us women."
"If they are not happy, we too would not be happy, as we will get our share", she added.
For her part, Binta Badjie explained that she is gardening to supplement the income of husband and give support toward the maintenance of their family. She said the income derived from her garden goes to assist the husband in areas like providing fish money.
Mansata Darboe, a woman gardener in Tujereng, said the vegetables produced from her garden are mostly consumed by her family and that it is only a small quantity that is sold. This, she said, helps the husband in the area of fish money.
She said she produces vegetables such as pepper, bitter tomatoes, cassava, potatoes, aubergines etc.
She also lamented the problems being faced in the maintenance of their families especially in the face of rising food prices and the education of their children.
Another gardener in Tujereng, Fatou Bojang, said they are not in gardening for business purposes but earn incomes to support their families.

Original_Search_Title: Revolution Possible if Country's Resources Not Utlized Effectively- Sanusi
Headline: Nigeria: Revolution in North African Countries Will Take Place in Country, If ... - Sanusi
Body_Text:
Enugu — Governor of the Central Bank of Nigeria, Malam Sanusi Lamido Sanusi has warned that the ongoing revolution in some North African Countries would happen in Nigeria if the country fails to utilize its abundant natural resources properly.
Speaking at Nsukka shortly after receiving the Degree of Doctor of Business Administration of the University of Nigeria, Nsukka during the 40th Convocation Ceremony of the institution, yesterday, Sanusi regretted that Nigeria has continued to swim in the ocean of underdevelopment despite its huge natural resources.
He advised Nigerians to initiate the development of the country, insisting that foreigners were not in the best position to address our developmental needs Nigeria.
He expressed hope that the problems of Nigeria would be addressed within 10 and 20 years if we do the right thing, urging Nigerians to have hope.
He lamented further despite the existence of huge oil and Gas, Cassava, Cotton, among others, Nigeria had continued import power, starch, among others.
The Central Bank boss said there was need to use Nigeria's abundant huge Natural resources to make (Nigeria) the "China of Africa" to avoid the revolution that took place in Egypt, Tunisia, among others.
"If we do not put our vast resources to use, if we don't take advantage of these potential, what is happening in North African will happen here." he stated.

Original_Search_Title: CBN Gov Warns Against Civil Unrest
Headline: Nigeria: CBN Gov Warns Against Civil Unrest
Body_Text:
Central Bank Governor, Dr. Sanusi Lamido Sanusi, warned, yesterday, that the current civil unrest in some countries in North Africa could be experienced in Nigeria if the nation's leadership failed to explore the enormous potentials that abound in the country.
Sanusi gave the warning even as President Goodluck Jonathan disclosed that the Federal Government's approval for the citing of six new universities in the six geo-political zones of the country was to give the youths opportunity of having access to tertiary education and by so doing explore the potentials inherent in them.
The CBN governor, who spoke shortly after receiving an honorary doctorate degree in Business Administration at the 40th convocation of the University of Nigeria, UNN, Nsukka, noted that Nigeria was blessed with abundant potentials which if properly harnessed would "astronomically" improve the nation's socio-political and economical development.
He expressed concern over the failure of the nation to take full advantage of the available potentials for economic development.
His words: "This is a country we must always have hope not out of pity but because it has great potentials to be realized. Universities are the conscience of the nation and a whole lot of potentials are developed here. We therefore need to take full advantage of these potentials because if we don't, what we are witnessing in Libya, Tunisia, and Egypt will happen here.
"We have oil and gas, but we import energy; we have large land resources but we import food; we have been number one producer of cassava in the world but we import starch. We have a large cotton belt but we import textiles from China. We have hides and skin but we don't have the necessary industry to develop it.
"By addressing each of these areas in a well structured and articulated manner, we'll remove this country out of poverty and be the China of Africa. This is not going to be done to us by the Chinese or the Americans but we are the ones to do it. We don't need to treat this country with despair and despondency, but with hope and ambition," he stated.
Sanusi was however hopeful that by the time the right things were done, the country would emerge a global giant in the foreseeable future.
In his speech, read by the Minister of Education, Prof. Raqquyatu Ahmed Rufai, Jonathan said his administration was committed to giving the youths of the country a pride of place, expressing optimism that the new universities would commence academic activities at the shortest possible time.
In their separate speeches at the convocation which witnessed the presentation of honorary doctorate degree awards to Rear Admiral Allison Madueke and Chief Cletus Ibeto, the Vice Chancellor, Pro-chancellor and Chancellor of the University, Prof Bartho Okolo, Prof S. O. Igwe and Alhaji Shehu Idris said that the university had recorded enormous achievements in the past years.
According to them, the UNN now ranks 3rd in Nigeria, 54th in Africa and 5,176 in the world because of the commitment of the administration in taking the school to greater height.

Original_Search_Title: The Keke NAPEP Contract Was Unilaterally Varied - Kpakol
Headline: Nigeria: The Keke NAPEP Contract Was Unilaterally Varied - Kpakol
Body_Text:
Dr Magnus Kpakol is the Senior Special Adviser to the President and the National Co-ordinator, National Poverty Alleviation Programme (NAPEP). In a recent session with journalists, he threw more light on the Keke NAPEP saga and other activities of the parastatal. Nkechi Isaac was there.
What are the components that Autobann has not supplied?
As you know, we ordered and paid for 5,000 units in May 2007, and let me mention that he presented his cheque to the bank on May 14, because he got paid within seven days including a weekend, but he's been telling the story all over that he got a bounced cheque and it took him two, three months to get paid. It's not true. Anybody can go to Equatorial Trust Bank (ETB); the statement is there. We got the money into our account on May 10, so we couldn't have paid him before May 10 because that was when the Central Bank credited our account. It's there for everybody to see. He presented the cheque on May 14 and got it on May 21. I don't think those seven days will be the reason we're still struggling for more than a year now.
Now, we got a little over 300 units. We've not received close to 2,000 units. But, that is secondary now because we have realised that what we thought we were getting was not what we were getting. This is a substantive issue.
When I sent my committee to Lagos on an investigative mission, they discovered from KORAN that the reason they could not sell the product (Keke NAPEP) was because people were complaining that it was breaking down - that it was not Piaggio. That was how we realised the products were not Piaggio.
The first 500 were Piaggio, then these ones that were being received were not Piaggio. But nobody ever told us that it was not Piaggio: KORAN did not tell us; Ofili did not tell us; nobody can tell you that they wrote to us, telling us that this is what happened.
When we discovered it was not Piaggio, that became a serious issue. So now the point we're making is that we need to find out how and why Autobann unilaterally varied the contract. So, I'm not even interested anymore in getting the balance of the nearly 2,000 tricycles, I'm interested in getting 4,500 units of Piaggio tricycles, because I only received 500.
He has varied the contract; it's a serious breach in the contract and a serious offence as far as the government is concerned. Even I don't have the powers to vary the contract - I will have to go to the President and plead and explain why I have to vary the contract. In that case, I will have to explain the variations in the contract price. Those are the core issues we have now.
In the contract, were there provisions for when a party defaults? Were there provisions of what will happen when the contractor defaults?
I imagine it's a pretty voluminous legal document and we have legal department that will better throw more light on that, but as the head of the organisation, there is a position that we stood with the bank that says they should provide us 5,000 tricycles and, if they fail, Union Bank will pay us back our money: that was part of the larger picture and that was what I was holding on to as the CEO. These are issues that will come up next and we are getting to that stage now.
On the issue of Keke NAPEP, what can you give as the quantum of the impact in terms of job creation and the monetary value that people are making?
I think the Keke NAPEP business is a significant and meaningful kind of business for Nigerians because of the demand for it. There is tremendous demand for it all across the country. On that score, it is good business and, if it is good business that can get people out of poverty, then we ought to support it , may be not by using government money to buy tricycle for every Nigerian but encouraging that kind of business, and I think we have done that very well.
In Abuja today, there are about 5,000 Keke NAPEP tricycles, but we know that we have put in not more that 2,000 tricycles in the FCT, but we have about 5,000. Many of them are not tricycles that we bought for people, so it shows that we are good catalysts in causing people to pick interest in the business and causing them to realize that they can make money, take care of themselves, send their children to school and put food on their table. It's pretty successful.
The present administration under President Jonathan is keen on fighting poverty. As the Senior Adviser and Head of NAPEP, in what ways are you keying in to make sure that his dreams of kicking poverty out of the streets and giving employment to Nigerians are achieved?
We are running with the dream. As a matter of fact, in a couple of weeks, there's a big meeting that the government has set up between the Minister of Finance, NAPEP, the head of NDE and SMEDAN and other parties to try and address this issue. The President is very keen on job creation and he is looking at how these agencies can work together to see that more jobs are created.
Speaking for NAPEP, what our emphasis should be and what we've been trying to do is to coordinate and monitor activities, and this monitoring is really important. It involves checking to see how the MDGs funds are being used, by enquiring, for example, about the impact of the conditional grant scheme money is affecting and impacting the lives of people.
Our job cuts across many areas; looking over the MDGs, dealing with health, education, state and local governments. It's not just about what NAPEP does, so our monitoring efforts are diverse and then all of this will come into the poverty eradication report of the President. So every year the President and the people will be able to see what has happened overtime.
Last week, there were stories about the President's desire to appoint a special assistant on job creation. How does this play out with your organisation?
Well, sometimes people will feel there's duplication of efforts, but if we have 50 ministries that are creating jobs, you will still have NAPEP. If we have somebody who is critically looking at a way of creating jobs, what will happen is that, that person will be working with us, SMEDAN and NDE to look for ways to create jobs.
It is the private sector that creates jobs and, basically, I think the government's angle is to create an environment that is good for the private sector. If we have a good, strong private sector, I think jobs will be created. I believe strongly that what the government is talking about is how government agencies will do what they are supposed to do to ensure that the environment is habitable for the market to thrive.
What is the status of what KORAN owes the Federal Government, not NAPEP because it is because of what happened that people are alleging that the money has been stolen, and what is the commitment of KORAN towards paying back the money?
First of all, people are not alleging that I stole the money, it's only one person at Autoban that is saying that. He's the one pretending to be many people saying the same thing. But the fact of the matter is that KORAN owes this money and we have handed KORAN over to EFCC to collect our money from them. We cannot wrestle with KORAN personally. We have used the legal channels, we've written to the Attorney-General, the EFCC and ICPC. So, we have taken the measures that a government office should take to try and recover our money, so we're now sitting back and waiting for the report. They're still working on it; in fact, I was told last week that there was a serious interactive session between the EFCC and KORAN.
We just recently heard of the partnership between NAPEP and Korea International Cooperation Agency (KOICA) to establish cassava processing centres in Nigeria, can you throw more light on that?
The project will produce big time processing facilities, a demonstration again so that people will see that this is what can be done. Perhaps a private individual might go to Korea and get some of these things. So, we are happy to partner with them in the demonstrations.
There are allegations that Autobann has been trying to malign your office and person on the pages of news papers on the Keke contract bid. Is it true?
Unfortunately, there are people who are very clever in coming to the media and manipulate information and deceive the people and I never know the benefit it is to them to malign people, especially when they know the story is false.
What happened is that we advertised for bids on tricycle for firms to market products. Autobann techniques thought from the letters he has written and from indications in the papers that we should have just handed the programme to him, since he supplied Keke passenger tricycles to us in the past and was angry that we opened it up to a bid and didn't just give it to him.
He also thought that I had set up a company called Trimidan in Lagos owned by some principals of KORAN in Lagos, so that I will buy tricycles from them. He didn't even know that we are not buying passenger tricycles. As far as I know, Trimidan makes passenger tricycles. What we advertised in the paper is a firm to market tricycles not passenger tricycles. So, obviously there was no intention to buy tricycles. We opened it up for public bidding and he didn't want to bid, and then jumped in a conclusion that I own Trimidan because he saw my picture on Trimidan's wall, which is absurd and mind boggling.
I see pictures of government officials in offices and shops; that does not mean they own the establishment. What I think is criminal is for Autoban, Ofili Okonkwo and his cronies to take the signature page of the document that was written by the Senate Committee on National Planning, Economic Affairs and Poverty Alleviation in February 2009, which was never published, and append it to a story that is developing in 2010 and say that the Senate has indicted me. I think there ought to be something criminal about it. I'm not a lawyer but I think that it should be investigated.

Original_Search_Title: A Bookjam Treat for Abuja Literati
Headline: Nigeria: A Bookjam Treat for Abuja Literati
Body_Text:
Abuja literati are eagerly looking forward to Friday, February 25 when they would get to interact with widely-published poet, Lola Shoneyin, whose debut prose fiction, The Secret Lives of Baba Segi's Wives, was recently released by Cassava Republic press.
Joining Lola at the BookJam stage is the popular "bad boy" actor, Femi Branch, who rose to national limelight as the spoilt, wealthy, ruthless Oscar in the now-rested TV soap, Domino.
Femi's collection of his three plays and fifty poems was recently published in his anthology, From Senbora.
From Lagos, corporate communications specialist, Kufre Ekanem, who sees himself more as a scribbler than a poet, would fly in to Abuja to "rant" about his debut poetry book, The Ant Eaters: a Collection of Rants and Anthems.
The BookJam is a book party; a collaboration of the Abuja Literary Society and Silverbird Lifestyle. It is anchored by co-host of the Abuja Poetry Slam, Jide Attah, and consists of book readings, book signings, raffle draw and a discussion by the guest authors.
In addition, there will be special Slam poetry performance by some of Abuja's finest Slam champions. In 2010 the Abuja BookJam featured several established and budding authors.
These include novelists Ifeoma Chinwuba and Myne Whitman; poets and multi-genre writers, Ahmed Maiwada, Denja Abdullahi, Seyi Adigun, Eugenia Abu; playwrights Adinoyi Ojo Onukaba and Abraham Nwankwo; motivational writers, Deji Fisho and Tolu Akanni, among others.
Held on the last Friday of every month at 6pm, the monthly book feast has become a stable staple in Abuja's literary calendar, promoting book sales, author-fan interaction and a literary culture in Nigeria's federal capital city.
Author of three poetry volumes, a children's book and, now, a novel, Lola is a multi-genre author who is finding success, and a home, in each genre. An alumnus of Ogun State University and the International Writers Programme at the University of Iowa, USA, Lola's poetry can hardly be described as "traditional".
Instead, her verse is associated with the terms, "daring", "different", "irreverent" and "avant-garde". Hers is a style that calls your attention, that uses satire creatively and compels you, the reader, to think about her message.
Her poetry books, So All the Time I Was Sitting on an Egg, Song of a Riverbird and For the Love of Flight deal on a pot pourri of issues, mainly womanhood and feminity, flights and the love of flying, man's inhumanity to woman, and social justice.
Her book for children, Mayowa and the Masquerades, tells the story of a city-boy's return to the village and the fun-filled adventure he and his friend undertakes.
Lola's recently published novel, The Secret Lives of Baba Segi's Wives, is generating much positive critical attention.
Described as a perceptive and entertaining debut novel set in modern-day Nigeria about four wives and one husband, the novel "explores the intricacies of family life and relationships through a wonderful cast of characters of one extended family".
Critics have variously described it as "fiercely funny", "endearing and provocative". In a recent review of the book, the critic, Richard Ali, summed:
"The Secret Lives of Baba Segi's Wives is a book that will be a popular reader's delight that will at the same time set the agenda of the gender debate in Academe over the subsequent years. Hers is an achievement worth heralding."
While many popular actors are branching out into music, Femi Branch, star of several popular soaps such as Domino, Tinsel and Spiders, has chosen the writer's path. His anthology, From Senbora, is a collection of 50 of his poems and his three plays. His poetry is rhythmical and some of his best, such as, "Ode to the Girl-child", are intensely evocative. But it is in his drama, that we find the born-actor. "Jungle Justice", one of the anthologised plays, will keep you laughing hilariously to the very end. His is a humourous satire that is refreshingly original.
Femi is a multi-talented artiste, who has over the past two decades, earned respect and popularity name in the African movie, stage and television industries. That Femi is celebrated well beyond these borders is evident in the multiple awards he has amassed in and out of Nigeria.
From Senbora is sold well beyond the African borders to raise funds for his foundation; The Femi Branch Readership Network, which is helping Nigerians who cannot read or write to acquire such vital life-skills.
Femi has starred in internationally-released movies such as Omen of Love, Twisted and A Place in the Stars, all released in the United States and the United Kingdom respectively.
His major roles in such popular family Soaps like Domino, Tinsel and Spiders has also won him thousands of fans and laurels in Nigeria, Ghana, South Africa and presently in the UK where it is still airing.
Femi has worked as a model in various Corporate Campaigns and Commercials as well as done voice-overs and radio jingles for such major brands as MTN, Bournvita, Milo, Mr Biggs' and Oceanic Bank.
Abasikufre Ekanem's debut poetry collection, The Ant Eaters, has been described as "a collection of state of consciousness poems with one consistent whisk: the ant. The ant literally crawls across every page as a painter's canvass. Every ant bears a message as import blends fluidly in graceful word play."
Kufre is better known for his day-job role as corporate affairs manager (West Africa) at Cadbury Plc. As a marketing and PR practitioner he has enjoyed a stellar career spanning teaching, insurance, journalism, theatre, printing, advertising, brand management and consulting over two decades. Kufre lives in Lagos, Nigeria with his wife and daughter.
In Kufre's The Ant Eaters, ants don't always mean ants.
Contributed By Ken Ike

Original_Search_Title: Keep Army Away From Elections - Besigye
Headline: Uganda: Keep Army Away From Elections - Besigye
Body_Text:
THE Inter party coalition (IPC) presidential candidate, Kizza Besigye, has asked the defence forces chief, Gen. Aronda Nyakairima, to keep the army away from elections.
"Leave the wanainchi to decide their fate by changing the leadership peacefully," he said.
Besigye reminded the army boss that he would one day be like those who commanded the army and left.
"Have you seen Mugisha Muntu, the former army commander, moving with escorts? It's because he never misused his army uniform. Those who are intimidating people should go slow and know that there is tomorrow," he cautioned.
Besigye was on Tuesday addressing hundreds of supporters at a rally at Kizinda play grounds in Bushenyi municipality. Earlier he addressed several mini-rallies in trading centres throughout the sub-counties of Bushenyi.
The IPC candidate also warned city businessman Hassan Bassajjabalaba over antagonising Bushenyi people using money.
Addressing a rally at Ibaare trading centre in Ibaare sub-county, Besigye appealed to residents to reject intimidation and threats that the Tunisia and Egypt riots against the incumbent presidents could move to Uganda.
"They should not deceive you that if you don't vote Museveni there will be war. Where will it come from? Use your vote to change leadership peacefully," he urged.
Besigye asked the voters to sacrifice February 18, roast cassava, pack it and camp at the polling station until results are declared. "I have played my part and it is now up to you. Had it not been vote rigging the system would have changed."
He said the NRM had purchased mini-buses to ferry people from one polling station to another to rig votes, adding that: "The register is full of ghost names and those are the names they want to use to rig elections."
Besigye told his supporters that Museveni was on his exit route, saying his trend in the presidential elections had declined from 75% in 1996 to 69% in 2001 and 58% in 2006.
He accused the president of turning the country into "Museveni and family Limited", explaining that he had put his family members and relatives in top government positions.
Besigye said few people have continued to enjoy government resources, while the majority are languishing in poverty.

Original_Search_Title: Cassava Republic Captures Book Industry
Headline: Nigeria: Cassava Republic Captures Book Industry
Body_Text:
Lagos — Cassava Republic Press, a publishing outfit based in Abuja, has churned out a number of books in the recent times which experts have observed emerged as major contestants in most recent local, national and international laurels.
In the story of one of these heaps of books, The Secret Lives of Baba Segi's Wives by Lola Shoneyin, a polygamist like Baba Segi, his collection of wives and a gaggle of children are the symbol of prosperity, success and validation of his manhood. Everything runs smoothly in the patriarchal home, until wife number four intrudes on this family romance. Bolanle, a graduate amongst the semi-literate wives, is hated from the start. Baba Segi's glee at bagging a graduate doesn't help matters. Worse, Bolanle's arrival threatens to do more than simply ruffle feathers. She's unwittingly set to expose a secret that her co-wives intend to protect at all costs.
The latest addition to the family bookshelf for kids include Mayowa and the Masquerades by Lola Shoneyin, Eno's Story by Ayodele Olofintuade, and eight titles by Fatima Akilu, including Timi's Dream Comes True, The Yellow Mosquito Net and Aliyyah Leans a New Dance.
Carlos Moore's book, Fela: This Bitch of a Life is also discovered to be making waves throughout the federation. Sade Adeniran was also in the country in May to launch her award-winning tale of a stolen childhood, Imagine This.
Towards the end of 2010, Adaobi Tricia Nwaubani, author of the award-winning I Do Not Come to you by Chance wrote a highly controversial article in the New York Times about Ngugi and the Nobel Prize. The piece sparked intense debate in the African literary community and provided an excellent opportunity for discussion and food for thought among the continent's brightest minds.
Eight of the books on the Millennium Development Goals by Fatima Akilu, published by Cassava Republic have been placed on the Nigerian primary school curriculum.
Two of the authors of this publishing outfit, Abidemi Sanusi, author of Eyo (Best Book) and Adaobi Tricia Nwaubani, I Do Not Come To You By Chance, (Best First Book) were shortlisted for the 2010 Commonwealth Writers' Prize. Adaobi was the eventual winner for New Writers. Adaobi also won the Betty Trask Award for new writers.
Cassava Republic is collaborating with the hyper-hip Kenyan- based magazine Kwani and the avant-garde South African journal Chimurenga on the upcoming Chimurenga Chronicle, a one-day-only edition of a fictional weekly newspaper backdated to the week of May 11 to 18, 2008, the first week of the xenophobic violence that rocked South Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Terrorism - Police Arrest Five Amauzam Youths
Headline: Nigeria: Terrorism - Police Arrest Five Amauzam Youths
Body_Text:
Enugu — No fewer than five youths of Amauzam Ugbawka autonomous community, Nkanu East Local Government Area of Enugu State, have been arrested by the police for acts of terrorism, including threats to lives of members of the community.
The arrests were prompted by a petition to the Commissioner of Police, Enugu State Command, by community leaders, signed by National President of Uzam General Assembly, Elder Felix Nwafor Mba and National Secretary, Elder Philemon Chukwu.
The petitioners stated that they were constrained as community leaders to bring to the urgent attention of the Commissioner of Police, a case of armed robbery attack and threat to the life of Mr. Afamefula Mba, security chairman of Amauzam Ugbawka, by a gang of hoodlums and questionable characters in the community.
Those accused in the petition of threatening the life of the security chairman include Onyebuchi Edemba, Okechukwu Mba, Ifeanyi Chukwuka, Sunday Edeh, Bright Mba, Chinwe Nwogogo, Osita Mba and Ifeanyi Mba.
The petitioners also alleged that the said persons have issued similar death threats to the lives of other members of Uzam General Assembly, the town union of the community.
They stated: "This petition is necessited by our desire on our part as community leaders to forestall the unintended unsavoury consequences, which the activities of this gang of hoodlums pose to the entire community, if left unchecked.
"We wish to further state as follows; On Saturday, January 29, 2011, the aforementioned hoodlums led other miscreants now at large to attack and rob Mr. Afamefula Mba, the security chairman of Amauzam Ugbawka autonomous community.
"Three of the hoodlums were armed with pistols, which they shot sporadically into the air while the others were in possession of dangerous weapons such as daggers and mini-axes. Among other things, they took away from the victim, Honda motorcycle, car, two cell phone handsets and N3,000. "Thereafter, this gang and its accomplices, numbering about 30, proceeded to beat Mr. Afamefula Mba to a state of coma and stripped him."
According to the petitioners, the gang continued its rampage on the night of January 30, 2011 by attacking the family of the victim, while he was receiving medical attention at the hospital and vandalised cassava blending machine in front of his compound.
They alleged that the gang also invaded the house of the National President, Elder David Nwafor Mba, held him hostage for hours, vandalised the electrical wirings and generator in his house, and threatened to burn down the house.
The complainants stated that the hoodlums summoned a meeting of the villagers the next day where they issued threats to other members of Uzam General Assembly, after which they engaged in a looting spree in the community, carting away belongings of defenseless villagers.
While noting that the case was beyond what the Divisional Police Officer could handle, the community leaders therefore implored the commissioner of police to use his good office to treat this complaint with the urgency and seriousness it deserves, adding that a stitch in time saves nine.
When contacted on phone, Police Public Relations Officer (PPRO) Enugu State Command, ASP Ebere Amaraizu, confirmed that some persons were arrested in Nkanu East for illegal possession of firearms and other allegations.
The police spokesman however said that investigations were ongoing on the matter as at press time.
There has been a history of terrorism in Amauzam Ugbawka town, where sometime in 2002, unknown persons beheaded the chairman of the vigilante group, Chukwuma Mba.

Original_Search_Title: FG May Dump Policy On Textiles, Others
Headline: Nigeria: FG May Dump Policy On Textiles, Others
Body_Text:
There are strong indications that the Federal Government may change its mind on the removal of textile materials, furniture, toothpicks and cassava products from import prohibition list which it announced in November last year.
The Finance Minister, Mr Olusegun Aganga, had in announcing the economic policy said that a circular for the implementation had been sent to the Customs Service and Destination Inspection Agents (DIAs).
But sources close to the Customs Service and DIAs said that the circular was not sent to them as claimed by the Ministry. In view of this, the Customs Service has maintained that the importation of the items remains prohibited.
Sources said that the non-implementation of the policy may not be unconnected with criticisms that trailed the policy announcement.
Members of the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) had attacked government for coming up with the policy at a time it had provided bail out funds for the textile industry.
MAN and other members of the organised private sector were said to have protested to the Ministry of Finance and the Presidency on the issue.
A source close to the Ministry said that signals received by the Ministry of Finance was among the reasons why it has not been able to enforce the implementation of the policy.
Our source said that government is in a dilemma over the situation, apparently for making the policy public, adding that this is why it has been reluctant to enforce it.
It would be recalled that political opponents of the government, including the former Vice President, Alhaji Atiku Abubakar had criticised the policy. On the other hand, importers and freight forwarders have been bitter with the government for failing to implement the policy.
While MAN has been of the opinion that the policy will have adverse effect on local manufacturers, freight forwarders have argued that the Federal Government was losing heavily on duties that would have accrued to it as most of the items are being smuggled into the country massively. It would be recalled that government had in 2008 placed ban on all types of textile materials into the country.
This was as a result of the pressure from Manufacturers Association of Nigeria (MAN) which argued that allowing importation of textiles into the country was killing local manufacturing firms.
However, since its ban, importers and freight forwarders have cried out that such ban was a stumbling block to international trade.
Besides, the products, except toothpicks, could be found in all parts of the country in massive form as they come into the country through the seaports and porous border routes.
A source close to the Ministry of Finance told THISDAY that one of the reasons why government removed textile materials from import prohibition list was to check high rate of smuggling on them.
The Federal government had in removing the items from import prohibition list, introduced a 20 per cent duty on the products and a 15 percent surcharge as new levy.
Government had also raised the age limit on importation of fairly used cars from 10 to 15 years to check smuggling of the vehicles through the border stations.
In the case of vehicles, importers who bring into any vehicle above the manufacture age of 10 years are made to pay heavily, including settlement of some officials of customs and other agencies before they are released.

Original_Search_Title: FG Loses N750 Billion Yearly to Smuggling
Headline: Nigeria: FG Loses N750 Billion Yearly to Smuggling
Body_Text:
Facts emerged at the weekend that the Federal Government was losing about N750 billion yearly to smuggling at the nation's borders and ports across the country.
The activities of smugglers were confirmed to be having adverse effects on local industries.
The monthly Economic Review of the Financial Derivatives Company Limited (FDC) for January 2011 put the amount lost last year to approximately 40 per cent of the country's projected total non-oil revenue in the fiscal year of 2010.
FDC, in its maiden review for the year, however, posited that the recent policy on the de-prohibition of the importation of certain items into the country would help curb the menace of smuggling.
The Federal Government recently lifted ban on items such as textiles, furniture, toothpick and cassava as well as energy drinks.
In the review, the FDC noted: "The government hopes to block the loopholes by which revenues it could have earned from duties imposed on imported items leaked out of the system. Take a look around and it becomes quite evident that the items removed from the prohibition list are in high demand in the market place.
"Smugglers have therefore exploited lapses in the immigration and customs service to get these de-listed items to the market in the past."
FDC also argued that the composition and assumption of the 2011 Appropriation bill did not reflect that it was "a budget of fiscal consolidation, inclusive of economic growth and employment generation," as claimed by President Goodluck Jonathan.
It based its argument on the premise that while aggregate spending in the proposed budget had been reduced by 18 per cent, capital expenditure, which was considered a necessary catalyst for growth, declined by 49 per cent, compared to the 0.5 per cent decline in recurrent expenditure.
"There seems to be contradictions between the budget philosophy and the pronouncements. The government in an attempt to support the cut in capital expenditure said the N1.005 trillion voted for capital expenditure compares favourably with the N919.5 billion actually utilised in the extended 15 months of the 2009 fiscal year, which is the largest amount of capital resources utilised by all the Ministries, Departments and Agencies (MDAs) in any fiscal year to date," the review pointed out.
Commenting on the nation's $500 million 10-year Eurobond which was floated last Friday, the FDC review stated that the yield on the issue would set a price benchmark for assessing Nigeria's sovereign risk for corporate organisations that intends to borrow from the international bond market as well as so the same for lenders.
The review also stressed that the offer was expected to impact positively on the private sector, believing that it would open up the international market for firms in need of short to medium-term international capital.
Tags: Business, Nigeria

Original_Search_Title: Lost Children - Dad, Mom Wants to Kill Me!
Headline: Uganda: Lost Children - Dad, Mom Wants to Kill Me!
Body_Text:
Mariam Namatovu, 13, is looking for her father, John Ndawula, suspected to have originated from Baale village in Masaka. After death threats from her mother, Namatovu sought refugee at the home of Pastor Daniel Luyombya of the Cornerstone Church in Nakwero village near Gayaza.
The P.6 pupil at Gayaza Church of Uganda Primary School explained that together with her mother, Nagiita, they have been surviving on casual work in the village. The two have been tilling land for people at a fee.
To add to the family income, Namatovu narrates, she has been working at a nearby stone quarry over the weekends.
Nagiita separated with Namatovu's father when she was still young. Namatovu says her father rented a room in one of Aidah's houses in Kyebando, before the separation. Namatovu rarely attends class because she has to do casual work with her mother during some week days. She says Nagiita takes long to pay her school fees even after being paid for the work in the garden. Namatovu can be contacted on 0782911401.
Who is Sentamu's dad?
Farouk Sentamu, 10, was picked by a Good Samaritan in Nakwero village near Gayaza. Margaret Nabagesera picked him on the verandah of a shop in Nakwero, where he used to spend the nights.
At the time he was picked, Sentamu had jiggers on her legs and hands. He explained that his 72-year-old grandfather, Musisi, could not care for him as he would drink a lot and at times sleep at in the bar.
Musisi's children have become uncontrollable after their mother abandoned him. Sentamu's mother died when he was young and his father has never been traced.
Mom sent me from home
Six-year-old Jordan Serunkuma of Good Hope Nursery School is stranded at the Old Kampala Police station where he was taken by a Good Samaritan, who picked him at Nakulabye on December 29.
Serunkuma explained to the Police that his mother, Harriet Nansubuga, a food vendor in Nakulabye, sent him away from home and his elder brother, Brian Kyobe, of P1, also lives on the streets. Serunkuma said their mother often returns home home late and rarely leaves them food.
He said his father, Stephen Sewagudde, deals in second-hand clothes in Juba and does not stay at their home. Serunkuma prefers staying with his aunt who sells cassava in Nakulabye. The in-charge of Child and Family Protection Unit at Old Kampala Police station Margaret Andiiru said they were looking for Serunkuma's mother to answer charges of child neglect.
Sebulime followed political rally fans and got lost
Arnold Sebulime, was picked on December 29 on Rubaga Road and handed over to the Old Kampala Police after he failed to find his way home.
The six-year-old says he studies at Bazzekukketa Primary School in Wakiso. His mother, Jane Namatovu, works in a rice shamba. He says his father, Godfrey Kyambadde of Kyeyitabya village, is a shamba man.
Julius Mutebi is Sebulime's teacher and his brother, Isaac Kazibwe, is in Top Class.
Asiimwe was sent to buy tomatoes and lost her way
Christine Asiimwe, 12, from Kyaruhuga village in western Uganda is currently at Kawempe Police station after getting lost. She had come to Kampala to visit her father, John Tumwesigye. When she was sent to buy tomatoes, she failed to trace her way back home. Asiimwe, who only understands Runyankole, explained that her father has a retail shop though she had not yet mastered the name of the area.
Nakimbugwe ran away from harassment while sick
A sick Fiona Nakimbugwe on Monday ran away from home because of harrassment, but ended up at Police, instead of a hospital.
By Monday, she had high fever and the Police took her for treatment as they traced for her parents.
A pupil of P.3 at Mirembe KCC School, Nakimbugwe said she wanted to go to her aunt, Annet Nassiwa, who owns a salon in Lukuli. Her mother is Nabukenya and her dad is Edward Mawanda.

Original_Search_Title: Orji - Strayed From Home, Rescued in Lagos Bus Stop
Headline: Nigeria: Orji - Strayed From Home, Rescued in Lagos Bus Stop
Body_Text:
Lagos — But for the quick intervention of a good Nigerian, Anne Ejuka of Tycoon Newspapers, the story of a 14-year-old girl, Uchechukwu Orji, from Inyi in Oji River Local Government Area of Enugu State, may have been a different one.
This is against the backdrop of the alarming crime level in the country especially kidnapping, ritual killings, child trafficking and other vices perpetrated by hoodlums in the society.
The teenage girl, apparently frustrated over alleged inhuman treatment from her guardians whom her parents entrusted her to live with in Lagos since last October, decided to go back to her village in Enugu State on Wednesday December 29, 2010.She set out with a polythene bag containing her clothes, incidentally without any money on her for the transport fare to Enugu. She did not even have any money to pay for the bus she boarded to take her to the nearest South East-bound bus terminal.
Uchechukwu, investigation reveals, did not even know the address of her guardians. She was met in a commercial bus by the good Nigerian while the conductor of the bus was quarrelling with her over her inability to pay a fare of N50. It was further learnt that after interrogation, Anne decided to take her to the office of the Police Public Relations Officer, Ikeja, Lagos, for further investigation and a clue that would eventually lead to transfer of the girl to her parents.
Daily Independent investigations revealed that the girl was attending Girls High School Inyi before one of her relations, Chibugo Mba, came from Lagos to pick her with the promise that she would be taken good care of and send her to school in Lagos. It was gathered that she came to Lagos on October 3, 2010 but shortly after her arrival, the same Chibugo who assured the parents that she was going to live with her, took her to another person popularly called CYCS wife or Mama Nmesaoma to live with. She further alleged that she was later taken to one Mama Chioma's house again to live. She claimed that Mama Chioma often subjected her to beating on slightest provocation until she threw her things out of her house. This, she explained prompted her to commence a search for the way home on Wednesday. She however was able to give out a phone number belonging to one Samuel from Enugu State whom she claimed to be her brother to the PPRO. When a call was put across, the man confirmed that the girl was her relation. But further investigations as at Thursday indicated that Samuel had not been picking his phone when called from the office of the PPRO for information on the girl's parents and how to reunite her with them.
Commenting on the incident, Frank Mba, Lagos PPRO, told journalists that the woman who brought the girl called him some minutes before they arrived in his office and informed him that she was in a bus with the girl.
"When they made enquiries, she said it was something they felt the police should know about. So I asked them to come over. I have interacted with her; she said she is from Inyi in Oji River, Enugu State.
Ironically, she could not trace the address of the home she ran away from," Mba noted. Commenting further on her travails, Uchechukwu said that she was about to enter SS1 before she was brought to Lagos, confirming that Chibugo was the person who came to pick her directly from the village. "She told my parents that I would live with her and her husband. I followed her willingly because I wanted to go. When we got to Lagos, she called one of their church members and told her that she had brought the girl she promised to bring to her. I reminded her that she told my parents that I was going to stay with her, asking why she was calling another person to pick me. But she told me to shut up," she remarked.
She claimed she was not being fed by the woman she was staying with, and that she spent only one day with the person who originally transferred her to another person. "In the morning I would accompany them to market. She would give me N55 and say that I should use N50 to feed and N5 for pure (sachet) water. That was what I would eat throughout the day. So I told them that I wanted to go back to my village," she added.
Uchechukwu stated that Chibugo's husband is a pastor, adding that she did not know that Chibugo was trying to send her to another person to live with. The woman she was sent to live with had two children named Chioma and Esther. "She used to threaten me that one day she would send me back to my village, claiming that she did not tell Chibugo that she needed somebody. She beat me with broom and wood used in turning garri (a country staple food made from cassava).
"At a time Chioma told me to go back to my village because her mother was wicked," the girl alleged. Anne who brought Uchechukwu to the police said that she was moved with pity and was concerned about the safety of the girl that prompted her to bring the girl to the police.
"I met her in a bus and the conductor was quarrelling with her over her inability to pay N50 bus fare. She insisted that she did not have N50 but ironically said she was going to Enugu. I picked her from Cele Bus Stop on Oshodi -Apapa Expressway," she recalled. Mba said that since he had succeeded in establishing a link with one of her relations in the village, while investigation is still on, he would ask his parents to send somebody to Lagos to come and fetch her. However, Mba explained that if he fails to get response from any member of her family, the girl would be transferred to the children's welfare centre.

Original_Search_Title: Why I Teach Aspiring Writers -Habila
Headline: Nigeria: Why I Teach Aspiring Writers -Habila
Body_Text:
Helon Habila is among the Nigerian writers making waves abroad. His adoring skill in literature was celebrated when he won Caine prize for African writing in 2001. His first novel, 'Waiting for an Angel' which won him the prize has since been translated into several European languages. He lives in the United States of America and became the first Chinua Achebe Fellow at Bard College, New York, between 2005 and 2006. Senior Correspondent, YEMI ADEBISI had an engaging moment with him in Port Harcourt during the literary festival where he spoke about his writing career and the future of African literature. Excerpt:
Your new book is all about Niger Delta. Is that a coincidence or deliberate?
It's about Niger Delta. As the name implies, Oil On Water, the book focuses on the issues of oil and its effect on the environment. It talks about the degradation of the environment. It's been a topical issue especially with the attendant kidnapping and other issues that surround it. I illustrate that through one of the oil workers whose wife was kidnapped and reporters looked for her to speak with her. I was approached by movie companies to write on Niger Delta actually but I told them I am not from Niger Delta. They told me that since I am a writer and a Nigerian, I should be able to do it for them and later on I had to comply with their request.
As a Nigerian, what is your reaction to the way government is handling Niger Delta issue?
I have always distinctively seen things through the eye of the small people, the people who are not heard. If you notice very well, virtually all my books address injustice done to people. It is the peace loving people who do not have voice in our society. I think it is really good for authors to say something about it. I try to capture the life of these individuals in my book. I think the government should do more than what it is doing. It is the duty of the government to take care of its people. Look at what happened in America, the moment we had that spillage, the government just hammered on them. It is not happening in Nigeria.
In the two books you wrote before, Measuring Time and Waiting For An Angel, you have writers and aspiring journalists as your main characters. Is it because of your background as a journalist?
Actually it is not that I sit down and decide that it has to be journalist or a writer anyway. It happens naturally to me. Different writers have different things that appeal to them. With me, I celebrate the journalist. I see the great things the journalists have done and what they can still do and yet they are not celebrated, though there are bad and corrupt ones. Nevertheless, they could do more than they are doing. I think they have not been honoured for what they have done. There are hard working and reputable journalists in the world. It's my way of celebrating them and also I am using them as a kind of metaphor. A journalist is a kind of person that asks questions and finds out things vividly in the course of carrying out his assignment. That's really the idea of an ideal journalist.
How has the award you won improved your writing or person?
There is this glamorisation that people believe you are just there rolling in millions. I am a teacher. I earn my living through teaching. Writing really is not enough to sustain for now. Most writers and publishers can't even afford to buy gas to move their vehicles. I write because of my conviction. I just want to express myself. The only difference is that the infrastructure is there in the states. One is fortunate to be in the West to do all this anyway.
How do you feel teaching aspiring writers?
Someone asked me that why have I not been assisting the aspiring writers? It is not an obligation. There is this misconception that you just have to set up a workshop and begin to train writers immediately you are known over the world as a writer. Most writers don't do this. I do it because I love doing it. I appreciate where I came from. I know what I suffered to become a writer. In terms of writing book, I had to do it myself. See how many writers would want to take his little money and organise a writing workshop? It is not easy. When chance comes, yes, you use it.
How is your growing up as a writer?
It makes all the difference. I was lonely so I really read. What happened was that we were in Gombe. All my brothers were sent back home to learn the local language. I was the only one left with my parents. Up till my secondary school days, I was lonely with my parents. I was sold into stories. That's really something that drifted me into writing. Much later I couldn't help it becoming a lover of the books. I never aspired to be a writer because I never knew one could just sit down and write.
You took a number of people in your class during the Garden City Literary Festival. What kind of impression would you infer from your students?
I think they were very eager. They asked a lot of questions. They had a lot of misconceptions too. They thought you could just sit down in the room quietly and quickly go out there and publish it. It's like writing today and publishing tomorrow. I could sympathise with their challenges. They are really willing but maybe never know the way out. Do you know people came all the way from Jalingo, Abuja, and other far places to the festival because of their interest in writing? They paid their way. One boy had no place even to sleep in this hotel and was lying down in the lobby. That's to tell you the kind of hunger they have for writing. I think we really need to create a structure to encourage the youth of this nation. A lot of people want to go out there and express themselves.
You are now based in the West, writing and lecturing. What's your impression about writers at home?
I think that there is a lot of ignorance about the process people think they need to go through before they get their work published. I know ANA is doing its best to promote writers and other stuffs like that. People need more information. Sometimes it's your duty to find out most of this information. Actually I found out about Caine prize myself. The Internet was not even like the way it is today. I got to school and began to fetch for information. I went to libraries too. Some of them think they are stuck in Nigeria here and that is why they are not probably making it. It is not going to be easier even if you choose to live in New York. You can't just get published because you are in New York. If you are really hungry and you get a good publisher, good for you. People need to stop seeing themselves as victims or handicap.
The recent publishers' conference treated an issue that had to do with you and your book. Cassava Republic reported that Longman is pirating its book, Waiting For An Angel, your book of course. When it happened, it became an issue. Longman said it was representing Penguin Books and that it had the distribution rights. Are you aware of this or did you give distribution rights to Longman?
Basically what happened was that these books were taken by Penguin Books and stocked. The books were not there. People would be telling me they want to get the book. I paused awhile. You are not making money from the book. I am not making money from the book. The books were not there. So I think, why can I get a Nigeria publisher that can market the book and bring in money? So I went through my agent and we did it perfectly. They were aware. I got their agreement.
How would you assess the transition from Penguin to Cassava Republic?
I think I want to really say I appreciate what these guys are doing in Nigeria especially Cassava Republic. I tell you it's not easy. They are pioneers and you know pioneers have to suffer.
What do you have to say about the trend of growth of new writers with foremost writers?
People should not compare ours with the generation of Chinua Achebe. Our content is not the same. It is not this generation that will judge us. This generation judges the books written 50 years ago. Likewise we shall be judged by the next generation maybe in another 50 years.
We are celebrating today, 50 years of Nigeria literature. Can you predict what it will be in another 20 years?
I think Nigeria literature has come of age. Imagine what Nigeria literature would have looked like without Chinua Achebe, Soyinka and their peers. We are underestimated and under-celebrated. I think the time has come for government to rise and celebrate stars in this country. They should name streets after Soyinka, Achebe and others. English Departments in most schools are all gone. If you don't celebrate these people, how do you want younger people to take after them?

Original_Search_Title: From Political Seat to High-Class Lakeside Town
Headline: Uganda: From Political Seat to High-Class Lakeside Town
Body_Text:
This recreational garden in Entebbe stands in place of a former market. Below, one of the structures coming up in the town
Oral literature has it that, the place earned its name from the Luganda word "ntebe" (chair). This was where, during the pre-colonial times, the office (seat) of the chief in Buganda who adjudicated in legal matters of the inhabitants, was found.
Later on, Sir Gerald Portal, one of the earliest British colonial commissioners in Uganda, used the area to conduct administrative business. Entebbe was the hub of political and economic activity.
So many years later, the political activity has moved to Kampala, leaving Entebbe, a peninsula on Lake Victoria, calm and laid back.
Steven Erute, an insurer who has just relocated there, has only praises to sing of his new locale.
"There is something I find irresistible about Entebbe. We all know each other and converge at Four Turkeys, Cassava Republic the various beaches on weekends to socialise," he says.
Robert Kiboli has different reasons for living in Entebbe. "The serenity here irresistible. I love Entebbe for the cool breeze, sunny days, the birds and fish, but most of all, it gives a child the right atmosphere to grow up in."
Nkumba Business College was upgraded to a University so Jackson Oboth says living in Entebbe has given him a chance to work and study at the same time.
"It is a melting pot of cultures. We always have traditional wedding ceremonies of the Acholi, Bagishu, Japadhola and Batoro in the area," says Phillip Corry who grew up there. "You can spot an Entebbe resident a mile away. They are often polite people and have strong religious ties. If one stays long enough in Entebbe, the locals get to know and recognise him/her as family."
Some elegant colonial era Elizabethan Architecture that used to house colonial masters is still in place, with large compounds. Some structures have been mowed down and replaced with new structures but the tree lined avenues remain. Lately, the traditional profile of Entebbe residents is changing from strictly civil servants to include the private sector.
There are accountants, pilots, vet doctors, aviation engineers, lecturers and working holiday travellers.
As the town battles to reassert its supremacy, its strategic position connecting Uganda to the outside world, by air and marine, is attracting an exodus of property developers, residents and buyers.
"A plot of land measuring between 50mx100m here goes for sh15m," says Glades Namuto, a new settler in Nakiwogo. "But land prices depend on one's haggling skills."
Downtown in Kitoro, a supermarket notice board announces a three square mile Busi Island at $2,800 per acre. A plush bungalow is tagged $2000.
Tirupati Property Developer has put up an estate in Nkumba, complete with a shopping center, entertainment facilities and playgrounds.
The lender friendly policies in banking institutions are enabling residents to secure loans and mortgages to either buy or develop their property.
