Aganga noted at the retreat that the whole idea of the transformation agenda was to create economic growth in the country and ensure the creation of jobs, stressing that the ministry would focus on the implementation of mandatory skills transfer to Nigeria by foreign construction companies as well as develop industrial clusters for the real sector.
The Bank of Industry, Department of Trade in the ministry and the other parastatals and agencies under the ministry have also mapped out clear plans to create additional three million jobs in the next three years as the first step towards the eradication of poverty in Nigeria. This was one of the highpoints of the communiqué issued at the end of a three-day retreat of the ministry in Abuja.
Backward integration
According to a statement from the ministry, as part of the strategies towards achieving this, the different departments and parastatals will develop a comprehensive backward integration programme aimed at improving innovation and productivity for rice, sugar, wheat, yam, potatoes, starch and palm produce, among others. The statement said the ministry would establish model industrial clusters in each geo-political zone across the country.
It said, "In line with the mandate to lead the nation's investment, job creation and economic growth aggressive investment drive, the ministry must be refocused and repositioned in order to effectively serve as the flag and hub of industrial revolution that will help Nigeria take its rightful place in global affairs. In this regard, departments, agencies and parastatals under the ministry have pledged to create not less than 3,100,850 new jobs within the next three years.
"To achieve effective export promotion, participants agreed on the need for increased efforts towards streamlining the nation's export produce and documentation as a way of facilitating trade through stronger collaboration with all relevant trade facilitation."
With the right awareness campaign and the momentum already being gathered for investment growth, the stage is set for Okonjo-Iweala to perform in the right direction.

Original_Search_Title: The Harsh Reality of Popular Reality Shows
Headline: Nigeria: The Harsh Reality of Popular Reality Shows
Body_Text:
Like blow flies attracted to a corpse, our youths have besieged the auditions and sets of reality television shows that have sprung across the country. These are sometimes troubled youths trying to find themselves or escape unfavorable circumstances of life.
Others are on a mission to find a shortcut to fortune and fame or infamy as the case might be. These shows include UBA's Dragon's Den, Malta Guinness Street Dance, MTN's Project Fame, Nigerian Breweries' Gulder Ultimate Search (GUS), Amstel Malta Box Office, Maltina Dance All, D'banj's Koko Mansion and Amstel Malta Box Office (AMBO), a production of Nigerian Breweries.
Big Brother Africa airs on cable television. This reality show features a number of contestants penned in an isolated house, while trying to avoid being evicted, and ultimately winning a large cash prize following a set number of days in confinement. Despite derision from critics, Big Brother has enjoyed growing popularity across the continent.
Big Brother Africa season 6, tagged Big Brother Amplified, has come and gone. It ran for 91 days and 24/7 leaving in its wake two winners with the grand prize of USD 200, 000 each, namely Karen Igho of Nigeria and Wendell Parson from Zimbabwe and also an intriguing article from Dele Momodu, the Ovation Magazine founder, about the show. The magazine publisher also fondly called Bob Dee by his friends and close associates was the Presidential Candidate of the National Conscience Party (NCP) in the 2011 April general elections.
I found Bob Dee's article titled, "The Karenplification Of Africa" and published in the THISDAY Newspaper, even more interesting than the show. From my experience, watching the housemates cooking, sleeping, fighting and engaged in other mundane tasks (24/7) is even worse than watching paint dry or grass grow. Daily highlights of about an hour provide a synopsis of the activities of the day and this I sometimes catch a glimpse of. But Bob Dee was a more loyal fan of the show than I was. This probably exemplifies his doggedness and see-things-through attitude to life. He asserts - and I do not begrudge him for keeping himself entertained with the antics of the housemates which is the essence of the show - in the opening paragraph of his write-up that he and his family were hooked on Big Brother - perhaps like pounded yam and Egusi soup. "Fellow Nigerians, I don't know what you have been watching lately on television but as for me and my house we have been hooked on BIG BROTHER AMPLIFIED. And I'm not ashamed to say it publicly". That's Bob Dee expressing himself with his usual candor. However, from the parental guidance warning by DSTV, surely Big Brother isn't a wholesome family diet?
Bob Dee also tells us that he wept like a baby at the heart wrenching and tear-jerking tales of the participants. "I had my own stint at weeping last Monday as Karen took the invisible Big Brother and her captive audience down the memory lane of her tortuous journey through life. I listened to every word she uttered and watched every one of her powerful delivery. Nothing escaped me. As I released torrents of tears, I phoned a friend who told me that I have good company in his house. His Ghanaian wife and mother-in-law were crying their eyes tearless and his wife had just succeeded in ruining her fresh makeup as she was on her way to work. The Karen story was a spellbinder. I was mesmerized by the story of her beleaguered childhood. It was a stuff of thriller."
As I read these tear-filled lines, my eyebrows drew together in a frown as if to embrace themselves.
Bob Dee is a young (relatively), enterprising family man. I assume therefore that he has young and possibly impressionable children. Had he won the last Nigerian presidential elections, his wife and children could very well have being Nigeria's First Family. It is therefore curious that he subjected this "potentially" (theoretically at least) First Family to the vile language, scenes of nudity, licentious and alcohol induced depravities of the Big Brother cellmates, I mean inmates, sorry I mean housemates.
Bob Dee dreams of a futuristic Big Brother in his write-up..."I wanted to see a platform that would reform some of our recalcitrant youths and mould them into decent members of our troubled society." This dream is a far cry from the current Big Brother reality.
Karen Igho went into the Big Brother enclosure as an uncouth, crass, raw, unpolished, crude human being. During the 91 days of isolation, she wasn't refined or reformed by the zoo keeper aka Big Brother. Rather he provided her alcohol to induce her to a higher energy level of entertainment all for our viewing pleasure. In the words of Bob Dee, "I also did not recognize the fact that the principal essence of the show was pure and unadulterated entertainment. Some of us saw it as a talent hunt, while others believed it was a quest for expertise in pretensions."
Is Karen Igho intrinsically a better person for participating and winning the reality show? I truly doubt it. She most likely still needs some reformation and polishing from a finishing school. She also needs to find her footing in how to string together a coherent sentence in the English Language. But she may have achieved her dreams of being on the show. Our values differ and this might be the leverage to higher grounds for her - whatever her higher or lower grounds are.
In the closing remarks of Bob Dee's write-up, he recognizes the real issues, "Her mother would have to pray and fast for her. It is not always easy to exorcise the demons of street-life. She must conquer her biggest enemy and that is alcohol. I'm perpetually troubled once Karen starts drinking. She is always going bananas. May God redeem this young girl and keep her safe..."
Our perception of reality is often our personal interpretation of events around us. Hence opinions, viewpoints or interpretation of realities differ. Our interpretation of reality is often informed by our childhood conditioning or upbringing, our values, life experiences and expectations. This develops into a patterned style of thinking. It is perhaps instructive that despite the hype of these reality shows, most of the previous winners have often faded or jaded into relative obscurity.
Ukah writes from Allen, Ikeja, Lagos.

Original_Search_Title: Trade And Investment Ministry Targets 3.1 Million Jobs
Headline: Nigeria: Trade And Investment Ministry Targets 3.1 Million Jobs
Body_Text:
The departments, agencies and parastatals under the Ministry of Trade and Investment have pledged to create additional 3,100,850 jobs within the next three years.
This was one of the highpoints of a communiqué issued at the end of a three-day retreat of the ministry in Abuja. The retreat was chaired by the Minister of Trade and Investment, Mr. Olusegun Aganga; and the Minister of State for Trade and Investment, Dr. Sam Ortom.
A breakdown of the job creation figures shows that the Bank of Industry will create one million jobs, the Department of Trade (two million jobs) and others (100,085 jobs).
As part of the strategies towards achieving this, the departments and parastatals will develop a comprehensive backward integration programme to improve innovation and productivity for rice, sugar, wheat, yam, potatoes, starch and palm produce, among others.
According to the communiqué, the ministry will establish model industrial clusters in each geo-political zone across the country.
It said, "In line with the mandate to lead the nation's investment, job creation and economic growth aggressive investment drive, the ministry must be refocused and repositioned in order to effectively serve as the flag and hub of industrial revolution that will help Nigeria take its rightful place in global affairs. In this regard, departments, agencies and parastatals under the ministry have pledged to create not less than 3,100,850 new jobs within the next three years.
"To achieve effective export promotion, participants agreed on the need for increased efforts towards streamlining the nation's export produce and documentation as a way of facilitating trade through stronger collaboration with all relevant trade facilitation," it added.
Speaking during the event, the Minister of Trade and Investment, Mr. Olusegun Aganga, said that the Federal Government was committed to providing the enabling environment for job creation, wealth generation and economic growth and development.
He said, "In other parts of the world such as the United Kingdom, Japan, Indonesia and Singapore, the Ministry of Trade and Investment is the most powerful and strategic ministry in terms of driving economic growth and development. But in Nigeria, we have not played our roles the way we should and that is why we have not been able to harness the abundant human and natural resources in the country for job creation and wealth generation.
"If Nigeria is going to make significant progress in economic growth and development, the Ministry of Trade and Investment has to play a major role. Currently, we are working out pro-job, pro-growth policies, which will be recommended to the Economic Management Team of President Goodluck Jonathan."
He added, "Our objective within the next three years is to significantly increase the manufacturing local content and linkages with other sectors of the economy and ensure global competitiveness with manufactured goods, by making made-in-Nigeria goods a major foreign exchange earner. Going forward, we intend to achieve rapid and sustained economic growth by broadening the nation's productive base.
"With a population of over 150 million people, we consider ourselves highly privileged to be in a position to influence policies, create jobs and implement policies that will positively affect the lives of Nigerians. We are here to serve fellow Nigerians. From what I have observed during the three-day retreat, we have what it takes to deliver on the President's transformation agenda".
In his remarks, the Minister of State for Trade and Investment, Dr. Samuel Ortom, said the staff of the ministry must be dedicated to their duties if the country must achieve the set objectives.
He said, "Now that we have a road map that will drive the ministry for the next four years, we need the dedication, hard work and cooperation of everybody to be able to drive the transformation agenda of the Federal Government.
"Nigerians see President Goodluck Jonathan's transformation agenda as the last hope. We believe that with God on our side, we will be able to create three million jobs within the next four years," he added.

Original_Search_Title: Bauchi Earmarks N339 Million for Ramadan Feeding
Headline: Nigeria: Bauchi Earmarks N339 Million for Ramadan Feeding
Body_Text:
Bauchi — The Bauchi State government has budgeted N339 million for Ramadan feeding in the state this year. Chairman of this year's Feeding System Committee, who is the former Commissioner of Finance in the state, Alhaji Aminu Hammayo disclosed this yesterday while addressing newsmen.
Alhaji Aminu said N100 million has been set aside for the purchase of food items for distribution to people of the state while N59 million would be distributed to people in the 20 local government areas of the state. He added that N50 million has been earmarked for feeding in Jumaat mosques in Bauchi metropolis and environs and another N 50 million for feeding in Jumaat mosques in local government areas. Hammayo further said that N80 million has been approved for feeding of women in the state
He said the seven man committee is vested with the responsibilities of ensuring feeding of less privileged people during the Ramadan period and also to monitor the exercise.
Alhaji Aminu said 82 mos-ques would serve as feeding centers within Bauchi metropolis and environs and 7 to 14 mosques would serve as feeding centers in each of the local government areas of the state.
He said the committee has put in place measures to ensure check and balances. "We have given them our menu and all feeding centers must stick to it. They are expected to serve yam, rice and beans or spaghetti in addition to drinks and fruits. Our target is to feed between 14,000 to 16,000 people daily," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan's Spouse Lectures on Protocol
Headline: Nigeria: Jonathan's Spouse Lectures on Protocol
Body_Text:
The recent five day retreat on protocol organized by the office of the First Lady was indeed revealing. It was an opportunity to build up the decorum of the spouses of the nation's political leaders and equip them for the challenges of dealing with the public.
It remarkably came against the background of the public spat last year between the First Lady and Governor Chibuke Amaechi at a public ceremony in Okrika, Rivers State. The retreat was also attended by wives of Governors, presiding officers of the National Assembly, service chiefs among other spouses of the nation's political leaders.
Declaring open the retreat that dwelt on protocol, etiquette and administration on their daily official functions at the serene environment of Obudu Cattle Ranch in Cross River State, Dame Jonathan said that the retreat was aimed at equipping the participants with the International Best Practices on Protocol, Etiquette and Administration in their daily official functions.
The first lady who expressed joy that the retreat was taking place in a beautiful environment said that she had no doubt that the Obudu environment will afford all the participants the opportunity of deep concentration and absorption of the lectures by experts that have been invited as resource persons at the retreat.
She said that the retreat was timely as the nation currently seeks positive transformation in all aspects of national life and that if things must be got right, there must be a radical transformation in the way things are done in the country.
"This is the main reason we are all here; to learn new approaches that are in conformity with international standards and best practices. The retreat therefore, will enable us acquire practical skills that are in consonance with the transformation agenda of the Goodluck / Sambo administration", she said.
The President's wife noted that the lectures and presentations at the retreat were designed to be practical and interactive, stressing that they were designed to provide knowledge that will not only expand their horizon but also boost confidence and help when they support their spouses in the conduct of their official duties.
According to her the retreat was organized to bring about style, substance and best global practices to bear in official and social engagements; instill in participants attitudes that protect sensitive national dignities at major formal and informal occasions and to gain insight into the principle and practice of smooth, graceful, orderly, decent, proper dress code and efficient conduct of functions involving national and international dignitaries.
Besides, she said that at the end of the retreat participants were expected to acquire proper communication skills with respect of public speaking and speech presentation; when to speak, what to say, what not to say and use of proper language in public in view of their dignified positions.
"At the end f this retreat we are all expected to have acquired an improved understanding of universally acceptable principles and best practices in Protocol, Etiquette, Courtesy, Effective Administration and Time Management. We would all also have gained new and additional knowledge needed to go through and manage international dynamics in important meetings", she submitted.
Dame Jonathan advised the participants to take the knowledge and skills acquired there to their various constituencies and help to impart them to identified targeted groups and individuals, pointing out that its overall effect would no doubt help in the transformation agenda.
She commended their host Governor Liyel Imoke and his wife, the hostess Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke for the warm reception accorded them right from their arrival in the state.
In her address of welcome, wife of the Cross River State governor, Mrs. Obioma Liyel Imoke commended Dame Jonathan for choosing the ranch resort for the retreat and said the retreat was intended to be a life changing experience which would benefit not just the participants but generations unborn.
"What better way to affirm the Women for Change and development Initiative which has reverberated across the Nation like a Tsunami in recent times. Courtesy again of Her Excellency, Dame (Dr) Patience Jonathan, the initiator and Grand Patron", she said.
Mrs. Imoke recalled that after the April general elections in the country when President Jonathan visited the state with his wife Dame Patience to have a rest at the mountain resort after the rigorous electioneering campaigns. The President and his wife according to her took time out of the working vacation to catch up with not just endless paper work, but also an opportunity for them to relive memories of their earlier days in the natural environment offered by Obudu.
"She immediately fell in love with this resort and never hesitated to voice her opinion. Distinguished Ladies and Gentlemen, It was during this visit that Her Excellency First muted the idea of organizing a retreat of this nature at The Obudu Mountain Resort. "I stand here today in all humility and total submission to the Almighty creator and I must give all the Glory to the almighty God who located this wonderful and breathtaking mountain Resort in Cross River State, which the State Government has now prepared and given to Nigeria as a pride and to the world for business and leisure".
"Thank you Your Excellency Ma, for making this possible. It is only a true mother that has the good of her children uppermost in her heart that constantly thinks of ways of uplifting her children and that is what we are benefitting from today, one of the many advantages of having a good mother", she said.
She said the state's tourism events calendar begins from August with the celebration of the Yakurr New Yam Festival popularly known as Leboku international new yam festival which sets the tone for the celebration of New Yam Festivals all over the State.
As a tourism state, Mrs. Obioma Imoke said that every November, the world sporting community congregate at the Obudu Mountain Resort for the highest paying Mountain Race in the world, while in the whole of December, the State celebrates the 32 day Christmas festival extravaganza, which climaxes with the two day Calabar carnival, an event which is known as Africa's Biggest Street Party!
She said the retreat opens the state's tourism calendar for this year on a high profile manner, adding, "It is my belief that we will take time off this very important business of training on etiquette and protocol to visit the alluring sites within the Obudu Mountain Resort which includes canopy walkway, the water parks at the bottom of the hill, the bird watching huts, the honey factory, the dairy section which is also home to the Resort Mascot Bull Donald and the natural spring waters all located within the resort".
Mrs. Imoke appealed to the participants as they savored the serene environment of the ranch resort, that they should also convince their husbands to rather spend the summer and other holidays at the ranch instead of Europe, USA, South Africa, Kenya and the rest, enthusing that, "We in Cross River State refer to this resort as the Switzerland of Nigeria, the tourism sector on Africa has named it one of the seven wonders of Africa".
It is believed that the retreat which was attended by the crème la crème of women in the country would produce effective results and that the participants would put into practice all the objectives of the retreat in their various offices and not allow personal ego and the exalted positions they find themselves to becloud their sense of reasoning and make them to think that the position is an eternal one.

Original_Search_Title: UDP Holds Rally in Serrekunda.
Headline: Gambia: UDP Holds Rally in Serrekunda.
Body_Text:
The United Democratic Party (UDP) last Saturday, 30th July, held a rally at Serrekunda (at the garage).
Speaking at the rally, Aji Yam Secka said come November 24th the Gambia will have a new President which will be Lawyer Ousainou Darboe, describing him (Ousainou) as a Gambian who is born and will die in Gambia.
She said president Jammeh said he got the support of the Gambian ladies but added that these are not the ladies of UDP. She said even though they don't have a TV station or a radio station but they still have the support of the Gambians. She said they still have strong support of the people because they are just from touring the country. She added that in some parts of the country many people have defected to UDP because they said they are hearing that the president wants to be crown a king in Gambia. She said she cannot say all what had happened in various part of the country but that they are grateful with Gambians.
Aji Yam informed the party supporters that all the defections they are seeing on the TV are false and said their supporters are still with the UDP. He said the APRC government knows very well why they are not giving the opposition the chance to be on the national TV.
Also speaking at the ceremony, Mr Kemeseng Jammeh dilated on the up coming elections, noting that the people should come in their numbers to vote for the UDP. He said when Ousainou takes power there will be presidential term limits of two terms for every president.
He called on the APRC to desist from tribalism, stressing that they are the only party that talks about various tribes in the country. He said we should be careful as to whom we give our votes.
Speaking at the rally, Hon. Babanding K.K. Daffeh, National Assembly Member for Kiang Central, started by advising the KMC Mayor Yankuba Colley to desist from Niamina land problems and concentrate on the KMC which, he described, as of no use to Gambians.
Hon. Daffeh said Mr Kebba Dibba of GRTS should be a professional journalist and should know which kind of people he should invite on his programme. He said Dibba should not forget the ethics of journalism. He said if one looks at the nature of that programme all the people invited their will be saying negative things about the oppotition. "How can you say any body not part of APRC is not a good citizen", he asked.
He dilated on the high prices of commodities, noting that the government is playing with money. He said all cannot be said but finally call on them to become one.
Hon Momodou L Sanneh, Minority Leader and Member for Kiang West, said the APRC government is a failure, noting that even tough the APRC said they want to eradicate corruption and now they are the most corrupt government.
Hon. Sanneh said "At least the last government has a GPTC and the children were going to school without no problem. Now he is giving millions of dalasi to schools while the kids don't have buses to take them to school. He is the only president giving millions to people and will tell Gambians that he will not come down from power unless he wants to. There is only one rich man in this country and that is him. People are afraid to speak their minds because of him. I will also like to advice Imam F atty to be an imam and stop criticizing the opposition."
Dembo Bojang said the Yaya Jammeh government is just like going back to slavery. He said people see every day accused persons on the TV who are on leg shackles and handcuffs like slaves. He said if we do not try to change this government we will all become slaves. He assured that the Bakau people are still UDP adding that the gathering they saw on TV at the APRC meeting there were not all from Bakau. He noted that most people who went to the meeting needed some money from president Jammeh as he the richest man in Gambia. He said some will pretend as if they are APRC but that November 2011 will tell everything.
He said many people who are following Yaya Jammeh are doing so because of money, adding that a lot of Gambian musicians do not have him in their hearts but will only sing about him to get money. He also said if the opposition are given the chance to be on the TV then the whole Gambia will know the truth.
Lawyer Ousainou Darboe who was the last speaker at the rally commended Femi Peters for his hard work and sacrifices for the party. He advised the civil servants to be careful with what they do whiles in office, citing the example of Ensa Badjie, former IGP, who he said was responsible for the prosecution of the UDP Campaign Manager, Femi Peters. He commended some members of the party, especially those who were at the registration centers. Darboe dilated on the "city of lights", noting that not everywhere in Banjul has lights.
He dilated on the high prices of commodities, especially sugar, which, he said, is one of the most essential needs of people, adding that the bag now costs up to D1,500.
" For Basiru Jawara, I had what you told the Observer and I will solve that matter with you. Please, I want the party militants to forget about Basiru. I will solve that matter with him", Darboe told the UDP supporters.
Darboe said this election is about change and to remove the government which always subjects people to unnecessary arrests and detentions and sending Gambians to live in imprisonment. He said the latest arrest was that of the renown Islamic scholar, Alh Ismaila Manjang.
He said the like Imam fatty are not doing their job in the right way as religious leaders. He said they should have defended those kind of religious leaders but instead are using their position to challenge the opposition.
He advised Gambians to come in their large numbers and vote against the APRC so that Gambians can have a fresh start. The party supporters turned out in their large numbers to attend the meeting.
The UDP Party Leader however did not dwell on the issue opposition parties coming together to contest the forthcoming November presidential election as was expected by some people, including opposition supporters and symphatisers.
Femi Peters, Lamin Dibba and Shyngle Nyassi were all at the rally but did not give statements.

Original_Search_Title: Good, Bad, Ugly Sides of Kaduna's New Night Life
Headline: Nigeria: Good, Bad, Ugly Sides of Kaduna's New Night Life
Body_Text:
Kaduna — Even after the curfew imposed on Kaduna after the recent post-election violence has been lifted, night life in Kaduna has slowed down into something else.
Since the explosion of a bomb in Obalende, the most popular relaxation joint in Kaduna on Sunday, 10 July, 2011, the once vibrant nightlife in the state is now a shadow of itself. From Obalende to Barnawa complex, Sabo, Yam Market, and even mammy markets, the fear of bomb and rumour of Boko Haram's planned attack had battered the bubbles in Kaduna nightlife.
Both operators of the relaxation joints and patrons are now conducting their activities in fear, hence the low turnout and patronage. At Barnawa complex, the very popular fizzy joint is not spared of the bomb scare. The turnout as a result of the bomb apprehension is poor and the operators are counting their losses. The complex is made up of various beer parlours, suya and barbecue joints, restaurants and super stores among others. Patrons of the joints cut across all strata in the society yet, the tempo of activities here is still low to date.
Lamenting the poor patronage, one Madam Hajara Bijimi who sells drinks, pepper soup, and peppered meet attributed it not only to the curfew but also the speculation that the Boko Haram sect was targeting the complex because big men patronize the place.
Madam Bijimi who started business in the complex in 1984, said she has lost majority of the big men who patronize her to the bomb scare. "Before, we stay here up to 1 - 2am but now everybody has to close before 11pm. I used to make about 50,000 naira daily but now I hardly make 5,000 naira. So this threat has really affected us negatively because many of us have been here for a very long time and we don't have other sources of livelihood," she cried.
One of the attendants of the beer parlours at the complex who pleaded anonymity told Weekly Trust that prior to the imposition of curfew in the state, the complex used to be one of the most happening places in Kaduna.
"When complex used to be complex, people stay here till 4am; until a time when thieves started disturbing us by stealing cars then the police instructed us to start closing by 12 midnight. In spite of that we still stay till around 1am but now due to the curfew, we close latest by 10:45. Now apart from the fear of thieves and the curfew in the state, people are afraid of going to public places because of the incessant spate of bombs in the state," he said.
We learnt that by the time it is 9pm, those who sell roasted and peppered fish start parking their items to close for the day. One of them said that she comes out earlier than before so that she can close on time and go back home.
Less quantities of consumables are being put on sales now. A barbecue seller said the quantity of meat they prepare has reduced because they have limited time to sell before the curfew hours set in due to fear of coming to the complex especially at night.
A popular suya seller along Badarawa, Alhaji Musa Mai Nama blamed the curfew for the downturn in his business, saying "before the curfew, I used to roast 30-40 chickens and open around 5pm till 12-1.am, but now I only roast 10 chickens out of which about four will remain because I have to close by 10.30pm if not when the soldiers come and find us still at our business areas, they punish us by telling us to enter into dirty drainages near our shops," he said.
A recharge card seller near NAF Club, Sani Hassan, 35, said his business has paralysed and that he is contemplating relocating to his home state, Katsina.
"I came to Kaduna to make meaning out of life and have a means of livelihood and it was okay before the curfew, but now sales has dropped drastically, I can hardly feed my family so I think I will relocate to my home town until things become normal again," he lamented.
Unlike before, the girls that walk around NAF Club in search of customers have fled following the curfew and the level of insecurity in the country, Weekly Trust observed when our correspondent visited the area twice last weekend.
A tea seller otherwise known as Mai Shayi near NEPA roundabout, Haruna Mato, said the restriction of time of operation of Okada riders would affect his business because he relies mainly on them for transportation.
"Before the government banned motorcycles from operating at night, I used to have costumers till the time of the curfew, but yesterday I stayed till 11.30 no customers came and I had to just close my shop and walk home that night," he lamented.
He called on the government to lift the ban on motorcycle operations especially since Ramadan is here, people will need okada riders to go to their places of worship for night prayers.
While some members of the society see the curfew as an infringement of their right of movement, some housewives whose husbands keep late night see it as an answered prayer because it forces their husbands back home earlier.
Speaking to one of such housewives who preferred anonymity said that since the curfew, she has been enjoying her husband's fellowship as the curfew forces him to return home earlier than before.
"When there was no curfew, my husband does not come back home until around 1.am or 2.am, in fact sometimes, he doesn't come home to sleep because he drinks and chases girls. But since the curfew, he has nowhere to sleep but his house because even the joints will have to close and go home," she said.
Okada riders said the ban would unleash untold hardship on them especially the civil servants among them who complement their meagre salaries with the commercial motorcycle business at night.
An okada rider on Waff Road, Abdullahi M. Sule said the state government was not fair to them at all. "From this weekend, fasting will start and for those of us who are Muslims, we will prefer to work at night than in the afternoon, because of the hot sun. The decision is not taken at the right time, government should review it," he said.
On his part, Ibrahim Abdullahi who operates around Kano Road bus stop asked the state government to explain the ban on their operation at night which he described as prime time.
A private motorcycle user who works with a newspaper company, Usman Ibn Lapai said the ban would not work and that it is worse than the curfew. "The government should look at it critically because the time restriction is worse than the curfew. By this act, the government has created means for the security personnel to be exploiting people because many violate the order," he said.
However, the Vice Chairman of the Amalgamated Commercial Motorcycle Owners and Riders Association of Nigerian (ACOMORAN), Kaduna State chapter, Ibrahim Mamman Daura said the government carried them along before taking the decision.
"Initially, they wanted to ban our operation entirely in the state like other states did but when we approached them through the SSG, they decided to limit our operation from 8.pm to 6.am which to us is not too bad because real Okada Riders work between 6. am to 8.pm. Those who operate at night are civil servants and security personnel," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Minimum And Maximum Wages And the Poverty Line
Headline: Nigeria: Minimum And Maximum Wages And the Poverty Line
Body_Text:
Eighteen thousand naira a month, or N600 a day, for one citizen working in the public service. What can N600 buy him or her in the market today? On a lucky day, perhaps a sizable fish or tuber of yam. Fifteen million naira and N14 million, or N500,000 and N467,000 a day, for another citizen, working in the same public service, this citizen being a senator or representative of the people. What can N500,000 buy him or her? Ah, come on, you say! I have based my calculations on the figures given in the report, "Legislators' Pay as Burden on Economy" in The Guardian of 19 June 2011.
They may not be exact, and there are other figures, some higher and some lower, that have been bandied about since the mind-boggling scandal of the looting of the treasury, legalised as legislators' salaries and allowances, began to get due attention.
Although the members of the National Assembly have received, almost exclusively, withering criticism of their greed and venality, it is obvious that they merely symbolise the national malaise that has kicked the country into the gutter. This orgy of looting, of "authority stealing" - as Fela memorably puts it - is replicated to near-equal degree and ferocity in every state house of assembly as well as in the federal and state executive cabinets. Where the legislators make laws or adopt resolutions to legitimise their highway robbery, the executives do it mostly by way of bogus or inflated contracts to themselves or their partners in crime. And the ruses for graft know no bounds. The world might wonder, for instance, what it is about furniture and cars in Nigeria that makes it necessary to replace them every year; but then, the world has long stopped wondering about us.
While we wait to see if the new class of senators and representatives will do the only honourable thing this scandal demands, which is to introduce a bill on the appropriate and just remuneration of all public servants, a few questions beg answers. Such as, What are the parameters for determining a minimum wage? And what logic led the Nigeria Labour Congress to accept a minimum wage of N18,000 as deserving or realistic and as the most the Federal and State governments could possibly pay?
I propose three commonsense considerations, with primary reference to the public sector where labour is tinged with the unquantifiable elements of duty and service. In the private sector, the matter would appear more straightforward, with economists harping on productivity, the proverbial supply and demand and the bottom line, while workers stress equitable remuneration relative to the surplus value they produce but which the owners of capital arrogate to themselves.
In either case, however, the first consideration ought to be the prevailing cost of living. A worker's wage ought to secure the basic necessities of food, shelter and clothing, and the near-equal necessities of health, children's school fees (to the extent applicable) and leisure. The assurance of these things guarantees that the worker will be sound in body and mind and so able to offer optimum productivity and replenish the labour force. In other words, the State must guarantee a minimum quality of life with the minimum wage. No citizen, let alone one employed by the state, should live below the poverty line.
The second factor is more practical: the actual capacity of the employer to pay. In strict economic terms, this capacity would depend on productivity and the bottom line. But this factor is highly mitigated in the public service where civic duty, and not a commodity to be sold at a higher price than the cost of its production, is required of the worker. In the public service, then, the most important consideration is the cost of living as a base, adjusted to take care of the level of responsibility, the training and experience required for the job, and prestige of office, this last being inseparable from training and responsibility.
But neither the legislators' self-awarded emoluments nor those approved by the Revenue Mobilization, Allocation and Fiscal Commission seem to take cognizance of any justifiable principle. RMAFC would have a senator and a representative hauling home a yearly fortune of N8.2 million and N6.4 million respectively. Which translates to about N22,485 and N17,405 a day. Making them earn more (or the same amount) in a day than the minimum wage public servant would earn in a month!
And this brings me to the third principle, which I frame in the form of a question: What, in the interest of social harmony, is the tolerable discrepancy between the highest and lowest paid public servant that a responsible government would allow? Please mark the phrases, "the interest of social harmony" and "a responsible government." At present, a chasm as wide as an ocean separates the least paid and the highest paid public servants. Need it be said that a government unmindful of such a corrosive divide creates by its own hands the conditions of lingering social unrest?
On a concluding note, it seems to me a scandal as well that the NLC has yet to mobilize workers to picket the national and state assemblies daily to force the honourable and distinguished legislators to give up their loot. NLC and the Nigerian people in general ought to be asking why a senator should earn over eight hundred times more than the minimum paid worker. And how much might the state and federal governments be able to pay as a living minimum wage if they lavished only a third, even half, of what is currently amassed for the top echelons of the bureaucracy? Or if we were to make a genuine effort at plugging the bottomless hole of corruption? Why would one of the poorest nations on earth be the one whose public service manufactures instant millionaires? Why are we bent on reversing the age-old wisdom that one goes to the private sector to make money and the public sector to render service? And just why aren't the masses in the streets to insist that the agent, the delegate, cannot enjoy a higher status than his or her principal? For if the Nigerian people are the collective principal, with a per capita income of $2,748 - according to World Bank figures for 2010 - how on earth can its agents be allowed to receive and keep a yearly emolument of $1.2 million and $1.12 million?
The NLC has threatened, and by the time this is published, may have begun a strike to "defend ... the fundamental human right of the Nigerian populace to live above poverty line." But it is beyond doubt that a single person, let alone a family of six (which ought to be the standard unit), cannot hope to live above the poverty line with N18,000 a month or N600 a day. NLC president, Abdulwahed Omar, would be better off calling for a clear determination of the poverty line, and then a renegotiation of the minimum wage. A very conservative guess might put this at N70,000.
It is obvious that Nigeria can pay it, if it can afford to pay a single senator N15 million a month, and - by Professor Sagay's calculation -N453.3 billion a year to legislators in the three tiers of government alone. If we include the figure for federal, state and local executives, and what is lost to corruption and waste, the treasury would be replenished by close to a trillion naira a year. Imagine that! Judiciously used, the Nigerian worker would then enjoy a minimum wage above the poverty line.
Ifowodo teaches poetry and literature at Texas State University in the United States.

Original_Search_Title: As Police Abandon Hunt for Daughter's Killers...
Headline: Nigeria: As Police Abandon Hunt for Daughter's Killers...
Body_Text:
It is a case which smells of murder but the police have not been able to crack it since September last year when three dead bodies were found in a studio. With them was found a plate of rice and some bottles of beer which they had apparently been drinking from. The family of one of the deceased, Goodness, claims on the day their daughter was found dead in the studio, she left home in the company of her friend, Ama, a young lady whose relationship with their daughter they have always frowned at even though the two friends remained inseparable.
Naturally, Ama was a suspect in the death of the three people and she was invited for questioning by the police. But what is now fuelling cries of protests from Goodness' parents is Ama was released without any explanation by the police. And the force no longer seem interested in pursuing the case which had caused residents of Tudun Baushe in Mararaba, Nasarawa State wondering what could have happened at the studio that led to the death of the three people.
Mrs Dajan, mother of late Goodness, said about 10pm on Saturday, September 18, 2010, Ama invited Goodness out to a place where the latter was found dead the following day along with two men. The aggrieved mother claimed the police have withheld all information relating to the death of her daughter neither has any person been persecuted. This has made her suspect they might be trying to sweep the case under the carpet.
It would be recalled that in September last year, an attempt was made to interview the DPO of 'A' Division of the Mararaba Police station over the issue. The DPO who simply gave his name as Usman replied, "There are better things journalists can write other than this murder case. Go and look at the road leading to Kabayi and write about it."
Later, when a new DPO was appointed for the division, CSP Aliyu Haruna, he confirmed to have heard of the incident months before he became DPO of 'A' Division, Mararaba but no one mentioned it to him when he took over from the former DPO. "I was then in Lafia," he said. "I am aware that the incident happened in Mararaba and the suspect was brought here. I am surprised that people are still interested in this case."
Mrs. Dajan said on the night of Saturday, September 18 when her daughter got back home after assisting her in the spot where she fries yams and akara for sale, the said friend, Ama, came into the house obscenely dressed and asked her daughter to escort her to a place. She said she remembered scolding Ama for her outrageous outfit, querying whether her parents saw her leave the house in those clothes. "She said nothing," Mrs Dajan said. "They said they were going out and I gave my daughter money to buy drugs for me when coming back to treat my toothache that night. I waited for long and never saw them. The last time I saw her was the night I gave her money for drugs. When the police reported to me that they found her dead, they did not allow me to see her corpse before taking her to Keffi where she was left decaying on the ground with the two others."
Mr. Mathew Dajan, father of Goodness recalls his daughter coming to him on that Saturday to ask for money to take passport photographs. "I told her to do it in the neighbourhood and she did. When I came from work on Sunday morning, I thought she was at her mother's stall assisting her to fry akara. In the afternoon, I heard she was at the police station. I concluded that her ordeal with the police would teach her to remain indoors at night, unknown to me she had been killed."
Mrs Dajan said while she kept waiting for her daughter to show up, by 5am on Sunday morning Ama came to their house barefoot and pretended to be looking for Goodness, but a younger brother of the deceased challenged her and asked her to go and bring their sister.
Goodness' parents alleged she was poisoned along with the other two identified as DJ of TIS Studio situated in Akwu Base 2 and one of his friends who assisted him in the business. "No one other than the police who investigated the case can tell how the suspects cleverly executed their plan to make the deceased persons lock themselves indoor," said Mrs. Dajan.
Landlord to the late DJ one Mr David resident in Akwu Base 5 said they all knew him as DJ and he had some boys working for him in the studio in Akwu Base 2. "He is very humorous and friendly to everyone and a perfect companion of my wife. The DJ woke up by 5 am that day on Saturday, September 18 and washed all his clothes. He was still outside chatting with his friends when one of his employees came to him. After their discussion, he brought some of his clothes and gave them to the boy. When the late DJ did not return home that night, we concluded that he was invited as DJ somewhere."
Mrs. Dajan said that when they called Goodness' phone on Sunday afternoon, it rang for a long time and stopped. When they tried it the second time, an unfamiliar voice picked it and told them to hold on, "This is a police station," the voice told them. When they called about an hour later, the same voice responded, "The owner of this line you are calling is dead."
The voice directed them to report at Akwu police station. When they went down there, they were directed to Division 'A' of Mararaba Police Command, Nasarawa State where the command had immediately directed that the corpses be taken to the medical centre in Keffi. The police collected N1, 000 from family members for a photograph of each deceased person but never gave any copy out when they discovered journalists were interested in the case. After occasional arrests and releases, the police told family members of the deceased that the prime suspect was mentally retarded but investigations were still ongoing.
Mrs. Dajan said her daughter had told her of some of Ama's bad behaviours. She said: "My daughter said she has many cases at Mararaba police station. On a particular occasion, she invited Goodness to a party in Keffi but she did not honour the invitation because I was not at home. A girl later came and told us that Ama said she intended to poison Goodness at the party. This information soured their relationship. They settled their differences in July and Goodness was killed in September."
Disclosing how the three people were found dead, the DJ's landlord, Mr David, said a boy working for the DJ travelled to Kaduna and returned on Sunday morning. The boy decided to remain at home to wash his clothes and return to the studio in the afternoon. When he reached the street, he saw from a distance that the studio's door was shut. He went home and checked again at 1pm when he was sure all Christians must have returned from worship. But he still discovered from the same distance that the studio was still shut. He then moved closer and discovered that there was no padlock on the door.
Mr. David said the boy pushed the door and realized it was locked from inside. With the help of neighbours, they pushed and forced the door open and saw the DJ, his friend, an Akwa Ibom man, and Goodness Dajan all lying dead with a food flask containing white rice. There were also bottles of beer with them in the studio.
The police took the remaining half-content of the beer to the medical centre for test but never disclosed the result to anyone, said the parents of Goodness.
The DPO of 'A' Division, Mararaba, CSP Haruna said, "The case is manslaughter and only the court has the right to release the suspects on bail. They can only be released if someone has made a direct report in court and the suspects are on trial. But no one mentioned the case to me when I resumed. It could be at the headquarters in Lafia."
There have been allegations that before Ama was released, the police negotiated for the sum of N230, 000 but when Ama's family could not afford it, they reduced the amount. But CSP Aliyu Haruna said he knew nothing about that.
Mrs Dajan said she is crying for justice over the death of her daughter and striving to take care of her family. She said even though she will not spend a kobo to get justice, she will stand firm until she gets justice for her daughter.

Original_Search_Title: Removing Hawkers By Force Might Not Solve the Problem
Headline: Ghana: Removing Hawkers By Force Might Not Solve the Problem
Body_Text:
The Accra Metropolitan Assembly (AMA) is in a Catch 22 situation. It has pledged to rid Accra of street hawkers, as part of its avowed aim of building a Millennium City. On the other hand, it has a duty to ensure that the forced eviction would not result in bringing harm to the people, and ultimately, the whole of society.
In a nation, where employment avenues are limited, selling on the streets has become a lucrative business for many school drop-outs and stop gap activity for school leavers looking for resources to move on in life.
On the Graphic Road especially, hawking has been dominated by yam sellers, who have virtually extended the yam market at Agbogbloshie to both sides of the road. It is not the best of sights to behold, as these sellers, many of whom are improperly dressed, push yams virtually into the faces of drivers and passengers alike.
The attempt to eject these yam sellers from the road turned nasty on Monday. According to various reports, a scuffle that broke out resulted in various degrees of injuries. The police were called in to intervene, and 11 yam sellers were arrested. At the time of going to press, the 11 culprits were being processed for court.
The Chronicle is not against the prosecution of those who flout the laws of the assembly. That is the legitimate duty of the AMA. We are uncomfortable though about the brute force with which the law is being enforced.
It is not long ago when a hawker had his ear chopped off by an over-zealous AMA guard. On Monday, The Chronicle reported the pathetic story of a cold water selling young woman incarcerated at the Nsawam Medium Security Prisons for two weeks, before her family could settle a fine of GHÂ¢600 imposed on her.
The official reason given by the AMA boss, Dr. Alfred Okoe Vanderpuije, is that the diminutive and frail looking 'ice water girl' had beaten up an ex-serviceman in the course of the assembly ridding people like her from the streets.
The excuses given by the AMA boss for being brutish against fellow human beings do not add up. Invariably, AMA guards turn physical on these poor hawkers. When the hawkers attempt to defend their lives and livelihoods, they are rounded up, sent to court at short notice, and charged with offences that have little or no bearing on their activities, and handed very stiff sentences.
Sometime this year, a Chronicle reporter was manhandled while reporting the ejection exercise.
The Chronicle is inviting civil society organisations, especially human rights activists, to take an interest in the AMA eviction exercises. We are of the opinion that the force being used is in excess of what is permitted under the laws of this Constitutional Republic.
In some way, the whole society is responsible for the situation where our streets are taken over by hawkers. We have not factored the welfare of these unfortunate members of society in national planning.
Most Ghanaians, including the AMA itself, did not act promptly when hawkers began trickling in until the whole thing developed into a flood.
The Chronicle would like to believe that, while getting hawkers off the streets, it is the duty of the state to find alternate means of livelihood for our brothers and sisters, sons and daughters who have turned our streets into market places. Putting them in prison might only bring temporary relief, especially, to the AMA.
In the end, we may all pay a higher price for mingling them with hardened criminals in jail.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Rate Drops to 10.2 Percent
Headline: Nigeria: Inflation Rate Drops to 10.2 Percent
Body_Text:
The National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) said the inflationary rate for the month of June 2011 has dropped to 10.2 percent from the initial 12.2 percent recorded in May.
This was stated in the monthly statistical release of NBS yesterday.
NBS linked the change in the month-on-month index with the upward movement of the prices of some food items like yam, fruits and cereals.
The average monthly food prices rose by 1.3 percent in June 2011 when compared with May 2011 figure while the level of the Composite Food Index was higher than the corresponding level a year ago by 9.2 percent.
The average annual rate of rise of the index was 12.7 percent for the 12-month period ending June 2011.

Original_Search_Title: Minimum Wage - Workers Divided Over Strike
Headline: Nigeria: Minimum Wage - Workers Divided Over Strike
Body_Text:
A cross-section of civil servants, particularly those within grade level 1-6 tentatively favoured by the wage increase approved by President Goodluck Jonathan, are not favourably disposed to tomorrow's nationwide warning strike called by the Nigeria Labour Congress, LEADERSHIP investigations can confirm.
The workers interviewed insisted that the warning strike and a plan to stretch the industrial action in later days were inimical to the welfare of the common man.
The NLC and the Trade Union Congress (TUC) last week slated the warning strike to press home the demand for the payment of N18, 000 minimum salary to workers and an upward review of salaries of workers on grade level 7 and above. President Jonathan recently signed the Minimum Wage Bill into law, making it illegal for any Nigerian worker to earn below N18,000 in monthly wages.
Investigations reveal that junior workers and top-ranking federal civil servants are indifferent to the strike, just as they labeled it a needless exercise in which only the masses would be the hardest hit. Most of the people who spoke with LEADERSHIP on condition of anonymity expressed concern that the strike was going to impact negatively on the junior workers rather than top-ranking government workers.
A female junior worker said: "The strike is not the solution. Our experience in the past has shown that junior workers are hardest hit during strikes. How much is our salary? During Yar'Adua's tenure the minimum wage for all junior staff was N7, 500. But when Jonathan came, he gave us what they called accelerated increase to N12, 500. How much is that? What can it buy? The salary even finishes before the end of the month. Right now, they are calling for a strike, many of them have their houses stocked with food items. I don't have any."
A staff of the health ministry also condemned the strike and described it as a way of punishing the poor, as well as crippling the economy to the disadvantage of the masses.
"I'm not happy about the strike. Why should I be happy? Even the big officers too are not happy but they will not tell you. There are entitlements they get, particularly the senior ones. So if the strike starts, they will not get them again. Everything will be on standstill," he said.
Meanwhile, investigations have shown that Lagosians are bracing up for the nationwide strike.
LEADERSHIP investigations revealed that banking halls on the Oba Akran area of Ikeja witnessed an upsurge in the volume of customers who turned in to make withdrawals. One customer who was on the street, which is home to several banks and spoke to LEADERSHIP on the condition of anonymity, said it would be disastrous not to get ready for the strike.
He said: "I have to put my house in order by getting some foodstuff and keeping some cash handy for the strike period. You should know what three days means in Lagos. I cannot take chances."
Another customer who stormed the banking hall of a first-generation bank after the ATM failed to dispense cash said labour did well by giving enough notice so that people could prepare for the strike.
The customer who gave his names as Julius Obidike said: "Whoever is not prepared for this strike would have himself to blame. I have come to withdraw enough money that should last me a week because this strike will dovetail into the weekend."
The situation was even more frenetic in the major markets in the sprawling mega city. At Ketu/Mile 12 market, traders enjoyed a boom in patronage from customers, mostly women, who bought foodstuff like yam, beans, rice and tomatoes.
In Dalemo Market, the human traffic was heavy as several people defied the drizzle of the afternoon to do their shopping in preparation for the strike. Judging by the situation yesterday, the picture would get more chaotic today.
Rivers State governor Rotimi Amaechi yesterday faulted the idea of the federal government fixing the minimum wage that the 36 state governments should pay to the civil servants under them.
Amaechi said this while declaring open the 1st Nigerian Bar Association Criminal Justice Reform Conference in Abuja. He insisted that since the civil servants under the state governments are employees of the various state governments, their wages were expected to be fixed by their employers.
He questioned the rationale behind the federal government's power to decide the distribution pattern of electricity after a state government had generated it.
Academic activities in the nation's universities may once again come to a halt as the Academic Staff Union of Universities, ASUU, Calabar Zone, has directed its principal officers in the zone to "immediately and urgently" call an emergency National Executive Council, NEC, meeting to decide on what the union described as the unwillingness of government to sign the amended bill on the retirement age of academics.
The Calabar zone of the union, in a resolution signed by the zonal co-coordinator, Dr. Charles Ononoju, chairman of University of Calabar branch, Dr. James Okpiliya, Dr. Nsing Ogar, Charles Onyebinama and Anyim of CRUTECH, Michael Okpara University and UNIUYO respectively said that the federal government has not shown any sign of seriousness in the signing of the amended bill on the retirement age of academicians.

Original_Search_Title: Kumasi And the Battle That Raged
Headline: Nigeria: Kumasi And the Battle That Raged
Body_Text:
There are so many factors and properties that make football the undisputable king of sports.
Apart from having launched a FIFA World Cup finals as far back as 1930, with several growth indices and crucibles over the decades and so much to talk about its administrators, players, referees, coaches and indeed other workers, football has become such a monster (in a manner of speaking, that is) that swallows everyone. In our dear country and indeed other countries, it is a terrific passion.
Football matches have a massive atmosphere and ambience about them, and more so when rivalry is involved. When Nigeria play Ghana, it can be likened to when Brazil play Argentina. You can also look at England vs Germany in Europe, or Japan vs Korea Republic in Asia.
In the first leg of the 10th All-Africa Games qualifying match between the National U-23 team, Dream Team, and the Black Meteors of Ghana, played at the Samuel Ogbemudia Stadium, Benin City on Saturday, 25th June, the Ghanaians scored first. Their approach to the match was war-like, with the mentality of the yam-pounder. Days before, there had been complaints of inadequate feeding and poor practice pitch by the Ghanaian delegation, which the NFF and the Edo State government promptly addressed.
However, the Ghanaian officials and players did not convey to the Ghanaian public the correction and adjustments that were made by the NFF and Edo State government, and the feeling of ill-treatment of the team persisted in the former Gold Coast.
It was in this context that the return leg match took place in Kumasi last Sunday.
On our part at the Nigeria Football Federation, we had no illusions as to what we had on hand and that informed the arrangements that were made. Our officials were on the GFA to inform us of the date, venue and time of the match but we did not have an idea until the mandatory 10 days before, when we were told the game would take place at the Baba Yara Sports Stadium, Kumasi on Sunday, 10th July.
Well informed of African football intrigues and schooled by the manner of the elimination of our National Women's team, Super Falcons by the Black Queens of Ghana from the All-Africa Women's Football Tournament (at the El-Wak Stadium, Accra when a last-minute penalty helped the Ghanaians to a 2-1 win), we planned for the second leg involving the men with every seriousness it deserved. We did not have so much money but when the GFA told us there was no way to fly our boys from Accra to Kumasi on Friday (two days before the match), we knew we had to charter an aircraft.
That was how we came about the Air Nigeria aircraft that flew the team to Kumasi direct. The rules say you cannot transport a visiting team by road once the distance is superior to 200 kilometres. The distance between Accra and Kumasi is 300 kilometres. But here, should the GFA continue to insist there was no way they could get an aircraft to fly the team, it would become a bother.
The NFF also sent an advance team of two officers, who contacted the Ghana Football Association and the Nigeria High Commission in Accra before setting off for Kumasi on Thursday, 7th July. The duo, alongside the Minister-Counsellor of the Nigeria High Commission, Mr. Andrew Idi, did not arrive in Kumasi until late in the night.

Original_Search_Title: Power Devolution Will Make for Technical Efficiency
Headline: Nigeria: Power Devolution Will Make for Technical Efficiency
Body_Text:
Mr. Olisa Agbakoba, SAN, is the former President of the Nigeria Bar Association. He has just sent a private bill to the National Assembly seeking a law to devolve critical economic and political power to the federating units.
Can you shed more light on what the bill for the devolution of power seeks to achieve, especially in view of the long-standing perception in some quarters that strengthening the federating units is like a shortcut to the disintegration of the country?
Let me start by saying that the bill for the devolution of power is not about breaking up Nigeria. We want Nigeria to be able to move forward. The bill suggests shifting a lot of comfort zones. It is simply a call for an administrative restructuring of the country for good governance. In the bill, I have proposed a subject-by-subject amendment of the constitution - the piece- meal approach I suggested while I was talking to the NBA. In the past, everybody viewed the process as an event - we were invited to Abuja - and it's opened, we were all kept in hotels, and after three months, we went away. But it didn't work. So if we see it as a process, then we can start to deal with the subject of amendment. The amendment being proposed in this bill is such that will put food on our tables, give us water, education, healthcare, good roads, electricity, etc. To do that, we need to make Nigeria a true federation through the devolution of power to the federating units in a way that makes them technically efficient.
Why do you think the states would better handle the issues you are advocating for devolution to them?
The federal government is very strong and powerful simply because it holds all the power in the Exclusive List of the constitution. There is the Concurrent List. On the Exclusive List, there are 68 items on which only the federal government can exercise power. On the Concurrent List, there are 30 items. But even on this Concurrent List, the state governments cannot exercise power on any item except with the permission of the federal government. This bill suggests that the way to go is to devolve power; to take those items on the Exclusive List that the federal government, obviously, does not need, and give to the states. Driver's licensing, for instance, I don't see the business of the federal government with the issuance of driver's licenses. Then we can also devolve power to the states in respect of all natural resources - except petroleum, because there would be too many forces against it. The federal government is not doing any-thing about mineral resources like gold, uranium, bauxite, coal, etc. You can allow the states that have these natural resources to exploit them. Nigeria has the third largest reservoir of sulphur-filled coal in the world. Yet, the federal government is not doing anything about this natural resource in the south-east. The kind of amendment being proposed is such that would make the federal government retain power that it would need to keep Nigeria one, like defence, foreign affairs, and other coercive powers, and devolve to the state's other powers and responsibilities that would enable them have technical efficiency. For me, things like education, healthcare, and water resources are issues for the states. Let us take the issue of the Ecological Fund, for example. This is a huge fund being administered by the federal government. I am from Anambra State, where erosion is eating up almost the entire state, and the man who is expected to fix this problem is in Abuja, hundreds of kilometres away from the problem. When he comes to see the problem, it is the state governor and other local officials that will take him to the erosion sites. This system has simply not worked. What this bill is saying is that he who wears the shoe knows where it pinches. The state governor, who knows these problems sites, should be given the responsibility of addressing them because he is better placed to do so.
What is the place of the geopolitical zones in the amendment you are proposing?
On the issue of the zones, rather than have six geopolitical zones for nothing, these six geopolitical zones can be recognised in the constitution, with power devolved to them so that issues common to each of the zones can be approached collectively with more efficiency. The south-west has already said they would like to have a regional economic commission. Now the governments of the zone can have common economic policies around a south-west development com- mission. This will make for cheaper and more efficient delivery of governance. Another issue is the courts. As a lawyer, I feel very sad that a case has to take about 15 years to get through the Court of Appeal and about 20 years to go through the Supreme Court. We need to have two systems of court. I don't know why the federal government should supervise the states in respect of local matters. It may be unwieldy for all 36 states to have 36 courts of appeal. But we can have regional courts of appeal. These courts are more accessible to the people and this can help to make governments more accountable. But when you have a Supreme Court in Abuja with little access to issues in the context of the zones, there is hardly any sense of urgency. With the devolution of power sought in this bill, we can have more accountability, stronger institutions, and, ultimately, more development. Corruption in our system today is accentuated by the fact that Abuja politicians are far away from the states and the people. This amendment will strengthen the states and make the centre less attractive. Politicians will now learn to play their politics in the context of the states. The reason everybody wants to be president is that we have a political pyramid and only one man can be president. The president is all powerful and he exercises enormous powers.
You talked about removal of petroleum from the list of items that can be devolved to the states. Don't you think this is unfair to the oil-producing states and may pose a big setback to the progress of your bill in the National Assembly?
The reason I singled out petroleum is that crude oil has played a unique role in our economy. So to immediately say petroleum should be part of the mineral resources that should go to the states would be a very difficult challenge. So like I said in my write-up, we have to recognise that we must devolve power to the states sensibly, tactically, strategically, and in phases. At the current level of our political and economic development and maturity, we can't devolve petroleum resources to the states; it will simply not work. But that does not stop us from devolving other resources. Let us go for the low-hanging fruits. I can assure you that if we say, devolve coal to where it occurs; nobody would make an issue out of it. If you say, devolve palm kernel, nobody would make an issue of it. Nigeria was once the largest exporter of palm kernel. If our palm kernel resources can be revived, it would surpass the N12 billion that the five south-east states receive today in monthly federal allocation. There are many resources that nobody is interested in now. For instance, yam in Abakiliki, cashew nuts, etc. In Greece, they don't have oil, they have tomatoes, potatoes, etc., and these are what run the country. The same with Turkey, they make the finest suits. Japan,of course, is another example. A lot of countries don't have these huge natural resources. The fiscal federalism debate should therefore recognise the need to give to the states what they produce, and keep petrol for now with the federal government.
What is your key impetus for this bill?
The key explanation is for Nigerians to realise we are very poor because we are politically disorganised. If you look at all the major international development charts, the human development index, and the like, we are at the bottom of everything. But in Nigeria, I see energy that I don't see in most African countries, apart from, say, South Africa and Egypt. Nigeria is big, but it is a big for nothing country. The reason it is a big for nothing country is that the economic model of development is geared towards consumption, not production. Governors sit down and consume, they don't think. They just get the monetary hand-out at the end of the month and they consume it. So it can't work. Whether my proposal is valid or not, we have to say to ourselves that the current model of running Nigeria has failed. For example, I don't see how anybody can run a newspaper by sitting in his office. The reason THISDAY comes out every day is that there is an energetic group of journalists who are committed to moving about and coming back together to get their stories published as editions of the newspaper. But in Nigeria, the governors spend their time traversing weddings, burials, seminars, and generally speaking, not doing things that are productive. They are not challenged to say, how I increase the internal revenue of my state, except, maybe, Governor Babatunde Fashola of Lagos State. That has to change. For that to change, would need the federal government to say, I'm not supporting you any- more.
How would you evaluate the call by states for increased revenue allocation?
I see the request by state governors for increased revenue as lack of clarity on their part. If I were President Goodluck Jonathan I would say, you will have the money, but you will get far more to do - new responsibilities. I think this my theory is infallible. Whether it would play out exactly like my bill predicts, is not my concern. But I want Nigerians to support the idea that we cannot run Nigeria from 36state capitals with governors who have practically no idea about how to generate revenue. Why did they go to campaign to be governors in the first place? Nobody asked them. I heard one governor saying the state has no money. If you can't run the state, you should resign. That's the challenge. Nigerians should tell them. That's the message I'm giving to Nigerians. If your governors cannot run the states, let them resign and let's look for people who can do it. Enough of this sitting in their capitals and sending their Commissioners of Finance to go and meet every month and pick up cheques, which they deposit in Abuja banks, and then the governors junket, doing nothing. That must stop. They must attend to the needs of the states, and the only way they can do so is for the federal government to keep them busy. If the federal government keeps them busy, they won't be going to parties, burials, and seminars. They will be sitting down to ask, how do we raise money, like I sit down in my office here and ask. I'm not bound to come to the office early in the morning, but I come because I have bills to pay. If I don't do so, nobody will do it for me. But if somebody gives me at the end of every month N60 million, I will be sleeping. This is economic sense. I would urge everybody to go and read el-Rufai's masterpiece on Friday (June 1) - for which they stupidly arrested him. The cost of governance is too high; enough is enough. We cannot use all our money to maintain one percent of Nigerians. If we must change that, the bill is my own contribution to how we can reduce the cost of governance. The challenge is for Nigerians to also join that debate.
Idea similar to what you are proposing had been floated by the civil society before - even though theirs was more of advocacy for a wholesale restructuring of the country - but they were not given any chance to progress through the law making processes of the National Assembly. Do you think anything has changed now or there is a new set of circumstances that is amenable to power devolution?
There are two things in the question. The first is that, hopefully, there is a critical mass of legislators in the National Assembly, represented by the opposition, who can push the bill. I have received calls from members of the National Assembly who said they are looking forward to this proposal. We hope also that within the PDP there are legislators who would see that this bill makes sense. So together, there can be a meaningful discussion. I see this as an entirely new opportunity for the National Assembly to take on the challenge so that they don't have this negative perception by Nigerians that they are just there to eat all our money. It's not good for them. I have a lot of friends there - for instance, the House of Representatives Speaker, Senate Majority Leader, who was my class- mate, and others - all fine gentlemen. But the unfortunate perception is that the National Assembly is not interested in us. So let them look at this bill constructively and tinker with it in whichever way they want. As I said, this is not final. If what this bill achieves is to get people thinking, then my work is done.
What should be the role of civil society in the effort to push the power devolution bill?
The second thing is that civil society has done extremely well in the last 15 years to keep the argument for a sovereign national conference going. But like I told my colleagues, sometimes you must be tactical. The truth is, given our lack of resources, we do not have the critical mass to sustain a people-led constitutional process. That is one challenge. The second are the obstacles that would confront us from those who don't want change. Don't underestimate how this would be discussed in the circles of those who don't want change. Anybody who wants to re- jig Nigeria is striking at the heart of how corruption plays out. So it's not going to be easy. What I have done - and hopefully civil society can also play a role - is to recognise that the National Assembly is the theatre of action, however imperfect. So take that space. And in taking that space, ours is to make sure that the National Assembly effects this power devolution in a way that makes Nigerians included in the discussion and gives Nigerians the final say. It may well be that we would need a referendum. But the National Assembly must be seen as the critical theatre of debate. When that debate takes place, the civil society's function is to press for an inclusive process. Civil society has produced at least two beautiful con- situations - one by the Pro-National Conference Organisation and the other by The Patriots. These are documents that can form part of the debate in the National Assembly.

Original_Search_Title: GSM, Ingredient of a Free Society
Headline: Nigeria: GSM, Ingredient of a Free Society
Body_Text:
Unknown to many Nigerians, especially the adolescents, the freedom they now enjoy, courtesy of the liberalization of the telecommunications sector, did not come easy.
