While some paid with their lives, some incurred economic misfortunes to get to this point, which to some Nigerians actually began with the annulment of the 1993 general presidential election, hitherto adjudged as the freest and fairest elections, before 2011 elections in over 50 years of nationhood of the Nigerian people.
Today, it is easier for a stranded car-owner at the other side of Lagos to call his or her mechanic to come to the rescue, then follow-up with the mechanic until he or she gets to the spot where the attention is required.
Also, today, it is no longer news that several people's lives have been saved since the coming of mobile communications in Nigeria in 2001. A case in point that quickly comes to mind was that of a widow, Mrs. Elebele Ihuoma who suddenly took ill at about 11pm, penultimate Friday. Her confused eldest daughter returned from work and saw her mother laying motionless, but breathing.
All she could remember was to buzz her mum's closest friend around their vicinity, Mrs. 'Tonia Domson, who resides within the same Oshodi suburb of Lagos and within a distance of about 800 meters away. It follows that Mrs. Domson swiftly drove down to Mrs. Elebele's house in the family car and put up a call to her family doctor before rushing her friend to the doctor's nearby clinic to ensure prompt attention; where the medical doctor on-call informed them after due examination that Mrs. Elebele had suffered a stroke. Now, to the glory of God she has fully recovered and the rest is better imagined than experienced if that call was not made by the daughter.
As a result of the liberalisation in Nigeria, citizens now air their views unbarred on how they are governed and should be governed on prints via SMS to the editor, call-in during television and radio programmes to express themselves. Just as it could not be forgotten in a hurry that recently President Goodluck Jonathan reversed his ban on Nigeria national football team from foreign matches, which he alluded to interactions he had with Nigerians through the media and mostly on his Facebook account.
Today, to save time, customers now call hair salon operators to know if they have chance to attend to them or should book for another date instead of getting to the salon and be told that the Barber or stylist has just left to no particular location.
Before now, many Nigerians residing in the urban areas rarely go home except for festivals such as Christmas, Easter, Sallah and New Yam. Nowadays, it has become literally a 'sin' not to get in touch with the family, especially where one's parents are still living, customarily at the rural areas; if one is in the city for one or two weeks.
All these have changed with the coming of GSM for local parlance and liberalisation of the telecommunications market for the experts and investors. In fact, most Nigerians think that GSM or the availability of mobile telephony is all about giving dividends as real freedom to the populace of Nigeria, following the return of democracy in 1999. Critically examining this, one would discover that it's not far from the truth. Nigerians have not had it so good in over 40 years and now they could call and talk as long as they want to anyone, anywhere via their mobile handsets.
Thus, encouraging one to ask what is liberalisation and democracy as well as how both could affect the level of freedom enjoyed in a given society like Nigeria.
Online encyclopedia, the wikipedia.org, described democracy as a form of government in which all citizens have an equal say in the decisions that affect their lives. Ideally, this includes equal and more or less direct participation in the proposal, development and passage of legislation into law. This definition encompasses social, economic and cultural conditions that allow the free and equal practice of political self-determination.
While there is no specific universally accepted definition of 'democracy', equality and freedom have both been identified as important characteristics.
And for 'liberalisation' it was described in general term as referring to a relaxation of previous government restrictions, usually in areas of social or economic policy. Therefore giving more powers to the people.
Most often, the term is used to refer to economic liberalization, especially trade liberalization or capital market liberalization.
However, in slight comparison between the two; liberalisation and democracy - A school of thought had pointed out recently that there is a distinct difference between liberalization and democratization, which are often thought to be the same concept.
So, liberalization, they argued could take place without democratization, and deals with a combination of policy and social change specialized to a certain issue such as the liberalization of government-held property for private purchase. Whereas yet another school of thought professed democratization as being more politically specialized that could arise from a liberalization, but works in a broader level of government.
There is no gain saying that mobile phone usage and adaptation in Nigeria have become a ritual to an extent that one industry observer once described GSM as General Street Madness (GSM), due to the fact that people who own GSM phones are often seen talking and laughing on the street all alone.
To some it has become status symbol as disclosed by Yemisi, a system administrator recently that the 'unfortunate trend' is that even her office cleaner's mobile phone, of course, a Blackberry and is costlier than her phone, simply because one has to literally belong to the rave of the moment.
For some people it has been about using technology to find their old friends and like the group chief executive officer, Teledom Group, Dr. Emmanuel Ekuwem, once said that telecommunications is like a water tap, which when opened would discharge water based on the kind of pipe - big or small - used to lay it. This means that the more the telecommunications industry is opened up, the more freedom it enables people to communicate. Ekuwem also noted that Communications in this sense could come through voice call, data; internet, World Wide Web (www), knowledge-base among other useful means it could be put to.
Therefore, it could be apt to state that democracy has brought Nigerians the necessary freedom they desire as a society, mostly since the advent of GSM in the country.
Like another industry observer argued, Nigeria gained true independence on May 29, 1999; that is, the day Military through former head of state, General Abdulsalami Abubakar, retired, handed over government to a democratically elected government led by former General Olusegun Obasanjo; who invariably capitalised on the freedom of democracy to liberalise the telecommunications sector.
Now, with over 90 million Nigerians talking, the freedom embedded in Information and Communications Technologies (ICT) and capsulated in GSM manifested during the just concluded 2011 general elections; as results were exchanged from various monitoring groups, data pulling became an alias for Nigerian youths and youths at heart and use of social media like Facebook and Twitter to name a few, through mobile phones.
It has now dawn on many Nigerians that democracy pays, after all, they are enjoying the freedom spurred by GSM technology, at least, chatting these days with one's parents and family from various parts of the world has permeated the Nigerian society to what a rural woman once described as 'oyibo gossip' - English gossip, for sake of using mobile phones at ease, to chat with family members in faraway lands like China, India and Europe.
Talking is known to engender a free mind and free mind begets free society which paves the way for dialogue and investment in addition to flourishing of businesses, especially in the small, medium enterprise (SMEs). At least, at the last count, Nigeria had over 50,000 commercial call centre operators, tagged 'umbrella people,' with Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) in billions of Dollars and employment created for Nigerians, thereby collapsing the 'Berlin walls' of doing business in Nigeria to a greater extent.
For Mr. Titi Omo-Ettu, president of the Association of Telecommunications Companies of Nigeria (ATCON), despite the over 90m subscriber base currently recorded, there is still room for over 50 million potential telecom consumers in Nigeria. Pointing out that in the next decade, 50 million telecommunications users are expected to be connected on broadband internet access.
He also noted that the emphasis hitherto placed on 'number of connected lines in Nigeria will be shifted to emphasis to 'number of users' of telecommunications services to reflect the true development requirement of empowering Nigerians with telecommunications.
Although there are still lots of infrastructure to be fixed, such as electricity power supply, good road network and security, Nigerian state deserves a path on the back and Nigerians for evolution into the rapidly emerging 'free' society accompanied with several reforms at all fronts. In fact, it has made the investment climate second to none on the continent of Africa and earned Nigeria the fastest growing telecom market in Africa, and second to China, globally.
Apart from the fact that talking in a given society eases tensions, it also aligns to one of the GSM operators marketing catchphrase ... 'Now Nigerians are talking' in other words things are looking up.
Who says that GSM telephony is not a democracy dividend!

Original_Search_Title: Justice in Atta Mills' Ghana Wears the Colours of NDC
Headline: Ghana: Justice in Atta Mills' Ghana Wears the Colours of NDC
Body_Text:
Accra — If you are a sympathiser of the National Democratic Congress (NDC), you can get away with murder. And that is official. In a nation headed by a so-called man of God, justice is so skewed that if you are seeking protection under the umbrella, you are immune from prosecution.
Immediately Prof. John Evans Atta Mills was proclaimed winner of the 2008 presidential election, three human beings were butchered to death at the Agbobloshie Market in Accra.
All the four victims were identified as sympathisers of the opposition New Patriotic Party (NPP). Their assailants were identified colleagues at the Agbobloshie Yam Market. What made the perpetrators special is their links with the ruling NDC.
Since January 2009, when the victims were reported dead, in man's inhumanity to man, the clamour for justice has fallen on deaf ears. The irony of the kind of just society being constructed in this land of our birth is that the President of the Republic does not lose any opportunity to hammer home the fact that his fumbling 'Better Ghana' agenda hinges on justice for all.
At the Sunyani Coronation Park, where he won the right to raise the flag of the umbrella into the 2012 presidential election, he welcomed his victory with a pledge to bring justice to all aggrieved Ghanaians.
At the time he was making this pledge, he might have known that his Attorney-General and Minister of Justice, Martin Amidu, had gone before Parliament and admitted that Anita De Sosoo, National Women's Organiser of the party, had her four wheel-drive vehicle plough through human beings at Abomosu in the Eastern Region during the Atiwa bye-election.
The Minister of Justice also stated that nine Ghanaians were maimed in the incident. But, the NDC Women's Organiser has no case to answer. It was legitimate, in the opinion of the Attorney-General, that the Women's Organiser left nine Ghanaians seriously injured.
All the nine victims were identified as sympathisers of the opposition New Patriotic Party. Under President John Evans Atta Mills Ghana, it is perfectly legitimate, in the opinion of the Government's chief legal brain, to crush human beings to the pulp, provided you are member of the ruling NDC.
Read the lips of the Attorney-General as captured by a Chronicle reporter in the House: "After a personal study of the docket, I have come to the conclusion that the evidence on the docket cannot cogently support the offences of dangerous driving and negligently causing harm, contrary to section 1 (1) of the road traffic Act. 2004."
According to the Minister, after studying the docket, it appears that it was in the process of escaping the irate vigilante crowd advancing on the vehicle, and the apparent danger to the lives of its occupants, that a number of people in the crowd were accidentally knocked down with some sustaining serious injuries.
Mr.Martin Amidu, one of the longest serving officers of the so-called revolution launched by Jerry John Rawlings told the House that it was clear from the docket that the mob, crowd or vigilante, created a very dangerous situation after stopping the vehicle that could not have been foreseen by the suspected driver.
One interesting thing about the De SoSoo affair is that neither she nor the driver reported the incident at any police station. One would have thought that after such an incident, any public officer worth her salt would have reported the matter at the nearest police station.
In this case, even when an official complaint had been lodged with the police, the NDC Women's Organiser would not assist our peace officers to unravel the mystery.
For me, as a Ghanaian, and flowing from the behaviour of the criminal justice system under the NDC, the party's Women's Organiser has been left off the hook. The nine Ghanaians who were 'seriously injured,' in the words of the Attorney-General himself, cannot access justice, simply because the perpetrator happens to be a top shot in the NDC.
On Saturday, when I heard President Atta Mills talk of the justice system and his government's determination to deal with all offenders, I nearly kicked the TV set away. I was annoyed by the attempt to create the impression that we have a government that cares for the Ghanaian.
Since the Professor moved to the Castle, the justice system has been so polluted that the national motto, Freedom and Justice, has lost its meaning. The other day, 20 little girls selling ice water on the streets in the national capital were arrested and summoned before the Accra Metropolitan Assembly Court in Accra.
Each of the kids was fined GH Â¢600 (six million cedis) or three years in jail. According to eyewitnesses, it took a few minutes to pronounce the guilty verdicts on these poor souls. They had no counsel.
Children selling ice water on the streets, certainly, cannot come from the most affluent of homes, so coming by GHc Â¢600 was always going to be challenging. None of the kids could afford the fine on the spot. All 20 girls were shipped to the Nsawam Medium Security Prisons.
I am told that their parents and guardians had to resort to borrowing of money before some could be released. Compare the trauma these poor little girls have gone through to the experience of the Member of Parliament for Asawaase, Mohammed Muntaka Mubarak, who took a woman, (certainly not his wife) on an all expenses paid by the state to Germany and Cote d'Ivoire.
When it came to the crunch, the President described it as an indiscretion on the part of the former minister of sports. What annoyed me about the verdict of the Commission on Human Rights and Administrative Justice (CHRAJ) is the assertion that the woman was not his girlfriend. How did CHRAJ arrive at that bunkum of a decision?
How did the state gain by the presence of the woman in Cote d'Ivoire and Germany?
I am beginning to nurse the feeling that the concept of the People's Court and the Public Tribunal system operated by some of the agents of the Provisional National Defence Council (PNDC) during the so-called revolution is gradually creeping back. In those days, the formal court system was an anathema to the revolutionaries. The system was not well-disposed towards the formal court system.
To the revolutionaries, the formal court system was using technicalities to get big fishes out of the net. That is why the People's Court and Tribunals came to dominate. Naturally, the revolutionary concept of justice did not go down well with judges.
Men and women with legal brains would tell you that the abduction and murder of the three high court judges had its genesis in the hatred for the justice system, and how it ground slowly.
That is why some of us are still worried about the threats to the Chief Justice and her judges recently. When the party chairman said if the judges do not clean their act, "we shall do it for them," what did he mean?
One of my disappointments with President John Evans Atta Mills, as head of state of this Republic, is his inability to call the party chairman to order when he made the assertions above. When Dr. Kwabena Adjei screamed:
"There are several ways of killing a cat," that threat should have been treated seriously. As usual, our 'Father for all' chose to keep quiet.
Prof. J.E.A. Mills might have won by a landslide in his desire to lead the NDC into the 2012 presidential election. He has so far failed to convince many Ghanaians that in his so-called 'Better Ghana Agenda,' some are not more equal than others.

Original_Search_Title: Govt to Sack Corrupt Officials
Headline: Nigeria: Govt to Sack Corrupt Officials
Body_Text:
Enugu — Governor Sullivan Chime of Enugu State has threatened to sack members of his cabinet who engage in corruption, saying that his new administration would not tolerate any form of graft or laxity in office.
Speaking on Friday after swearing in 25 commissioners and 12 special advisers, Chime declared that he would not have wished for a better team of highly qualified individuals to work with in his new administration. Chime said even though much had been accomplished in his first term, a lot more needed to be done in the new dispensation, adding that the nature of the task ahead required all the diligence and dedication that could be mustered to accomplish it. Describing his first term as a period of learning, Chime further stressed that the business of government would be attended to with much more seriousness than in his first term and warned that any of the new officials who was found wanting in his duty would be shown the way out.
He said, "I want to assure you that in this term; we will be more serious than we were in the first term. We took time to look at your credentials and I can say that no person can wish for a better team to work with.
"So it is obvious we mean business this time around, this is no time for politics. I declare zero tolerance for corruption and for lackadaisical attitude to work. Everybody must sit up and if we find that you are not up and doing, then you may leave us."
He further urged the appointees to ensure that they declared their assets before resuming their duties disclosing that they will be attending a retreat this week to help prepare them for the onerous task ahead.
Responding on behalf of his colleagues, the new Attorney General and Commissioner for Justice, Mr. Anthony Ani, thanked the governor for giving them the opportunity to serve the State and assured him that they would meet his expectations of them.
Ndigbo in Lagos and Ohanaeze, the umbrella body of Igbo speaking groups, will at New Africa College, Mowo, Badagry council, Lagos State, on Saturday August 6, 2011 celebrate their new yam festival. The theme of this year's event, according to Eze Ndigbo, Badagry, Eze Michael Uchegbu, is Suo asusu Igbo ebe obula i no; kuzie asusu Igbo ebe obula I no; kwalite omen a ala Igbo ebe Obula i no, which means: "Speak Igbo language where ever you are; teach Igbo language where ever you are; promote Igbo culture and identity where ever you are." The monarch also said Igbo socio cultural organisations and cultural troupes in Lagos will entertain at the event, where he will present his new book titled, "Igbo language - Book 5."

Original_Search_Title: Okorocha's Victory Not Failure of Imo PDP - Nwachukwu
Headline: Nigeria: Okorocha's Victory Not Failure of Imo PDP - Nwachukwu
Body_Text:
Austin Nwachukwu was the representative of Ehime Mbano/Ihitte Uboma/Obowo federal constituency of Imo State in the sixth National Assembly. He was among the 11 members of the house of representatives that were suspended for accusing the leadership of financial misappropriation. In this interview, he says Governor Rochas Okorocha is a child of necessity whose victory did not mean a failure of the PDP in Imo State. Excerpts:
In spite of you effort, you couldn't return to the House through the last elections in April. Has that got anything to do with the purported problem between you and former governor, Ikedi Ohakim?
Rumours will always be there, but let me put it straight. I was not at logger head with my former governor, Chief Ikedi Ohakim, as far as I know. But I know for a fact that he probably has seen my rising profile and felt threatened, that this man will go places, and therefore decided to scuttle my desire of being re-elected to represent the people.
But is it true that you actually planned to contest the governorship seat with him?
It is only God that gives power, you can speculate, you can think and plan but before you were born, the bible says what you will be is known to God. I am not here to say that I want to be governor of Imo State, but if destiny and the people of Imo State see me as somebody who could govern them, why not? I am competent; I am qualified to be governor.
But don't you think your ambition hit the rocks because you were not in the right track with the godfathers who decide the political calculations?
My ambition has not hit the rocks because as we speak now, I am in court over the outcome of the primaries which I won overwhelmingly. The people of Okigwe voted for me overwhelmingly, and those people who feel that power lies in their hands thought that they would scuttle the wishes and aspirations of the people. That is why I am in the federal high court challenging the outcome of the kangaroo setting that saw the wrong candidate being put in the House of Reps to represent Okigwe federal constituency.
There were reports that you were asked to step down for somebody, how correct is that?
Like I said earlier, an incumbent member should have the first go when it comes to position in the National Assembly, more so, I had just finished one term. Democracy will suffer if every time you keep turning out fresh people to make laws for the governance of the nation. I see that as a waste of tax payers' money, especially when those who are known to have done very well are denied the opportunity of returning. I know I had done very well.
If you look at my contribution on the floor of the House and what I attracted to my people, you will find out that nobody wouldn't have wanted me to go back, except God didn't want it. Where I come from in Okigwe South, nobody ever had any agreement to rotate the seat. If you are popular, effective and qualified, you will be asked to represent the constituency. And I must beat my chest to say that, since 1999, I am the only one from this constituency who has attracted infrastructural development.
So what in actual sense is your relationship with the former governor?
Ohakim ran elections under the PPA. I am a strong stalwart of the PDP, having won elections into the house at the same time that he won his election. When his election was being challenge by Ararume who was then the flag bearer of the PDP, I supported Ararume because he was the flag bearer of my party. But when Ararume lost in the court, I moved ahead to support Ohakim because he was governor of the state and he eventually defected to meet us in the PDP.
I want to put it clearly now that I am the only National Assembly member from Okigwe zone, where Ohakim comes from, that stood by him. I encourage him, I was a good party loyalist, but to my surprise, it was rumoured that he did not want me back at the National Assembly; reason being that my profile was rising. However, as a charismatic politician, I don't see anybody stopping me, only God can stop me. This was proved when I came out to campaign and PDP won in that place overwhelmingly.If I did not come out to campaign, PDP would have lost that zone. So how would a grassroots politician like me step down for a neophyte, who just came from the blues?
When you saw the handwriting on the wall, did you at anytime try to reach out to Ohakim and other aggrieved persons?
There was nothing to be reconciled because there was no issue on ground. You don't give what you don't have prior to the primaries. There was no skirmish, no quarrel or issues between myself and the governor. I was only told on the day of the primaries that the governor did not want me to come back. But I felt that if the governor didn't want me, he should have told me so that I don't waste my resources, energy and time campaigning.
I was only told this on the day of the primaries and until today the governor himself has not told me that he did not want me to be re-elected. I do not want to join issues with him because today, the governor that did not want me turned out not wanted by the people.
It is like the story of the green vegetable that the old woman was planning to use in coking her yam porridge to nourish her body; meanwhile the green vegetable was also planning to purge the old woman. So if you say you are pointing one finger at somebody, the remaining fingers are pointing at you. We must learn to obey the voice of the people because the voice of the people is the voice of God.
But what happened that you members of the PDP could not deliver Imo State. How did you fall to APGA?
I will say again that the voice of the people is the voice of God. The people of Imo State felt that there should be a change. There were so many issues that led to PDP's loss in Imo State. The current governor, Chief Okorocha, came as a child of necessity and when he came, the people rejoiced; they didn't like the last part of the previous administration. In fact, there were many stories that bedevilled that government; so the people demonstrated what they wanted in the polls by voting out the sitting governor.
Does that not indicate that all of you in the PDP failed?
The people voted for individuals not for party. PDP won so many seats in the House of Reps, state assembly and so on. What they did was a demonstration of their right to choose who will govern them. I think Nigeria is drifting towards the era of voting for the individual and not the party. It is like a good cook cooking a bad food; nobody will eat the food. Proper elections were conducted in Imo State; the people came out and exercised their franchise; they were not chastised by anybody. So, it was not the PDP that lost, but an individual who lost the support of his people.
There have been spate of bombings in some parts of the country including Abuja. Most Nigerians see it as failure on the part of the leadership, what is your take on that?
It is a new development in Nigeria. They are happening in Afghanistan, Yemen, Pakistan and other countries but it is new to us, and that is why we see it as something strange.
I won't say that it is failure on the part of government; it only poses a challenge. I believe that the president will tackle the situation.
The country is not under siege; the government will handle it the way it should be handled. It is most regrettable that the last one happened at the police headquarters.

Original_Search_Title: Preparation for New Yam Festival Heightens
Headline: Nigeria: Preparation for New Yam Festival Heightens
Body_Text:
Lagos — Preparations for 2011 edition of new yam festival in Igbo ukwu, Aguata Local Government Area of Anambra state have reached fever pitch.
As a mark of this development, Idu II of Igbo Ukwu, Igwe Dr. Martin Ezeh recently led a delegation of traditional rulers from Anambra state to NTDC headquarters, Abuja to solicit support of the apex tourism regulatory agency.
In his address, Igwe Martin lauded the NTDC boss for what he described as revolutionalization of the tourism sector in the country.
"Your revolution in the tourism industry cannot be over emphasized in time. We know what you are to this New Yam Festival and it cannot be overestimated," Igwe martin said.
"Therefore, we humbly wish to inform you that this year's National New Yam Festival Iwa Ji Ndi Igbo 2011 will take place from 23rd -27th August, 2011 at the National Yam House, IgboUkwu, Aguata LGA of Anambra AState."
Stating further, Igwe Martin disclosed that 2011 edition of National New Yam festival will feature a colloquium on August 23rd at Awka the state capital, 24th -movement of carnival train from Awka to IgboUkwu with road show that ends at National Museum Igboukwu.
The festival will also mark the opening of National Art Exhibition by National Gallery of art on August 24,with musical performance by Perrykoma.
Other activities lined up for 26th are, cultural dance/ Masquerade display by the 21 Local Governments of the State. Best Wine Taper, Best Artist, Best Dancing troupe and Best performing masquerade competitions with all night musical performance by Osadebe Jnr.
Grand finale is slated for August 27, and will feature Iwa Ji, Awards, wrestling, biggest yam competition, cultural dance display by the five eastern states with Delta, Bayelsa and Rivers State.
In preparation for the festival, Runsewe promised to provide the community with promotional materials like T- shirts, caps, brochures, gift items and NTDC branded leather works while the national Yam House located in igbo ukwu will be refurbished.
The DG also promised to provide a virtual tour for the Owerre-Ezukala Cave stating that it was a natural monument which people, including foreigners should be able to visit via the computer if they cannot visit physically.
Reaffirming his special relationship with the Idu, the NTDC helmsman eulogized the Igbo leader referring to him as a man who is very passionate about the development of his people.

Original_Search_Title: How Onitsha Can Help Boost Governor Peter Obi's Image
Headline: Nigeria: How Onitsha Can Help Boost Governor Peter Obi's Image
Body_Text:
I believe that Governor Peter Obi of Anambra State has done more than Dr Chris Ngige did when he was governor between 2003 and 2006. But the perception among many people of Anambra State is that Ngige is an action man while Obi is a talker.
There are many reasons for this. One is that Obi is more of an idealist, while Ngige is more of a pragmatist. Another is that Ngige is more of a showman, while Obi is more of a conservative in his ways. Furthermore, Ngige is more of a sprinter, while Obi is more of a long-distance runner. Ngige gives the people what they can see and relate with immediately, while Obi gives them what will be useful to them in the long run.
Obi's strategy is not bad, but the problem is that in a land where the people have been denied the essential and basic things of life, patience is usually a scarce commodity. And in governance, the most popular leaders are those who not only do those things that will serve the people best but also those who are good at massaging the ego of the people by doing those things that will make them shine.
In terms of location, Anambra State is the most strategic of the five Igbo states. It can even be said that it is more strategically located than all the states of the former Eastern Region. The reason is that most Nigerians travelling from the western and northern parts of Nigeria to other parts of the old Eastern Region pass through Anambra State. It is true that there is another entrance into the South-east through Enugu State as well as another entrance into the old Eastern Region through Delta State, yet the entrance through Anambra State attracts more traffic. And Onitsha is the gateway.
One landmark that truly depicts the South-East is the Niger Bridge in Onitsha. For most Igbos, once that nest-shaped bridge is sighted from Asaba, it evokes a feeling of "home at last." Once an Igbo person crosses that bridge into Onitsha, if his vehicle develops a fault, he does not feel as vulnerable as if it were to be before the bridge. So there is a feeling Igbos attach to Onitsha even though it has never been an official capital of the Eastern Region or the short-lived Republic of Biafra.
Given this status as the gateway to the old Eastern Region, it presents an ample opportunity for a governor to use it to shine as a performer. When we talk about Onitsha, we are not even talking of the entire city of Onitsha. We are talking of an area of about five kilometers from the Niger Bridge to Limca Roundabout and from the Upper Iweka bridge to somewhere after the People's Club headquarters on the Owerri Road.
It was sad seeing the Upper Iweka loop overgrown with weeds. Governor Babatunde Fashola has used such loops as gardens of well-mown grass and well-trimmed flowers in the last four years. The sad situation is that when one steps into Onitsha, a feeling of chaos greets such a person. It is good that the sellers of goats, chickens, yams and the like that used to litter the entrance into Onitsha had been relocated.
But if Governor Peter Obi can beautify the two sides of these two mentioned roads with flowers, grass and painted stones, he will not just be worming his way into the hearts of Anambra people, he will also be selling himself to other Igbos and former Easterners as a man to look out for in Nigerian politics. Obi needs to start doing things that will make the people spread the good news about him rather than paying for media space on TV or newspaper to list such achievements. Word of mouth is stronger than advertising in marketing. And anyone who knows how to exploit it has everything to gain.

Original_Search_Title: When Voters Are Apathetic
Headline: Nigeria: When Voters Are Apathetic
Body_Text:
HAVE you ever been embarrassed beyond measure? Don't ever pray for it. That is exactly what happens when you invite say 500 people to a party and only about 50 reluctantly turn up.
Our lesson for today is drawn from the experience of one of our friends, Araka, in the late 1960s, just before the end of the Nigerian civil war. It was in Lagos before the advent of the Pentecostal movement and Nigerians were not seriously born again, the modern way. The Towns' Unions and their youth accompaniment were in vogue then.
Araka had a lot of money. He was in the 'Pay and Roll', where they paid some and rolled some. Such a man thought he had no reason to begin to associate with less significant mortals of the towns unions. After some five years of marriage, a baby arrived for the Arakas and it was time to celebrate. Our friend slaughtered a big cow and took a big band. On that fateful day, the party was scheduled for 1 p.m. But at 4 p.m. virtually all the seats were still empty but the band was already playing. The street children had started assuming the seats on the high table.
Our friend became visibly worried. "Joe, where are the people, the town's union people and the youths?" he kept asking me in a tone that seemed to suggest that I was keeping them. I went on my Vesper machine and flew round, trying to appeal to the people to come and chop but it was already too late.
The people had made up their minds that if hunger was on assignment to kill the poor, then, hunger must learn to meet the poor in his house. That was how Araka had the best party that never was. After that day, our friend became a self-appointed agent of Saint Luke who, in Acts of the Apostles (chapter 20 verse 35), opined that: "It is more blessed to give than to receive".
We have gone at this great length to illustrate what Nigerians are
doing to the Independent National Electoral Commission, INEC, in a
way, the modern day Araka. We gave INEC the serious assignment of organising some elections for us. Preparatory to this, INEC registered some 70 million people. The embarrassment for all of us, particularly the INEC, is that the Commission proceeded to the market, bought all the fat cows, procured all the available bags of rice, the yams, the oil and the necessary condiments and prepared dinner for 70 million people but alas, only 25 million came for the dinner.
This portrays INEC in bad light: as a tax eating parasite, overpaid and underproductive. We are quick at imagining that the billions of Naira allocated to INEC has flown into the individual pockets of the officials; whereas a good part of the money went into preparing the dinner for 70 million people. The cure for hangover, they say, is more drinking. In the same vein, the cure for the colossal waste now staring us on the face is more waste. INEC must now spend more money to dispose off the debris of the dinner that would have been eaten by the 45 million people who were absent. That's not all. We have now sent INEC running from pillar to post, trying to find out why the 45 million people were absent from the dinner. They are now holding all shades of seminars and workshops, resulting in wastes and more wastes. INEC must take heart because things must get worse before they get better. In just the same way that God uses the most foolish things to confound the wise, it is taking the very negative aspects of these elections to indicate that we are beginning to arrive at the desired results. The avalanche of invalid votes and the perceived voter apathy, although undesirable, are the most visible indicators that we are beginning to get real votes as against massive votes of the massive rigging era. In that era, records could easily show 90 percent voter turnout because all available ballot papers were simply thumb-printed and dumped into the ballot boxes. The propensity to rig bears a direct correlation with voter turnout - the more rigging there is, the higher the voter turnout figure.
Again, what we are seeing as voter apathy could also indicate that there is something wrong with our voters' registration. We must quickly go back to the books and find out whether there are still cases of the registration of the Mike Tysons and others based on newspaper photographs because under the free and fair elections, which we are gradually approaching, those ones are not going to show up in any polling station. In a general election, though, 100 percent voter turnout is impossible anywhere in the world. If we were to conclude registration in the evening of one day and go for an election the following morning, 100 percent turnout would still be unattainable.
This underscores the need for regular review of the voters' register. Nigerians must be told that despite the imperfections of our systems, elections still remain a critical part of the democratic process; and the existence of free elections provides a major difference between democracies and totalitarian or authoritarian regimes. Refusing to vote is voting against yourself and each time this happens, you shall have lost the moral right to complain about whatever form of government you get. Herein lies the daunting task before the political parties and their candidates who are, after all, the principal beneficiaries of informed voting.

Original_Search_Title: Essumeja Youth Halt Celebration of Akwasidae
Headline: Ghana: Essumeja Youth Halt Celebration of Akwasidae
Body_Text:
Kumasi — The youth of Essumeja in the Bekwai Municipality have restrained the Aduana Stool elders from observing the Akwasidae festival, or face their wrath.
They said until the stool elders prove to be proactive, they (youth) would not countenance what they say are unnecessary practices, and that the youth and community would not gain anything from such traditional practices.
The youth, led by one Osei Hyiaman, have communicated their threat, supposed to put the fear of God in the elders, through the Registrar of the Essumeja Traditional Council.
The Gyasehene, Nana Twum Barima Appau, and the Sanaahene, Nana Owusu Koko, have confirmed that the contents of the letter had been read out to the elders at a Traditional Council meeting.
The Chronicle has gathered that the Krontihene and acting President of the Traditional Council, Nana Ntim Banahene, had lodged a formal complaint with the local police, in case the youth carry out their threat.
Left with no option, the elders have obliged, as a result of which they have not been able to observe the Akwasidae since April 17, 2011.
The youth have accused the elders of failure on their part to ensure that a chief is nominated and installed seven years after the death of the chief, thus stifling the development of the town.
According to them, the elders seem not to be bothered about the near stalemate of the Essumeja Stool Affair, which is pending before the Asanteman Council.
For some time now, the Essumeja Youth Association has been mounting pressure on the Queenmother and kingmakers of the Traditional area to ensure that a chief is installed without delay.
Nana Adwoa Obro Pokuaa Nyankronpon is currently contesting her eligibility as Queenmother of Essumeja before the Asanteman Council, after her role was challenged by another royal, Madam Adwoa Aboraa, for which a five-member Judicial Committee, headed by the Achirensuahene, Agyewodin Adu Gyamfi Ampem, was appointed in May 2008.
The committee, on March 16, 2010, established in its report that Nana Adwoa Pokuaa Obro Nyankropon was not a royal, and that she was imposed on the people some 31 years ago.
But, before the Asanteman Council could endorse the decision of the committee, which was unanimous and premised on the evidence regarding the respective family trees of the parties involved, the Queenmother raised an objection to the findings of the committee, whose membership comprises Nana Owusu Ankoma, Seikwahene; Kyeame Boakye Yam, Nana Sabronum and Nana Adu Baa Ansah, Yamfohene, insisting that she is a royal of the stool.
Following that, the Asanteman Council agreed to re-examine evidence, including video recordings of proceedings in three days (March 29 to 31, 2010) against the committee's report.
When the Council met on April 1, 2010 to pronounce verdict, 16 chiefs, including the Manso-Nkwantahene, Bechemhene, Asokwa Mamponghene, Asafo Akwamu, Offinsohene, Tafohene, Mabanhene, Ejisuhene, Bekwaihene, Kokofuhene, Oyoko Bremanhene, Kwamanghene and the Adontenhene, overturned the committee's report by siding with the claims of the Queenmother that she was eligible to the stool.
The Hiahene and the paramount chiefs of Banda, Adankrangya, Nsuta, Ankobiahene, Adumhene, Atipinhene and the Nkosuohene were in support of the stand of the committee.
April 8, 2010 was then fixed for final determination, but the Queenmother failed to turn up, claiming she was indisposed, according to a medical report presented by a special aide, one Akwasi Dwomoh, who has since told the Council that it was a lie.
When the Asanteman Council resumed sitting on Essumeja over three days, (November 15-17, 2010), Nana Nyankropon was literally given a technical knockout, when she fumbled in arguing her case before the august council, headed by Otumfuo Osei Tutu II, the Asantehene.
The challenger, Madam Adwoa Aboraa, presented her family tree to convince the Council that the late Odeneho Oduro Numapau hailed from Tikrom, and therefore was not of Essumeja royal blood, therefore, his installation of the Queenmother was absolutely improper.
Testifying through her spokesperson, Kwabena Osei Bonsu, Madam Aboraa said of the 16 chiefs who ascended the Essumeja Stool, only three were not from the Essumeja Sefwi Aduana royal household.
She said because the late Oduro Numapau was not from the royal house, and that the elders invoked the Ntamkesie (great oath) against him at Manhyia, but luckily for him, Otumfuo Opoku Ware II, told the elders that he had absolved the Ntamkesie, and that they should allow him on to the stool.
She said Oduro Numapau never saw eye to eye with the then Queenmother, Nana Frema II, and even used force to take the royal regalia in her possession, which were later retrieved by an order of a court through an executive instrument.
It was narrated that it was on his death that Oduro Numapau installed Obro Nyankropon as the Queenmother, even though she was not a royal.
Madam Aboraa said: "We have waited all this while to regain our rightful possession, hence the Ntamkesie against the Queenmother."
The Queenmother was, however, saved by the bell, as the Kokofuhene, Barima Kwasi Offe Okogyiaseuo went to her rescue.
Looking confused and completely dazed by the weight of evidence against her, the Kokofuhene, her neighbour, had no option than to step in to save the Queenmother of Essumeja from the onslaught of her challenger.
Following the Kokofuhene's intervention, the case was adjourned for a week, to enable the Kokofuhene advice the Queenmother.
But, the much-anticipated resolution in December 2010 could not come on, as the Queenmother rejected the mediation efforts by Barima Offe Kwasi Okogyeasuo.
She told Otumfuo that she wished to give her testimony and contest the claim that she was not a legitimate Queenmother, to which Otumfuo obliged.
When the Asanteman Council met on February 17, 2011, the Queenmother sent in an excuse-duty from a Bekwai hospital to the effect that she needed a one-month rest.
Otumfuo Osei Tutu II had no other alternative, but to grant her excuse.
The determination of the Essumeja case, which has been in a stalemate since February this year, would set the stage for the enstoolment of a chief for the town.
It would also pave the way for the successful celebration of the Odwira festival, which has repeatedly been postponed.
It has been Otumfuo's wish to settle the Essumeja issue as soon as possible to enable him tackle other equally important issues like that of the Kumawu and Adansi Stool affairs.
The Essumeja Aduana Stool has been vacant since the death of Odeneho Oduro Numapau II in 2003, thus creating a vacuum in the composition of Asanteman, which is currently without a Benkumhene in the person of a substantive chief of Essumeja, who heads the Benkum Division of the Asante Kingdom.
It is in view of these developments that the youth want the Essumeja Stool elders to assert themselves and ensure that the Essumeja chieftaincy dispute is determined without any further delay.

Original_Search_Title: You Must Return, U.S. Tells Beneficiaries of Exchange Programmes
Headline: Nigeria: You Must Return, U.S. Tells Beneficiaries of Exchange Programmes
Body_Text:
No fewer than 43 Nigerians who have been selected into various academic and exchange programmes in the United States of America prepare in pursuit of further knowledge in chosen areas have been told to return at the end of their programmes to contribute to the development of the country.
This group of Nigerians, who were recently hosted by the U.S. Ambassador, Mr. Terrence McCulley, to a pre-departure orientation at the American embassy in Abuja were also offered tips on how to make the best of the opportunity of travelling to the states on such prestigious scholarships.
The ambassador advised the beneficiaries to be good ambassadors of the country while in the United States, urging them to return at the end of their programme to contribute to the political and economic growth of the country. "Clearly, for many alumni, the exchange experience does not stop when they return home," he said.
The ambassador, who was also elated at the presence of alumni of some of the programmes said of them: "All of you who have been accepted to participate in U.S. Department of State academic and exchange programs are among the brightest brains in Nigeria."
"Many of you went through a rigorous selection process and for you to have been accepted into the programme is a testimony of your accomplishments as a scholar, government official or a private entrepreneur."
McCulley said the alumni should find many ways to implement what they had learned while on the programme, added: "Around the globe, alumni are fostering mutual understanding by sharing their talents, knowledge, experiences, and time with their communities."
Stressing that they could also have an equally impressive local impact as they do a global scale, he said this could range from starting civic organisations, advocating human rights, to organising disaster-relief efforts, being some areas alumni have returned home with ideas and acted upon. Speaking on how this had influenced Nigerians, he said, in Nigeria, one individual alumnus was so inspired by her International Visitor Leadership Programme experience, that she returned to her former secondary school and established a scholarship programme to assist young girls further their education, while another, who is devoted to environmental awareness, brought together fellow alumni and organised a cleanup of trash at a Lagos area beach.
He also said the U.S. Mission staff from Abuja and Lagos, as well as alumni members would share practical experiences regarding studying and living in the United States, adding: "They will provide up-to-date information regarding privileges, obligations, J-1 visa status, health insurance, policy, tax policy among others, which are important for you to know."
He said the essence of the U.S. academic and exchange programmes was to promote mutual understanding between the citizens of the United States and those of other countries, including Nigeria.
The ambassador disclosed that each year, more than 40,000 participants from countries throughout the world, including the United States, had opportunities to build common bonds, share ideas and further mutual understanding through such programmes sponsored by its government.
These exchanges, according to him, include the prestigious Fulbright, International Visitor Leadership (IVLP), Hubert H Humphrey Fellowship, Study of the United States Institutes for Faculties & Student Leaders and Fulbright Foreign Language Teaching Assistant (FLTA) programme.
Among fellow alumni of U.S. Government sponsored exchange programmes worldwide are 47 Nobel Laureates, thousands of cabinet-level ministers, and more than 300 current and former chiefs of state or heads of government, he said.
Exchange programme alumni from Africa include the Presidents of Guinea, Togo, Kenya, and Mozambique.
In Nigeria, such programmes have produced prominent alumni, including Dr. Attahiru Jega, who is current Chairman of the Independent National Electoral Commission, Dr. Alex Ekwueme, former Vice President of Nigeria; and Professor Adebowale Ibibope Adefuye, currently serving as Nigerian Ambassador to the United States.
"As you travel to the United States, many of you will miss your favorite Nigerian dishes, including "pounded yam" "tuwon shinkafa" "amala" and "gari." I urge you to have a taste of American food - including a hot dog and a hamburger," the ambassador told them.

Original_Search_Title: ACN Members Will Re-Direct National Assembly Focus - Ajayi
Headline: Nigeria: ACN Members Will Re-Direct National Assembly Focus - Ajayi
Body_Text:
Adeyinka Ajayi, a barrister at law, is House of Representatives member for Osun Federal Constituency II of Osun State comprising Ifelodun/Boripe/Odo-Otin. In this interview with Correspondent, Gbenga Faturoti, Ajayi, who was elected on the platform of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN), speaks on the recent general elections, the focus of his political party in the current National Assembly and what should Nigerians expect from NASS among other issues. Excerpts:
What is your assessment of the just-concluded general elections vis-à-vis performance of the independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) and electorate?
The 2011 general elections marked a great move and shift from the old tendencies that characterised the atmosphere of our democratic processes in Nigeria. It has encouraged a large of people, who hitherto would have preferred to sit on the fence, to be part of politicking. It has encouraged the much-touted 'one man one vote'. It is a great shift from the old tendencies of the past and it is a welcome development.
What are the areas you would want improvement in subsequent electoral processes?
I think it is institutional framework that requires surgical operation. What I mean by institutional framework is INEC as presently constituted. Because we have had a useful or significant progress in the right direction does not take away the fact that we are still talking about the process of constituting the INEC; the process of nominating INEC members, the process of funding INEC and the whole issues that are institutional. We are fortunate to have at the top the likes of Professor Attahiru Jega; we could have had somebody like Professor Maurice Iwu. It was just by sheer luck. We could have had an institution that would be grossly and inadequately funded. We could have a process that would turn up a system that would have just be a replica of 2003 and 2007 general elections. So, what we are saying is that the institutional framework of our electoral system still needs overhauling. Unless and until we do that, we cannot guarantee that what happened in 2011 will continue to improve until such a time that we can go to sleep and say that our electoral process is semi-perfect or acceptable by whatever standard.
What about the areas of polling booths delineation?
A lot of these issues are secondary; once the institutional framework is right, other things would follow. Polling booths delineation is the total purview of INEC. It is not the real problem that we have now. It is about participation at different levels. So I don't see this constituting a problem. Yet it is important. It is significant. How do we want to do it to avoid concentration in certain area? I don't see those issues impacting, in any significant way, negatively in the process of the general elections or by any way the outcome of those elections.
You are talking of full participation of Nigerians in future elections. Are you saying there was apathy in the just-concluded exercise?
Definitely, if things are done the right way, more Nigerian would participate because then you have a credible system; you have a process that you can think, you can plan for or pre-determine your course of action. Once you have a system that calls for services in those regards, the level of participation will definitely increase. If you have a lot of people believing that their votes will count, can have an impact or say who represents or who governs them, of course the level of participation will increase. Participation will improve if we can reduce the incidence of violence to the barest minimum. Violence has been so much elevated to a level that we cannot claim to have democracy and it was both ways. Violence as regards the perpetrators and violence as regards people wanting to resist it both call for certain degree of violence or preparation for or against violence. Whichever way you look at it, it is balance of terror; you have two guns, at least I should carry one gun to sustain balance of terror. But it discourages participation. If I hear gun shot at a campaign rally, it is unlikely I would want to come out on the day of election to vote. The culture of violence is also significant that we need to reduce it, so that we can improve participation. It is a sad thing, for we are moving in the right direction with violence before, during and after election. What led to violence after the presidential election are issues of national security and national significance, that at this point and because of the role the society has bestowed on me now, I have to apply caution in volunteering an opinion. But the issues have national outlook that they should be looked at more seriously and critically for the sake of unity of our dear nation.
Activities of Boko Haram in the northern part of the country are becoming worrisome. What do you think the government can do to curtail it?
No responsible government will tolerate militia in any form. Any responsible government will take charge of the internal and external security, to the extent that the militia will exist internally. Every responsible government will go out of its way to curtail and contain such militia until they are wiped out. Therefore, the existence of any tribal militia group is dangerous to the continued co-existence of this country, and therefore the government must do all it can to dissuade, discourage and dis-member such group. It applies to all militia groups - whether it is Boko Haram, OPC or Egbesu boys. No responsible government should let it strive because if you cannot control their existence and evolution, it is dangerous for the existence of the unity of the country.
What will be focus of your party, ACN, which is currently the main opposition in the NASS, in the new NASS?
We have, as a party, an agenda that is still being documented. But I cannot for this moment say we have a six-point or seven-point agenda for the people of the country. However, I can give you the general framework of the ACN for NASS. The issue of proper democratisation of Nigeria is paramount to the ACN, true federalism in all its ramifications and fiscal federalism. These issues can still be broken down into several important issues confronting the country, but that is the very key issues that would be paramount to ACN in the National Assembly.
What should Nigerians expect from the new lawmakers?
Members of ACN will help to shift the focus of the National Assembly. We are determined and committed to shifting that focus by performing strictly legislative roles and invisible oversight functions that will enthrone proper democracy. We will increase and highlight the legislative roles which are what we are elected to do and then streamline and re-engineer the oversight roles. We will shift the attention of oversight from what it has now become: business-as-usual - when you hear that members of NASS are coming to your parastatal, what you are thinking about is Ghana-must-go. How do I settle them? Let me give them something to keep quiet. That focus must change. We need the critical minds to do that. Fortunately, we are now the major opposition party in the parliament; we think that will give impetus on how to do it. I expect Nigerians to look out for a more robust parliament in the next four years.
Zoning has remained important model for sharing appointments and others things in the country and this arrangement has been seriously criticised by some Nigerians as not good in considering important issues. What is opinion on the matter?
Like you said, PDP has greatly highlighted and exploited this so-called zoning arrangement, but we must be honest to say that this issue of zoning or federal character is not peculiar to PDP. It is actually part of our constitution. Our constitution says we should respect the federal nature of Nigeria in appointments both at the federal and state levels. So it is not by accident that the zoning has become part of our culture in Nigeria. The way and manner the ruling party has implemented it has, however, being skewed against the wishes and dictates of normal democracy. They throw up candidates just for the sake of throwing up candidates. For example, a sensitive issue is the gender equality issue; that you must have 35 per cent women in this or that. Are we now, therefore, saying that if we don't have women who had volunteered to participate, we should go to the streets and force them to participate? I think, no. If there are women who are available and competent and have put themselves up for appointments, it is easier for you to take them. But for elective office, it would be a little difficult because if the electorate decide to vote for men, are you saying democracy should not strive? If the majority had voted for men and not women, are you supposed to cancel the victory of the man to take care of the 35 per cent for the women? I think this is wrong and has no correlation with the issue of zoning. The concept of zoning is to give everybody sense of belonging, and that is why federal character is entrenched in our constitution. And for as long as that is still the law of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, we ought to abide by it. But I expect us to have serious reservation about the way and manner the PDP has played out the issue of zoning, which is not in tandem with the aspect of the constitution and the federal character.
In recent time, there was controversy involving the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) governor and NASS members on the allegation that the lawmakers were spending about one quarter of the nation's budget. Do you agree that the system should continue in the new NASS or what do you think?
As an individual, I think it is not okay. The ACN definitely is feeling more passionate than a lot of us think individually. It is one of the issues that we are going to work on in the new NASS. The era of NASS and the executive having more money at their disposal must stop. The Nigerian government, both the executive and legislature, unfortunately excluding the judiciary, are the most lucrative offices that you can ever see in the world. We are paying better than American parliamentarians or American executive. We are paying far better than parliamentarians and executives of Britain, Germany, Italy, Japan and everywhere in the world. Nigerian government, excluding the judiciary, is the best paid; that is why it has become a do-or-die thing. It is not seen as a mean to an end anymore; it is seen as an end in itself. (People feel "if) I make it to government, I am settled for life". That has to stop. We cannot continue to run such a top heavy government and leave so little to develop our infrastructure and you expect the country to make progress. We would just be moving round a circle. The ACN, as part of our agenda, is going to NASS to fight, as much as we can, to ensure that such top heavy system that we have in Nigeria is reviewed. Though it will be a bit difficult because we are not in the majority and this is democracy, but we are going to make a lot impact.
In what way do you intend to impact on the people of your immediate constituency?
What I can give my people is robust legislation. I am not an executive. I do not have executive power. I do not have power to award contracts. I don't have power to sink borehole or construct roads. That is part of what ACN is going into the NASS to fight again. This so-called constituency project is a way of comprising the legislature. It is a way to put some money in the hands of legislators, so that you are compromised and the executive can push you left, right and centre. And the fact remains that we have nothing against the legislature being the representatives of people; having a say in what they have done in their constituency. We are supposed to be representing that area. So we should have a voice. We are supposed to able to say that in Odo-Otin, water is their problem; so please, the executive, when you are planning your budget for next year, ensure that people of Odo-Otin get water. Road is the problem of people of Boripe; please, the executive, when you are making your plan for next year, consider the people of Boripe and so on and so forth. The idea of giving the project to the legislature as constituency project means compromising that legislature because he has now assumed executive role; he determines where to put it and who gets the contract. We have broken the mandate the people have given to us. The people did not give us power to execute projects. Our job is to legislate. The senators are legislators 100 per cent. The House of Representatives are legislators and representatives. That is why we are the only custodians of the public. We legislate and represent. When you give back from the little that you have and you begin to delve into the roles of executive, I think you are beginning to lose the essence of democracy on the separation of power.
Your party cleared all the legislative seats in your state, both in NASS and House of Assembly, and the argument was that there cannot be serious argument and well thought-out legislation and that the processes would be a one-way traffic.
Fortunately, this is life; you can't have your cake and eat it. It is either you want democracy or you don't want it. If the people of Osun State decide to vote one way, that is democracy; unless you are saying that we should sacrifice democracy at the altar of what you think is good. Should we stop the people of Osogbo from voting the way they want, just because you want an opposition? The crux of the matter is that Osun State people at the moment are so tired of PDP that they would rather just go anywhere. It does not necessary mean that we are the best alternative; but they just do not want the PDP. That is a challenge to us in ACN; so it is time for sober reflection, not time for celebration. It tells us to sit back, think of how we would justify the confidence that the people of Osun have reposed in us. The people have said we have totally rejected the PDP and that we are entrusting this whole state to you without any dissenting voice. We don't want anybody to disturb you. We don't want anybody to poke his nose; we want you to have time to do what you know best. That is a serious challenge. Don't forget that Osun is the only state in the South West of Nigeria that voted overwhelmingly for ACN presidential candidate. Even Lagos that is considered as our stronghold voted PDP. The atmosphere in Osun should really be seen as a serious embarrassment to the people that constituted the PDP in the state. It is a serious challenge that the people of Osun said "we reject you and we are giving these people a clean chance". I am sure the electorate will come back in four years time and ask how we used that opportunity. So, it is a serious challenge, but I can assure you that ACN government is seriously looking at that challenge. If people say they want you, it is what you do with what was entrusted into your hands that will justify whether the people of Osun have made a mistake or not. It has never happened in Osun to have a clean shift. No.
The monthly allocation to states and local governments and the sharing formula have remained controversial among Nigerians, particularly when the centre holds more money than what is given to both 36 states and 774 local councils. What do you think should be done?
When you have sentimentally-created entity, it is unlikely that you will avoid such problem. Unfortunately, I talk about this top heavy system. The centre is top heavy; so we need a lot of money to run and sustain the top. The money given to states and local councils cannot even pay salaries of workers, not to talk about developmental projects in those states or local governments.
The fundamental problem that has created is that people now clamour for creation of more local governments. That is part of the problems that we have now. We are so much concerned about the centre and it has killed the spirit of entrepreneurship, as well as the spirit of looking inward in our state and local councils. It has also killed the idea of not waiting for crumbs that fall from Abuja. The Lagos State governor and his contingents are working hard and championing the issue of derivation. I think derivation is the next stage of development of Nigeria. If you don't let me have access to what I make or produce, it will continue to encourage the system that we have now that the federal will continue to take so much just to run businesses that does not concern it. I don't know the business of the Federal Government in schools in my village? But there would be so much money accruing to the federal, and it will continue to have huge expenses to support that institution, when you can't even do substantial project that the community will benefit from.
So, derivation is not only important in this regard, but crucial to the development and survival of Nigeria. We will not stop to talk about it and not stop to pursue it; it is equitable to say that whatever I toiled to produce, I take the majority and just keep a little for the common purse. I think it is the best way to address this problem. I believe it is the law of nature that if I have one tuber of yam, you won't tell me to bring three quarter now, especially if I need the whole tuber of yam to survive. You take N100 from my economy and you return N15. What can I do with N15 when the N100 I made, in the first place, is not even enough to solve my problem. You tap all the oil in the Niger Delta, take it to the centre while creating ecological problem in that environment in the process of exploitation. What comes back to the Niger Delta is not up to 20 per cent of what is required to cater for the ecological problem that you created.
What then do you expect the community to do to develop? Derivation is equitable; it is the way forward and it will also encourage other states to look inward. That is why the government of Rauf Aregbesola is looking inward in Osun State. He (Aregbesola) wants to open up the economy of Osun.
The governor is implementing programmes that are designed to generate a lot of commercial activities within the state such that it will become a viable state and not depend on crumbs from Abuja. So derivation is good, is equitable and should be seriously pursued until we achieve it.

Original_Search_Title: Enough of the Hate Politics
Headline: Nigeria: Enough of the Hate Politics
Body_Text:
It is only in Nigeria that people like former Governor Aliyu Akwe Doma of Nasaraswa State can do whatever they did while in government and get away with it.
Otherwise, how could the former government have the guts to send cronies on a hatchet job so soon? It would have been in the interest of Doma to lay low and enjoy the peace of post-governorship life despite the poor legacy he bequeathed to the people of Nasarawa State.
Talking of politics of hate, who actually started it, was it Doma or is it Governor Umaru Tanko Al-Makura? It is surprising that Doman and his men now accuse Al-Makura of engaging in politics of hatred. Is it that the guilty are afraid that their own shadows are chasing them?
The latest turn of event from the former governor's camp is not surprising.
Al-Makura was really humble when he led a high profile campaign towards becoming the governor of Nasarawa State. He refused to play it dirty with Doma as he respects and hold the person of the former governor in high esteem as he demonstrated this in one of his numerous interviews when he said "Doma is my elder brother. I have nothing against him." Al-Makura at all cost resisted the immense pressure piled on him to expose too numerous shady seals that were on-going under Doma. Interestingly, those who were asking for the exposure did not only bring dossiers, they were people working with Doma in government. Now, this is the same Al-Makura that is being attacked.
It is still very fresh in the people's memory how Doma made the PDP space an inhabitable one for Al-Makura. In concert with national officials of the party and some highly placed officials in government, Al-Makura was not only harassed, intimidated, arrested severally and detained as well as emasculated politically; he was forced out of the PDP. They did not stop even when he decamped to the Congress for Progressive Change (CPC), Al-Makura, members of his campaign team and his teaming supporters were arrested and detained, all with the intent of neutralizing Al-Makura's rise. Despite all these, Al-Makura refused to act against Doma as we are witnessing in some states, Governor Al-Makura instead maintained his humble disposition not just to the opposition but the royal fathers whom Doma has set against him earlier on.
The former governor chased away emirs from their palaces when CPC presidential candidate came on visits in the prelude to the April elections, all with the intent of pulling the carpet from under the feet of Al-Makura. What do you call this kind of politics other than that of politics of hate? Doma and his men know better. That singular action of Doma portrayed the emirs and chiefs in bad light.
What democracy dividends are the Doma people enumerating that their principal has provided? Is it the over head water tank whose contract was not only over inflated that subsequently collapse and killed some innocent souls, or the Government House Conference room that was bereft of construction standard, or is it the paper yam project that was dead on arrival or what? Doma did all these white elephant projects at time Al-Makura was far away from the state. And the people saw are all living witness.
Shortly after the elections when Doma was returning to his Doma kinsmen, he met stiff resistance from the youths of the town who said they did not have any use for him as they did not feel the impact of his four years' regime as governor of the state. It took the intervention of a former governor who pacified the angry youths to allow Doma home. Would he blame the governor for this too?
It is common knowledge in Nasarawa state, that the last four years was a government of Doma, by Doma ad for Doma alone. It was a government in which no state official can approve any fund over 10, 000 naira until Doma approved.
The governorship election provided an opportunity for the people to not only breathe a sigh of relief, they chose to elect who they were sure would meet their aspirations. It would be in the interest of Doma to allow sleeping dogs to lie. Let him allow the state and the people to wriggle out of the heavy debt burden he has unleashed on them and to work towards rediscovering the potential inherent in them so that they can witness development.
Maku wrote from Nasarawa Eggon, Nasarawa State

Original_Search_Title: Big Brother Amplified - Heads You Lose, Tails You Win!
Headline: Namibia: Big Brother Amplified - Heads You Lose, Tails You Win!
Body_Text:
Awesomeness has left the Heads house.
Yip, the divalicious Confidence and Weza have been banished to kingdoms far, far away.
Good on them! And shame on those of you - yes, SHAME - who voted for hella, I don't know what you voted for!! Or who. You sure didn't vote for entertainment.
Heads is now like 'India: A Love Story' without Jade and Lukas, the Nama Awards without Lady May, 'Grey's Anatomy' without Meredith and 'Jacob's Cross' without Jacob. You get?!
God alone knows what some viewers were thinking. Have to assume they weren't!
So just shimmy that shame, shem, shayum and shiem and shovel it in front of your TV screens - I hope it keeps you warm through all the cold fronts.
As for me, all I know is that I no longer have confidence in the Heads house. And through these long, cold nights I'll be asking where'sa the Weza.
OK, OK, I know I'm starting to get a wee bit melodramatic.
Weza is still in the hood, albeit the Tails hood!
And Confidence. Sighhhhhhhhh I'll miss her, a whole lot of people will miss her, but I'm sure the woman who puts the ooh into boobs, the aaah into 'Say Aah' (a-la Busta Rhymes) and the ass into sass is putting Kimora Lee to shame wherever she is.
Go Confidence!
Goss flossies
That being said, I really fear - as in advent of The Rapture fear - for Milli and Filli, aka Felicia.
Eish, those two girls have had the very reason for their existence wrenched from them.
Wrench-ed. Believe it!!
Would love to know how many hours they clocked up gassing about Confi and, to a lesser extent, Weza.
During the day, at night, during the night, in the morning, the tjatjarag twins were as singleminded as a virgin in a convent.
Forget about those exercise pedometers Biggie gave housemates to clock up their movement megabytes, wish he'd given Milli, Kenya's finest, and Malawi's Filli goss-o-meters.
When it comes to jaw-jaw on Confi and Weza, the duo's combined carbon footprint has probably pushed the world to the edge of the global warming precipice.
Hmmmmmm, maybe Milli and Filli saw it as a career opportunity. You know, like a double act - the Milli Vanilli Filiblusters!!
Maybe it boosted their confidence. Maybe they were hoping some of the other two's shine would rub off on them. Whatever!
Anyway, with Weza and Confi 'out the way', the Heads mates resorted to playing hide and seek the other night!
Ooooooooooh, isn't that exciting; doesn't it make your toes tingle in anticipation!!
Brave souls. Bless 'em all.
Go figure
And, of course, I don't mind who you vote for really, truly. Still, not sure I trust this week's figures.
Especially after an old graph was initially left up on the official BBA site, after the delay in announcing the final country votes, and in the light of the continued absence of the vote percentage breakdown.
No one's going to tell me that roly poly Zeus, intelligent as he is, got more votes than Confi and Weza. Or that sweet Hanni, honey that she is, is a Heads headliner, etc, etc. Errrrr, was someone cooking more than just yams?!
This week's voting stats are generally interesting. Naija's Vina, who normally wins six votes, was down to two countries; Weza, who was top in six countries last time was down to one country, reportedly; Confi who usually wins the one-country battle on percentages is reportedly out, but no percentages are provided; Vimbai, who usually gets two to three country votes (if I recall correctly) ratchets up to five!Stay Tuned!

Original_Search_Title: Anambra Monarchs, Masquerades On War Path
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra Monarchs, Masquerades On War Path
Body_Text:
Awka — For allegedly destroying property, harassing innocent people and raping women in Orumba North Local Government Area of Anambra State, the Traditional Rulers' Council in the area has given promoters of masquerades stringent conditions.
According to the council, failure of promoters of masquerades to abide by the conditions will incur the full weight of the law.
Recently, masquerades invaded the Anglican Church at Ajali, the local government headquarters, where they destroyed property worth thousands of Naira.
They also reportedly invaded people's homes and demanded ransom for no justifiable reason, while some of them allegedly raped women when they chased them into their compounds.
Worried by the development, traditional rulers in the area, at a meeting at the local government headquarters, said as custodians of the peoples' culture and tradition, they would no longer keep quiet while selfish people use masquerades to instil fear in people.
The traditional ruler of Oko and chairman of the traditional rulers' council in the area, Professor Laz Ekwueme, who signed the statement issued by the royal fathers after their meeting, said all masquerades in the local government had been given up to August 15, 2011, to regularize their operations before the commencement of the new yam festivals when the masquerades usually hold sway.
The public notice issued to all masquerade operators read: "Every community shall form a masquerade committee which shall register all extant masquerades stating name and address, family and ward of the custodian of the masquerade and its assigned number from the traditional ruler.
"The register of masquerades shall be lodged in the palace of the traditional of the community and no two masquerades should wear the same number to be pasted on the chest and back of each masquerade.
"Unless invited and accompanied by one from community of operation, masquerades shall operate only in their own communities and should not wander into the neighbouring towns. Henceforth, no masquerade shall harass, disturb or in any way molest unconcerned individuals going about their business on public highways or in the markets, churches, schools and meeting places.
"Masquerades, which can carry only whips made of fresh green wood, should not chase anybody into his house.
