"Any masquerade that fails to abide by the rules will be arrested by the police and may be prosecuted for acts of indiscipline likely to cause a breach of the peace."

Original_Search_Title: I Eat Moderately to Avoid Overweight-Pastor Ituah
Headline: Nigeria: I Eat Moderately to Avoid Overweight-Pastor Ituah
Body_Text:
Pastor Ituah Ighodalo is the founder of Trinity House. A Motivational Speaker, Philanthropist and an Accountant by profession, Ighodalo is our guest in his edition of Celebrity Tummy Talk. He speaks on his healthy living as well as his recreation time as a servant of God.
How do you relax on weekends?
I read newspapers and watch TV. Also, I spend quality time with my family. That's good recreation for me.
Do you engage in any sporting activity?
Sometimes, I play soccer with my people and that is the much I can do. I try to play gulf but it is highly demanding and for the fact that I can't overwork myself. I prefer to play soccer once in a while.
What kind of food do you like?
I don't eat much. I don't know what I like but I know what I hate. I don't eat goat meat and I'm not a heavy eater. I eat more vegetables, fish and I drink lots of water.
What was your attitude to food, while you were growing up ?
I grew up in a well-disciplined home. And my mother was very finicky about food. She gave us what she considered was a balanced diet. I had everything I wanted as a child. My mum used to like amala a lots which I didn't like. So, I prefer to eat eba or pounded yam. She likes eko (agidi) which I didn't like.
As a strict mother, she was always pressurising us to start eating some of these foods. so, anytime she compelled us to eat what we don't like, we usually console ourselves that some day; we would be free to eat only what we like.
How do you feel if your mother forces you to eat what you don't like?
You there not dare flaunt my mum's order. You have to eat it, she was very nice but strict. She was running our home, and so, it was what she gave to us that we ate. We only had privilege to request for something which she would oblige us . Most of the food she gave us was nice food. There was nothing wrong with the food, but we were just exhibiting childhood pranks.
How you react to food provided in your school?
We were allowed to bring food to the school, so if you don't like a particular food, then, you have a choice to seek solace in your grump box and eat whatever is left from it. I went to King's College and the food provided to us there wasn't bad. What I don't like then was semovita. We were well taken care of.
What's your state of origin?
Edo State but my mother was a Yoruba woman.
What is the traditional food of the Edo people?
It's pounded yam.
And you hate pounded yam with passion?
Not really, but I don't like heavy food. I like to be light. And I'm not crazy about food. I eat fruits, vegetables and fish.
So, what fruit are we talking about here?
Any fruit aside banana.
Has your wife prepared a particular meal that makes you salivate or remember your mother's meal?
Not really. My mother and wife have different styles. My wife is more anglo in nature while my mother is traditional. I don't really like traditional food.
So, you prefer your wife's meal?
Don't let my mother hear that oh. Anyway she is dead. She wouldn't hear you. But, my wife is not a bad cook.
You are not looking 50. What is the secret?
No secret. It's just by the grace of God. It is not deliberate but the way God has made it. So, I can't really say for now.
Can you say your food in-take contributes to your fitness?
I deliberately worked on my shape and I'm careful about what I eat. As a pastor, there is lots of fasting. And I'm not a food freak.
Is fasting part of discipline?
It takes discipline to fast. Naturally, the body is hungry but if you can overcome the first two days of fasting, the other days will be better off.

Original_Search_Title: Maina Reappears in Jos As Varsity Holds Peace Talk
Headline: Nigeria: Maina Reappears in Jos As Varsity Holds Peace Talk
Body_Text:
Jos — The immediate past General officer commanding 3rd Amoured Division, GOC, Maj. Gen. Saleh Maina made a surprised appearance in Jos, Plateau state after his redeployment by the army high command to chair an occasion on peace building in the troubled state.
It was put together by the department of Mass communication of the university of Jos.
In attendance were experts from different field, including, traditional rulers, academia, the media and the security personnel and foreign personnel who are expected to brainstorm on ways to end lingering violence that continued unabated in the state in recent times.
General Maina who commended the initiators for organizing the programme and urged participants to make best use of it, stressing that it is the best opportunity to address the security challenges of the state.
The gathering which is an initiative of the Mass communication department of the University of Jos , in conjunction with the centre for conflict management and peace studies of the institution was organise to look for possible solutions to the crises.
The programme tagged, "International Conference on Africa, Media and peace building" was mainly to tap the role of ,media who have the ability and strength to make or mar any conflict in the society and apply it in resolving the Jos civil disturbances.
Earlier in his welcome speech, the Vice Chancellor of the Institute, Prof. Sonny Tyoden disclosed that the University decided to take up the challenge of organising the programme, which according to him, is first of its kind since the establishment of the university, because the management believes that the mass media has the knife and the yam and is capable of correcting the anomalies of the state.
On his part, the Chairman Traditional council of chiefs in the stated and the Gbong Gwom Jos, Da Jacob Gyang Buba challenges the management of the University of Jos and the National Institute of Policy and Strategic Studies, NIPSS to step up it efforts and engage its researchers into finding the root causes, the history and tradition of plateau state.
According to the first class chief, this would help in shaping minds of the people towards knowing the problem of the state thereby putting to rest the lingering trouble in the state.
Delivering his goodwill message, Director General, Institute for peace and conflict resolution, Abuja Dr. Joseph Golwa, called on the Media to give priority to 'peace journalism' adding the persistent reference to Africa as the continent of conflict, diseases and hunger is not a good compliment.

Original_Search_Title: Auto Crash Claims One at Auchi
Headline: Nigeria: Auto Crash Claims One at Auchi
Body_Text:
Benin — An autocrash that occurred at Ewu slope on the Benin-Auchi expressway has claimed a life, leaving many passengers seriously injured.
The accident happened at about 9:30a.m when a trailer crashed into four other cars. Eyewitness account said the accident was caused by the activities of park operators, popularly called "agberos".
According to an eyewitness, one of the "agberos" had chased a private car for allegedly picking a passenger along the way without their approval.
The eyewitness said the park operator caught-up with the private vehicle at the slope and blocked it with his car causing a queue of about four cars on the highway.
He lamented that the trailer, apparently oblivious of the incident, ran over the vehicles, as it could not apply instant brake.
One of the "agberos," who was interrogating the private car driver, reportedly died on the way to the Irrua Specialist Teaching Hospital, and his remains was deposited at the morgue of the hospital.
Other victims of the accident who were severely injured are currently receiving treatment at ISTH.
Men of the Operation ThunderStorm, set-up by the Edo State government, helped convey the dead and injured victims to the Irrua Specialist Teaching Hospital.
When this reporter visited the scene, the wreckage of vehicles involved in the accident were being cleared off the road by men of the Federal Road Safety Commission (FRSC).
Foodstuff, including garri, yams and fruits, sold by some women along the Benin-Auchi road that were destroyed during the accident, littered the road.
Ewu slope is now regarded as death trap, following series of accidents that have occurred at the spot.

Original_Search_Title: 'Fight' With Emem Isong Propelled me Into Acting -NSE Etim
Headline: Nigeria: 'Fight' With Emem Isong Propelled me Into Acting -NSE Etim
Body_Text:
When did you discover you had a flair for acting?
It was when I finally decided that I was going to do this, that was after "Reloaded". But I always had a thing for Sophie Lauren and then I saw Lil Sid. I knew I wanted to be a woman like that but I wasn't sure how it will work here. It really wasn't a dream per se and I got into acting earlier; after I graduated from the university, just to prove a point to my mother that I could do what I wanted to do.
I was happy at first but I wasn't satisfied. I guess because I was still in search of myself, I rushed out, got into banking and into other industries. I did radio a bit, did a bit of compere on stage. Then, I went into banking again. I also did a bit of fashion. But after Emen Isong fought with me in my kitchen; I still remember that because it was what propelled me - that and the script of "Reloaded".
How do you mean, she fought with you in your kitchen?
It was not a physical fight but she kept saying:
"It is because you are not confident enough to go into acting, that is why you keep running out."
And, I thought to myself that how can anyone say that about me. That sort of propelled me and I took up the challenge with the script of "Reloaded" and I made it. This is where it has brought me thus far.
How did you get into "Reloaded" and what was it like being on the set for the first time?
Emem just said to me that she has a script that she was working on and she wanted me to take a look at it. When she showed it to me, it was basically to have an input because normally, as a writer and a story teller, you basically have to get feelers about what people think of your story - just to know how it will do in the market.
I told her:
"I like the story. Let's see what we can do."
And, I had a bit of input in "Reloaded". Then she said to me:
"Listen, I would like you to take this character."
And, I said no way. I am not an actor.
So, we argued about it in the kitchen; which was why she said to me that I am not confident. I am not this, I am not that. Then I said:
"Okay, let's give it a shot."
And yes, I was afraid. I still get scared before I go on set because I am my own competition. Nothing for me to compete with; there was nothing. It was a case of just-fresh, nothing really was there for me to compare myself to. It was scary.
And then, there was Lancelot Imasuen. There was Rita on set; though Rita has been my friend from way back. There was also Stephanie; there was Uche Jombo; there was Ini (Edo); there was Ramsey (Nouah). I have been on set with Ramsey before but it was like a whole new Nollywood. I mean, I was like fish out of water but they made me very comfortable, which I think was what helped me.
As an actor, you have experienced both worlds - on stage and on set. If you have to choose, which would you rather do?
It can't be compared. Stage and screen are two different worlds entirely. For stage, you feel like a demi-god. You are in control of your audience. You don't see anybody, nobody sees you. For you, it is like this is my space; you are the lord and master of the space: "Now, take me as I am!" That is stage for you.
With screen, if I dry up (drying up is like forgetting your lines), what happens is that I get corrected. On stage, there is no such thing so, if you make a mistake, you have got to blend it and it's got to look like it is okay. So, stage needs a lot of practice and I give kudos to stage actors. I know that I will still do stage because, it is one of the things that I really love and being in control is a good thing.
Being your first after a long while on set, how did you feel being nominated for the AMAA Awards and later winning the award for Best Leading Actress at the Nigerian Entertainment Awards for your role in "Reloaded"?
For me, a nomination for the 'African Oscars', as I call it, is as good as a win. That recognition is second to none. What else would I be asking for? It was a first time and it was amazing. Did you see the names that were on set? I would have said:
"Take the awards back, please, to the rightful owner."
Do you understand that that is how I felt? I think no better honour could have been bestowed on me than that. It was so amazing; I was totally speechless!
Growing up, what influenced you as a person? What motivated you to make a success of yourself and career?
I come from a close-knit family; from a family that was relatively large. Because, for me, six kids is large. We were like a family and then, losing my dad at a tender age and watching my mother being a woman that I will always love to be.
She was able to keep us together. We still go to the same church. That is rare these days. We go to the same church. We have the same beliefs. We are still in the same line; that is really hard to do. So, it is not the case of: "Oh, I am here and there, it is still like that." Things like that have helped mould me; so when I do things, it is always about what would Martha think (that is my mother's name).
I call her Martha when I want to piss her off and then she will laugh. I always wonder what she will think. Sometimes, I think - let my father not go and rumble in the grave and things like that. It hurts but I think it has helped me. I think that much as we say we do not want our loved ones to go, it is a formidable one that has really helped (to build) whom I have become today.
Do you think you are living your dream? Are you doing what you have always wanted to do?
If I say no now, it is because I have not yet reached the moon, literally. I will say yes, I am living my dream because I am an actor and I am doing everything I am passionate about - acting, writing, cooking. Those are the things I love (doing), so it is not a case of me doing anything because I am going to make money out of it. I am doing them because I love them and when you love them, all other things will be added unto them.
When it comes to style, what influences the look you put together every time you step out of your home?
I am more bohemian in my dressing. But at the same time, because, sometimes, I can be like an ardent, I like to wear my jeans and tank top. Then, I have my designer who is constantly trying to make me a lady. When I am going out in public, Wange does a lot for me, especially if I am going out. She would say:
"You have to wear this."
She sinks it with my personality and it works. I think my style is not fashion-based because I feel that style transcends fashion.
Women are generally into shoes, bags, jewelleries... What are you into?
I am into food!
You said you love to cook. Where did you get that from? Why cooking?
I will tell you a story that I don't think I have told before; except to my close friends. At 13, my father made me pound yam and I cooked egusi soup. I made the soup. There was egusi on one side, water on one side and oil on another side. Then, the pounded yam had 'co-co' in it. My father ate the food and I saw the pain in his eyes. I said to myself I wish I could wipe that off his face.
Since then, it was a case of me trying always to impress with my food; trying to cook but I don't eat egusi soup till tomorrow. I don't like it.
When I left my first banking job, what I did was sit at home for a year cooking. I bought all the cookery books I could get. I was just trying to look for something in me. I went to Johannesburg, South Africa, for a few months as well. For about five, six months, it was all about food. And, I came back broke and looked for another bank job. By that time, it was a case of me and food. That is it.
What is your favourite?
Because I cook, I don't have a favourite. I like everything as long as it is healthy.
How do you relax when you are not working?
My couch should best explain that; my couch potato. I love to lay on the couch a lot. I read a lot. It is as bad as reading three books at the same time, depending on my mood. Then, I play a lot of Scrabble as well. I watch soccer, I love soccer. Sometimes, I go out with friends. It is always nice to hang out with friends.
What would you love to be remembered for?
I will love to be remembered for the lives I have touched, either by making them laugh or by lending a helping hand or by just being there. Sometimes, a word of encouragement is a lot more than money and there is a lot of pain right now. I am on the board of this charity programme. It is called 'The Empowerment Programme'. It is for young girls really.
Here, you know we don't talk about a lot of things that have happened to us. We love to bottle them up and this informs you; this makes you who you are in future. But when we learn to speak out, when we talk about our problems - people don't talk about rape, people don't talk about sexual harassment, people don't talk about even death. They bottle them up but when a woman can talk about these things, it makes her a better woman. She is a better woman in her relationship and as a mother. For instance, sex education is not taught to a lot of kids or maybe they are beginning to talk about it now.
Talk about sexual harassment in Nollywood. What is it like? Have you ever been harassed?
I will answer you the way I answered them at Harvard. The same views I have of sexual harassment in banks, schools, hospitals, everywhere. Because we are in the limelight, everyone seems to think that it is more in our industry. It has never happened to me but I can't say it does not happen. It is bound to happen when you have men and women together.
It can happen either way; women can harass a man, men can harass a woman. It all depends and it happens in all industries. We are not the only ones.
Do you have a role model?
My mentors are my mother, if I can be like her, I think I will be completely fulfilled. My other mentor is a neuro-surgeon, Ben Cason. He was able to become the best in his line despite his shortcomings; he was disadvantaged and he fought through it all and he is the best in his line.

Original_Search_Title: Secret Behind my 62 Years on Throne, by Ekiti Oba
Headline: Nigeria: Secret Behind my 62 Years on Throne, by Ekiti Oba
Body_Text:
Ado-Ekiti — Ekiti State, the nation's fountain of knowledge is probably the state with the highest number of recognised traditional rulers in the country with a record of not less than 126 monarchs, each falling on the three different grades, A, B and C depending on the historical antecedents.
One of the communities in Ekiti, Ikoro-Ekiti, had lived for over 24 years without a monarch until recently when the controversies surrounding the Oba stool dispute was resolved.
Records show that very few Ekiti Obas were in grade A category in the old Ondo State. It was during the tenure of the first civilian governor of the state, Niyi Adebayo that a re-grading was made and the number of Obas in the top rank increased to 31.
Their grading was not devoid of rancour and protest from some Obas who claimed they ought to be in the first class rank. Among such Obas is the Onire of Ire-Ekiti.
Although those saddled with the task then had argued that the re grading was based on the facts and document presented from the exercise at that time. Presently, 31 Obas are in grade A, 37 in B and 58 in C, which is the last.
Among the grade A Obas, 17 are foremost and there are the pelupelu in the Ekiti Obas parlance.
Saturday Independent findings revealed that the state province, right from the onset had 16 Obas, referred to as the Pelupelu.
This included the Deji of Akure and Olobo of Obo in the modern Kwara State.
But because the duo had severed relationship with Ekiti, three new entries were accommodated increasing the pelupelu from its traditional 16 members to 17. The three new Obas in the pelupelu cadre are the Atta of Ayede, Elemure of Emure and Olomuo of Omuo Ekiti.
The pelupelu's superiority and prestige above other Obas in the state is predicated on the fact that the history of each of the members is directly traced to the Yoruba source, Ile-Ife.
Recently, of the traditional rulers, the Alasa of Ilasa -Ekiti, Oba Abel Ajibola, rolled out drums, as he celebrated his 62nd anniversary on the throne as the longest reigning monarch in the state.
Ilasa Ekiti is in Ekiti East Local Government area of the state.
Alasa, according to history, is the longest serving traditional ruler in the whole of old Ondo State, which comprises of over 50 kingdoms and hundreds of villages and towns.
The people of the community renowned for mass production of yam in the western part of the country did not hide their feelings and approval of the reign of the Oba Ajibola, which was demonstrated in the massive turn-out.
The celebrant, who is well above 100-year-old, also exhibited uncommon features as he walked without stick and read without glasses.
Asked about the secret of his longevity, the Oba Ajibola attributed it to the support, loyalty and peaceful disposition of his subjects towards his ruler ship in the community.
His words, "I have served my people well in the last 62 years. They loved me so well that they used to organise prayers for me, so that I can live long, which God has answered. They want me to be their king for a very long time.
"I want to tell you today that if they want me to die, there are ways they can do it. They can start waging war against me and before you know it, I will die. Despite my age, they still love me and want me to continue in this position," the Ajibola disclosed.
While addressing his subjects at his palace after the church service, he urged them to be committed to the development of the town, adding that they should not allow political differences to reap the kingdom apart as being witnessed in some communities
"One thing I have always thank God for is this, since I assumed this position, no life or property has been lost to political upheavals. I have always been conducting the affairs of my people very well and this, in no small measure, has guarded against political acrimony," he said.
One of the daughters of the monarch, Jolaade Onipede, and former Special Adviser and two-time commissioner in the state stormed the St. John's Anglican Church and led other dignitaries to the venue of the thanksgiving service to rejoice with the traditional ruler, who is described as a pride of traditional institution in the state.
Tracing his journey to the throne, the monarch, who is now over 100 years, said he ascended the exalted seat in 1949 citing God's grace and supports of his subjects as secret behind his long reign and longevity.
The monarch recounted that he never envisaged the position as at that time, saying he was busy with his trade as a professional and successful local weaver and fabric maker before the mantle of leadership suddenly fell on him.
Alasa commended President Goodluck Jonathan for taking step in assigning constitutional roles to traditional rulers in the country, just as he appealed to the National Assembly to rework the constitution to allow for expanded roles for monarchs, particularly to adjudicate on some categories of matters in Nigeria.
The traditional ruler posited that some of the cases being tried by conventional courts should have been taken to the palace for the traditional chiefs to dispose off to allow speedy trial of issues in courts.
The monarch also solicited the creation of Ekamefa local government with headquarters in Ilasa-Ekiti, saying this will help in developing the area and bring governance close to the downtrodden masses.
The monarch, who urged Governor Kayode Fayemi to boost mass production of yam in the community, which he said could be of industrial benefit to the state if harnessed, urged the speedy completion of the yam barn project initiated by the immediate past administration in the town.

Original_Search_Title: Growing up As Fela's Daughter Was Fun - Yeni
Headline: Nigeria: Growing up As Fela's Daughter Was Fun - Yeni
Body_Text:
In a few days' time, Yeni Anikulapo-Kuti will be 50 years old and she will celebrate the milestone with a big bash. The ever young- looking lady who likes to keep her beautiful hair and eye lashes natural at all times spoke with Deputy Editor, Saturday, Charles Okogene, and Senior Reporter, Ifeoma Meze, on how it feels to be 50 and her life as Fela's daughter
You are one of the few Nigerian women that are blessed with natural long dark hair and you have never been spotted wearing a weavon or wig. Can you give us some tips on how you take care of it?
The thing about using weavon frequently is that the natural hair will lose its natural strength because it is not getting air. I think it is better, no matter if your hair is long or short, to look after what God gave you. You will hardly ever see me using false eye lashes, false nails because at the end of the day, that is what people will know you for. I don't want people to know me as someone that uses artificial things. I keep everything around me natural.
What special things do you apply to your nails, or hair to make them grow?
Because of my age, my nails don't grow like they used to grow before. So now, I have to keep using nail treatments to keep my nails from breaking. For the hair, I moisturise it every night before I sleep with a hair product my daughter introduced me to. It's a hair moisturiser; I use Kera Kel as well to moisturise my hair every night. If I moisturise it for three nights, then I will oil it for the remaining four nights and every night I braid it into two. It's become like a ritual for me. I know that I will not sleep until I braid my hair. Even if I get home by 4.00 a.m., I must braid my hair before I sleep.
So it will be hard for you to wear a weaveon or wig?
It is not possible. I always wonder if anything happens to my hair, what will I do. I don't pray so. I think I will just go with my low cut if I don't have hair anymore. I don't believe in saying never because nobody knows what is in the future.
Tell us about your kind of fashion; it seems everything you put on is specially made for you?
What I wear is not in anyway specially made for me. It's not customised for me or anything, but I take my time in selecting what I want. It depends on what it is. There are things that have been customised for me. When I go shopping, I can shop for like six hours at a stretch. Unless you are a 'shopaholic' like me, you cannot go shopping with me. My daughter can't go shopping with me because she will complain and complain. I will go into every single shop looking for things that will catch my eyes.
And you don't go for things that every other person will go for because yours are always looking different?
I don't know because these earrings I am wearing, I bought them in a shop in New York. I was walking down the street and it is a Chinese shop and I've been into several shops and I just got to this shop and saw the earrings. They were the only pair there and I got them. I took them quickly, so it depends. I have my beads made for me, I have a woman that I call and I'll give her a list of what I want and she will make them. If she has something special or other designs of beads that she has already made, she will call me and ask if I want to see them and if I like them, I buy.
Is it the same as your dresses? Do you go for designer?
Not necessary. I have a very good tailor. He's a young guy. He knows what I like, he'll sit down and we discuss what I want him to make for me. I might see some styles on TV that I really like, I'll just take a picture and I'll show him. He's been working with me for a couple of years so he knows what I want and how I want it. If I see something in a magazine that is nice, what I might take from that picture is maybe the sleeves or the neck, then I'll take another one from another magazine and I'll say okay add this neck to that sleeve so that's how I choose my styles. I don't deal with European designers but Africans.
You deal mostly with African designers. Any specific reason?
Because I prefer if I'm asked by a journalist like you, 'oh what are you wearing?' I'll hate to say I'm wearing a Versace or Gucci or whoever they are. Why I'm I publicising them when we have our own designers in Nigeria. I rather do publicity for an African designer rather than for Versace who doesn't know that I exist. I rather advertise Deola Sego who knows me and has made clothes for me before. It's not like I don't wear foreign designers but I don't go out of my way to buy them or publicise them in the newspaper.
You are turning 50 in few days. How is the party going to be?
Personally I would have loved to have done a quiet ceremony between myself and my darling. Just run away to one island somewhere and celebrate it. Spend very little money but they will lynch me at the Shrine if I try that. We will have a party here, anyway.
You look much younger than your age. Do you also do natural food to keep the young look?
Thank you very much, that is a complement. I feel I look old. What do I do? I try to diet once in a while, but I don't succeed because I love food a lot. I do exercise. I have started exercising quite regularly. I try to dance at least once or twice a week. To me in all fairness, I think a lot of African food is very natural. Our Efo (vegetable), I eat a lot of efo, beans and palm oil are natural. They can be fattening but they are natural. I don't eat pounded yam anymore because of weight, but I love amala, ewedu and beans. I use body cream that has shea butter. Shea butter is good for the hair and skin. I use it for my hair and it's very good. Shea butter is one of the secrets of African beauty that most people don't know.
At 50 would you say you have really accomplished all that you want?
I don't think many people can say that. I definitely can't say I have achieved what I want to achieve. I am not saying that I am not grateful to God for what I have got. I am grateful for what I have but I wish I could achieve a lot more.
Let me put it this way. Did you look forward to 50 or it just happened on you?
I looked forward to it. I looked forward to 30, I looked forward to 40, and I looked forward to 21, now I am looking forward to 50. One of the reasons I am excited about being 50 is that I can eat whatever I like; I can put on as much weight as I want. One of the reasons is that I am going to allow myself to get fat. I have been exercising because I wanted to be slim till I am 50 and after I am 50, I want to be eating what I want, at least I have done half a century.
Do you feel 50 then?
Let me put it like this way. When we were growing up, at 16 or 21, if someone said he was 50, we would shout that the person was old. Now that I am 50, I don't feel it. When my daughter told me that I am old, I asked her 'who is old?' You find that you called somebody old before but now you don't feel old. I feel that I have lived my life but I don't feel old like in those days that a 50-year-old is like 90 years old to me.
How did you get yourself into music business?
I retired from Femi's band when I was 43. I cannot even imagine me not being in music because when I was in secondary school, I was in most of the cultural dances. It got to a stage when I was going to sit for my O'Level examination, my friends ostracised me because everybody was studying and I was the only Form Five girl dancing with Form Four and three students at that time. I was the most unserious person in my class because I was still dancing in the cultural group but I didn't feel bad about it. It was when my friends now threatened to stay away from me that I decided to face my studies. I missed dancing. When I came out of school and went into NIJ (Nigerian Institute of Journalism) and secretarial college, I remember missing it because what I really wanted to do was dancing. My father could not afford to send me to a dancing school in England. I got a normal job and was working until my Femi said that he was starting his band. I was about 23 to 24 years old then. I left that job and joined him. I did not think about leaving the job twice. I was among the people that planned the choreography.
Even in my father's band, I would have but it was just that with his wives, there was serious politics and I didn't want to get involved in wives politics thing. I am sure I would have found a way to dance whatever the case because I was not happy doing my 9-5 job. The call of the stage was stronger. Even after I got married, I told my ex-husband that I could not leave dancing. He met me there and I told him I would go back after I had my daughter. I told him that if people asked him why did he allow his wife to dance, he should not mind them because that was what I wanted to do. So I cannot imagine doing any other thing. Even now that I don't dance professionally, I can't say I don't miss it. I do miss it. It's just that there are younger people doing it and I don't have that level of energy anymore. I can still dance, I still practice but not like before.
What was it like growing up as Fela's daughter? What was that thing you hated and what did you like as Fela's daughter?
The way I live my life right now is that I don't think of anything negative. I try not to dwell on anything negative. Negative things happen to all of us, I try to think of the positive things in my life so I will not dwell on the past. I mean growing up as Fela's daughter there were people who did not want to be your friend. They don't want to be associated with Fela's children. Femi, Sola and I faced that a lot because at that time, they were raiding Fela for Indian hemp, raiding Fela for many other things but I look at it now and say he who laughs last, laughs.... So I rather not dwell on such things. Those were the kind of little pressures, but to me, they were all a learning experience so I don't dwell on it. The positive things are that at that young age, everything happening around Fela was like one big party. Having one big party all the time. Going to Fela's house for us was big fun because we were staying with my mother. I think I find it more positive now that I am much older. I loved my father's music as a kid, now I don't just love it, I respect it.
You refer to him with his first name 'Fela,' you don't call him dad or papa?
No, he did not let us call him daddy. We had to call him Fela. He liked it that way.
What is it like running a place like Shrine?
The politics I was running from in my father's band is the same politics I face on a daily basis here at the Shrine. Running the Shrine is very political. You are dealing with so many human beings, so there is always one problem or the another. Running a place like this is not hard; it's dealing with individual problems and stress. I guess growing up in my father's house has thought me how to deal with this kind of place. It's not something that stresses me that much because I am used to it. It's not a big deal.
Many people describe you as a very tough woman. Do you agree with that. Are you really tough?
Maybe because I don't suffer fools gladly. I prefer to deal on the truth. If a challenge comes, I face it head on. I am going to shout about it. I don't like beating around the bush maybe that is where the iron lady name came about because I will deal with the problem immediately. I am someone that if something is disturbing me, I cannot keep it inside. I have to say it out, if I don't, it can just grow into a cancer inside me. That is why people think I am tough. But people, who really know me, will tell you that I am just a emotional person who cries by mere watching movies that are very emotional.
Do you like it that they call you tough?
It's like a myth. I like it. Like they used to call my grandmother Iya aje and they call my father Omo iya aje. My grandmother was not a witch, but people thought she was a witch; they fear you and think you are just one kind of powerful person.
Would you say that the attitude to life, treat things as they come, face them squarely, gave you the power to step into the leadership position that was created by the death of your father and your mother?
One of my beliefs in life is that the creator does not put what you cannot deal with in your path. At a time you might feel your world is coming to an end but at the end of the day, it is what you can deal with. I don't think God gives you a problem that you cannot deal with. It's just like a woman and a baby. A woman who cannot carry her baby is a joker. When a woman says 'my baby is too heavy' your baby is not too heavy. God will give you a baby that you can carry. That is my belief; God has given me what I can deal with and not what I cannot deal with.
What can you say has been your toughest challenge?
The death of my sister, Sola, was the toughest time of my life. The death of my parents was sad as well. Fela dying, my mother dying, it's sad but we all pray that our parents die before us, but you don't expect your younger sister or brother to die before you. Her death was the toughest time of my life because after she died, I thought my world was coming to an end. I didn't want to wake up in the mornings, I was very depressed, I could not eat so I think that was my toughest time.
How did you overcome it?
It took me time. Time heals all wounds they say. You don't forget, but the pain is not as bad as it was then. I still miss her. The colour of my ****aso ebi**** for my birthday is purple, that was my sisters favourite colour. I do things thinking of my sister a lot. Let's stop before I start crying.
Your marriage during the time you were still into dancing and music did not work out. Was it as a result of the break up that you refuse to marry again?
No. I don't know why I don't want to marry again. As a woman, you pursue your career and face only your career without thinking of marriage. My work was very important to me. We were travelling on tour a lot. I faced my baby and my work and marriage did not come into the picture at all. I don't really want to marry again.
Looking back now, you don't you regret not remarrying?
Not at all. Not one bit. I have someone that we are very much together, we love each other and everything, but I always wonder, 'could we live together?' Not that we don't spend time together, we spend a lot of time together. At this age, we have become so set and we all know that marriage is give and take. But when you have been living on your own for so long, it's not that you can't give and take, but it's just that will you be successful at it. I always wonder if marriage will spoil what we have, but I love him very much. I am not sure that he will accept me with all my clothes because I love clothes so much. In my room I have so many loads.
How much are you willing to spend on a dress?
It depends. I will not disclose that but I can go for the cheapest and the most expensive. That is me. I can pass a shop and they say three for five pounds, I will enter there. I can pass another place and they say its 100, 000 for one dress, I can enter there. I am not choosy.
What about shoes, how many pairs do you have?
I cannot say, I will be ashamed but I can assure you that quite a large amount. My brother once counted my shoes and he was amazed. I didn't tell him I had some hidden somewhere. I have shoes in all colours.
People say your style is eccentric, how would you describe your style? At 50 people will expect you to tie iro and buba.
I do tie iro and buba if occasion calls for it. I like buba too but I will not tie my own at the stomach. I will tie it down in a sexy way. I don't know if I am eccentric with my fashion. I will say I am different. The way I dress is me. I love to dress this way. I wear anything that catches my fancy.
You also like jewels a lot; in fact some think you overdo it.
People might think I overdo my beads. Everybody is entitled to his or her own opinion. I love beads a lot. I can say that people who wear plenty gold are overdoing it. Gold is not my style because this is a country where there are many hungry people, why should I be boasting of my wealth. At some point, I told myself that I am old enough to wear gold, maybe I should start wearing gold too. So I priced one once and they said one million naira. I can't do that, no.

Original_Search_Title: Gemade on PDP Chairmanship
Headline: Nigeria: Gemade on PDP Chairmanship
Body_Text:
Lagos — Until last year when he joined the debate on Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) zoning arrangement following the death of President Umaru Yar'Adua, Engineer Barnabas Gemade, had not been much in the news, since his hazy exit as national chairman of the party in 2001.
But even as he literally kept to himself, the former Works Minister could hardly be said to be oblivious of the workings of his intrigue-infested party.
The Senator-elect for Benue Northeast Senatorial District, was however in the news recently, when he faulted the method the party has adopted in choosing its national chairmen since inception in 1999. He attributed the situation where successive occupants of the seat were always removed under controversial circumstances to absence of internal democratic process in the party's affairs.
The one-time Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of Benue Cement Company (BCC) noted that there was no way an occupant of the chairmanship position could have been easily removed before the expiration of his tenure, if he was duly elected at a National Convention of the party.
He therefore warned against a repeat of the trend in choosing the party's next National Chairman.
"The biggest problem associated with the National Chairmanship of the PDP up till this point, to my mind, is that most of the occupants did not go through a free and fair election to become Chairmen. I believe I am the only Chairman that was ever elected at the National Convention. So, my advice is that we must ensure that our next National Chairman is popularly elected at the National Convention for him to serve out his tenure. In my own case, I was elected and my tenure was two years and I served that tenure to the end of it. But if you are selected and assigned, then, you can also be removed at any time. And that is the crux of the matter. We ought to democratise the issue of National Chairmanship of PDP," he cautioned.
Many see Gemade's position as that of a man who wore the shoe and knew exactly where it pinched. They believe few people in the party had the capacity to understand the problem better than Gemade.
However, despite his claim of being duly elected at a national convention of the PDP, observers noted that Gemade's emergence had more to do with the high wire politics in the party. Many in fact, saw his election as outright imposition by the then President Olusegun Obasanjo, who was in total control of the party's machinery. The convention, which took place at the Eagles Square, Abuja, was believed to have been structured to favour Gemade than the much fancied late Sunday Awoniyi. Barely a year afterwards, Gemade was removed as the PDP national chairman in 2001, when it was obvious that he had exhausted his grace with Obasanjo's imperial presidency.
Even then, Gemade's exit was hardly surprising. In fact, Solomon Lar, the party's first national chairman, who worked round the clock to ensure Obasanjo's victory in the 1999 presidential polls was not allowed re-election in that convention because he was said to have fallen out of favour with Obasanjo.
The tenure of Gemade's successor, Audu Ogbeh, appeared to be the most dramatic of all the chairmen. It was widely reported that Obasanjo asked Ogbeh to resign as PDP chairman while both of them were having a meal of pounded yam. Ogbeh was said to have been bold at condemning the injustice and lawlessness in the PDP.
After Ogbeh's resignation, Obasanjo went for broke and recruited Ahmadu Ali, a retired Military Officer to run the affairs of the party. Unlike his predecessor, however, Ali's relationship with ex-President remained cordial and he served out his tenure in 2007.
With Yar'Adua as President, the PDP chairmanship post got zoned to the South-East from the north central and Vincent Ogbulafor emerged to succeed Ali. Many had however thought that with the end of Obasanjo's administration, occupants of PDP chairmanship position would no longer be tossed around and removed at will. But that was not to be.
After a three-year reign, Ogbulafor was forced to resign under controversial circumstances.
Former Governor of Enugu State, Okwesilieze Nwodo was brought in to replace Ogbulafor as ratified by the National Executive Committee (NEC) of the party despite protest by some members of the party against his candidature. Nwodo's voyage as the PDP helmsman, however, came to an abrupt end on January 18, this year, after he was suspected to have surreptitiously supported Atiku Abubakar, the main challenger to Jonathan for the party's presidential ticket.
It is against this background that perceptive Nigerians are yet to understand what exactly could be the case with the exalted position that is currently occupied by Bello Haliru Mohammed in acting capacity. Observers feel that perhaps it may make sense for the PDP to accept the suggestion of Gemade by allowing the next chairman to emerge via free and fair process in a properly conducted national convention.
There are however, those who still believe that it may not really make any difference.
Mohammed Abdulrahman, Kogi State PDP Vice Chairman said: "Gemade may be right to some extent, given that those that were elected did not last on the seat. Another thing however is that the chairmen themselves don't reach out to members whenever they assume office. That is why the members would not be able to do anything whenever they are removed".
Born on September 4, 1948, Gemade, had held many prominent offices in the past. He was the chairman of the defunct Congress for National Consensus (CNC). He was also the Chief Executive Officer (CEO) of the Benue Cement Company (BCC) from 1985 to 1992. He was a member of the 1994 -1995 Constitutional Conference.

Original_Search_Title: The Glamorous West African Gala Night
Headline: Kenya: The Glamorous West African Gala Night
Body_Text:
On Saturday night, there was pomp and colour at the Intercontinental Hotel in Nairobi as our West African brothers and sisters held a gala night. The proceeds from the event will be used to increase the number of girls under the 'Girl Child Education Programme'.
It was a stunning affair held under the banner of the West African Women Trust Fund, a group started 14 years ago in Nairobi whose membership includes women from Nigeria, Ghana, Cameroon and Ivory Coast. Its aim was to share their cultures with their Kenyan friends and since then, the annual gala night has evolved into a cultural experience that gives an insight into the rich heritage of various traditions, customs, food, music, fashion and dance of the West African people.
With sponsorship from Kenya Airways, the High Commissions of Ghana and Nigeria, East Africa Breweries Ltd, Ecobank, Bank of Africa, UNHCR among others, the event was spectacular with plenty of exotic dishes on offer. On the menu was the popular pounded yam also known as 'fufu', goat soup also known as 'pepe soup', Atieke food from Ivory Coast, Chicken Yassa from Senegal and Egusi Stew from Nigeria.The ladies and gentlemen in attendance were clad in the signature West African clothing.

Original_Search_Title: Jonathan Must Do Just One Term
Headline: Nigeria: Jonathan Must Do Just One Term
Body_Text:
Yes, you heard me right: President Goodluck Jonathan must do only one term and quit in 2015. Yes, I am aware that by right he is entitled to two terms, going by Section 137 (1) of the Constitution which says: "A person shall not be qualified for election to the office of President if... (b) he has been elected to such office at any two previous elections."
A President is elected, of course, in a general election. Jonathan only became president by default last year after the death of President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua, so this is the first time he has been elected president. Yes, I'm aware that he has promised to do only one term and go. But Jonathan is a politician. By my training, I am sceptical, even cynical, with politicians. Their words are not their bond. Politicians!
When Chief Olusegun Obasanjo was being pressured to run for president in 1999, he turned it down, saying: "How many presidents do you want to make out of me? I've been as high as possible (military head of state, 1976-79) and as low as I can be (prisoner, 1995-98)." Guess what? Obasanjo not only agreed to run, he started planning for second term as soon as he tasted power again.
He did not stop at that; he went for a third term that failed spectacularly as he unsuccessfully attempted to change the constitution to suit his whims. So, politicians are something else. Ordinarily, Jonathan shouldn't have contested in the last election because of the "zoning" (power rotation) arrangement in his party, the PDP, but the death of Yar'Adua had inevitably altered the equation and I would have been surprised, pleasantly, if he had relinquished his right to run.
Many have argued, convincingly in some instances, that Jonathan's decision to run had stoked tension and contributed to the carnage in the far North in the aftermath of the presidential election. Going for another term in 2015 could, therefore, further damage the Nigerian union, in the opinion of some analysts. I've even heard people say over and over again that the US has predicted that Nigeria would break up in 2015.
The prophecy is about to be fulfilled, they enthuse-or lament. (For the record, the U.S. National Intelligence Council commissioned a study which report was released by the CIA in 2005. One of the experts suggested that Nigeria could fail in 10 years, given the economic and political mismanagement of the country). The post-election mayhem has forced a thinking that the doomsday is around the corner.
I am not suggesting that Jonathan should do only one term because I am afraid that Nigeria could break up if he decides to go for a second term. That could well be a possibility, but I'm not even interested in that. Rather, having taken a look at the Nigerian situation and the failure of leadership over the decades, I am inclined to believe that the second-term syndrome is largely responsible for the poor performance of our leaders. My argument is not going to be fool-proof, but if we take a second look with an open mind, we cannot easily discount it. It would come up with its own problems, but one problem is better than the other.
This is how the current system works. A governor or president is elected into office. He did not win the election by his might alone. A lot of people worked for him. Politicians and godfathers worked for him. Businessmen and women contributed financially to his campaign. So the governor or the president spends the first four years settling political IOUs. For the president especially, his first cabinet is usually made up of those who lost governorship or parliamentary elections. It is called compensation. So the cabinet is not primed to perform ab initio; it is just a "thank you" team to appease and comfort supporters. For instance, the PDP candidates who lost governorship elections in 1999 ended up in Obasanjo's first cabinet. That administration was brought in dead.
Second, the president or governor, as soon as he is sworn in, begins to plan for second term. He does not want to be beholden to any financier again, so he begins to build a war chest. This is usually done through slush funds. Contracts are insanely inflated; some are never executed. Corruption rises to high heavens. Most of the contracts are awarded to fronts and cronies. What we end up with is a situation where more and more funds leave the government treasury while less and less projects get executed. The general idea is that the money will eventually be used to buy up the votes and compromise opinion and community leaders. Therefore, the issue of performance in office is not always the matter to be discussed at election times; it is usually who has brought out the most "resources" to purchase the votes.
Third, a president or governor eyeing a second term may not perform very well because of the belief that there would be a second chance for him to redeem himself. So he spends precious time appeasing his godfathers and amassing campaign funds with the belief that even if he does not deliver the goods at first sitting, he will "repeat" and spend the remaining four years getting it right. We end up with four precious years being wasted in the life of a nation or state. One of the most tragic aspects is that the second term may even be worse as the governor is also distracted by public disenchantment with his performance in office and may spend the remaining years fighting his enemies to protect his "legacy".
Based on these three reasons, I would humbly suggest to Jonathan that he should make up his mind to do only one term so that he can deliver once and for all. It would be to the benefit of all. The first thing he would be able to do, if he has made up his mind to leave in 2015, is that he will be free to choose a cabinet of proven performers. Nigerians are watching him. He has won an election with an overwhelming spread across the country. Whether or not people voted for him out of sentiments would not matter any longer after May 29; all Nigerians want is good governance. His choice of cabinet members will be the first yardstick to judge how far he wants to go. A wishy-washy team and Nigerians would give up on him from day one.
I'm a realist-politics is about interests. People don't support you for nothing. Those who invested time, money and material in Jonathan's campaign will surely want something in return. I know that very well. Those who worked their heads off during the campaign did not do it for God's sake; they expect a reward. And I will say there is nothing wrong in having full-time politicians in the cabinet. It is normal. It is to be expected. However, there are ministries that are too sensitive to be left to these jobbers. They can still be compensated with ministerial appointments, but it should not be core ministries. The success of any administration depends on the performance of certain ministries, notably finance, education, petroleum, health and works. There are others I have not listed here. Jonathan would be making the mistake of his life if he settles political IOUs with sensitive ministries. Even if he is going to appoint politicians, it must be those who have something to offer.
Furthermore, there are tough decisions that Jonathan may not be able to take if he is thinking ahead of re-election. If he makes up his mind to do only one term, he would be ready to "die once and for all". Some of these decisions require political will. They include damning the consequences, as long as it is in the national interest. One of such is the power sector. We are tired of darkness. Whether it is through privatisation or public investment, Nigerians badly need electricity. We don't care who is providing it as long as we can chase away darkness from our midst. If NEPA or PHCN or whatever it is called has to be sold for us to start living in the modern age, so be it. It is none of my business. Let there be light, please!
Finally, we need political reforms. We need far-reaching restructuring of the country in a way that the states and councils will be stronger and Nigerians will feel government the more.
We need institutional reforms so that accountability will be a given rather than an aberration. Indeed, I crave a constitutional reform to give the governors and the president a maximum of one term of five or six years for the reasons I have listed above.
This particular amendment can take effect from 2015, so it will be a new dispensation in which Jonathan himself will not be a beneficiary. Who knows, it may even take care of the anxiety and emotion of power rotation.
Power goes back and forth every five or six years. In that case, it will soon go round and there should be less carnage in the land.
My proposal is not without its own flaws; if a governor or president is hopeless, spending only one term would not solve his problem. But at least we can get rid of him after five years rather than get stuck for eight years. That's the point.
And Four Other Things...
Kaduna's Bitter Division
I've been hearing of the bitter internal division in Kaduna along ethno-religious lines, much to my discomfort. In fact, the capital city is effectively divided into two: one for Hausa/Fulani Muslims and the other for the rest, namely Christians and Southern Muslims. If we think this is not a cause for worry, we must be joking.
The results of the presidential and governorship elections in the state have further laid credence to the claim that it is a sharply divided state. Look at the figures: Muhammadu Buhari (CPC) 1,334,244 votes, Goodluck Jonathan (PDP) 1,190,179 in the presidential election; Patrick Yakowa (PDP) 1,339,487 votes, Haruna Kajuru (CPC) 1,114,987 in the governorship poll.
Look closely at the turnout and the figures for the two top candidates in each election. You'll discover that the state is almost 50-50. Fiercely 50-50. I hope I'm not being alarmist, but there is a long way to go to unite this state. I'm worried. Over to you, Kaduna leaders.
ACN's Resurgence
The biggest gainer in the 2011 general election is the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN), without any doubt, with all but one South-west state now under its care. But, really, it is a resurgence. ACN (initially called AC) actually rose from the ashes of Alliance for Democracy (AD)-which still pretends to exist-in 2006 following a re-strategising on the part of Asiwaju Bola Ahmed Tinubu and his associates. AD used to be the party of the South-west, clearing the six states in the 1999 governorship and presidential elections and taking 17 out of the 18 Senate seats. The ill-fated alliance with the PDP in 2003-meant to help a Yoruba, President Olusegun Obasanjo, win re-election-turned out to be AD/ACN's greatest undoing as Obasanjo used the opportunity to "deliver" the South-west to the PDP. However, the ACN governors would soon discover that only performance will matter at the end of the day. If they don't perform better than the PDP governors they displaced, then they should expect a backlash in the next elections.
Sanusi's N150k Gamble
I want to buy cows worth N200,000 or tipperloads of sand worth N300,000. From July 2012, according to the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN), I will have to devise a way of paying for this because I will not be allowed to withdraw more than N150,000 from my account in a day. Put some other way, it means the maximum cash you can take from your account on any given day is N150,000 (or N1 million if it is a corporate body). The CBN wants to encourage cashless or electronic instruments such as PoS (via what we call ATM cards), money transfer, cheques and drafts. The CBN governor, Mallam Sanusi Lamido Sanusi, may not like to hear this, but I think he has just pushed a policy that may increase the population of the unbanked. The Nigerians I know would rather keep their cash at home to do business. Meanwhile, with poor internet connection and frequent system breakdowns, how would the PoS work efficiently? Or will yam sellers start collecting cheques?
The Malaria Mess
The World Health Organisation (WHO) country representative to Nigeria, Dr David Okello, was quoted last week as saying the global body had prohibited the prescription and use of Artemisinin monotherapies and other less efficacious medicines such as Artesunate, Chloroquine and Amalar to treat malaria. On phone with me last night, the Information Officer of WHO Nigeria Office, Dr. Laokun Soyinka, said none of the drugs is banned; in fact, Amalar is actually good during pregnancy. What has happened is that to prevent resistance to a particular drug, WHO recommends combined therapy (ACT) rather than monotherapy. Of course, Chloroquine has become useless; using it is like giving orange juice to malaria.

Original_Search_Title: The Good, Bad And Ugly Sides of Elections Coverage in Benue
Headline: Nigeria: The Good, Bad And Ugly Sides of Elections Coverage in Benue
Body_Text:
Makurdi — Covering the April general elections in Benue, the acclaimed "Food Basket of Nigeria", was both challenging and interesting for me. Challenging because having come from the North-Central State, I was caught between standing on the side of professionalism and facing the challenge of being accused of being subjective in my reporting.
On the other hand, I found the assignment interesting because it provided me with temporary relief from the strains and stress of head office work. Apart from covering the elections from the political reporting perspective of hard news, there were also the side attractions that are usually common with both the theatre and theatrics of Benue politics.
It is against this backdrop that I have chosen to relay my three weeks experience in Benue from the perspective of the good the bad and the ugly.
The Good: It was indeed home coming for a reporter whose professional sojourn had taken me very far away from home. The sight of friends, family members and professional colleagues made me feel at 'home'. There was also the pleasure for me to, once again, patronize my favourite delicacy: pounded yam and rat meat soup which I had missed over the years.
It was also a good experience finding myself not only giving situation reports but also reporting on the outcome of elections in my State; elections which had drawn so much hype, attention, excitement and even high expectations from within Benue and elsewhere in the country.
The Bad: Benue State is christened as Food Basket of the Nation. But the basket also contains 'basket mouths' of speculations and rumours; especially during political electioneering seasons. This was manifest during the period of my three weeks stay in Benue during the April polls.
For instance while in Oturkpo, country home of Senate President, David Mark, during the National Assembly elections that were rescheduled, I had more than an overdose of the Benue rumour mill. No sooner had accreditation started even without the commencement of voting than rumours started making the 'airwaves' that the Senate President had been defeated by his opponent. Before long the 'news' of Mark's defeat was online on Facebook. Political editors from Abuja and Lagos started inundating visiting reporters to Benue, including yours sincerely, with calls about this 'anticipated' major political upset in the Food Basket State.
There were also the loathsome, even frustrating period of waiting for long hours and sometimes keeping forced vigil at the collation centres to get elections results- which in some cases ended up not being used- as well as bitter encounters with overzealous; stern faced security operatives.
One particular incident with security operatives at the INEC office Makurdi, easily comes to my mind. On the eve of the National Assembly election, I went to the INEC office in the company of my visiting colleagues from the Punch, Sun and Tribune to get an update of preparations for the polls.
At the gate, we were prevented from entering the premises by gun wielding mobile Policemen on grounds that we were not carrying our INEC accredited Press tags. Even when we showed them individual identity cards of our media organizations and explained to them that the tags were reserved for the Election Day proper, the red-eyed Policemen threatened to shoot if we dared them. Not even the intervention of the INEC PRO in the state calmed the nerves of these overzealous men in uniforms who told us to our faces that there was no difference between us and party thugs...
Then the ugly... the Nigerian political climates are theatres of the absurd, particularly during elections, and Benue state is no exception. From Makurdi, the state capital to Oturkpo in the southern zone to Gboko in the Northern zone, there were much ugly political incidents such as ballot box snatching and abduction of INEC staff by political thugs.
I witnessed two ugly incidents during the period under review that are unforgettable. Interestingly, both incidents took place right within the precincts of government House, Makurdi.
Case number one: A party youth suspected to be an ACN supporter approached the gate of government House one evening. Consciously or otherwise, he raised his two fingers in the air and muttered in Tiv: "Shoo chanji"- the slogan of the opposition party which translates into "the political dance in Benue has changed" with the emergence of the ACN.
Almost immediately, he was seized by a group of PDP youths who had made it a point of milling around at the gate, waiting for their share of "the PDP's national cake. Poor youth! He was beaten black and blue; almost to a state of unconsciousness before security operatives came to his rescue. It was difficult to tell whether or not he survived the attack...
Case number two: On the governorship election day, Gabriel Suswam and his wife, Yemisi, had gone to the Government House polling unit at about 11: 30 am for accreditation. As they made their way to their vehicle to go back and wait for the commencement of voting, a lady surged through the crowd that milled around and made towards the governor.
The lady who was crying on top of her voice defiled security and was about to get to Suswam before she was grabbed off by security operatives. When the governor eventually drove off, I became curious and sought to know who and why the young lady was rushing to get the governor's attention.
Her story was as unco-ordinated as it was funny. She had previously approached the governor for some financial assistance to undergo a surgery. According to her, Suswam gave her N350,000. But rather than go for the surgery, the lady who claimed to be a PDP supporter produced offensive handbills against the opposition ACN.
"When I produced the handbills I went to Judge's Quarters to distribute but I was attacked and beaten up by ACN supporters," she said. "I was even locked up at the police station for three days. I took a chartered taxi to see the governor in government house but when I went inside the taxi driver whom I asked to wait for me by the gate drove away with my bag containing the remaining money the governor earlier gave me. This is why I came to see him so that he can give me more money...," said this lady who also claimed to be a student of UNIJOS.
After listening to her story, it was difficult to tell whether this lady's mind was sane or not; whether or not to report her story. But it was a story in the theatre of the absurd that climaxed my three weeks of election coverage in Benue during which I encountered the good, the bad and the ugly sides of Benue's political history.

Original_Search_Title: Political Economy at the Time of Goodluck
Headline: Nigeria: Political Economy at the Time of Goodluck
Body_Text:
It ought not, on the surface, be a source of concern. We are referring here to the hearty handshakes, back slapping and the stampede to be the first to congratulate the President after his victory in the recently concluded presidential elections. However, there was an eerie sense of déjà vu about the economic consequences of the gathering at the presidential villa in Abuja last week.
For right there, doing the congratulations, were all manner of representatives of special interests groups. There is of course nothing particularly wrong par se in being the arrowhead of a special interest or pressure group. What is disconcerting is when it is put into the context of Nigeria's current political economic framework. It is within this context that there appears to be something to be perturbed about, even something that is perhaps untoward.
The anxiety about the raison d'être of the gathering is because Nigeria, as this newspaper has often pointed out in the course of the last decade, lacks a formalized competition framework. For this reason, it is one of the great contradictions of the Nigerian firmament over the last two and a half decades that the country has attempted to move to a 'market driven economy' without pro-competition and fair trading laws. The contradiction in this proposition is glaring. For if you do not have a competition framework backed up by a legally established anti-monopoly and fair trading regulatory agency, all sorts of problems come up.
For a start, what you will end up practicing will be not so much a 'market-driven' economy but a variant of state capitalism. This is what the great afro-beat exponent and astute observer of social trends, the late Fela Anikulapo Kuti, memorably described as 'paddy-paddy government'. As the experience of the past 25 years has shown, paddy-paddy government or cronyism does not aid but actually distorts the operations of a market economy.
Without strongly enforced competition laws the market is distorted. More debilitating is what amounts in effect to short changing the consumer. The distortion means that prices are fixed, innovation stifled and new or potential entrants have a mountain and more to climb. This cannot be anyone's interpretation of what constitutes a market driven economy. This is why, it is to be hoped, that the gathering in Aso Rock on the hour of victory will not be interpreted as a pointer to what the Jonathan presidency holds in store. It is even more disconcerting when put in the framework of the proposition which is now being accepted as a conventional wisdom, that the 2011 Nigerian presidential election is the most expensive ever held in Africa. There may be an element of hyperbole in this but there could also be a grain of truth.
What is clearly disconcerting here is the glaring lack of any attempt at all to, even perfunctorily, observe the campaign finance guidelines. Everyone, participant and umpire alike, have simply ignored section 91 of the 2010 electoral act. It has to be wearily observed that unlike in the previous presidential elections where luncheons and dinners were held as ways of raising funds, the 2011 presidential candidates hardly solicited for funds using such platforms. They have moved away from dependence on individual donors and looked up to corporate bodies for support instead.
This is against the grain of what we would have hoped will be the trend in the Obama age. If we care to recall, Barack Obama as a candidate raised record amounts of financial support relying on small individual donors. This must be a source of concern. For the conflict of interests manifested in the Aso Rock gathering is unambiguous. How do we shield a president armed with his own mandate from this potential conflict of interest? Placed within the context of the gathering, are we going to see policies enforced or geared towards seeing an end to the importation of refined petroleum products? Should we within this context expect to see an end to round tripping, usirous interest rates and the manipulation of the financial and stock markets? How will the overbearing self interests of special interests groups affect or, should we say, distort the president's anticipated initiatives in the electricity, rail, infrastructure and other sectors? This question needs an urgent response.
This is therefore a clarion call to alert a president armed with a fresh mandate that he if does not rein in the special interests groups and put the national interest above all others, then there will be none of the expected and hoped-for paradigm shift. Here we may borrow from the great Islamic scholar, Ibn Tahamud, and his allegory of the interplay about the changing nomenclature of the man in power. If, before the elections, Dr. Goodluck Jonathan was the 'prophet unarmed', he is now, courtesy of a pan-Nigerian mandate, 'the prophet armed'. Therefore since he now holds both the yam and the knife at all times the interest of the nation must override that of a self seeking cabal. Dr. Jonathan now represents the symbol of the hopes and aspirations of the people.
In conclusion we call upon the president to ensure a level playing field for all. He must induce legislative initiatives to undo all the anti competition bugbears distorting the operations of a real market economy. Only this course of action can create jobs and develop the 'real sectors'. It is an historical burden and at the same a course of action which he has to take. It is only through this that the Goodluck in terms of prosperity can spread to all Nigerians.

Original_Search_Title: Will Post Election Violence Affect Easter Celebrations?
Headline: Nigeria: Will Post Election Violence Affect Easter Celebrations?
Body_Text:
Shortly after basking on the euphoria of success recorded in last Saturday's presidential elections, it dawned on us that most Nigerians have little or no knowledge that Easter was just round the corner, two days to the forthcoming gubernatorial elections. With the heat of post election violence just cooling off in some parts of Northern Nigeria, many people seem less apprehensive of the Easter period, let alone preparing towards its celebration. LEADERSHIP WEEKEND's Aliyu Mohammed Baba and Victoria Ozohu Mayaki investigate the views of Nigerians to the fiesta.
At the onset it looked like a mirage, that the 2011 general election would record success given the period of just nine months provided for its preparations. On the contrary and against all odds, the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) under the leadership of its chairman, Professor Atahiru Jega, amidst anticipated fears from his critics proved them wrong with the successes recorded so far with one more to go.
Meanwhile, Nigeria had received commendations both locally and internationally for the peaceful conduct of elections so far, and even though the election outcomes were preceded with several reactions, most observers (locally and internationally) still score the process high and applaud the attitude and peaceful conduct of Nigerian electorates. In various comments, Nigeria is specially acknowledged as culminating towards a stable democracy.
However, despite the immediate intervention by the country's security agents to curb the post election reactions across the affected states, the societal disturbance caused is presumed by many political pundits to affect both the Easter celebrations and gubernatorial elections slated for Tuesday, 26th April, 2011. One indication to the anticipated affects will be a low turn-out by families (especially those in the north) to travel home to celebrate the event as against what's obtained in non-electioneering years.
Mr. Wale, a motor mechanic residing at Lugbe district expressed dissatisfaction with the crisis and allayed fears of government's probable inability to appropriately calm the situation before the commencement of the gubernatorial polls. When asked if he was preparing for the Easter celebration, he was quick to answer that his family lives in Kaduna and he had already communicated with them that he would not be coming home for the festive period. Similarly, Mrs. Caroline who is a cosmetics trader at Kuje in Abuja also disclosed she intended to travel to her hometown for the Easter break, but with the security situation on ground, she had changed her mind and would rather remain in Abuja for the break.
Majority of the people showed strong willingness to participate in the gubernatorial elections, and were more ready than ever to defy the Easter celebrations if need be.
Mr. Simon, a native of Kafanchan in Kaduna State, and supporter of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) expressed his strong willingness to participate in the gubernatorial polls, showing optimism that the curfew will be lifted before the election day. In his words: "Though I am a Christian, the situation of the country is more dear than the Easter celebrations. Even if I am given five minutes freedom from the curfew, I would make sure I reach my hometown to vote. At least the government would give us the chance to exercise our franchise." Also, Mr. Jude, a commercial driver who plies Abuja to Kaduna route, indicated his intent to hike transport fares as soon as the Easter season steps in. When quizzed about his reasons, he answered, "Don't you know I am putting myself at risk by plying the route in this period of crisis? That is why I have to include the cost of the risk."
For the Easter pricelist prospects, a survey at the Wuse central market showed that prices of food commodities are currently on the hike. Mallam Dahiru who sells yam said a tuber of yam is sold at N500 now. He associated the hike to the Easter season. This is virtually similar with other food items. A rubber basket of tomatoes now sold at N1,200, a bag of rice, N8,400 and a bag of beans, N12,000. Whatever the situation may be, the Easter celebration has come and must be celebrated in whatever little way.

Original_Search_Title: Long-Term Investment Faces Increasing Hurdles - Report
Headline: Tanzania: Long-Term Investment Faces Increasing Hurdles - Report
Body_Text:
The ability to make long-term investments is diminishing at a time when the need for long-term capital is significantly increasing, the World Economic Forum (WEF) has said in a new report titled: The Future of Long-term Investing.
The report says that in 2009, long-term institutional asset owners held slightly under half of the world's professionally managed assets - about $27 trillion out of $65 trillion. The report finds that long-term investors as a whole have been able to allocate only 25 per cent of their capital to long-term investments, and this figure is likely to fall as a result of the economic crisis and increasing regulation.
In a statement emailed to The Citizen on Sunday late last month, WEF says Long-term investors should work to better address principal-agent misalignment in their investment process. It quotes WEF's associate director for media, Kai Bucher, saying that increasing constraints to long-term investing will limit the availability of long-term capital in the global economy.
"Long-term capital is more necessary than ever to fund important needs (e.g. infrastructure, innovation and the transition to a low-carbon economy); it acts as a counter-cyclical force in financial markets and can impact the time horizon of corporate managers," the statement reads in part.
The report finds that there are considerable internal challenges that must be overcome when making long-term investments. Many institutions have material short-term financial obligations that must be funded with short-term investments.
Furthermore, investors must overcome organizational conservatism and principal-agency considerations to make long-term decisions. These additional internal constraints make funding difficult to find for projects with short-term losses but potentially significant long-term gains.
