"This report highlights the significant barriers that must be overcome in order to execute an effective long-term investing strategy and the broader economic implications of those constraints," said Mr Tony Tan Keng-Yam, deputy chairman and executive director, Government of Singapore Investment Corporation, and chair of the steering committee for the World Economic Forum's Long-term Investing project.
In addition to internal challenges, there are significant regulatory pressures that also impact the ability to make long-term investments. For instance, the report notes, mark-to market accounting and strict capital requirements can limit the ability to make long-term investments and promote pro-cyclical behaviour on the part of the long-term investors.

Original_Search_Title: Ringo, Tuku to Wow Durbanites
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ringo, Tuku to Wow Durbanites
Body_Text:
AWARD-WINNING Zimbabwean guitarist-vocalist Oliver "Tuku" Mtukudzi and local muso Ringo Madlingozi are expected to wow Durban fans on Thursday.
The two will be performing at a concert to celebrate the 31st anniversary of Zimbabwe's independence.
The respected musicians will be performing together for the first time in a concert at the Olive Convention Centre (Ice Rink).
The gig starts at 8pm.
The aim of the concert is also to promote unity and solidarity among South Africans and Zimbabweans living in the country.
Ringo had fans begging for more just two weeks ago when he shared the stage with Lira at the Durban International Convention Centre.
It is expected that fans will be delighted to see the two veterans performing the hit collaboration of Into yam.
They will both have their own full bands live on stage.
Mtukudzi's career spans more than three decades, with 15 albums which have earned him a number of awards including the Kora award for the Best Arrangement in 2003 and the National Arts Merit Award (NAMA) in 2002.

Original_Search_Title: Chief of Tamale, Naa Dakpema Alhassan Dawuni
Headline: Ghana: Chief of Tamale, Naa Dakpema Alhassan Dawuni
Body_Text:
It is all well and good that the police administration has hinted of reviewing all the evidence in respect of the murder of Ya-Na Yakubu Andani II. The failure to identify those behind the heinous murder of the Overlord of the Dagbon Traditional Area is a blot on the conscience of this nation.
The Chronicle welcomes every attempt to identify the murderers and bring them to book, as stated by the Inspector General of Police, Paul Tawiah Quaye, in an interview with state-run Daily Graphic. We hope that in trying to find the murderers and nail them, the police would not allow themselves to be caught in the political game that officials of Prof. John Evans Atta Mills' administration have played with the heinous crime.
We would like to believe that the police would, this time, be more professional and not kowtow to the whims and caprices of officialdom, who are using the incident to shore up support in the northern half of the country.
It has been obvious from the way the crime was packaged in the National Democratic Congress (NDC) manifesto, to attempt to give their opponents bloody noses in the Dagbon area, by exploiting the unfortunate incident to the party's electoral advantage that politics has taken over the fight for justice.
For instance, the whole exercise has been conducted as if the other side to the dispute does not matter. We would like to believe that the exercise at establishing the truth could only be conducted when there is an open mind. That is why the Abudu gate should also be engaged in the meticulous effort to establish the truth. In any case, reports indicate that the Abudu's also lost some of their members in the mayhem.
We would like to caution the Inspector General of Police to insulate the police from Government sentiment in this fresh attempt to find the killers. It is being whispered in private, and the word doing the rounds about this Inspector General, is that he has been too willing to do the bidding of the NDC administration, which is why the image of the police, as an independent professional body, is taking a battering.
More than two years after some traders at the Agbogbloshie Yam Market were butchered in the aftermath of the NDC victory in the 2008 presidential and parliamentary elections, the Inspector General of Police and his officers have largely remained uninterested in the sordid deed. There have been instances when complaints of serious crimes on the campaign trail have been ignored by the police administration. A check on the alleged backgrounds of the perpetrators have invariably pointed to their association with the ruling NDC.
This stance, we dare state, has affected the image of our peace officers dearly. We would like the Inspector General of Police to use the opportunity for a fresh enquiry into the Ya-Na's murder, to restore confidence in the Ghanaian that the police, as an institution, is not doing any politician's bidding.
We hope that with the campaign for the 2012 Presidential and Legislative elections, unofficially kicking off, with one of the NDC candidates using his incumbency as head of state of this Republic to tour the regions campaigning in the name of commissioning projects, the police would be on their guard.
Evidence emerging on the political terrain, indicates that there will be more at stake in the run-up to the election than originally bargained for. That is reason for the IGP and his men and women to stand up and be counted.

Original_Search_Title: Rainstorm Causes Havoc At Suhum
Headline: Ghana: Rainstorm Causes Havoc At Suhum
Body_Text:
A heavy rainstorm that greeted the capital of the Suhum Kraboa Coaltar District Assembly (SKCAD), Suhum, on Sunday, around 2:30 p.m., has left in its wake, glaring signs of massive destruction.
The rain, which lasted for almost two hours, destroyed items, ranging from household appliances such as televisions, fridges to food among other physical materials.
About 30 houses, chop bars, drinking spots, among other physical infrastructure, were not spared by the storm.
The thousands of residents, who have been rendered homeless, could not help, but shed uncontrollable tears, and as they counted their losses at the time of visit of the Eastern File.
One area, which was badly affected by the rain, was the Suhum central market, where goods such as second hand clothing, yam, and books among other valuable items running into thousands of Ghana cedis, have been washed away.
Even though no life was lost, it was reported that one person, who was severely injured and taken to the Suhum Government Hospital for treatment, was later discharged.
Speaking to a cross-section of media practitioners shortly after a three hour tour around the affected areas, the Member of Parliament (MP) for the Suhum constituency, Frederick Opare-Ansah, thanked God that even though thousands of valuable items had been destroyed, no life was lost during the event.
According to him, beyond the immediate situation, what was extremely worrying was the tendency of the area to experience an outbreak of cholera, since the rain deposited unhygienic materials into the flooded houses of some residents.
To this end, the MP, who is also the Minority Chief Whip in the House of Parliament, assured the residents of his readiness to provide them with some items to ease their plight, and called on the Assembly, the National Disaster Management Organisation (NADMO) and other organisations to come to the aid of the affected people.
On his part, the District Chief Executive (DCE) of the area, Samuel Kwabi, also gave the assurance of the preparedness of his office to assist the affected persons.
He made the promise when he earlier visited the area with his team, including NADMO officials, some members of the Assembly and the District Police Command, to ascertain the extent of damage, shortly after the rain subsided.
The DCE was quick to appeal to residents of the area to desist from building in low-lying areas to avert such disasters.
According to him, it was disheartening when residents politicised development initiated by governments to improve and avert certain preventable natural phenomena, adding that disasters did not know any political party, and were no respecter of persons or political colours.
Thus, he launched a passionate appeal to them to support the Assembly ensure that regulations governing building and establishments were duly followed, in order to create an enabling working environment for all.

Original_Search_Title: At Mile 12 Market, It is 24 Hour Service
Headline: Nigeria: At Mile 12 Market, It is 24 Hour Service
Body_Text:
Time does not really matter at Mile 12 market, easily one of the most cosmopolitan and biggest markets in Lagos.Whether it is 2 a.m, 4 p.m or 11 p.m ,it is business as always Yams from Benue,Niger,Ebonyi and Kogi States; potatoes from Plateau; pepper and tomatoes from Kano,Sokoto,Katsina,Taraba and Kebbi; fruits from Adamawa and Niger; fish from Delta, Akwa Ibom and Borno; name them.
Neither the heavy vehicular traffic nor the muddy environment is sufficient to discourage the hundres of buyers that troop there daily to make purchases. The comparatively bargain prices are the major attraction.
As you make to enter the market, you are bound to encounter the ubiquitous alabarus (local carriers)holding tenaciuosly to their baskets of various sizes, and in some cases, wheelbarrows to carry your goods to your car or bus.
They are also willing to take you to where, in their opinion, you will get a good bargain, whatever quantity you want to buy.
There are also those offering you a pair of rain boots for a fee to enable you traverse the length and breath of the mashy environment, especially during the rainy season.
The many trucks carrying goods from across the country to the market are able to discharge only at night because of the heavy traffic on the Ketu-Ikorodu road.
Waiting for them are scores of buyers also from different parts of the country who want to make purchases and arrange for transportation before returning to their different bases.
A leader of the Association of Tomato Sellers, Alhaji Lawal Bihiya Adamu, throwing light on the mode of operations at the market said: "We have different types of trucks coming from different parts of the country on a daily basis, even at mid-night.
"Because our items are perishable goods, it is difficult for trucks to move during the day. Most of the goods are brought in at night, and there are people standing by to off-load while some buyers are also waiting to buy.
"We have goods coming from within the country as well as from neighbouring countries. For instance, tomatoes come from Kano, Zaria, Kastina, and Plateau while onions come from Niger Republic, Cameroun, Lake Chad."
An 'Alabaru' on duty and a taxi cab loaded with tomatoes at the market
The market was relocated from Iddo some 36 years ago when it was getting inadequate for the volume of transactions taking place. Even now, Mile 12 has become rather too small. The market association is in the process of constructing a motor park worth N80 million so that trucks can move freely.
About three years ago, traders at the market embarked on a strike to protest what they called outrageous revenue collection by agents of government, causing scarcity of food items. Alhaji Wadata Musa, Vice-Chairman, Onions Sellers Association, however says the matter has now been resolved.
Alhaji Wasiu Bilam, Chairman of Ifesowapo Association of Yam Sellers said business gets difficult during bthe rainy season.
Another challenge is the extortion of money from truck drivers on the highways by government agents. "They make us operate as if we are moving from one border to another. We pay more on the road and that is why there is increase in the prices of foodstuffs."

Original_Search_Title: Relief as Father Reunites With Family After Four-Month Captivity
Headline: Tanzania: Relief as Father Reunites With Family After Four-Month Captivity
Body_Text:
March 18 would forever be etched in the mind of businessman John Senzige and members of his close knit family.
It was the day his wife Tatu, 40, and six of their children breathed a sigh of relief for seeing him back home and alive.
For four dark months, Mr Senzige remained in bondage, in the Indian Ocean, under the watchful eye of murderous Somali pirates. Together with 20 other hostages, 14 of whom were Tanzanians, they prayed day and night to live to see their loved ones again.
On Saturday, the Senzige's congregated at his Kigogo CCM residence in Dar es Salaam for thanksgiving, just three days after he stepped on Tanzanian soil following their hijack on October 31 last year.
For a moment, the marry making took away the uncertainty and suffering that the family underwent with the absence of their sole bread winner. The 50 year old Senzige is a trader, plying between the city and Comoro where he sold cereals and other food staffs.
"We are happy as a family that he is back and in the comfort of his home. The long lonely and anxious days we spent waiting are now behind us," said a delighted Tatu.
The woman said she had been worried about the safety of her husband when it was reported that the pirates had shot dead one of the hostages and tossed the body in the water."During his absence one of the children was sent away from college for lack of Sh400, 000 in fees. It immediately downed on me that the burden to raise the family alone will be unbearable."
Ms Tatu said they were also pleased for the rest of the families whose loved ones shared the ordeal in the sea with Mr Senzige.With the shock behind his back, the father of six children now faces the challenge of finding his foot and continuing with his usual business.
"It is however going to be a hard task for me because I lost everything I had to the pirates. They took all our money and destroyed business contracts that I carried with me," he narrated while opting to keep the lost amount a secret.
He said however he is undeterred and will try to pick up despite the setback. He said he usually exported onions, tobacco, yams and Irish potatoes to Comoros and brought back a few items that he sold locally.
That fateful Sunday, passengers aboard MV Zulfikar, were hijacked by the Somali pirates along Mafia coast en-route to Dar es Salaam from Comoro.
Mr Senzige said there were a total of 21 passengers and eight crew members, including one who was shot dead by the pirates whose activities in the region has severely affected shipping industry and raised the cost of doing business due to insecurity.
The pirates told them that they were heading to Somalia and would hold the ship until its owner paid a ransom amounting to $15m (more than Sh21 billion). Mr Senzige said that they were all kept in one room on their ship, fed on one meal per day, and if they were lucky given a cup of water.
The ship owner declined paying the ransom, claiming that the ship was seized on the Tanzania coast. As such, he said, the government should carry the burden. Failure to get response from the owner made the pirates decide that the ship should be used in raising the ransom.
"They told us that because we are poor and cannot raise the demanded amount, we should help them in hijacking other ships as a way of collecting the ransom," narrated Mr Senzige.
During the time when they were held captive, they hijacked about nine ships. Their main tasks was loading and offloading weapons plus other products seized in other ships.
"Our daily routine was not specified as the pirates do not have specific plans, sometimes we slept the whole day while other days we were busy in the high seas searching for ships to hijack," said Mr Senzige. They also spent a considerable time in Somalia.
The Tanzanian is considered a hero because he and a crew captive enabled the ship to be rescued in Madagascar before the government sent a plane to fly them back home. Their luck came when the pirates run out of fuel during one of the roaming days.
Mr Senzige said they accompanied some of the pirates into the mainland to attempt to get fuel. While there, the crew member who knew to speak the foreign language used the opportunity to alert the Madagascan officials of their hijack.
"The officials immediately held the pirate whom we accompanied, gave us fuel and escorted us to the ship, where they captured other pirates who had remained in the ship," he added.He said that they made a call to the Comorian embassy in Malagasy which also arranged with the Tanzanian government on how they could be brought back to the country.
Mr Senzige urges the government to help them as everything which they had was taken away by the pirates and the businessmen do not know how to start again.

Original_Search_Title: How We Can Protect Our Votes - Amaechi
Headline: Nigeria: How We Can Protect Our Votes - Amaechi
Body_Text:
Penultimate Tuesday, Rivers State Governor, Rt. Hon Chibuike Rotimi Amaechi took the battle to retain his seat to Okrika, the homestead of his main opponent, Dr. Abiye Sekibo of the Action Congress of Nigeria, ACN. He spoke to reporters after the rally on why he is seeking re-election, his fears for the April polls and how to make the elections credible. Excerpts:
Rotimi Amaechi
Ahead of the polls, are you satisfied with the preparations of the Independent National Electoral Commission, INEC?
I am not in a position to tell you whether they are prepared or not. But do I have confidence in INEC? Yes! Will votes count? It will be dependent not on INEC but on the people. During my campaigns, I always tell people: when you vote, sit down there. When they finish counting, upload it in public glare so nobody will rig.
Rigging happens when people go home after voting. But if all of us finish voting and stay there, they will announce the result. If they say they are going to the local government, we will say we are following you and watch them hand over the results.
It happened under Babangida. How suddenly did we lose that? Even before the June 12, 1993 presidential election, immediately you pick the electoral materials everybody is following you. That time, there was no GSM and internet here but everybody knows the result.
They will follow you to the Local Government headquarters; you announce the result, they will follow you to the state. That was why then we did not have massive rigging we are having now. It was under Abacha's transition programme that people started rigging in a such a manner that it became embarrassing to the country.
As far as INEC is concerned, I think they are prepared for free and fair election but the people have their own role to play. I am preaching: 'Don't allow them rig us out. Vote, wait until they announce the result. When they announce the result follow it until the next turn and don't go until they announce the next one.' If we all do that throughout the country, there will be no rigging.
Don't you entertain any fears about the polls?
I have always talked about complacency especially for my own election. They say 'he is popular. The governor has done well, everybody likes him. My vote doesn't matter, so let me sleep.' If out of 2.4 million voters, 1.3 million do that and other 1.1 million vote for the other party, you can never say what would happen. So, what we are doing is telling people: come out and vote.
The second fear is the fact that some people still have doubt that votes won't count. There are those who say 'there will be too much insecurity, don't go and lose your life.' That is why I say the government has a responsibility to secure everybody's life.
There is a difference between Governor Amaechi and candidate Amaechi. Candidate Amaechi will be running the election, Governor Amaechi will still govern the state until May 29. Therefore, Governor Amaechi has a responsibility to ensure that lives and property are protected. So go out and vote, be rest assured of the protection of security agencies.
Across board what are the chances of your party, the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP at the polls?
I am not in a position to make that assessment. The only thing I can say is I doubt if any of the parties can match PDP. Are we going to lose ground? Only the governors can say what the situation looks like in their states. In Rivers State, we will do everything possible to mobilise the electorate to come out and vote.
By the grace of God, we will not lose ground in Rivers State. President Goodluck Jonathan will have nearly 100 per cent in Rivers State. I hope we will not lose ground in other states.
At the rally in Okrika, you said it was payback time for Okrika people. Could you clarify that statement?
I left University in 1987. By 1988, I started working with Chief Rufus Ada George and Dr. Peter Odili. Chief Ada-George was an aspirant in our camp running for governorship. Sergeant Awuse was an aspirant in the SDP and because Ada-George was from Okrika, my people felt that it was wrong for any Ikwere man to support an Okrika man But I dared it and supported an Okrika man and Chief Ada-George became governor.
One day, we went to elect officers of the party at the LGA level and I dared contest for secretaryship. They could not take that. When I arrived for the election, they began to beat me. I was admitted at a clinic. The following week, the election was on, I came, ran and won. So, that was one vote for Chief Ada-George because the secretary was an automatic delegate to the governorship primary.
They were surprised that I came after they beat me up the previous week. So, that is what I mean by pay back time. If I supported your son to be governor, it is your turn to support me and this time there will be no violence, nobody is beating them for supporting me.
Apart from supporting their son to be governor in the past, what have you done for Okrika since you assumed power?
You heard me speak, when I said let their son come and stand and tell us what he had done for Okrika. Their son was SSG and a minister. I say to people that today, I have done just two raods in my local government area as governor.
The second one is I am rebuilding the road in my village. The rest roads were done when I was a speaker. So as SSG, why didn't he attract those things to his community? The Ring Road we drove on was built by this government.
How do you see the challenge and opposition from that area?
Their son was launching a campaign in Port Harcourt. You would expect that by the time we arrived Okrika we would not see the kind of crowd that we met on ground. The fact that we saw that kind of crowd was a surprise to me. It was courageous that we hired NTA and AIT to show it live.
Were you actually expecting such a crowd?
Yes. You listened to them now. Was I the one that was saying they lost so many persons, there was no security in the past, etc? Okrika was a war zone. Chiefs were flogged, bishops were...I don't want to go into the details. You better ask them.
It was when I came that I imposed a curfew, chased away the criminals that they had trained and kept in the place, and went after their guns that they were liberated. You can ask them. You heard them speak, one after the other.
You heard the woman, who said they found it difficult going to ease themselves because of fear of being attacked. At the rally, a female member of the House of Assembly said she was disaffected in the PDP but would not leave the party.
What have you done to reconcile aggrieved members of the PDP?
The only member who was disaffected that we have not reconciled with is the former minister of Transport, Abiye Sekibo. Chief Austin Opara is still with us, he is the chairman of the reconciliation committee.
At a press conference in Lagos, Sekibo alleged that your government frittered away N980 billion of the state in three years...
You should ask him whether it was frittering away Eleme Junction that used to keep people lying down there till 3.a.m everyday for N7 billion or the mono-rail that is going under N49 billion or the secondary schools that we are building -now it is N4.5 billion because we are increasing the capacity to 1000 students?
We are building 24 of such schools and if I win a second term, I will build another 24 to make them 48. Anybody who sees it will say 'Oh, you can turn it into a university, Polytechnic, etc. I just showed a bank how our university is going to look like. They are doing the infrastructure work -water, roads, electricity, etc.
When they finish, we will start building.
Ask him, he lives in Abuja, he does not know what is going on in Port Harcourt. He just came back a few months ago to run for governorship. The difference between us is that when he served as SSG there was nothing called due process. Here, there is a law called Public Procurement Law, which we now refer to as due process. We hired the expert from a bank and said if you save us XYZ, you get XYZ, your job is to save money for us. If you apply due process and save XYZ, this is the commission for XYZ.
Tell him that part of the funds we are spending is as a result of his activities in Okrika. The Insecurity in the state started from Okrika and we are spending huge amount of money trying to protect the state. So if he didn't cause us that problem, we won't be spending money looking for criminals, buying equipment for the police, etc. Virtually all the vehicles that they are using, we bought. You saw APCs, we bought more than 10.
Before he became SSG, Port Harcourt was quiet. It was after him that we ran into crisis. We bought vehicles for Joint Patrol between the Police and the Army. Even the Air Force and Navy joined in the patrol to get Port Harcourt to the situation it is now.
But you were part of the government then?
They should ask the former governor. I went to him to complain and I said 'I don't like the way people are being killed in Okrika.' My wife was the first person that said it at the rally. I was very frustrated and I told my wife, 'you cannot replace life, there is no duplicate. If we burn down Government House, we can rebuild it. If we burn the State House of Assembly, we can rebuild it but if you kill, you cannot replace life.'
Why don't you ask: 'where is the Ijaw National Congress (INC) President?' He is in Paris. We flew him to Paris. Just because he was supporting us, organising the Ijaws to support, he was shot. If I didn't fly him overseas he would have died. He was organising Okrika people and the riverine people, saying, 'we have no other candidate whether our son is running or not, our candidate is Amaechi.'
He started receiving threat messages. Then he went to Yenagoa, on his way back from Yenagoa, he was shot. I was here when they called me that if we didn't fly him out that night he would die. That same night, I had to hire an air ambulance to fly him to Paris.
I don't like discussing the last government because Dr Peter Odili is my mentor. He is my former boss. If Dr Abiye Sekibo was facing issues, we won't be discussing the last government. That was why I say, 'can we please address issues?' But because he is not addressing issues that is why you see us talk about this. I am reminding him of all these because he is throwing stones that he should not be throwing.
How much of a threat is Sekibo to you?
He is no threat
We saw so many people in his campaign vehicles heading to Okrika from his rally in Port Harcourt...
So many people? There were three buses. Let's assume there were 50 persons per buses, multiply three by 50, you get 150. Didn't you see more than 150 persons at the rally? When we were going we saw three buses leaving Okrika. Okay, let's say each bus took 100 persons, did you see just 300 persons at the rally? Is that a threat? That is why I said they should shown his rally live to see whether they can fill the stadium.
Anytime we hold a rally at the stadium, the sitting capacity is 16,000, we fill that with over 10,000 persons standing outside and inside. At the Presidential rally, you saw the crowd, over 27,000, even if there was a stampede...
What efforts have the government made to compensate victims of the stampede?
We supported in the burials and are giving assistance to the victims. I was affected personally in two ways. The woman leader is from my village. My sister's mother-in-law died. The woman said she had been looking for me and so she wanted to come and see me at the rally.
Could you comment on allegations that your government moved against Sekibo's campaign by sealing his campaign over non-payment of N50 million tenement rate?
If I knew that it was that lucrative I would have ran for the chairmanship. Maybe after governorship, I will run for chairmanship (laughter). I told the chairman, 'you people collect N50 million tenement rate and you say you don't have money to run your governments.'
I was not involved. In the first place, I was out of the country when it happened. If you know my kind of politics, I would have picked out the information and I would have stopped them.
Why are you going for second term?
I want to consolidate on what I have started. There are very huge projects that I dare ACN to try. I wish they can say let these parties govern for six- six months before the man who election can take over so that you can see.
It requires courage to look at your wallet and say you want to build a school for N4.5 billion. As I talk to you now, Saipem is threatening to pull out of site. We are meeting with them tomorrow. They are doing 180MW of power at $195 million. If you add the extension of the gas pipeline, it takes it to about $200 million. We started with $50 million from Paris Club.
You know we are saving one billion naira every month? Now, we have paid well over $140 million. They have done the turbine, they are now dealing with the transformers and they will deliver in October. So we want to raise funds.
I heard again at the press conference, they accused me of taking a bond of N130 billion. That is not correct. We said if necessary we will take a bond of N100 billion but we have not taken. We borrowed N30 billion. He wants to be a governor, he should know that governments are not run without loan.
The fact that he is using loan to campaign shows that his is not fit to be governor. Part of the economics of governance is loan. So, he is elevating his lack of economic knowledge. He should have an economic team.
My economic team is chasing me about to borrow and I will say wait. Let's look at the N30 billion we borrowed. We borrowed for the following roads and flyovers: there is a flyover on the new road we are building to the airport, it is a federal road; the East -West road is a federal road and the flyover is on a federal road and the Federal Government has not paid us back. We have to complete that before the next loan is taken. We specifically allocated part of the N30 billion for these projects.
We borrowed for another flyover at Agip junction, it is part of the N30 billion. What makes the loan very interesting is we have started paying back. Every month they deduct N2.6 billion from our bank account, both the interest and principal, and I am told by October, we would have finished paying back. So what is he making noise about loan?
He should know that nobody can run government without borrowing. If he says we borrow money to share among ourselves that is a different thing. To say it is wrong to borrow means that he lacks the knowledge or capacity to administer the state because you must have loan as a component of administration.
Before he was saying that we were not saving one billion naira every month. He has withdrawn from that because we have replied that he can ask IBTC, First Trustees and Skye Bank. IBTC invests the money. First Trustees is the trustee and Skye Bank is the custodian of the money.
There was one month we received only seven billion naira, salary was N5.1 billion, we still saved one billion for that month because the law says we must set aside one billion naira every month and the money is about N36 billion.
All this is part of the N980 billion he was talking about, then the Ring Road in his village, the 14 primary schools and 10 health centres in his community.
He said the cost of the primary schools and health centres were outrageous. He thought it was the same six classroom blocks they used to build. He should go and see them. There is a basket ball court, football field, a track and a play ground for the children. Inside the school, there are 14 classrooms, 16 toilets, an office for the headmaster, a classroom for the nursery in case you want to establish nursery school, a library (e and hard copy), an ICT classroom, an auditorium for 500 children with a smart board.
Tell him that part of the N980 billion is we build secondary schools that take two students per room with toilet facilities. So for 1000 students, you have 500 rooms, 500 toilets, 1000 beds, 1000 wardrobes or closets. Our schools are located on 21 hectares of land We are building currently 450 that are under construction, target is 750.
Given the kind of space needed for the schools, how far have you gone in resolving the issue with people in riverine areas like Okrika where land is scarce?
The argument, which I was making to the wife of the President is that you can't have such a school- the one in Port Harcourt has football field, basketball court and a playground but the one we have in Okrika does not have any of those things because of surrounding houses.
The second argument we made to the First Lady is you can never tell who is a pedophile in those houses surrounding the schools. In one of the schools that I visited, one person was pounding yam and it was disturbing the students who were learning.
I then told the wife of the President that there was need for us to buy the surrounding houses, pay them off and demolish the houses to make way for football field and play ground. I think we need to reconcile that with the community because the community will be losing if their children are not able to enjoy those facilities.

Original_Search_Title: Two Die in Auto Accident On Lagos-Ibadan Expressway
Headline: Nigeria: Two Die in Auto Accident On Lagos-Ibadan Expressway
Body_Text:
Ibadan — TWO men yesterday died in an auto crash that occurred on the ever-busy Lagos-Ibadan expressway when a lorry loaded with yam tubers skidded-off the road and crashed. Also, three other passengers in the lorry sustained injuries and were rushed to a nearby hospital for treatment.
According to one of the survivors, it was the sudden burst of two front tyres of the lorry with number plate KEBBI 261 ZUR that made it lose control and emptied its content on the two men who were said to be sitting on the yam tubers before the accident occurred.
The accident which reportedly happened around 6am yesterday resulted in traffic snarl as many vehicles on the expressway could not pass.
Narrating her experience to newsmen, the survivor said, "we set off from Abuja at about 3.00 a.m. but when we got here (Iyana Agbowo) at about 6.00. a.m, two front tyres of the lorry burst and the vehicle skidded-off the road to the other side and later fell on its side".

Original_Search_Title: RUPFA to Assist Women in Inter Border Trade
Headline: Ghana: RUPFA to Assist Women in Inter Border Trade
Body_Text:
Mr. Richard Ashaley, the Executive Director of Rural Urban Partnership for Africa (RUPFA), a non- governmental Organization based in Africa, has said his organization would partner with security agencies in the country for free flow of in inter border trade.
"We would see how best in our way these market women can bring their goods from Ghana to Po, a suburb of Ouagadugou, the capital of Burkina Faso, devoid of any harassment and intimidation from any quarters," he said.
He made this known to journalists when the NGO paid a working visit to the neighboring country recently.
According to Mr. Ashaley, the member countries of the Economic Community of West Africa States (ECOWAS) are entitled to free movement of goods and therefore citizens, particularly women should not encounter hindrances trading within the subregion.
"One of the major objectives of RUPFA is curbing rural-urban migration and it would try with the help of the security agencies to do all within its power to assist the women of Po to trade with their Ghana counterparts in order to make a living.
"We would also liaise with the Ghana Police Service (GPS), the Immigration Service especially and all stakeholders involved in inter country trade in order to achieve this purpose," he stressed.
Women who trade along routes concerned have claimed they were requested to pay certain unwarranted amounts of money and this was not right because it amounted to extortion of money. "Those involved thought we do not know our right as citizens of ECOWAS member country."
They requested that RUPFA come to their assistance by helping them to bring down their goods (tubers of yam) from Ghana to Burkina Faso so that they can make living.
"We believe that since you are Ghanaians we believe your intervention would scare the Police away from us; we do not do any other business than the buying and selling of tubers of yam," they said.
"Transportation cost is also affecting the trade, travelling all the way to Ghana to bring these tubers of yaw cost us a lot," one market woman disclosed.
The women also complained of lack of funds to purchase goods in large quantities, hence loans and credits would be welcomed.

Original_Search_Title: Sima in the Cooler After Being Found Guilty of Cocaine Possession
Headline: Ghana: Sima in the Cooler After Being Found Guilty of Cocaine Possession
Body_Text:
The-39-year-old Sima, a professional model, was found guilty on two counts of attempting to export unlawful substances, and possession of unlawful substances suspected to be cocaine, and was sentenced to 15 years jail term on each count, to run concurrently.
The trial judge, Justice Charles Quist, said the defence put up by the accused person was subterfuge to throw dust into the eyes of the court, stressing the prosecution had led enough evidence to warrant the conviction of the popular model.
The judgement of the court came as surprise to Mr. James Agalga, counsel for the convict, who before the commencement of the sentence, called on the court to deal leniently with his client, in view of the fact that he was a first time offender, and also a role-model to many people across the world.
The counsel, including journalists and well-wishers, had come to court with the intention of getting a new date for judgement to be given, as the parties in the case had filed their written addresses in the case, only to be given the surprise of an instant judgement in the case.
Sima, who is a Togolese of Ghanaian origin, and currently a German, was arrested at the Kotoka International Airport (KIA) on the night of Monday, September 7, 2009, during departure formalities, for a scheduled travel to Germany, with tubers of yam containing substances confirmed to be cocaine.
Sima, a resident of both Germany and Tema Community 10 in the Greater Accra Region, denied the accusations leveled against him, and further indicated that he bought the tubers of yams on Graphic Road in Accra.
However, Sima, upon interrogation by the Narcotics Control Board (NACOB) personnel after his arrest, mentioned one Salifu as the one who gave him the substance.
The convict also pointed out that his baggage, which he had already checked in at the airport, was tampered with before he was asked to identify his luggage, noting that the zip was broken, and the security personnel had uninterrupted access to it.
Moreso, Sima accused the security personnel, including NACOB officials who conducted a search of his house, of packing his perfumes, watches and sun glasses into their pockets, yet he had not yet been given any document indicating the items taken from his residence.
According to him, he had suffered brutalities in the hands of the security personnel some years back, on his business activities on the Ghana to Togo road, and did not want to go through the same ordeal again, so had to confess to committing the crime.
Evidence
In his evidence-in-chief, Sima admitted that the security personnel who conducted a search of his residence at Tema Community 10 after his arrest, found a scrapper, knife, and glue allegedly used in stuffing the illegal substance in scooped portions of tubers of yam, but questioned how glue could be used to fasten wet yams together.
His evidence was that the glue found in his house was used to mend his belt and shoes, and, therefore, could not have been used on the yams that he was sending to Germany.

Original_Search_Title: Ikwerre Funerals, It's 'One Million Naira Or No Deal'
Headline: Nigeria: Ikwerre Funerals, It's 'One Million Naira Or No Deal'
Body_Text:
Bashiru Abdullahi, our reporter in Port Harcourt, covered a funeral ceremony. Here he brings you some funeral rites associated with the Ikwerre ethnic group in Rivers.
The Ikwerre people are jokingly referred to as 'landlords' by residents of Port Harcourt, and other parts of Rivers State. This, perhaps is because the Ikwerres, are generally believed to be the original owners of the land, and thus, the custodians of the rich cultural heritage of Rivers people, even though other groups living around the seaside, see themselves as having greater ownership of the oil reserve in the state, as against the Ikwerres who live outside the waters called uplands. None the less, the Ikwerres are said to constitute the highest population in the state.
One clear thing about the Ikwerres and their culture is their firm attachment to burial ceremonies which they mark with flamboyance. Ikwerre people have very high regard for burials, and that is why it is such an expensive venture for them .One of the most important aspects of their culture is to give a befitting burial to their dead kinsmen and women, especially those who died as elderly, rich or held traditional titles.
This is a cultural phenomenon which has been inherited over the generations, and is believed to be the greatest sign of traditional respect and honour paid to the departed by relations, friends and well wishers. It is something that is almost difficult to do away with, despite the huge amount and demanding tasks involved. A minimum of one million naira is required for a burial of average standard. But where an individual isn't the first son of a family, he or she has little to worry about, even a first daughter has no worries. Any adult in the ladder that happens to be the first male child is in trouble where a funeral is to be staged by the family.
Elder Sunny Chinwo is 70-year-old. He is one of the Ikwerre Elders living in Ogbunabali, Port Harcourt. He is preparing for the burial ceremony of his late father, Chinwo Amadi, a retired soldier, who fought in the Second World War, and died a respected traditional title holder among the Ikwerres.
The burial ceremony is slated for the 15th of March, 2011. As the first son, who according to the Ikwerre tradition has to shoulder the most expensive and demanding responsibilities, Elder Sunny is striving to come up with over four hundred thousand naira as his contribution to raising N1.6 million required to pay their last respect to Amadi, who died last year at the age of 88.
"When an old man dies and is blessed with about 2 to 3 sons, the Ikwerre tradition demands that the first son bears the responsibility of paying a higher amount for this burial, while the younger ones pay less. As it is now, I am expected to contribute four hundred thousand naira, and my brothers will raise the remaining, to make up the sum of N 1.6 million to give our father a befitting burial," he told this reporter.
For a respected elderly person like Chinwo Amadi, the Ikwerre culture demands that different types of animals to be slaughtered, and several kinds of food stuff and drinks to be provided for the burial celebration. Elder Sunny says, "At least three cows, a number of Dogs, Tortoise, Goats, Fowl and 16 bags of yams are expected from sons and daughters of the deceased, so as to feed the teeming number of people attending the ceremony. In our case, the representatives of our father are coming from different communities in Rivers. They are the custodians of the tradition" he noted.
Elder Sunny told this reporter that long before now, his Ikwerre forefathers used human beings as sacrifices for befitting burial ceremonies, before the rites were replaced with the use of cows owing to civilization, human right laws, religion and a sense of decency.
The first son to the deceased slaughters all the cows. He shares the meat among the representatives of his father or mother. As he does this, the representative of his father spreads money on him showing appreciation, which indicates that he has honoured their departed brother or sister, and thus receives their blessings.
In the event the first son does not want to slaughter the animals, the tradition compels him to pay one hundred and fifty thousand naira. He is also the first to push down the soil to cover the casket during interment.
Where the first son fails to share the costs for funeral as demanded by the Ikwerre tradition, numerous consequences may befall him. There would be expression of disapproval from the elderly, and mockery from the communities and members of the clan against him, while he may even be banned from inheriting the properties left behind by the deceased, who may be his father, mother or any other relative, as Elder Sunny Chinwo explains.
The burial ceremony in Ikwerre land usually attracts a large number of people coming from far and near, depending on the age, and class of the person so celebrated. For the rich, elderly and traditional rulers, it is a bigger celebration, attracting family relatives from different communities, villages and towns. However, every son, daughter, brother and sister of the deceased must be in attendance.
The transfer the corpse from the mortuary to the funeral ground, is usually characterized by some sort of VIP treatment in the Ikwerre kingdom, to show love and respect to their own. This, however, is not totally a cultural thing, but a mixture of modern values.
At the usually busy Timariv traffic junction in Port Harcourt, traffic is brought to a standstill to allow a convoy of vehicles escorting the body of an Ikwerre woman, with a blazing siren accompanied by many hired buses conveying passengers to the funeral ground. From afar, one assumed it was one of Nigeria's top government officials, if not for the traditional singing, chanting and dancing. Often, relatives and friends are fully mobilised to turn up in cars .The belief is that the longer the convoy, the more the show of respect to the deceased. In many cases, sirens are blown to lead the convoy with casket placed for all to see in the boot of bullion vans. The caskets have different designs, some look like ships. Many are bought. They cost differently depending on the materials used. An average casket for the lower income person , costs between N30,000 to N50,000, while expensive ones may cost as high as one million naira and above. Some are even imported on special demand by family of deceased. Around areas where caskets are sold in Port Harcourt, there are commercial ambulances stationed by owners to convey the corpses.
Elder Sunny Chinwo told this reporter that when it is a burial issue, money must be sourced to do what the tradition demands for the deceased "even if he or she couldn't get such money while alive." The burial is a last respect and hence has to be done, he said.
When a corpse, slated for a burial ceremony is moved to the funeral ground, six gunshots are fired to communicate the arrival of the corpse.
On the burial day, mostly Saturdays, sons and the daughters of the deceased visit the maternal home of their fathers or mothers. They take along with them, 2 goats, 16 tubers of yams, while the maternal family in return, donate a she-goat, and about 20 tubers of yam.
The burial day is normally preceded by a traditional wake keep. People coming from far and near to witness the funeral drink throughout the night, singing and dancing to traditional music- the idea is to dance away sorrows and celebrate the life spent by the deceased, presuming that he or she lived well. Traditional and modern guitars, band, and piano, among others, are commonly used in Ikwerre funerals.
One Saturday, Gada road in Ogbunabali, Port Harcourt was blocked with a simple message: 'No road' this was because of a burial ceremony in honour of one Mrs. Rose Ndada Chinwo, fondly called Wadanne, a mother of four who died on the 21st of May 2010 , at the age of 58.
Canopies were put in place to provide a suitable shade for the large numbers of people coming to attend her burial celebration, among them are people and relatives coming from the United States of America.
Some sort of VIP treatment accompanied the body of Wadanne as she was brought out from the mortuary. Trumpets blew out with sounds of sorrow, while some men came, dressed in uniform attires (undertakers) brought the expensive looking coffin containing the body, and took it to a well dressed room, with beautifully decorated cotton lining. This room was where she was kept before the final destination. One of daughters of Waddanne was seen restlessly walking around the crowd, with the enlarged photograph of her mother showing final respect to the mother as tradition demands, while relatives consoled her.
It must be pointed out that the Ikwerre people do not forget their Christian faith while celebrating and putting the dead to rest. Clergy men are seen around reading quotations from the Holy bible while praying for the departed.
The sons, daughters and grand children of Wadanne both male and female were all dressed in a blue wrapper and white T-shirts, while other relatives, mostly women dressed in a uniform attire.
The kind of life an Ikwerre man or woman lived can best be known at his or her burial ceremony. Individuals are invited and given the chance to make comments on the deceased. Wadanne was described as a caring, honest and God-fearing woman, by both tenants and church members who attended the burial.
As that was going on, women were busy serving foods and drinks, cooked in the family compound of the deceased.
After the Christian prayers, the body of the deceased Wadanne was finally laid in the grave, accompanied by more sounding trumpets, band, singing, and a mixture of joy and cries. The first son of the deceased was the first to push down three shovels of soil to cover the grave, after which other family members are allowed to push down more soil, amidst singing in Ikwerre language, and uncontrollable tears from those in attendance.
After Wadanne's burial, the Ikwerre traditional culture took over fully from the church. Young men and women dressed in another uniform traditional attire, played more traditional musical instruments such as gongs and danced to its rhythm, while elders coming from different communities are seen drinking different category of wines while chatting away. It is believed to be part of the requirement by the Ikwerre tradition of giving a befitting burial for loved ones.
Wadanne's grave cost thirty thousand naira to fix- cementing inside and at the top. But fixing of graves usually depends on the financial position of the deceased's family. Some graves are cemented inside and on the top, while others are dressed with marble or granite. Some graves are established at the deceased's residence, in the case of some traditional title holders or rich men- though depending on family decision, while many others are in public cemeteries.

Original_Search_Title: If You Must Have a Wedding
Headline: Nigeria: If You Must Have a Wedding
Body_Text:
It wasn't meant to be a wedding. They had only invited us to their daughter's naming ceremony. I went in with the two little outfits I had carefully wrapped and placed in a gift bag and the special dish of dodo and fried and peppered gizzard I had elected to make for the occasion and found their living room filled with about 20 adults. The children running around were numerous, but they were soon banished to the bedroom, where I guess they found the pleasures of watching Sponge-Bob Square Pants more satisfying than listening to dull adults. The newborn was cradled in white and carried by her mother, who was still puffy and round in the face, evidence of the recent pregnancy, and the tell-tale extra-dark neck: trophies of a successful gestation period.
It was to be a Muslim ceremony and we all took our positions on the rugs and throw pillows carefully scattered around the room. The Alfa then asked if the baby's parents were married to each other, to which they answered no. I whispered to my husband that that was an odd question but in reply he sent me a querulous look and I keep quiet, careful not to disturb the ceremony. Then the Alfa said, to my surprise, that he might as well marry the couple before naming the baby. What then took place was surely a shotgun wedding, if I ever saw one. It lasted for the whole of five minutes and we carried on with the task of giving the beautiful baby a name.
I didn't have a blackberry phone then, so I couldn't send instant messages to my friends telling them about the extra fast ceremony. It was just as well, because I hadn't actually given much thought to what I had just witnessed. To be honest, there had been no indication that the man was reluctant to marry the lady, neither was there any objection from the lady's part. In fact, she made a quick dash to her bedroom to tie an aso oke wrapper on top of her already glamorous lace blouse and wrapper, to signify the importance of the five-minute wedding. My husband was quick to point out on our way home that I was probably miffed because the couple had cheated us out of a party. He knew how much I loved attending wedding parties.
But I still couldn't get the matter off my mind. I bet I am not the only one who believes that when a person gets married, there should be a lot of solemnity and ceremony involved. Some people argue that weddings have become commercialised carnivals with people more interested in picking outfits, colour schemes, flowers and venues, instead of focussing on what really matters: the couple. I cannot refute the claim that some weddings are just an opportunity for both families to show off their wealth, and in cases where one family is richer than the other, and then it becomes an opportunity for one family to oppress the other.
Even the couples concerned are sometimes carried away by all glamour and lose sense of priority. I know of a couple who do not have a car, yet intend to rent a million naira marquee for their wedding reception in the summer. The groom lives off handouts from wealthier relatives and the bride just graduated from university. Here in the UK, many couples plan fantastic weddings and pay for everything using credit cards while sharing a local-council-owned flat with another couple. The logic for such spending is to have a fantastic once-in-a lifetime party, stuff memories are made from. Needless to say that the lack of cash usually puts untold strain on couples and may lead to break-ups. One such couple separated after five years together and two children. They still had eight thousand pounds debt to pay from their wedding five years previously.
But the alternative is too miserable to consider: a quick ceremony with very few or no relatives, repeating meaningless vows in parrot fashion sometimes before a very apathetic registrar in a registry office and perhaps worst of all, going to work the next day like nothing life-changing occurred. No gathering of elders, no breaking of kola nuts, no decanters to be filled with Schnapps, no dressed up goat with a bow tie, no tubers of yam as big as a four year old child, no dancing aunties demanding to be 'sprayed' money by the groom's friends, no intermediaries carrying ridiculous love stories back and forth from both parties, no native drummers spicing up the occasion with startlingly loud and sometimes tuneless drumming.
People get married in the way that best suits them. But is there a secret formula to getting it right? I think that while the decision as to whether there should be a carnival or just lunch at Tasty Fried Chicken depends on the couple, the secret formula might be in never leaving out the cultural part of the ceremony. Permit me to explain.
How many people know that all of the processes of the western style wedding ceremony we all like have deep rooted traditional significance in these Western cultures. The first person to wear a white wedding dress was Princess Phillipa of England in 1406, before Queen Victoria made wearing white for a wedding popular in 1840.Wedding bouquets are deemed to be a symbol of love and happiness. A proper bridal bouquet should have herbs like dill and bulbs of garlic. The garlic was to ward off evil spirits and the dill would make the bride only ever lust for her husband.
What about the 'something old, something new, something borrowed something blue' custom? I read on the Internet that "Something old" symbolises continuity with the bride's family and the past. "Something new" means optimism and hope for the bride's new life ahead. "Something borrowed" is usually an item from a happily married friend or family member, whose good fortune in marriage is supposed to carry over to the new bride. The borrowed item also reminds the bride that she can depend on her friends and family.
Now if we aren't descendants of Phillipa or Victoria, then we don't necessarily have to follow that tradition, do we? And what is the point of holding a bunch of flowers whose names you don't even know?
Whether you have a five-minute ceremony or a whole day fiesta, don't forget to include the traditional bits as well. It might not be the perfect recipe for happily ever after, but it surely is a step in the right direction. Costs can be kept to a minimum if necessary by keeping the ceremony small. The best part of this is that the prayers for the couple and the goodwill are usually free.

Original_Search_Title: You Cannot Steal Our Brains
Headline: Nigeria: You Cannot Steal Our Brains
Body_Text:
We must protect our brains from Senator Omisore and his gangsters. It is just late November last year and it already looks like there has been 180 degrees turn of events since Justice Isa Ayo Salami alleged foul play over the attempt to shift him from the Court of Appeal and promote him to redundancy at the Supreme Court.
We have not forgotten that Justice Salami cried foul on his nomination to the Supreme Court. The tradition is that the President of the Court of Appeal retires on the post. More importantly, the consent of the nominee must be sought and obtained. In this instance, it was not. The nomination, it would appear, is a punitive measure for Salami's obduracy and at the same time a strategic move to prepare the way for favourable outcomes for Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) on litigations that would surely emanate from April elections at the Court of Appeal.
This is not a harebrained speculation. It should be recalled that before the Court of Appeal in Ibadan delivered judgement on the Osun State Governorship Election Petition Appeal, PDP went to town to accuse the appellate court of bias on account of the upturning of the victories of the party in Edo, Ondo and Ekiti States. The poser that was raised then was whether Osun State would also go the way of the others in this observed regularity of unfavourable judgement pattern. It is not whether PDP rigged elections and deserved to be kicked out. It was a cruel, disingenuous and despicable tactics used by litigants to arm-twist the court to deliver judgement in their favour. But the legal system had taken care of this tendency by barring any party - or seeming non-party - to a case before the court from commenting on it. You cannot comment on a case that is subjudice, the popular saying goes, and it amounts to contempt of court to do so.
The PDP went further to stack its card by arguing that the court cannot just cancel the results in 10 local governments and on the basis of the remaining 20 declare the then Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN) candidate and the appellant, Ogbeni Rauf Aregbesola, the winner. But of course, what the law requires is simple majority of the votes spread on the basis of at least a quarter of the votes in two-thirds of the local governments in the state. All these were meant to corral the court to dismissing the appeal. In the midst of this siege on the court, the judges were courageous, bold and firm and refused to be intimidated. We were all relieved, however, as the appeal succeeded and Aregbesola was declared winner while Prince Olagunsoye Oyinlola was kicked out.
The PDP was initially jolted by this outcome as it went into hiding while torrents of praises poured in for the court and condemnations on PDP, INEC and the previous courts that handled the matter. It was when the euphoria died down and Governor Aregbesola was settling down to business that Senator Omisore emerged from hiding to impugn the court and its judgement. This baton of dishonour later passed on to the President of NBA who by way of innuendo illegally lent the weight of the respectable association on his own jaundiced view - that the Court of Appeal was compromised on the Osun State Governorship Election Petition Appeal. From then on, Justice Salami and the beneficiaries of justice were put on the defensive. Omisore alleged that the judgement was bought, without offering any shard of evidence. It is like the burden of proof is now on the accused, against the established legal norm that he who asserts must prove. This was still going on when the move to dump Justice Salami on the Supreme Court was made.
It is curious how Senator Omisore, who was not a party to the case, emerged as the ugly face of a bad loser. Prince Oyinlola had conceded defeat and quietly slithered like a worm to his native Okuku where he will be nursing his wounds (or counting his blessings) over pounded yam and bush meat washed down with freshly tapped palm-wine, when he is not teeing off at the golf course nearby in Ada.
Omisore's ambition to be governor of Osun State was killed by the judgement - he is the real loser. He can continue to bang his head on the wall; it is not going to change anything. In putting Justice Salami to task, he is futilely attempting to steal our brains and make us to forget that it was the PDP that had been subverting the electoral will of the people and perverting the course of justice. He wants us to forget so soon the Ido Osi debacle in Ekiti State, how the needed votes were manufactured for Segun Oni and the saga of INEC REC, Ayoka Adebayo, who had a pang of conscience and resigned on account of pressure put on her by the evil triumvirate of Prof Maurice Iwu, former IG Mike Okiro and Prof Dora Akunyili to announce a concocted result, failing which she was declared wanted until she shamefully capitulated. He wants us to forget the call log of the conversation between Oyinlola's counsel, Kunle Kalejaye, and Justice Thomas Naron while the tribunal was sitting. He wants us to forget the rebuke Naron's tribunal got from the Court of Appeal for rejecting relevant evidence from the Aregbesola team, including the forensic report of Adrian Forty and police report. More importantly, he wants us to forget the bases on which the results in 10 councils were cancelled.
Have we forgotten so soon how in Boripe, there were more votes for PDP than registered voters; how one Alhaji Nofiu signed the results for nine out of 11 wards in Ife Central; how one man with a scar on his thumb thumb-printed almost all the ballot papers in several wards in a local government; the documented evidence of ballot snatching, violence, thuggery and intimidation of voters; how results were altered and INEC's forms mutilated and altered by the PDP in conjunction with INEC in the 10 councils?
This puerile attempt to turn the tide is the greatest fraud of the century, a gigantic swindle. There has been no effort to review the legal reasoning on which the appeal court arrived at its decision. All that has been done is an unrelenting assault on the judges in the vain hope that, as Joseph Goebbels would say, a lie repeated too often would eventually assume the status of truth.
Well, the joke is on them. Their shenanigan has consumed Chief Justice Katsina Alu. Having been pointedly accused of trying to subvert justice on the Sokoto State Governorship Election Petition Appeal, I do not see how he is going to escape judgement day. He must go! His futile manoeuvre in the NJC amounts to the last acts of a snake that has been beheaded. Its silence and eventual passage is a matter of time. The most exalted judicial office in the land has been sullied with the accusation against him.
Salami, the honest, God-fearing and courageous judge, is never on trial. It is Senator Omisore, Naron, Garba and other PDP gangsters that are on trial in the court of public opinion. One is down, others will surely follow. The countdown has begun.
Fasure is Deputy Director, Osun State Bureau of Communications and Strategy.

Original_Search_Title: I'm an Old But Not a Tired Journalist - Odebiyi
Headline: Nigeria: I'm an Old But Not a Tired Journalist - Odebiyi
Body_Text:
Lagos — Dele Odebiyi, former Nigeria Union of Journalists (NUJ) chairman, Lagos chapter, teacher and author, understands the nitty gritty of his profession, and has witnessed the different phases of the practice. He worked with Daily Times of Nigeria (DTN) when it was the pride of the newspaper industry. He was also there when the economic crunch took a swipe at the industry to the extent of working and not receiving salaries for 24 months. The odds notwithstanding, he is proud to have taken the path he trod and is still treading and should he reincarnate, he would still want to be a journalist.
Odebiyi was born on June 16, 1946 in Gbogan, Osun State. He schooled at Olofin Memorial School, St. Paul's Anglican Central School, Gbogan, 1957; Anglican Secondary Modern School, Gbogan, 1958 to 1960 where he obtained his Modern III. Through private studies he obtained GCE 'O and A levels from the University of London. He taught briefly at Ebenezer Comprehensive High School, Ijaiye, Ojokoro, Lagos before enrolling at the Times Journalism Institute (TJI), which consolidated his employment with Daily Times in 1979, serving under their employment until October 2006.
Odebiyi is the last serving editor of the Sunday Times title. Ever since his retirement from DTN, he has been teaching two courses (Newsman's English and Graphics of Communications) at TJI. From these two courses, he has also published books, one already completed and the other on the verge of completion.
He explained why he had to quit teaching at Ebenezer Comprehensive High School for journalism: "It is a matter of choice. If you look at it very critically, there is virtually no difference between teaching and journalism, the only difference is that as a teacher you do that within the confines of the classroom while as a journalist you teach, inform, educate and entertains. When you sit down and edit a reporter's story, you are marking the reporter's script. That is why all these time all of them have been married in me. I write, I have been on radio for some time and I have done a lot of television programmes. There is no week I don't have reasons to teach people. When you are talking about PhD, I don't have it but I have got lots of experience to share."
He weighs the difference between the training of journalists during their time and the current trend: "In our days schools that taught journalism in this country were University of Nigeria, Nsukka, University of Lagos (UNILAG) and of course the TJI, the larger number of people we had at that time were people who trained in the job and they were good. The basic thing is that journalism, even as at today, thrives on English language. If you were trained at TJI and you didn't get it right, it would be very difficult for you to get it right elsewhere. Most of the people who taught there were people who had been reporters, sub-editors and people who had really done the job in various ways for years. We respect academic, they are all right but there is a clear-cut difference between academic expertise and practical experience embedded.
"You cannot be a journalist if you are a dullard and if you don't have the basic qualities that make you a news person. In DTN, to become an editor, you must have gone through 10 to 15 years on the job training. It is not a punishment; you have to show some degree of excellence. Sometimes, because of the training that you are given, it is expected that you perform excellently, so sometimes if you get it wrong, you are mocked, 'who told you to write English in Egun, you must have gone to one school in the interior villages,' and the editor tears your script, asking you to go and write another one within a short time frame. It was a real embarrassment wrapped into funs, but it really prepared us. That is why if anyone had the kind of training we had in those days, there is no way you can fear anybody, you can only respect people."
Besides emphasising that journalism is not the only career affected by the fallen standards, he points a few reasons as to why the situation continues to deteriorate: "I want to assert without being immodest that the training of journalist is not the only thing that has suffered a reverse, even lawyers, doctors and several others. There is this general cry that standards have fallen. And we say how do standards fall? A child that is coming out of the secondary school is as empty as anything. The parents too rather than help them to study are funding them through some academic fraudulent means. A good number of teachers today are people who themselves are supposed to be in the class to be taught. In our days, you sit down in the class to teach both on Saturdays and Sundays without anybody giving you a dime. You are only interested in knowing how many students would pass your course. You do all those things for free and the students knew and appreciated it. Today, that natural commitment is no longer there; everybody is looking for what to 'chop'. I don't blame any of them; you don't give what you don't have. A teacher that misses the opportunity for good grounding has nothing to give to the coming children. Let us be fair, I am not saying all teachers are like that, but a good majority of them fall into that category. If you don't have that natural element of teaching, teaching is not just about anybody going there to carry chalk, a good number of people who enter the classrooms today do so because there is no other thing to do, no single commitment, and of course the children are already lazy. The parents are busy looking for special centres or people who would help their children to write the exams. The child has been given the orientation that he has to cheat his way out, any teacher who has the commitment to assist that child to pass, wouldn't bother. When you look at the totality of the whole thing today, it is not only journalism."
Despite the highly volatile ups and downs state of the industry, Odebiyi would still be a journalist anytime any day.
According to him, "I have a son reading computer science. When he finishes I would like him to come into this profession to learn journalism. I have my reasons. What I have discovered over time is that, once you get involved in journalism, you cannot get out of it again. You cannot be a good businessman. I think that the problem that we have is systemic. If journalism were allowed to be practiced the way it is supposed to be practiced and we don't rely essentially on what Mr. A. would give to me as a politician. I don't look at things bigger than my resources because they don't constitute anything to me, so it has to do with the individuals. You see some people in the profession today, who are running the race of money; by the end of the day they would have nothing to show for it. But if you have your integrity, you have everything. If I have the opportunity of making a choice that if I die and reincarnate, I would still opt to be a journalist all over again".
One thing Odebiyi knows so well is the agony of journalists not collecting salaries for several months.
In his words, "it is a crime against God and humanity for somebody to labour for you and you don't pay him. The Bible says that a labourer must be paid before the sweat on his brow dries up. That means immediately the services is rendered. That brings us to the issue of ethics. We say journalists must not take bribe or collect brown envelope. But you are punishing him, damaging the basis of his ethics. No human being wants to do evil; as a result people who employ journalists should please change their hearts. "Though I remember in those days newspapers were being sold for just three pence, but today to buy a single copy of newspaper, you need to cough out N150 at a time when the country itself is in a financial mess. For some families, when they have N150, the mother and children would feed, if it means buying roasted yam and water they would manage it. For somebody to now use that money and give to a vendor in the morning is not easy. This is added to the fact that newspapers don't thrive on copy sales. We have to pray hard, and prayers not being matched with action would lead us nowhere."
On how he feels about himself, "What do you come here to do on earth? When a person is born, there are certain basic demand and expectation. One, the child grows. I have grown. I found myself as a teacher and then as a journalist, and in the process, it pleases God to give me a wife, I do not have to go about looking for children. The children are not trouble making children. We have succeeded in managing our little resources to give them the little education we were able to afford. Today we are grandparents. And we have a small quarter; we are not paying to any landlord. Without being immodest, if after retirement, we are not paying to any landlord and not owing anybody, we don't dress to kill but we are not the worst in the environment, and beyond all, we have that God -given contentment. If something comes our way, we are okay, if nothing comes, we don't cry. Nothing worries me now, everything that comes my way I take. We are not rich neither are we paupers."
Odebiyi, is almost 70 and literally not retired.
"Is it not super fulfillment for somebody to be nearing 70 and God gives you the agility to be moving around without anything going wrong with your brains. I still write up till now. There are only two things that can make a journalist retire, one, if he is so sick that he can no longer use his hands or write and with physical deficiency. But if your brain is still working, you are still on. The day you breathe your last, that is the day you rest. If I can do nothing, at least, I should be able to impact knowledge to the younger generation, as this is the only legacy you can leave behind. If you run too much after material things, you would kill the children because rather than struggle for themselves, they would be busy fighting and killing themselves for what you have supposedly bequeathed to them and you would be thinking you have put down assets. God would determine when the rest would come," he said.

Original_Search_Title: 'How to Curtail Jos Killings'
Headline: Nigeria: 'How to Curtail Jos Killings'
Body_Text:
The perennial ethno-religious crises rocking the city of Jos and other parts of Plateau State can only be brought to an end when all residents sheathe their swords and agree to turn a new leaf in their relationship with each other.
This was the opinion of an Islamic cleric and member of the presidential advisory committee on the Jos crises, Gwani Muhammad Alkali Adam. According to him, the discontent among the people of Jos was deeply rooted and could only be healed when all the people decide to live in harmony with each other.
He said, resolving the Jos crises only required trust in each other and that was a collective responsibility of all Jos residents and other Nigerians. He added "The Jos crisis can be resolved by you, me and everybody, from one with lawmaking authority to a yam seller on the street."
"The government has been trying for so long to bring an end to this problem and the problem in Jos is not the fault of the government. I am a true Muslim and I am not going to blackmail anybody on the basis of tribe or religion. We all know the genesis of the crisis in Jos, it is not the state government's fault at all," he maintained.

Original_Search_Title: Love is Commitment, Not Coitus
Headline: Nigeria: Love is Commitment, Not Coitus
Body_Text:
Lagos — Auntie Agatha the vintage "love doctor" cum "relationship coach" was really in her best element last Sunday, the eve of St. Valentine's Day, as she did what she knows best (counseling on relationships and marriage). At about 1.00 p.m., her fans were already seated in the hall to learn at Auntie Agatha's feet. It was the third edition of Just Before Valentine themed "the other side of Valentine" put together by Independent Newspapers Limited (INL) and supported by Viju Milk.
Really, the event was an interesting time-out with the relationship expert. It had in attendance experienced married men.
Engineer Olu Adedoja, a man whose marriage is 49 years old, while speaking at the occasion narrated how he met his wife in 1955. Though he tried everything he could to go to bed with her then fiancé, but she refused. Philandering young Adedoja, who was bent on satisfying his sexual urge met another young girl who he had her carnal knowledge within two days. This experience, according to him, made him to have a change of mind towards his fiancé as he described her as, "a decent girl who is not ready to jeopardise her future because of few minutes' sexual pleasure."
Adedoja, despite his almost half a century experience in marital life, confessed that he and his wife cannot stay an hour without quarrelling, but this, according to him, have been their strong point. "My wife is old and I am equally old, our children are no longer with us, both of us stay alone. Whenever my wife is in the kitchen cooking, I am also there washing plates. My wife is not too strong now, so I wash plates. I love pounded yam and since she can't, I pound yam in the house. I wash her underwear which I did on Wednesday," he said to the admiration of all present. He however advised while urging singles present to be sensitive and conduct themselves in good manners.
Also speaking at the event was Poju Atobatele, who enjoined singles to seek "God's face before you embark on any relationship. God is the bedrock of every good thing."
The interactive session, which enjoyed active participation by the audience, had in attendance Auntie Agatha herself, head personnel Independent Newspapers Limited (INL), Kehinde Okeowo; and Emma Effiong-Bright as the panelist.
A young bachelor, who has 20 married women interested in having illicit affair with him, asked if anything was not wrong with him. This according to him made him shave his dreadlocks. He confessed that he is not happy with this development because he is afraid such occurrence can be part of him when he eventually gets married.
Reacting to that, Effiong-Bright said, the young man needs to take stock of his life. "You don't need to take to their advances. What are your visions and how far do you see yourself in life?
