Their excuse of not enjoying good marital bliss should not be your excuse for failure. Don't be a willing tool," he said with passion. Also reacting to that, Antie Agatha urged the young bachelor to check his life because, "something definitely is wrong if about 20 married women are making passes at you, then something definitely is wrong with your way of life. What signals are you throwing to these women? Check your way of life and re-order your steps."
Another troubled bachelor asked why "young single girl prefer married men"? Okeowo while doing justice to the question stated that the so-called young girls believe that married men are more caring and they have enough resources to take care of young girls. Okeowo said it is not about money but urged young eligible bachelors to grow and get more mature because maturity plays good role in every relationship. Auntie Agatha on the other hand said older men seem to understand better the nitty-gritty of relationships, take care of women and they understand women better as the young men don't have time for pampering girls as they are out busy looking for money.
A young man asked if there is nothing wrong in women proposing to men, this Auntie Agatha said should be done with maturity and the man should be wise enough not to treat the woman with disdain. Okeowo cautioned such women not to "throw it at the man's face that she wants him rather she should do it tactically.
Finally Auntie Agatha, the hostess, warned girls not to be willing tools at the hands of men because of "Valentine craze. Sex is not love rather love is commitment, sharing, tolerance and patience. Watch out, men are naturally hunters while women are their prey, be vigilant and wise."

Original_Search_Title: Women-Charming Juju Man Arrested in Accra
Headline: Ghana: Women-Charming Juju Man Arrested in Accra
Body_Text:
A NATIVE of Tevi in neighbouring Togo, Kwadzo Zombo Amenyikpo, 34, is in police custody at the Old Fadama police station, for allegedly harassing female traders at the Agbogbloshie market in Accra, and possessing a firearm without lawful authority.
During his arrest on February 14, this year, the James Town Police Crime Officer, Deputy Superintendent of Police (DSP) Alex Buahin, said Kwadzo had in his possession two charms, one of which he uses to attract women to his beck and call, and the other, which enables him to disappear at any given time.
Other items found on him included a locally manufactured pistol, with an empty cartridge and live ammunition, and a catapult.
According to the Crime Officer, Kwadzo's ill-behaviour towards the market women became unbearable, and was reported to the Ghana Private Road Transport Union (GPRTU) Task Force in the market, which failed to arrest him, because he pulled out a pistol.
DSP Buahin noted that the suspect went into hiding for the past three weeks, and was arrested by the same task force on St. Valentine's Day. Kwadzo is being processed for court.
In another development, 20-year old Kwasi Anthony, who was being investigated by a police detective for allegedly using a fake GH ¢50 to purchase some second-hand clothing at the Tema station in Accra last Thursday, was unrepentant, as he dipped his hand into the police investigator's purse, and stole an amount of GH ¢95 at the Ministries District Police station
According to the District Commander, Assistant Superintendent of Police (ASP) Albert Fii Ochil, the alleged thief, after stealing Detective Inspector Olivia Ajavon's money, hid it in his pants while in police custody. He said Kwasi discreetly handed the stolen money over to a cell-mate for safe keeping.
Later in the day, ASP Ochil said even though Kwasi Anthony had no money when he was brought to the police station on Thursday at about 10 a.m., he gave out an amount of GH ¢5 to be used to buy him some food.
Kwasi claimed he sells yam tubers on the Graphic Road in Accra.
The police, according to ASP Ochil, became suspicious of the situation, and during a search on the suspect, an amount of GH ¢93 was retrieved. Kwasi Anthony told the police he had used GH ¢2 for food already.
Meanwhile, Kwasi Anthony went to the Tema station bus terminal in Accra on Thursday with the supposed GH ¢50 note to buy some second hand clothing, but the dealer noticed that the money was a counterfeit.
ASP Ochil noted that the authenticity of the currency would be established by the Bank of Ghana, as investigations into the matter continue.

Original_Search_Title: The 'Rigging' We Will Not Discuss
Headline: Nigeria: The 'Rigging' We Will Not Discuss
Body_Text:
We have a huge problem on our hands. We are facing the scary prospect of having the worst organised election in recent memory. Let no one delude Nigerians with the 'by the grace of God, Insha Allah' singsongs that we love to cling on too with a minimal input from our human angle.
The general belief in our political system is that 'rigging' starts and ends with, ballot box snatching, stuffing or voting by under aged or fictitious names which must have been a strategy by some politicians who want to outsmart opponents anyway.
Giving the situation we have at the moment, it should be clear to all Nigerians that we are in for some drama before, during and after the April elections. As I write, most of the candidates for the elections do not even know their status. There are court orders from nominees, losers at party primaries, INEC, political parties and just about anyone who wants a piece of the cake.
As I write, merely a few weeks to the election, we do not know a substantial number of candidates for most of the elective positions from the local councils to the presidency. Things are so muddled up the supposed 'voters' are obviously not being educated enough by those they ought to choose from.
Because there's so much hypocrisy in the political field, we are being suffused with 'a holier-than-thou' posturing by the different political parties. We are literarily being suffocated with warped ideas about what a free and fair election is by those posturing as pro-democracy activists.
If democracy is about choice, it all means the voting public must be educated about the candidates by both the parties and the candidates themselves. That has not been happening. At the end of the day, we might just be faced with choosing between six and half a dozen.
As political parties and INEC battle out the obedience to the Electoral Act or lack of it, we should realise that we are in for a very long night. The flawed optimism for a free and fair election hinged only on the integrity of the Chairman of the Electoral Commission, Prof. Jega amazes every sane adult in Nigeria and beyond.
The selfishness of our politicians is what would ultimately push this country out of the precipice post April. If some of them love to provide service as they want us to believe, how come none of them wants to respect the people by giving them the room to choose?
The battle for who appears on the INEC list that is going on at the nation's capital, Abuja should be seen as a big conspiracy by all those that are making it possible. If not for the urge to grab power, INEC and politicians would have had enough time to sort things out before elections are held.
One is yet to unravel what the whole country would achieve with the fire service disposition to this year's elections. But let no one be deceived, most of our politicians thrive better under the most confusing situations. They hate scrutiny because there are too many cans of worms and as such, they double their effort to make sure the environment is chaotic.
With all the horse trading going on at the moment, how can we guarantee free and fair polls when every politician who indicates interest for any post believes he has already won the election? Why are alliances failing? Why does each politician believe that he or she is the best thing that happened to the people after pounded yam?
Now we are faced with the deafening silence from candidates and parties, we do not know who they are and what their antecedents are, how then would they allow us to make a choice come April? We have seen all the intrigues during the voter registration. The stealing of the DDC machines, the purchase of voter registration cards and the monopolisation of the DDC machines by certain party chieftains.
As it is, the urge to rig has somewhat been made an art by most of our politicians and they would stop at nothing to achieve their aim. Very sad for a country in dire need of authentic leaders chosen by the people.
The political debates that expose the mental depth of candidates and their capacity to deliver on their promises are not being witnessed by potential voters in a country where political torn coats are a dozen for penny. It pays them also that we don't get to 'know' them well so that we would continue to be under their dominance.
However, no matter how the politicians try to blindfold the mandate givers, it is now obvious, given world events that the people can be fooled some of the time but definitely not all the time. The people can definitely read in between the lines and very soon, regional, religious and ethnic manipulators would realise that the world has moved beyond such inanities.
By the way, may we hear the manifestoes of parties while they allow the candidates to who ought to be on the driver's seats tell us how well they intend to lead us?

Original_Search_Title: Women With Largest Earlobes?
Headline: West Africa: Women With Largest Earlobes?
Body_Text:
Jalingo — The Mumuye people are found mostly in the hilly northern region of Taraba State. One of the stories you may have heard about the Mumuye is that they have very long ears, long enough for them to sit on.
But that is not quite true. Their old women do have long ears with wide, round holes in the earlobes. But if you are beginning to think that it was a kind of deformation caused by an epidemic or a curse by a god, you will be surprised to know that it was a fashion, a test and a transition rite still very dear to those who partook in it.
Lenbba Masho is about 75 years old and she lives in the Mumuye village of Panti Sawa, in Yorro local government. She told our correspondent that it was a tradition for every young girl to perform what today would have been referred to as lobectomy--incising wide, round holes in the lobe and inserting sticks into them. The sticks help in expanding and stretching the thin lobes.
A fair complexioned old woman with tribal marks and a mop of white hair covered by a pink beret , came out of a thatched hut compound fenced by woven reeds, with a stooping gait, and greeted our correspondent in a Mumuye dialect, beaming a welcome smile.
"During our time, every young girl would have her ears incised like this," pointing to her own ears. "It was part of our culture to carve the ears as a mark of beauty , and a transition rite from girlhood to adulthood," she enthused.
Because of that, she said, she is not ashamed that her exotic ears are rare sights these days. "I am still proud to carry my ears like this. It shows where I come from, and the generation I belong to. So many things have changed, and now we are at the twilight of our generation.So why should I bother about having these kind of ears?"
Masho described the rite as a painful experience in which a girl's earlobes will be pierced with razor or any sharp object, and a stick put inside. After sometime, the stick will be removed and a bigger one inserted until after three months when the wound would have been healed, and the stick will fall off by itself.
But the sense of pride she brought to her family, and the feeling of fulfillment that ran through her veins after the rite was performed on her, numbed the pains, she said. Adding that if she had known a man before the rite was performed on her, the earlobes would have broken, and she would have brought shame to her family.
"I felt happy and fulfilled because I brought pride to my family, and I showed I was pure enough for young men to woo me," saying the tradition cannot continue nowadays, because of promiscuity in the society, and the fact that the people who used to perform the rite have all died.
According to her, after the rite, there would be a very big ceremony at the village square, where those who have successfully passed it would be rubbed with red powder all over their bodies, and they will sit with young men and drink Burukutu. Each couple gulping the local brew in one calabash. Then the couples would dance from one end of the square to the other.
The ceremony had different dialectal names , but was basically the same kind of feast which all Mumuye's partook in, and cherished dearly. Tsomah and Gbaree are the dialectal names of the ceremony that today would have been considered harmful, because of the mutilation of the earlobes.
Ezekiel Emmanuel is a 46-year-old Mumuye man working in Jalingo, and he said he only witnessed the ceremony once in his lifetime. But it was enthralling, and he would cherish the memories forever.
The rite had so much significance that if a girl passed the test and eventually got married, it would be a taboo for the husband to beat her and injure the ears. But it was forbidden for pregnant girls to go through the rite.
"If you passed the test successfully, your parents and husband would honour you by giving you a precious gift. But, if you get married and you have a misunderstanding with your husband, and he mistakenly or intentionally injured the ears, he would be made to pay seven nickels of gold in atonement," Masho said proudly.
At the serene foothills of Zavonranti our correspondent met another old woman with the trademark pierced earlobes. She was munching roasted yam, and seemed not to like the idea of somebody interrupting her lunch. After her lunch, she stood at the gate of her small compound and recounted her experience as a maiden. Speaking in a low, crackly voice, she gave her name as Cinapo Yakoko, and guessed she would be 89 years old.
She said it was a thing of pride to carve the ears at her time, and does not feel bad being one of the few that still have the ears. "I only feel a sense of loss for my generation which valued everything that was pure and beautiful. Ours was a society bound by communal values--values that gave us the wisdom to discern what was right or wrong. But the same cannot be said of this present generation. My time has passed, so I don't care about what happens now," she said.
She said the happiest day in her life was the day she danced in the village square with young men, having successfully carved her ears, saying the rite had a binding influence on the love between her and her late husband.
"I cannot even remember that it was a painful experience, because after I had successfully pierced my ears, my husband had so much respect for me. I believe the love that he showered on me until he died, was as a result of me passing that rite which was considered a test," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Roasted Yam Turns Urban Delicacy
Headline: Nigeria: Roasted Yam Turns Urban Delicacy
Body_Text:
Roasted yam was once associated with the aged and village life. But it is gradually becoming an urban delicacy because of the manner Nigerians eat these days.
Even though it is roasted and sold by the road side, people still relish it because of its local appeal and mode of preparation reminds one of mama's kitchen. Speaking on how it is prepared, Zulaiyat, a vendor, said, "I buy the yams, then I buy the ingredients which include red oil, meat, seasoning, pepper and onions and I make the sauce at home before coming out with my wares. I set fire with charcoal, then put yams on the hot fire, as the yams are roasting, I keep turning and scrapping to remove the burnt parts; I also poke it with a knife to know if it is done inside".
She continues when it is ready, I cut it to pieces, depending on how much the customer wants. If you want to roast yam, you will buy quality yams, cut them into pieces or put them whole on the fire, use a hot glowing, smokeless fire to brown the food quickly, heat the grid before using, this makes the yam to brown quickly. Turn the yams repeatedly to prevent over cooking on one side; also, you must peel the yam before starting to roast it."
Okene, who was met eating the yam stated that "I ride Keke NAPEP, so whenever I am hungry, I eat this yam, because it is a hard food; it stays in my stomach till in the night when I close and go home to eat my wife's meal, it is also cheaper than going to a restaurant. Here, with just a hundred Naira, you will eat a very large quantity, she also knows how to make the sauce, it is very sweet,"
Godwin, is a civil servant who eats roasted yams, and he said: "I like eating solid food, besides, yam is for men who are hard working; I eat it because it is cheap and sweet, it gives you a sense of village life, and the women here know how to roast the yam, to keep you coming back. During weekends when I don't come to work, I find myself missing the yams, it is very sweet and rich."

Original_Search_Title: Political Killings - IPAC Can Be Tested With 2011 Elections - Nnaji
Headline: Nigeria: Political Killings - IPAC Can Be Tested With 2011 Elections - Nnaji
Body_Text:
Prof. Godswill Iheanyichukuwu Nnaji is the Chairman of the Inter-Party Advisory Council (IPAC), the regulatory body of the 63 political parties in Nigeria. He is also the national chairman of Better Nigeria Progressive Party (BNPP). In this interview with Chibuzo Ukaibe, the professor of economics says that the parties had resolved to avoid killings but, instead, to carry out issue-based campaigns. He also sheds light on the economic policy of the country. Excerpts:
How would you describe the recently conducted party primaries as it relates to the question of internal democracy?
Almost everything about Nigeria's political and economic development can be ascribed to a teething problem. It is a learning process. Apparently, there is a difference between a kid learning how to walk and a man almost 50 years old learning to walk properly.
We are in a situation where since the 50s we have been involved in politics yet, there has been no consistency. My father does not remember any democratic dispensation this long. So we don't have democratic root to be proud of. For instance, I have been in the United States for 24 years and it is like these democratic things are basic things and we need to do more than this. We have seen it done in Europe, US, South East Asia and co. so we wish it could just happen like that here over night. But Rome was not built in a day.
It does not matter how smart you are if you want to learn a foreign language, you will be like a bay first babbling, almost insulting yourself not knowing what you are doing because it is new. That is the case of Nigeria. So what we see in Nigeria and ascribe as not working, might not be true; things are working. It is just that they are working at a pace we cannot control. I was in New York when the first woman was appointed to the Supreme Court in United States in the 1980's. Yet, they had had independence more than 200 years before.
Imagine how long it took them. But here 11 years ago we were under military government. Today we are under democracy and we want to get it all right just like that. We can't. There is no way we won't fall, get up, stumble and rise. I am not defending our problems, but I am saying as it is. However what they have attained we can get there faster. People do not even understand the primaries. It needs to be so transparent that we know who is already winning not just that one day and one place gathering they do in eagles square and lift one guy up.
In the US, you know who is winning and how many votes he needs to get the ticket, because he goes from state to state. But we can't translate it here even though something close to that is in our electoral act. We are supposed to know who is actually leading from the states and maybe the other people will just step down for the guy who is leading because they don't want unnecessary confrontation at the end. It should not be a surprise thing.
When Clinton conceded to Obama, it was not a surprise thing, she saw it coming. If that is the process we claim to be copying from America why are we not doing it? So these are no primaries, but we shall get there. Somewhere we shall arrange ourselves where the president, governors and other elective post contestants shall emerge and we shall see the process by which they emerge particularly in the major parties the smaller ones can do it any how they like. So we are still evolving.
What do you make of the lack of political ideology in our political parties the way it is leading us in the wrong direction?
It will not lead us in the wrong direction. Over time, some people said that the progressive forces will amalgamate; others said whether we like it or not we know the parties that are conservatives and those that are credible. I don't want to give the label. Why must we be copy cats? We are trying to copy the United States but we are not copying it rightly and we want to copy them more and more. Leave Nigerians to do it their way which is what we are doing now.
When we take this ideology from Europe and you hang on here then you are asking for left, right and center. Now you are limiting everybody to three parties. But I have to tell you that there are countries like in India, it is said there are about 1000 parties there. Do we have 1000 ideological differences in our literature; no. lets us not kill the way we are doing it. Let us not be so carried away by democrats, republicans, conservatives. Things don't work like that. There are parties whose interest is to protect the environment and they shout it to the roof tops whether they win elections or not.
Others are just out there focusing on education or women issues we need them. Now they don't want to form political parties so why bother whether they have ideological base or not. One day a candidate will emerge that will be attractive to everybody and it will not matter which party.
Some call PDP an unlikely amalgamation of people who do not have anything in common but power. Do you think it will remain like that?I don't know, I cannot argue that. But one thing has joined them together. People complain about internal democracy, but one thing that concerns me is whether the democracy development will not be the only game in town? People who have no business in politics are being forced into it to be active participants.
I am not asking anybody not to contest, but when it becomes the only business in town, you now have the over 70 million that INEC plans to register wanting to contest.
As an economist, what are your views about the present economic policies of this administration?
We have a 2011 budget that is heavy on consumption and light on investment. If we continue that way, we shall have problems and continue to complain because what we are consuming is not what we are producing. We are not talking of consuming Garri or Coco yam, even the rice we consume is from Thailand. The beans we consume are from Burkina Faso. I am talking about the basic foods. More than 70 percent of what we consume come from outside. That is not a way to grow a country. Any company that has left the shores of Nigeria should be blocked, and not by raising taxes on them. They should be blocked out rightly.
You will not uproot yourself from Nigeria, go elsewhere where you think labour is cheaper, produce there and come and sell to the 150 millions Nigerians here. So if that is done they will be afraid to move, because if you do so, you lose the market. ECOWAS should not be at the neck of Nigeria. Any government that can't do that should be voted out because at that rate nobody wants to produce in Nigeria.
Now we have 18,000 minimum wages who is going to pay it? You must be able to secure Nigerian borders, not by the military, but economically so that those here can benefit from it. Things are hard but they can be made better, but you don't make it better by selling out the demand. But you can't blame it all on this administration?
What have they done to stop it? Are they not seeing it? If you allow that, who will agree to stay here to produce without light and with high labour cost? At 18000, you have a high labor cost. It cost South East Asia their economic growth in the late 1990's. What the labour union is doing here is simply saying those that have jobs, particularly in the civil service, which by the way they are protecting is give them more money and keep them satisfied.
Unfortunately, they are less than five percent of the country. At 18,000, how many people can a way side café employ? These are jobs that are going to die because the cost of doing business is too high. Some university graduates were working for my friend for 15,000 naira a month; that is about level 8 staff in government sector. Now you are saying level one is 18,000 and you say we can carry it? It will ruin this economy. These are the issues. An administration that has done its homework should be able to know which policies are feasible or not. Those that are not feasible they change them. Must they pander to labor, who is mostly civil servants? And then you say that any employer that has over 20 employees should pay that wage. It means that most of the private sector jobs will go because they can't pay.
What is IPAC doing to ensure that the political space ahead of April 2011 is violent free?
Can you give me an example of political violence in this period? What I am trying to say is that somehow, the political parties came together by themselves to sanitize their conduct and behavior over the election to make Nigeria election dignifying. If you watch carefully electoral violence has been minimal. But what we see as local violence has some other underpinnings. The bombing in eagles square, whether it is electoral violence we don't know. It is not clear to us. The Jos crisis is that electoral violence? It was there before now. Even the bombing on New Year's Eve, are those electoral connected? It is difficult to prove. These are pockets of things that have gotten more scientific and deadly because the bombs have become available everywhere.
But these shades of violence have some other underpinnings like religious matters and so on. I remember we used to hear about Harry Marshal, Bola Ige, and Funsho Williams and so on. These ones were before we came. IPAC can be tested with this 2011 election. We have agreed as political parties that we cannot keep harming ourselves. We should go on road shows to tell people that political parties cannot condone killing ourselves, or involving in electoral malfeasance of any type. We will not let those, we should be better than, teach us this game just because we got fractured along the line. We know what is right and we will stick to it.
Why have the series of alliances and moves to oust the PDP by some opposition parties not sailed through?
It is because of the politics. When you bell the cat, who will you crown? That is one issue. Yes there will be so many people that will want to lead the struggle but at the end of it, whom are you getting the power for? How are you going to share the booty? They tell you that robbery is not a difficult thing but the difficulty is how they share the booty after the robbery. That is where they kill themselves. How to distribute the spoils of war? Again, who funds the push PDP campaign? A mafia or a Godfather who has the deep pocket to do it, but will the spoil belong to him or her? These are matters of serious consideration that makes it difficult.
So far, most of the campaigns have been devoid of issues; what is IPAC doing about it?
We are doing more of police work to make sure that people don't misbehave. The issue will come out. All along, we have been doing internal party issues. One problem with that which people try to avoid is that if you hit your opponent too hard within the party then everybody knows your problem and knows how to get at you. The primaries are over and now we are looking at what they have for Nigeria.
If they were smart they would have been thinking of how to develop their party before they emerged. Now the question is all those things you campaigned on before to get yourself elected as candidate how they will help Nigeria in general. Are we going to believe you? So we are looking at it seriously. Given the gamut of plethora of candidates we can't have but address the issues.
I remember in 2007 people dodged debates because they were afraid to be confronted. If you think you have won you don't want to lose some points by making some careless mistakes and so you avoid the campaigns. We pray that it does not happen this time because it will be bad. People should be able to canvass what they stand for and tell us what their party stands for.

Original_Search_Title: 'Traditional Norms Better in Checking Corruption'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Traditional Norms Better in Checking Corruption'
Body_Text:
Daniel Abomtse is the Ter Tyoshin, a second class traditional ruler in Gwer West local government area of Benue State. Barely months on the throne, Abomtse reveals in this interview with Correspondent Terna Doki, in Makurdi, the challenges of his kingdom and laments that the colonialists distorted their cultural values in many respects. He explains why the Tiv people do not celebrate Yam festivals and appeals to government to turn attention to his domain, which is badly in need of infrastructural development, among other issues. Excerpts...
Looking at the totality of the culture of the Tyoshin people, which aspects do youth think are outdated and should be completely discarded?
In Tiv culture, when you marry, you must test the fidelity and virginity of the women. Once you confirm that the virginity is still intact, you buy, in those days you buy a cloth and send it to the mother in-law and the cloth must be Tiv traditional cloth. It could be "Anger" or "Ivavtyor" and send it to your mother in-law. And if you make a hole in the cloth you send, it means the sanctity or virginity of the woman has been damaged. And the mother in-laws will now begin to weep that the daughter has put them to shame. I know that this practice has been discarded by modernity. We elope with girls nowadays and sometimes for years and begin to have children even before we inform the parents. And yet we still accept. That is one aspect that has been discarded. The other areas which we are still calling for them to be upheld has to do with deaths. When death occurs in the society, it is always blamed on deistic occurrence or witchcraft practices, propelled by elders who are vast in it. So in this aspect, you cannot say that the whole Tiv society is educated or Christianised. So, on this I can tell you that, when it comes to deaths occurring within a community and going in for inquest, let the elders perform their rites. For instance, if a king dies and the king is a traditional ruler and it happens that he is also a knight in the Roman Catholic Church, the knight people will come and say, traditional people go away. No! He was made of tradition first. Tradition came before religion. Our parents were living and checking the society much more efficiently before the white men came and introduced his religion to us.
Your submissions about the presence of the Whiteman in your domain appear neutral. You have condemned and also applauded them in some areas, why so?
Lets balance up the whole thing. Where you see good aspect of his life, copy it or uphold it. Where you see good things in the modernity, cherish them as we now cherish education. But where religion conflicts with the culture of the people and the culture seems to be supreme in certain aspects, we respect the areas that have a kind of sanctity. Not all aspects of culture can be discarded. For instance, our culture demands that when an elder is talking, the youth keeps quiet. And before you go for marriage, the custom demands that you bring the most elderly person to the people you intend to take their daughter as wife. If that is not done, then marriage is not concretised. And it also holds that if you, apart from the church wedding and court knot-tying, you will discover that traditional marriage still upholds. You can go and perform the traditional rites for marriage. It is still there, you can't destroy it. The parents must give you conditions of accepting you as an in-law into the family. And you fulfill it. It is still practiced. Its not every aspect of the culture is bad. So where we see areas of improvement, lets us improve upon, because culture is not static, it is dynamic.

Original_Search_Title: A Salute to Artisans
Headline: Nigeria: A Salute to Artisans
Body_Text:
'Kaya, Kaya!' I heard the sound; it was one of those men that normally push wheel barrows in the market. I watched him clear his way with the loud shout. Everyone around recognises it as signal that you clear the way, as he pushes his heavy overloaded wheel barrow. Under the afternoon sun he slaps his bare feet heavily on the ground as he trots after his barrow. It was a sight that touched my heart as I watched his sweat soaked back pass by me.
We had parked by the roadside, and across the street was a welder doing what he does best. I was completely engrossed, as I watched this man go about his work. It is hard labour, a job that requires so much energy, concentration, and manual dexterity. With so much practice this welder went about his job so adeptly, looking at him handle the irons and other tools of trade you'd think there was nothing to this job which even entails working with fire and so much noise. Yet there's someone who relies on it to make a living.
As we drove off people with so many jobs like his kept coming to mind: the plumber who comes to your house. This Guy fixes everything wrong with your sanitary wares from taps to toilet bowls. This guy pushes his hands under the sink, inside the bathtub box, even into the toilet bowl should the need arise, but at the end of the day he goes home to his bathroom covered in mildew as opposed to the tiles he had laid from wall to wall in someone else's home, to squat over a hole steaming with stench from other peoples' waste.
The 'maiguard' this guy is the allotted the first room you meet in the house but it's more often than not the smallest. He may be a different person from one house to another but his is always the small room by the gate. His job requires he guards big beautiful houses which he may never step into except by chance.
Then the electrician comes to mind, we flip the lights as we go through our houses expecting it to work each time but do you ever wonder about the intricate wiring that goes into walls and ceilings of our homes? How about the masons? The bricklayers that built these houses suffered cuts, bruises and other accidents that are just considered all part of the job. All for a house they don't dream of living in. The road builders that till the soil, break rocks if need be, to build the roads on which mansions are built, may never have cause to follow that road ever once it's been completed.
Apart from the artisans many come to mind that do jobs, provide very important services that we just take for granted, the services they provide are in most times necessary for life to be comfortable for others but yet their jobs are looked down on, they are not really rewarded enough for the services they provide for others to enjoy life comfortably.
Theirs are selfless services that require so much energy but somehow their services are so cheap compared to the services they provide. They do the dirty jobs most times full of risks and danger but the society gratifies them with stipends. I made case for the 'yellow fever' traffic warden right in the middle of the road in the harmattan cold or under the heat of the sun, inhaling all manner of emissions from the cars all the while knowing a brake failure, an irresponsible driver and it could be all over for him. I salute them each time I witness their devil- may- care attitude as they break into impressive dance steps even as they wave drivers on or to stop at their junctions.
I thought I was through till Hajo decided the 'mai daddawa'(locust bean maker), deserves a salute too. I could not hold my laughter, for she was on a plate of 'tuwo' and 'miyar kuka'( baobab leaves soup) it was hilarious. 'Dadawa' is the main ingredient in that dish. She wouldn't agree it was 'santi' that brought about her salute. 'Look, the locust beans maker is found in every tribe in Nigeria. This woman makes this condiment necessary in a lot of our traditional soups.
Do you know the effort that goes into making 'daddawa'? And despite the difficulty in making it, and the stink as it ferments, the smell, as they work on it, it's still the cheapest soup condiment in the market. Even as no northerner will certify a 'miyar' 'kuka' bona fide without a well cooked 'daddawa' giving it its very unique essence as the backbone of the soup.' With the expression on Hajo's face and the determination with which she was making her case, I sure was glad Hajo wasn't a 'daddawa' maker for she certainly would have caused a hike in price!
Somehow it's like, those with the most difficult or the dirtiest jobs are actually the least paid, but why? Have you ever thought of the amount of time and energy that goes into "fura' making?, 'Man gyada'(groundnut oil) is basically still made the same old way, women exert so much energy, put in so much labour for these things to come into being.
After watching them at work you can't help but thank God that you came at a time there's less dependency on the mortar. The skilful artisan who carves it out of a tree, a genius in his own right, for in spite improvising with dried yam flakes we know nothing compares to a well pounded yam!
A salute to these artful creators who use their brain and brawn to make inputs in our lives this is in appreciation. Salute! to these people who provide us essential services we take for granted, you compliment our lives. Take heart, you live in a nation where the teacher important as he is to the society, knows that his reward will only come in heaven. I pray you find yours there too, for should you decide to charge for your sweat on per hour ratings there'd be chaos.

Original_Search_Title: Robbery Suspect Opens Fire at Policemen in a Bid to Escape
Headline: Nigeria: Robbery Suspect Opens Fire at Policemen in a Bid to Escape
Body_Text:
An amazing drama unfolded itself last Wednesday at Lafenwa Police division in Ogun State, after a robbery suspect suddenly pulled out a pistol from his waist and opened fire, in his bid to avoid arrest. The fearful development, saw most people at the station taking to their heels. Unfortunately, a man was hit by a stray bullet.
The situation was however, saved by some policemen who fired the suspect in the hand as he attempted to escape through the gate, consequent upon which his gun fell.
The suspect who later identified himself as Sunday Eroh, was reportedly found with N1.5 million and a bag containing arms and ammunition. The Benue State born, who resides in Okokomaiko area of Lagos State, reportedly visited his friend last Wednesday, in the latter's mechanic workshop in Ogun State.
Trouble started for Sunday, after attempt by his friend, Friday Onoh to ascertain the content of a bag he came with, met stiff resistance. Out of curiosity, Friday was said to have secretly searched the bag when Sunday went to eat in a buka, only to discover to his shock that the contents were fire arms and ammunition.
Apparently scared, Friday was said to have tricked his friend right to the police station where he handed him over to the police.
According to him, "no one believed me at first, until a Corporal decided to invite Sunday to the counter. At that point, he suspected I could be up to something and he refused. When the policeman insisted he went inside, he tucked his hand into his trouser,brought out a gun and started shooting. I never knew he was this vicious. I just wanted to stay out of trouble that was why I went to the police".
During investigation, the suspect according to the Ogun State Commissioner of Police, Mr Musa Daura, revealed that he stole the money from his girl-friend back in Lagos State, whom he claimed was a commercial sex worker. Describing the suspect as a defiant robbery suspect, Daura noted that he could be a member of a suspected robbery gang who apparently came to Ogun to spend his loot, wondering where a commercial sex worker could get such amount as claimed by Sunday.
But in this interview, Sunday admitted to be a member of a robbery gang that had been terrorizing residents of Okokomaiko area and its environs but maintained that he stole the recovered amount from his girlfriend whose name he gave as Angela.
According to him, " Honestly, I stole the money from my girlfriend who is a commercial sex worker at TVM Yam hotel located at Alabagun, in Okokomaiko. I will not lie to you. Yes, I am a robber and I got into robbery during my okada business, after I met some young men who promised I could get good money from the business they would introduce me into" .
Asked how many times he had gone on an operation with members of his gang, he kept mute and simply stated that they were responsible for a number of operations along Badagry expressway.
Amazingly, his friend Friday who handed him over to the police was also arrested. But clarifying the command's position, the CP said after investigation was concluded, he would be set free.
As Police kill two suspects, arrest one
A four-man robbery gang engaged a team of policemen attached to Ikenne division in Ogun State, in a shoot-out that lasted fifteen minutes, Monday.
However at the end of the gun duel at Cemetery road, two suspected members of the robbery gang were shot dead. Another member of the gang according to the command's spokesman, Mr Olumuyiwa Adejobi, an Assistant Superintendent of Police(ASP), escaped with bullet wound while the forth was arrested by the policemen.
The arrested suspect who identified himself as John David, during preliminary investigation revealed that members of his gang were fleeing from a robbery scene in Lagos. According to him, they snatched a Toyota Camry- vehicle from its owner at Maroko, in Victoria Island area of Lagos the previous day.
Owner of the Toyota Camry with plate number ER 339 LSR according to him, was intercepted at about 12 noon and ordered to surrender the keys, claiming they did not harm him.
Continuing, the 28-year-old suspect said, " We were four in number and we all live in Lagos. We were into snatching of vehicles and selling same in Ogun State. I only joined the gang three months ago and had gone on operation with them four times. In one of the operations, policemen from Lagos shot one of our colleagues in the leg. After that, we had to stay away from robbery for a month before we decided to embark on this one".
At the end of the gun duel, the command's spokesman said two locally made cut-to size guns, cartridges and the abandoned stolen Camry were recovered at the scene, adding that the suspect had made useful statement to detectives at the State Criminal Investigation Department, Elewe-Eran, that could lead to the arrest of his fleeing colleague.

Original_Search_Title: Police Rescue 'Witch' From Angry Mob
Headline: Nigeria: Police Rescue 'Witch' From Angry Mob
Body_Text:
The entire Iwajuwa street in Gowon Estate area of Lagos was yesterday thrown into disarray following the discovery of a lady right on top of the roof of a three storey- building on number 4.
The lady's confession on how she found herself on the roof startled all present as she claimed she was a witch on a mission.
Although she did not reveal her identity but she kept begging to be allowed to fly back to Cross River State, where she claimed to have come from.
Occupants of the building were jolted from sleep at the wee hours of yesterday morning, following a loud bang on the roof. None of them could however, go out to ascertain the cause of the bang which they mistook for an explosion.
However at about 5am, one of the occupants who was on his way to work, was reportedly attracted by a strange noise from the top of the building where to his surprise he noticed a human form and thereby raised an alarm.
Effort to bring down the lady who had a scary look failed at first. But when she was eventually brought down, it was difficult to ascertain whether she was a man or a woman. Immediately, the inquisitive occupants decided to know how she got there.
After must persuasion, the lady, revealed that she was a witch sent all the way from Obubra in Cross Rivers State to afflict her victim with epilepsy.She claimed to have flown from Cross Rivers to Lagos on a coco yam leaf. However, trouble started for her upon reaching the building of her victim whom she refused to disclose his name.
According to her, " when I got there, dogs started barking on sensing my presence. In my confused state, I lost balance and landed hard on the roof in my human form".
After her revelation, she was dragged to the middle of the road where an angry mob descended on her and tore her clothes into shreds, where she was discovered to be a lady on seeing her flabby breasts. The angry mob, who demanded at the same time for more confession, insisted to no avail that she revealed who her victim was..
But at a point, she reportedly became violent and stood up to challenge her attackers. She reportedly threw stones and dangerous objects at them, causing everyone to take to his heels , with mothers desperately calling their children to flee the scene.
But some brave young men were able to disarm her and were at the verge of lynching her when policemen from Gowon Estate arrived and saved the situation. She reportedly put up a stiff resistance and attempted to bite any of the policemen, until she was handcuffed and whisked away.
One of the residents who spoke to Crime Alert on condition of anonymity said, " we heard the loud noise on the roof at about 3am . We could not ascertain whether it was an explosion or robbery operation. We all stayed awake until someone raised alarm that somebody was on top of the building. We know there is nothing like witchcraft in the law that is why most people wanted to kill her before the police arrived and saved her."

Original_Search_Title: A Nation Blighted By Religion
Headline: Nigeria: A Nation Blighted By Religion
Body_Text:
In Nigeria, we are very religious. The number of churches and mosques in every street corner across the country testifies to the fact that we believe in God as the solver of every problem - spiritual, economic and political. The Muslims pray five times a day, as is demanded by the teachings of the Holy Quran.
The Christians (those of the Pentecostal movement), evoke Holy Ghost fire to consume everything consumable that seem to be hindrances to their success in life.
The less charismatic ones of the Catholic, Methodist, Anglican and Baptist denominations pray in their own ways for breakthroughs and God's intervention in their lives, the life of their family members, their financial situation and the way the country is governed.
If you think that religious genuflecting ends in the Church or mosques, you must be kidding. People still worship Ogun, Sango, Obatala, Orunmila, Igbe and Esu. People still offer sacrifices of esuru (water yam), epo pupa (palm oil), efun (white chalk) etc at junctions in places as cosmopolitan and civilised as Lagos and in fact, even in places as dangerous as Third Mainland Bridge. They do it by night.
In Nigeria, we do not pray for ourselves; we go to the pastor to bind and cast out the evil spirit that we believe may be dwelling in us and making it difficult for us to excel in our chosen endeavour; we go to the imam for adua and prescribed repetitive verses; we ask for ointments and material mediums; we want to see and hear God answer us here and now, like the God that answers by fire in the Old Testament. It is therefore not surprising to see churches like Jehovah Sharp Sharp Church.
To many religious people when the answer to a prayer is taking time in coming, we lose hope in the efficacy of the process outlined by a particular man of God and even doubt 'his powers' and closeness to God or his deity. We are impatient; we leave him and his slow ways to a more efficacious and effective man of God.
There is religion all around us. There is religion in our conversation, in our politics and in our business.
Be careful when someone tells you "I am a Christian, I will not cheat you". The truth is that the commodity he just sold to you is fake and he has just reaped you off your hard-earned naira. The fake drug merchants that Dora Akunyili fought to the point of almost death in her days as National Agency for Food and Drug Administration and Control (NAFDAC) Director General, were adherents of some kind of religion. Be very careful when some people tell you that "Islam forbids me to lie"; When they tell you good morning, you might want to double check that it is in fact not midnight. We are quick to brandish our faith as the basis for which people should believe us, accept us, trust us, follow us, open their homes and their entire lives to us.
Right now, they even tell us that a particular candidate is the one 'appointed' and 'ordained' by God to lead us to the Promised Land. Where did the ordination take place? Who was the officiating minister? But a lot of people are likely to believe this and act on the basis of how frequent this campaign goes on television, radio and the print media. Where else to gain currency and a stamp of authority than the mass media? We make superstars of people. For those who understand scriptures, does this not amount to using the name of the Lord in vain?
There is a problem when a political leader goes to a spiritual leader for 'endorsement' (prayers, blessings, if you insist) in a very religiously divided country like ours and allow himself to be seen by the whole world in a position of submission, nay in a position of weakness.
We tend to want to play on God's intelligence and emotions like He does not know the number of strands of hair on our head; like He did not design every freckle on our face even the ones we cannot see with our naked eyes.
In Nigeria, what we care about is whether the president is a Christian or a Muslim and what agenda he will be pushing when he gets into power.
We care about what mode of worship will be dominant in Aso Rock. We care about if the number-one citizen carries the Bible, then the number two should clutch the Quran.
Minutes after they get into office, they are at each other's throat because of distrust and greed and hidden agenda and selfishness, then it becomes a case of when two elephants fight the grass and trees suffer.
Sometimes I wonder what they think about peace, love (love your neighbour as yourself) tolerance and the need to serve the greater good of humanity.
Surely most of them do not read the Books.
Politicians are not men of faith; they are not miracle workers.
They should therefore not be judged by what they pray to in the morning. They should be assessed by the lives they have affected with their policies.
Men in office donate magnificent buildings to God. To God? Well, thank God He does not dwell in places made by mortal men, places built by the sinful, blood-filled hands of men.
A particular leader of a certain African country built a Basilica in his country for God, a country full of hungry men and women, of homeless children and destitute, of humans eking out a living and with no clothes on their backs.
Religion is supposed to draw us to God whatever that gives us inner peace; it should also make us closer to our neighbour, be our brothers (and sisters) keeper and watch out for each other. This is the only place we can be human.
Why do we sacrifice humanity on the altar of bigotry? Why do we allow men in power and in the corridor of power point to our differences instead of our strength?

Original_Search_Title: Finding the Spirit Miles Away From Home
Headline: Uganda: Finding the Spirit Miles Away From Home
Body_Text:
It is December of 2008, and I am in Savannah, Georgia with my best friend and her sister. It is a confusing time.
I am happy to be with Joanna in a whole new country. We are running around malls, and visiting islands. We are putting up our first 'independent' Christmas tree. But I am sad. In fact, we are both sad, because for the first time, we are not going to be in Kampala for Christmas.
We put on brave faces. We say to each other, "This year, we are going to create new Christmas traditions." We secretly buy presents and slip them under the Christmas tree. On Christmas morning, we drive to the neighbourhood church. We nod and attempt to join the choir in an eager, off-key rendition of "Silent Night." I try very hard not to think about the KPC Cantata. But I do not succeed; in my mind's eye, those Kampalans become veritable angels--so gorgeous, so vibrant, so Christmas-y is their singing.
For lunch, we are invited to eat with friends of Joanna's sister. We are relieved, because then, we do not have to cook. When we arrive, we find ourselves in the midst of a large, boisterous family. They welcome us, and present us, with a spread of mac and cheese. There is more food--lots of it--but it is not matooke, or chapatti, or rice and minced meat; it's not fried tilapia with lemon slices, or smoked fish with sweet potatoes. It is good food--but it tastes like it was meant for someone else.
In the morning--I forgot to mention this--right before church, we called home to say, "Merry Christmas!" With an eight hour difference, we had to get it in quick. Over the phone, my brothers and sisters described their Christmas presents to me. "Daddy got us clothes from Ethiopia," they said. "They are cool! You should have seen us at church, we were so smart!" I found myself fighting back tears from my eyes, but still I managed to say all bright and cheerful, "That's nice! You better send me pictures!"
Christmas, I think, is the best time of the year. It is a time to spend with family or with the people you love. If you're with your family this year, hug them close. Buy something for your mother that she has been meaning to buy for a while. Talk to that uncle you hardly ever see. Help out in the kitchen with a smile. Dance with your family under the stars. Make some more unforgettable memories. I'll be spending Christmas with friends again this year, but without the blues, this time. You see, I have come to realise that friends, well, they are the family you get to choose.
Happy holidays, everyone!

Original_Search_Title: Don't Break Your Nails. Hire a Chef
Headline: Uganda: Don't Break Your Nails. Hire a Chef
Body_Text:
Before the first cock crowed, she would be up and light the fire, throw on a black pot of tea and get down to peeling about three bunches of matooke.
Then if she thought she needed a hand, she would wake up one daughter to fetch the water, one son to split the wood, one grandchild to sweep the compound and another son to pick out the fattened rooster from the coop and slaughter it. By 5am, the saucepan of matooke was cooking on the fire and she was now dicing the meat as she struggled to see her work table with the aid of the paraffin lamp - tadooba.
This was my grandmother putting together a Christmas meal for her children and grandchildren who had travelled from the big city to Maguluka village in Kalungu East, Masaka district. By the time she was done at 2pm, she had missed church and smelled like she had walked out of a burning game park. We had talked about how much we were going to eat, which part of the chicken was to be picked out, that when the food was finally put in front of us we could only eat as much as our stomachs could carry.
Most of the time, half of the food went to waste because grandma always cooked too much. Today she is too old to care. If the visitors do not come with someone to do the cooking, then the little cousins will throw together a regular meal. From where I am sitting people do more cooking, baking, gift giving, binge drinking and merrymaking than celebrating the life of the man who came to save them, Jesus Christ.
I am not saying that don't pull all the stops, but the idea of waking up at the crack of dawn to cook a single meal is just too much to bear. Shopping, baking, chopping, dicing, spicing, seasoning, grating, pounding, making the fire, fanning it - will somebody please get that baby out of the saucepan?
Festive cooking is just too much a burden; better to hire a chef. With a chef, you sit down and share ideas on the menu. You present your ideas and the chef presents professional advice. While sometimes your cooking may taste the same, the chef knows how to cook different dishes of chicken, three servings of meat. Going with the chef to the market helps you pick out the best spices for every dish.
And these chefs know prices of every corner of the market. On D-day, all you do is pick up the chef, hand her the knives, saucepans and sigiri and she gets busy as you hit the showers. Then you and your whole family can dress up and go to church and actually sink into that sermon.
When you are doing the cooking, you promise to go for the 8am service but by 8am the meat is not even diced. So, you push it to 10am but by then you are only peeling the Irish potatoes. At 11am you excuse yourself and run to church before the service ends at 12:30pm.
While there, all you can think about is that the pilao is possibly burning. You are tempted to call and ask if the turkey is tender, but instead force yourself to listen to the preacher talk about baby Jesus in a manger. But with a chef, your soul can follow the three wise men and picture the peace and sweetness of that holy night.
No hustle, no broken finger nails and no spices stinking in your weave-on. Some chefs are so good they even wait for you to finish eating and they do the dishes. All you need is your wallet.
A friend of mine has hired a chef to cook at her house for Shs 50,000. But watch out for the chefs who come with big bags. They will make off with a kilo of meat, two kilos of rice, half a kilo of sugar, plates, spoons and saucepans. Now go out and find that chef.

Original_Search_Title: Christmas At Ole Sereni
Headline: Kenya: Christmas At Ole Sereni
Body_Text:
What are your plans for celebrating Christmas this year? High taxes, rising inflation, government tariffs, import mismanagement as well as fuel and food shortages have seen the cost of living in 2011 hit a record high!
It is no surprise then that this year many Kenyans will be tightening their belts when it comes to Christmas holidays.
If you are staying in Nairobi, this need not be a depressing festive season.
Ole Sereni Hotel, amongst others venues, have a great programme lined up for Christmas Eve, Christmas Day, Boxing Day and New Year's Eve.
Here's a bit of what to expect.
CHRISTMAS EVETea @ Sh1,000 per person
Start at 4pm with tea and coffee set up on the Waterhole Terrace (named Chai Lounge for the occasion).
It is no ordinary tea setting - there will be a musical performance, as well as some exquisite selection of gourmet sandwiches and nibbles: tuna and cucumber, egg and watercress, cheese, glazed ham and red onion sandwiches.
Mini smoked salmon and cream cheese muffins. Mini scones, individual Victoria sponges and chocolate fudge cakes and glazed strawberry tartlets, just to name a few of the delicacies to expect.
Dinner at the Ngong Pool Bar @ Sh3,000 per person
The barbecue and live station feast starts with items like chicken liver parfait, red onion jam, brioche, marinated baby mozzarella and olive bread croutons.
For mains you can try whole rib of aged Kenyan beef carvery style, char sui pork from the wok, or even the glazed whole chicken on the spit.
You could also try the seafood selection: prawns in the shell brushed with garlic butter, fish fillet wrapped in foil with lemon and herbs and whole baked kingfish. There are several vegetarian choices as well.
This is an eat-as-much-as-you-can barbeque so try a little of everything, but leave room for some of the individualised desserts.
CHRISTMAS DAYBush Breakfast - from Sh5,000 a person
Start the big day in a unique way with breakfast on the wild side. It starts at 6am with a cup of freshly brewed coffee at the hotel.
Depart Ole Sereni at 6.15am for an hour and a half long game drive in the Nairobi National Park, then head to the Impala Observation Point where a feast will be laid out.
With the option of adding champagne, the several course breakfast starts with fresh juices, fruits and cereals (including original Swiss Bircher muesli) followed by a selection of baked goods: croissants, chocolate croissants, cinnamon rolls, muffins and Danish pastries.
Hot breakfast includes different types of sausage, bacon, beefsteaks, sautéed mushrooms and leeks, creamed spinach, sautéed sweet potatoes, eggs and pancakes. The breakfast ends at 10am.
Christmas Lunch at the Big Five @ Sh4,200 per person, children under 10 free
The main meal on the big day is a sumptuous feast set at the Big Five restaurant overlooking the park. A magician performing tricks at tables and a live band will entertain guests as they sip the welcome drink and troop to the sushi table, salad table, and various other sections, including the dessert table.
There will be fine dining items such as platter of beetroot; cured, poached and smoked salmon; lemon and herb mayonnaise; pressed duck and foie gras terrine with fig and apple chutney; brioche toast; individual prawn cocktails with local lemon and brown bread wafer; and platter of home smoked chicken with organic rucola.
There will also be the traditional turkey with a variety of accompaniments, and even a coastal table with prawns and kingfish.
The desserts will include Yule log, Christmas pudding, chocolate and espresso mousses, cakes and a lot more.
If these aren't enough, you will get mini mince pies and chocolates packed in a box to take home.

Original_Search_Title: Country's Defective Political Equation
Headline: Nigeria: Country's Defective Political Equation
Body_Text:
TWO people were travelling in a car: the car owner and a companion. The glasses on the windows were raised up, sealing the car from outside atmosphere, which was presumed to be unfavourable to the driver of the car.
The travelling companion started sweating. He opined whether the glass should be wound down. The owner/driver said 'no'! The air conditioner was on, as such, there was no need to let in hot air. He fiddled with some knobs. The journey continued, and by now the passenger was sweating profusely. Without waiting for clearance from the driver, he wound down the glass to let in fresh air, (certainly not Dr. Jonathan's type) and let the driver of the car continue with his make-believe story. This short story illustrates the reality of the Nigerian situation. Humans have travelled a long way in what they term the path of development. They have modified their habitats, their methods of production, transportation, and communication.
Some misguided few within them even want to modify their societal structure, whereby instead of having a man/woman family structure, (from whose union humans reproduce themselves) they are now actively promoting homosexuality and lesbianism, from whose union spread diseases such as HIV/AIDS. In the days of old, tribes, races, clans, and so on, fought each other over the control of territories, or even for the acquisition of women. We are still fighting each other over control of territories, (even though the inhabited areas of the world are still comparatively small) and over things of emotional values to us. The governance system ought to have seen improvement: from contest on the battlefield to contests in parliaments. But elections in Black Africa are still fought as real physical combats: with deaths and mayhem following each election. Nigerian elections have conformed to the African standard. (We lead Africa according to its character you may say.) In addition to the attempt to stop irrational killings in the quest for power, democratic governance was aimed at ensuring a stable political system where human excellence could find expression.
The physical infrastructure that the scientific spirit has made feasible becomes meaningless in so far as after the completion of an edifice, a lunatic individual, group, or nation will be tempted or allowed to raze it to the ground. A nation or a people should know that you need stability in order to build anything, even if it is simply a symbiotic relationship. Killings are now fashionable Nigerian enterprises. These killings are now more in the North than in any other area of the Nigerian federation. The more the killings go on, the more the damage to the dream of the united Nigerian nation. That must be the mission or objective of its perpetrators.
What they fail to understand is this: even if Nigerians must go their separate ways, you need to have good relations with your neighbours in order to prosper. Who are direct beneficiaries from the treasuries of the respective federal, state, and local governments? I am inclined to believe that it is the employees of such governments: the political leaders and appointees of such governments, and the contractor-class. These are tiny in relation to the remaining Nigerian population. The rest of the population each engages in an enterprise suitable to him or her.
Political, religious, or senseless ethnic violence thwarts this. (This was practically demonstrated to the writer when on the way to Kano from Kaduna, roadside sellers of Irish potatoes said that the prices were high because the traders could not travel to the growing area in Plateau State due to the latest eruption of conflict. If one may ask: what is being fought for in Plateau State? Is it still the Jos North Local Government election? Is it reprisal killing that will know no end? Is it a religious agenda that says that there can only be adherents of only one particular faith in Plateau State? One would have thought that Dr Goodluck Jonathan would be interested in finding answers to these questions.
But from the perception of the uninformed like me, Jonathan is more interested in settling his score with Sylva in Bayelsa State than in the political stability of Nigeria. After that, it will be business as usual: distribution of the largesse to favoured ones, and preparing for continuity in office till death do us apart, maybe. There may be many well-meaning Nigerians who believe that they have something to offer in the proper governance of this nation. To my thinking, the elections at national level in April this year were the clearest indicators that many Nigerians are incapable of thinking outside their religious or tribal boxes. And many more Nigerians see governance at federal level simply as access to wealth.
If the nation is drifting, or if many sections are on fire, as long as Abuja and the oil wells are not on fire, let the music play on. And the masses keep dying senselessly, plus a stray corps member here and there. There are many hotspots in Western societies: there are the ghettoes, the Bronx areas, the Paris suburbs and so on. If you miss your way and stray into Harlem, you are as good as dead. So the capitalist, White supremacists, ensure that the volcano does not erupt to far places: the policy of containment. What is really tragic is that Nigeria cannot act as an integrated whole. This tragedy is sweet music to the ears of those on the saddle.
If Nigerians are so fractured into tribal, religious, ethnic enclaves, then they cannot come together to oust an unjust government. If a president or governor allows innocent people to be butchered to death, he sits at ease on his throne in so far as he belongs to a divide: a religious safe haven for instance. Nigeria is a tragedy. But the greater tragedy is being a Nigerian.

Original_Search_Title: Citizens in Diaspora Toast Uhuru
Headline: Zambia: Citizens in Diaspora Toast Uhuru
Body_Text:
Zambians in the diaspora are always hard-working and never miss a chance to relax away from their busy schedules when an opportunity arises.
Some celebrate the weekend, a birthday, an engagement party, amatebeto, a kitchen party, a wedding, and a baby shower, even a graduation ceremony for a college or university student.
You can always find Zambians having a good time to talk about prospects at home and what the future holds.
This year, the last two weekends of October, Zambians in the USA and Canada had 47 reasons to celebrate and to remind themselves of where they have come from.
The reason is Zambia celebrated its 47th year of Independence and of-course with a new PF government in power.
Zambians residing in Washington, Baltimore, Philadelphia, Chicago, Connecticut, Michigan, Washington State, Los Angeles, Ottawa, Toronto and Vancouver moved into celebration mode with a number of events scheduled to mark the anniversary.
In Canada, the first ever Zambia-Canada Cultural Exchange was held, strengthening ties between the two countries and raising awareness in Canada about Zambia's rich culture.
The event kicked off with a diplomatic reception in Ottawa followed by a fashion show dubbed 'Tubalange Collection' that featured works of Zambian fashion designers Emilia Torabi and Jana Hanzel.
The evening culminated with a performance by one of Zambia's premiere bands, the Amayenge Cultural Ensemble.
"They just thrilled the people," said Dr Nevers Mumba, Zambia's outgoing High Commissioner to Canada, noting that the fashion show was also a hit.
The opening night set the tone for an eight day celebration of Zambian art, photography, music and fashion that began in Ottawa and concluded in Toronto.
Canadians were able to experience the marvels of Zambia through the stunning photos of Stephen Robinson, one of Zambia's top photographers, art work from Zambian luminaries including Mulenga Chafilwa which were also on display.
On October 24, an Independence Day concert was held in Toronto. Canadians and Zambian officials found time to meet in between the fashion shows and concerts to discuss ways of enhancing cultural and business ties between Zambia and Canada.
Dr Mumba said, "It has really been a thrill for me to see this work out the way it has. Every country is trying to get the attention of Canada. An event like this raises the bar for Zambia and improves other aspects of diplomatic relations."
The event could not have taken place without the help of Barrick Gold Corporation who are the owners of Lumwana Mine in Zambia.
"Barrick has just been amazing in its commitment to help increase Zambia's exposure in Canada. This is the first time Zambia has received so much prominence," hesaid.
National Arts Council Chairperson Mulenga Kapwepwe who headed the delegation to Canada also expressed happiness to the fact that artists from Zambia on this mission got wide exposure in the Canadian media.
"Through this interaction, Zambia was able to show the Canadians that there is a lot of good talent out there. We were able to make contacts at different levels which we hope will develop into further co-operation," she noted.
In Washington DC, Zambians in DC, Maryland and Virginia (DMV) staged what they called an "Independence Bash" at the Montgomery Co-Education Association Hall in Rockville.
The function was graced by Zambian Ambassador to the US Sheila Siwela who was accompanied by some Zambian diplomatic staff. The highlight of the "bash" was the historical picture slide show of Zambia's history, its people, culture and tradition.
Attire of the night was the Zambian traditional dress. It came in variety of styles and a surprising array of colours and prints.
The food, very Zambian and very sumptuous too - the most popular dish was Nshima served with fresh and dry fish, free-range chicken, ifisashi, dried beans, spinach and collards, dried caterpillars, sweet potatoes, peanuts, and cooked cabbage.
To spice it all, DJ Eddie Mwelwa (from Connecticut) rocked a lot of Dandy Krazy music and other popular Zambian tunes that kept Zambians on the floor most of the night.
The organisers of the function, Doreen Blount and Theresa Daka who are residents of Maryland couldn't have been happier as the "bash' attracted more than 250 people.
"This is the biggest number of people we have ever had coming together in a long time. We hope to attract more Zambians next time. It is one avenue of knowing each other," said Doreen.
In Los Angeles, the Independence Ball was a mixture of fun and business. The October 24 LA Team (as they prefer to call themselves), organised a grand corporate celebration - a seat-down hotel arrangement with a three meal course at the luxurious LA Marriot Hotel.
The gala event drew a record attendance of about 170 for an evening of music, dancing, and presentation of awards to deserving Zambians in the LA community and glittering fashion.
The fashion show was organised by Sylvia Litana of AIM - Global Corporation. The model clothes were provided by Africana Imports, a Zambian owned business and Africa Vibes Magazine, a Cameroonian owned business.
The big surprise of the night was simple and straight forward. A video clip of an interview of First Republican President Dr Kenneth Kaunda by CNN on a programme called African Voices that was "flighted" on two big overheard screens at the Meridien Event Centre followed by an earlier recorded discussion between Dr Kaunda and the Master of Ceremonies Robert Sichinga Jr., in which Dr. Kaunda addressed the guests in attendance.
In the clip, the former President encouraged the Diaspora to work together and play a part in Zambia's growth. He also asked everyone in attendance to have a great evening.
The Ball also presented Awards to deserving Zambians in the LA Community for their tireless efforts in promoting Zambia in good light be it in business or politics.
For instance Ms Elizabeth Sichinga received an award for facilitating the Zambia's Africa-USA Business Executive Conference held in Las Vegas early this year. She also arranged a container full of books that have been shipped to Zambia for distribution to various schools.
Towani Clarke from Lusaka was recognised for showcasing Zambian businesses trying to "infiltrate" the American market. Towani was basically scouting for Zambians in the Diaspora to be her agents.
Mbumwae Suba based in New Jersey and Chairperson of Subayu was honoured for her work in empowering Zambians in the diaspora through business education and literacy programmes.
Nomsa Chali of Nomra African Delight was honoured for facilitating Zambians in the US to order, online, foodstuff for delivery in Zambia while Chibeza Nyirenda of SG Centre in LA for being involved in Health Care training and Dr Lydia Chowa of Kyani for promoting a wellness and health drink.
Robert Sichinga Jr of Strategic Shift was awarded for helping facilitate trade between the US and Zambia. As Master of Ceremonies, Robert concluded by saying,"The Independence Ball was overwhelming, well attended and professionally handled."
Dandy Krazy also added the Independence excitement to Zambians in Connecticut, Chicago and Seattle. He was also expected to perform in Orlando before returning home.
He is also shooting a video entitled "Ulubuto" a song that talks about a beautiful woman who is appreciated by the husband.
"The Zambian community in the US has been very good and very appreciative of my hit single Don't Ukubeba. It has been a hit wherever I have performed," he said.
And CQ, OC and Whyson have also been in the USA in the last three weeks performing in Indiana and are scheduled to perform in Chicago on November, 12 2011.
They have also had a share of publicity on internet radios Zambezi fm online and the ZambiaTalk blog radio.
The author is a Press Secretary at the Embassy of the Republic of Zambia in Washington DC.

Original_Search_Title: Coimbra Re-Visited
Headline: Zimbabwe: Coimbra Re-Visited
Body_Text:
OVER the years, whenever I reviewed the hugely popular Cascais Portuguese Restaurant, which used to be on Samora Machel Avenue in the CBD, I felt compelled to write about its main competitor, Coimbra, with its own myriad fans, a few hundred metres away soon afterwards: in the interest of fairness.And vice-versa!
Sometimes I looked at both Lusitanian eateries in the same article, staging an imaginary cook-off between their respective chefs. Almost invariably I decided Cascais' piri-piri chicken was the best in the country, but Coimbra often cooked better chips.
Now Cascais has changed name to Paula's Place, moving away from the gloomy (at night) CBD, with its broken streets and pavements, no street lighting, perpetually out-of-order traffic robots, missing road white lines, pan-handlers, pimps, prostitutes, muggers, druggies and "drivers" who should never be allowed behind a wheel this side of hell.Paula's is now at the Samora Machel intersection with Glenara and has taken the local catering scene by storm.
Lunch and supper it pumps. It obviously attracted much trade from established competitors and the many newcomers opening almost weekly, under the sad illusion catering is a limitless cash cow and licence to print money!I fairly gushingly reviewed Paula's Place in this newspaper in August, having visited on the holiday Tuesday, five days after they'd opened to an appreciative public; the Greendale Good Food & Wine Appreciation Society held its October lunch there. On both occasions every chair was taken; people without bookings were sometimes politely turned away.One story, almost certainly apocryphal, relates to a party of turbaned Sikhs rocking up for late lunch a week or two ago. "Do you have a reservation?" they were asked civilly at the door."Oh, no, my dear sir, goodness me...we're not those sort of Indians!"
"Yellow Doors"But I digress. As usual! On Thursday night I re-visited Coimbra. It has lost none of its popularity and I've been eating there since it was a rather picaresque (no picturesque!) shebeen, nicknamed "Yellow Doors", where patrons drinking illegally were advised to keep a local $20 note handy in case the cops raided the gaffe!Parking, last week, was at least 100 metres from the door.
I was given positively the last seat in the main semi-alfresco dining area on a breathless muggy night. All around me I heard breathless talk of "tiger": the 50th annual Kariba Invitation Tiger Fishing Tournament, which I was leaving for less than 30 hours later.I told a tourism professional from Vic Falls how several top travel agents from across the globe, brought here at vast expense by ZTA for the Sanganai expo, were less than impressed with this country when, having stopped at Rusape, en route to Nyanga, in a fruitless attempt to buy up-to-date (or indeed any) maps of the area they were questioned, abused and threatened by stern-faced plain-clothed government functionaries for the heinous offence of photographing a kombi queue and street market.She topped that with a tale of a honeymoon couple who, having left the Falls with an ivory bracelet but no CITES certificate, were arrested at Bole International Airport in Addis Ababa, flying back to the UK via Ethiopian Airlines, and spent two nights incarcerated in durance vile in smelly foul prison cells until the US$120 artifact's provenance could be verified.What a way to end a honeymoon! Truly, Africa wins again!
