Depending on what you are like in real life, the games you play in the bedroom should introduce a totally new character. And many of these games come with 'props' - call them toys - if you wish.
The uniforms, the batons (not that you plan on whacking anyone), the massage oils, name it; just tap into your wild side. Everybody has one; it is the degree that varies.

Original_Search_Title: Vietnamese Cuisine Delights Nairobians
Headline: Kenya: Vietnamese Cuisine Delights Nairobians
Body_Text:
Chef Visit Programme, for one week starting last Monday, their guests had the pleasure of sampling Vietnamese food courtesy of visiting chefs Philippe Bischoff, the Executive Chef of Fairmont Nile City in Cairo, and Mai Phuong Nguyen, Vietnamese Chef de Cuisine at Saigon Bleu Fairmont Nile City. The Guest Chef Visit Programme gives international chefs from various Fairmont hotels and resorts around the globe the opportunity to provide local clientele with gastronomical delights from exotic locations stretching from the Far East to North America.
The Vietnamese-French Cuisine tour featured signature dishes from the Saigon Bleu Restaurant such as tantalising Duck rillettes with Vietnamese coriander on French baguette, zesty Galangal and lemongrass-scented chicken coconut soup, live Chicken Pho, Crisp fried sea bass with garlic, red chili and dill sauce, luscious Sautéed beef tenderloin with hot basil, shallots and young pepper, and popular Stir fried vegetables and sweet potatoes. Some of the heart-melting desserts included Mango with sticky rice and sweetened coconut milk and dulcified Passion fruit soufflé.
Tatu Restaurant showcased these savoury dishes from May 10-14 and guests had the opportunity to meet and interact with Executive Chef Philippe Bischoff.
Bischoff was the third Guest Chef being hosted at Fairmont The Norfolk. Previous guests included Executive Chef Christoph Ruffer, Fairmont Hamburg, Germany, and Executive Chef Maryan Gandon, Fairmont Monte Carlo, Monaco.
Philippe Bischoff who has unparalleled culinary skills in Vietnamese-French cuisines has worked in some of the world's finest establishments such as Hotel de la Paix, Geneva; Auberge du Raisin, Culy, Switzerland; Victoria & Alfred Hotel, Cape Town, South Africa; Mandarin Oriental, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia; and is currently based at Fairmont Nile City, Cairo, Egypt.
While his co-chef Mai first learnt the secrets of Vietnamese dishes from her mother's kitchen. Her exposure to different cultures has heightened her proficiency in crafting unique dishes. Her outstanding skills led her to be the catering chef for welcoming parties for both ex-US President Bill Clinton and ex-Russian President Vladimir V. Putin. If you missed the action last week, be sure to catch the Executive Chef of London Savoy, UK, who is set to dazzle Nairobians in the course of the year.

Original_Search_Title: Sweet Potato Prices Are Infleunced By Weather Not Fuel
Headline: Uganda: Sweet Potato Prices Are Infleunced By Weather Not Fuel
Body_Text:
For every customer that comes around at his stall, Mr Lawrence Musaayi, a sweet potato vendor in Owino market makes sure to say the price for each sweet potato pile that he has made.
To entice the customers, he promises to reduce the price if one buys more than a pile though they would end up buying one.
"With these perishable products, you have to make sure you sell them off so that they don't get damaged. If you don't sell the potatoes as soon as possible, you stand to lose out," he explains.
He says that within a week, the potatoes should be sold out. If possible, they should be sold out in less than a week.
Ms Janat Nakawesa, a sweet potato vendor at Nakaseero market, agrees with Mr Musaayi and says that if potatoes are kept in a sack, it will take only four days to get spoiled and rot.
However, she says that for one to keep the potatoes for two weeks or even a month, they can sort out the damaged and undamaged potatoes and separate them. In that case, if the potato is not damaged, it can stay for a longer period of time. "You can start sorting the potatoes when you have had the sack for four days," she advises.
If you have to keep sweet potatoes for a month, then that means that you bought way too many. Otherwise, Ms Nakawesa says that the sweet potatoes come next to matooke in terms of market for them.
Ms Nakawesa and Mr Musaayi say that the sweet potatoes that are sold and eaten in the country come from different areas of the country though most of them come from the central region. The regions that grow sweet potatoes include; Namulonge, Wakiso, Mukono, Gomba, Bombo, Luwero, Bugerere, Kyaggwe, Masaka, Gulu, Busoga region, Masindi, Hoima, Mbale and Soroti.
Ms Nakawesa says that the best sweet potatoes come from Masaka, Soroti, Mbale and Busoga region. "Though, most people love the ones from Busoga mostly because they are silky when prepared."
Many times, it is hard to tell the quality of the potato- whether it is good or bad. Ms Nakawesa says that one can ask from whom they are buying from and will be told which potatoes are nice. However, Mr Musaayi says that in this day and age, he sells to finish off what he has bought so that he doesn't entertain people who look for particular types of sweet potatoes.
"Most people who sell these potatoes also don't differentiate them; so, you will be lied to anyway because what one wants is to sell off everything."
The potatoes, according to Ms Nakawesa, will take up to three or four months before they grow. Since the rains just started two months back, she says that the potatoes are still few on market (not as much as expected).
She predicts that with the rains that are constantly falling now, the potatoes will be more in the month of July. "In July and August, we shall have too many potatoes floating. The price of a sack may fall down to Shs60,000."
According to Mr Musaayi, for foods like sweet potatoes, it's not the increase in prices of fuel or any other commodity that will determine the prices but rather the rainfall. "It's in July where one will get to have the same number of potatoes we are selling now at a lesser price.
While Mr Musaayi says that the rains have not helped much in bringing in more potatoes on market and thus affecting the prices, Ms Nakawesa says that the rains have helped because the sack has reduced by Shs20,000. A sack costs between Shs70,000 and Shs160,000.

Original_Search_Title: Kemri to Develop Nyama Choma-Flavoured Condoms Soon
Headline: Kenya: Kemri to Develop Nyama Choma-Flavoured Condoms Soon
Body_Text:
The shortage of condoms has forced some Kenyans to wash and re-use theirs, and that spurred the government-funded medical research agency Kemri into action.
Yesterday scientists at the Kenya Medical Research Institute announced that they would soon be manufacturing and launching a new range of flavoured condoms.
However, unlike those in existence already, which have exotic and fruity flavours such as strawberry, chocolate and banana, the new Kemri condoms will be flavoured with tastes of traditional African foods.
There will be a nyama choma flavour, a pilau flavour, a sweet potato flavour and for those into hot and spicy encounters there will also be a kachumbari flavoured condom on sale in the shops within a few weeks time"We've asked men and women what they want," said Kemri spokesperson Shama Mbaya. "They told us they love the taste of meat, not bananas. And as a result a group of new flavours have been developed, including the Nyama Choma, which reproduces the taste of charcoal-grilled goat ribs."

Original_Search_Title: Coalition Squabbles Could Spark Violence - Nyagah
Headline: Kenya: Coalition Squabbles Could Spark Violence - Nyagah
Body_Text:
Nairobi — The push and pull between the coalition principals over appointments to key state jobs could lead to a throwback to the 2008 post-election violence, a cabinet minister has warned.
Co-operative Minister Joseph Nyagah raised the red flag over the degeneration of relations between President Kibaki and Prime Minister Raila Odinga, which he said was brewing a similar atmosphere to the one that preceded the 2007 presidential general election.
"Kenyans should condemn these wrangles which have divided the country into two parallel lines. Kenyans should not allow politicians to divide them or else the country may break away," said Nyagah.
The minister took issue with the hard-line tactics adopted by the handlers of the two principals over the recent nominations for the positions of Chief Justice, Attorney General, Director of Public Prosecutions and Controller of budget.
"I condemn in the strongest terms possible those who surround the two principals for trying to create division between the two. These are the people causing the fights. Let us support them (the principals) for the sake of the country which must remain our number one priority."
"It will be very unfortunate if Kenyans allow another round of violence. Politicians are out to get power oblivious of the consequences," he said.
Nyaga made the remarks over the weekend during the launch of the Kabondo Sweet Potato Cooperative Society in Rachuonyo South district.

Original_Search_Title: Short-Changed And Angry
Headline: Zimbabwe: Short-Changed And Angry
Body_Text:
Harare — People in Zimbabwe are becoming angry about the lack of small denominations in circulation and tempers are fraying as a result: A policeman recently shot dead a taxi assistant for failing to give him the correct change.
After the formation of a coalition government in February 2008, the hyperinflation-afflicted economy was dollarized - with the US dollar and South African rand most widely used, but the Botswana pula, the Zambian kwacha and the Mozambican metical also in common use.
To avoid disputes, taxis now give out travel vouchers when they run short of change - and the problem is not just in the transport sector.
Sipho Mpofu, a public sector employee, went grocery shopping last week and was given a brown voucher instead of change. "When I asked them what it was for, they told me that they could not provide me with change and the voucher worth five rand would allow me to use their toilets for free. I threw away the offending piece of paper because I knew I was being ripped off."
The lack of change angers many consumers, who are now trying to make purchases in round numbers. Shops use items such as tomatoes, matches, eggs, potatoes, candles, bananas, sweets, pens, pencils or vouchers in lieu of change.
Mpofu said the use of "unwanted" grocery items was a "huge inconvenience".
The lack of change angers many consumers, who are now trying to make purchases in round numbers
"Right now I have a huge pile of matches, candles and sweets which I have no use for. In fact, they pose a threat should they be set alight accidentally." He said he had to hide the sweets from his children.
Financial journalist Paul Nyakazeya said consumers were effectively being forced to buy items they did not want.
"At the end of the day, when calculations are made, it may be discovered that the goods consumers end up taking as change... make up a substantial percentage of their monthly groceries... The best way out of this quagmire for the consumers would be the widespread introduction of an electronic system to purchase commodities."
But, with frequent power cuts, especially in rural areas, Nyakazeya acknowledged it would be very difficult to make such a system work.
Economist David Mupamhadzi told IRIN the authorities urgently needed to introduce smaller currency denominations, especially for the South African rand: Many service providers round up the bill, making goods and services more expensive, eroding disposable incomes and boosting inflation, he said.
[ This report does not necessarily reflect the views of the United Nations ]

Original_Search_Title: Laced Wedding Beer Lands 150 in Hospital
Headline: Rwanda: Laced Wedding Beer Lands 150 in Hospital
Body_Text:
Gisagara — 151 residents of Murambi village, Ramba cell in Mamba sector, Gisagara District, were rushed to Gakoma Hospital after consuming poisoned beer at a wedding.
According to local residents and authorities, the residents complained of serious stomach-aches, headaches and diarrhoea.
The first patients were admitted at the hospital Sunday night, on Christmas. Earlier in the day, they had attended a local marriage ceremony.
An eyewitness told The New Times: "Everyone who consumed the sorghum beer which was prepared for the feast fell sick".
"It is only those who drank soda and banana juice that were not affected ...It was very terrible. One would start complaining of stomach-aches and headaches, but within a period of about 15 minutes they would no longer be able to walk steadily," said Jean Damascene Ngezahayo, another eyewitness.
"We started transporting them on traditional stretchers, but the number of patients was growing very quickly. We called for ambulances but they could not carry all the affected, until when a military vehicle arrived".
Residents suspect that the locally-made sorghum beer might have been contaminated as people were busy preparing for the reception of the marriage ceremony.
"I am very disappointed and saddened by what happened. About 50 members of my family were affected, including three of my children and my wife. Anyone who did this wanted to kill all of us," said Narcisse Kanyandekwe, the father of the bride.
At the time The New Times visited Gakoma Hospital on Tuesday no patient had been discharged, but most were steadily recovering.
Inside the hospital, some of the sick lay down outside the consultation room waiting for doctors.
Efforts to get a comment from the director of the hospital, Dr Fulgence Nkikabahizi, were futile as he declined to comment.
The Ministry of Health has deployed a team of experts to follow up the situation, though results of the tests carried out to determine the cause were not yet out as samples had been sent to Kigali.
This paper also learnt that no patient was allowed to leave the hospital until the real cause of their complications was established.
Family members who sought to visit their relatives were barred from accessing hospital premises.
Possible Cholera outbreak
In a related development, seven people were admitted at Gakoma Hospital following an outbreak of what is suspected to be cholera, according to local residents.
All the affected are residents of Runazi village, Ramba cell in Mamba sector.
Local authorities said the first patient was admitted two weeks ago, with more cases surfacing in the course of last week.
All the patients presented the same symptoms: stomach-aches, diarrhoea and sometimes vomiting.
By press time, a four year child had succumbed to the infection, with at least five others still in hospital.
The acting Executive Secretary of Mamba Sector, Gilbert Nyirimanzi, said the disease could have been caused by poor hygiene, though medical tests have yet to reveal the cause.
"But we could not co-relate the two incidents as they happened at different times. We are still waiting for medical tests to see whether they are linked or not", Nyirimanzi said.
"We appeal to residents to remain calm because we are working closely with medical experts to ascertain the real cause in both cases".
Dr Nkikabahizi declined to comment on this case as well.

Original_Search_Title: ABL Crowns 80th Anniversary With Stakeholders' Luncheon
Headline: Ghana: ABL Crowns 80th Anniversary With Stakeholders' Luncheon
Body_Text:
Accra Brewery Limited (ABL), a subsidiary of SABMiller plc, as part of its 80th anniversary celebrations held a stakeholders' luncheon, over the weekend.
At the luncheon, Greg Metcalf, MD of Accra Brewery Limited said, "Accra Brewery is the first non-traditional manufacturing industry in Ghana and indeed in West Africa. When we say, "Truly Ghanaian" we say it with conviction, and with genuine pride.
Accra Brewery Limited was established during the great world Depression of the 1930s. The first few years proved to be very difficult for the pioneer brewing industry. 80 years on, we have faced many challenges but have come through them all, triumphant.
We believe those qualities of courage and faith continues to define who we are as a business and a team.
In 1997, SABMiller Plc, then known as South African Brewery, bought shares in Overseas Brewery Limited and by virtue of this partnership, Accra Brewery became a subsidiary of SABMiller. Just a couple weeks ago SABMiller announced a massive capital injection to a tune of $260million into four African countries namely Tanzania, Zambia, Uganda and Ghana."
The company's products are 'Stone Strong Lager', one of the core brands which recently won a prestigious Gold Quality Award at the International Monde Awards in Belgium; and 'Chibuku Shake Shake', a recent addition to our range, an opaque beer with very strong African traditions, and what makes it particularly exciting is that it is produced wholly from locally grown maize and sorghum, a huge step forward for our industry which is highly reliant on imported raw materials".
He also added that Voltic Limited, Ghana's number one mineral water was also part of the Accra Brewery range of products. He thanked employees, his predecessors, executive committee members, suppliers and partners, for their massive contribution to Accra Brewery's success.
The MD also thanked present and successive governments for creating an enabling environment to allow the business to thrive.
In her address, the Minister of Trade and Industry, Ms. Hannah Tetteh stated that "I am pleased to be celebrating 80 years of a truly Ghanaian business and a business that has been with us through different stages of our lives.
Dr. Charles Mensa, Chairman of Accra Brewery Board and chairman of the event, in his closing remarks congratulated Accra Brewery for achieving this milestone. He asked Ghanaians to support Accra Brewery Limited, not only because it brews good beer but also, for being innovative and using locally produced raw materials.

Original_Search_Title: Prostate Cancer Awareness - Know the Facts
Headline: Ghana: Prostate Cancer Awareness - Know the Facts
Body_Text:
September is designated prostate cancer awareness month. Prostate cancer occurs in the prostate gland which is found just beneath the urinary bladder of men. It is about the size of 2 cashew nuts unshelled and its main purpose is to produce secretions which make up part of the semen produced by men.
Like all other cancers, prostate cancer occurs when there is uncontrolled dividing of prostate cells which have undergone change or have become abnormal. These abnormal cells group together to form a tumor and they can invade the whole prostate gland and go beyond its walls into other parts of the body.
Not all prostate cancers are the same. There are slow growing and fast growing types of prostate cancer. A man may have prostate cancer, but will not die from it because it is slow growing. Others may have an aggressive type which spreads fast and may cause death.
Like any disease, the development of prostate cancer is associated with certain risk factors. A risk factor is something that increases your chances of getting a particular disease or condition.
What are the Risk Factors for Prostate Cancer?
These include: (1) Men over the age of 50 years; (2) Black men; (3) Family History (if your father or brother has had prostate cancer); (4) Infection and inflammation of the prostate; (5) Obesity or excessive weight; (6) Diet high in fat, red meats or dairy products; and (7) Lack of exercise. In some western countries 8 out of 10 men with prostate cancer are older than 65 years of age.
Symptoms of Prostate Cancer
Prostate cancer, depending on the stage of the disease, may or may not present with symptoms. Some of the symptoms may also be similar to symptoms that men with enlarged prostates or Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia (BPH) may experience. This condition is not cancerous. Symptoms of prostate cancer include:
Poor stream of urine, difficulty starting urination, dribbling after finishing urination, pain with urination, blood in the semen, urinary frequency especially at night; painful ejaculation, unintentional weight loss and pain in lower back, pelvis and hips
Screening Tests for Prostate Cancer
A Prostate cancer patient has a greater than 95% chance of survival if it detected early before it has spread beyond the prostate gland. Screening for it is easy and there are two ways
By a digital rectal exam (DRE) - For this exam, a doctor uses a lubricated gloved finger to exam the prostate through the rectum. He or she can feel the size, shape and consistency of the gland.
If there are hard areas felt further tests are needed
Another method to screen for prostate cancer is by a blood test which measures the amount of a chemical called a PSA (Protein Specific Antigen) in your blood. It is more relevant for people who are 50 years and above, because prostate cancer tends to occur in men older than 50 years.
Men who have prostate cancer may have a high level PSA and further tests may have to be done. Some other prostate problems which are not cancer may also present with high PSA levels and so your physician will explain what other tests would be needed. When cancer is suspected a prostate biopsy is usually done.
Discuss with your doctor if you need to be screened. There are advantages and disadvantages to screening for prostate cancer. The decision to do so is determined by the risk factors mentioned above.
Every adult person can reduce their risk for prostate and other cancers by adopting a healthy lifestyle, healthy diet consisting of fruit and vegetables, fewer intakes of fatty foods, increased fiber as in whole wheat grains, oats, sorghum, beans etc., regular exercise, not smoking and alcohol in moderation
Treatment of Prostate Cancer
Early non aggressive forms can be treated by 'watchful waiting'. The cancer's progress is tracked by your doctor, however, such observation can be switched to other treatment methods if the cancer changes.
Aggressive ones may be treated by methods including surgery, radiation therapy or drug therapy. Each method has its advantages and disadvantages. Careful discussion with your doctor is important at all times. Do not feel shy in discussing these issues with your regular doctor.
Finally, a healthy lifestyle is a key prevention tool to avoid many other diseases. We are quickly developing diseases of the more developed countries due to unhealthy life styles we are adopting. Exercising by walking and controlling portion sizes of foods especially carbohydrates will go a long way to prevent heart disease, strokes, hypertension, diabetes and cancers.

Original_Search_Title: Mob Bays for Tombo Brewer's Blood
Headline: Namibia: Mob Bays for Tombo Brewer's Blood
Body_Text:
Walvis Bay — Police had to fend off a mob of around 400 angry residents from killing a traditional homebrew maker, whom they accused of fatally poisoning a patron at his drinking place.
Gabriel Shindonti, a tombo brewer in the high-density informal settlement of Ekutu Lya Tika, was assaulted by the mob during a public meeting held between municipal officials, traders and patrons of the market.
The name of the deceased, who confidential police sources claimed died of a heart attack while drinking Shindonti's brew, could not be established at the time of going to press.
The meeting was convened to discuss the patron's death. He collapsed moments after he took a few sips from Shindonti's tombo, an alcoholic beverage made from sorghum.
Patrons and fellow traders accused Shindonti of poisoning the reveller who died last Friday
Traders at the site mostly sell roasted meat and vegetables, while the majority brew traditional drinks such as tombo, okatokele, epwaka and shafuluka - a favourite pastime for many patrons at the site.
The death of the patron angered residents who consequently demanded a meeting with the municipal authorities here.
According to eyewitnesses, the meeting took a sudden turn when a group of people attacked Shindonti after he had requested the angry mob to provide evidence to back their claims that he had engineered the death.
Miraculously, the victim survived an onslaught of beatings and kicks to his body that lasted for nearly 10 minutes.
Stones were also thrown at him before the police came to his rescue.
During the commotion, the police arrested a young man who had a stone in his hand, which he intended to throw at Shindonti who was being hauled off to safety in a police van.
Another man was also arrested at the scene after a stone he threw smashed a canopy window of a police truck.
This angered the mob, which followed the police to the Kuisebmond police station charge office to demand the release of the two men.
The angry crowd stood outside the entrance of the station for nearly three hours.
Shindonti, under heavy police guard, was taken to hospital for treatment but his condition could not be confirmed at the time of going to press.
No comment was immediately available from the police.

Original_Search_Title: EABL Rules Out Rights Issue to Finance Kenya Breweries Purchase
Headline: Kenya: EABL Rules Out Rights Issue to Finance Kenya Breweries Purchase
Body_Text:
East African Breweries (EABL) has ruled out selling any extra shares to finance the re-purchase of a 20 per cent stake of Kenya Breweries (KBL) from South African beer maker SABMiller.
The brewer said it "has access to several sources of funding and is currently finalising (financing) arrangements."
"Any claims that EABL will be funding the acquisition by way of rights issue is pure speculation," said the EABL Group head of communications, Joseph Sunday, adding that the brewer "does not need to issue equity to purchase KBL," also quashing speculation that the company will float a bonus issue.
Standard Investment Bank had written in a research note sent to investors early last month that it saw EABL using a rights issue "as the most likely form of financing for the transaction, and we believe Diageo would be keen to participate in a rights offer given Africa's importance to the group in sales growth."
Analysts at Sterling Capital attributed a 3.9 per cent increase in EABL's share price on Thursday to "speculation about a likely bonus issue."EABL's share price has increased to Sh162 from Sh155 since Thursday, a 4.5 per cent increase, which came after the brewer's shareholders asked if the management was considering a bonus share issue to finance the Sh23 billion KBL acquisition."The brewer was backed by significant foreign support coupled with speculations about a likely bonus issue," said a market report by Sterling Capital on Friday.
The last time the EABL share traded above Sh160 was in early October.
Eric Musau, a research analyst at Standard Investment Bank, said that one week rally was speculative, since there were no material announcements to warrant the increase.
"If that is what investors were waiting for then it is unlikely that the prices will last at this level for very long," said Mr Musau.EABL has reserves of Sh11.2 billion as per its annual statement for the year ended 30 June 2011. The share has lost 22 per cent in value from January's Sh208 to yesterday's Sh162 price.
The NSE 20 Share Index has shed 25 per cent since the beginning of the year to 3,387.51 points from 4,495.41 points.
Robert Munuku, a research analyst at Drummond Investment Bank, said that in light of the depressed earnings, it is likely that demand for the stock will be maintained from investors who want stability.
"Blue chips are the most reliable stocks at the moment," said Mr Munuku.
Blue chip companies traditionally have a fairly stable share price even in tough times and pay dividend which is the best alternative for investors in the absence of capital gains. EABL has a 5.43 per cent dividend yield or Sh8.75 dividend per share.
"They are moving to sorghum from barley to cut costs and at the moment anything that will cut costs is good for investors," said Mr Munuku.

Original_Search_Title: Darfuri Markets Facing Inflation
Headline: Sudan: Darfuri Markets Facing Inflation
Body_Text:
El Fahser — Markets across Darfur are experiencing an unprecedented rise in the price of food grains, citizens from various cities told Radio Dabanga on Thursday.
Citizens of El Fasher, El Geneina and Nyala complained of scarcity of food grains in the market, especially sorghum and corn. The commodities that are available in the market are very expensive.
"A sack of millets that cost 280 pounds yesterday costs nearly 340 pounds today. Many other food grains have become similarly expensive," a citizen in El Fasher told Radio Dabanga. Various other cities in Darfur are experiencing similar price hikes.
He also explained that millet, a staple food grain in Darfur, had been missing from the market until Wednesday and could only be found in small quantities on Thursday. Citizens who spoke to Radio Dabanga accused merchants of hoarding. "They want to sell it high prices next summer," one them said.
A trader, on the other hand, denied this allegation and told Radio Dabanga, "We are not storing anything in our warehouses. There is just lack of supply in the market."

Original_Search_Title: Unity State Governor Concerned By Humanitarian Response in Mayom County
Headline: Sudan: Unity State Governor Concerned By Humanitarian Response in Mayom County
Body_Text:
Bentiu — Mayom county is facing serious food shortages and health facility problems after clashes between the South Sudan army, the Sudan People's Liberation Army (SPLA), and rebel militias.
The government of the Unity state has been critical of the delayed humanitarian aid response.
On the 28 October heavy fighting took place between the SPLA and the rebel South Sudan Liberation Army (SSLA) in which 75 people were killed.
The day before the attack the SSLA released a statement "calling upon all NGOs and UN personnel to leave Unity State within a week for their own safety".
The governor of Unity state, Taban Deng Gai, flew to Mayom on the 31 October with SPLA chief of staff, James Hoth Mai, and South Sudan assistant deputy defence minister, Majak Agot Atem, to asses the civilian and militarily situation in the area after the clash.
Deng held an emergency meeting on Friday calling upon the non-governmental organisations (NGOs) in Unity state to offer the residents of Mayom county urgent assistance.
"I went to Mayom and I found out big problem, a bag of sorghum of 90kg is going for more than 1000 South Sudanese Pounds [US$370], and actually it is not there in the market, the market is just clear," said Deng, asking "what are you doing? Do you have any plans and we want you to update us?"
Deng urged NGOs to assist in assessing the road that connect Unity and Lake states and noted that the road connecting Unity state to Khartoum has been closed South Sudan's independence in July.
World Food Programme (WFP) staff flew in to Mayom county last week to assess the situation of those hit by the clashes.
Nhial Puol, WFP field monitor, told Sudan Tribune "the situation is really getting worse because there is not even a single bag in the market for people to buy".
Deng expressed his anger regarding the delayed humanitarian response.

Original_Search_Title: Citizens Set Police Cars On Fire
Headline: Sudan: Citizens Set Police Cars On Fire
Body_Text:
Kassala — Enraged citizens set two police cars on fire and pelted stones at the anti-riot police office in eastern Sudan's Kassala city on Tuesday.
The actions were meant to be a protest against the killing of a teenager by the anti-riot police on Tuesday.
The deceased, 13-year-old Mohammed Idris was killed by bullet wounds to his head, after which his body was taken to the Kassala hospital morgue.
Another boy, 12-year-old Mahmoud Ali Karar sustained serious injuries when a bullet his his pelvic region. Karar was also rushed to the city hospital.
Police spokesperson Colonel Ahmed Touhami said in a press statement that Mahmoud Ali Karar was caught in a crossfire between the anti-riot police and smugglers who were smuggling sorghum into Ethiopia.
Kassala has seen a anti-government protesters take to the streets since three weeks.

Original_Search_Title: Upper Nile Governor Addresses Youth in Nairobi
Headline: Kenya: Upper Nile Governor Addresses Youth in Nairobi
Body_Text:
Juba — The Upper Nile Youth Association in Kenya was yesterday briefed by the Upper Nile Governor Simon Kun Puoch. The governor addressed the youth about the ongoing rapid development activities and competitiveness with the rest of the world. He said that H.E the President of the Republic is very serious about the development in all the ten states in the shortest time possible so that the citizens can enjoy the fruits of peace which they have struggled for.
The governor also briefed the youth about the security situation in the country and particularly in Upper Nile State. He said the situation is calm and that government security forces are in charge.
Responding to a question from Mr. Dau Mathew, the chairman of Upper Nile Youth Association, the governor explained that even though there is high inflation resulting from the depreciation of the South Sudan Pound, the situation is being dealt with seriously by institutions such as the Ministry of Finance and Economic Planning as well as the Bank of South Sudan.
The governor also explained that the government has also taken measures to contain the situation by regulating food prices as well as reducing taxes on goods and commodities so as to reduce the burden on the citizens. He reported that the price of staple food such as sorghum has come down in most of the states.
He also encouraged the youth to work hard in education and pledged the support of the government in these efforts. He also reiterated the commitment of the government to address issues affecting the youth.

Original_Search_Title: Feed in Tariff to See Final Revision By New Committee
Headline: Ethiopia: Feed in Tariff to See Final Revision By New Committee
Body_Text:
The prices offered in the bill are very cheap when compared to other African countries. Kenya offers 0.12 dollars for a kilowatt-hour for a firm energy generation of 500Kw from hydro power while Ethiopia offers 0.08 dollars.
The bill for the feed in tariff proclamation which allows the private sector to engage in power generation is to see a last revision by a committee to be established by the Ministry of Water & Energy (MoWE) in the coming weeks.
The committee will be comprised of experts from the Ministry and the Ethiopian Electric Agency, according to Wondimu Tekle, state minister for the Ministry.
"It will revise and give final comments on the bill before it is sent to the Council of Ministers," he told Fortune.
The bill prepared by the Ethiopian Electric Agency (EEA) has seen three revisions before being presented to the ministry. It allows for power generated from different sources by the private sector to be sold into the national grid.
However, different stakeholders from the private sectors have been complaining about the tariff rates set in the bill claiming that it does not consider the investment cost of generation of power and will not attract investors.
The bill has set tariff rates for generation of electricity from eight sources available locally including solar, wind and bagasse, which is a fibrous matter that remains after sugarcane or sorghum stalks are crushed to extract their juice and used as a biofuel and coal. This complaint was raised during a meeting between stakeholders to comment on the first draft in November of last year. Stakeholders included the Addis Abeba Chamber of Commerce & Sectoral Association, the Ethiopian Electric & Power Corporation (EPPCo) and experts from GIZ, a German International Corporation as well as the Addis Abeba Institute of Technology (AAIT)
However, despite the incessant efforts of the stakeholders from the private sector to have the rates increased by taking experiences of other African countries, the bill did not see any revision in tariff. It was submitted to the Ministry in March of 2011.
The tariffs in the bill vary for power generated from firm energy, where power is available at all times during the contract period, and for non-firm energy which is generated when it is available.
From these rates, the maximum is set for power generated from geothermal, biomass, baggase and wind, while the minimum rate is allotted for power generated from hydropower, coal, natural gas and oil shale.
A tariff of 0.10 dollars for kilowatt-hour is allotted for firm energy generating 500Kw of electricity from biomass, wind, geothermal and baggase, while 0.06 dollars for kilowatt-hour is allotted for firm energy generated from coal, oil shale and natural gas. The bill also sets 0.08 dollars for a kilowatt-hour for power generated from hydropower.
Those representing the private sector consider the rates offered in the bill insignificant and discourage the sector from venturing into power generation due to the high cost of capital investment and technology.
At these prices, an investor will not be able to repay its expenses and make profits, says an expert who was involved in the review process and wished to remain anonymous.
The prices offered are very cheap when compared to other African countries. Kenya, which some of the stakeholders consider as having an attractive feed in tariff, offers 0.12 dollars for a kilowatt-hour for a firm energy generation of 500Kw from hydro power and 0.22 dollars for firm energy generation from solar energy.
Declining to comment on the issue before the committee gives the final comment; Wondimu contends that the tariff rates are set considering the purchasing power of the people. Whatever the experience of other countries, it should be adopted in Ethiopia's context, he argues.
The EPPCo, the sole power generating company, charges 0.042 dollars for a kilowatt-hour for households and 0.042 dollars for commercial purposes. This is the lowest tariff when compared to sub Saharan African countries, according to a comparative study of electricity tariffs used in Africa, conducted by the Unions of Producers, Transporters and Distributers of Electrical Power in Africa (UPDEA).
However, the state owned Power Company has yet to fulfil all the demands of the country.
Ethiopia has the potential to generate 45,000Mw, 5,000Mw and 10,000Mw of electricity from hydro, wind and geothermal, respectively. However, it only generates 1,842Mw, 172.3Mw and 7.3MW, respectively.
Availability of electricity cannot be guaranteed without diversifying energy sources by engaging the private sector with encouraging investment rewards, an expert from Addis Abeba Institute of Technology (AAIT), who is closer to the issue told Fortune.
The government plans to increase electricity coverage from 41pc in 2009/10 to 75pc in 2014/15, according to its five year economic development plan.

Original_Search_Title: Rufaro Marketing Fights for Survival
Headline: Zimbabwe: Rufaro Marketing Fights for Survival
Body_Text:
RUFARO Marketing (RM) has always been a problem child for the City of Harare. Despite the frequent change of guard at the city's liquor division, its operations have remained critical.
In this Question & Answer with The Financial Gazette (FG), its chairperson, Philip Mataranyika (PM), speaks of the challenges at RM, the solutions and what the future holds for the business. Excerpts:
It is just about two years since you were appointed chairperson of RM; how would you describe your stay so far?
My stay at RM is essentially in two parts: 2009/10 was a year of understanding the business, noting the industry cycles and how they impact the RM business. In the same year, as a board, we made some management changes to realign the business to the new vision of the board.
In 2010/11, new management took over and had also to go through the same process of understanding the business and industry dynamics. Some notable achievements were: Volumes and turnover grew by 189 percent end of year 2009; old supplier relations were improved and new ones established; some outlets were refurbished and ambience improved; refrigeration capacity was enhanced; power back up (generators) were introduced; entertainment (TVs, DStv and decoders) was enhanced in the outlets and industrial relations stabilised.
Would you say you have been successful in your efforts to turn around RM over the past two years?
has not turned around yet; we are still in the recovery mode, just like many businesses in Zimbabwe post-dollarisation. The successes we can talk about are those I have just highlighted.
FG: In brief, what would you say are the challenges confronting the liquor business and what were their origins?
The RM challenges are mainly recapitalisation for working capital, outlet refurbishment/ upgrades, old equipment, tired infrastructure that is mainly sorghum-based yet sorghum is on the decline; declining business model - public bar concept dying, high fixed overhead business model, rampant illicit brews, direct competition from newly established bottle stores, night clubs, etc., with new and modern feel.
The origins of these problems was the promulgation of the Competition Act of 1996 that repealed the Local Authorities Monopolies Act - this removed the protection RM enjoyed over the years. It also resulted in overtrading in the liquor industry.
There hasn't been any recapitalisation of the business despite the advent of competition. We have also witnessed the gradual decline in the public bar concept, where the RM business is mainly entrenched.
The transition from Zimbabwe dollar to multi-currency affected us as well. RM is a cash business and as a result, the liquid assets that the business had at the time of changeover were totally wiped out. Other businesses have had shareholder capital injected to close the gap created during the transition period and RM did not.
However, as a board, we are aware of the problems of our business and we are putting together plans to trade out of the problems.
How do you hope to get around these challenges?
We have five strategies that we are actively pursuing, namely: (a) recapitalisation; (b) changing the business model to realign it to modern trends by concentrating on low-cost high-turnover channel (bottle store channel concept); (c) streamlining operations - dispose or lease poor performing outlets; (d) sweat the assets - utilise fully the non-productive land through strategic alliances with other players; such as the fuel and fast foods players; discussions with such players are in progress; (e) disposal of none-core assets; and (e) rebranding.
How come other liquor businesses such as Ingwebu in Bulawayo, Pungwe in Mutare and Delta in Harare, etc., do not seem to have problems at the magnitude we see at RM? Is this not reflective of bad leadership, poor systems and an unworkable business model?
The business models are different. RM is 100 percent into retail while Ingwebu and Pungwe are in both retail and brewing, and Delta are into brewing without any local brew competition. Delta are continually recapitalising in line with the dictates of the market. Ingwebu has similar problems on their retail business, the brewery is subsidising the retail business, they placed adverts at the beginning of the year to franchise part of the retail business where some of their outlets were closed.
seems to have bigger problems. This is consistent with our size: RM is much bigger than Ingwebu and Pungwe in terms of business and infrastructure. Its not a leadership problem, but a tired business model as alluded to earlier that requires rejuvenation and realignment, which is what we are working on.
What were the reasons for the dismissal of Tonderai Chigumadzi, the chief executive officer (CEO) for RM?
Contrary to media and grapevine reports, Chigumadzi resigned to pursue personal interests. He was instrumental in the achievements that I outlined earlier.
When are you likely to replace him?
The board will soon consider his replacement.
Rufaro Marketing has had a high turnover of CEOs and yet the problems have remained. By dismissing CEOs, are you not tinkering with the symptoms as opposed to addressing the root cause of the problems - under-capitalisation?
I do not know the reasons of high turnover of CEOs in the past, or whether that statement is correct, but the changes in 2010 (after nine years) were meant to realign the vision of the business to that of the new board. The current separation with Chigumadzi is resignation not dismissal.
They say a poor workman always blames his tools. Are you not making Chigumadzi a scapegoat like what we saw at Air Zimbabwe where Peter Chikumba was dismissed early this year for reasons that were beyond him?
Chigumadzi has never been used as a scapegoat, as indicated earlier, he resigned on his own accord, it was his personal decision, which the board respected.
Are you getting the necessary shareholder support noting that one of the major challenges militating against progress at RM is that of poor capitalisation, which requires funding from the Harare City Council?
The support is coming though slow. The shareholder is aware of what needs to be done to move the business forward. The board's regular communication with the shareholder is through two council representatives who sit on the board. On behalf the board, I would like to take this opportunity to thank them both for the support and wise counsel they give us all the time.
Do you think your turnaround plans will get the necessary buy-in from the city fathers and what makes you confident that you will get the necessary support this time around?
As highlighted earlier, the shareholder is represented on the board. The shareholder respects decisions of the board and are convinced the board's proposals are the way to go. The disposal proceeds of non-core assets will alleviate the current problems even if the shareholder fails to inject some capital into the business.
What is the future like for RM?
The future of RM mainly hinges on what I would prefer to call three Rs: Remodelling, recapitalisation and reviving the business through implementation of all the strategies that we have come up with.

Original_Search_Title: FG Spends N5.7 Billion On Garri, Other Foods in Five Months
Headline: Nigeria: FG Spends N5.7 Billion On Garri, Other Foods in Five Months
Body_Text:
The federal government awarded contracts worth N5.07 billion for the procurement of assorted food commodities for the national strategic food reserve under the Guaranteed Minimum Price (GMP) between March and August this year. Within the same period, the Bureau of Public Procurement (BPP) said, another N19.15 billion went into the construction and rehabilitation of key roads in four geopolitical zones of the country.
But analysts have expressed reservations about the list of contracts published by BPP under the presidency, saying that the roads mentioned remain in deplorable conditions, just as the prices of grains remain high in spite of efforts by the government to keep food prices low.
John Okon, a former banker in Lagos, has urged finance minister Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala to move quickly to block loopholes that have made implementation of such projects sloppy.
According to him, if due process is observed, moneys would be spent on what they are meant for, and the impact would be positively felt.
According to the publication, funds have been made available for the procurement of maize, millet, soybean, sorghum, garri and paddy rice. The BPP said a total of N5.07 billion was spent in the five months on procurement of 95,000 metric tons (MT) worth of the commodities.
Analysts have also advised that government set up a monitoring team to always follow up on projects to ensure that funds allotted are truly spent. They expressed dissatisfaction with the state of roads, saying government's claim on huge expenditure is not justifiable.
For instance, the BPP said N1.8 billion was released for the rehabilitation of the Ondo-Ore road in Ondo State. According to the agency, the money was released to the Federal Ministry of Works as the implementing agency on August 17 to ensure completion in nine months' time.
Road users said there is no evidence that government has an intention of working on the road. Because of the bad state of the road, armed robbers have been robbing travellers with ease.
Other roads earmarked for construction and rehabilitation include Igbo-Olomi at the Oyo State's border with Ogun State at the cost of N1.38 billion; Odo-Oba-Takie-Gambari-Otte, Oyo State border along Ibadan-Oyo-Ogbomsho-Ilorin road (45.3 km) in Oyo State at the cost of N2.14 billion; reconstruction/rehabilitation of Gbongan-Owo-Oyo road in Oyo State at the cost of N6.92 billion; rehabilitation of Spur from Onittsha-Owerri road to Okija-Afor Ukpor-Ebenator-Ezinifite in Nnewi South local government area of Anambra State at the cost of N1.79 billion; construction of Jalingo-Kuna-Lau road in Taraba section 2 at the cost of N1.43 billion; and award of contract for the reinstatement of washout and completion of the rehabilitation of Sokoto-Illela road, including repairs of Usman Danfodiyo University road in Sokoto at the cost of N2.26 billion.
Bismarck Rewane, chief executive officer of Financial Derivatives Company (FDC) , said the impact of not rehabilitating the roads quickly or spending funds meant for projects judiciously is bad for economic growth.
For instance, he said, it takes three days to bring goods from Lokoja to Lagos, compared to the six hours from Lokoja to Abuja. With that, the cost of goods and services would have gone four times higher for goods from Lokoja to Lagos.
Abe said that, without good transportation, goods cannot be moved from one place to another, and as such the economy will suffer. He also blamed the poor implementation on corruption.

Original_Search_Title: There Is an Urgent Need to Provide Safe, Affordable Alcohol to the Poor
Headline: Kenya: There Is an Urgent Need to Provide Safe, Affordable Alcohol to the Poor
Body_Text:
Madness is defined as doing something the same way, over and over again, each time expecting a different result.
The spectacle of media coverage of chiefs and Administration Police pouring down gallons of dirty, dangerous illicit brews is a case in point.
We seem to have convinced ourselves that this dramatic action, especially when accompanied by twig-bearing women chanting obscenities over their non-existent sex lives, will solve the problem of drinking illicit brews in society.
Then we congratulated Parliament for passing the so-called 'Mututho law'.
This law is conceptually an attempt to restrict supply to everyone, even those who drink perfectly safe legal products.
But we convinced ourselves that the Mututho laws shall save lives and deliver young men into their matrimonial beds with rejuvenated libido.
We ought to notice now that the recurring tragedy of death by lethal consumption of illicit brews continues yet our only prescription is restricting supply.
If we don't try a different approach, we must be deluded!
I suggest a very simple proposition: we have a supply problem of safe, affordable alcohol at the low-end of the market.
Supply restrictions make the problem worse, not better. The demand for alcohol cannot be wished away and even poor people demand it.
If we don't allow well-meaning entrepreneurs to satisfy this demand by providing safe, affordable brews, including traditional African ones, the demand at the low-end of the market will continue to be satisfied by criminals.
Our fundamental challenge is an alcohol policy that delivers safe and affordable alcohol to the poor, not one that restricts.
But the underlying science in the manufacture of alcohol is relevant to viable policy formulation.
Fermentation is the primary process of producing alcohol in which, 'sugar' derived from a variety of sources; barley, wheat, maize, sorghum, grapes, sugarcane or white sugar itself, are provided as food substrate to a family of organisms called yeast.
When yeast feeds on the food substrate, they produce ethyl alcohol as a by-product of the fermentation process.
Beer, busaa, muratina and wine are fermented products.
But after fermentation, a further process of refining alcohol is possible.
This essentially involves heating the fermented morass in controlled conditions, collecting the resultant alcoholic vapour at specified temperature, cooling and bottling the 'liquor', or liquid alcohol otherwise called 'spirit'.
This process is called distillation. Gin, vodka, and chang'aa are 'spirits'.
As a general rule, fermented products are, in relative terms 'safer' than distilled products. This is in relation to the body's ability to process and get rid of the alcohol.
Fermented products are also easier to process than spirits. Yet the purpose of distillation is precisely to offer extremely high portions of alcohol in small volumes, which is a real challenge to the body chemistry.
Therefore, 'spirits' are, by definition, more dangerous.
Cheap liquor such as poorly distilled chang'aa, is extremely dangerous.
Raw distilled alcohol is pungent, corrosive and very unpalatable, and the process of producing safe spirits requires careful distillation, masking and blending, all of which are very precise and expensive.
We must seek to supply safe and affordable alcohol at the low-end of the market. Efforts to restrict supply will never solve the problem; it will cause more deaths.
Further, it is fermented products, such as keg beer and certain other traditional fermented brews that our alcohol policy must facilitate for the low-end of the alcohol market, not spirits.
Finally, there is an effective way of stopping leakage into the market of manufactured raw industrial alcohol unfit for human consumption.
Science has produced chemical deterrents that smell and induce vomiting if they are ingested.
The law should require manufacturers of raw industrial alcohol to add at source, portions of these smelly chemicals to their industrial products to prevent any diversion into human consumption.
Mr Ngugi is a consultant in public affairs and policy (kirirowangugi@gmail.com).

Original_Search_Title: Deputy Ministers Receive Orientation Over Their Role
Headline: South Sudan: Deputy Ministers Receive Orientation Over Their Role
Body_Text:
Juba — Deputy ministers in South Sudan on Wednesday took part in an induction workshop which discussed their roles as the first deputy ministers in the world's newest country.
Last month South Sudan's President Salva Kiir formed his first cabinet since independence on 9 July. The new cabinet is composed of 29 ministers and 27 deputy ministers.
Since 2005 South Sudan has governed itself with its own army, parliament and cabinet but the positions of deputy ministers is new to the country.
The induction exercise, organised by the minister of cabinet affairs, Deng Alor Kuol, aimed to inform the deputy ministers about the cabinet system, working with parliament, as well as basic government procedures including financial procedures and the civil service.
In his opening speech, the Vice President, Riek Machar, stressed the importance of the exercise, saying the people of South Sudan were anxious in their aspirations which should now be delivered by the new government of the independent state.
Machar said over 60 percent of the current workforce in South Sudan are unqualified, adding that it was time to make serious reform in the public service in order to establish a qualified public service that can deliver services to the people.
He said the government should deliver and prove that existence of ministers in the cabinet is not about enjoying luxuries such as "cars, houses and fat salaries" which he said is the current view ordinary citizens about the government.
He pointed out that absorption and reintegration of qualified civil servants who are returning from North Sudan should be conducted alongside reforms to weed out unqualified civil servants and replace them with more competent professionals.
He reminded the deputy ministers about the statement of South Sudan's President on corruption, which Machar read to the nation on his behalf earlier this week. In the statement, the President declared that corruption cases such as the dura (sorghum) saga of 2008 and any other illegal contracts be investigated and the culprits prosecuted.
He also added that he would seek assistance from other countries to repatriate the diverted public funds by individual officials.
Machar further also said that other corrupt practices include the loss of hundreds of millions of dollars every year from an unauthorised and dishonest taxation system.
He said that land in South Sudan often sold by people who do not have the legal right to sell it. The Vice President also warned the deputies not to become involved in illegal contracts and projects.

Original_Search_Title: Kiir Vows to Fight Corruption in Government
Headline: South Sudan: Kiir Vows to Fight Corruption in Government
Body_Text:
Juba — The president of South Sudan, Salva Kiir, has vowed to fight corruption in the government, nearly a month after the formation of his first cabinet since the country's independence on 9 July 2011.
In a statement read on his behalf on Monday at a press conference held by the vice president, Riek Machar he reiterated his commitment to ensuring that South Sudan "enters a new era of good governance, democracy, accountability and transparency."
He was speaking hours after the President left Juba for New York to attend a meeting of the UN General Assembly.
"I am committed to setting a new standard for transparency and accountability in our public finances and in setting a new standard for public officials. Beyond the 100 day action already launched, I will take five critical steps as a visible sign of the new government's commitment," read Machar.
Kiir said the cases of the diversion of funds will be investigated including those on the "Dura [sorghum] saga" in which hundreds of millions of dollars were looted through a false company contracted by the ministry of finance to deliver dura to the ten states in 2008.
He added that other government contracts, particularly those on large infrastructure projects, will be investigated and the results will be made public. The President also acknowledged that there are concerns about sales of land during the transitional period, saying these will be reviewed and future land sales will be carefully regulated, following new procurement legislation.
He said he has instructed the national parliament to enact laws on accountability so that all government contracts as of now onwards will
"We will enforce the obligation of all public officials to publish their income and financial assets. We will provide an opportunity of an anonymous return account for civil servants and public officials to return any diverted funds, obligating them to publish their assets from the date of independence," he added.
Kiir said he would seek the assistance of countries such as the Switzerland, the US, the UK and Australia to recover and repatriate diverted funds.
He said he will appoint senior advisers from the African region to the Governor of the Central Bank of South Sudan, the Auditor General and the Minister of Finance and Economic Planning and to strengthen the anti-corruption commission.
Machar told journalists that an investigation has been carried out into the arrest of UN personnel on 18 August by police in Juba.
He explained that the investigation, conducted under the chairmanship of Gordon Micah Kur, deputy inspector general of South Sudan Police Service, found a number of officers responsible for the unlawful arrest.
He added that some of the officers have been suspended and others were stripped of their ranks.
Machar said the government regretted the conduct and appealed to the UN Mission in South Sudan (UNMISS) to assist the newly independent state in training the police in the area of respect for human rights.
At the meeting Barnaba Marial Benjamin, minister of information, said he expects Kiir to meet the US president, Obama, during his visit to the 66th meeting of the UN general assembly to discuss issues such as poverty an terrorism.
South Sudan became the 193rd member of the UN on 15 July 2011, six days after it became an independent state.

Original_Search_Title: EU Navfor Flagship Protects Food Aid
Headline: Somalia: EU Navfor Flagship Protects Food Aid
Body_Text:
The German frigate FGS BAYERN, part of the Operation ATALANTA counter-piracy mission off the Horn of Africa, has recently escorted the merchant vessel "SEA MASTER I" safely into the port of Bossasso on the northern coast of Somalia.
The ship was loaded with humanitarian-aid as part of the World Food Programme aid to those in need in Somalia and her cargo was several thousand tons of urgently needed food, including sorghum and vegetable oil.
EU NAVFOR Somalia is a counter-piracy taskforce operating in the area of the Gulf of Aden and the Indian Ocean responsible for deterring, preventing and repressing acts of piracy, for the safe escort of ships carrying humanitarian aid of the World Food Program and vessels of African Union Mission in Somalia (AMISOM) and to protect other vulnerable vessels. Additionally, EU NAVFOR also monitors fishing activity off the coast of Somalia

Original_Search_Title: Food, Goods Prices Rise
Headline: Rwanda: Food, Goods Prices Rise
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwanda 's inflation remains at single digit but food and commodity prices in major markets in Kigali have soared.
Places such as Kimironko, Nyabugogo, Kimisagara and Nyamirambo have seen an increment in prices since April this year.
A survey conducted by East African Business Week shows a sack of charcoal has gone up Rwf 7000(about $11) from Rwf 5,500($9.2) three weeks ago.
Silver fish (Ndagara) cost Rwf 1000($1.7) a kilo, from Rwf 600($1) several months ago . A 25kg bag of maize remains at Rwf 8000($13), while wheat flour is down from Rwf 1,400($2.3) to Rwf 1,300($2.2).
Ms Ange Mukandahiro, a market vendor at Nyabugogo says prices of most essentials have been rising abnormally since last year December.
She said a kilo of beans increased to Rwf 400($0.7) from Rwf 350(0.6) in April while sorghum is now at Rwf 400($0.7) from Rwf 250($0.4).
Sweat potatoes range from Rwf 150($0.25) to Rwf 180($0.3) from Rwf 70($0.1). Mr Peter Muvunyi, a wholesaler at Kimironko market says the price is hurting consumers more than traders.
" Employees whether in government or private sector are really facing the pinch. Their salary remains constant yet prices of food stuff and essential commodities goes up, he said.
"Before I used to sell five sacks of sugar a day now I sell one sack in three weeks," Muvunyi complained. Many Traders we met say that majority of People have reduced buying essential commodities.
"Before I used to get at least 30,000franc($50) a day, now I hardly get 5,000 franc($8.3)," says Mukandahiro, adding that , " If I got a job of 50,000 franc($83.3) a month I will immediately leave this business."
Ms Josephine Mukadoli another vendor is worried that the situation might worsen. " Since October last year prices of everything have increased," she lamented.
Mr Clement Gapira, an employee in a bank in Kigali says that the world is going through inflation and it is not peculiar to Rwanda alone.
"Things could have been worse if we did not have a responsiblegovernment," Gapira adds, " You can see at least the government made effort to reduce fuel tax by 100 franc($0.2) to contain the inflation among other interventions," he says.

Original_Search_Title: High River Taxes Blamed for Increase in Prices Upper Nile and Unity
Headline: Sudan: High River Taxes Blamed for Increase in Prices Upper Nile and Unity
Body_Text:
Bentiu — Boat drivers say they are having to pay higher taxes to government officials between Upper Nile and Unity state, after North Sudan's Khartoum government abolished trade into newly independent South Sudan.
The docking site for motor boats in Rubkotna Port which links Unity and Upper Nile state, it is the only route used by traders and local business men to bring goods into the state. The three day trip between Malakal and Bentiu costs 100 South Sudanese pounds (SSP) per passenger.
Boat drivers complain that they are having to pay increased taxes in both Unity and Upper Nile states. Nyanya Patai Tutbuor, who travels between Malakal and Bentiu on a weekly basis told Sudan Tribune on Friday that he was "not felling happy about the high imposed taxation".
"We put much of our blame on the government on putting 13 docking stations along the river, all in all we pay money and each docking station [totaling] about 460 SSP".
Sometimes the authorities also force boat drivers to take people for free Tutbuor said.
As well as high travel costs civilians in Unity state also complain of high prices especially on food commodities and others items in the markets.
Genena Machar Turual state director of taxation chamber denied taxes were too high saying his government's policy was not to tax food items.
"The issues of taxation has been brought down, by general governor Taban Deng Gai. He issued a decree that there would be no taxes charged for food commodities especially, the sorghum, sugar, soft drinks, maize and any others consumable goods, [with the] exception of alcohol and others items like timbers, iron bar to be taxed", said Turual.
Turual further added, "We don't charge consumable goods because the north has closed down our border, this can encourage the businessmen to have a successful run up their work".
Governor Taban Deng has urged the citizens not to put the blame on him pointing out that traders have the right to double prices for their goods.
In Mayom County the prices of commodities has become worse, a 100 kilogram sack of sorghum cost about 300 SSP but costs around 1,000 SSP in Bentui the state capital.

Original_Search_Title: Warrap Links Rising Food Prices to Closure of North-South Borders
Headline: Sudan: Warrap Links Rising Food Prices to Closure of North-South Borders
Body_Text:
Juba — The government of Warrap State on Tuesday broke its silence on rising food prices in the area and said it was due to the closure of supply routes between Sudan and newly-independent South Sudan.
Makuc Makuc Ngongdit, a political advisor to state governor Nyandeng Malek, said in an interview with Sudan Tribune that food and prices of other basic commodities have increased as a result of the closure by authorities in Khartoum.
Until July 9 Sudan was a one country, with South Sudan running its own affairs in a semi-autonomous government, as part of a 2005 peace ending decades of war. A referendum earlier this year paved the way for the secession of South Sudan.
South Sudan's independence has thrown up many challenges for the two nations. Many post-independence issues remain unresolved, including citizenship, border demarcation, wealth sharing and the status of the contested border region of Abyei.
While the African Union, through an established high level panel under former South Africa President Thabo Mbeki, is exerting efforts to resolve differences between the two countries, officials in the south have accused Khartoum of unilaterally closing borders and giving support to both political and armed opposition groups operating against the Juba-based government. Khartoum deny this.
"Prices of basic commodities have increased. This is because the government in Khartoum has closed the supplying routes not only for Warrap but for other neighbouring states at the borderline", said Makuc.
He said that some in South Sudan fail to accept that the issue of soaring prices being a national issue. All South Sudan states that neighbour Sudan; Northern Bahr el Ghazal, Unity, Western Bahr el Ghazal and Upper Nile state have suffered, he said.
The goods sold in the markets in these states come from North Sudan and are expensive because of transport and other costs, he explained
Around 358, mostly returnees from North Sudan, are reported to have died from hunger in Warrap state. Governor Malek and other high ranking officials in her administration were quick to deny this and attributed the cause of death to lack of adequate medical supplies in the area.
The state assembly has tasked a parliamentary committee to investigate the cause of the deaths.
Reverend Amet Amet Kuol, the chairperson of the state's peace and reconciliation committee in an interview with Sudan Tribune last week said representatives of the victims showed his committee more than 35 graves of people reported to have died of hunger. He said more graves were shown to an executive committee appointed by the state administration.
But the senior government official who is also Secretary General of the South Sudan's ruling party, the Sudan People's Liberation Movement (SPLM) in the state, categorically refuted these reports claiming the prevalence of hunger in the area was "political".
Makuc described security situation in the area as "calm and normal".
"Generally, the security situation as of now is relatively calm and normal. The issue of cattle rustling and sectional fights between the two clans of Luackoth and Akok which you may have heard in Tonj East County has been resolved", said Makuc.
He said a company of the South Sudan army (SPLA) has been deployed to the area. "There is no any other problem there. Law and order has been restored by the deployment of the SPLA. The government is now looking for culprits", he said.
He appealed for cooperation between the executive and the legislative branches of the state's administration, saying they need to work together to deliver basic service to the electorate, with a unified development policy and programs.
Kuac Rou Mawien, a resident of Kuacjok, capital of Warrap State, in a separate telephone interview with Sudan Tribune confirmed the increase in prices in basic commodities in the area.
"The market is generally expensive," he said.
"Food commodities are very, very expensive. All the traders have raised up, doubling charges which ordinary people do not afford", said Mawien complaining that other basic commodities are hardly affordable because of high demand.
"There are days like yesterday I was in the market hunting for sorghum without success. The prices are constantly climbing up. This was why I failed to get [some sorghum] not because they are not available. It was because I was not able to afford selling prices. The money I had was less than the selling price", he explained.

Original_Search_Title: Man Gets 15 Years for Defilement
Headline: Uganda: Man Gets 15 Years for Defilement
Body_Text:
The High Court in Moroto on Monday sentenced a Karimojong man to 15 years in Luzira prison for aggravated defilement. Lady Justice Margret Oguli Oumo convicted Robert Loyep, 26, during a backlog clearing session in Karamoja.
Court heard that on June 10, 2010 at Losikait village, Irriri sub-county in Napak district, Loyep defiled a 10-year-old girl.
Prosecution added that Loyep sent the neighbour's daughter for some salt and a knife while weeding his sorghum garden.
However, when the girl delivered the items, Loyep grabbed her and forced her into sex in the garden. He was caught in the act by two women who were looking for firewood.
They tried to separate him from the victim, but failed. He was arrested and taken to the Police. Loyep pleaded guilty and was convicted by the court.

Original_Search_Title: Village Exhibits Namibia's Diverse Cultures
Headline: Namibia: Village Exhibits Namibia's Diverse Cultures
Body_Text:
THE multi-cultural Okahandja Cultural Village was launched recently and opened its doors to the public almost two months ago.
The village is just on the outskirts of Okahandja town, the gateway to the North, North-Eastern Namibia and the coast.
The village, which is a brain-child of the Mbundu family, consists of homesteads from seven of Namibia's cultural groups the San, Tswana, Caprivians, Damara, Ovahimba/Ovaherero and Owambo.
Daily activities at the village include village tours, traditional dances, San hunting tours, practical demonstrations of traditional technologies, okapana braai and potjie-making facilities, traditional cooking, camping and fresh vegetables.
"This village is the only one-stop cultural centre in the country of its kind. It aims at teaching and preserving Namibian cultures, come and learn about the cultures of Namibia and the huts are spread over a large piece of land.
"There are people living in some of the traditional homesteads currently. The idea is to have real people living in all homesteads in the village. To this end, arrangements are underway to have all homesteads occupied by real traditional people soon," explained owner Bertha Mbundu.
She added that a limited amount of livestock will be kept to demonstrate traditional animal husbandry in the village, while traditional produce like omahangu, sorghum, beans, ground beans, and fresh vegetables are grown at the village and could be processed into various beverages and meals.
"We do also invite communities to perform traditional dances as well as to make traditional artefacts for display and sale for their benefit," Mbundu noted.
The village is also ideal for general education for all Namibians and educational institutions could make use of this village for cultural research and development, according to Mbundu, who said the village plans to have a restaurant which will serve traditional, local and modern foods, a conference centre, souvenir shop, a kiosk selling food and vegetables straight from the garden, a number of en-suite rooms and self-catering units for overnight guests.

Original_Search_Title: When UNSC Enters the Fray With the Khartoum Regime, Darfur IDPs Suffer
Headline: Sudan: When UNSC Enters the Fray With the Khartoum Regime, Darfur IDPs Suffer
Body_Text:
The proverb says; "when two elephants fight it's the grass that suffers". Similarly, when the genocidal regime of Khartoum fights with the UNSC it is the IDPs and refugees of Darfur who suffer.
When judges of the pre-trail chamber at the International Criminal Court(ICC), issued a warrant of the arrest for al- Bashir, charging him with crimes of genocide and crimes against humanity; the National Congress Party (NCP) of the fugitive president immediately enacted its vengeance and on 3 March 2009, expelling thirteen of the world's most distinguished humanitarian organisations from Darfur. The expelled organisations were providing the IDPs with water, food, medicine, health services, and other essential life-saving materials.
In order to fathom the depth of the aid chasm left in IDP camps after the expulsion of the NGOs; I contacted Sheikh Zakaria Mohamed from Kalma IDP camp and Halima Abdulla from Abu Shouk IDP camp.
They unanimously said that the expelled NGOs used to provide 13kg of food per person per month, but today people are given only 3kg. They added that this is sometimes delayed by three or four months. Therefore, the IDPs are forced into hard labour such as making bricks around the IDPs camps.
Halima from Abu Shouk IDP camp said that it's also unwise for them to leave the camps, looking for work, because they fear being arrested or harassed by the Janjaweed militias who are forever patrolling around the camps.
"We queue through the night to fetch water from the tap and when you fail to get it, we have to fill one jerkin from rain water at the pool in the camp," said Halima.
Sheikh Zakaria from Kalma explained that a family of 20 persons or more depend on the food distribution card of one person, due to ban on registration for new cards.
"When you feel ill there is nowhere to get treatment, because the two doctors of the centre select serious cases among the sick people," said Zakaria.
Other IDPs who I personally talked in El-Geneina camps complained that the regime pushed the World Food Program (WFP) to let Sudan Government take over distribution of relief items through its chamber of commerce, which the IDPs vehemently disagree with.
Frankly speaking; it's true that finding a political solution to the conflict in Darfur has negatively affected the IDPs and refugees inthe camps; most of the camps, if not all, receive only salt and sorghum and no other items.
No feeding centres for the children, no pre-natal clinics, no trauma centres, no ambulances (the IDPs are using wheelbarrows to carry patients), no tents or shelters to protect the IDPs from rain water, no water because most of the camps were equipped to receive a limited number of people in 2005 and now the numbers have significantly increased. Therefore, suicide; delivery problems; and diseases like fistula, malnutrition are rampant among the IDPs.
On 29July 2011 the UN Security Council decided to extend the mandate of African Union-United Nation Hybrid operation in Darfur (UNAMID) for one year and to empower the mandate of UNAMID; and to create coordination between the UNAMID, the UN Interim Security Forces of Abyei (UNSFA) and the UN Mission in South Sudan (UNMISS).
However, on 3 Aug 2011, the Sudan government was highly critical of the UNSC resolution, saying it's interfering in Sudan's internal affairs and that the resolution attempts to distort the image of the country. Hence the Government of Sudan reaffirmed that any attempt to impose any new obligation, is contrary to what has been agreed upon and will lead Sudan to refuse cooperation and to disengage from any previous obligation related to the acceptance of the mission and its deployment.
Now as heavy clouds of dispute between Sudan Government and UNSC gather inthe sky; the IDPs and refugees of Darfur are very worried that this fight may compound their injuries.
This is a particular fear in today's climate, as the regime of Khartoum resorted to cowardly behaviour; attacking UNAMID and humanitarian aid workers on the ground, to force UNSC not to take steps towards the implementation.
Of course; I am not saying UNSC should stop voting for resolutions because of the empty threats of Al-Bashir, but what are the precautions put forward by the UNSC to stop the criminal Al-Bashir from pouring his venom of anger on the vulnerable IDPs and refugees?
However, personally I do not expect quick implementation to the UNSC resolutions as was the case in Libya, when it is clear that UNSC has two kinds of resolutions; toothed and toothless.
The writer is based in Nairobi

Original_Search_Title: Only a Total Overhaul Can Offer the Definitive Way Out for the New Republic!
Headline: South Sudan: Only a Total Overhaul Can Offer the Definitive Way Out for the New Republic!
Body_Text:
While it is completely understandable for us to go on deservedly congratulating ourselves over our Independence and sharing the 'free at last' expression with fellow compatriots, there is an obliging need that we seriously consider those valuable advices that came from our generous guests who joined us in the celebration.
We will have to demonstrate a great deal of political maturity when we come to reflect on the participations shown by our many friends, as some expressed words of encouragement and gave advice, while others preferred physical expressions either drawing our attention by their actions, comments or body language. This is what friends are for, aren't they? They have done their bits and it is now our sole responsibility to choose which ones to take and which ones to discard. As it always goes the final decision is ours for it is but the beginning of being independent.
