She allegedly said: "Zanu PF (President Mugabe's party) members been attacking MDC president Tsvangirai alleging he is pro-homosexuals yet Robert Mugabe has practiced homosexuality with (Professor) Jonathan Moyo (former Information minister) and Canaan Banana (Zimbabwe's first ceremonial president)."
The late Mr Banana was jailed for sodomising his bodyguard.
Mr Tsvangirai came under fire recently after he told the BBC in an interview that he wanted to see the rights of homosexuals protected in Zimbabwe's new constitution.
Prof Moyo, now an influential Zanu PF legislator, has been calling on Mr Tsvangirai to step down after the PM ended his alleged 12 day marriage with a Harare businesswoman last month.
The State is against Mrs Kirenyi being granted bail claiming that she faces a serious offence and could be harmed by President Mugabe's supporters.
The veteran ruler says gays are worse than dogs and pigs. Last month, he threatened to severely punish Zimbabwean youths caught practicing homosexuality.
He also called British Prime Minister David Cameroon satanic after his statement that Britain will not provide aid to states that criminalise gays.

Original_Search_Title: Four Busted With K8.5m Counterfeit
Headline: Zambia: Four Busted With K8.5m Counterfeit
Body_Text:
FOUR people have been arrested in Lusaka for allegedly being found with K8.5 million in counterfeit notes.
The country has, of late, seen a rise in the circulation of 'fake' Kwacha notes, particularly the K50, 000 note, which has mostly been discovered in night clubs and bars.
In the latest incident, two of the suspects were apprehended in Kamwala trading area by the Drug Enforcement Commission (DEC) while the motor vehicle they were using was seized.
DEC spokesperson, Samuel Silomba, said the other two suspects were apprehended in Lusaka's Avondale residential area. All the four would appear in court soon.
"DEC has arrested Tom Kabemba, 26, a businessman of Lusaka's Avondale area, Chanda Thema, 31, Victor Mulenga, 30, a businessman of Chingola, and Bernard Kabanji, 29.
"They were arrested and jointly charged with being in possession of K8, 500, 000 counterfeit notes," he said.
Mr Silomba said Mr Kabemba and Mr Thema were arrested at 'On the Run' shop in Kamwala area, while Mr Mulenga and Mr Kabanji were apprehended from Avondale.
The Commission has seized an unregistered vehicle which was allegedly being used by the two suspects picked up from Kamwala.
Meanwhile, the Commission has arrested Olipa Kapombe, 23, a housewife of Kamuchanga in Mufulira on the Copperbelt, for allegedly trafficking in 98.1 kilogrammes of cannabis.
The drug was concealed in three sacks and two bags.
The Commission, in a joint operation with the Community Crime Prevention Unit of Mporokoso, also arrested Joseph Mulenga, 32, of Kapata Village in Mporokoso for allegedly trafficking in 15 kilogrammes of cannabis concealed in a grain house and rolls of the same drug wrapped in banana leaves.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Races to 19.2 Percent
Headline: Tanzania: Inflation Races to 19.2 Percent
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — Prices of consumer goods continued to skyrocket last month as inflation rate rose to 19.2 per cent.
The National Bureau of Statistics said yesterday that the rise in the inflation rate in November was the result of high food, fuel and non-alcoholic beverages prices.
The annual headline inflation rate for November increased from 17.9 per cent recorded the previous month.
It has been increasing steadily since the beginning of the year from 6.4 per cent in January, reaching double digits for the first time in about five years in June at 10.9 per cent.
The NBS inflation report for November said food and energy were the most volatile components in the total national consumer price index (NCPI) at 47 per cent and 9.2 per cent, respectively.
Food items that contributed to the increase included rice (8.8 per cent), bread (two per cent), maize flour (1.7 per cent), meat (4.8 per cent) and raw bananas (five per cent).Non-food inflation rose by 1.9 per cent for clothing materials, 1.3 per cent for carpets, 3.2 per cent for blankets and 1.3 per cent for household appliances.
The rate of price increase for other items such as bicycles rose by 3.3 per cent, car batteries by 2.6 per cent, hotel accommodation by 2.5 per cent and grinding by 1.8 per cent.
In the region, Uganda's inflation rate dropped to 29 per cent in November from 30.4 per cent in October.
In Kenya, it increased to 19.7 per cent in November from 18.9 per cent in the previous month, with the rise being attributed to higher food inflation.
Inflation has increased at a time when the shilling is on a roller coaster ride against the US dollar. Economists warn that this will continue to push up prices of basic commodities. The rise in prices of imported food and non-food items has been attributed to the weakening of the shilling against the dollar.
On Thursday, the shilling exchanged at 1,665 per dollar in commercial banks. The NBS report indicates that the purchasing power of the shilling has continued to decline. Sh100 purchasing power declined to Sh80.29 between September 2010 and November 2011.

Original_Search_Title: The Born Free Generation's Reflections On Jamhuri Day
Headline: Kenya: The Born Free Generation's Reflections On Jamhuri Day
Body_Text:
Time magazine wrote about the first Jamuhuri Day back in 1964 thus: "Independence celebrations are as common in Africa as bananas, but last week Kenya outdid itself with it's second in a year.
"For all practical purposes, the former British colony gained independence last year, but to facilitate administrative changeovers,
Britain retained symbolic sovereignty by making Kenya a dominion. "Last week saw the progression from mere uhuru (freedom) to Jamuhuri (republic). Though Kenya remains a member of the Commonwealth, Jomo Kenyatta, who had been Prime Minister, duly changed his title to President, replacing Queen Elizabeth as head of state."
I missed the big Independence Day bash in December 1963 and the Jamuhuri Day bash a year later by less than half a dozen years, so I couldn't presume to tell you what it was like at the time. Growing up however, I did hear a few stories from those who were there. I was told for instance that Kenyan nuns in the Loreto order of the Roman Catholic Church were told Uhuru/Independence would be a terrible thing and would only bring the devils work and so they should pray against its ever happening.
Granted after the harassing of white nuns and priests after independence a couple of years earlier in what was then still referred to as the Belgian Congo but later became Zaire and is now the Democratic Republic of Congo, perhaps you could forgive the Kenyan Catholic nuns for being paranoid.
I was also told how those who went to the celebrations and others around the country partied the night away doing the twist, the highlife and other dances of the era to the golden tones of Mr Gideon Victor and the Kenya Police Band amongst others. There were the world class music celebrities such as Miriam Makeba and Harry Belafonte (if you don't know who they are ask your parents or look them up) who attended the celebrations and added some Hollywood-style glamour to the occasion.
As the New York Times reported at the time of the 1963 independence celebrations, "Jubilation swept through the new country. With Britain's Union Jack replaced by the black, red and green flag of the new states political power in Britain's last East African colonial holding slipped from the grasp of its 55,759 whites and was taken up by its 8,365,942 Africans."
Kenya was the 35th independent state to emerge in Africa. In a matter of days, she became the 18th member of the British Commonwealth and the 113th member of the United Nations. Other than the people of Kenya, amongst the main stars of our independence was Mzee Jomo Kenyatta then aged 73 and fresh from a seven year stint in prison followed by a period of restriction (kind of like house arrest).
Another star was Tom Mboya, the 33-year-old Minister of Justice and Constitutional Affairs who was universally acknowledged as "bright, aggressive and politically acute." Also present was Joseph A. Z. Murumbi, the 52-year-old half-Maasai half-Goan Minister of State, a political tactician described by the New York times as, "affable and relaxed, an extensive reader with a collection of nearly 3,000 books."
One of the brightest stars who would outlive the other three by a couple of decades was Jaramogi Oginga Odinga, Minister of Home Affairs, and also aged 52. Jaramogi, who was later knifed in the back by Jomo and Tom, had been a teacher and businessman who the New York Times described as being "given to singing and dancing at any public opportunity. This misleads his opponents: he is, in fact, one of the most powerful and astute political leaders in the country."
All those years ago, the writer from the New York Times wrote: "The Kenya these men will govern is not yet a unified nation. It is an uneasy collection of 48 tribes caught up in a cross-hatching of hostilities and suspicions that are often no more than a spear's length from violence." He added: "Civil war is only a gun sight away in the desert reaches of the Northern Frontier District, because of demands that the area's predominantly Somali population be allowed to break from Kenya and join neighbouring Somalia, the republic to the east."
He observed, "The district is under virtual military occupation. Kenyan troops are dug in being barbed- wire emplacements, and there have been 17 armed attacks in the last six weeks. Rifle- carrying shiftas, or bandits, alleged to be from Somalia are said to have slipped into the district and ambushed Kenyan Army patrols, hurled grenades at isolated police posts, kidnapped hostages and attempted assassinations." How interesting to see how far we have come and how things have changed, or have they?

Original_Search_Title: Subsidy - Jonathan Deceived us During Election - -LP
Headline: Nigeria: Subsidy - Jonathan Deceived us During Election - -LP
Body_Text:
Abuja — The last may not have been heard of the intrigues that surrounded last general election as the Chairman of the Labour Party (LP), Chief Dan Nwanyanwu yesterday accused President Goodluck Jonathan of shielding critical information especially as it pertains the controversial removal of oil subsidy insisting that had the President mentioned he has such intention, the party would not have supported his candidature during the election.
He said throughout the negotiations, Jonathan never gave them the inkling he has such an agenda as what he told them is quite different from what he is about implementing which also did not feature in his campaign messages.
Insisting that LP has no regret for supporting Jonathan at that time, Chief Nwanyanwu said the sudden u-turn has been considered as deceptive and not in the best interest of the country.
Nwanyanwu spoke at the end of the LP National Executive Committee (NEC) meeting held in Abuja.
He said those who are "pushing President Jonathan into implementing this anti-populist programme will not be around when the consequences confronts Mr. President frontally and they will not come up to say they asked him to implement this obnoxious policy".
He said the party has constituted a committee to dissect the 2012 budget presented by President Jonathan to the National Assembly and to come up with the LP position on the issue.
Asked to reconcile the support the party gave to President Jonathan during the campaign and election and the present position of the party on the President;s policies, particularly the removal of oil subsidy, Chief Nwanyanwu stated that the party has no regret in supporting him because he was the best of al the candidates at the time the party supported him.
He however said their support would not undermine the fact that the party will support other stakeholders in the Nigeria project to kick against the removal of oil subsidy.
Reading the text of the resolution of NEC members, Nwanyanwu stated: "Our party finds it difficult to believe, that the same president who went round the country during presidential campaigns promising Nigerians better life ahead, is now busy imposing draconian economic policies whose socio-economic consequences on the nation would be patently unbearable. Mr. President hardly needs any reminder that in the present circumstance of our beloved country, petroleum subsidy removal is a banana peel that his government should not make the mistake of stepping on.
"Nigerians refuse to accept the lame excuses being given by government as explanation for the failure of our four refineries to operate optimally, efficiently and deliver on their mandate, despite the huge resources expended to build and maintain them over the years.
"It is even more incomprehensible how the country failed to establish new refineries in the light of enormous resources accrued to governments from various sources in many decades.
"This is why the Labour party joins our dear compatriots throughout the nation in calling on the federal government to consider the more progress option of embarking on renewal and modernization of our crippling and dilapidated infrastructure, such as railways, roads, electricity, water, housing, educational and health institutions, etc, etc, in order to rekindle the lost confidence and trust of Nigerians in their government. Nigerians have been robbed so many times through unfulfilled promises and dashing of their hopes by governments.
"Labour party is of the firm view that in consideration of the dire economic strait in which the country presently finds itself, the exploration of other sources of revenue through effective blockage of economic leakages in the ports, such as, wreckless import waivers, over invoicing of contracts, appropriate taxation of the rich, efficient and prudent management of oil and gas sector of the economy.
"In conclusion, we wish to reiterate that in the interest of our nascent democracy, peace , security and stability of our dear fatherland, the federal government must follow the path of dialogue and negotiation with Labour and other stakeholders to arrive at an acceptable solution to this contentious matter".
Those who attended the NEC meeting included the governorship candidate of the party in Plateau State and former Deputy Governor of the state, Mrs. Paullen Talen, who was a member of the Board of Trustees (BoT) of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP); Speaker of Ondo State House of Assembly and other members of Ondo State House of Assembly.

Original_Search_Title: Andre Ayew Shortlisted for CAF Player of the Year
Headline: Africa: Andre Ayew Shortlisted for CAF Player of the Year
Body_Text:
Ghana's Andre Ayew has been named on the five-man shortlist for the 2011 Caf Footballer of the Year award.
The Olympique Marseille midfielder is joined in the running for the award by Samuel Eto'o Fils of Cameroon, Mali's Seidu Keyta, Moussa Sow of Senegal and Yaya Toure of Cote d'Ivoire.
Asamoah Gyan and Kevin-Prince Boateng dropped out of the 10-man shortlist after the final list was revealed.
According to CAF, the CAF Technical, Football and Media Committees decided on the choice of shortlists for the other categories taking into account the performance of the nominees from January to November, 2011.
Ayew, 21, who is one of Ghana's key performers, helped the Black Stars pick silver at the 2010 Nations Cup and followed up with a great display at the World Cup that same year where the team reached the quarter-finals.
The winner will be announced at the awards ceremony that comes off in Accra, the Ghana capital on Thursday, December 22.
It will be the third time the Ghanaian capital will be hosting the annual event after 2006 and 2009.
The shortlisted nominees are (in alphabetical order);
African Player of the Year 2011
(a) Andre Ayew (Olympique Marseille and Ghana)
(b) Samuel Eto'o (Anzhi Makhachkala and Cameroon)
(c) Seydou Keita (Barcelona and Mali)
(d) Moussa Sow (Lille and Senegal)
(e) Yaya Toure (Manchester City and Cote d'Ivoire)
African Player of the Year 2011 (Based in Africa)
(a) Oussama Darragi (Esperance Sportive de Tunis and Tunisia)
(b) Zouhir Dhaouadi (Club Africain and Tunisia)
(c) Banana Yaya (Esperance Sportive de Tunis and Cameroon)
National Team of the Year
(a) Botswana
(b) Cote d'Ivoire
(c) Niger
(d) Tunisia
Women's National Team of the Year
(a) Cameroon
(b) Nigeria
(c) South Africa
Club of the Year
(a) Esperance Sportive de Tunis (Tunisia)
(b)Maghreb Association Sportive of Fez (Morocco)
(c) Wydad Athletic Club (Morocco)
Most Promising Talent of the Year
(a) Ahmed Elshenawy (El-Masry and Egypt)
(b) Souleymane Coulibaly (Tottenham Hotspurs and Cote d'Ivoire)
(c) Ahmed Musa (VVV Venlo and Nigeria)
Coach of the Year
(a) Harouna Doula (Niger)
(b) Nabil Maaloul (Esperance Sportive de Tunis) - Tunisia
(c) Stanley Tshosane (Botswana)
Women's Footballer of the Year
(a) Nompumelelo Nyandani (South Africa)
(b) Perpetua Nkwocha (Nigeria)
(c) Miriam Paixao Silva (Equatorial Guinea)
Referee of the Year
(a) Alioum Neant (Cameroon)
(b) Noumandiez Doue (Cote d'Ivoire)
(c) Djamel Haimoudi (Algeria)
Legend Award
Mustapha Hadji (Morocco)
Austin 'Jay-Jay' Okocha (Nigeria)

Original_Search_Title: Goodbye Waziri (1)
Headline: Nigeria: Goodbye Waziri (1)
Body_Text:
IT seems like Nigeria has woken from a terrible dream in which the fight against corruption was put on hold and the corruption fighters were sent home.
Recently, President Goodluck Jonathan gave the sleeping giant a sharp prod when he sacked Mrs. Farida Waziri and signalled through the appointment of Ibrahim Lamorde that the fight against corruption is not over yet.
From the time of her appointment as executive chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) in May 2008, Waziri seemed to enjoy an unusual degree of political protection from some people who were key players in President Umaru Yar'Adua's administration.
As Yar'Adua's health deteriorated, these people gained control of certain parts of the Presidency and the functions of government.
There were others who managed varying levels of control of other parts of the Presidency and the government as a whole. This was a dark time for Nigeria's emerging democracy.
The links between Waziri's benefactors and disgraced former Delta State Governor James Ibori were starting to become a significant liability of the cabal.
Nuhu Ribadu was despatched from office to make way for these cabal to operate and Waziri was installed as chairman of the EFCC to protect them.
Following her appointment, Waziri set about her job with zeal. She was immediately put to the task of thwarting the efforts of the UK Metropolitan Police Force's investigations into Ibori's affairs.
One week after her confirmation as new chairperson of the EFCC, Waziri indicated a significant change in the EFCC's direction with respect to the investigation of Ibori.
Henry Imasekha was a classmate, personal friend and close business associate of Ibori. He is the owner of Berkeley Group of Companies, Brisbane Investment Ltd, Bromley Investment Ltd, and was also chairman of Wilbros Nigeria.
Imasekha allegedly cooperated with Ibori in the diversion of funds to acquire Wilbros, a U.S. oil service firm in Port Harcourt, in 2007.
Imasekha bought Wilbros for $155 million, using his new company, Ascot Offshore Nigeria Limited, and funds provided by Ibori.
Imasekha had been under investigation by the EFCC and, in an effort to avoid arrest and possible arraignment with Ibori, he fled Nigeria.
He then travelled extensively between Accra, Ghana, South Africa and Florida, where he maintains a multimillion-dollar flat in Aventura, Miami.
Through Waziri's direct intervention, charges in Nigeria against Imasekha were dropped, whereupon he returned to Nigeria and occupied his eighth floor apartment at the Ocean Parade Towers on Banana Island, near Ikoyi, Lagos.
In August 2008, Yar'Adua appointed Mr. David Edevbie as his Principal Private Secretary, to replace Mr. Steve Orosanye, who had held the position for nearly 10 years.
Edevbie was Ibori's Commissioner of Finance and had been under investigation by the EFCC.
With Ribadu removed, the charges of money laundering had been dropped and the cabal around the President became stronger.
Waziri's efforts to protect Ibori and Imasekha were only partially successful.
Her actions had the side effect of straining relations with foreign agencies beyond their elastic limit and seriously damaged the valuable reputation of the EFCC.
By late 2008, Waziri's benefactors had become increasingly worried about the Ibori connection.
They set Waziri to the task of insulating them from the Metropolitan Police's investigations.
The methodology was simple, lawyers for each party would simply write to Waziri and seek a letter clearing their clients of any money laundering activities or connection with cases under investigation by the Metropolitan Police.
This blatant attempt to pervert the course of justice disgusted foreign anti-corruption agencies that had been working closely with the EFCC.
Soon, foreign anti-corruption agencies refused to meet with Waziri after previous experiences where, they alleged, they briefed her on cases against prominent Nigerians they were pursuing only to have Waziri reveal details of the cases to those being investigated in return for payment.
Waziri was thwarting Nigeria's foreign partners in the fight against corruption and they had come to the end of their patience with her.
The degree of distrust in Waziri became apparent on March 14, 2009 when Chief Ojo Maduekwe, who was at that time Nigeria's Minister of Foreign Affairs, telephoned Robin Sanders, the U.S. Ambassador to Nigeria.
Maduekwe invited Sanders to a private lunch to discuss the African Union Mission in Somalia.
Upon arrival Sanders was surprised to find another guest who Maduekwe had not mentioned, Waziri, chairman of the EFCC.
Sanders's anger at the deception was obvious to Maduekwe. She told Maduekwe that she 'was under instructions not to have any contact with Mrs. Waziri,' and demanded that Waziri be asked to leave.
Maduekwe, who appeared 'stunned,' told the ambassador that 'he understood there was a big impasse between our two nations over the EFCC, but did not interpret this as a direct instruction from Washington.'
At Sanders's insistence, Maduekwe's wife escorted Waziri to the door.
The ambassador and the minister then proceeded with their meeting. Meanwhile, Waziri's benefactors were becoming increasingly uncomfortable about their association with Ibori and the V-Mobile case, in which Ibori, Bola Tinubu and Victor Attah were alleged to have used funds from the governments of Delta, Lagos and Akwa Ibom states to invest in V-Mobile and to have converted the proceeds of the transactions to their personal use.
Funds from the transactions in Access Bank had been frozen by the EFCC. Waziri was not finished. She was now on a roll and the letter-writing was flowing.
On July 6, 2009 she responded to a letter from solicitors for Mohammed Buba Marwa, who was at that time Nigeria's ambassador to South Africa.
Marwa was being investigated over alleged kickbacks for contracts with Tata, signed by him, to supply vehicles for Nigeria's military and police when he was military attaché in New York in 1996.
In her letter of response, Waziri stated that investigations had been closed and there was not sufficient evidence to warrant prosecution.
It is alleged that she made this statement in the full knowledge that, in 2007, Marwa had written a letter of confession to then President Obasanjo Obasanjo, stating that he, Marwa, had helped the late Head of State, Sani Abacha, launder millions of dollars skimmed off the Tata vehicle deal, for which Marwa reportedly received $12 million.

Original_Search_Title: Where Parents Don't Talk to Kids Below Three
Headline: Uganda: Where Parents Don't Talk to Kids Below Three
Body_Text:
In the breakfast room at Le Meridien President hotel, men and women walked about with signs of a sleepless night written all over their faces.
For these delegates from Africa, Europe, Asia and America, the time difference was taking a toll on them. Some chewing on gumball to mask the morning bad breath from lack of brushing. We had not brushed nor had a change of clothes in a day and two days for others because our luggage was stuck somewhere between Jomo Kenyatta International airport in Nairobi, Kenya and Dakar, Senegal.
At different tables, I could hear some of the guests complain to their colleagues about their luggage Kenya Airways had not delivered three days later. We were in the francophone west for the International Conference on Family Planning 2011 to examine cutting edge research and programmes that are helping to advance the health and wealth of families and nations worldwide.
Like many ,I did not foresee anyway KQ would leave behind my luggage on a one-way flight straight to Senegal, I thought hand luggage was stressful and unnecessary. But when we disembarked at Leopold Sedar Senghor International airport in Dakar, there were just about 10 pieces of luggage on the conveyor belt and a man with a list of over 100 names whose luggage was back at JKI in a store. It was only Saturday, we had Sunday and so we excused them hoping and praying that it would arrive soon.
But on Sunday, the man returned again with yet another list of over 100 names from Sunday's flight making a total of about 300 people with no luggage. Most of the guests who flew Kenya Airways did not have their luggage that some speakers had to make presentations at the opening ceremony wearing nothing more than their travel clothes, jeans and a T-shirt. For Florence Machio, a speaker, her presentation material was sitting somewhere in Nairobi that she had to redo everything in one day.
"Dear Kenya Airways, being the pride of Africa means respecting your African travellers just because we don't scream at you when you constantly delay flights or lose our luggage does not mean that we don't get annoyed!
Today, without any explanation you did not load on the plane luggage for more than 100 passengers to Dakar. This is the father of all disrespect to your passengers on the continent", Ms Machio from Kenya shared on her Facebook page.
When we returned on Monday, one of the operations officers of the airline handed everyone about CFA Francs 40,000 (about $84). In Senegal with this money, one can get one pair of jeans, two T-shirts and a few necessities like a toothbrush and paste.
By day five, we still didn't have luggage and had recycled the only clothes in possession long enough for anyone to tell you by your usual attire. At the airport all hell broke loose. This was the day I was to learn that you can actually demand value for your money from airline personnel if they do not respect your rights as a customer. All along I thought if you as much as sneeze in defiance, CIA whisks you off. The West Africans must have grown up knowing better.
They didn't waste time grabbing the only KQ officer in sight by the collar and holding him hostage. That Tuesday evening, a near riot broke out at the airport in Dakar. The police had to be called to protect the officer who looked visibly shaken from the riotous crowd while his boss remained hidden yards away in his office.
Passengers insisted to be addressed by the KQ office from Nairobi. Police confiscated cameras and deleted the pictures that angry passengers were taking. It was tiring commuting to and from the airport everyday only to be given regrets and sent back.
"We will never fly KQ again, I am going to sue you", shouted one of the aggrieved passengers.
But the Operations Manager, Alassanne Diouf, would have none of it, "Go and sue if you want, if you do not fly with us someone else will", he shouted back.
The passenger only left when Diouf promised that Kenya Airways would chatter a flight to bring luggage to Dakar the next day. And they did, two days to the end of the conference.
Biting economy
This was my maiden trip to a French-speaking country and it was going to be a long one too. At the airport you are besieged by men selling sim cards, airtime and quick foreign exchange and women hawking bananas and peanuts. My first conversations with a Senegalese went like this. Him saying things I did not understand and me replying, "Hmmmm? What what what?"
Even where I tried using common words they could understand like names of places, I had to stress them in French; otherwise, I also looked like I was speaking alien. I realized I had to consult my vocal code, stress by Rs and speak a little louder to be heard. Good enough the people were warm and welcoming that if they did not understand what you were saying, they would look out for someone who understands English to help.
At the airport parking lot, I was met by a sea of the oldest most rusted weather beaten jalopy taxis only rivalled by Ethiopia's. And it's not like you could avoid jumping onto a Car Rapide because they are the main form of transport supplemented by clean yellow cabs that charge very high fares. But the Car Rapides can get very crowded that rumour has it they tip over and fall off the road
Lucky for me, there was a hotel shuttle waiting. Driving from the airport offered nothing interesting to the eye but at least the road network in town is devoid of potholes that I am used to here where we have partially tarmac potholes for roads. My hotel was sitting by the ocean with a beautiful view. From here I could get a glimpse of the historical Goree Island or what remains of what was a drop off point for slaves from across Africa in the slave trade until 1700.
Dakar has one of the best road networks in the world. But in the same breath, it has one of the most expensive economies. At Le Meridian President where I was staying, a meal cost about Shs 80,000 and a soda Shs 11,000. I thought that maybe it was a high-end hotel but just a kilometre away in a downtown restaurant things are no different.
A meal you would get in a decent restaurant in Kampala for Shs15,000 costs Shs 30,000 while a soda costs Shs 6,000 in a makeshift restaurant. During a lunch break, the Butambala Woman MP, Mariam Nalubega told of her sleepless nights. When she was booking, she thought the service at her hotel would match the 100 dollars she was paying. When she arrived, her bed was a three by six and her room buzzing with mosquitoes.
As if that was not enough, she said, she had to spend the nights awake shooing off mosquitoes and listening to barking dogs. And yet she couldn't change hotels because every hotel was fully booked. It was no different for Kinkizi East MP Dr Chris Baryomunsi and other delegates who were representing Uganda at the conference.
Time for prayer in Senegal, a country with a 94% Muslim population, is respected to the dot. Retailers leave their shops open and go out to pray but no one dares steal anything. At different taxi stages, taxi drivers abandon their vehicles and head out for prayers. Unlike Uganda where our traditional wear is reserved for special occasions like weddings, parties and holidays in favour of western style suits and dresses, Senegalese wear their traditional clothes to office, official functions, the market and to business meetings.
Women are expected to observe maximum standards of decent dressing, in brightly coloured boubous - the voluminous traditional gowns with dramatic matching head wraps, only excused when they are dancing.
On a Monday, we headed out to a district called Thies, about two hours' drive from Dakar where the land is dry, the food tasty and the culture interesting. We were told that in some tribes like the Wolof, parents are not supposed to talk or communicate with their babies at all. It is believed that when they talk to their babies, they are putting evil spirits in the baby. This affects the growth and development of the babys brain between ages 0-3.
Molly Melching, the Executive Director of Tostan, an NGO directed at social transformation and development, says only 11% of Senegalese children can read after three years in school. They go to school unprepared, cannot understand and they don't know how to talk or communicate with other people.
But at least the children know how to dance, and dance they do. Here I learnt that the pop culture dances famous on the streets of America like Break Dance or B-boying and booty shaking originate from West Africa, or at least parts of it from Senegal were they are in fact a tribal dance. Senegal is also popular for its music and some of the best African artists.
While attending a village meeting that was opened with a traditional dance, we the visitors were caught in our tracks when at first the women were dancing and flipping their skirts open in what looked like a kind of seduction.
Something akin to what Congolese Tshala Mwana serves Uganda when she stages a show here. I thought that it might be a mistake for them to flash parts of their upper thighs as they danced but when the children joined the dance; they also pulled up their skirts, and we realized this is part of the cultural dance.
These dances have sparked off riots in Arab countries like in 2007 when Senegalese dancers went to perform in an Arab country and there were riots from protesting Muslims.
The interest with which especially men watched and cheered the dancers showed how much guests enjoyed the show.

Original_Search_Title: Shortlists for CAF Awards 2011 Revealed
Headline: Africa: Shortlists for CAF Awards 2011 Revealed
Body_Text:
The Confederation of African Football (CAF) on WednesdayTuesday, December 13, 2011 revealed the final shortlists for the various categories for the CAF Awards 2011.
The shortlists for the 2011 African Player of the Year and 2011 African Player of the Year (Based in Africa) were decided by votes by the Head coaches or Technical Directors of the National Associations affiliated to CAF.
The CAF Technical, Football and Media Committees decided on the choice of shortlists for the other categories taking into account the performance of the nominees from January to November, 2011.
The winners will be unveiled at the Awards Gala scheduled for Thursday, December 22 at the Banquet Hall, State House in Accra. The award ceremony is to reward excellence in African football on and off the pitch.
It will be the third time the Ghanaian capital will be hosting the annual event after 2006 and 2009.
The shortlisted nominees are (in alphabetical order);
African Player of the Year 2011
(a) Andre Ayew (Olympique Marseille and Ghana)
(b) Samuel Eto'o (Anzhi Makhachkala and Cameroon)
(c) Seydou Keita (Barcelona and Mali)
(d) Moussa Sow (Lille and Senegal)
(e) Yaya Toure (Manchester City and Cote d'Ivoire)
African Player of the Year 2011 (Based in Africa)
(a) Oussama Darragi (Esperance Sportive de Tunis and Tunisia)
(b) Zouhir Dhaouadi (Club Africain and Tunisia)
(c) Banana Yaya (Esperance Sportive de Tunis and Cameroon)
National Team of the Year
(a) Botswana
(b) Cote d'Ivoire
(c) Niger
(d) Tunisia
Women's National Team of the Year
(a) Cameroon
(b) Nigeria
(c) South Africa
Club of the Year
(a) Esperance Sportive de Tunis (Tunisia)
(b)Maghreb Association Sportive of Fez (Morocco)
(c) Wydad Athletic Club (Morocco)
Most Promising Talent of the Year
(a) Ahmed Elshenawy (El-Masry and Egypt)
(b) Souleymane Coulibaly (Tottenham Hotspurs and Cote d'Ivoire)
(c) Ahmed Musa (VVV Venlo and Nigeria)
Coach of the Year
(a) Harouna Doula (Niger)
(b) Nabil Maaloul (Esperance Sportive de Tunis) - Tunisia
(c) Stanley Tshosane (Botswana)
Women's Footballer of the Year
(a) Nompumelelo Nyandani (South Africa)
(b) Perpetua Nkwocha (Nigeria)
(c) Miriam Paixao Silva (Equatorial Guinea)
Referee of the Year
(a) Alioum Neant (Cameroon)
(b) Noumandiez Doue (Cote d'Ivoire)
(c) Djamel Haimoudi (Algeria)
Legend Award
Mustapha Hadji (Morocco)
Austin 'Jay-Jay' Okocha (Nigeria)

Original_Search_Title: A Rediscovered Fast Life On Sea
Headline: Nigeria: A Rediscovered Fast Life On Sea
Body_Text:
JUWE OLUWAFEMI writes on the several top-notch business executives living the fast life on sea. They say it's an experience they won't trade for anything in this world because of the therapeutic feeling they get from it.
For Gbenga Ogunedojutimi and his popular refrain, which is the nursery rhyme: "All work and no play makes Jack a dull boy," you could tell, there's no dull moment for him.
Gbenga is truly one dude who loves to live life to the hilt. As a businessman with vast business concerns in the oil and gas sector, sprawling as far as the West coast of Africa, and one or two countries in Europe, Gbenga has not stopped telling his friends he is not a dull Jack; and to impress it on all those who care to hear him out, at the end of every week, it is now a tradition for him to take a boat ride from the Tarzan Jetty at Lekki Phase One to as far as the Suntan Badagry Beach coastal water area.
His companions are, a few selected friends, the soothing gentle waves of the sea that turns sheet after sheet of its endless pages, and of course, his favourite bottle of Champagne--- Krug, Clos du Mesnil.
For Gbenga and his select group of friends, the amusement is the sight of the rustic seaside, the mini desert along the Badagry Coastal Region and fishes that stray to the sea surface.
"From the seashore to the deepest depths," observes Gbenga, "The Ocean is home to some of the most diverse life on earth. There are big sea creatures and little ones; long and short ones, multi-coloured and drab ones, and those that never stop swimming as we speed past. Such sight is breathtaking and it makes me forget the troubles of this life."
With a paltry N50, 000, the Yemisi Osawaeh family who have formed a new habit of visiting the country on vacation from their London base in the United Kingdom says the entire family has been having a swell time of their lives on the Lagos Lagoon. Yemisi who is Wande's wife, specifically mentioned her sailing experience from the Lagos Yacht Club to Tarkwa Bay, courtesy of a boat rider, Oketola Oni, popularly called Wumbe who ensures the family misses out from none of the fun. "We sailed a lightening sailboat from the yacht club and anchored in Tarkwa Bay about 45 minutes later. We swam ashore and went body boarding. The waves were huge, but lots of fun. After a while of playing in the sand, and chatting with the locals, we swam back to the boat, and sailed home in safety under the expertise and watchful eyes of Wumbe," an excited Yemisi said.
From the dramatic coastlines and idyllic fishing villages to sea-bathing, promenades and donkey rides, the popularity of the seaside has led to its enduring status as a quintessential experience.
Hence, the sea has become the rendezvous of those who can afford such luxury. It is typical of such patrons to take a ride out of town from the hustle and bustle of Lagos on boat, accompanied by all of its wild excitement. Without doubt, the beautiful yachts and boats that adore the Lagos lagoon waterfronts give a glimpse of how adventurous life on sea can really be.
A look at the cosy interiors, safety gadgets and calibre of people who ride on these luxury boats speak of how expensive and classy such trips can really be.
In some parts of Lekki and other coastal areas of Lagos like Ikoyi and Victoria Island, hardly would one find houses that are situated by the waterfronts without catching the sight of beautiful cruise boats idly mooring on the Lagoon.
Interestingly, this latest trend from a tripartite angle is a win-win case for those involved in the game. While it is becoming big business for the boat men who need the money, it also serves as a kind of help for the real owners of these boats who mostly are in far away countries and would not want their boats just anchored by the sea-side, idling away. And for the fun loving patrons who cannot afford the very expensive short cruise boats, excitedly and expectedly, they readily opt for them.
Before now, it seems only a few with the "means" really savour the pleasure of a sea ride, but that trend is fast changing, because nowadays, there is a trend initiated by some experienced and influential riders to give those who desire an experience on the sea but without the heavy financial muscle back-up, the opportunity to do so at a "reasonable fee."
The Lagos Yacht Club is a case in point where members have better negotiations with riders who supervise yachts owned by club members who always want to savour a lifetime experience on the sea.
Wumbe, pointing at a boat, says, it has been lying ideal for six months because the owner practically lives in Canada, and as such, offers the ride to family members who can maintain it, or other commercial riders who need it for a while. The bottom-line, Wumbe says, "Is in order to warm the engine," adding, "I give out my contact to many who seek fun from the sea, and engage boat owners on deals that will service their boats and still leave something in our pockets."
Fredericks and his girl friend have formed the habit of having the time-out, almost every weekend to sail on the Lagoon aboard one of the beautiful boats berthing at the Civic Centre waterfront along Ozumba Mbadiwe, Victoria Island, Lagos.
The experience is usually grand because of the extra comfort and mileage of some of the boats that are entirely left in the care of the riders and technicians who decide when they are due for maintenance.
For one of such trips, Fredericks says he coughed out N10, 000 for a ride from the Civic Centre waterfront to Banana Island and back to the take-off point. "It was fun. At least my woman was happy, it was as though we were both on honeymoon," he enthuses.
But he notes that the rider cashes on the availability of the boats and the confidence the owners repose on him. "Often times, he calls me to tell me there's a boat available whenever one is readily at his disposal, courtesy of an owner is probably out of the country. So anytime there is one available and we are free, we go on a boat ride into the deep waters in the heart of Lagos and at times, on the outskirts of Lagos. If we are not free, we introduce new friends and colleagues that can pay for the service to our rider."
Another rider with a boat nicknamed 'Sea Cat' - a luxury cruise sea boat, notes that a boat makes no sense if it does not propel on the water and give the desired splendid ride.
He observes that while some boat owners prefer flying around, abandoning their cruise boats, the rides take advantage of their absence by taking patrons on a short ride on the Lagoon and smile to the banks. Never mind that these junketing real owners voluntarily give their boats to reliable riders who they expect will not only contract the boats out for some fees but also bear its maintenance cost and everything that goes with it.
But that, according to him, does not mean that the owners are regretting buying the boats. "It is just that business sometimes take them out for between three to four months with the boat rusting away," another boat owner observes.
There is however no illegality in the business that seems to be thriving for the riders. Wumbe however explains that riders must be registered, and the real owner must approve of it and often times even give what distance to cover on the sea. He said this became a recent caveat, from the real owners of many of these boats following the disappearance of many boats. He cited the case of a cruise boat of a church member which was stolen and found on the waters in Ghana. "In fact, the boat had been painted and we had given up any hope of finding it again, but somehow, somebody identified it and informed us. We rushed down to Accra n there it was. Now it is back to Lagos waters, but some of its exquisite furnishings have been badly tampered with," he said.

Original_Search_Title: His Domain - Surviving the Festivities
Headline: Uganda: His Domain - Surviving the Festivities
Body_Text:
Ask any man, and they will tell you that the month of December is such a complex one when it comes to their finances and we are not talking about the- "Oh I'm getting a hefty bonus this year and I don't know what to do with it' kind of financial complexity!
It's like Armageddon for many men during the festival season. It starts off with the salary coming into their accounts by mid-December. Who honestly expects that money to last even a week? As if that's not enough, the requests for Christmas gifts come from your long lost cousin in Gombe, a village you last visited during your grandmother's funeral rites, to your ex-girlfriend! It's a complex list.
By the way that list always has the following that must get a gift and if you don't get them one, your chances of starting the year as lonely as a cloud are high: your wife, children, parents, brothers, sisters, sisters to your wife, brothers to your wife, your father and mother-in-law and their pet dog too gets a Christmas card!
The dilemma for the man doesn't end with Christmas gifts. There is the food, trips upcountry to take to Jjaja some beef, then if your wife is a little cuckoo, a "family trip to Kidepo National Park" to watch giraffes feed themselves from a mile away with binoculars atop a jeep is a must too!
Now compute all that in monetary terms and then add the following to that pile of figures you have come up with and you will see why chances of migraines affecting men during festive seasons is quite high. There is rent to pay, school fees to clear come January and the wife's car needs servicing!
That my friends, is the dilemma of most men during the month of December. Whereas that might appear a burden, in actual fact Christmas time, (a time that stretches from approximately December 15 till December 31), is a merry-making time filled with fun, with those you love. It's characterised with parties, drinking, eating cake like it's going out of fashion and generally relaxing while the New Year begins.
So in essence, all those gifts and presents we give to our relatives, wives and...did I hear ex-girlfriends- are all justifiable in a way. But the problem many men face is that the cost of merry-making normally takes a huge toll on their pockets. And now with the economy going bananas, it's a headache for many men to find the right balance of merry-making yet not denting the bank balance.
Here is an example of how things have changed drastically for the man. Three years ago you could afford to buy your grandparents in the village four kilos of sugar with each kilo going for Shs2,000 and even chip in with some kilos of beef and trip down there to see them.
Now that is quite an uphill task. If it's not the price of sugar that is exorbitant, then it's the price of petrol that's maddening. In other words what was such a basic thing like visiting your grannies over the Christmas period, appears to be an impossibility now.
So what should a man do this Christmas period bearing in mind the economic hardships we are facing? Well I'm no financial analyst, but the following tips could come in handy;
•Buy a packet of cards (they are over 30 cards in one packet) and list all people that you need to give them and you do just that. Give them cards full stop.... not cards and a box of chocolates, etc!
•Instead of spending all this money on petrol, going upcountry to have Christmas with the Jjajja's for once just change the plan. How about sending them some money via Mobile Money say Shs100,000 and top that up with a call to them explaining how you wanted so badly to be with them but because of the hard times, you can't do that! Trust me, they will appreciate your honesty.
•Please don't ask your wife what Christmas gift she would like. You will prompt her to say, "A trip to Dubai honey!" We don't want that. Just go buy her a watch and take it to her with a smile and tell her, "Honey merry Christmas and this is my gift to you!"
•Remember to save some money that will start you off to the next year!
•Avoid borrowing. Goodness leave within your means.
rkalumba@ug.nationmedia.com

Original_Search_Title: Come On, as a Country We Can Do Better
Headline: Uganda: Come On Uganda, We Can Do Better
Body_Text:
Once a high profile Ugandan politician and member of parliament asked us, what are we missing here in Uganda? We seem to have everything that will take us to the forefront, yet we are still struggling!
You pulled the words out of my mouth honourable! You tell us why you and tens of your colleagues are at the helm, sitting in the pilot seat steering Uganda into the future, you tell us why we are not there? Not even close!
Many people who return from journeys abroad tend to compare Uganda with the country they visited and results are often not in favour of Uganda. For "some reason" my balance always tips more in favour of Uganda, maybe because my love for Uganda blinds me from seeing so much of the negative, while hoping that the future will bring us more good news. But then sitting and wishing will only enhance the phenomenon of "day dreaming".
One way of knowing the facts that will make us go forward, is establishing an inventory of what we have compared to what others have, or even better, what we have that others don't ! Without knowledge, without a proper understanding of Ugandan assets, we will take everything for granted and that is precisely why we are stagnant. The concrete has dried and our feet are stuck.
Maybe it will be the most used line to describe Ugandan weather as one of the best in the world. However, we have repeated it so many times that we have forgotten that the weather is actually one of the best assets we have. People in Europe and North America seek refuge from their cold weather, people in places with hot climate look for cooler places and we have what they look for, all year long! In most tourist oriented European countries and islands, six month of winter means "no business" and if something goes wrong during the peak season, 'such as financial crisis' then the whole year is doomed!
In my travels, we were driven miles far and away to visit sites such as waterfalls, ones that are not even close to the might and beauty of Murchison falls! We have toured mountains by people who have no clue how Ruwenzori looks like. We even visited places of worship for which we had to pay an entrance fee, and there was no source of the River Nile, no gorillas, no game drives nor the largest lake in the world to complement the journey! It seems people out there are making money from anything!
A tour in our craft market will also show you that apart from a mainly imported handcrafts, there is only a slight evolution in producing some bikes and bodabodas congested with villagers, their families, a banana bunch and a goat all on one bike!
Although the old foreign explorers discovered the best features of Africa, the expedition is still going on. Today I heard that Rayyan Air, one of the most successful budget airlines in Europe has been lately hit by recession with the number of its European customers going down, so in a bid to overcome this crisis, they might consider flying in the African skies! I am yet to see the day when Africa will wake up from its slumber and realise its own potential, until then, I will keep writing!

Original_Search_Title: Tunisia's Espérance to Play its First Match at the Club Soccer World Cup
Headline: Tunisia's Espérance to Play its First Match at the Club Soccer World Cup
Body_Text:
Tunisia's premier soccer club, Espérance Sportive Tunisienne (EST), is scheduled play their Qatari rival Al Sadd at the Club Soccer World Cup, tomorrow, December 11th, in Nagoya Stadium, Japan at 8am.
Participating in this international competition will be a unique experience for the Tunisian team. Esperance will not only represent Tunisia, but all of Africa, as they defend their championship title against each continent's foremost club soccer team.
The draw, held by the Competition Committee of FIFA, decided that the African champions will play against Asia's championship team: the Qataris Al-Sadd. Both teams are looking forward to their first victory in such a significant competition, particularly since the winner will get the chance to play against the Spanish team, FC Barcelona, on December 14th.
Coach of Espérance, Nabil Maaloul, stated that his team has taken the time it needs to prepare for, and adapt to, the differences in climate and time zone. "I hope that I can leave my mark on this club's heritage", he added.
The Espérance players who are likely to participate are: Ben Chérifa, Afful, Chemmam, Hicheri, Banana, Korbi, Traoui, Darragi, Msakni, Bouazzi and Ndjang.

Original_Search_Title: Okocha, Nkwocha, Musa Nominated
Headline: Nigeria: Okocha, Nkwocha, Musa Nominated
Body_Text:
Ex-Nigerian International and former Captain of Super Eagles, Austin Jay-Jay Okocha, former African Women Player of the Year, Perpetua Nkwocha and Super Eagles winger, Ahmed Musa of VVV Venlo of Holland have been nominated for other categories segment of the 2011 GLO-CAF Awards.
The Confederation of African football released the list of nominees on Wednesday in Cairo. The list which was compiled through a vigorous exercise by the Confederation of African Football, took into account the performance of the nominees from January to November, 2011.
Okocha was nominated together with ex- Moroccan international, Mustapha Hadji for the Legend Award while Perpetua who distinguished herself at the last African women championship in South Africa was nominated for Women Footballer of the Year.
Ahmed Musa, a very resourceful Nigerian player who played for the U-20 team, Flying Eagles at the last World Cup in Colombia, and also featured for both the National U-23 side and the Super Eagles at various times was nominated for Most Promising Talent of the Year Award.
The winners will be announced at the Awards Gala on Thursday, December 22, at the Banquet Hall, State House and Accra.
The City of Accra is already agog with excitement as it prepares to host the event with the nominated award winners, especially Ghanaian players on the list forming the subject of discussion among football fans and youth across the city. It will be the third time the Ghanaian capital will be hosting the annual event after 2006 and 2009.
The nominees (in alphabetical order) include: National Team of the Year: (a) Botswana (b) Cote d'Ivoire (c) Libya (d) Niger (e) Tunisia (CHAN Team).Women's National Team of the Year: (a) Cameroon (b) Nigeria (c) South Africa
Club of the Year: (a) Esperance (Tunisia) (b) MAS Fes (Morocco) (c) Wydad Athletic Club (Morocco)
Coach of the Year: (a) Harouna Doula (Niger National Team) (b) Nabil Maaloul (Esperance- Tunisia (c) Rachid Taoussi(MAS Fes- Morocco) (d) Stanley Tshosane (Botswana National Team)
Most Promising Talent: (a) Ahmed Elshenawy (Elmasry- Egypt) (b) Ahmed Musa (VVV Venlo - Nigeria) (c) Souleymane Coulibaly (Tottenham Hotspurs-Cote D'Ivoire) (d) Banana Yaya (Esperance- Cameroon)
Women's Footballer of the Year: (a) Nompumelelo Nyandani (South Africa) (b) Miriam Paixao Silva (Equatorial Guinea) (c ) Perpetua Nkwocha (Nigeria)
Referee Award (a) Alioum Neant (Cameroon) (b) Djamel Haimoudi (Algeria) (c) Noumandiez Doue (Cote d'Ivoire).

Original_Search_Title: Americans Bloated With Propaganda
Headline: Zimbabwe: Americans Bloated With Propaganda
Body_Text:
With the Statue of Liberty facing East, the hemisphere the brigandsa who decimated Amerindians associated with repression, the United States gave itself the undeserved moniker, "the Free World." The uninitiated believe the propaganda that all is lekker in America, even our own senior musician, Thomas Mapfumo dedicated a song, Big in America, to that myth prompting another Chimurenga musician, Robbie Chagumuka, may his soul rest in peace, to counter with the song, Big in Zimbabwe.
Be that as it may, in the US, so-called prestigious publications will print the most outrageous information without attribution, investigation or proof of very serious charges.
When Americans think of propagandised people, they think of the now defunct Soviet Union or Nazi Germany or perhaps a banana republic dictatorship of the sort supported by their governments.
Very few of them would think of themselves as being under the sway of a government and corporations who work hand-in-glove to tell outright falsehoods or hide important information that is inconvenient for them.Americans are victims of a government intent on keeping them misinformed or silent in the face of its wrongdoing worldwide, but they work hand-in-hand with a media almost entirely owned by corporations.
The interests of the people are rarely in sync with the interests of these corporations, and the result are media which work with the government which consciously works to misdirect our attention or have us believe outright lies.
Almost every major news story gives the world an example of this terrible phenomenon. We are all told Iran is on the verge of acquiring nuclear weapons capability, but we are not told that it has the right to do so as a signatory of the Non Proliferation Treaty.
To add insult to injury, there isn't any proof that Iran has even acquired this capability.
But Israel is chomping at the bit to attack Iran, and its lackey state, the United States of America, will not stand in its way. The corporate media never tell us that Israel, a country which is not a signatory of the NPT, has an arsenal of an unknown number of nuclear weapons.
A simple and easily provable fact, that the nation which casts itself as the victim of a potential nuclear power is itself nuclear, is rarely mentioned. One might read the New York Times and Washington
Post everyday and watch one of the broadcast or cable news networks at night, and not be aware of this information.
"Government sources say," "we are barred from reporting from inside hence we cannot verify this report" are the buzz phrases for falsehoods told in the name of the state. So-called prestigious publications will print the most outrageous information without attribution, investigation or proof of very serious charges. The Washington Post prints a story, quoting an anonymous source, claiming that Gaddafi's Libya not only was in possession of a mustard gas stockpile, but also that it was supplied by Iran. When Israel and/or the US attack Iran none of the highly paid anchor men or women or reporters who have access to the administration will stray far from the official party line. That is why one president is as dangerous as any other. The system rewarded the liars who drove America to war against Iraq. The anti-Iranian warmongers will probably fare equally well.
Foreign policy has long been the domain of the big lie, but reporting or the lack of reporting on important domestic issues are either twisted in favor of the powerful or ignored altogether. The sins of omission are as dangerous as the sins of the bald faced lie. Did any major newspaper report on the week-long Georgia prison strike which took place one year ago? This phenomenal story of incarcerated Bloods and Crips working hand in hand with members of Aryan nation should have been frontpage news. But it was ignored, and the country lost an opportunity to be informed that their nation has the largest prison system on the planet and that it profits quite literally from their slave labour.
The press corps in Libya, who actively assisted Nato in destroying that nation, committed international war crimes in the process. They will never reveal their own evil doing nor will they be called to account by a public that doesn't even realise the magnitude of their crimes.
Anyone who is resistant to the notion that the US is awash in propaganda need look no further than the case of Anwar al-Awlaki. Barack Obama ordered the killing of an American citizen who was never charged with a crime. He then directed his minions to reveal to the New York Times the existence of a secret, extra legal memo which justifies his actions. The Times no doubt celebrated their role as court favourites and ignored the role that a newspaper ought to play in reporting a story that should have resulted in a presidential impeachment.
Americans love to say that they live in the greatest country in the world.
Such a nation will be hard pressed to admit that they can trust little of the information they get.
The nexus of a corrupt governmental structure and a corporate media results in nonsense on a good day and misinformation on a bad one.
The US is not great, it is just powerful and dangerous.

Original_Search_Title: Sudan, Rwanda Storm Cecafa Semis
Headline: Africa: Sudan, Rwanda Storm Cecafa Semis
Body_Text:
SUDAN, full of confidence befitting the only Cecafa nation going to next year's African Cup of Nations, emphatically stormed into the semi-finals of the Challenge Cup with a 2-0 defeat of Burundi at the National Stadium on Monday.
Rwanda were the second qualifiers on the day when they won a intriguingly close battle with the far from cowed "small" Zanzibar, winners of the trophy only one previous time, 1995.
On account of earlier good performances Burundi were deemed by some as a possible banana skin for Sudan but their experienced players led by example, going about the business of scuttling all of Burundi's strategy.
Amir Abdelnabi and Musa Mohammed scored on either side of half time.
After 70 minutes, the Sudanese were down to 10 men, following the dismissal of Nagmeldin Abdalla, but they had already imposed themselves on the match and there was no comeback for Burundi.
But Burundi blew the chance to hang on to hope by missing a penalty kick.
Sudan had shown early intent by insistent attack of the Burundi goal. Khalifa Ahmed forced a save of a booming shot from Burundi 'keeper Vladimir Niyonkuru in the 16th minute.
Abdelnabi headed home a right wing cross past Niyonkuru four minutes from halftime. Burundi's efforts of a comeback included Fuady Ndayisenga forcing Sudan' 'keeper Elmoiz Mahgoub to scrabble a save.
Sudan sealed victory when Musa Mohammed picked up the ball in the box; beat his marker before slotting the ball home from 16 yards in the 60th minute.
Burundi, not dead and out, had skipper Floribert Ndayisaba striking the woodwork with a ferocious free kick on 62 minutes. Sudan lost a man 20 minutes from time after Nagmeldin Abdalla was sent off for retaliating against Karim Nizigiyamana's apparent rough play.
Burundi were then to miss a after Bashir Koko brought down Ndayisenga in the box when Hassan Hakizimana's kick hit the crossbar and bounced out.
During the second game, Meddie Kagere's winning score two minutes from the end was a contender for the goal-of-the-tournament. Veteran Rwanda captain Olivier Karekezi head down and back from the box for the ball to take a sweet bounce from where Kagere blasted in a stunning volley high into the far corner.
Jean Baptiste Mugiraneza headed Rwanda into a 39th minute lead, rising unmarked from a Haruna Niyonzima kick from the right corner flag.
Zanzibar levelled within a minute of the second half substitute Abdulrahman Mohammed succeeding with his first possession. Kassim Suleiman struck a long pass into the box and Mohammed had brilliant control, shrugged off the last defender, before beating an onrushing Jean Claude Ndori.
Unbeaten from their Group 'A' preliminaries, Rwanda were hot pre-match favourites, also tipped as possible outright winners of this year's title. But the Amavubi met a brilliantly skillful Zanzibar with wonderful fighting spirit. Rwanda will face Sudan in the semis.

Original_Search_Title: It's Time Parliament 'Advised' President Museveni to Step Aside
Headline: Uganda: It's Time Parliament 'Advised' President Museveni to Step Aside
Body_Text:
As of last week, a third senior government official had told the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) of Parliament that the President directed them to effect an inflated, scandalous and allegedly fraudulent payment of Shs142.6 billion to controversial businessman Hassan Basajjabalaba.
Former Minister of Finance Syda Bbumba, former Attorney General Khiddu Makubuya and Central Bank Governor Emmanuel Tumusiime Mutebile pointed the accusing finger at the President.
Once upon a time, a newspaper editorial described PAC as the "Public Amusement Committee" for its penchant for carrying out investigations and coming out with damning and sensational allegations about misuse of public money. Next, they sit back and nothing happens as those accused and suspected of corruption are let to go scot-free.
This puts into question the wisdom of carrying out the probes in the first place.
But PAC and many other committees of Parliament have a record of playing hard ball with the very small fish; the small time government officials, who a radio pundit described as "petty banana thieves." Here Parliament usually acts with gusto sometimes leading to a conviction.
Many of those who are either interdicted or end up behind the prison gate, go kicking and truly or falsely screaming that they were acting on "orders from very high above." What is not in doubt, however, is that an environment that makes it easy and convenient to steal public funds has been created and immovably entrenched by a multiplicity of factors.
These stem from the fact that for the last 25 years, Museveni's government has 'succeeded itself' over and over again. His 'new government' includes the same old fellows who logically do not audit the 'past government' as would be the practice in case of a real change of guards.
So, the rot becomes part of the furniture and more is built on top of it. It becomes a safe habit, then a right to steal from the public. Secondly, to achieve loyalty and perpetuate itself in power, there has been a tendency to appoint 'reliable' people and those with 'experience.' Unfortunately, these are mainly the same old people and more so from the same ethnic group as the President.
Any attempts at ensuring order, honesty and integrity would end up injuring relatives, friends and in-laws. And this is what makes the work of PAC and most of the commissions of inquiry appear like a circus for the amusement of the public. They are mainly dealing with matters to do with 'untouchables.'
Amused as it may be, the public bears the brunt of the corruption circus. For about five years, a tax collected as a road fund was added on every litre of fuel consumed by motorist. Yet the potholes are taking over the roads at an alarming rate. The number of people in the labour force demonstrating and striking over pay and better working conditions is increasing yet the economy keeps 'growing steadily.'
The electricity tariff has almost doubled over the last five years, but load-shedding is on the increase. Hospitals, which have turned into hospices over the years, have been overwhelmed and are now beginning to close down because they can no longer act as places safe enough for people to die with dignity. So, what do we do? It is better for Parliament and Uganda at large to stop wasting time probing corruption and instead come up with a plan to break the status quo cited above.
The Yoweri Museveni era is now impotent and must be phased out slowly with the guarantee of his safety and protection of his 'reasonable interests' being paramount. Parliament should begin a process and debates to give Museveni an opportunity to reshuffle his Cabinet appointing an 'heir' he is comfortable with to act as a president for the remainder of his term as Museveni directs her/him from the background.
For starters, it may be anyone of his choice, including a close member of his family who would then not be eligible to lead after this term ends. Laws may be designed to ensure immunity from prosecution for any commissions and omissions for Museveni and a selected few of his acolytes during his reign starting from January 1986 to the date when he appoints the 'heir.'
The debate may then be widened with national discussions and conferences about a Uganda post-the Museveni hegemony. Without this, PAC and any other efforts will remain acts of futility; lamenting and amusing the public as Uganda retrogresses. We have to bite the bullet and start at the earliest on Project Uganda Without Museveni. Otherwise, elections, demonstrations, investigations and commissions of inquiry will end up predictably in frustration.
Mr Sengoba is a commentator on political and social issues.
nicholassengoba@yahoo.com

Original_Search_Title: St Lawrence's Entrepreneurial Lessons Paying Off
Headline: Uganda: St Lawrence's Entrepreneurial Lessons Paying Off
Body_Text:
When St. Lawrence University (SLAU) opened its doors to the public five years ago, students were hesitant to join, regarding it as an institution for 'failures'.
But Brian Kayongo who had scored 15 points from St. Augustine's College Wakiso in an Arts combination had the confidence to join the university.
Reason? This was an opportunity for him to have a hands-on experience, since they were still few students and the student ratio to equipment like computers was 2:1.
As one of the pioneer graduates with a bachelor in Industrial Art and Design, Kayongo says, their lecturers spent a few hours teaching them and allowed them time to think of anything they could do on their own.
So as early as second year, Kayongo was running his own photo studio in Namungona and on a good day, he made Shs 20, 000 in profits.
By the time he graduated in 2010, Kayongo had scored a first class degree and he had good use of the Shs1m that the university awarded him. Kayongo's quest for entrepreneurship skills is what this young university is promoting.
On November 26, when the university held its second graduation, Kayongo was one of the pioneer alumni whose success story touched many. He has not only acquired a photo studio of his own but he also owns an art and crafts shop.
Unique of this varsity is that entrepreneurial skills are spread to other members of the public especially market vendors. Lawrence Mukiibi the chairman board of governors explains that while this is one way of giving back to the community, such a group of people need these skills to improve their sales and expand their businesses.
Last year, 30 vendors from the markets of Kisekka, Owino, Kasubi and Nankulabye attained certificates in entrepreneurship. This year, 50 were awarded.
However, although these skills are important, John Sebandeke, a primary three drop-out from Kalerwe market says had the course not been free, he would not have enrolled. But now that he has the skills, he hopes to employ people to run his banana stalls as he plans for other business ventures.
Mukiibi also noted that the university has an annual entrepreneurship contest, where contestants come up with business plans and the winner walks away with a cash award. With such opportunities, Mukiibi says, he does not expect any graduates from SLAU to search for jobs.
The retired Archbishop of Kampala, Emmanuel Cardinal Wamala, the guest of honor, encouraged graduates to be exemplary citizens with tight morals and reject such issues as gay rights.
"My dear children, like your motto says, 'Light your candle', please use your education to save the country from all evil," Wamala said.
Prof Gilbert Bukenya the university chancellor, asked graduates to use their degrees and diplomas to boost confidence and uplift the standards of the nation in this economic crisis.
"Some of you may think that school is over, but remember that education still continues; as alumni, use entrepreneurial skills and talents to enhance development and meet the needs of society," he said.
In the last five years, SLAU has grown in leaps. Its current infrastructure can run two universities; the student's population has risen to about 3,000 students who, according to Dr Frank Kakinda, the vice chancellor, not only come for the good infrastructure but for the quality of education as well.
And like its sister campuses, the university hosts a good number of international students and has since started SLAU campuses in Rwanda. They hope to spread to the different East African countries.
At the occasion, 333 graduates received diplomas, certificates and degrees. Of these, 186 were male and 147 female. Dr Kakinda attributed the high number of female graduands to the emphasis on girl child education.
