A win for Wydad or a score draw in the second leg of the final and the Moroccans raise the trophy, pocket 1.5 million dollars and secure a place the FIFA Club World Cup in Japan during December.
Victory for the Tunisians and they take the spoils while a repeat of the goalless first leg in Casablanca last Sunday and the biggest club fixture on the continent goes down to a penalty shoot-out.
Each club won the competition once in the 1990s when it was called the African Champions Cup and Esperance have lost three finals since, including a humiliating 6-1 thrashing last year from Democratic Republic of Congo outfit TP Mazembe.
Neither team has a proud record in Confederation of African Football (CAF) finals with Esperance winning four of eight for a 50 percent success rate and Wyad only two of five for a 40 percent rate.
But all those failures will be forgotten by the side that emerges triumphant from the fourth Champions League clash between them in as many months following 2-2 and 0-0 stalemates in Morocco and a 0-0 deadlock in Tunisia.
The first leg followed a predictable pattern until the closing stages with a Wydad team encouraged by a 70,000 crowd at the Mohammed V Stadium controlling possession and creating a few scoring chances.
Defender Yassine Rami and Congo Brazzaville-born sharp-shooter Fabrice Ondama went closest for the 'Red and whites' while goalkeeper Moez Ben Cherifia produced several spectacular saves for the 'Blood and Gold'.
Esperance coach Nabil Maaloul has proved a master tactician during the campaign and his second-half introduction of offensive midfielder Oussama Darragi had a significant impact.
Club captain Darragi created space and fellow midfielders Mejdi Traoui and Khaled Mouelhi were not far off target as the visitors sought the away victory that would have virtually guaranteed them becoming African champions a second time.
"My players are determind to win the Champions League this year and engrave their names in Tunisian football history. This is a very special match for a club that has been chasing this trophy for a long time," stressed 49-year-old Maaloul.
After winning the domestic league and cup double, success over Wydad would complete a memorable year for Esperance just 12 months after suffering a record-equalling overall loss in the Champions League final.
Wydad handler Michel Decastel is Swiss, six years older than Maaloul, greyer around the temples, and spent a season with Esperance during a coaching career that has taken him to several African countries.
He remains optimistic that Wydad can emulate neighbours Raja Casablanca, who were held 0-0 by Esperance in the 1999 final only to force a similar result on the road and win 4-3 on penalties.
"An injury to our brilliant striker Mouhssine Iajour during the first leg left us with only one outright attacker for much of the game. The return match will be different and we have every chance of success," he insisted.
Experienced Iajour and younger Ondama have snatched five goals each in the Champions League and appear the biggest threats to an Esperance defence featuring Cameroonian giant Yaya Banana.
Esperance midfielder Youssef Msakni has also struck five Champions League goals during the 10-month campaign and Darragi and another Cameroonian import, Yannick Ndjeng, four apiece.
Despite what Raja achieved 12 years ago, tradition favours Esperance with eight of 10 clubs who forced a goalless away draw in the first leg of the final going on to lift a trophy that symbolises continental supremacy. - ANP/AFP

Original_Search_Title: African Best Player Awards - Two Cameroonians Nominated
Headline: African Best Player Awards - Two Cameroonians Nominated
Body_Text:
Eto'o is among nominees for players outside the continent while Yaya Banana represents the country in the category of players' resident on the continent.
Cameroon maybe facing dwindling fortunes at the level of the football national team but the players continues to fly the flag of the nation high within football circles, thanks to their individual talents. Two Cameroonians feature among the nominee for the 2011 GLO-CAF African best player award. Samuel Eto'o will be representing the country in the category of players based outside the continent while Yaya Bana was nominated among the players eveolving on the African continent.
Eto'o may not be among the favourites to win the trophy this time but the fact that he features among the best ten on the continent, is a source of pride to the nation. Eto'o is a top drawer performer, having won the coveted title four times; three consecutively and has won a lot in his career. In 2011, he won just a single trophy with Inter Milan, the Italian Cup and was named man of the match in the final, having scored two of the three goals. He scored 21 goals in 35 matches in the championship with lots of decisive passes too. In spite of his four goals in the qualifiers Cameroon failed to qualify for the 2012 CAN. He was transferred for a three-year contract in August 2011 for 27 million Euros to FK Anzhi Makhatchkala, in the Russian championship.
The favourite in this category is Moussa. The Senegalese striker born in France won the French championship and French Cup with Lille in 2011. He scored 25 goals in the championship with two hat-tricks, making him the most deadly attacker in the championship. He is also part of the new generation of the Senegalese national team which prevailed over Cameroon and DR Congo in the 2012 CAN qualifiers. Sow scored 5 goals in the qualifiers and will be one of those to watch in Gabon and Equatorial Guinea.
Yaya Banana is a budding tall and strong Cameroonian defender playing with Esperance since 2009. He won the Tunisian Cup and the Championship title and qualified for the final of the Champions League. He has been an influential national team player with Cameroon's U-20 team which reached the finals of the African Youth Championship in South Africa and reached the quarter-finals of the FIFA U-20 World Cup in Colombia this year. His talent caught the eye of Dennis Lavagne who summoned him for the first time to join the senior national team. However, the player wasn't able to honour the rendezvous of the LG tournament in Morocco due to the involvement of his club in the final of the African Champions League against Wydad of Casablanca. The winner will be decided by votes from the Head Coaches or the Technical Directors of the National Associations affiliated to CAF.
The Awards Gala will be held on on Thursday, December 22 in Accra, Ghana. It will be the third time the Ghanaian capital will be hosting the annual event after 2006 and 2009.

Original_Search_Title: Edward Sadomba in CAF Top 10
Headline: Zimbabwe: Edward Sadomba in CAF Top 10
Body_Text:
EDWARD SADOMBA'S international profile rose to another level on Wednesday when the Zimbabwe international scored a first for his country by being named by the Confederation of African Football among the best 10 players plying their trade on the continent.
The Al-Hilal talismanic forward may have failed to inspire the Warriors to the 2012 African Cup of Nations finals but his exploits for the Sudanese giants did not go unnoticed.
He secured a rare feat by Zimbabwean players being recognised by Caf and the continental body's panelists for his performances in 2011.
Sadomba's inclusion, among the top 10, means he is now in line to vie for a top five finish after, which Caf will unveil the best three players from those plying their trade in Africa at a ceremony to be held in Accra, Ghana, on December 22.
It will be the third time the Ghanaian capital will be hosting the annual event after staging the 2006 and 2009 events.
According to reports from Accra, the nominees for the 2011 Player of the Year (Based in Africa) and the 2011 Player of the Year were unveiled at a function presided over by Kwesi Nyantakyi, Caf executive committee member, and president of the Ghana Football Association.
Ten nominees each were named for the two categories based on the performance and contribution of the players to their clubs and national teams during the year under review.
For the first time, there are 10 nominees for the African Player of the Year (Based in Africa), contrary to the five for last year.
The lists was compiled through a vigorous exercise by the Technical, Football and Media Committees of Caf, taking into account the performance of the player for his national team and club from January to November, 2011.
Sadomba, scorer of seven goals in 12 Champions League matches for Al Hilal this year, could win the tournament's Golden Boot if none of his nearest rivals manage to find the target in the second leg of the competition's final.
But the former Dynamos striker somehow just could not transform his club form to the Warriors and was part of Norman Mapeza's class that failed to secure a place at the Nations Cup finals to be co-hosted by Gabon and Equatorial Guinea in January.
Sadomba was, however, a regular member of the Warriors squad that lost the Group A qualifying race to Mali with the Al Hilal forward who inspired the Sudanese giants to a Champions League semi-final place, only missing Zimbabwe's last qualifier against Cape Verde on October 8 due to a shoulder injury.
In the race for Africa's best player, Sadomba will compete against the likes of Nigerian Kalu Uche, who plays for Enyimba, Botswana international Jerome Ramatlhakwane of South African Division One side Vasco da Gama, whose goals helped the Zebras surprise everyone and become the first team to qualify for the Nations Cup from a tough five-team group that included seasoned campaigners like Tunisia and Togo.
The list also includes the duo of Cameroonian defender Banana Yaya and striker Youssef Msakni of Tunisian giants Esperance as well as Libyan and Al Ittihad goalkeeper Samir Aboud who helped his country qualify for the Nations Cup, conceding just one goal in the qualifiers.
Despite reaching another personal milestone Sadomba remained modest and attributed the success to support from teammates, family and fans.
Sadomba, speaking from his base in Khartoum, also felt that just being nominated among the top 10 was a big honour in itself although he is eager to claim the big prize.
"I just want to thank God for this blessing, I always put everything before God and it's because of God's wish that you get to be recognised like this. I also want to thank my family, my teammates and the supporters for all the support they gave me during the year.
"It is a great honour to be among Africa top 10 and flying your country's flag high up there," Sadomba said.
Nominees for the 2011 African Player of the Year (Based in Africa)
The nominees are in alphabetical order:
1. Ayoub El Khahiqi
Date of birth: 11th September 1986
Club: WAC (Morocco)
The right winger has been part of WAC's successful campaign in this year's Champions League where they have reached the finals. He won the 2010 CAF Confederation Cup and Trone Cups with his previous club, FUS Rabat before joining WAC.
2. Banana Yaya
Date of birth: 12th July 1991
Club: Esperance of Tunis (Tunisia)
The tall and strong Cameroonian defender has been playing with Esperance since 2009. He won the Tunisian Cup and the Championship title and qualified for the final of the Champions League. He has been an influential national team player with Cameroon's U-20 team which reached the finals of the African Youth Championship in South Africa and reached the quarter-finals of the FIFA U-20 World Cup in Colombia this year (2011).
3. Edward Sadomba
Date of birth: 31st August 1983
Club: Al Hilal (Sudan)
The Zimbabwean international extended his prominence this season with his goals leading Al Hilal to the semi-final of the Orange CAF Champions League. Sadomba scored seven of Al Hilal's 12 goals in the continental club competition. He however failed to assist his home country Zimbabwe qualify for 2012 CAN.
4. Idrissa Laouali
Date of birth: 9th November 1979
Club: ASFA (Burkina Faso)
The midfielder had a good run with his Burkina Faso club, ASFA and was part of the Niger national team (Mena) which made it to the quarter finals of CHAN 2011 in Sudan. Niger made its first ever finals of a continental senior tournament at CHAN and also qualified for AFCON 2012 finals for the first time. His experience makes him one of the key figures of the national team of Niger.
5. Jerome Ramatlhakwane
Date of birth: 29th October 1985
Club: Vasco da Gama (South Africa)
The Botswana international stunned everyone as his national team became the first team to qualify for the 2012 AFCON in a group that was difficult to pick out a favourite. He finished second top goals scorer in the AFCON 2012 qualifiers with 5 goals. He scored both goals in Botswana's double 1-0 victories over Tunisia. Like most southern African players, he plays professional football in South Africa.
6.Kalu Uche
Date of birth: 16th May 1990
Club: Enyimba (Nigeria).
Uche emerged as Enyimba's top goal scorer in the Champions League campaign with 4 goals. He led the team to the semi-final of the competition before bowing out to by WAC of Casablanca.
7. Oussama Darragi
Date of birth: 3rd April 1987
Club: Esperance (Tunisia)
He has had a successful year winning a number of silverware. He won the Tunisian championship and the Tunisian Cup with Esperance. In February he won the second edition of CHAN in Sudan and has reached Champions League final with Esperance. The 24-year-old midfielder has become one of the pillars of the national team which has qualified for the 2012 CAN.
8. Samir Aboud
Date of birth: 29th September 1972
Club: Al Ittihad (Libya)
The Al Ittihad keeper, who just celebrated his 39th birthday, is the pillar of the defence of the national team which qualified for the 2012 CAN. He conceded just a goal in the qualifiers. He will be participating in the CAN for the second time after playing with Libya in AFCON 2006 held in Egypt.
9. Youssef Msakni
Date of birth: 28th October 1990
Club: Esperance (Tunisia)
Msakni won CHAN with Tunisia in February this year in Sudan. He also won the Cup and the national championship with Esperance. He has made it a second consecutive time in the cafe Champions League final after Esperance qualified for 2011 two leg final. The young striker is part of the Carthage Eagles which has qualified for 2012 AFCON finals.
10. Zouheir Dhaouadi
Date of birth: 1st January 1988
Club: Club Africain (Tunisia)
He won the CHAN in February and was voted the best player and top scorer. The offensive midfielder also showcased his skills in the Orange CAF Confederation Cup as his club, Club Africain made its way to the final.
2011 African Player of the Year:
Adel Taarabt (Morocco and Queens Park Rangers)
Andre Ayew (Ghana and Olympique de Marseille)
Asamoah Gyan (Ghana and Al-Ain)
Didier Drogba (Cote d' Ivoire and Chelsea)
Kevin-Prince Boateng (Ghana and AC Milan)
Moussa Sow (Senegal and Lille)
Samuel Eto'o (Cameroon and Anzhi)
Seydou Keita (Mali and Barcelona)
Yao Kouassi Gervais "Gervinho" (Cote d'Ivoire and Arsenal)
Yaya Toure (Cote d'Ivoire and Manchester City)

Original_Search_Title: Nigerians Missing in CAF Awards
Headline: Nigerians Missing in CAF Awards
Body_Text:
Confederation of African Football yesterday released its ten man shortlist for the African footballer of the year award. And for the second year in the row, no Nigerian player is in the top 10. It is not surprising following the nose-diving in fortunes of Nigerian football in the past one year. In the award for local based player in the league in Africa, Enyimba's Kalu Uche made the cut. But Nigeria's West African rival Ghana has three players in the top 10. Asamoah Gyan, Andre Ayew and Kevin Prince Boateng are on the list. Ivory Coast also boasts of the trio of Didier Drogba, Gervinho and Yaya Toure. They will face a tough challenge from four-time winner Samuel Eto'o from Cameroon.
Also named in the shortlist are Moroccan Adel Tarrabt, Moussa Sow of Senegal and Mali's Seydou Keita.
Gyan and Ayew were instrumental for the Black Stars as they reached the 2012 Africa Cup of Nations with a perfect record while Boateng was key in AC Milan's annexation of the Serie A title. Gyan scored an amazing late equaliser for the Black Stars in the match against England in March which could rank as Africa's biggest game of the year even though it was a friendly.
Ivory Coast romped through their qualifier with Toure, Drogba and Gervinho playing important roles in their unbeaten run while Gervinho helped Lille to win the French league title before he moved to Arsenal in August. Eto'o, who has already won the award four times, was included in the shortlist despite failing to achieve anything significant with Cameroon and Inter Milan before he moved to Russia to play for Anzhi. Sow, who finished as the top scorer in the French top flight last season, was also key as Senegal edged past Cameroon to qualify for the 2012 Africa Cup of Nations.
Shortlist for local players: Ayoub Elkhiqi (Morocco and WAC, Yaya Banana (Cameroon and EST), Edward Sadomba (Zimbabwe and Al Hilal), Idrissa Laouali (Niger and ASFA), Jerome Ramatlhakwane (Botswana and Vasco da Gama), Kalu Uche (Nigeria and Enyimba), Oussama Darragi (Tunisia and EST), Samir Aboud (Libya and Al-Etihad), Youssef Msakni (Tunisia and EST), Zouhir Dhaouadi (Tunisia and Club Africain).

Original_Search_Title: Kayanja Testifies Against Fellow Pastors
Headline: Uganda: Kayanja Testifies Against Fellow Pastors
Body_Text:
Kampala — The Leader of Rubaga Miracle Centre Cathedral, Pastor Robert Kayanja, yesterday testified against his fellow pastors and two believers, who allegedly conspired to tarnish his 30-year-old ministry by implicating him in sodomy activities.
Pr. Kayanja, accompanied by his wife and believers, told Buganda Road Magistrate's Court presided over by Mr Patrick Wekesa, that the pastors implicated him in an act which has made many believers abandon his church.
"I don't sell bananas or cows. I deal with human beings; a man's name is all that he has. If someone deliberately desires to destroy it, then he has destroyed everything it stands for," Pr. Kayanja told the fully-packed courtroom.
Pastors Moses Solomon Male of Arising for Christ, Martin Sempa, Michael David Kyazze, and Bob Robert Kayiira of Omega Healing Ministries are facing charges of conspiracy to tarnish Pr. Kayanja's name by alleging that he sodomised his believers.
The accused, who are jointly charged with Dorothy Kyomuhendo and artiste David Mukalazi, denied the charges. Trial Magistrate, Wekesa extended the matter to November 14 for further cross-examination of the witness.

Original_Search_Title: GLO/CAF African Footballer of the Year Nominees
Headline: Africa: GLO/CAF Footballer of the Year - CAF Snubs Nigerian Players Again
Body_Text:
For the second consecutive year running no Nigerian player has been found worthy to make the 10-man shortlist for the 2011 CAF African Footballer of the Year Award.
The list compiled by the Technical, Football and Media Committees of the Confederation of African Football (CAF) takes into consideration the performance of players for both country and club between January and November of the award year.
Interestingly, the list released Wednesday by CAF had three Ghanaian players competing for the most cherished football honour in the continent.
Black Stars' Asamoah Gyan leads the trio that includes France-based Andre Ayew and Kevin Prince Boateng of Italian AC Milan.
Elephants of Cote d' Ivoire similarly have three players in the list with the trio of Didier Drogba (Chelsea), Yaya Toure (Man City) and Gervinho (Arsenal).
They will face a tough challenge from four-time winner, Samuel Eto'o Fils, of Cameroon while Moroccan Adel Tarrabt, Moussa Sow of Senegal and Mali's Seydou Keita complete the shortlist expected to be pruned down to just three before the winner will emerge next month.
The only Nigerian nominated in the award for players based in the African continent is Kalu Uche of Enyimba. He has nine other players to battle for the award.
Speaking at the announcement of the shortlists Wednesday, the President of the Ghana Football Association and executive committee member of CAF, Mr Kwesi Nyantekyi stated that this was the first time that ten nominees were going to be selected instead of the usual five.
"For the first time, there are 10 nominees for the African Player of the Year (Based in Africa), contrary to the five for last year," stressed the GFA boss.
According to him, the choice of 10 nominees for players based in Africa was in response to the rising trend of the performance of local-based players in competitions such as the African Nations Championship (CHAN), Orange CAF Champions League, Orange CAF Confederation Cup, the qualifying series for the 2012 Africa Cup of Nations amongst others.
He also commended Globacom for the company's support in the successful organization of the CAF Awards, adding that the awards has witnessed many pluses since 2005, making it one of the biggest events of the African football calendar.
He stated that the Awards Gala will be held on Thursday, December 22 in Accra; adding that the Ghanaian capital had earlier hosted the annual event in 2006 and 2009.
THE NOMINEES
African Player of the Year:
Andrew Ayew of Marseille, Samuel Eto'o of Anzi Makhatchakala (Russia), Didier Drogba of Chelsea, Asamoah Gyan of Al In (UAE), Yaya Toure of Manchester City, Kevin - Prince Boateng of AC Milan, Seydou Keita of Barcelona, Yao Koussi Gervinho of Arsenal, Moussa Sow of Lille and Adel Taarabt of Queen Park Rangers.
African Player of the Year (Based in Africa):
Ayoub El Khahiqi of WAC (Morocco), Banana Yaya of Esperance of Tunis (Tunisia), Edward Sadomba of El Hilal (Sudan) Idrissa Laouali of ASFA (Burkina Faso), Jerome Ramatlhakwane of Vasco da Gama (South Africa) Kalu Uche of Enyimba (Nigeria), Oussama Darragi of Esperance (Tunisia), Samir Aboud of Al Ittihad (Libya), Youssef Msakni of Esperance (Tunisia) and Zouheir Dhaouadi of Club Africain (Tunisia)

Original_Search_Title: Two Cameroonians in Fac Champions League Final
Headline: Africa: Two Cameroonians in Fac Champions League Final
Body_Text:
They both play for Esperance of Tunis.
Though no Cameroonian club qualified for the final of the prestigious African champions League, Cameroon will be represented by two players; Yaya Banana and Guy Roger Toindouba. Both footballers, play for the Tunisian club side, Esperance of Tunis which is engaged in the final of the Orange African champions league against Al Wydad of Morocco. Yaya Banana was called up to the national team for the very first time by Dennis Lavagne to take part in the LG tournament in Morocco and a friendly match against Algeria. Toindouba on the contrary has flirted with the other categories of national teams and the military football squad.
Meanwhile the Tunisian club side Esperance has resumed intense training in preparation for the crucial encounter against Morocco's Al Wydad Casablanca in the first leg of Orange CAF Champions League final scheduled for November 6th in Casablanca. Almost all the Esperance players except for midfielder Magdy Traoui, who is suffering from an injury, are in training. Coach Nabil Maaloul has most of his squad available for selection but midfielder Khaled Mouilhy is ruled out.
Esperance lost 2-1 to the newly promoted Bani Khelad in a friendly match on Saturday. Esperance took the lead through an own goal from Abdul Salam Bouhosh before the visitors scored twice through Naim Berrabt. Esperance of Tunisia qualified for a second consecutive African Champions League final by defeating Sudanese visitors Al-Hilal 2-0 Saturday.
The predictable victory behind closed doors at the national stadium in a Tunis suburb completed a 3-0 aggregate success for the 'Blood and Gold', who won the competition in 1994 and have been losing finalists three times since.
Deprived of crowd support after trouble in a group match last month, Esperance took the lead seven minutes into the second half through Youssef Msakni and Wajdi Bouazza added a second in the final minute. Msakni grabbed the lone goal of the first leg in Omdurman two weeks earlier and has so far struck five goals in the competition this season; two less than leading scorer Edward Sadomba from Hilal. Defeat was particularly bitter for the Sudanese side, who axed Serb coach Milutin Sredojevic after the first-leg loss, as they were failing at the penultimate stage for the third time within five years.

Original_Search_Title: Kayanja Accuses Pastors of Maligning Name in Sodomy Case
Headline: Uganda: Kayanja Accuses Pastors of Maligning Name in Sodomy Case
Body_Text:
The Leader of Rubaga Miracle Center Cathedral, Pastor Robert Kayanja this morning took to the witness stand to testify against four fellow pastors who together with two believers allegedly conspired to malign him.
Pr. Kayanja accompanied by his wife and believers told the Magistrates Court at Buganda Road before Mr Patrick Wekesa that fellow pastors implicated him to an act which has led to many believers abandoning his church.
"...I don't sell bananas or cows. I deal with human beings; a man's name is all that he has. If someone deliberately desires to destroy it, then he has destroyed everything it stands for," testified Pr. Kayanja in a fully packed courtroom tightly guarded by the police.
The pastors; Moses Solomon Male of Arising for Christ, Martin Sempa, Michael David Kyazze, and Bob Robert Kayiira of Omega Healing Ministries are facing charges of alleged conspiracy to tarnish Pr Kayanja's name that he sodomised believers.
They are jointly charged with believers, Ms Dorothy Kyomuhendo and artiste David Mukalazi.
Prosecution led by Mr Stephen Asaba, alleges that between 2008 and 2009 at various places within Kampala District, the four pastors; Male, Sempa, Kyazze and Kayiira conspired with Kyomuhendo, a State House aide and a local gospel artiste Mukalazi to injure Pr. Kayanja's reputation.
Pr Kayanja's testimony continues in court this afternoon with cross examination by defence lawyers.
ekasozi@ug.nationmedia.com

Original_Search_Title: Tuju Set for Kisumu Return After Stoning Incident
Headline: Kenya: Tuju Set for Kisumu Return After Stoning Incident
Body_Text:
Presidential aspirant Raphael Tuju his expected to tour Kisumu on the weekend, days after his entourage was stoned in the lakeside city.
Mr Tuju said Wednesday he squarely blames Prime Minister Raila Odinga for the incident as the people involved sung "Agwambo and ODM slogans."
Mr Odinga is referred as Agwambo by his staunch supporters. Agwambo means something out of the ordinary in Dholuo.
The former Rarieda MP who is one of President Kibaki's advisors said since Mr Odinga is the ODM party leader "the buck stops with him."
"ODM is a party with a large following. They are partners in the current coalition government. The Prime Minister's party instead of helping with investigations following an incident of violence is busy sinning propaganda," Mr Tuju told journalists at his campaign offices in Lavington, Nairobi.
Mr Odinga has, however, since condemned the violence and advocated political tolerance.
On Tuesday, ODM secretary general Anyang Nyong'o and Kisumu Town East MP Shakeel Shabbir where the stoning of Mr Tuju's team occurred said the event was stage managed to create a wrong picture of youth from Kondele area and heighten political temperatures in the country.
The two said after an ODM parliamentary group meeting that the party's investigations showed none of the people caught on camera throwing stones came from the area.
But Mr Tuju said: "I like to tell the media and this country that I was not surprised (by ODM statement). It is part of communist and Hitler style propaganda to release such outrageous statements that people are so shocked they cannot even respond."
"Throwing stones is primitive behaviour. Propaganda to defend stone throwing is even more primitive. Kenyans can live with lies and propaganda associated with opinion polls. But propaganda should not be used to abet violence in the political process," Mr Tuju said.
Mr Tuju said Mr Odinga should have sought factual information from relevant state agencies on stone throwing before ODM released the statement terming it propaganda.
"They should not announce on one day that they condemn violence and following day make statements that wink at the youth who perpetrated the violence," Mr Tuju said.
Denying that the disruption of his tour was stage managed, Mr Tuju said: "I have no record of stone throwing in my constituency."
He regretted that unemployed youths were being misused to cause violence after being paid peanuts.
Mr Tuju said he will return to Kisumu this weekend and also attend a church service "as no one can chase me from my home area."
The National Cohesion and Integration Commission, some politicians and religious leaders have condemned the incident and called on Kenyans to learn to accommodate divergent views.
Last Friday, the youth who stoned Mr Tuju's entourage said he had not apologised over the violence that erupted in Kisumu in 2005 when he led the banana team that was campaigning for the proposed constitution to the town. A number of people died as a result of the mayhem.

Original_Search_Title: CAF Unveils Nominees for 2011 Player of the Year and Player of the Year (based Local)
Headline: Africa: CAF Unveils Nominees for 2011 Player of the Year and Player of the Year (based Local)
Body_Text:
The Confederation of African Football(CAF) on Wednesday in Accra unveiled the nominees for the 2011 Player of the Year and the Player of the Year (Based in Africa).
The nominees were revealed by Kwesi Nyantakyi, CAF Executive Committee member and president of the Ghana Football Association.
Ten nominees each were named for the two categories based on the performance and contribution of the players to their clubs and national teams during the year under review. For the first time, there are 10 nominees for the African Player of the Year (Based in Africa), contrary to the five for last year.
The lists was compiled through a vigorous exercise by the Technical, Football and Media Committees of CAF, taking into account the performance of the player for his national team and club from January to November, 2011.
The nominees are (in alphabetical order):
2011 African Player of the Year (Based in Africa)
Ayoub Elkhiqi (Morocco and WAC)
Yaya Banana (Cameroon and EST)
Edward Sadomba (Zimbabwe and Al Hilal)
Idrissa Laouali (Niger and ASFA)
Jerome Ramatlhakwane (Botswana and Vasco da Gama)
Kalu Uche (Nigeria and Enyimba)
Oussama Darragi (Tunisia and EST)
Samir Aboud (Libya and Al-Etihad)
Youssef Msakni (Tunisia and EST)
Zouhir Dhaouadi (Tunisia and Club Africain)
2011African Player of the Year
Adel Taarabt (Morocco and Queens Park Rangers)
Andre Ayew (Ghana and Olympique de Marseille)
Asamoah Gyan (Ghana and Al-Ain)
Didier Drogba (Cote d' Ivoire and Chelsea)
Kevin-Prince Boateng (Ghana and AC Milan)
Moussa Sow (Senegal and Lille)
Samuel Eto'o (Cameroon and Anzhi)
Seydou Keita (Mali and Barcelona)
Yao Kouassi Gervais "Gervinho" (Cote d'Ivoire and Arsenal)
Yaya Toure (Cote d'Ivoire and Manchester City)
The winner will be decided by votes from the Head Coaches or the Technical Directors of the National Associations affiliated to CAF.
The Awards Gala will be held on on Thursday, December 22 in Accra, Ghana. It will be the third time the Ghanaian capital will be hosting the annual event after 2006 and 2009.

Original_Search_Title: Continent Player of the Year 2011 - Based Local
Headline: Africa: Continent Player of the Year 2011 - Based Local
Body_Text:
Herewith the profiles of the nominees for the 2011 African Player of the Year (Based in Africa);
1.Ayoub El Khahiqi
Date of birth: 11th September 1986
Club: WAC (Morocco)
The right winger has been part of WAC's successful campaign in this year's Champions League where they have reached the finals. He won the 2010 CAF Confederation Cup and Trone Cups with his previous club, FUS Rabat before joining WAC.
2. Banana Yaya
Date of birth: 12th July 1991
Club: Esperance of Tunis (Tunisia)
The tall and strong Cameroonian defender has been playing with Esperance since 2009. He won the Tunisian Cup and the Championship title and qualified for the final of the Champions League. He has been an influential national team player with Cameroon's U-20 team which reached the finals of the African Youth Championship in South Africa and reached the quarter-finals of the FIFA U-20 World Cup in Colombia this year (2011).
3.Edward Sadomba
Date of birth: 31st August 1983
Club: El Hilal (Sudan)
The Zimbabwean international extended his prominence this season with his goals leading Al Hilal to the semi-final of the Orange CAF Champions League. Sadomba scored seven of El Hilal's 12 goals in the continental club competition. He however failed to assist his home country Zimbabwe qualify for 2012 CAN.
4.Idrissa Laouali
Date of birth: 9th November 1979
Club: ASFA (Burkina Faso)
The midfielder had a good run with his Burkina Faso club, ASFA and was part of the Niger national team (Mena) which made it to the quarter finals of CHAN 2011 in Sudan. Niger made its first ever finals of a continental senior tournament at CHAN and also qualified for AFCON 2012 finals for the first time. His experience makes him one of the key figures of the national team of Niger.
5.Jerome Ramatlhakwane
Date of birth: 29th October 1985
Club: Vasco da Gama (South Africa)
The Botswana international stunned everyone as his national team became the first team to qualify for the 2012 AFCON in a group that was difficult to pick out a favourite. He finished second top goals scorer in the AFCON 2012 qualifiers with 5 goals. He scored both goals in Botswana's double 1-0 victories over Tunisia. Like most southern African players, he plays professional football in South Africa.
6.Kalu Uche
Date of birth: 16th May 1990
Club: Enyimba (Nigeria)
Uche emerged as Enyimba's top goal scorer in the Champions League campaign with 4 goals. He led the team to the semi-final of the competition before bowing out to by WAC of Casablanca.
7.Oussama Darragi
Date of birth: 3rd April 1987
Club: Esperance (Tunisia)
He has had a successful year winning a number of silverware. He won the Tunisian championship and the Tunisian Cup with Esperance. In February he won the second edition of CHAN in Sudan and has reached Champions League final with Esperance. The 24-year-old midfielder has become one of the pillars of the national team which has qualified for the 2012 CAN.
8.Samir Aboud
Date of birth: 29th September 1972
Club: Al Ittihad (Libya)
The Al Ittihad keeper, who just celebrated his 39th birthday, is the pillar of the defence of the national team which qualified for the 2012 CAN. He conceded just a goal in the qualifiers. He will be participating in the CAN for the second time after playing with Libya in AFCON 2006 held in Egypt.
9.Youssef Msakni
Date of birth: 28th October 1990
Club: Esperance (Tunisia)
Msakni won CHAN with Tunisia in February this year in Sudan. He also won the Cup and the national championship with Esperance. He has made it a second consecutive time in the cafe Champions League final after Esperance qualified for 2011 two leg final. The young striker is part of the Carthage Eagles which has qualified for 2012 AFCON finals.
10. Zouheir Dhaouadi
Date of birth: 1st January 1988
Club: Club Africain (Tunisia)
He won the CHAN in February and was voted the best player and top scorer. The offensive midfielder also showcased his skills in the Orange CAF Confederation Cup as his club, Club Africain made its way to the final.

Original_Search_Title: Esperance Hoping to Be Second Time Lucky
Headline: Africa: Esperance Hoping to Be Second Time Lucky
Body_Text:
Tunisian giants, Esperance are hoping to be second time lucky as they host Wydad Athletic Club of Morocco on Saturday in the second leg final of the 2011 Orange CAF Champions League.
The Stade 7 November in Rades near Tunis will be the focus of the whole continent and the rest of the world as they journey to the 2011 edition of the premier continental club championship reaches its final lap.
After a barren draw in the first leg in Casablanca last week, both teams have equal chances of annexing the most sought after title in African club football, which has eluded them for well over a decade. An electrifying atmosphere involving the two North African sides is expected to draw the curtains on the competition which commenced way back in January. Despite the fact that the three previous meetings between the two sides this season has failed to produce a winner, that jinx will be broken this time.
Making their second successive appearance at the final, the Tunisians, winners in 1994 appear poised to rewrite the history books after a nightmarish 6-1 aggregate loss to TP Mazembe of DR Congo last year. However, the 'Blood and Gold' are still haunted by the successive final losses in 1999 and 2000 to Raja Casablanca (Morocco) and Hearts of Oak (Ghana) respectively, but coach Nabil Maaloul and his charges are better placed to erase their trophy-shy record.
On their last three occasions in the final, Esperance has failed to make the most at their backyard, despite the huge task of a five-goal deficit last year. In 1999, they lost on penalties to Raja Casablanca before suffering a 1-2 loss to Hearts of Oak the following year, which played a significant role in their inability to land the title on both occasions.
This season, the likes of Oussama Darragi, Youssef Msakni, Mejdi Traoui, Khaled Korbi, Ghanaian-import Harrison Afful, Cameroon duo Yaya Banana and Yannick N'Djeng and Malian Idrissa Coulibaly have displayed an output which puts Esperance ahead of their opponents in the return encounter.
The Tunisians consider the game so crucial to the extent that national team coach Sami Trabelsi has left out Esperance players for an international friendly against Algeria on Saturday in Blida as part of preparations for the 2012 Africa Cup of Nations.
"Wydad are a very good side. They are very physical and mentally strong. We know much about them and with determination, we can beat them," Afful said ahead of the second leg.
The case is rather the opposite for Wydad, who have been dealt a major blow with doubts over the availability of first choice goalie Nadir Lamyaghri for the return encounter. The 35-year old captain and his club's Player of the month (October) sustained an injury at training on Tuesday and has been ruled out of action for at least four weeks.
In his absence, Yassine Bounou is likely to fill the 'big shoes' of Lamyaghri, who has been a pillar in the 'Red and Whites' journey till this stage. The sad development may compel Swiss coach Michel Decastel, who is aiming to follow the footprints of Faouzi Benzarti, as one of few coaches to have won African titles with different clubs to revive his notes for the game. The Swiss guided CS Sfaxien of Tunisia to the CAF Confederation Cup title in 2007
The winner will pocket $1.5 million and a ticket to represent the continent at the FIFA Club World Cup scheduled for 8-18 December in Japan whilst runner-up goes home with $1 million.
Fixture
Esperance (Tunisia) vs. WAC (Moroco) (0-0)

Original_Search_Title: Printer Earns Tidy Profit From 'Green' Paperbags
Headline: Kenya: Printer Earns Tidy Profit From 'Green' Paperbags
Body_Text:
Over the last decade or so, the world has woken up to the reality that there is need to conserve the environment even as companies seek to make a profit by exploiting natural resources.
Kenyans have not been left behind and scores of companies -- some big, some small -- have been playing their part in finding "green" answers to consumer needs.
One of them is Ann Wambugu's small printing firm, Tech Word Communications, who has been playing her part in environmental conservation by making bio-degradeable bags from paper, thus reducing the use of plastics while still making a profit from the venture.
While there are companies involved in recycling plastic, Tech World ensures that none gets to the streets in the first place by manufacturing "green" paper bags for a variety of corporate, government and other clients.
"I am passionate about shielding our society from a future environmental catastrophe," said Mrs Wambugu from her Accra Road offices, Nairobi, where "green" khaki bags of different sizes hang on display.
Computer specialist
Gift and shopping bags are the most popular with her clients. Big institutions such as Equity Bank, Sarova Group of Hotels, Longhorn Publishers, Nairobi Water Company and the Kenya Nut Company are some of Mrs Wambugu's customers. But like most businesses, her's had a humble beginning.
A computer specialist, Mrs Wambugu cut her teeth at a printing company in Nairobi, Colourprint, where she worked in the accounts department from 1997. Her experience at the firm was eye-opening. After only a year in employment, she registered her Tech World firm.
"I am the type that likes to be responsible for my own efforts. Working at Colourprint was fun, but I needed to see the direct rewards of my efforts - something that is almost impossible to achieve in an employment."
Ms Wambugu informed her father of the plan to set up a firm and abandon formal employment. He could hear nothing of it. "My father was concerned that his huge investment in my education all the way to university was going to waste." As it turned out, nobody was going to stop the determined young woman from realising her dream. In 2001, Mrs Wambugu tendered her resignation and with only a month's salary as capital, she set out on the daunting path of entrepreneurship. She started off by seeking petty printing jobs. Without enough money to acquire an office, Mrs Wambugu operated from a friend's kiosk on Dubois Road, Nairobi. When her income became steady, Mrs Wambugu decided to move into a proper office.
"A friend was carrying out her business from a tiny office on River Road and she was looking for someone to share rent with," she said.
Mrs Wambugu's share of the rent was Sh500 per month, quite a challenging task then. Her big break come at a time when the going was so tough that she was considering returning to formal employment.
"A lawyer asked for a big order of letterheads and business cards. Being my first big business, I set myself the challenge of delivering an extraordinary product."
That she did, and the lawyer was so impressed that he started directing traffic to Mrs Wambugu's fledgeling business.
She opened her own office from the proceeds. At some point, she was almost ejected out of her office as the person she had presumed to be the landlord disappeared and the real owners surfaced with a four- month rent arrears demand.
She survived the ordeal and in 2006 stumbled on her big idea. "I had visited a children's home in Kawangware where a white missionary was training them the art of printing paper bags.
An exciting idea then grew in me and in 2007 Tech World Communications ventured into the business big time." It was what the market was looking for and the government's ban on plastics boosted her cause.
The fast-growing business generates profits of about Sh6 million per month. As a result of rapid growth, this month the company will leave its offices on Accra Road for a more upmarket address in Ngara.
Struggling business
"I wish my father was here to see me now," she said with sadness written all over her face. He died in 2005 when the business was struggling. He had loaned her Sh40,000 to finance her first business computer.
"When he realised that my strengths are in enterprise, he wholeheartedly supported me." Mrs Wambugu is proud to be finally able to make a difference in people's lives.
"It is what I have always wanted to do -- to see my business helping the community to prosper." The handles of her bags are made from knitted banana fibre which is supplied by families in Murang'a.
A kilo fetches Sh30 and some families can supply up to 100 kilos per month. The business, nonetheless, has its challenges. The intermittent operational nature of paper maker PanPaper Mills has made her turn to more expensive imports.
"We source our paper from Mutindi factory in Tanzania, a company struggling with efficiency and operational challenges," she said. For this reason, the bags have become slightly more expensive.
A bag costs between Sh30 and Sh95. Mr Cedrick Mbiyu of the Sarova Group said the bags fit into their corporate philosophy. "As a business that directly feeds on a proper ecological environment, eco-friendly bags rhyme with our identity. Other than that, we love the way Mrs Wambugu runs her business in a quality intensive and customer-centred manner," he said.
hchege@ke.nationmedia.com

Original_Search_Title: Marrakech lg Cup - Lions in Camp
Headline: Cameroon/Morocco: Marrakech lg Cup - Lions in Camp
Body_Text:
They are due to face Sudan, Morocco in the LG tournament and Algeria in a friendly encounter.
The Indomitable Lions are currently undergoing training in Marrakech ahead of the LG tournament due to begin on November 11. The Lions are scheduled to play against Sudan and host Morocco before moving ahead to challenge Algeria in a friendly encounter on November 15.
Though Dennis Lavagne arrived in Morocco on Sunday, he stayed behind in Casablanca while the rest of the delegation proceeded to Marrakech to witness the away leg of the finals of the African champions' league so as to judge the performance of two Cameroonian players, Yaya Banana and Joseph Yannick Ndjeng. The first players to honour Lavagne's maiden call are, Effala Konguep and Mathew Adongcho. Meanwhile Chedjou sustained an injury and will not be taking part in the tournament but will however travel to Morocco to be examined by the medical staff of the Indomitable Lions before flying back to Europe for treatment.
The Cameroonian delegation to Morocco is headed by the director of high level sports, Blaise Omgbwa and is made up of Ndtoungou Mpile, Pierre Mbarga, Boubakary Sidiki the team doctor, Daniel Tecky, kit manager, Boubakary and the team press officer, Linus Pascal Fouda. However, there is the conspicuous absence of the security officer of the Lions, Bienvenu Mbida, who is expected to join the rest of the team tomorrow. In a related development, the sports equipment company, Puma, has released a new jersey for the Indomitable Lions for the next two years. The captain of the Indomitable, Samuel Eto'o Fils, who scored two goals with Anzhi last weekend, took part in the ceremony in London from where he will join the rest of the squad for the LG Cup Africa tournament.

Original_Search_Title: At Home On the Road
Headline: Kenya: At Home On the Road
Body_Text:
When asked whether automobiles should be banned from the town centre of Amsterdam, a Dutch politician responded:
"The chances of Amsterdam becoming a car-free city are about the same as the chances of me getting involved in a relationship.
"If it ever comes to that, I'll calculate what it is going to cost me and I will not go through with it after all!"
Now, I don't know what traffic tribulations led to this exchange, but I do know that Nairobi traffic is very much like my relationships: at a complete standstill and nobody seems to know what to do!
Actually, I suspect that the problem with traffic is rooted in the life experiences of the drivers. For example, the deadlocks at our roundabouts mirror the deadlocks that have seized our households.
Nearly every chore, from cooking to dishwashing and child rearing, is the subject of a heated debate followed by a tense standoff. Is it any wonder that people are not willing to give way on the roads?
Hidden intentions
A modern householder has to be very daring and clever to navigate their way through the relational, financial, operational and technical challenges that are part of today's house and family.
Indeed, we should naturally expect the ducking and weaving that result from this life-obstacle course to spill over onto our road system.
Some people change lanes so often that it is impossible to guess where they might be headed.
These weavers need to hide their intentions from other hostile road / home users who will swiftly react to any information by impeding progress.
The weavers are a mild irritation because they are travelling in the same direction you are. Of more concern are overlappers who disrupt traffic going in the opposite direction.
Overlappers are 'bachelor drivers' - a description of attitude more than marital status. A BD is determined to get where she is going regardless of the needs or feelings of anyone else.
Such a person is more suited to the bachelor lifestyle of the car racetrack than the family lifestyle of the open highway.
Elongated car park
Dangerous overtaking is another behaviour that is likely to stem from the family. When one has been stuck behind a slow heavy vehicle for a long period of time, she is likely to attempt a do-or-die overtaking manoeuvre.
This usually happens on a narrow, hilly, two-way road. At home, this happens around the time of retirement when the blocked spouse takes a sharp turn to America from whence she sends money transfers!
The most irritating road- (and home-) user is of course the obstructionist. This is a person who does not think that the road is a road at all, but rather an elongated car park.
At home, the obstructionist is usually stretched out on the only sofa in the living room watching the only channel that he is interested in on the only TV.
On the road, the obstructionist stops the car wherever he pleases - usually in a lane of moving cars - and strolls off to do some shopping, usually for bananas!
If you are evaluating a potential spouse this Saturday, analyse their driving!

Original_Search_Title: The Task Before the Big Boss - Keshi, NFF And Siasia
Headline: Nigeria: The Task Before the Big Boss - Keshi, NFF And Siasia
Body_Text:
As usual, another change of guard in the coaching personnel of the Super Eagles has just taken place. One has lost count of the men who have been in charge at the senior national team since Dutchman; Clemens Westerhof made his mark with Nigeria.
Apart from the fact that the new coach is the seventh ex-international to handle the Eagles since 1949 when we joined the world football family, Keshi is the fourth in the last six years to be so appointed.
There is none with any credible result to show for his stint.
From Dan Anyiam of the UK Tourists era to Patrick Ekeji the story has remained largely unchanged. Paul Hamilton, Christian Chukwu, Austin Eguavoen and Samson Siasia all walked this familiar route in the same manner as their predecessors.
Yet, there is so much excitement in some quarters that another ex international, 49- year old, Illah-born Stephen Okechukwu Keshi, has come to salvage what is left of Nigeria's prestige after 'ordinary' Guinea snatched the 2012 Africa Cup of Nations ticket from us.
The Big Boss as Keshi was known in his playing days with the Eagles, is savouring every moment, mouthing his preparedness to do the job to his ability. Nobody should begrudge the Big Boss for that. It is his era and should be allowed to enjoy every minute of it.
His coming may not have been widely heralded as that of Siasia because of the current state of the game in the country. He nonetheless deserves respect for his previous duties for Nigeria as both captain of the senior national team as well as an assistant to Amodu Shaibu.
But very soon, Keshi will realise that the job he has walked into is almost the toughest anyone can find in this country.
The import of what i am saying here may not be strange to him. He is a good friend of Siasia and both of them may have compared notes on the job in the past. Already, the same banana peel that Siasia stepped on to crash with a thud has again been placed at the very route Keshi is expected to take to the promised land of football.
The same manner in which Siasia got carried away without examining the contract before signing it is what Keshi is also toeing. What on earth allowed Siasia sign the dotted lines without arguing with the men at the Glass House in Abuja over the manner in which he can be eased out of the job remains baffling.
Without holding brief for the Bayelsa-born coach, every ball fan in the land knew the task ahead of Siasia. It was going to be a total overhauling of the Eagles, a task that was going to stretch beyond the 2012 Nations Cup.
However, with the conviction that he has done it in the past with the junior team, Siasia accepted the challenge with that disadvantage starring him in the face.
He didn't remember that both ex internationals like Hamilton and Tunde Disu who had similarly won bronze and silver at the Under-20 failed with the senior team.
But by the time he started the qualifiers with a three points deficit carried over from the brief Eguavoen stint, he soon realised that it will take a miracle to stop Guinea from not finishing ahead of Nigeria. Ball crazy Nigerians were not ready to settle for less because Siasia committed himself.
As the saying goes, the rest is history now. Siasia has become another failed ex international at the Eagles job. From the reports of Keshi's appointment, the former Super Eagles captain is expected to qualify Nigeria for the 2013 Nations Cup to be hosted by South Africa. In addition, the Big Boss is also to qualify Nigeria for the 2014 World Cup in Brazil as well as take the Eagles to the quarter final stage of the competition.
The first six months contract during which the Eagles are expected to qualify for the Nations Cup 2013 is to automatically activate the three-year deal. There seems to be some logic here. It is a standard practice in civilised football nations.
But looking at the peculiar Nigerian situation where the head coach is both the manager and scout, it is an arduous task.
No doubt, Keshi is lucky to have inherited a team with semblance of making it big in the near future. He is unlike Siasia who started from near scratch after the bulk of the 2010 World Cup squad had been shown the exit from the Eagles.
Yet, for Keshi to give Nigerians the quality team they truly yearn for, it will take some time to evolve. That may even stretch beyond the 2013 qualifiers. It is against this background that one views the World Cup quarter final target with trepidation.
Even when the Super Eagles were rated the fifth best team in the world prior to the USA '94, we never made it beyond the second round.
Since Keshi is expected to continue the rebuilding of a much credible Eagles, saddling the manager with such target appears not well thought out.
Nobody gave Westerhof such a tall order yet it took the coach five clear years to fashion the team now generally regarded as the golden era of Nigerian football. It is amusing that federal character has also come to play a part in the choice of assistants for Keshi. Although the board of the federation realised the folly of such directive by reversing itself, it no doubt has created a sort of tension for the coach in his search for suitable assistants.
Even if he may not admit it, Keshi is now under the impression of understanding the body language of the wise-men at the Glass House if he is to succeed in this job. There is no point sacrificing merit on the altar of ethnicity.
Who cared about where Otto Gloria and Westerhof originated from?
Even as the Eagles remain a rallying point for Nigerians, what matters most is seeing our boys return to winning ways. The tribes of those in charge of the coaching crew count little.

Original_Search_Title: Police Nab Suspected Ritualist in Calabar
Headline: Nigeria: Police Nab Suspected Ritualist in Calabar
Body_Text:
Calabar — A suspected ritual killer (names withheld) has been arrested by the Police in Calabar, Cross River State, for allegedly kidnapping a 13-year-old girl, who hawks banana, for ritual purposes.
The suspect reportedly kidnapped the teenage girl, Tuesday, and put her in a deep freezer at State Housing Estate, Calabar.
Confirming the incident, Mr. Jeremiah Adim, Zone 6 Public Relations Office, said the suspect was receiving treatment at an undisclosed Police Clinic as a result of the serious beating he received from an angry mob before the police came to his rescue.
He added that the suspect picked up the victim at Mbukpa in Calabar South Local Government Area under the pretext of buying the entire banana. However, on reaching his warehouse at the Marian Market, he allegedly ordered the girl to undress.
He said that it was at that point that the girl shouted for help which attracted the neighbours. They found the girl inside a deep freezer.
He said the girl, whose name was not given, made a statement, adding that investigations were ongoing and that the suspect will face the wrath of the law.
The suspect, a major dealer in foreign rice and tomato, allegedly owns more than eight shops in Marian Market in Calabar Municipality and a number of shops at the Etim Edem Park n Calabar South council.
Following the incident, hoodlums in the area went on rampage, yesterday, looting shops said to belong to the suspect at Marian Market and carting away bags of rice and tomatoes and other valuables before security men where drafted to the market to keep the peace.

Original_Search_Title: Esperance Get Chance to Make Amends
Headline: Tunisia/Morocco: Esperance Get Chance to Make Amends
Body_Text:
Tunisian club Esperance is hoping to shake off the "bridesmaid" tag that has stuck to them in the African Champions League for many years starting this weekend in the Orange CAF Champions League final.
The Tunisians face Wydad Casablanca in a two-leg final with the first at the Mohamed V Stadium in the Moroccan commercial capital on Sunday and the return match in Tunisia six days later.
After defeating defending champions Zamalek of Egypt 3-1 overall in their first final appearance 17 years ago, the 'Blood and Gold' reached three more and lost each one.
Raja Casablanca of Morocco pipped them for the 1999 title after a Tunis penalty shoot-out and the pain was even more severe a year later when they were beaten at home and away by Hearts of Oak from Ghana.
But nothing that had gone before in countless African campaigns since 1971 prepared Esperance for the humiliation of last year when they were crushed 5-0 away to Democratic Republic of Congo outfit Tout Puissant Mazembe.
Adding insult to injury was crowd trouble during the return match which ended 1-1 in a near deserted 60,000-capacity national stadium on the outskirts of the Tunisian capital.
It was only the second time in the 47-year history of the premier Pan-African club competition that a final had been won by a five-goal margin with Algerian outfit JET crushing Congolese rivals Vita three decades ago.
Esperance rang the changes on the field and in the dugout and one year later they are back in the final and most African football pundits are backing the Tunisians for a second title.
The clubs also clashed during the group stage with Wydad coming from two goals behind to draw 2-2 at home before erecting a defensive barricade in Tunis and forcing a goalless stalemate.
Esperance had the less stressful semi-finals passage, achieving a noteworthy 1-0 victory over Al-Hilal of Sudan in sauna-like Omdurman before winning 2-0 behind closed doors at home.
Crowd trouble has plagued several Tunisian clubs in Africa this year and the 'Blood and Gold' were punished for incidents during a 4-0 drubbing of Algerian side Mouloudia Alger in another mini-league encounter.
Esperance boast a 23-6 goal tally from 12 matches en route to the final and lost only once -- a meaningless second round qualifier at ASPAC of Benin who had been thrashed 5-0 in the first leg.
Youssef Msakni, who claimed two of the goals that sank Hilal, tops the scoring charts with five and Cameroonian Yannick Ndjeng and Oussama Darragi have struck four each.
Malian Dramane Traore scored twice and Guy-Roger Toindouba once early in the campaign and another Cameroonian, giant defender Yaya Banana, got the goal in Egypt that forced a 1-1 draw with Al-Ahly and sealed Group B honours.
Coach Nabil Maaloul played for Esperance only to leave early in 1994, the year they conquered Africa by defeating Etoile Filante of Burkina Faso, Stade Malien of Mali, Iwuanyanwu Nationale of Nigeria, Mouloudia Oran of Algeria and Zamalek.
He is a deep thinker and not afraid to make unpopular decisions like leaving midfielder and club and national team captain Darragi on the bench away to record six-time champions Ahly.
"It was a tactical ploy because I knew Ahly would attack at home from the kick off. We had to press their midfield and I kept Oussama out because he is more an attacker than a defender," explained the 49-year-old handler.
"Playing at home second will suit us although in the African Champions League you learn to take nothing for granted," he said ahead of a showdown that will enrich the winners by 1.5 million dollars.
It will also give Esperance or Wydad a place at the FIFA Club World Cup between December 8 and 18 in Japan with Lionel Messi-inspired Barcelona and Brazilians Santos among a seven-strong field.

Original_Search_Title: Conducting Census Is Sensitive - Makama
Headline: Nigeria: Conducting Census Is Sensitive - Makama
Body_Text:
The immediate past Chairman of the National Population Commission (NPC), Mr. Suma'ila Makama, has said that conducting a census requires a lot of courage and sensitisation.
Makama made the observation in Abuja on Monday night at a dinner organised by the UN Population Fund to mark the Day of Seven Billion during which he was honoured with as a parting gift for his work at the NPC.
"The road to census in Nigeria is littered with many banana peels; its manipulation and politicisation must be avoided at all levels."
According to him, the job of conducting the census is supposed to be a simple statistical exercise but this is usually not the case in Nigeria.
"The exercise is full of land mines and has the potential of destabilising the government and the country if enough care is not taken to make it worthy of acceptance nationally and internationally."
Makama blamed the problem on the predetermination of census figures by some people who, he said, would not accept anything that did not meet their expectations.
He said that the concern most people had during his tenure was never an under-count but an over-count of census figures.
The former NPC chairman said that it was through the use of cutting edge technology and transparency that the 2006 National Housing and Population Census was able to stand the test of time.

Original_Search_Title: Airports Modernisation - FAAN Boss Ready to Step On Toes
Headline: Nigeria: Airports Modernisation - FAAN Boss Ready to Step On Toes
Body_Text:
The Managing Director of the Federal Airports Authority of Nigeria, George Uriesi, has expressed determination to modernise the nation's airports by upgrading the facilities to international standard.
Expressing concern over infrastructural decay at the airports, Uriesi said that his administration was ready to do everything possible to address the problem in order to take the industry to the next level.
He said that in doing this, the Authority would be ready to step on toes to be able to achieve desired result.
He spoke with newsmen at the Murtala Mohammed Airport, Lagos, and agreed that the Nigerian airports as they were composed today cannot compete favourably with its counterparts in the world.
He explained that the vision of the current management was to drive a change in all the 22 airports in the country whereby Nigerians would be proud, stressing that the environment of the airports were eyesores.
"I'm not afraid of banana peels. That is why I said what needed to be done would be done. The last thing on my mind is banana peels. There will be a lot of negativity because it's not easy to drive a change. I have to do what I have to do and expect that it would be done. I'm prepared for it, absolutely, let's see how it goes.
"I'm not excited as the Managing Director of FAAN at all. It's a huge responsibility on my shoulder. I want people to arrive at Murtala Mohammed International Airport, Lagos and be amazed at the level of development. We will not turn the Lagos Airport to Cape Town International Airport, South Africa now, but we will do that in the long run."
On the proposed re-modelling of airports in the country, Uriesi said that the tactics had changed, saying that the former Minister of Aviation, Mrs. Fidelia Njeze's mission was to re-construct some of the major airports in the country, but the priority of the present administration was to "do a touch up" on the existing airports in the country.
It would be recalled that Njeze had promised the nation that the government would construct new airports and demolish the existing ones because of their old fashioned, decaying structure with the sum of N90 billion, but the current Minister of Aviation, Mrs. Stella Oduah, said the main concern of the present administration was to re-model 12 airports in the country with N19 billion.
He, however, assured that the funds for the re-modelling of the airports were readily available and that the agency would start the project very soon.
"If you are very fast, it will be 24 months to build a terminal and our airports cannot wait for that. The re-modellling of airports under Njeze has changed. The remodeling we are doing now is a touch-up and not the entire construction of new terminal buildings," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Racism in Football
Headline: Nigeria: Racism in Football
Body_Text:
The phenomenon called racism in football recently reared its ugly head despite sustained high-profile campaigns against it by the world's football governing body (FIFA) as well as the European Football Association (UEFA).
Within two weeks, there were allegations of racial abuse in England and Spain. After the match between Manchester United and Liverpool, United's left-back, Patrice Evra, alleged that Liverpool's Uruguayan striker, Luis Suarez, subjected him to a string of slurs during the match. In Spain, Savilla's Malian striker, Frederic Kanoute, reported a racial abuse on him my Barcelona's Spanish midfielder, Cesc Fabregas in their 0-0 La Liga draw at the Nou Camp. Last weekend, Chelsea's captain John Terry was alleged to have called Queen's Park Rangers' (QPR) defender, Anton Ferdinand, an unprintable racist name during QPR's defeat of Chelsea in the Premier League.
The English Football Association (FA) has launched an investigation into the case involving John Terry, following video footage evidence suggesting that such term was used. If the allegation of racist abuse against the Chelsea captain turns out to be true, there are chances that those involving Suarez and Fabregas were also true. The palpable implication of this condemnable act is that the fight against what seemed outdated and old-fashioned has actually not been won.
From banana throwing on to the pitch, monkey gestures, racist jibe and racist chants from fans, there is no denial about the abuse that "coloured" players continue to suffer on and off the field of play. Through greater societal tolerance, globalisation and campaigns such as the Kick It Out and Show Racism The Red Card, much has been achieved against racism compared to the 1980s and early 90s when it was rampant. However, the involvement of players themselves in the abuse of fellow players is a dangerous setback in the fight against this inhumanity. There are fears in some quarters that racism may not be eradicated in football after all, because it has been institutionalised. For instance, only two of the 92 professional football league managers in England and Whales are black.
Football authorities in Europe have an ingloriously poor record of investigation and punishment measures in cases of racial abuse. In 2007, Joleon Lescott (then of Everton) reported to the FA that his teammate, Joseph Yobo, was racially abused by Newcastle's Emre Belozoglu. That allegation was deemed unproven to the astonishment of teammates who also heard the language used. UEFA could only manage an unconvincing fine of 15,000 pounds against the Croatian Football Federation over racist chanting at England's Emile Heskey in 2008. These examples do not reflect genuine commitment to the fight against racism in football.
The way forward is for FIFA and UEFA to vigorously pursue a technology-based solution to confirming allegations of racism after which severe punishment should be meted out to perpetrators.

Original_Search_Title: Nation's Charcoal Stove in BBC Challenge
Headline: Uganda: Nation's Charcoal Stove in BBC Challenge
Body_Text:
Uganda's Ugastove that saves charcoal consumption by use of less fuel is among 12 initiatives across the globe listed for a BBC world challenge in energy saving innovations.
The charcoal stove initiative endorsed by the National Environment Management Authority (NEMA), reduces both deforestation and greenhouse gas emissions, with a claimed annual saving of one ton of carbon emissions for every stove.
Less need for charcoal also means more money for poor households to spend on other necessities.
Designed by a Ugandan entrepreneur, UgaStoves has a layer of clay insulation that greatly improves fuel efficiency.
The entrepreneur is mass-producing the stoves for domestic and commercial.
The Public Relations Officer NEMA, Ms Naomi Karekaho, said such innovations will come a long way in saving forest cover which keeps diminishing yearly.
"Gas is a good option but it is expensive for most regular charcoal consumers. If one uses less charcoal to cook, less trees are cut for that purpose," Ms Karekaho said.
Ugastoves is already getting international carbon credit finance to sell its stoves at subsidised prices.
Other competitions from Africa include Egypt's paper-making from rice husks, Nile water lilies and banana stalks and Kenya's Vava coffee production which is environmentally friendly.

Original_Search_Title: Litter Bugs - a Case for National Discipline
Headline: Zimbabwe: Litter Bugs - a Case for National Discipline
Body_Text:
Reflections Back in the village in the land of milk, honey and dust or Guruve if you like, morals are a vital cog in oiling the communal engine that determines the cycle of life, defining the route taken by village politics, even.
Village Wikileaks, in a recently released cable, systematically intercepted from the chief's information department have it that as the sun buried itself imperceptibly in yonder western horizon one wintry sunset, Mambongi, the village idiot, famed for craziness and fouling the well from which everyone drinks, and of course, deemed the epitome of what is bad in the village, was at it again.
This time around, Mambongi alighted from a bus right in the land of milk, honey and dust, hobbled behind a bush, squatting to answer to the call of nature. One thing Mambongi forgot to do was look around.
Minutes later, he redressed took off and, driven by the ancient rhythm of life to see what happens behind, he looked back and, Lo and Behold! Mambongi found this other villager taking his excreta into a plastic bag and dashing away with it.
You see, there was an instant high-speed chase, with the villager outsprinting Mambongi until he stopped.
So spirited was the chase that the story send chicken laughing throughout the village. A huffing and puffing Mambongi, couldn't quite make of it in his mind what this villager would do with the excreta and went to report to the chief. Mambongi feared the villager would use juju and damage him somehow.
The trial date was to come and hey, Mambongi couldn't quite convince the chief and his assessors what his real problem was. The villager who picked Mambongi's droppings was ideologically clear, that kind of litter belonged to the toilet. Nowhere else! In the village ideological clarity is important.
"Your Worship, Mambongi did it close to my home boundary fence and I felt insulted. He fouled my vicinity. I decided to take his stool to the toilet. I cannot accept litter close to my house, worse still, this kind of litter!
"My worship what does a man have to do with that which he has deliberately thrown away? Suppose he had not seen me collect his litter, what would happen?
Your Worship, Mambongi had abandoned his litter and anyone or anything had access to it. It is my humble submission Your Worship, that I be praised for helping him put his litter in the right place. If
anything he should pay me. That is my submission your worship," said the villager closing his defence case.
The chief, whose grey-haired assessors are a fountain of wisdom and knowledge ordered Mambongi to deposit a goat with the chief as fine for fouling his land and deposit another goat as a fine to the villager, who laboured to carry his litter to the right place, "without safety clothing."
In the village, a goat is too expensive and that punishment was deterrent, too deterrent and a lesson to Mambongi and those of his kind. The full import of this installment dear reader is that away from the village, Zimbabwe has this problem of litter bugs. Wherever you go and whatever you do, there is lack of national discipline when it comes to disposing litter.
When driving along the highways you see this huge chicken bus spitting banana and orange peels through the windows, you see small trendy and posh cars spiting beer and drink cans through the windows and wonder what country this is. Talk of the juice cards and dear reader, the area just around the TollGate, those slips!
On street pavements, you see smartly dressed man and women, throwing litter all over the place as if there are no litter boxes and bins. Is the problem in our stars or ourselves? This villager, who was recently in the Republic of Korea, can testify that in the 14 days he stayed in the land of crabs, dogs and octopus, he did not see a single person throw away litter. There was literary no litter on the ground. In one of the nights in yonder Gyeongju City, there was this drunk, so sloshed that he staggered his way home.
He smoked but still remembered that he needed to dispose off the stub. In his drunken stupor, he staggered to the pavement until he reached the litter box and threw in his litter. He staggered on, thereafter, relieved that he had done justice to his litter. The drunk intermittently fell and rose, swearing and cursing but still remembered no litter on the ground.
But back home, how many of us even make efforts to do the same. Sober even! Look at our allays and the foul smell that wafts from there and so, what nation are we?
A nation of litter bugs? A nation of Mambongis, fouling the communal habitat? Fouling the centre of our livelihoods! There is need for a shared national vision, shared national discipline and national commitment to ring fence our cleanliness, to ring fence our humanism, to ring fence our desire to excel in creating a community of smart people, people who know where litter belongs and hence people who just cannot throw litter everywhere. Going forward, once we share that vision and discipline one does not need to be monitored to know where you throw litter.
It is a culture that we have allowed to creep into our minds and this is the reason why even our women, whose bodies we so revere in the village no longer care where to drop their pants and relieve themselves, just watch when cross-border buses park by the roadside.
This warped idea, especially in Harare, where the red-dressed women are left to clean up the city and more often than not, you here a litter bug, saying it is their job. "If we don't litter the roads they will be fired, for there will be no job for them." That is too silly for responsible people.
We can not be worse than baboons, who know where to urinate and do it on the same spot every night that a crystal ball of dried urine gathers on the spot. Ask these men who use the urine as an aphrodisiac!
But the point is that we need to be more organised as a country and do the right things when it comes to disposing our litter.
This villager wishes this great land of Munhumutapa enacts a law that gives law enforcers options for very deterrent punishment. Our country is good, it is attractive and let us put together a national strategy to ring fence our beauty. Why should baboons be better organised than ourselves?
In our individual capacity, we should all feel obliged to ensure we have a country free of our reckless disposal of litter, for our acts reflect on our national identity and our national ethos.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni - I Am the Ssabagabe of Uganda
Headline: Uganda: Museveni - I Am the Ssabagabe of Uganda
Body_Text:
Following the burial of Ankole's Prince John Patrick Barigye last Sunday at which President Yoweri Museveni was conspicuously absent, Michael Mubangizi reproduces a slightly edited article containing Museveni's strong views on the Obugabe.
The article, first published by The Monitor on December 23-31, 1993, and written by Ogen Kevin Aliro (RIP) and Dismas Nkunda, is derived from a media conference he addressed at State House Entebbe.
The President made lengthy introductory remarks, before fielding questions from journalists
First of all, some papers, like Cheeye's [Uganda Confidential], have been busy calling me ekirumira habiri. Now, ekirumira habiri is a snake which has got two mouths. I don't know if it is extinct these days, but we used to see it when we were young. One mouth that side, one mouth this side.
This is the [description] Cheeye has been giving me. And the other one, this Wafula Oguttu [The Monitor editor-in-chief]. The two apparently are in cahoots; they are always working together, to discredit the government. They are saying that Museveni is a double talker. In Buganda he is a monarchist, because he wants Baganda votes.
When he goes to Mbarara, he is against the monarchy. This is what Cheeye has been writing, so many times. Then he goes on to say, this is because Museveni is of the Basiita clan and these Basiita once had a drum in Ankole and it seems Museveni wants to get this drum again.
You cannot believe that educated people can do such things. It is incredible. So for me when I see what you write, it's like an entertainment film, not a serious one. Maybe that is why these people [pointing at press secretary, Hope Kivengere] wanted me to talk to you, although I think it is not really important, it's up to you.
Africa's problem
Now, the position of the government is as follows. It is not our business to be monarchists or republicans. Maybe there are some monarchists [in government]. I see some of them. But we want to stabilise the country so that we can transform the economy. The other day when I was in Nairobi, I told those people that the whole of East Africa in 1989 had GDP of $18.6bn.
If you add on Nigeria with $20bn, the GDP comes to [about] $40 billion. But Belgium, which is one tenth of the land area of Uganda, had a GDP of $l79bn! This is the real problem of Africa. Incredible underdevelopment. That is why I fought and, by the way, as long as I am healthy, I will not allow all these monkey tricks.
I will resist them, one way or the other. All those who are not serious just playing here and...the tribes, religions, clans, jumping up and down, eh!
Africa must develop. We will develop. You are the ones who invited me to talk to you. Africa will develop now, we are not going back, finished. As for me, whether I am in the government or not, because I have been in government for only the last eight years, but even when I was out of government I struggled for Africa.
