So, I am giving you notice - all of you who are here that Africa is not going to go back. We are moving. Now, to start embroiling me -he is pro-monarchy, he is anti-monarchy - I am not either of those things, I am a revolutionary. My job is to transform society. That is why I fought. To run from a backward society into a modern society. This is my job. This is my mission.
Now, when it comes to local issues, whether we should have a king here, in Alur ...the other day I received the king of the Alur - I had not heard of him before. They said, there was an Alur king, I said where is he, can I see him? He came here and saw me. Now, if there is another king who comes, it is my job to see all these kings and all the priests, all the sheikhs.
Buganda's case
All I want is peace in Uganda and you cannot have peace without democracy - the people to express what they want. So, we try to assess the feelings of these people and some people say we cannot live unless there is a monarchy. So we say; oh, you cannot live unless there is a monarchy, okay, if it will help you to live, have a monarchy, please have one.
Others say we shall die if there is a monarchy. Ha, you will die if there is a monarchy. Then if that is the case, don't die, please let's not have a monarchy. That is all. There is no other interest of this government. You fail to see ours is a revolutionary job. To transform backwardness into modernity. When you claim you are writing ...just wasting ink.
Why don't you use this ink for lip-stick or for something else - the one you are wasting!
So, when it comes to the issue of Barigye, Prince Barigye -I don't call Barigye Omugabe - oh, sorry. He is not Omugabe as far as I am concerned. I don't recognise him because he did not satisfy my criteria as the chief Mugabe - because I am also the Chief Mugabe of Uganda. I happen to be the Ssabagabe although I am not hereditary, but I am there as the Ssabagabe - the head of all the Bagabes. I was not satisfied that Barigye followed the proper procedure.
When I assessed as President of Uganda that the Baganda were worried - well, a significant number of them, at least those who speak publicly, they seem to want their traditional system back - headed by the Kabaka who is the Ssabataka. And they have been pressuring me since 1982! Even in the bush, they would come and say, Oh! Ssebo tulwana - "We are fighting but will the Kabaka comeback?"
Then I would say, "Look here, this is not the time." For instance in 1982, we had a conference at Kikuyu in Bulemezi, near River Mayanja. I think it was in July. It was a meeting between me and [the late Dr Andrew Lutakome] Kayiira [then leader of the Uganda Freedom Movement]. He was creating problems for us among the Baganda, trying to cause a division between the Movement and the Baganda.
So we said okay, we don't want to attack you - why don't you come and we have a meeting, and we called all the elders at Kikunyu, and we sat and asked Kayiira, tell us your problem. Then he started this business, "We want to know whether the Kabakaship will be back..."
I said no, we are not going to discuss that now because our job is to defeat the Milton Obote regime; not to decide whether there should be a Kabaka or not. The question of Kabaka will be decided by the people. The whole meeting supported me although Kayiira was trying to use Bugandaism and all that.
Kayiira was trying to opportunistically show that he is a Muganda. Of course I am not a Muganda - and I do not want to be a Muganda. I have got my own tribe. But I can help Baganda in their aspirations, if they are legitimate and don't conflict with the wider interests of the African people. When we came in 1986, again the elders came.
They said we want you to restore the Kabakaship. I said you have to wait [as] I am busy pacifying the country. Then they said they want to have Emipiira gy'ebika (clan soccer tournament). I thought about it and said, okay the mupiira is no problem. "And we want to call Prince Mutebi Ssabataka." I said okay, the Sabataka - what does Ssabataka mean? They told me. Okay, that sounds alright if that is what you want. This was 1986 or 1987.
Now, as we are approaching the constitution, the Baganda became very worried and I noticed it. It is my job to notice; that is why I am the General Manager of Uganda.
Special case
They (monarchists) thought that when it comes to Parliament, although the monarchy might be popular in Buganda, the national Parliament might not push the idea through, because Baganda in Uganda are a minority. So, to pacify Uganda - and Buganda is a reasonable part of Uganda - I decided to pull this matter out of the constitution process and deal with it politically so that the Baganda get assured that Uganda will not oppress them.
Baganda should not feel oppressed in Uganda. And I said okay, I will handle it, through the Army Council and they passed a resolution and we went to Parliament. It was not easy because many people did not understand it, but I persuaded them and these monarchists got their Kabaka. We also redefined the Kabakaship because the Kabakaship you are talking about today is not the Kabakaship of the 1890s or 1880s.
Now, we could not pass legislation for Buganda alone. So, in the amendment to the 1967 Constitution, we removed the obstacle because there was Article 118 which said there should never be any monarchy in Uganda. Monarchies may now be restored among those tribes who so wish, the amendment said. But [according] to that amendment, the monarch will not interfere in politics, administration and so on...
Baganda wanted to have a coronation even before the amendment, but I told Prince Mutebi that this was not acceptable; there had to be an amendment. You remember they had to postpone their coronation. That was also caused by some lawyers who had said the coronation could go on without the amendment.
Ankole case
My job is to create consensus. So I called a meeting of the [Nkore] Cultural Trust and then these ministers from the area that had been voicing opposition, because you know that is how I operate. In fact, they said: "oh, that was very good and they even suggested a date - September 4. That on September 4, we shall call a meeting and you can come. So I waited for them but they did not call me.
Of course, I was busy. (Laughter.)
Then I heard that the Omugabe had been crowned on November 20. In fact, I laughed because in Ankole there are no such things done secretly. When I rang somebody, I said: "Omugabe nibamwemeka, omubwesherekye nk'amaarwa gataine pamiti?"
You know when you are going to brew tonto you always go to the chief and get a permit. Now, if you go and brew this banana beer without a permit, they call it "Amaarwa g'obwesherekye." Now, how can Omugabe be treated as clandestinely as brewed beer!
At the Rwakitura meeting, RC V chairmen, councillors and NRC members were all there. The one of Ntungamo (RC V chairman) - somebody named Karazaarwe - said they did not want [Obugabe].
Okay, you say your councils don't accept the coronation, how about the Members of Parliament? I got some to speak in favour, like Elly Karuhanga. Although for him he was not saying that he supported the monarchy. He was saying that according to the law, according to the amendment, Barigye could be Omugabe without any further consultations. That was Karuhanga's position.
Okay, if that is the case, you go to court. You have made yourself Omugabe where the government doesn't accept, other Banyankore don't accept.
However, my advice was that even if legally you are Omugabe, politically it is not good to be Omugabe over people who do not want. I did not accept the idea of relying just on the law - even if the law was on his side. I would not accept that he becomes Omugabe just by virtue of the law - whether people want it or not.
So, that is the position. What I reject most emphatically from some of the papers is the idea of trying to say the government has got double standards. There are no double standards. There is only one standard. The standard of the NRM is to do what the people want - if it is not dangerous to themselves. But some of these things are not harmful, like the cultural thing if it is well handled, no problem.
(After Museveni's marathon lecture, he took questions from journalists)
If Barigye had accepted to be a king of only Bahima, would you have recognised him?
Museveni: No. I would not encourage that because... may I first tell you, for instance, since you are interested in those sub-groups, the RC V chairman of Ntungamo who read the memorandum, [and] another one of Mbarara - are Bahima.
Of all the three RC V chairmen [of Mbarara, Bushenyi and Ntungamo districts that made up Ankole in 1993], the only one who is a Mwiru is of Bushenyi.
Many of the councillors from Nyabushozi, Kazo, which are predominantly Hima areas, voted against. I am a Muhima of no small stature.
I did not vote against but I did not encourage either. So Barigye, why should he be king of Bahima? Why should he divide us? Should a king come to strengthen or weaken?
Besides, even if he was to be the king of Bahima only, let it also be discussed. I wanted to have them (monarchies) back but not force them on those who don't want, that was my political intention because I am first and foremost a political person. I want to do something politically acceptable to the people or liked by the people.
Now, if our intentions were misrepresented by legal draftsmen, if the Barigyes go to court and get a court ruling which says, "You can become a king for two people, even if 48 people do not want you," then we shall be faced with a political problem and we shall see what to do.
Maybe we shall go back to Parliament and see what to do. The political aim of this is to see that the people of Uganda are not oppressed by anybody. But politically speaking, even if the law was saying he can be a king against the wish of the majority of the people, I would say the law be changed.
What about Buganda?
Museveni: Buganda, what the people want, why should they be unsafe? I initiated all this mainly because of Baganda. They are the ones who were putting pressure on me. Now, as long as the Baganda like the institution, there is no problem. But in future if the Baganda do not like it, then how will you force it on them?
Mr President, my argument is that you say you don't recognise the Omugabe, but the law doesn't give you that power. That if the President doesn't recognise the Omugabe, he will not be restored.
Museveni: No, no, my friend, I am an administrator and I administer according to the law. So, if you do something which in my interpretation is wrong, I stop you. Then if you think I have stopped you wrongly, contrary to the law, you go to court.
And if the court declares you out of order?
Museveni: But I must govern the country. I can't sit here and ask the court to govern. The court is not the one which administers. You see, I am the one who is administering and I administer according to the law. So, when I see you trying to do something which in my interpretation is not according to the law, I stop you.
I may be wrong, but you can go to the court and you get the court to declare me wrong. In which case I will withdraw or if I still think what the court has done is not in public interest, then I go back to the legislature to get another law, so that the law expresses better the public will.
Mr President, I am Sheila Kawamara from The New Vision. Already there is division among the Banyankore. Some of them have publicly said they are going to replicate what is happening in Rwanda and Burundi; how prepared are you to handle this?
Museveni: How are they going to replicate it?
Kawamara: They are going to fight amongst themselves, the peasants.
Museveni: That is absolute rubbish. How are they going to do it? We shall shoot whoever starts that. You are forgetting there is a government here. Anybody who does something like that we shall just, you know what we shall do with them. Don't you know?
Tamale Mirundi (Lipoota): But senior army officers are involved.
Museveni: Any senior army officer involved against the orders of the Commander-in-Chief will just go in jail (laughter). It is as simple as that.
Kevin Aliro (The Monitor): But you haven't arrested them.
Museveni: No, arrest who?
Mirundi: The Tinyefuzas.
Museveni: But this was not against an order. Apparently what happened was, these people sent out invitations. Remember originally they had said they were going to commemorate the death [and have] the last funeral rites of the former king. These army officers, I asked them afterwards [and] they didn't seem to know there was a coronation plan.
In fact, the majority of the people who went did not know there was going to be a coronation. So, the real conspirators are few. Even some of the [Nkore] Cultural Trust people didn't know. There was a small group, I think they are called Abakuza- some people who have been acting as regents. So, if these army officers got the invitation and they went, I don't see why I should blame them.
Mr President, how will you enforce your government declaration that Barigye's coronation is 'null and void'?
Museveni: For instance, if he starts touring Ankole that he is Omugabe, I just tell him to go back to his house. That is how I enforce it. Now, what he calls himself is not a problem to me. But actually to go around and carry himself around as if he is Omugabe; that is what I am not going to allow. And I have already instructed the police in writing, they are very well informed, they know what to do.
Why didn't you include the issue of consultation or referendum on the monarchy in the amended article of the 1967 constitution?
Actually we debated the question of a referendum. It was brought up but people thought that a referendum was not necessary and that maybe it could be divisive. People thought that if Omugabe or Kabaka comes, he can be easily recognised, not by mass consultation through a referendum, but by consulting through the representatives of the different groups within that tribe or community.
For instance, in Buganda we never had a referendum but from the memoranda submitted to the Odoki Commission, many, many people, RC Vs, RC IIIs in Buganda said they wanted Kabaka back.
So really, even without a referendum you could see that there was reasonable support for this thing. So a referendum, apart from the expense, mark you there is also money involved...if you are going to organise a referendum for the Kyabazinga, the Alur king, the Omugabe of Ankole, Rutakirwa of Kigezi; the reluctance had to do with the amount of work involved and expenses. We debated it, but we discouraged it.
James Tumusiime (The New Vision): Mr President, Barigye has three hats; he is a prince, he is a Ugandan, he now claims to be a king. As an individual, he would want to go to Bushenyi, to Ntungamo. There are people who have threatened him that if he goes, he will not leave their place alive. I would like you to [clarify on] Barigye the Ugandan, the prince and the individual.
Museveni: The prince can go wherever he wants in Uganda as long as he does not carry himself as a king. But he does not expect people to fall down to worship him, but if they do, voluntarily, that we may ignore. But if he goes to do business in Ishaka, he just goes in a car and does business.
Ogen Kevin Aliro (The Monitor): I see contradictions in your position. On one side you talk of everybody upholding the law, including the President. But at the same time you say that if the courts rule that Barigye is legally Omugabe then you may return to the NRC to review the law...and NRC members say you overwhelm them and get your way always.
Museveni: How? By osmosis or what?
Kevin: Well, just your personality overwhelms them.
Museveni: That means my personality expresses the democratic will of the people.
Kevin: And if the law is again reviewed and amended, I see the problem of a domineering president getting the law changed as and when it fits his own political interests.
Museveni: "His, his!" What is his now in this case?
Kevin: You have said that if the courts so decide, you shall go back to the NRC and get the law reviewed.
Museveni: But why will I go back? Because of my interest, or because I see a problem on the ground?
Kevin: Because you are also an actor in that arena; that is part of the problem. There are those who will say Museveni has his personal problems with Barigye.
Museveni: Personal?
Kevin: Yeah, and many people believe it. That is why I even feel you are wrong if you blame journalists for the crisis in Ankole. Whatever they write sort of reflects what is going on. That is one, and secondly, like this Maj Gen Tinyefuza, really I think you just can't wave off his problem, that he was just invited there. There are video tapes where he is recorded more or less calling for a separate Hima homeland. Why not confront the problem directly?
Museveni: In this case Museveni has no 'his' problem, but Museveni must reflect in the law, what the people on the ground want. NRM will always side with the population if what the population is fighting for is legitimate and not a danger to the interest of the country. You said Tinyefuza said what?
[Someone]: They have video tapes where they try to stir up Hima sentiments
Museveni: What is catastrophic about that?
Kevin: First of all, you will have a problem with Buganda if they say they want to cut off Sembabule add it to Kazo and Nyabushozi to form a separate Himaland. And I think a person like Tinyefuza for ages has been your lieutenant and he should know better than that.
Museveni: But that was his opinion. We govern the army through the High Command and the Army Council. We have never discussed this issue of Obugabe. We only discussed it the other time when in Gulu. But it was only vacating cultural sites. That was all. Now, if Tinyefuza [had gone] and said that he opposed the Gulu resolution, he would be in jail the following day.
You can be sure about that because he would be opposing an army decision. But if he takes a position on something we have never discussed, then I don't see why I should take action against him even if he is wrong.
The third thing that can put Tinyefuza or anybody else in problems is if the Commander-in-Chief says don't do this and you do it; then you are sure that you will be in very serious trouble. It is not correct to say army officers should not have opinions or say what they want to say. Or that whatever they say is engineered by Museveni or Museveni is answerable for it.
Anonymous voice: One of these days you may have a Muhima soldier refusing to salute a Mwiru officer.
Museveni: My friend, we are not part of that crowd. It can't happen. I used to hear about such things [in UNLA] in 1985 and 1986 when we had just come. Not in NRA. Our level of organisation is higher.

Original_Search_Title: Purely and Simply Illogical
Headline: Namibia: Purely and Simply Illogical
Body_Text:
When people are arguing from the existence of a correlation to a conclusion about a casual connection, they are particularly prone to neglect the possibility of alternative explanations.
It's quite tempting to know from my learned colleagues at the National Sports Commission (NSC) as to how they arrived at some of the candidates on the nominees list for the 2011 year-end sports awards.
In logic textbooks, arguments, especially deductive ones, are very neat with the premises clearly distinguished from the conclusion and the conclusion indicated by the word "therefore".
Now tell me how did the panel of experts apply their minds when they compiled a list of nominees or accepted the poorly thought-out list from the seemingly reckless or ignorant sporting disciplines without applying logic.
For starters, the Namibian rugby fifteen is a strong candidate for the prestigious team of the year award, but the team's coach Johan Diergaardt is a notable omission from the list of best coach of the year award. It makes absolute nonsense of logical thinking, so to speak.
Merely asserting something, no matter how loudly doesn't make it true. Confident assertion is no substitute for argument, even though, most of us in our uncritical moments can be easily persuaded by people who seem to know what they are talking about, whether or not they really do. Boxer Japhet Uutoni is a nominee for the umpteenth time following his silver medal at the 2010 Commonwealth Games in India and the panel argues that the flyweight boxer deserves his place among the elites, because the likes of Frank Fredericks previously also received accolades for their exploits on the athletics track during the Commonwealth Games. It's quite interesting to note that in athletics, Frank was competing against the world's best sprinters since there are no restrictions for professional and amateur athletes, while professional boxers are not permitted to compete in the very same event. I'm just saying.
The entire nominee list makes a mockery of logic and is not even worth the piece of paper it is has been written on -­ or how does one explain the situation where a badminton coach is nominated for the coach of the year award in the absence of any nominee from this particular discipline in the individual awards.
It also goes beyond any comprehension as to why the Namibian fistball team is hailed as local heroes for merely participating in the World Cup. Namibia is the only country on the entire African continent involved in competitive fistball because of the vast German presence in this country.
That brings us to the debate that if our national jukskei team wins a provincial tournament in neigbouring South Africa - it should also be considered world beaters and have its name engraved in the golden pages of our archives?
Our self-styled sport gurus have been on a wild goose chase for a considerable amount of time now and unless somebody steps in and applies the brakes - what is genuinely meant to be Namibia's august event will become the laughing stock of the athletes' hard work during the year.
The entire process and criteria need a serious revamp because having fulltime sports administrators and volunteers in one pot is tantamount to comparing bananas to potatoes. I rest my case.

Original_Search_Title: Prosecution Seeks Life Sentence for Former Youth Minister
Headline: Rwanda: Prosecution Seeks Life Sentence for Former Youth Minister
Body_Text:
Arusha — Prosecution concluded on Thursday the presentation of its closing arguments by requesting the International Criminal Tribunal for Rwanda (ICTR) to hand down a maximum sentence of life imprisonment against former Rwandan Youth Minister, Callixte Nzabonimana.
"The prosecution asks life imprisonment for each of the counts charged against Nzabonimana," Senior Trial Attorney Paul Ng'arua pleaded before the Chamber presided by Judge Solomy Balungi Bossa. Nzabonimana is charged with conspiracy to commit genocide, genocide, direct and public incitement to commit genocide, extermination and murders.
According to the Prosecution, Nzabonimana was the main instigator of massacres of Tutsis in his native prefecture of Gitarama (central Rwanda) in 1994.
"Nzabonimana was not a Minister in Norway or Sweden but a Minister in Rwanda. He was seen as a lion in Gitarama prefecture in 1994. He stood up and called upon the population to begin the killings against Tutsis, and his orders were carried out", the Kenyan Attorney charged.
Ng'arua added that the accused "claimed to have a final solution for the threats posed by Tutsis in the churches and elsewhere". He asserted that shortly after this speech, Tutsis were hunted down and killed in schools and churches where they had sought refuge.
According to the lawyer, some of the killers then went to see Nzabonimana claiming: "Sir, with the weapons you gave us, we killed two Tutsis." The defendant allegedly congratulated them, gave them money and banana beer.
At last, Paul Ng'arua rejected the accused defence of alibi according to which he was hiding at the French embassy on April 8 and 9, and therefore could not have been organizing and supervising massacres in Nyabikenke at the same time. "People who sought refuge at the French embassy could get in and out of the premise without any control," he said, asking the Chamber to dismiss the alibi.
The ex-minister was arrested in Tanzania on February 18, 2008, and his trial started on November 9, 2009.

Original_Search_Title: Police Seize 700 Litres of Illicit Brew in Mwea, Murang'a
Headline: Kenya: Police Seize 700 Litres of Illicit Brew in Mwea, Murang'a
Body_Text:
More than 700 litres of illicit brews were destroyed and equipment confiscated by police and provincial administration officers from Murang'a East, Mwea and Kirinyaga West districts at the weekend. The one day operation against the brewing of the illegal brews was conducted along the banks of Sagana river that borders the three districts.
The officers led by Murang'a East district commissioner George Natembeya and his Kirinyaga East counterpart Rhoda Onyancha also seized a boat used to ferry the lethal liquor locally known as Kang'ara from Kirinyaga to the other side of Murang'a. During the operation several drums and plastic containers used in the brewing the drinks were also destroyed.
According to Natembeya the operation was jeopardized by a certain government official in the provincial administration who is alleged to have tipped the brewers who escaped before the arrival of the police. Only one suspected brewer was arrested in Kimathi area in Kirinyaga East district but in the other side of Murang'a no arrests were made as the operators of the illegal trade escaped the dragnet after being tipped off.
The officers were shocked to find filthy brewing dens situated in bushes along the river on Kirinyaga and Mwea areas where several litres of the brew were hidden waiting to be ferried across the river to be sold and consumed in Murang'a. Natembeya accused chiefs from both Kirinyaga and Mwea for laxity in curbing the illicit brews business and for colluding with the brewers.
"Brewing of the illegal brews does not take place in my area of jurisdiction but on the other side of the river (Kirinyaga West and Mwea districts) and chiefs from that area must wake up," he said. The officers combed all the bushes from Kimathi location in Murang'a and also braved the heavy rain all the way to Mwea through Kirinyaga and it was evident that brewers had just escaped minutes before their arrival as Sufurias and boiling bananas were seen at several spots.

Original_Search_Title: Politics Behind National Assembly Committees
Headline: Nigeria: Politics Behind National Assembly Committees
Body_Text:
THE seventh section of the National Assembly started on a dramatic note, as members of different political parties could not agree on who to be the chairmen of the various committees.
Senate President David Mark, who was billed to announce the membership of the committees ahead of the last recess in July, could not do so until September.
Sources in the senate confirmed that the senate helmsman was not only overwhelmed by lobbying by his colleagues, but also stretched thin by lobbyists for ministerial positions.
It was learnt that Mark, at a stage, had to abandon everything that had to do with the work of selection committee to enable him to concentrate on ministerial screening and lobbyists for ministerial post.
The situation in the senate appeared peculiar at this time in view of manner the Senate President emerged.
On record, he happens to have emerged by consensus. All the six geopolitical zones endorsed his candidature as Senate President ahead of the June 6 inauguration of the senate.
Thus, unlike what happened in 2007, when he slugged it out with compatriot, Senator George Akume, Mark has no enemies to fight or think about in the new Senate.
But by human nature, it is difficult to please all, especially in view of dispositions of politicians whose interests are bound to clash.
In sharing 56 committees, it appears practically impossible for Mark to satisfy all; everyone was looking forward to the first slip of the soldier -turned -politician, who managed the last senate with a level of decorum.
Mark himself was careful to tread, trying as possible not to make mistakes.
Sources had confirmed that Mark set aside issues of composition of Senate standing committees after the ministerial screening in July so as to avoid dangerous mistakes, which are usually capitalised on in the emergence of the proverbial 'axis of evil'.
Being a major player in the creation of 'banana peels' that rocked the senate in the past sessions, especially from the fourth and fifth sessions of the assembly, Mark was all the more circumspect this time.
Thus, the Senate had to drag itself through the last days of July to fill the gap of inactivity as a result of the non-composition of committees.
The situation was not helped by the scuffle over the composition of the body of principal officers of the upper chamber.
The Northeast had alleged that it was being marginalised in the scheme of things and it would settle for no other position than the Senate leader.
But that would upturn the applecart as far as Mark's Senate presidency was concerned. The understanding had been there that with Mark emerging as Senate President, Senator Victor Ndoma-Egba, SAN, who served as deputy Senate Leader in the sixth Senate would assume the leadership position and then Senator Ike Ekweremadu would emerge Deputy Senate President.
A late rally for the position of Senate leader by the Northeast was, however, strongly rebuffed by the South-South, which stood by Senator Ndoma-Egba.
The Northeast actually carried out the threat of rejecting the position of Deputy Majority Leader zoned to the area, but reason prevailed and the body of Senate Principal Officers was finally constituted late July.
As senators proceeded on the annual recess at the end of July, all eyes were on Mark. Everyone was set for the composition of the standing committees, even as senators went home with grumbles, following the non- announcement of the committees on the last day of sitting in July.
Upon resumption on September 13, there were rumblings as senators were eager to hear the senate president pronounce their committees.
The much-expected announcement came in the open on September 15, as both the senate and the House of Representatives announced their standing committees.
While Mark announced 56 committees, up from 54 in the last session, the speaker of House of Representatives, Hon. Aminu Tambuwal, announced the composition of 80 standing committees.
In the course of this, Tambuwal had to find a way to please the 252 members of the house whose vote made him speaker on June 6.
Mark only had to find a way to make his 198 bosses (as he usually describes the senators) happy in constituting the committees.
Going by the geopolitical configuration, it emerged that the touchiest zone in both the senate and the House of Representatives is the south- west.
Tambuwal also had a difficult task handling the zone, which he short- changed to emerge speaker.
Indeed, it was a tug of war to see the majority Leader of the House, Hon. Mulikat Adeola -Akande, emerge from the zone.
The PDP also has six members from the South- West. The stage of the current scenario, as far as the standing of the Southwest in the National Assembly is concerned, had been set during the April elections.
It became certain also that the fate of the southwest would largely be decided by the whims of the other five geopolitical zones as far as committees' postings are concerned.
When it turned out that the southwest lost the speakership position to the Northwest in June, it became clearer that even high-wired politics could not redeem a bad situation.
Thus, notwithstanding the camaraderie that was built between Speaker Tambuwal and the leadership of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN) ahead of the House election in June, the reality was sure to play out in the colour of the committees.
While the leaders of ACN were expecting 16 standing committees from Tambuwal , with the proviso that the committees would be delivered to the party leaders who would then pick the right members, it became clear that such could only amount to fantasies.
Tambuwal announced the composition of the standing committees that relegated the ACN to the background and, thus, diminished the perceived kingmaker role the party played in his emergence.
Sources said the Speaker was told that he had to be wary of the antics of the opposition parties in the house and that a combination of the Congress for Progressive Change (CPC) and the CAN was making surreptitious attempts to run the House by relying on the support they gave him in June.
The Speaker had to beware of the secret agenda of the opposition parties to lead him by the nose.
He was told of the need to realise that this is a PDP dominated House and if he allows the opposition to lead him against the dictates of his own party, he would one day discover that the opposition couldn't save him.
It appears that the Speaker heeded the advice in announcing the standing as well as in agreeing to the choice of Hon. Adeola- Akande as majority leader.
In the senate, Mark had a tough battle warding off the influence of former governors who are now senators.
The group of former governors was said to have met Mark to demand that they should all emerge as committee chairmen, having run their states in the recent past.
That added to the pressure from some senators who are regarded as very close to the Senate President who persistently demanded particular committees.
In contention were committees on Appropriation, Communication, Petroleum (Downstream and Upstream) and Works among others.
But to resolve the matter in his own favour, Mark was said to have asked each senator to name five committees on which they would want to serve in order of priority.
Many of them were said to have clustered around the so-called grade 'A' committees, with Appropriation recording the biggest request.
With such a data at his disposal, the job was a bit reduced for Mark. He was said to have ensured that every senator emerge on the list of, at least, three of the committees they originally requested for.
Sources said where a senator is not the chairman of a committee, he would either be the vice chairman or member of the preferred committee.
This was said to have reduced the tense situation that usually trails committee membership.

Original_Search_Title: Imbalu, When Boys Become Men
Headline: Uganda: Imbalu, When Boys Become Men
Body_Text:
Circumcision or 'Imbalu' in Bugisu region is the initiation of young men into adulthood by removing the foreskin of the penis. This very old custom has many mysterious origins.
Legend has it that the removal of the foreskin was initially a punishment meted out to a man who preyed on other men's wives. However, when the man recovered, he resumed his vice with such skill that other men followed suit.
Another origin of the famous custom is that a Gisu ancestor who wanted to marry a Kalenjin girl from the Barwa clan in Kenya was asked to make a covenant of circumcision with this clan including his ancestors. "He loved the girl so much that he agreed," the chairman Bamasaba Cultural Board, Mr Charles Wakwabubi said.
So powerful is the tradition today, that a Gisu man who is not circumcised is usually forced to do it once the circumcision year starts. That is why many a boy gets circumcised when they turn 15 years in order to avoid shame when caught and forced to circumcise while older. Imbalu is so powerfully intertwined in the culture that any dead Gisu man who is not circumcised will be subjected to the 'knife' before he is buried.
Gisu women have also been taught to secretly report their uncircumcised husbands to an elder who eventually takes it up with them. Although the desire to be circumcised is believed to be spiritually inspired; where the boys are allegedly influenced by the ancestral spirit of Imbalu, many Bagisu have reportedly fled the region and taken refuge in other districts for fear of the practice.
It is believed that when the circumcision season starts in August, during even years, with the blowing of horns and playing of Kadodi music, the boys are inspired to announce their intentions to be circumcised. The surgeons (Bashebi) also get possesed by the same spirit and start trembling involuntarily.
Mr Wakwabubi says during the circumcision season, the boys begin dancing around the village with their relatives in preparation for the official launching of Imbalu at Mutoto, the traditional circumcision ground. "When a boy asks for permission to get circumcised, he receives counsel from elderly male relatives about the challenges of 'the knife', adulthood and what Imbalu means to the Bamasaba clan. This is usually done to prepare the boy to withstand 'the knife' because if he shows fear, he will be shunned by women and other men," said Mr Wakwabubi.
A senior surgeon, Rtd Lt. Hajji Yusuf Wamboga says at times the boys can be charmed with a traditional root called Idyanyi or Ityanyi that drives them into a longing to be circumcised. "At times when boys grow older and they do not want to get circumcised, we give them this Idyanyi in porridge, local brew or in tea to inspire them and give them Dutch courage and a longing for circumcision," said Lt. Wamboga, adding that the burial of an uncircumcised dead man can lead to the death of the entire clan.
Although literate and Christian Bamasaba parents have opted to take their children for circumcision in hospitals, many a traditionalist regard it as abominable and an abuse to the Gishu culture. "In traditional Bugisu such children are usually undressed in public to ascertain whether they are circumcised or not. They are not allowed to preside over clan meetings because they are not man enough but can attend and listen," says Lt. Wamboga.
Lt. Wamboga says during circumcision, all candidates stand motionless in an enclosure surrounded by men wielding all sorts of sticks and pangas. Whereas the circumcision posture varies from one area to another, the candidates are expected to stand firm and display the highest level of bravery because it is part of the initiation.
Usually, there is one surgeon and an assistant. The assistant pulls the foreskin and marks the position to be cut with a whitish clay mixture. He puts his thumb on the glans and pulls the prepuce (loose fold of skin covering the glans) until it is stretched and holds it in position.
The surgeon then swiftly cuts off the fore skin and steps on it immediately, just to stop it from 'disappearing'. "The whistle goes and the circumcised boy then has to jump up and down to signify that the process is complete. This is followed by ululations, dancing and giving of gifts to the boy who has withstood 'the knife'. A coward does not benefit from all this," said Lt. Wamboga. He adds that the surgeon visits the newly circumcised boy, washes the boy's hands using malwa, medicine and water before feeding him.
Lt. Wamboga says this signifies washing away of boyhood and initiating him into adulthood. "This boy is later given firewood, a panga, a hoe, an axe and a cooking stone," said Lt. Wamboga.
The boy is then urged to go out and marry a young girl and not an old woman who has failed in marriage. "They are advised to produce a boy for another circumcision ceremony," said Lt. Wamboga. The boy is later taken to the father's house and given chicken. He walks around the house while plucking the feathers of the chicken.
The chicken is then roasted with bananas and eaten by relatives and the surgeon as a sign of saying farewell to boyhood and to the father's home. Many an elder in Bugisu believe Imbalu has a lot to do with natural aptitude or prowess.
dmafabi@ug.nationmedia.com

Original_Search_Title: Country Is a Banana Republic - Falana
Headline: Nigeria: Country Is a Banana Republic - Falana
Body_Text:
What do you think of President Goodluck Jonathan's leadership?
I do not have faith in the government of the Peoples Democratic Party because it does not have programmes that can take Nigeria out of the woods.
It also has engaged in systematic destruction of the country, through anti-people policies and programmes such as incessant increases in the prices of petroleum products, the removal of the so-called fuel subsidy, reckless devaluation of the national currency, infrastructural decay and collapse of values.
This is a regime that has exposed Nigeria to ridicule in the comity of democratic nations. Added to the crisis of relevance are insecurity of lives and property, mass unemployment, mass disenchantment and, of course, unprecedented looting of the treasury.
Just last week, the three ex-governors that were being arrested by the EFCC are influential members of the PDP, and they are being accused of looting the treasury to the tune of over N100 billion. How can one have confidence in such a government?
Government's intention to remove oil subsidy by next year, is it timely, taking into consideration the hardship and calamitous circumstances the masses are confronted with, due to a weakening economy?
You should start by asking yourself, is there really fuel subsidy. How can a country that is the sixth largest producer of oil in the Organisation of Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC) engage in the wholesale importation of petroleum products? It is simply illogical. This regime has been in power for over 12 years now.
Why has it been impossible to fix the country's refineries and build a new one so that the country can earn good money by exporting petroleum products apart from crude oil? In other words, apart from satisfying the domestic requirements, we should be exporting to earn good money and channel such funds to address socio-economic problems in the country.
But what are we told, anytime there is increase in the prizes of petroleum products in the international market, Nigerians must suffer. When the prizes are low, Nigerians must suffer. Who are the people benefitting from the so-called subsidy; some big boys and girls? They are in the good books of the regime. They import petroleum products after enjoying the so-called subsidy.
The bulk of the fuel is taken to neighbouring countries with the connivance of the government and sold at sky rocketing prices; not on behalf of Nigeria but on their own behalf. Nigerians must take advantage of this provocative announcement to find out what happens in other countries.
Over 10 years ago, I virtually suggested to the government to build refineries. Because, even if you remove the so-called subsidy tomorrow, and you don't have your refineries in place, that will not be the end of the increase.
Because anytime there is problem in the international market, they will still increase the prices of petroleum products imported to the country. All they are simply saying is that government is going to deregulate, so that anybody can bring in what he likes and sell; including of course, toxic fuel which was the experience Nigerians had under the Sanni Abacha junta. This is not the answer to the problems in the country.
Can you proffer an alternative solution to fuel subsidy removal?
It is the irresponsibility of the government to look for lasting solution to the country's problems that is the bane of our crisis. Venezuela is a developing and oil producing country like Nigeria, it has 18 refineries. Another alternative is for us to build refineries, but in the interim, go across the border to neighbouring countries and refine crude oil.
You don't have to pay, give them crude, and use their refineries to produce petroleum products. Again, we suggested, ask your oil companies to build refineries, pay them, engage in trade by barter, give them crude so you don't have to withdraw money from your foreign reserve to pay for imported petroleum products.
But they are not interested in these suggestions. It is the same problem you have with the devaluation of the currency. You are increasing wages with the right hand, and you are taking the benefits with the left hand by destroying your currency at the instigation of the International Monetary Fund (IMF) and World Bank.
Why are they not asking the United States government to devalue the dollar? Why are they not asking the British government to devalue the pounds? Are they not going through similar or worse economic conditions?
So, why are these people just insulting us and provoking the Nigerian people. What offence have we committed to be treated like slaves just because we have a gang of rulers who cannot discipline themselves, who cannot run the country?
Nigerians must not suffer. Why must Nigeria import toothpick, toothpaste, secondhand cloths, including pants and bra. That is what they are withdrawing foreign resources to buy and Nigerians must suffer.
If they want to deregulate the oil sector, deregulate the government. Put up the government for sale. Because what is the use of government, if you cannot guaranty security of lives and property. You cannot put kids in schools; you cannot get jobs for people. So, is there any basis for government?
Boko Haram has become a nightmarish phenomenon in Nigeria, Are you not worried that the country is degenerating into a terrorist nation as proclaimed by the United Nations?
Nigeria can still not be classified has a terrorist nation. There is no doubt that the failure of intelligence, that we have continued to witness in the country, has given a lot of room for extremist groups to take on the Nigerian state.
The lack of political will on the part of the government to challenge the culture of impunity has continued to promote mindless violence and terrorist attacks in the country.
In the last 12 years of the PDP-led government, over 20,000 Nigerians have been killed in ethno-religious and politically-motivated attacks, not one single person has been brought to book.
So, for the boys who have hijacked the monopoly of violence in the country, there is no government as far as they are concerned, because the basic duty of a modern government is to guaranty law and order and secure lives and property.
Once a government fails to meet that basic responsibility, you cannot talk of rule of law, or law and order or political stability. So, I would think, the terrorist groups are simply taking advantage of a weak or a failed state to unleash terror on the society.
But if you had a government that can address the root cause of violence in our country, Nigeria cannot be said to be a terrorist state.
Recently, the Presidential Committee on Security Challenges in the North-East submitted its findings and made recommendations inter-alia that the Federal Government should consider the option of dialogue and negotiate with the sect. On government's part, they have been dilly-dallying or at a loss on how to confront the sect. How do you think they should tackle the sect?
The government has already accepted to enter into dialogue with the group through the former president, Chief Olusegun Obasanjo, but because of his own dubious role in the promotion of violence in the country, and the emergence of Boko Haram sect, he does not have the moral right to play the role that was assigned to him (Obasanjo) by the government.
You will recall that the man who had the misfortune of meeting him on behalf of the group was eliminated within 24 hours of holding a parley with the General and his entourage.
But the regime is simply looking for an opportunity to justify its own lack of vision and commitment to national ideals and goals. If you address the problems of mass poverty in the midst of plenty, youth unemployment, illiteracy in the society, you won't have an army of young and able-bodied people engaging in violence against the Nigerian people.
A thousand and one panels of inquiry whose recommendations are going to decorate the shelf will not provide the solution that are required to restore law and order, justice and fair play, in our society.
And in any case, those who have inflicted violence in the country have not been brought to book. So, there is no way you are going to stop terrorism.
There are demands for restructuring of the country for a true federal system, should there be a restructuring and convocation of a sovereign national conference?
I don't think I want to join those who are making demands for restructuring, state creation and the rest of it, without addressing the basic responsibilities of government.
If you promote fiscal federalism, if you have more money for the states without addressing the redistribution of wealth in the country, job creation, looting of the treasury, you will merely be transferring crisis from the centre to the state and local governments.
So, those who are genuinely demanding for restructuring of the country should also restructure poverty and unemployment, they must restructure ignorance or banish them or reduce them to the barest minimum.
What changes will you like to be effected in the 1999 Constitution?
I want a complete review of the 1999 Constitution, because it was imposed on the country by the Abdulsalami Abubakar junta. In fact, what you call the 1999 Constitution is Decree 24 of 1999.
So we need a sovereign national conference, the conference of the Nigerian people that will not be guillotined to write a new constitution for the country.
So far, the CBN governor's policy according to a generality of the masses has a hidden agenda and is pro-North. Is that true?
It may not be fair if we review the policies being implemented by Central Bank as those of Sanusi Lamido, in the sense that, he is working according to the dictate of the Western countries, talking of the IMF and the World Bank, backed by the pro-imperialist Jonathan regime.
If a gang of people decides to destroy the economy, wherever they come from, and the law has caught up with them, why does anybody want to lose a sleep? If you want to have non-interest banks, whether you call it Islamic or Christian banks, why should anybody lose his head?
I told my Christian friends, if you want to challenge the introduction of Islamic banking, that will not charge interest, that will service the poor, then go and establish your Christian bank.
That is the only way you can challenge that policy. But to say that a bank that operates in about 50 countries in the world should not be allowed in Nigeria on the basis of religious sentiments is arrant nonsense.
And I think some of our people are too hypocritical. Christian churches are establishing universities that the children of the congregation, the children of the poor, cannot attend. There are no complaints.
Nigeria must go to war because Muslims say they want to establish non-interest bank, which benefits the poor, regardless of their religion.
People must be sensible! We have Christian schools in Nigeria; we have Muslim schools. If a group of Ifa worshippers want to establish Olodumare (name of the Yoruba deity) Bank that will give loans to the poor, I will welcome it.
But if somebody has stolen, as in the case of Madam Ibru, over a N100 billion, and he is brought to book, please there should be no sentiments about it.
How would you access the government's fight against corruption through its various agencies like the EFCC, ICPC and Code of Conduct Bureau, bearing in mind that the immunity clause in Section 308 of the Constitution, which is protective of corruption, still persists?
A corrupt government cannot fight corruption. Government should stop deceiving themselves. Only Nigerians, on their own, can force the government to fight corruption.
If Nigerians are mobilised, if the followership is prepared to cut the excesses of government officials, you can fight corruption. But certainly, not the way we are going about it.
We can tame the menace of corruption if the media, the civil society groups are prepared to do so. But certainly, don't expect government that is the most corrupt institution in the country to fight corruption.
All the arrests that are being made now and the media hypes that accompany them will fizzle out as soon as the governors are charged and are granted bail. The cases will be subjected to indefinite adjournment.
And that will be the end of the matter. All those who were charged in 2007 are having a field day in town. Some of them are now in the Senate of the Federal Republic of Nigeria.
The only man, who is regretting now, is Chief James Ibori, who made the mistake of running out of town, believing that he was escaping to a safe haven. If he were around today, he would be in the Senate of the Federal Republic of Nigeria doing his business and helping to fix the government and put people in government all over the place.
But he is on his way to jail because he has been arrested by those who are operating under some form of rule of law.
But here, this is a Banana Republic; you cannot jail a big man.
The law in a neo-colonial environment is not meant to harass or intimidate the rich, it is meant to intimidate the poor.
There is a general belief that the Judiciary is corrupt. If so, what advice would you give the new Chief Justice in order to effect positive changes in the Judiciary?
As the new Chief Judge of Nigeria, the Honourable Justice Dahiru Mustapha has rightly said that the Judiciary is an extension or reflection of the society. So, you do not expect a corrupt society to have a corrupt-free Judiciary.
The struggle to fight corruption in the larger society would definitely reflect on the Judiciary. But because of the crucial role that judges play in the sustenance of law and order in the society, they are expected to live above board.
Again like I said, Nigerians have to fight corruption, whether in the Executive, in the Legislature or in the Judiciary. There is no doubt that there is corruption in the Judiciary.
But I believe, given the political will on the part of the Chief Judge, I believe in association with the Nigerian Bar Association to ensure that the disturbing phenomenon of corruption in the Judiciary is confronted headlong.
Do you think Nigeria needs a change of system of government because there is a school of thought that says that the presidential system has failed us because it creates opportunity for public officers to rule without knowing or feeling the needs of the led. Could you suggest another system of government that will benefit Nigerians?
There is no doubt that the presidential system of government is very expensive, but what has made it worse is the culture of impunity on the part of Nigerian rulers. There is no government in the world that is as expensive as the one we are operating in Nigeria.
There is no president in the world that earns a higher salary than President Goodluck Jonathan; there is no governor in the world that earns anything near what our governors take home. Nigerian legislators are the highest paid in the world.
The Nigerian public officer receives the highest 'esta-code' in the world. Not under the law, but outside the provisions of the law. One governor the other day said that his security vote in a year is N6.5 billion; that is $43 million a year.
President Obama receives $400,000 a year. A Nigerian senator earns $1.7 million a year. Yet, this is one of the poorest nations on earth. If Nigerians know all this facts, the revolution that will take place here will be worse than the Arab situation.

Original_Search_Title: Reading Tent Opens in Kampala
Headline: Uganda: Reading Tent Opens in Kampala
Body_Text:
HUNDREDS of children Thursday attended the reading tent at Garden city in Kampala.
The event has many related activities under the banner of providing reading, storytelling, drawing, painting, and other activities to help promote reading among children specifically in primary and lower secondary schools.
"We want to encourage children to read with pleasure and relaxation without being forced to read," said the Uganda Children's writers and illustrators association (UCWIA) chairperson, Evangeline Ledi Barongo.
Evangeline also noted that children will also make some handwork from banana fibre.
"Every child should have the opportunity to learn how to read and listen to storytelling because the reading as a culture can actually enrich children's views to life, teach them about self-identity , morals and the beauty of African culture in an interesting and colorful way," Barongo said.
The event hosts 32 schools with 30 children each.
She added that the children will engage in silent reading, read-aloud, marathons, spellings and word identification, storytelling story writing, painting and bookmaking.
At the end of the reading tent, copies of the published children storybooks by Ugandan authors will be donated to each school to enable them establish school libraries with substantial collections of local story books.
The reading tent is one of National Book Trust of Uganda (NABOTU) activities aimed at promoting a reading culture in Uganda since 1997 and the event has been sponsored by children international.
The ministry of public service has also displayed the national Archives which have got correspondence files of the earliest records as far as 1888 covering activities of The British Imperial East African Company and other notable records of the former kingdoms of Buganda, Bunyoro and Busoga territory.

Original_Search_Title: Tambuwal, Na'abba Eulogise Ume-Ezeoke
Headline: Nigeria: Tambuwal, Na'abba Eulogise Ume-Ezeoke
Body_Text:
Second republic Speaker of the House of Representatives and former National Chairman of the All Nigeria People's Party (ANPP), late Edwin Ume- Ezeoke did not die a happy man, former Speaker of the House of Representatives Alhaji Ghali Umar Na'abba has said.
Speaking during the valedictory session held in honour of the late Ume- Ezeoke, Na'abba said the former ANPP boss did not die as a happy man because he was unhappy about the kind of politics played in the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), adding that he was a man of peace and consensus.
Na'abba said he knew him very well as they worked together to ensure that the elections of 2001 was properly done but the late speaker was sad over that he had to defected to the All Nigerian Peoples Party (ANPP). This was principally because the elections were characterized by exclusion, he said.
"It is the responsibility of the legislature to see to it that democracy survives and is sustained. This is because there is no need for people to die unhappily because of political frustration. We must conduct ourselves in a manner that promotes politics and democracy and emulate his peaceful nature of consensus," Na'abba said.
On his part, Speaker Aminu Waziri Tambuwal described Ume-Ezeoke was "a statesman due to his tremendous ability to bring people of diverse background together, his relentless passion and patriotic zeal, his ability to inspire others and his self-possessed personality."
Tambuwal explained that "one of the late speaker's attributes as a leader and statesman was his capability to integrate people of diverse political and ideological leanings towards the attainment of natural goal. This explains why he emerged speaker from opposition party in the Second Republic and remained in office even after the working agreement between NPN and NPP collapsed. Thanks to his sterling leadership qualities, the 'banana peel' was not thrown his path throughout the period he presided over the House of Representatives."

Original_Search_Title: 'Walvis Bay' Loads Bananas From Columbia for Europe Market
Headline: Namibia: 'Walvis Bay' Loads Bananas From Columbia for Europe Market
Body_Text:
BELIEVE it or not, there is a Danish container vessel bearing the name of 'Walvis Bay' which has recently begun operations -loading bananas from Columbia for the European market.
A web-report from a Tobias Gourlay said Maersk Walvis Bay reached the port of Puerto Bolívar last Friday, marking the beginning of the Danish shipping giant's operations at the Colombian facility.
"The 350 TEU-capacity vessel arrived with 150 empty containers and will be loaded up with 80 40ft containers of bananas for markets in Europe and the Mediterranean," Gourlay wrote.
Walvis Bay will apparently exclusively serve the Panama-Ecuador and Ecuador-Panama routes.
"The vessel was definitely named after Walvis Bay in Namibia," a Maersk official told The Namibian from Germany. "We name our vessels after some of the ports we operate from, and Walvis Bay is one of them."
One of Mearsk's newest and largest vessel, the WAFMAX-class (West Africa MAX) container vessel called at the port of Walvis Bay in June. Mearsk Namibia operates from Walvis Bay.

Original_Search_Title: The Nation is Under a Yoke - Menakaya
Headline: Nigeria: The Nation is Under a Yoke - Menakaya
Body_Text:
What is your assessment of this country?
To me, I will say I knew Nigerian colony of British Empire. And independence was granted to us, it's not easy to say what we have gone through but my description of Nigeria is that Nigeria is like an old man climbing a standing hill. We had it somehow good in those years when Nigeria was supposed to be under a yoke, but today I think we are under a greater yoke than what we had in those days. That is why I described it as an old man climbing a standing hill he cannot get anywhere; they say two steps up and three down are the best than standing on one spot. We have been very unfortunate to have government without any direction like a vector quantity that has no direction or magnitude. We are not moving anywhere, we are not going anywhere.
We've had it long a time, but today we have a country where there are so many laws that are not implemented or enforced. When we talk about state, sovereign national conference or whatever you call it, all those things can be put on a paper. Just like education, you can educate anybody in a class room but you have done nothing until the parson is prompt to use it and needs to put into practice what you have taught him. In Nigeria we have conferences everyday call it whatever you choose to call it but there is never any time we have implementation of the outcomes.
What do I mean by not implementing it? When you have a country where there are no sanctions, what do you think you are doing? Somebody steals a fowl because he is hungry or even something less and goes to prison. Another parson steals billions you honour him by publishing it in the newspapers and that is the end of it, you won't know whether he went to prison, whether he was sanctioned, the next day you see him asking to be given appointment in the government. These are part of our problems. And what is the reason for this? Another one is tribalism, if I do something because I am an Igbo, the Igbos do not even care about what I did wrong, the most important thing is where do I come from? They will support me and fight for me for being a thief and it goes on and on all over the place.
So this is one of the greatest problems we have, I have said it everywhere that no country can survive without sanctions. When some people talk about Malaysia, Singapore even India, I told them that they are developing rapidly because they are disciplined. The governments are disciplined and the people are disciplined. In the Nigeria of today, corruption thrives with legs untamed. In Nigeria, We had a short example when Buhari and Idiagbon were in government for a short period.
Nigerians behaved because all those that misbehaved were sanctioned by the law which we already have, and if you don't have such an implementation there can be no progress. And anarchy will prevail as we see today. We are running a government that is full of anarchy. That is what is killing all the things we do in this country.
There is no power because of corruption. We know where the money is going no one is in doubt. You create all sources of impossible avenues to run down the state and promote some individuals. This is why we have a country developing with a lot of smart fellows all over the places such that we have individuals who are richer than the state. Our government is built on immunity, it is not done anywhere in the world. If a governor kills he has the immunity to kill, if he steals he has immunity to steal. In the last 51 years, all I can say as a summary is that Nigeria is on motion without movement.
How do we solve these problems?
By having somebody or a group of people or a government that has direction that believes without fear or favour the right thing has to be done at all times. Ghana solved their own problems, they had a parson in Jerry Rawlings who came in and said no, this thing must be over and it became over. We have example also in Nigeria in December 1983 and before General Mohammed Buhari and Brigadier Tunde Idiagbon were pushed out, Nigerians behaved well because they knew the leaders were upright. When the head is rotten the body will follow suit and vice versa. You can even drop banana pills on the road somebody will ask you without any authority, my dear go and pick that thing and you will go back and pick it. Every Nigerian has the power to discipline but today its anarchy everywhere and this is one of our greatest problems. When we become disciplined, things will work. We have problem in the electricity sector, the police- we know what everyone says about them, if you are for them it means you have given money if you are against them, you might be shot. So this is what has been happening in this country and it has to be checked. We pray for the new government we have today, we hope they have direction, we pray for them to have courage to do the right thing. Courage is absolutely important in Nigeria.
Remember the oat taking to do good to all manners of people, to correct the wrongs of the country and encourage the right thing. To me this is one of the greatest problems we have in this country.
The general belief among the opposition is that President Goodluck Jonathan doesn't have the capacity to deliver.
I don't know what they saw in those who ruled us before him. Of all our rulers, only President Jonathan and the late Umaru Yar'adua were graduates that have ever ruled Nigeria. No, leadership is a function of knowing why and knowing how. Knowing why you have to be well educated to know why you are doing A,B,C,D.
Education doesn't mean only going to the university, you have to be properly trained, you have to be properly exposed to know what is right from wrong before you know how to do it. In 1999, we gave a figure that power will go up from here to there I was made to understand that we generated a lot of power and carried them in wrong files and all the files failed and that was the end of all the whole issue and we went back to square one. I believe that Jonathan if he has courage can change a lot of things in Nigeria.
Let's forget where Jonathan comes from, let us all come together and support him because his achievement is our achievement and his failure is our failure. There is no other country you will run to that will be better than your own; we must build it up together. To me Jonathan seems to be listening but when the troubles are too many confusion sets in. Let us just encourage him, let us pray for him.
But so far has he shown courage?
I cannot judge him. Hundred days is so small to access anybody's performance in office.
He has been there for almost two years now.
No he was there on a wrong garment; he was not wearing his own toga. This is a country which has a president and a vice president. In some countries, it is automatic, if a president is ill or absent, his deputy takes over. In Nigeria, we have to curl all sorts of words to enable Jonathan take over the leadership of the country. We made rubbish of our constitution with the so called doctrine of necessity. So when Jonathan came in through the doctrine of necessity, he stands with one led, but when you have proper authority you stand firmly on both legs. So he came in standing on one leg and could not perform, he could not do much now that he is standing on two legs let us see what he can do, let us support him. Boko Haram is a new one, who is he? What is the aim? Is it to destroy or to make? Some people have said openly that if they are not so and so the country will be ungovernable.
Who is supposed to do something about this, is it not the president?
It's the president that was why I used the word courage. Let us pray for him to have courage, let us believe that he has courage to tackle those things.
Recently in his speech, he said he needs not be a General or a Goliath to rule Nigeria. What is your take on this?
I cannot say he has not got the courage, but let us pray for him to muster enough courage because you need a lot of courage to perform. And in Nigeria if you start to perform you should not look back, don't look at Igbo man, Yoruba man, Hausa man, Fulani man or any tribe, go for the right thing and do the right thing and stand by it. That is the kind of courage I mean.
When you were talking initially you said Buhari -Idiagbon government was disciplined. But the same man offered himself on two different elections, you didn't vote for him. Why?
It was probably because he was no longer singing the best song he sang in those days. In fact the Buhari music we heard in 2007 and 2011 was not the same music we heard in December 1983. The music changed and the electorate too changed and this is not good for Buhari and the nation. The kind of music he is singing lately is making it difficult to say it was Buhari or it was Idiagbon. And because the combination of Idiagbon is no more there, Buhari lost.
Can you do an appraisal on the health care system?
The health care system was neglected for too long a time. When people like us qualified as doctors, all we needed was man power we had encouragement, we had most things we needed to function and improvised as much as possible. A doctor can work in a hospital without a generator and still performed but today it is not the same. The policy needs to be completely re-organised and we have had people with no knowledge of medicine becoming Minister of Health. I thank God that today we have a young man, a bright man and a medical doctor who knows his onions because, he who wears the shoe know where it pinches.
I feel ashamed when Nigerians go to India for medical treatment, I feel ashamed when Nigerians go to South Africa for medical treatment, when you get there in South Africa, UK or USA you will discover that you will be treated by a Nigerian. Why don't we use them? Why don't we do the right thing? Why do we have conferences every day without implementation of the reports on those conferences? These are the problems we have; again why is our government not putting money where it should go. World Health Organisation (WHO) recommended certain percentage of every country's budget for health; we are not using up to 10% or 15% of their recommendations. The health delivery in this country we should have had all it takes to deliver but the support is very negligible.
Doctors in Nigeria butcher patients but when they travel abroad they behave themselves, why are doctors behaving like this?
When you were minister I insist, I won't accept that doctors butcher patients. But probably most of the doctors in Nigeria have divided attention. When we started practising in this country it is either you are working for the government that pays your salaries or you are in private practice; there must be a line drawn between the two. You don't have to be in government to be a good doctor, you can practice on your own. I practiced before I joined government and have been practicing on my own since I left government. I am sure I am not one of the butchers you are talking about. But I don't even see, because we have laws governing the practice of medicine. You know your rights and your limitations you don't treat a patient which you cannot treat, you have right to refer. But when you have divided attention, you run from one office to the other it is not right. There should be medical insurance for all doctors which in other countries it is called negligence insurance for which you are penalised when the need arises.
There must be regulations in ever thing we do in this country. Nigeria medical council has a branch which is into those problems. Two weeks ago, a doctor was suspended for life almost, two were suspended for six months. This is part of the things Nigerians want to see and I urge the council to do more. Every hospital in this country even if it is a clinic should be registered. And who registers them? Most of the doctors in government hospitals have their own private hospitals. They are killing government hospitals because even though government pays them, they corner patients in public hospitals to their own hospital and this is greed and wickedness. In some cases patients pay for medical bills in the private hospitals through the skin of their teeth. Government must reverse this trend through legislations.
Today, when doctors in government hospitals are on strike, the impact is not felt much because the rich visit private hospitals.
Only the poor who can not afford the bills die in public hospitals.
When you were Minister of Health what did you do to make doctors who are employed by government to actually concentrate on their job?
If you remember those days when I came in doctors were on strike for almost six months. I had a very wonderful dialogue with them and the strike stopped immediately. As a past practitioner, I took my time to study the problem and I told them to give me a period of few months to solve the problems and they obliged me. And that was the end of it and we started working together, changed a few things, tried to change their lots but I didn't stay long enough. You must remember that roll back malarial was organised by the ministry when I was there at no cost to Nigerians, by their deeds they said in the bible you shall know them. If you can get an article written by economics about that short period I did some work you will understand how we have planned to go.
Before I left, we did approvals that every single local government will have a minimum of 10 million to organise primary health in the grass roots. It was approved by the National Assembly, the money was released I don't see much done anywhere on that. In Nigeria there is no continuity if you go, another parson will come and say this bad man he did this and did that, and tries to change all the things you did whether he is knowledgeable or not. We should create a government of continuity. When I was a minister, the Minister of Health from Ghana has been there for over 10 years, the Minister of Labour from Zambia, Uganda and Namibia have been there for a very long time following policies and programmes that will lead to a better health care delivery. Here some last for just 5 months or there about what do you expect? So many things have to be done in this country to get it right.
What were the circumstances that made you to leave?
I can say am a bit controversial, I am not easily persuaded, I did come into government not as a young man. I have practiced medicine for about 36 years or more, I have my private practice, I have one ambition which is to make a difference. But I was told in the system I needed not to clean it because if I cleaned it, my enemies around will benefit from it. I said no, am not a medical doctor for a group I was a minister for the whole of Nigeria poised to improve health care delivery for everybody and we need money to do that and WHO said that we needed so much. When in Nigeria the GDP for every Nigerian health wise was less than 50 kobo how can you provide so much in a very big country like Nigeria.
When we were there, we started a concerted effort to win a lot of things, we started journal repatriation where we piloted vaccinations not only for Nigerians but for all our neighbours. If we don't eliminate polio virus in Niger Republic and it is cleared in Nigeria, the boundaries are porous, it would spread to us. So whatever we did in Nigeria, we did in Niger Republic, Cameroon, Chad and in Benin Republic. When I came we started house to house immunization. Before it used to be in schools or here and there, making it impossible to reach the children. So we started house to house, if we visited house A and there were so many children, they would come out and the experiment paid a lot. We vaccinated children at the age of five totaling about 13 million and 40 million in general. Vaccination then was about 10% today I am sure it must be much more than that probably over 60%. But most of the time the policies are changed
What did you do on HIV/AIDS?
I will say one thing, before 1999 there were so many conferences, so many researches done on HIV/AIDS, the reports were there but locked up in the closet. When we came to office, we opened the closet and brought it out and told Nigerians to look at where we are, we are in danger; everybody should know it and the campaign started. We wrote to United Nations to tell them that please don't attend our funeral but help us now. That was the paper and the committee I chaired and help started coming. Some mediators wrote me that I blacklisted their states, some said they were the red spots, I said no, the whole country was endangered. We have had ministers of health who worked so hard to prepare all these papers but they were not known, so the war against HIV/AIDS started, I won't say during my time but at least we brought it out of the closet. We have all sorts of potentials in this country individually Nigerians have done extremely well. Most Nigerians have created both wealth and employment, all we need is a government that will put direction and that will have courage to act and sanction ills. Corruption is endemic and must be stopped otherwise we cannot move, I repeat we are like an old man climbing a standing hill.
Are you a politician?
Am a professional first and all of us are politicians.
What is your take on Igbo presidency?
Everybody have said it, there must be equity. We were left out with South/South today they have produced the president in Jonathan, only Ndigbo are left out. Maybe one day after probably we have seen that this is not a tribal thing, Nigerians will then now say let us go for the best and Igbo stands for the best.
In 2015, we shall not gun for president, we shall work for it, nobody is going to die. We will work for it and if God wills, we will get it and if God wills, we will change Nigeria for good. We still believe that we were brutalised in the war and we were treated as such. If you go to Igbo land or South-East today, the distance you should cover in five seconds, you might have to do that in one hour. There are no roads anywhere, there is no infrastructure anywhere we are completely neglected. A Nigerian president of Igbo extraction will give Ndigbo a sense of belonging and unite the country.
Are you sure if an Igbo man becomes the president these problems will be solved? The Yoruba's had President Olusegun Obasanjo who you worked under as minister, yet they claimed to be worse for it.
The most important thing is to have a man who has direction, who has courage, who initiates and implements beautiful policies. And we have them in abundance.

Original_Search_Title: Lillian Mbabazi Thrills At Uganda's 49th Independence Day Celebrations
Headline: Rwanda: Lillian Mbabazi Thrills At Uganda's 49th Independence Day Celebrations
Body_Text:
