With a big basket of the commodity selling for between N19,500 and N12,000 as against the former price of between N8,500 and N9,000. Prices of frozen fish, and vegetable oil have also skyrocketed. The cost of frozen fish has increased with a medium sized fish selling for between N250 and N350 as against N130 and N180, its initial price.

Original_Search_Title: "Criminal Den" Discovered At Elwa Junction
Headline: Liberia: "Criminal Den" Discovered At Elwa Junction
Body_Text:
An investigation by this paper has discovered what appears to be a hideout for several alleged hidden criminals in the ELWA Junction Community. The ELWA Junction Community is situated in Paynesville City, outside Monrovia.
The investigation unearthed that over 30 well built men and more than 17 females habitually congregate in a garage called Mr. Parker Garage located in the ELWA Junction Community in the evening hours to abuse drugs, gamble and strategize how to carry out criminal activities among others.
A gray vehicle, Trooper Jeep, with license plate 22 JUNE, parked in the garage, is permanently used by the alleged criminals as lodging place, the investigation divulged.
The investigation further divulged that the alleged criminals, who also engage in thievery in the ELWA Community and other communities in Monrovia and its environs, usually threaten to harm the owners of Mr. Parker Garage, if they (owners of Mr. Parker Garage) meddle in their alleged criminal activities or ask them to leave the garage.
One of the three joint owners of the garage, who spoke to this paper yesterday on straight agreement of anonymity for "fear of reprisal" from the alleged criminals, confirmed that numerous threats have been made on their lives by the "criminals."
He said they (owners of Mr. Parker Garage) are living in fear, adding that the alleged criminals are "dangerous", let the government please come to help us and raid this place."
Our investigation also gathered that by 8:30 PM up to 3:00 AM, the alleged criminals post themselves in the darkness in the area and among other things attack individuals passing by, and in most instances snatch away cell phones, money and other valuables.
The investigation detailed that two entertainment centers (names withheld) in the community play host to the alleged criminals from as early as 8:00 AM up to 3:00 PM, allegedly promoting cod commercial sex and trading in alcoholic beverages and drugs, which specifically include cocaine and marijuana.
The investigation descriptively identified the exact location in the ELWA Junction Community where the alleged criminals are considering as their den like this: Banana Island Community, opposite the JEETY TRADING CORP., adjacent the newly constructed National Social Security and Welfare Corporation's (NASSCORP) Commercial Building (right hand side). Investigation continues.

Original_Search_Title: Four Residents Die After Drinking Toxic Brew
Headline: Kenya: Four Residents Die After Drinking Toxic Brew
Body_Text:
Four people died on Monday at Muchatha village in Banana, Kiambu, after drinking a toxic brew.
Three others were admitted to Tigoni Hospital after losing their sight. They drank the brew on Sunday at a local bar owned by a former administrator.
Ms Patricia Nyokabi, who lost her 40-year-old son Samuel Kamau said: "My daughter called me today (yesterday) and told me that Kamau was complaining of a sight problem and also abdominal pains. I decided to go and see him but when I arrived, he had already died."
According to residents who stormed local bars and disposed of all brews in plastic containers, Mr Job Njoroge from Njoro village, Mr John Waiganjo from Nguruwe village and Ms Faith Wanja died after taking the toxic drinks.
"It's only days after eight people died in Ruiru and 19 in Nyandarua. Three months ago, 10 people died here in Banana after taking the deadly brews. How many more people will the government wait to die in order to take action?" asked Mr James Wanjohi, a resident.
Administrators, led by area district commissioner Albert Kimathi, visited the area, promising to act.
Residents, however, accused them of laxity in the fight against illicit brews.
Elsewhere, former Cabinet minister G.G. Kariuki accused police officers of sleeping on the job. He said they were to blame for the deaths caused by the drinks.
Mr Kariuki said most of the law enforcement officers turned a blind eye on brewers and sellers of the drinks after receiving bribes.
He was speaking at Kiamana village in Nyahururu District at the weekend. Nineteen people died in the area last week after consuming toxic brews.
"Why did the provincial administration have to wait for the presidential directive for them to swing into action and raid the beer dens? It shows that all along they knew where the brewers operated from but turned a blind eye since they work in cahoots with them after receiving bribes," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Nokia Siemens Networks to Boost Capacity in the Country
Headline: Nigeria: Nokia Siemens Networks to Boost Capacity in the Country
Body_Text:
Lagos — As part of efforts to boost Information and Communication Technology capacity utilizations among government's agencies and businesses in the country, Nokia Siemens Networks has opened a new office in Nigeria.
The Head of Africa Region, Nokia Siemens Networks, Mr. Dimitri Diliani, said the opening of the Nigerian office was part of its 'Internet for every African' vision, adding that Nigeria is an important country in this vision.
Diliani who spoke at the inauguration of the office at the Banana Island in Lagos, said having point of presence in Nigeria was as part of the company's renewed focus on the African continent.
He explained that Nokia Siemens Networks restructured and separated its Africa operations at the beginning of 2011.
Diliani said, "The African market is growing at a strong pace and we felt the need to provide additional focus to support this growth. Our new office in Lagos will serve as an integral part of our strategy to strengthen our operations in the continent and deliver superior services to our customers."
The Country Director for Nokia Siemens networks, Mr. Raphael Udeogu in a presentation at the event bemoaned the low utilisation of ICT resources in Nigeria with reference to the results of the 2011 Connectivity Scorecard, which is the first study to rank 50 countries worldwide on ICT infrastructure deployment and the extent to which the infrastructure is put to efficient use.
He said, "On a benchmark scale of 10, Nigeria scores a low '1.09' and ranks 23rd on the list of developing countries (or resource & efficiency driven economies) measured in the overall study."
Even though there is a positive increase in mobile penetration and a strong demand for Internet services, there needs to be a strong improvement in broadband services, literacy rates, business infrastructure and ICT utilisation in business and public sectors, he said.

Original_Search_Title: Maaloul- We Achieved What We Were Looking for
Headline: Africa: Maaloul- We Achieved What We Were Looking for
Body_Text:
Esperance coach Nabil Maaloul expressed his happiness at his team's qualification for the CAF Champions League semi finals after holding Egyptian giants Al Ahly to a 1-1 draw in Cairo. Maaloul said that his team was now halfway towards winning the title they lost last season to DR Congo's TP Mazembe. The coach told Cafonline.com after the game that he expected the Cairo game to go the way it did with what he thought Ahly would do spot on.
How do you evaluate your 1-1 draw and your semi final qualification?
I expected how the game went on and had told that to my players before the game. It was a tough match for both of the teams as they searched for the qualification ticket. We had a good game till the last minute when we knew that WAC was losing to MC Alger which motivated Ahly players. We tried to stop their key players Ahmed Fathy , Sayed Moawad and Abou Treika and we did that well and also had some scoring chances.
And why you kept your star Oussam Darragui on the bench?
It was a tactical ploy because l knew that Ahly was going to attack from the very beginning playing at their home ground. We had to press on their midfield and that's why I kept Darragui out as he is more of an attacker than a defender. I introduced him in the second half to press on Ahly's defence later in the game.
Do you consider this game key to winning the Champions League?
What I was looking for is winning the group so that I can have the second leg of the semi final and final at home. In the Champions League nothing could be expected.
It was obvious that you relied on the free kicks and Banana scored from one of them. Did you train specifically at that?
Yes we concentrated on that because I knew our players are taller than Ahly ones. Banana is 190 cm tall and he took the lead for us.
How do you see Ahly's exit from the Champions League?
Ahly is a great champion with an amazing history, but we were the better side on Friday and we achieved qualification. It was a tough match.

Original_Search_Title: To Some of Us, Nobody's Good
Headline: Tanzania: To Some of Us, Nobody's Good
Body_Text:
There's this other wordy fellow who frequents one of your favourite watering establishments along the Bagamoyo Road. He's certainly an adherent of the culture of "kutesa" - Kiswahili slang referring to having fun in a manner that makes others know you're having fun. The idea is to elicit wivu, that is, envy.
He's typical of the Wabongo who do things, not because a man has, but rather to draw others' attention to the fact they're persons of substance. Waipate laivu! If you do something and nobody's noticing, they're apt to say, what's the use?
Now this guy (call him Jay if you like), is a unique chap, to say the least, because of the way he talks with derision about everybody else. It's like he sees "no good in nobody". His favourite rhetoric query is: Ana nini yule? What does that guy have?
Like this time round, when somebody at our table remarks on Sele's Prado which, word has it, comes at not less than Sh45 million."So what? What's a Prado? In any case, it's a bloody mtumba, isn't it?" says Jay.
"Yes, but everybody buying a vehicle in Bongo except, of course, the filthy rich, our MPs, parastatal chiefs and thieves... goes for a mtumba, don't they?" says Aluu who's seated face to face with Jay.
"Yeah, I know, so why does Sele beat his chest about his ka-Prado like he owns the world? People drive Hummers and they're not bragging like he does!"
You don't participate in this talk because you know next to nothing about cars and, but most of all, you aren't keen on people's wealth or lack of it. One might say the Ujamaa gamba on you is refusing to slough off.
Soon, a young fellow walks in and walks past our table, accompanied by an even younger lady who, it later transpires, is his girlfriend. She's the kind that makes heads turn, for sure. You can actually hear the "Wows!" and all manner of gasps from the men in the grocery.
"That's a chick and a half the guy has for a girlfriend. Very beautiful, isn't she?" says a fellow at our table.
There's an unanimous "Yesss!" Well... almost unanimous, because Jay has a very different view.
He says: "Beautiful? What beauty? I know that ka-girl, her apparently pretty face is nothing but a product of mkorogo, that dangerous mixture of skin eroding chemicals capable of changing a charcoal black woman's face into that of virtual Mzungu."
"I don't think so, I've known Mariam since her childhood and she has always been like that... actually we used to call her 'White' while she was in primary school - they're white by nature in her family," says Zablon, a middle aged fellow.
Another drinker expresses his admiration for Mariam's generous posterior, but our friend will hear none of it.
"There's nothing there? It's all fake?"
"Fake? How can a woman's posterior be fake? Are you suggesting she's wearing padded undies?"
"No, it's worse than that!"
What do you mean?"
"You don't know? That posterior is a product of Chinese creams you apply to any part of your anatomy that you want enlarged and voilà ! You've got it... the Chinese are no joke!
"I don't think so... I know her mother, she has the same features as Mariam and she has been like that since the time China was only renowned for Communism under Chairman Mao tse Tung, not for anatomy enhancing creams, pirated music, fake mobile phones and other scams," says Zablon.
It's clear this crappy talk is not leading nobody anywhere and there's an all round relief when Sacky, this fellow from the land of banana eaters, walks in and greets everybody with lots of ceremony that we all forget about the loose talk.
"Give everybody one-one," says Sacky. He had been on a trip that took him to the UK, Uganda, Dubai, etc, etc. Or so it is said. You're sure he's dying to share his experiences with the crowd.
As he shakes hands with others, Jay leans to you and whispers: "Look at him, he's buying this big round just to impress, and wants us to believe he's generous, but he's not!"
"Why do you say so?"
"The money is not his... he's been given that money by the company to buy beers for all and sundry, so that he gets the information he wants."
"Are sure?" you ask.
"I am... the money journalists spend on beer is not from their salaries, I swear!"
The fellow sounds so convincing you'd believe him, but then, you're a journalist yourself and know better.
"Some people!" that's all you can say to yourself.

Original_Search_Title: Al Ahly Miss Out On Semi-Finals
Headline: Egypt: Al Ahly Miss Out On Semi-Finals After a Draw With Esperance
Body_Text:
Egyptian side Al Ahly record six-time winners of the African Champions League is out of this year's competition after being held at home to a 1-1 draw by Tunisia's Esperance in a match they need to win to qualify for the semi-finals.
Esperance scored first in 17th minute when Cameroonian defender Yaya Banana rose above the Ahly defence to head in past goalkeeper Ahmed Adel and silence the large crowd in Cairo Stadium.
Ahly's draw in the last group stage match meant that they finished tied on 7 points with Morocco's Wydad who lost 3-1 in Algeria to MC Moulodia. The Moroccans piped the Egyptians to second place on head to head rule and now join Esperance in the semi-finals.
Ahly had restored hope for a win when midfielder Mohamed Aboutreika equalized in 55th minute through another header. Substitute Walid Soliman sent in a corner and Aboutreika nodded it home sending the crowd wild with delight. The midfielder could have ensured an Ahly victory later on in the match but fluffed two gilt-edged chances with only the keeper Moez Ben Cherifia to beat. Ahly could also have snatched a win which would have taken them through when Mohamed Fadl's header was cleared off the line and Dominique da Silva following up could only put his effort wide of goal.
The pulsating match fizzled into a draw much to the agony of Ahly and its fans and delight for Wydad across in Algeria.
Esperance finished top in Group B with ten points and will now meet the either Cameroon's Cottonsport Garoua or Al Hilal of Sudan in the semi-finals. Wydad will play Group A winners Nigeria's Enyimba in the other semi-final.

Original_Search_Title: A Place Where Faith and Love Clash
Headline: Kenya: A Place Where Faith and Love Clash
Body_Text:
I once knew a guy who sang in the church choir. He was a bit of a mystery, this guy; kept to himself mostly, spoke softly and always walked with his head bowed low as if in prayer.
He was a man of very few words. But in church it dawned on you that he was something special because in the choir this guy was transformed into something phenomenal.
He came alive. He bristled with energy. So let's say you were seated in church, nursing a hangover and trying to muster as much humility before the good Lord by trying not to acknowledge the fact that you were indeed bored and sleepy.
And just when you were thinking, for the umpteenth time that morning, that perhaps you should have just stayed in bed, the choir started to sing and you heard this voice.
This voice that fills the church and fills your spirit with the good word. Think Youssou N'Dour singing 'Halleluiyah'.
This guy was a human canary; he had a voice built for reaping souls - even the hangover ones. Especially the hangover ones.
So anyway, he was - as you probably have guessed - born again (you know, he had accepted Jesus Christ as his personal savior).
But as fate would have it - and as a testimony that, indeed, the heart is not so smart - his faith constantly clashed with his heart.
He always fell for the women who didn't 'walk in the light'; women who didn't share his spiritual disposition.
Fell for them like an orange off a tree. Fell for them hard. And so his life became a constant battle between evil and good, between love and faith, all powerful emotions in their own right and all pulling him in opposite directions.
He was a torn man, which, perhaps, explained why he always walked with his head constantly bowed down.
Anyway, since he was a man who knew which side of his toast was buttered, he constantly managed to make the right call... but one day his luck ran out.
He met someone, a lady, another 'non-believer'. But this time he couldn't muster the strength to say no. So he started dating her and the murmurs in the choir started.
He became the outlaw, the one who 'jumped in bed' with the enemy. Finally, he had to leave that church and move to another one where his dark life was not known.
The choir was never the same without his voice. That much I can tell you. The Bible doesn't make bones about this topic in 2 Corinthians 6:14, saying:
"Do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers. For what fellowship has righteousness with lawlessness? And what communion has light with darkness?"
Deuteronomy 22:10 is even more explicit: "Do not plow with an ox and a donkey yoked together." So yes, oranges and bananas should be in different baskets. Fine.
Everyone, ultimately, goes to God where matters of the heart are involved. You pray that you end up with a good spouse (or with any kind of spouse, as exhibited by the army of women who headed to KICC not too long ago to pray for husbands).
You go before Him to give you the strength not to raise your hand to a woman who is driving you up the wall. And you go before Him to pledge holy matrimony when you meet 'The One.'
God is the ultimate intervener and from what I know of Him, he is a merciful God. A God who doesn't judge.
There is a silent hostility (for lack of a better word) that the church fraternity exudes when a 'non-believer' tries dating inside the flock.
They are treated like an outsider, a spoiler, a tainted being who has no business mingling with the faithful. Light and darkness, they say, cannot walk together.
As one believer I engaged told me, "If the non-believer is willing and has been convicted by the lives of the believers, he will accept Jesus.
But if the believers are swayed by the life and interaction of the non-believer, they will fall."
"But what if he really loves her in a way that no other believer can?"
"Doesn't matter, it won't work," he dismissed me, "because they are headed in two different directions."
"Have you ever heard of acceptance?" I whined.
"This is not about acceptance," he said, "this is about destiny, purpose and eternity!" I gave up.
And that's the thing; the guys who are a part of these groups are always - and perhaps rightfully so - very protective of the 'sisters' in these groups.
A non-believer who is interested in one of them is immediately treated not as a man first, but as a non-believer, regardless of his intentions.
This is my take: it is hard enough finding love. So what if you find it out of church?

Original_Search_Title: Ahly Hosts Esperance in a "Must Win" Game
Headline: Africa: Ahly Hosts Esperance in a "Must Win" Game
Body_Text:
Egyptian giants Al Ahly entertain their Tunisian counterparts Esperance as the Orange CAF Champions League group stage comes to an end on Friday.
Ahly enters the match with 6 points in the 3rd position in Group B, three points adrift of the leaders Esperance. Ahly needs nothing short of a win by 2 clear goals to make the semis without waiting for the other group match that sees Algerian MC Alger take on Moroccan's WAC in Algiers.
The fixture is to be played at the 74,100 fans Cairo International Stadium, built i1958 as Nasser Stadium, (after the late Egyptian president Gamal Abdel Nasser.) It was renovated in 2004 with the cost of 150 million Egyptian pounds, and hosted opening and final match of the 2006 African Cup of Nations, both won by host Egypt.
CAF referees committee announced that the game will be officiated by the Sudanese Khaled Abdul Rahman assisted by Waleed Mohamed, Aref Hasab El Rasool and 4th official Alfadel Abu Shanab.
Ahly has started their preparations for the game just after finishing their Day 5 match versus WAC, and had a training session on WAC's training ground before getting back to Cairo on Tuesday night to go on with their preparations.
Manager Manuel Jose expressed his belief and trust in Ahly players saying they can win the game and ensure qualification to the semi finals even when are expected to achieve a minimum 2-0 victory.
Jose placed his bet on Ahly supporters that they will stand behind the team till they achieve victory. The Portuguese admitted that Group B was very tough and that the all-Arab fixtures were tricky, considering that if Ahly was playing in the other group they could have qualified easily.
Jose also blamed the "behind closed doors" match versus WAC in Day 1, saying that if Ahly fans attended that match the team could have won.
Jose insisted on the importance of Ahly's victory and qualification as the only continental hope for Egypt after the national team's failure to qualify for the 2012 Africa Cup of Nations. He said everybody will do his best to win and that he is very optimistic despite the fact that their opponents will give them some hard time..
On the other side Esperance enters the game looking to qualify should they draw or win and would like to keep first place in the group. The Tunisians know that a draw or even defeat by a single goal will be enough to get them a semi final berth. Esperance arrived in Cairo on Wednesday with a 35 men delegation led by Club chairman Hamdy El Moadeb.
Esperance officials were relieved after defender Yaya Banana was cleared to participate after confirmation that the booking he received against MC Alger was his first.
Manager Nabil Maaloul said that he arrived in Cairo looking for a good game against his Egyptian counterparts. Maaloul said that Ahly's draw with WAC added importance to that game and that Friday will be the decisive day in Cairo.

Original_Search_Title: Nokia Siemens Shows Interest in CDMA Bail-Out
Headline: Nigeria: Nokia Siemens Shows Interest in CDMA Bail-Out
Body_Text:
OPerators of the Code Division Multiple Access, CDMA, may still achieve their aim of competing favourably with the Global system for Mobile communications, GSM services providers, otherwise known in Nigeria as the major operators.
This is as telecom infrastructure providers, Nokia Siemens at the weekend, while opening office in Nigeria, indicated interest in providing them with solutions that can leverage their activities to meet that tall order.
The fortunes of the Nigerian CDMA operators experienced a nosedive shortly after the GSM operators opened shop in August 2001 and since then, while the GSM operators keep increasing in numbers and financial records, their CDMA counterparts have been depleting in both numbers and bank balance.
Although things began to look bright in 2006 when their regional licences were upgraded to Unified Access Services Licences, the operators could not sustain the tempo and from a position of strength in terms of the number of operators, there are today, just about only two active CDMA operators, Starcomms and Visafone.
All hope not lost
However, Nokia Siemens says all hope is not lost. According to its Nigerian Country Director, Raphael Udogu, "we are looking at solutions which they can use and share, to cut cost and still remain competitive which has been lacking.
This is why they have not been growing at par with bigger operators because they didn't have enough fund to invest in infrastructure that could have taken their growth to the level of the big operators. We are going to sit with them to design the strategy and solutions that can help them come from under. We are discussing that and that is one of the reasons why we are here"
Earlier in his presentation, Head of Africa Region, Nokia Siemens Networks, Mr Dimitri Diliani, said that his company has a vision to make 'Internet for every African' a reality.
According to him, Nigeria is an important country in this vision and so as part of the company's renewed focus on the African continent, Nokia Siemens Networks restructured and separated its Africa operations at the beginning of 2011.
The African market is growing at a strong pace and we felt the need to provide additional focus to support this growth. Our new office in Lagos will serve as an integral part of our strategy to strengthen our operations in the continent and deliver superior services to our customers," He added.
Low connectivity index
Meanwhile, Nigeria's connectivity index score card went all time low to 1.09 percent ,according to a recent study, known as Connectivity Scorecard, by a Fellow of the London Business School, Prof. Leonard Waverman.
The study said that Nigeria retains the 23rd position among the resource and efficiency-driven economies on the Connectivity Scorecard 2011 index. This means that between 2009 and 2010, Nigeria maintained a stagnated infrastructure growth, retaining 23rd position even when mobile penetration grew from 49 percent in 2009 to 57 percent in 2010.
Reacting to the result of the study, Information and Communications Technology ICT, infrastructure providers, Nokia Siemens, at the weekend hinted that Nigeria needed active participation of its public sector to grow the country's ICT infrastructure to the level that can sustain the economy outside oil.
The company through its country Director, Raphael Udogu, in a ceremony to mark the opening of its Nigerian office at the Banana Island Ikoyi Lagos, said that one of the problems mitigating against the growth of infrastructure in Nigeria was the low participation of the country's public sector.
He noted that while mobile penetration was growing in appreciable form, the country's infrastructural development was still at low ebb because the public sector was sitting tight on almost all areas of ICT participation.
He advised that a consistent awareness campaign and training, was needed to get the Nigerian public sector to know the importance of ICT in every level of its activities. For him, this would ensure that the private sector gets the relevant support it needed to make the public private participation, PPP strategy solving problems in Nigeria.
Connectivity Scorecard is a global ICT index which ranks 50 countries, not only on their deployment of ICT infrastructure, but also to measure the extent to which governments, businesses and consumers make use of connectivity technologies to enhance social and economic prosperity.
In the study, Nigeria scores 1.09 and retains the 23rd position among the resource and efficiency-driven economies on the Connectivity Scorecard 2011 index.
Relatively better performance
With this score, Nigeria continues to rank among the bottom five countries along with India, Pakistan, Kenya and Bangladesh. However, the country posts a relatively better performance in the consumer segment, led by a high mobile phone penetration rate. Its performance on the business and public sector components is very poor, indicating significant room for improvement.
The Scorecard in 2011 highlights the continued need for investment in information and communications technology (ICT) to stimulate a return to economic growth.
Udogu however promised that his company was going to play its own part by designing solutions tailored at solving peculiar problems faced by telecommunications operators in Nigeria and called on the government to pursue the issue of power generation with all vigour, saying it could be the tonic that would wake the public sector up, eventually.
Nokia Siemens Networks is a leading global enabler of telecommunications services. The company provides a complete portfolio of mobile, fixed and converged network technology, as well as professional services including consultancy and systems integration, deployment, maintenance and managed services.

Original_Search_Title: The Many Harams in the Country
Headline: Nigeria: The Many Harams in the Country
Body_Text:
The Boko Haram issue has woken us to the many Harams in the country. Yes, there are indeed many Harams in Nigeria. Yet, its patrons want us to believe it is a "Great Nation of Good People".
I think that is a very good starting point. I believe creating an ordinary slogan to hoodwink the people is itself Haram. How can people charged with making the nation great fail to do so and yet coin a slogan and impose it on the rest of us. Do they believe that a simple slogan will make the nation great? What is so great about Nigeria anyway? Is it because we are spared the nasty experiences of natural disasters such as earthquakes, hurricanes and volcanoes? This is just a product of divine intervention and not because of our actions or inactions. In the absence of natural disasters we have decided to foment ethnic clashes, religious fights, inter and intra-community wars, armed robbery, kidnappings, vandalizing pipelines, crushing innocent people on the highways and other heinous crimes such as ritual killings. Are these not really Harams?
Now back to the Boko Haram phenomenon. I am at lost as to what really the sect intends to achieve by the nature of their struggle. But I am beginning to be highly suspicious of some of the claims attributed to the group. More so, the shallow way supposed claims by its assumed spokespersons are rehashed by people in Government and even the Media. Oftentimes such claims are not even subjected to simple reasoning let alone an in-depth analysis. Let me use two of such recent occurrences to actually illustrate my point.
I come from Adamawa State and reside in Mubi. I took interest in the supposed Boko Haram attack on Gombi Town, which is less than 80 kilometres from Mubi. And because my cousin Ahmed Isa Hassan is the acting Chairman of the Local Government, I was deeply concerned. I went to Gombi to find out what really happened. This was days before the arrest of some of the perpetrators of the attack by security agents and the governor's statement that these were in fact thieves, not Boko Haram.
What happened in Gombi was simply an Armed Robbery attack organized and coordinated by an ex-youth corper who served in UBA Gombi. The Bank happened to be one of the places attacked. The Robbers ate bananas, snacks and drank water in broad daylight in the month of Ramadan when Muslims were fasting. Yet we were led to believe that it was a Boko Haram attack. More laughable was the supposed admission by a spokesperson of the group. How could that be possible? Non-Muslims involved in executing the jihadist agenda of Boko Haram? No way. Something is definitely amiss.
The second incidence was the robbery in Alkaleri. I was on my way to Mubi when I ran into the incident, nearly becoming a victim. Right in front of me a Honda Accord car was intercepted by soldiers at a road block a few minutes after the incident. The boot was full of bullets. Three persons were in the car. All of them were arrested by the soldiers. I stopped and had a brief interaction with both the soldiers and arrested persons. What was apparent from my understanding of the situation was that they were part of the robbery gang that raided the Unity Bank branch in Alkaleri. Perhaps they lost their way and took the main highway instead of the detour taken by the rest of the gang. Those three were all non-Muslims.
The robbers were said have shouted "Allahu Akbar". Did that happen? Even some of the Gombi hoodlums usually shout Allahu Akbar. Are these incidences choreographed by some other interest groups? And to what end is the whole thing? These are critical questions begging for answers from both the Media and the Security agencies. It is too shallow to just attribute every happening to Boko Haram without in-depth investigation.
Having looked at the incidences more closely, is there something in all these beyond the ordinary? Who really is behind some of these occurrences? It is beyond just Boko Haram. Now we have MASSOB claiming intelligence information about Boko Haram and some groups in the Niger Delta are accusing Northern elders of attempts to destabilize the Jonathan Presidency; we need to look beyond Boko Haram for solutions to the current insecurity in Nigeria. Before the late Yar'adua took the bold step to grant Amnesty to Niger Delta militants no one accused Southern leaders of destabilising a northern presidency. Somehow, someone is trying to take the nation for a ride. The Boko Haram is a menace quite alright, but there are many other Harams now masked by the Boko Haram. We need to rip open tear their "corporate" veil.
If our concern is the "haram" in Boko Haram, what are we to do of the several other Harams in the nation? There is governance Haram in Nigeria. There is judicial Haram. There is law enforcement Haram, there is corruption Haram and many, many more Harams in the country. Is there anything we are doing to address these Harams beyond simple sloganeering? Is anyone willing to take the bull by the horn? Is anyone bold enough to step on highly sensitive toes in order to tackle the many glaring Harams bedevilling the Nation?
Take the Jos crisis as an instance. Is it not a shame, 10 years since the crisis started no one has been prosecuted for perpetrating any of the open crimes we witness on daily basis? Are our security agencies so weak that they have been unable or unwilling to apprehend anyone for the serial killings witnessed? What of the Government of Plateau State, have they ever made an effort to bring perpetrators to book? Worst still, the Government, the Media and the Human Rights Groups all end up being biased in reporting occurrences on the plateau. When one group is killed, all government and media apparatus are deployed to the scene, but when some other group is killed the matter is quickly hushed up.
Sajoh wrote from Adamawa State University, Mubi

Original_Search_Title: Wikileaks and the Nation's Tragedy
Headline: Nigeria: Wikileaks and the Nation's Tragedy
Body_Text:
Even before the latest revelations from WikiLeaks on how high level Nigerian public officers serially belittled themselves by 'singing' to American diplomats on important national issues that ordinarily, should have classified or belonged to the innermost recesses of official secrets of the nation; the erstwhile NAFDAC boss and former Information Minister, Dora Akunyili, had given the world an inkling as to why the habit came naturally for her in the recent past.
In the wake of the attempted bombing of an American airline last year over which Farouk Abdul-Mutallab is currently facing trial, it was Akunyili who betrayed the most pathetic manifestation of inferiority complex in all but stripping the accused of his Nigerian citizenship, just to reassure America that Nigeria was not a 'terrorist' state as if anyone had ever suggested it was in the first place.
Were Nigeria a different nation where the rush to appease or to be in the good books of the West has not been turned into a national past-time, her reaction would have been more circumspect like we routinely see in the reaction of the leadership of civilized nations to the travails of their nationals abroad.
Her brief would have been limited to the condemnation of the act in the strongest possible terms. A public official from a 'civilized' nation would have followed by stating that the nation was still following the development and awaiting further facts from the on-going investigation of the whole thing. More importantly, because we are compelled to live the blatant lie that Nigeria was not yet a banana republic, she should have rounded up by stating that she hoped the accused would receive a fair trial and that Nigerian observers would be allowed to attend the trial.
Akunyili did none of these in the immediate aftermath of the botched bombing. Even when it emerged that Farouk's father did what even most American parents would have refrained from doing by alerting their local embassy that his son could have indoctrinated by Middle-Eastern extremists, she was more concerned with reassuring Washington that Nigeria was not a terrorist state.
No one suggested that America become terrorist state overnight just because Timothy McVeigh obliterated the Alfred P. Murrah federal bombing in Oklahoma killing 168 people in revenge for the storming of the headquarters of his religious sect at Waco, Texas, two years earlier, where over eighty followers perished. The same goes for Britain, and the convicted shoe-bomber Robert Reed. These facts were apparently lost on her.
The WikiLeaks revelations were obviously totally in her character, given her antecedents. But this discourse is less about the conduct of a single individual. It ha more to do with the embarrassing betrayal of our trust and the oath of office by scores of Nigerian public officers at different times.
The tragedy, of course, is that worst could still be revealed from the same source in future. There could be even more damaging revelations on how both General Abacha, and Chief Moshood Abiola met their maker, or perhaps even Murtala Muhammad, if the leaks go that far back, like I suspect they could eventually. Again, it does not surprise me that there are already spirited efforts from some quarters to discredit the leaks in typical Nigerian fashion.
But while I agree with the suggestion that all the information from the leaks must pass be rigorously investigated for fairness, I do not share in the haste to discredit their source or the blatant denial that the meetings with American diplomats actually took place. It is not for nothing that Julian Assange - the founder of WikiLeaks was hounded and persecuted in the West. And if America, which is the original source of all the information with all its claims to liberty and the supremacy of the freedom of expression could go to such diabolical extent to halt prevent the publication of the stolen diplomatic cables, there must be some credible facts in them that it does not want to share with the rest of the world. The revelations could obviously be injurious to its national interests.
And such 'national interests' explain why I was so miffed at the disgraceful conduct of our public officers in this sordid affair. Indeed, there are so many adjectives we could use to describe their conduct, but the first one that routinely popped-up in my mind was treason, pure and simple. The second was acute inferiority complex.
With respect to the first; well, it is almost inconceivable to cast even low-level Indian or Chinese officials discussing such extensive details regarding the health and survival of any of their leaders talk less of an entire President with American diplomats unless they happened to be paid agents of the CIA. On that, I think, we have very little margin for error.
Of the second, obviously the WikiLeaks 'singers' simply behaved true to the Nigerian factor; in which case, our reaction to even the most sensitive or important national issue is often compromised by insatiable greed, ethnicity, political consideration, selfishness and the total paucity of genuine patriotism so crucial for the forging of national cohesion and the survival of all nations.
And make no mistake about it: in countries with more discerning views of patriotism and national security, people have been shot for lesser indiscretion. And if at all people were shot for merely being 'accessories to the fact of treason' in the past, passing on vital information on the health of our terminally ill president to a foreign and competing power when the same information could be used for any purpose ranks high in my classification of treason.
I don't know what they teach them at the National Institute for Policy and Strategic Studies (NIPSS) at Kuru, but what is obvious from the conduct of the individuals named in the leaks is not all our elected or appointed officials possess the slightest knowledge on the essential ingredients of international relations.
What the leaks suggest is that in the misguided, or perhaps naïve notion that she was talking to a 'friend'; Akunyili divulged vital state secrets to the American Ambassador who, like the rest of her staff, could easily have been a trained CIA agent, like almost all American diplomats are since the Secret Service was created in Washington D.C. on July 5, 1865 to suppress counterfeit currency. The same 'agents' simply passed on the information to their superiors in the State Department for action in fulfilment of their duties and the defence of their national interests! End of story.
Nigerian officials routinely boast to sundry acquaintances and family that they have 'friends' in high places. And for the pathetic class of Nigerian public officials that emerged especially after character, nationalism and self-esteem in the corridors of power died with Murtala Muhammad; friends don't come higher than the American Ambassador of his side-kicks.
Listing on the protocol list of the American Embassy is the new status symbol to many of our politicians and elites. It ranks next in importance only to the social status assured by the brutal looting of our public treasuries. To this clue-less generation of politicians and public officers, betraying the nation is easy and cheap. To most of them, the nation remains abstraction, or, with apologies to the late sage, Obafemi Awolowo, a mere geographical expression.
The WikiLeaks revelations are very painful for all Nigerians with a streak of patriotism left in them; but as I also told a friend of mine who was a witness to such ugly traits from public officers at close quarters during his recently concluded tour of duty in the presidency, it also explains why the nation remains where it is: at the very base of its immense potentials.

Original_Search_Title: New Queen of Track, Field Is Born
Headline: Zimbabwe: New Queen of Track, Field Is Born
Body_Text:
Growing up admiring the marvellous talents of American starlet, Marion Jones, who elevated basic running to an art form, Ursula Ndlovu has a dream to be a professional athlete.
She also grew up seeing American runner Wilma Rudolph and Zimbabwean-born Julia Sakala excelling in their disciplines and this motivated her to follow their tracks.
Ask any official from Bulawayo province that were part of the technical team in Gwanda during the Youth Games last month and they will tell you that Ndlovu was one of the influential athletes who helped Bulawayo to third position.
Standarsport caught up with the track and field events queen when she had visited her mother in Harare. Clad in a designer gym skirt complemented by a Buddie sleeveless top, Ndlovu (17) sat on her mother's double bed at their one-room cabin in Hatcliffe and curved a banana smile as she took time to admire the latest medals she won at the just ended National Youth Games held in Gwanda last month.
Ndlovu, a Form Four student at Msitheli High School helped the Bulawayo province to third position, seeing it amass a total of 28 medals with the little girl chalking four medals: two gold and two silver.
Ndlovu, an all-rounder, specialises in short distance athletics, long jump with high jump being her favourite. During the just ended event, Ndlovu mined gold in the 400m track event before repeating the same feat in the 4xmake, winning gold again.
She went on to scoop two silver medals in the high jump and the long jump in the Under-20 category. Asked about how all this started, the camera-shy athlete said she started taking part in sporting activities at the age of 15 when she was in Form Two at Mpopoma High School.
Ursula took us down memory lane on how she won some of her medals. "I got my first medal at the National Youth Games that were held in Marondera in 2009 when I won bronze in high jump.
I did not give up as I did the same at the National Youth Games that were held in Harare in 2010 and again it was a bronze medal from high jump. I have already told you that's my favourite of them all," she said.
The young track and field events queen went on a medal gathering spree this year winning gold in the 400m, silver in the 100m races, gold in high jump, another gold in high jump in the Nash Games that were held in Harare before striking gold again in high jump at the second edition of the Nash Games that were held in Bindura.
The four new medals have brought her medals tally to 14. Ndlovu stays with her grandmother in Bulawayo's Pelandaba suburb, together with her brother Edwin (11) who is more into soccer than athletics.
Ursula has already told herself that she has to make a living through sports.
"I am looking forward to continuing with this fine form. I hope that after finishing school I will get sponsorship to other countries where I will have a chance to specialise in my sporting activities as well as my school work.
I am also looking for a club that will assist me in nurturing my talent to become a complete athlete who can compete at the highest level," the soft-spoken athlete said.

Original_Search_Title: Kampala On Its Way to Being a Clean City
Headline: Uganda: Kampala On Its Way to Being a Clean City
Body_Text:
ANYBODY familiar with the term "persistent objector" will agree that it aptly describes President Yoweri Museveni's take on the state of Uganda's capital city, Kampala.
For many years now, the President has relentlessly attacked the perpetual dirt and filth that had come to characterise the capital city; arguing that it simply was not kosher to have a capital city in which marabou storks and big green flies feel perfectly at home and are therefore to be found all over the place.
As marabou storks (locally known as karoli) and those huge greenflies are creatures that are usually to be found in places that are stinking filthy, the message was quite clear: something ought to be done to clean up the capital. And real quick at that. And the President wasn't the only high profile figure to decry the state of the capital ; in which uncollected garbage piles all over the place and many people do not help matters by continually littering, making an already bad situation worse.
A few years ago when renowned American Televangelist Joyce Meyer, known for speaking her mind came to Kampala, she made a remark that was a bit too frank for many people's liking. She described Kampala as dirty and disorganised; unworthy of a second visit and declared that unless it was improved, she would not be back for another visit.
Maybe the biggest problem has been possibly two-fold: lack of strong, strategic and accountable leadership in the city, coupled with a nonchalant society that has come to accept the status quo, unwilling to challenge the trend and happy to go along with matters.
It is arguably the President's persistent objection that culminated into the Constitutional Amendment Act 2005 which stipulated that Kampala would henceforth be administered by the Central Government. This was followed by the Kampala City Authority Act which operationalised the amendment. The city is now managed by an executive director appointed by the President and to be fair, whose coming has brought with it plenty of change.
For all intents and purposes, it would be quite apt for a huge banner or billboard to be put up at every entrance to the city to proclaim that Kampala is now "under new management".
Slowly but surely, the dirt is decreasing; the uncollected garbage is less and less and so is the indiscriminate and disorganised positioning of vendors all over the streets. The entire mess made Kampala a huge risk in terms of sheer health, but also cut its streets out as highly risky for anyone, especially the pedestrians and those on motorbikes - particularly the boda bodas -because the congestion has been so severe that safety was always in doubt with thousands of cars, motorbikes, bicycles, carts and pedestrians all crammed on both street and sidewalk.
The situation exacerbated by vendors who narrow the streets by cramming the sidewalks.
The result of the mess is well documented: regular outbreaks of cholera, food poisoning, high casualty rates of those who move on boda bodass, many of them losing life and limb. Security itself has been a challenge, with many thieves and robbers taking advantage of the mess to relieve people of their property. It has never been easy to police the city in such a situation. Fire outbreaks have always been regular and costly. Environmental degradation has been untold; with pollution very high- the Nakivubo Channel, the city's biggest outflow of water from surface run-off, clogged with all manner of garbage.
In short, the city has been hell for the law-abiding and a perfect haven for those who love mayhem and pandemonium and are comfortable living on the side of the law. With the city "now under new management", firm decisions are now being taken; decisions that are calculated to restore order to the city so that progress is not sacrificed at the altar of populism. Vendors who had previously been on the sidewalk of every street (and in many cases right in the middle of certain streets) have been relocated to gazetted market areas. If no place had been got for them, that would have been totally inhuman because they are also out to take an honest living.
Apart from ensuring order in the city; this is an excellent step to minimise unfair competition in which those who pay expensively to rent shops are shortchanged by vendors who park on street and sidewalk and lap up customers who would be purchasing from shops. I suspect that suggestions to have flea markets - once a week or sobazaars in which, for example, an entire street can be cordoned off and turned into a market for a few hours-will be listened to: after all even cities like New York have them.
All this ties in well with the Anti-litter Bill, a proposed law to maintain a clean environment by criminalising indiscriminate dumping of rubbish and establishing regulations and guidelines for the orderly disposal of the same. The Bill started as a Private Members Bill in the 8th Parliament, but Government is now taking it up and it will soon be presented to Cabinet, before its onward journey to Parliament for debate and enactment.
The Bill in which yours truly has had a hand will not be for the city alone; but across the country, and it is expected that it will help reverse a tradition of indiscriminate disposal of garbage where you find even those in posh cars who you expect have a possibly higher standard of civilised conduct, casually tossing used beer or soda cans, or banana peels through the window as they speed along!
By all indications, all this is just the beginning of a transition towards a cleaner city and country and a population which is environmentally conscious and friendly.

Original_Search_Title: Nansana Is Booming
Headline: Uganda: Nansana Is Booming
Body_Text:
EIGHTY-year-old Janet Najeeno Mujoona has lived in Nansana all her life, but has been awestruck by the changes she has seen in the recent years.
Found in Wakiso district on Hoima Road, Nansana is growing at a supersonic speed. Not so long ago it was a forest with a few residents. Its locals lived like any of our rural folk. Clearly Nansana was a village.
"Nansana was a forest with many fruits like mangoes, guavas and jack fruit. People used to live in houses made of dry banana leaves (ebisoowa). Those who lived in houses made of mud were regarded the rich ones," says Mujoona.
Esero Kizito was the rich man of the time because he owned a bakery in Kibuye that supplied bread to the whole of Kampala. His nickname was Bakery Kizito, explains Mujoona. Kizito was also the first to build a modern bungalow in the area which is still standing to date.
If you visited Nansana today, you will find a place called Nansana Masitoowa and another called Nansana Yesu Amala. The former was named after a man called Ssengooba who gave up his mud and wattle house for a coffee business. Whoever visited Nansana and wanted to trade in coffee or cotton, was sent to masitoowa.
Nansana Yesu Amala, on the other hand was named after a man called Wamala. He was a butcher and whenever people thanked him for his services he would tell them Yesu amala yakola byonna (Jesus is enough he does everything).
In the past, Nansana was believed to be haunted by a ghost called Mayanja which used to appear when the moon had just come up. It is because the ghost used to appear when the moon is out, that King Daudi Chwa called this place Nansana.
Currently, the area has embraced people from all walks of life and as such many buildings have been constructed, both commercial and residential.
Along the main road you find so many storied buildings and off the main road people have built posh bungalows.
Lasto Kizito, 35, of Bakato property consultants Nansana says, it is almost impossible for one to get a plot of land of 100 by 100 feet on the main road. However, if you chanced on one it would cost between sh250m and sh300m. This price goes for all pieces of land in the heart of the town (that is Nabweru Road to Yesu Amala). From Nabweru Road to Lubigi one can get the same piece of land at sh150m.
A small plot of land of 50 by 70 feet on the main road costs between sh70m to sh80m.
When you go 200 metres away from the main road a plot of land of 50 by 100 feet ranges from sh25m to sh40m. Small plots of 60 by 35 feet cost sh13m.
Some years back people used to look at Nansana as very far from the city centre, but this has changed with the Northern Bypass, which has made the place accessible and a busy business hub.
The storied buildings along the main road and Nansana Town Council Authority laws that require people to construct storied building in town have made Nansana town look gorgeous.

Original_Search_Title: Diescho's Omission As ECN Commissioner Confirms Namibia's DNA of Low Expectations
Headline: South Africa: Diescho's Omission As ECN Commissioner Confirms Namibia's DNA of Low Expectations
Body_Text:
PRESIDENT Jacob Zuma of South Africa nominated a few weeks ago the relatively inexperienced Justice Moegoeng Moegoeng as the next Chief Justice of the Constitutional Court.
Zuma's nomination was met with fierce resistance from various quarters of South African society, including opposition parties, university academics, the legal fraternity and the country's largest trade union, Cosatu.
Opinion articles and letters from the country's leading professors and civil society actors dominated small and major newspapers debating the nomination. This led to the Judicial Service Commission subjecting Justice Moegoeng to a publicly televised two-day interview through which eventually the majority of the commissioners gave him the thumbs-up.
is piece on Joseph Diescho's application and omission as commissioner for the Electoral Commission of Namibia (ECN) with a discussion about process in South Africa? The point I seek to highlight here is that while Jacob Zuma may have pushed South Africans to accept a candidate based on his own low expectations of office, progressive forces did not accept Zuma's low expectations to be theirs.
When Joseph Diescho applied to be ECN commissioner, we assumed logically that given his educational qualifications, professional experience and towering role as public intellectual, he would make the final cut as ECN commissioner. Logically, in the eyes of some he ought to have become the chairperson of a body that has come to represent inefficiency and incompetence.
We also assumed (again logically that is) that another suitably qualified candidate Kaijata Kangueehi, a lawyer and former university lecturer, would make the final cut. However, when President Hifikepunye Pohamba chose ECN commissioners less than two weeks ago, these two Namibians were glaringly omitted. Instead, what Pohamba confirmed through his choices are the low expectations that have come to be the hallmark of his own presidency and the country.
We have now also bought into this logic of low expectations to the point of singling out the most mundane of things about him - humility and accessibility. These have less to do with the choices that he makes as a leader than the absence of serious thought that accompanies his choices as is the case of the ECN.
Unlike in South Africa or any other demanding society, university academics, trade unions and other forces of civil society looked at Diescho's omission as a non-event, bar a few SMSs, newspaper reports and non-sharp analysis that relegated this issue to the back pages. Similarly, President Pohamba recently appointed commissioners to the National Planning Commission (NPC).
Alas, the commissioners represent nothing new or refreshing, neither do they represent a healthy mix of ideas and practice. Again, the University of Namibia academics who ordinarily ought to have one of their own on that commission, would not even bother to raise concern about how a National Planning Commission appoints commissioners strictly drawn from sectors other than the University.
What do these things say about us? What lessons are we learning from other countries? Are we a serious state or merely a country satisfied with less and less to the point of sliding into a banana republic? While South Africans and other serious nations would debate the merits of candidates and not necessarily their network in government, the contrary is true of Namibia.
We are a country of low expectations. Since low expectations are part of our DNA, we have accepted a consensus based on the lowest common denominator when it comes to qualifications and fitness for office. We are a country without scrutiny and questions are hardly asked about the qualifications and skill-set of the individuals who are appointed to important positions. There is no hard test when it comes to these as our DNA is less and less interested in what individuals offer to offices and the country.
Diescho's exclusion from the ECN confirms our allergies (now embedded in our DNA) to competent men and women, which are undermining what would otherwise have been a progressive society.
Alfredo Tjiurimo Hengari is a PhD-fellow in political science and researcher at the Centre for Political Research at the University of Paris I Panthéon-Sorbonne, France.

Original_Search_Title: Musicians to Light Up Dar On 50th Independency Anniversary
Headline: Tanzania: Musicians to Light Up Dar On 50th Independency Anniversary
Body_Text:
AT LEAST 40 local musicians will take to the stage at Diamond Jubilee Hall in Dar es Salaam on December 8, in a festival to commemorate the 50th Independence anniversary.
Addressing reporters in Dar es Salaam on Thursday, the Chairperson of the 'Old Music' artists, Waziri Ali, said that the event aims at strengthening cooperation between the 'oldies' and new generation musicians.
He said, at the festival, music artists of the new generation would sing the old songs and the elderly musicians would sing the new generation's songs such as 'Bongo Fleva' so as to add flavour in the music.
"We want to entertain our fans in a special way as we mark the 50 years of independence," he said.
He noted that there was an interpretation in the society that music artists were not working collectively but during the festival, he said, the society would be forced to change their mindset and observe how the musicians in the country were devoted to each other.
The Chairperson of Tanzania Flavour Unit (TFU), Hamis Mwinjuma (Mwana FA), said that the festival among other things would at large extent help them to exchange ideas with the elderly musicians and he said, they expect to learn a lot from them.
"We want to mingle with the elders who will tell us what flavours we are missing in our music compared to their hey days," he said.
Mwana FA added that in commemorating 50 years of independence, music has passed through different stages and it was better for them to go on stage and show their solidarity towards their achievements.
A veteran musician, King Kikii, also urged for more support from their fans and other stakeholders so that their music could be promoted. Banana Zoro said that the festival with a message "50 years old, 50 years youth" encourages people to come out in their numbers to participate because it would be a historical event and would provide fans with a memorable entertainment.

Original_Search_Title: Arusha Wazees' Play Host to Zanzibar House of Representatives
Headline: Tanzania: Arusha Wazees' Play Host to Zanzibar House of Representatives
Body_Text:
THE Arusha Wazee Sports Club is this weekend playing host to more than 100 legislators from the Zanzibar House of Representatives, including more than 10 senior cabinet ministers led by the Speaker of the House Pandu Ameir Kificho who will be there on a sporting and cultural visit.
The visitors who will arrive in Arusha on Thursday are scheduled to visit several institutions and companies including some tourist attractions in and around Arusha. They will visit, among other places, the newly established Nelson Mandela African Institute of Science and Technology as well as the newly established Hughes Motor Factory in Usa River.
The Chairman of the Wazee Club Danford Mpumilwa said yesterday that the delegation will also visit the nearby Tarangire National Park.
Mpumilwa added that the visit will end this Sunday with a sports bonanza at the Club's General Tyre grounds along Njiro road, at which there will be several sporting activities including a football match between the two sides. In the evening there will be a farewell dinner at the same grounds.
The Chairman said that this was an annual visit which takes place soon after the House's annual budget session. It is also part of the annual programme which sees the Wazees visiting Zanzibar during the Easter holidays while the Zanzibaris visit Arusha in early September.
He added that the visit was also part of Arusha's efforts to promote the Union apart from enhancing international relations between Arusha and Tanzania and the international community.
The Arusha Wazee Club has a membership of more than 150 individuals from more than 20 different countries who live, work and do business in Arusha's local and international institutions.
Mpumilwa paid tribute to several individuals and companies who are playing a major role in making the visit a success. These include the Vodacom Tanzania Limited, Tanzania Breweries Limited, Bonite Bottlers, Will Enterprises, Banana Wine, MegaTraders, Hughes Motors, AICC, the EAC Legislative Assembly, TANAPA, the Arusha Times and several individuals including members of the Club.

Original_Search_Title: Safari Marathon 2011 to Dish Out 7m/- in Cash Prizes
Headline: Tanzania: Safari Marathon 2011 to Dish Out 7m/- in Cash Prizes
Body_Text:
Arusha — ORGANISERS of the 'Safari International Marathon,' which takes place on the 18th of September in Arusha City, intend to dish out cash prizes worth 7m/- to the race winners.
The Chairman of the event's organizing committee Chris Honest stated in Arusha that close to 1000 participants are expected to take part in this year's 'Safari Marathon' with 360 of them, mostly non-Tanzanians have already registered for the race online.
The Flagship event for the 2011 Safari Marathon remains the 21 kilometre Half-Marathon for both men and women and the first winner in each category stands to pocket 1m/-, the second winners are to get 600,000/- each while the third winners (men and women) get 300,000/- and those finishing in the 4th and 5th positions stand to earn 100,000/- each from the two categories.
The Half Marathon lap will also attract people with physical disabilities who will tackle the race on wheelchairs and the top winners from both the men and women categories will be rewarded with 400,000/- each, those finishing second are to get 300,000/- each while the third and fourth winners from each categories stand to earn 200,000/- and 100,000/- respectively.
Then there is the corporate challenge race of 5 kilometres to be tackled by local companies, institutions and firms in which the first winner gets 300,000/-, the second winner 200,000/-, the third 100,000/- while the fourth and 5th finishers earn 50,000/- each.
More than ten local primary schools, both private and government owned will participate in the new racing category of Safari Marathon to be known as 'Kids Run' set to cover 5 kilometres' lap along the same route to be undertaken by the 'Family fun race.'
The race co-ordinator Victor Mollel said the Tanzania Tourist Board (TTB) is among the official sponsors of the Safari Marathon being organized under the Ujumbe Ink of Arusha.
Zara Tanzania adventure Safaris of Moshi, Bonite Bottlers also of Moshi, Hughes Motors (Ford), Sunny Safaris and Banana Investments of Arusha are the other supporters.
According to Mollel next year Safari Marathon will include the full 42 kilometres Marathon.
This year, all participants will be awarded with certificates while the top 300 finishers stand to get medals.

Original_Search_Title: Top Athletes for Arusha Marathon
Headline: Tanzania: Top Athletes for Arusha Marathon
Body_Text:
Arusha — Preparations are being finalised for this year's edition of Safari International Marathon which will take place here on September 18.
The race is expected to attract over 1,000 athletes, 360 of whom have already confirmed their participation in the annual event, according to the event coordinator Victor Mollel.
The event intended to promote athletics in Tanzania is being held shortly after the end of 2001 IAAF World Championship in Daegu, South Korea where the country could not even manage a bronze medal.
It is also aimed to inspire young athletes in the northern regions, notably Arusha and Manyara, where most of the star athletes who brought the country fame in the past originate as well as promote the tourism sector.
Mollel said the race would feature at least five events, including a 21km race (half marathon) for the renowned athletes and another 21km race for people with disabilities."In keeping with this competition, we welcome the participation of disabled athletes in the Wheel Chair Marathon (21km). A push rim wheelchair competition will begin immediately after the normal race," he said.
There will also be a five kilometre Corporate Challenge where teams from companies, associations and various institutions will compete for prizes mainly in order to enhance strong teamwork and company spirit.
Two other events to feature in the competition are the Family Run and Kids Competition under which groups of family members and kids will run for five- kilometre each, explained Mollel, who is the sales and marketing director of the Arusha Ujumbe Ink Limited which publishes travel journals.
"Kids competition has been established primarily to provide motivation for young children into athletics, encourage a community-based health and fitness programme to children who would otherwise have no access to regular physical activity", he added.
He pointed out that children needed tools to become physically fit for life, while also incorporating goal-setting, character building and education and added that the race would be graced by live music, barbecue and drinks.
Most of the events, including the half marathon and Wheel Chair Marathon, will start and end at the Sheikh Amri Abeid Stadium in the heart of the town, while the Corporate Challenge and Family Run/Kids Competition will start and end at the adjacent Arusha Declaration roundabout.
According to Christostom Honest, the organising committee chairman, registration fee for those intending to take part in the event is Sh3,000 for local residents, Sh10,000 for those from the other East African countries and foreign residents with permits, and $ 50 for non-residents.
He urged those interested in participating in the race to register on-line even a day before the event takes place on Sunday September 18, since experience has shown that in such events the competitors turn out in the last minutes.
"They can register on-line by going to the website" he said, adding that preparations were on course.. After validation they will receive i on where they can pay and collect their running bibs", he said.He mentioned the Tanzania Association of Tour Operators, the Tanzania Tourist Board (TTB), Ujumbe Ink Ltd, Moshi-based Zara Tours and Adventure, Bonite Bottlers also of Moshi (through Coca Cola drink), Hugh Motors, Sun Safari and Banana Investments as major sponsors.
The winners will get cash prizes ranging from Sh1 million to Sh50,000 while the first 300 finishers for the half marathon race will be awarded with a medal each.The inaugural Safari International Marathon took place here last year and attracted 506 participants, including 24 from outside Africa."The project is divised to be on-going and become the most popular event amongst young and old athletes and all East Africans in general", he pointed out.

Original_Search_Title: Cota Students Perform Backstage
Headline: Namibia: Cota Students Perform Backstage
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Students from the College of the Arts (Cota) staged an impressive performance at the courtyard of the college last Wednesday evening to expose the achievement of young stage artists.
They pulled in a mixed audience with a distinctive taste in music and drama dancing non stop for a whole hour. Soon after their introduction on stage, the students of the African Performance Art (APA) took matters into their own hands and wowing the fans with energetic, calculated and inspirational performances.
Head of Department (HOD) of APA, Angelika Schroeder, explains that their main reasons for such an event like was to expose the students on stage rather than in classroom.
"We believe that the more students perform on stage the more they will become skilled and professional," says Schroeder.
Apart from music and dancing, students also made many fantasies. One of the pretenses focused on a tribal marriage. "To come up with this drama is to educate people more about the intermarriage and to encourage them to know one another and how their families are connected first before marriage," said Dominic Hunenge one of the organizer and lecture of the students
Esmeralda Cloete, one of the students on stage, says the college is an awesome place and she feels happier performing live on stage. "As we are in a group and first year students we have major as Dancer, Music and Actress but at the end we end up doing one thing," Cloete says.
Natax Natanael, Fisher Spangy and Hileni Namalambo, some members of the audience says they couldn't believe it seeing thee students performing the way they did, which makes them stress free. The event was organised Schroeder, Hunenge, Banana Shekupe, Ishmael Sam, Anne-Gretha Nyhaug, Goran Bogstrand, Beatrix Munyama and Silvester Biwa.

Original_Search_Title: Bus Accidents Kill 10, Leave Scores Injured
Headline: Uganda: Bus Accidents Kill 10, Leave Scores Injured
Body_Text:
The mood was sombre for relatives and eyewitnesses at Ahakikoregi Trading Centre in western Uganda yesterday, following two fatal road accidents on the Mbarara-Kabale highway and Mbarara-Bushenyi road.
While wild pacing gripped the corridors at Kabale Regional Referral and KIU Ishaka Hospitals as medical workers administered first aid to victims of the accidents.
The police in the two areas said the accidents killed 10 passengers and left scores injured.
In the first accident that claimed nine lives on Mbarara-Kabale highway, two UK nationals-Ms And Sue and Mr Peter Wordford, were among the survivors after a bus they were travelling in collided with a speeding lorry carrying 50 people and banana to Rubare market in Ntungamo district.
"It's completely a bad day seeing people dying early this morning. I hope we shall improve," Mr Wordford said at Kabale Hospital.
Ms Evelyn Atuhaire and Gilbert Byarugaba, both survivors, blamed the bus driver for the accident.
Kabale traffic boss Anthony Kasolo attributed the accident to reckless driving.
"The Akamba bus was bound for Kigali from Kampala and tried to overtake another lorry at a sharp corner before it had a head-on collision with the lorry," Mr Kasolo said.
Mr Kasolo said all the dead were traders heading for Rubare market in Ntungamo for banana and were all seated in the cabin of the lorry.
He said all the injured passengers were admitted to Kabale Hospital.
Bushenyi accident
In the second accident, a tour of Queen Elizabeth did not come to pass for 14-year-old Gabriel.
Ndyanabo died instantly while 39 others from St Mukasa Preparatory Seminary in Sheema District survived when the driver of the bus they were travelling in lost control and overturned into a valley.
The pupils were destined for Queen Elizabeth National Park for a study tour.
The Regional Traffic Police Officer South western, Mr Donasiano Opwonya, said the injured were rushed to Kampala International Teaching Hospital in Bushenyi.
Mr Opwonya said the school had hired a bus belonging to Maryhill High School and the driver was trying to enter the main road but failed to brake and overturned several times before plunging into the valley.
He said by the time of the accident, 69 people were aboard the bus comprising 60 pupils and 9 teachers.
The school nurse was admitted incritical condition.

Original_Search_Title: National Beauty Contest to Showcase Collections Today
Headline: Tanzania: National Beauty Contest to Showcase Collections Today
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — Fashion and style are element of beauty that by all standards are inseparable.
With the Seprember 12 finals looming, Miss Tanzania 2011 contestants will tonight prove this point as they showcase different collections from different designers at the Karimjee Hall in Dar es Salaam.
The event will also feature New York based designer Rosemary Kokuhilwa aka fashion Junkie who has conducted various fashion shows worldwide.
Speaking to The Citizen early on in the week, the Redds brand manager Victoria Kimaro said that Redds as a brand believes in giving contestants the opportunity to showcase their talent because this is what Miss Tanzania has always been about.
"This concert will enable contestants to meet with various experts of the industry for such as designers, fashion institutions and marketing companies who can discover contestants' hidden abilities," she said.
Kimaro added that fashion as an upcoming sector has had a great impact greater impact in the lives of former beauty pageants as some have turned out to become successful models.
Today's event will feature musical performances from Banana and Channel O's award nominee Shaa.

Original_Search_Title: Carjackings, Robberies On the Rise in Nairobi
Headline: Kenya: Carjackings, Robberies On the Rise in Nairobi
Body_Text:
Eleven suspected gangsters were last week killed by police and six firearms recovered.
According to security firms monitoring crime trends in the country, there has been an average of about ten carjackings in the city daily. Cases of burglary is high in residential areas of Runda, Rosslyn and Lavington.
Three robbery incidents were reported on August 24. One motorist was attacked by an armed gang along Mombasa road and robbed of his car. On the same day, a taxi driver was robbed of his car in Thome Estate. A businessman was also shot dead in South B by armed gangsters who had been trailing his car as he drove to a female companion's residence. A member of a gang that had carjacked a matatu driver in Dandora and robbed him was lynched by a mob. His accomplices escaped.
Two other suspected gangsters who robbed pedestrians in Sunton Estate, Kasarani were shot dead and a toy pistol, stolen mobile phones and wallets recovered.
On August 25, two suspected gangsters were shot dead in Lavington Estate and two others arrested but their accomplice escaped. A firearm was recovered. Two others had been arrested the previous day in Runda Estate after they jumped into a compound with the intention of stealing. On August 22 gangsters broke into a house in the highly secured Rosslyn Estate and stole household goods
Security firm Senaca East Africa communications officer Paul Macharia says the trend is worrying because armed criminals are now moving in large numbers to overpower police and security guards. "Over the past three weeks, burglars moving in a group of over ten have been targeting residences in Banana area on a daily basis," Macharia said

Original_Search_Title: The Plight of Widows
Headline: Cameroon: The Plight of Widows
Body_Text:
In Cameroon, widows are often go through to humiliating and degrading rituals to prove their innocence in their husband's death.
Tradition in many parts of Cameroon dictates that there is no such thing as natural death. Widows are therefore subjected to various rituals to determine whether or not they are responsible for their husband's death. Elise Makouintcheu went through such an ordeal in November 2010.
At the end of the two-month period, she was taken to a stream for a ritual bath. "I felt very humiliated, because I had to walk naked through the village to get to the stream. It was at daybreak but there were still people in the streets," Elise recounts.
After the bath and several rituals, the grieving widow was asked, by one of her sisters-in-law, to urinate in the presence of everyone in order to prove her innocence in her husband's death. "My bladder was blocked because of the stress and the crowd. I only managed to do it an hour later," Elise recounts.
Abuse
Anne Marie Ewolo, who comes from another part of the country, was also put through a widowhood rite earlier this year. When her husband died, she was made to lie on the floor next to the corpse, for three days. She made sure to grieve and appear sad throughout the ordeal, for fear of being suspected of having played a part in her spouse's death.
After the funeral, she was taken to a river, stripped naked and covered in mud, with a banana tree trunk on her head. Along the way, she was repeatedly hit by her sisters-in-law, who blamed her for their brother's death.
After the bath, the in-laws forced her to have sexual relations with one of her brothers-in-law. She was then shaved and made to wear a white boubou (a traditional African garment). She is still not allowed to wear clothes of any other colour, remarry, have sexual relations, use beauty products or wear jewellery.
The duration of this period of abstinence, which is usually between to three years, is often at the discretion of the in-laws.
No rights for the widow
Widowhood rites vary greatly. But they share the tendency of being degrading and humiliating in nature. Educated and financially independent women are less willing to submit to such rituals. However, women who are ignorant of their rights or have no income are often victims of financial blackmail by the family-in-law. Some widows have been disowned and expelled from the marital home.
