If it has to be my decision, then I would say let's play at dear old Rufaro, with 40 000 screaming fans, let's make the Liberians feel very much away from home, and let's make full use of the artificial surface and our 12th man, the fans.
Yeah, there are concerns about security at Rufaro but if the tickets are pre-sold and fans are asked to come to the ground at the right time, rather than everyone coming at 2pm, then we can eliminate the organizational nightmare.
The Return Of Chitembwe And Pasuwa's Graduation
It felt really good to see Lloyd Chitembwe back on the bench, at his beloved CAPS United, and I was one of the privileged witnesses to his homecoming at Gwanzura.
He is a good coach and you can't really say he failed in his first spell, when he took CAPS United to the final of the BancABC Sup8r Cup, and was kicked out just before the final.
He has always loved Makepekepe, having won three league titles with the club to become the most decorated player ever at the Green Machine - better than Shacky Tauro, better than Joel Shambo, better than Stanford Mtizwa.
I just hope the CAPS United fans can give him time to build his team in his fashion and, even if he fails to bring all three points from the Colliery, just pile the support behind him.
Callisto Pasuwa also made the graduation to a head coach and he started with a bang after leading Dynamos to a 4-1 win over Shooting Stars.
Good start but that's what Lloyd Mutasa did exactly in his first competitive match when he led Dynamos to a 4-1 win over MC Alger in a Champions League tie.
We all felt he was the Messiah before it all started going downhill.
But Pasuwa knows that there are things that define what is Dynamos and one of them is the team's way of starting a game by praying across the goalline.
It's their identity as a team and it's good that Pasuwa has restored that and, if you check, you will find that even TP Mazembe have copied that and are now praying across the goalline before matches.
A Man Called Takaendesa Jongwe
It has been all tough going for Maxwell Takaendesa Jongwe who found the responsibility that comes with coaching CAPS United just a touch too tough a mission and left without winning a game.
He has suddenly become the subject of some sick jokes.
I felt for Jongwe on Saturday as the CAPS United fans rained all sorts of insults on the poor coach and, to his credit, he didn't respond by pointing a finger at them.
Instead, he just clapped his hands.
I don't believe Jongwe is a bad coach and there is no reason why the media should treat him as an outcast simply because he failed at find the winning formula at CAPS United.
He is an honest man, hardworking if you want to know, and he took a chance and it failed to work.
We should not pile the agony on the man but let him rebuild his career at Kiglon, where he seems to get it right all the time, and Jongwe needs the same support, from the media, that goes to coaches like Pasuwa, Chunga and Chitembwe.
Yes, he failed at CAPS United but that should not be the end of the road and let's not turn him into a villain because he is not the first, and might not be the last coach, to fail at the Green Machine.
Many have walked away from the Green Machine or Dynamos, after having been deemed failures, and gone on to rebuild their careers elsewhere, including successful ones, like is the case with Rahman Gumbo.
Come on Jongwe, you can rise and shine again.

Original_Search_Title: Positives And the Pitfalls for EPL's Top Four
Headline: Kenya: Positives And the Pitfalls for EPL's Top Four
Body_Text:
The English Premier League season has started with a bang. There were mixed results for different clubs but Manchester City and Bolton stood out after a 4-0 thumping of their opponents.
Manchester United's win over West Brom was clouded by worries about David de Gea. Some say he is suffering from early season jitters and will get better. There's no doubt his performance did not reassure the Red Devil faithful. Despite the criticism, he has received the backing of some. They compare him to Dane Peter Schmeichel. Any comparison methinks is premature. The Spaniard has to prove he's a top goalkeeper on the field. No amount of off- field public relations campaigns will wipe out his short-comings. I have a gut feeling he will struggle. I reckon
Ferguson will be content to rely on his defensive players to limit any damage. Andre Villas-Boas got a first taste of the EPL as a full time manager. I am sure it was not the welcome he anticipated. He complained about Stoke City's hard tackling and rued his teams' missed chances. Chelsea played well with Fernando Torres winning praise for his determination on the field. The challenge for Villas-Boas will be to keep all his experienced top players happy. Already Nicolas Anelka is unhappy about not being involved in the Stoke City game. Solomon Kalou who has always been overlooked will now have to fight for playing time with Romelu Lukaku who hasbeen dubbed the next Didier Drogba. Boas' still faces a big challenge in midfield.
His predecessor Carlo Ancelotti missed a playmaker last season and his inability to groom Josh McEacharan means Villas-Boas has to sign a tried and tested creative midfielder. After four attempts to sign a top striker in the calibre of Carlos Tevez, it seems Manchester City have finally found a gem. Sergio Aguero proved he's worth the hype. He was mesmerising on his debut and his eye for goal was nothing short of admirable. Roberto Mancini is probably the most envied manager in the world. Like a spoilt kid he gets what he asks for. With Samir Nasri reportedly joining the Sky Blues, Mancini faces a squad selection headache. If Carlos Tevez stays, will he play ahead of Dzeko, Aguero and Balotelli? David Silva has cut a niche for himself as Man City's playmaker will Mancini risk limiting the Spaniard's influence in favour of Samir Nasri? Because of Nasri's experience he is most likely to get the nod ahead of Adam Johnson, who I reckon is England's next big name. What will be the implication of these squad selections? Some will say it is a good problem to have but it will only remain good if Manchester City continue winning.
Finally, everyone is talking about Arsenal's disintegration. It is really difficult to see how the club will survive the exits of Cesc Fabregas and Samir Nasri without able replacements. It's clear from last season there were shortcomings that needed to be addressed but Arsene Wenger does not seem to be in a hurry to sign players. He seems to have unshakeable confidence in his squad which many fans do not share. With a potential 'banana skin' tie against Udinese in the Champions League second-leg qualifier and big games against Liverpool and Manchester United, it will be interesting to see if fans will still remain faithful to the "Arsene Knows" slogan at the end of this month.

Original_Search_Title: Unfolding Business Opportunities in South Sudan
Headline: Tanzania: Unfolding Business Opportunities in South Sudan
Body_Text:
On the July 9, 2011 the world witnessed the birth of the newest nation on earth. This is South Sudan which saw the light of the day as an independent nation following the Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) with the Khartoum Government way back in 2005. The birth of this new nation among other things, unveils a number of opportunities for those within and outside South Sudan.
The author of this column is currently in Juba South Sudan for a short term assignment on building the capacity of the ministry of Finance and Economic Planning of the Government of South Sudan.
As part of his individual social responsibility, he would like to humbly share with the readers of this weekly column some of the potential business opportunities in the newly born South Sudan.
Governance institutions
As the new nation gets built amidst the background of war, there are many governance institutions, structures, systems, policies, strategies and positions that will need to be developed. Few existing ones will need to be improved in various ways. Given the rather very limited quantity and quality of sons and daughters of South Sudan to undertake these overwhelming functions one sees opportunities for outsiders including Tanzanians.
The need for advisory, supportive, technical assistance, mentoring and coaching positions as part of capacity building and enhancement in South Sudan should be foreseen by enterprising minds. For institutions and individuals who are willing and able to tap such opportunities, it is a matter of acting and acting before it is too late.
Physical infrastructure
Parallel to building institutions of governance the Government of South Sudan will need to build a number of physical infrastructure. These include roads, bridges, railways and power supply. Keeping in mind that South Sudan is larger than Uganda, Rwanda and Burundi put together, the kilometres of roads that have to be built in this country with huge petro-dollar potential are colossal and provide huge opportunities. Other badly needed infrastructure is in the area of real estate for public and private sector.
A mere observation in Juba clearly shows huge needs for buildings for public sector in form of government offices. Some offices are currently in prefabricated houses and need replacement soon. Government officers still stay in hotels due to accommodation problems.
There is huge demand for accommodation for private/residential market as well as offices. There are foreseen individual as well as corporate movers and shakers world will be locating to South Sudan to tap the economic potentials of this seemingly sleeping giant. Cost of accommodation in Juba is not below $100$ (over Sh150,000) per night for a very normal hotel.
This is a hotel that would have cost not more Sh40,000 in Dar es Salaam. The figures tell it all about deficits in this market where the supply is much short of demand despite of rather rapid construction works.
The infrastructure sector therefore provides myriad of opportunities. They range from designing; supply of inputs like manpower of various levels, building equipment and materials like cement and steel; actual implementation of construction work and many more as limited by imaginations and the sky.
Contractors who feel squeezed out by larger and more powerful competitors in Tanzania and indeed in other countries may get a relief in South Sudan where the market does not seem to be saturated.
Small and big businesses
In Juba, small businesses including kiosks, restaurants, hotels, water selling, secretarial services including Internet cafes are dominated by small entrepreneurs from Kenya, Uganda, Ethiopia, Eritrea and China. The latter are also in rather big businesses such as in Juba Beijing Hotel. Banks from Kenya such as Kenya Commercial Bank and Equity Bank have already been on the ground in Juba long before the independence of South Sudan.
One wonders where Tanzanians are. It is known that Exim Bank of Tanzania is present in Eritrea but no Tanzania bank has extended its tentacles to Juba. Services like insurance, microfinance, consultancies and many others are still short of supply in relation to demand for the same. Researchers and advisors in the dynamics of internationalization process of small and medium size enterprises (the author of this article included) have a lot to study in this area.
Supplying basic goods
Most basic goods including foodstuffs like rice, maize, beans as well as banana are imported to Juba mainly from Uganda and Kenya. This also applies for textiles, beer, soap and other basics. Hotels, guest houses, restaurants, bars as well as camping sites are mostly owned by Kenyans and Ethiopians.
The only commodity of Tanzanian origin that was observed in some Juba shops is Azam maize flower. Besides this 'made in Tanzania' commodity one can spot some Maasai herbal medicine traders in Juba. But it cannot be said for sure that Maasai entrepreneurs are from Tanzania as they might be originating from Kenya or Uganda as well. Back in 2006 there were some onions from Mang'ola valley in Karatu on sale in Juba. If and when South Sudan joins the East African Community (EAC), the business opportunities will be more magnified given the common market status of EAC. But it will only benefit those who make themselves ready.
Implications for Tanzania
The private sector generally has to identify, scan and scope the opportunities and share with others as partly done in this article. The sharing can also be done in seminars, workshops and business meetings.
It only needs courage, aggressiveness, boldness and confidence to benefit from the uncountable unfolding opportunities in South Sudan. At the level of the government, it would be a good idea to set up a Tanzanian representation on ground in Juba with a focus on inter alia, economic diplomacy to identify and concretize the many unfolding opportunities in this great virgin land of South Sudan. .
The author is a lecturer at Mzumbe University Business School

Original_Search_Title: Two Armed Gangsters Shot Dead in Kiambu
Headline: Kenya: Two Armed Gangsters Shot Dead in Kiambu
Body_Text:
Two armed gangsters were on Wednesday night shot dead by police at Ruaka in Kiambu.
James warui, a taxi driver at Ngara, said a woman hired him to pick up a patient at Banana in Kiambu and take him to to Nazareth Hospital. When they reached Mucatha, a kilometre away from Banana , they picked a man and proceeded to Banana where they picked up two more men. Warui said the woman had not told him they would pick other people on the road. He said one of the men they picked immediately drew a gun and pointed it at him and ordered him to move to the back seat. "He ordered me to sit at the back and threatened to shot me if I raised the alarm," Warui said. The thugs went round Banana area for about three hours.
After the vehicle ran out of fuel, Warui said the thugs were picked by a colleague. "I went to Karuri police station and reported the matter where the police acted fast and waylaid the gangsters," he said. Kiambu police boss Samuel Mukindia said his officers received a tip off from the public that there were suspicious people in Banana village who were going round with a vehicle since morning. "We got a tip off from the members of the public of suspicious characters who had been driving around the town for hours and we pursued the matter," Mukindia said
He said t the police caught up with the vehicle which sped off after two people alighted. The officers, who were in plain clothes, approached the two men. One of whom drew a gun prompting the officers to shot them down. "My officers were prompted to shot down the two men after one of them drew a pistol," Mukindia said. Mukindia however said that police have launched on a manhunt for two men and a woman who who fled and asked the members of the public to report to any police station of suspicious people and vowed to fight on crime in kiambu area adding that the law will catch up with the culprits.

Original_Search_Title: Psychology And Sociology of Looting
Headline: Uganda: Psychology And Sociology of Looting
Body_Text:
I was in my early teens when Uganda suffered its first bout of industrial scale looting. I was barely out of my teens when the next happened and, within a year, a potential third one was nipped in the bud when the conquering armies of the NRA strictly forbade looting as they swept to power in January 1986.
The teenagers rampaging all over the British cities of London, Birmingham, Liverpool, Nottingham and Manchester last week brought back depressing memories of our own social breakdown.
When it first happened in Kampala on April 12, 1979, it was a completely new social phenomenon. We did not even have a local word for it, and so it became luutiingi, such was its newness. When it broke out, one day after the Tanzanian People's Defence Forces (Jeshi la Wanainchi) and the guerrillas of the Uganda National Liberation Front chased Idi Amin from Kampala, I witnessed it first hand.
At the backend of our home was a property with a customs bonded warehouse. Somehow the local folk managed to break into it, and through the fence my family saw them cart away sacks of sugar and drive out brand new Massey Fergusson tractors and Honda Civic salon cars (those days Uganda imported only brand new cars). As the looting intensified, our dad, a man of stern moral rectitude, told us to keep away, "because a Government commission of inquiry would be instituted into the looting, and the culprits punished", or words to that effect.
We heeded the warning, and opted instead to go into town, on a Datsun pickup vehicle with our uncle, the hotelier Edward Muwaguzi, to take in the euphoria of freedom from Amin's chains and the sudden (free) availability of hitherto scarce things. I still recall seeing a citizen emerge struggling with a huge black sofa from UCB head office (Uganda Commercial Bank, now Cham Towers), and heading towards Kisenyi under incredibly back-breaking weight. Others raided Winits, the winemaker in Industrial Area, while close by many consumed acids and laboratory concoctions, to fatal effect.
The next paroxysms of looting happened in July 1985. On the morning of Saturday July 27, I left home for Kololo to pick my buddy Sam Mugoya for that afternoon's league game between Express and KCC FC at Nakivubo. I had just crossed the Kitante golf course when, out of the blue, shooting began as helicopter gunships criss-crossed the Kololo-Nakasero neighbourhood.
I run with another man, crouching and panting along Lower Kololo Terrace, and the two of us just managed to make it into the house as the Mugoya family locked themselves in. I later learnt that the gentleman was the Magistrate (and later-to-be Justice) Tabaro. The military coup that overthrew Milton Obote was unfolding right before our eyes; the looting took three days.
Deprivation versus Depravity
Without excusing it, the Ugandan looting of '79 and '85 was borne of being deprived - it came off a broken down economy, where essential and non-essential things were simply not there. But last week's British looting seems to be more about depraved minds - a moral breakdown, a culture of selfishness and greed by young people for things they have not worked for, and a failure by parents to rein in the behaviour and expectations of hot-blooded youngsters. What explains the torching of a building that you have looted anyway? Why burn it? It's depravity.
The English mobs seemed to have lists of things they want - they tweeted and Face-booked where to meet and loot. And they were all 'cheap' material things - sneakers and flat-screen TVs, jewellery and perfumes. It is said that in Clapham Junction, South West London, all shops were looted except the bookstore Waterstones. It would be like raiding Win-a-Classic, Ssebaggala & Sons, Bata, and Master Electronics, but not touching Aristoc.
There's an account of a girl who stole a TV, could not sleep because of guilt, and reported herself to police; the mother of a 24-year-old university graduate talks of her daughter, "she didn't want a TV. She doesn't even know why she took it." It is the mob; it is the herd instinct, the kind that could have seen me participate in 1979, or the type that you see at staff retreats, when the quietest guy in office shocks workmates with a crazy dance.
British teenagers are not in need; they just want and demand. I am a little familiar with Tottenham, where the riots began - I have eaten at The Banana Tree restaurant, run by a Ugandan called Baguma; I have worshipped at St. Anne's, the church John Wood, my former teacher at Budo, still pastors; I do visit, on the odd occasion am in town, the shopping district of Wood Green to admire the electronics and buy a couple of books. Tottenham is not exactly a deprived place - it is covetous teenagers brought up in a culture of entitlement that prowl its streets. Last week, they shopped with violence.
If my childhood memory serves me right, we never had iron bars on shop fronts before 1979 - the luutiingi changed all that. Ugly burglar proofing and moral degeneration are the scars of our material deprivation. For England, who knows what the scars of moral depravity will be. But in both Uganda and the UK, looters' short-term gain will lead(s) to long-term consequences.

Original_Search_Title: Scrap Secrecy Bill, Defiant Marchers Tell SA Govt
Headline: South Africa: Scrap Secrecy Bill, Defiant Marchers Tell SA Govt
Body_Text:
As the Protection of Information Bill (aka Secrecy Bill) is on the brink of putting South Africa at the same level with secretive societies such as China, North Korea and various African banana republics, the entire civil society united under Right2Know remains defiant in the face of repression.
An estimated 800 protesters marched to the Constitution Hill in Johannesburg over the weekend to tell the ANC-led government: "stop and scrap it."
"We reject the bill in its current form and we have come here to say that no democracy can flourish under the veil of secrecy," Congress of South African Trade Unions (COSATU) president Sdumo Dlamini told protestors gathered at Wits University in Braamfontein on Saturday, 13 August 2011.
COSATU is a partner in the ANC tripartite alliance, but the organisation has been very critical of vast seas of corruption currently streaming through certain government and ruling party's circles.
Create a culture of impunity
Critics worry that the proposed legislation will institutionalise and worsen corruption, and create a culture of impunity as perpetrators will certainly not be held accountable due to the 'unfair' classification of incriminating information.
Under the bill, corruption whistleblowers will be criminalised. And any media who publishes any classified information, or any ordinary citizen found in possession of any classified document, will be jailed for up to 25 years.
Media analysts say the legislation will curtail free reporting and almost kill investigative journalism a shameful end for a country often labelled Africa's media freedom haven.
Challenge to all sectors of society
Former intelligence minister Ronnie Kasrils told protesters the bill was not only a challenge for the media, but to all sectors of society.
Wits University Prof Franz Kruger said limiting the flow of information will not transform the media, but extending the flow of information to citizens will help communities to be well-informed and be able to openly discuss the issues of the day. Other speakers included Nobel Prize Literature winner Nadine Gordimer and Mail & Guardian newspaper editor-in-chief Nic Dawes.
But the SA government, which seems to be saying to its people that dura lex sed lex (the law is harsh but it is the law), shows no sign of listening to the masses and compromising on the repressive legislation, which the ANC-dominated ad-hoc parliamentary committee wants to finalise by 23 September this year.
Struggle only beginning
And on Saturday, angry and frustrated protesters stated clearly that their struggle against neo-repression and neo-apartheid policies was only beginning.
Carrying placards that read, "we have the right to know", "Free press = Free SA", "Speak now or be gagged forever", "No information means no accountability" and "25 years in jail for uncovering corruption", black, white and Indians,young and old marched in the streets of Braamfontein in harmony.
They sang revolutionary songs and mocked the government, sending a message of war and defiance to the Zuma administration and his ruling ANC.
Addressing protestors at the Constitution Hill, Right2Know coordinator Siphiwe Segodi said: "We are saying that, if this bill is not radically changed in order to address what needs to be addressed, we will be left with no choice but to take it here."
As he spoke, the Constitutional Court stood silent and reflective, waiting for D-Day, when it will serve as the ultimate platform to rule on the constitutionality of the contested legislation.

Original_Search_Title: Crafting a Living From Artwork
Headline: Uganda: Crafting a Living From Artwork
Body_Text:
Kampala, Uganda — Gone are the days when one needed a lot of capital to start up a business. Of recent, innovation is what it takes.
The biggest resource that Hassan Kato an art designer needs is not capital as it is always for entrepreneurs but time.
"With the nature of art business, it needs a lot of attention in order to come out with a piece that is appealing to the customers. The raw materials are cheaply got but time is my biggest constraint because I do the shading manually", said Kato.
Ink, paint, pencils, wood, clay, paper and at times banana fibers are his key raw materials.
Operating in the period when almost every thing is expensively bought, his products donot come cheap either. Kato solely depends on this business for a living.
"In order to match my expenditures, am always forced to raise my charges especially when the prices for necessities rise. It's through this business that I sustain my family", he explained.
He started off by selling small drawings as sketches of human beings at a small price as UG shs 200 (less than $0.1) but later as he got experience he started dealing in other products as flower vessels, designing posters and mottos for businesses and schools.
He now earns about 1000,000 ($ 385) per year.
Kato has no specific customers that he targets but whoever picks interest in his art pieces.
"I have been hired to paint posters for institutions such as schools and companies but still I also paint for small shops and saloons around my area of residence. I don't segregate among customers. I can work for who ever negotiate a good price", he added.
In addition to dealing in art pieces, Kato is always assigned to design people's compounds and at times designing clothes and school/ company budges.
Juliet Nanzirri has also ventured into the same art business.
Being located near a wild life center, she targets a variety of customers ranging from foreigners to students from all over Uganda who always tour the place.
The foreigners usually prefer buying in dollars to the local shilling.
The Dollar rate constraints in the country have made Nanziri go through tough times as it has always been hard for her and her other colleagues to identify and brand a stable price to their works.
"When selling to foreigners, we can't establish a uniform price yet we almost show case the same pieces since we are both nature artists because of the fluctuating dollar. This has constrained us because the foreigners prefer buying in dollars", said Allan Mugarurra Juliet's work mate.
However, economic analysts believe that for any business to prosper there is always an explanation of either a prior loss or any other constraint. The challenges within the business such as the low earnings cannot scare a way one from turning his/her innovative idea into reality.

Original_Search_Title: Mission Accomplished
Headline: Tanzania: Mission Accomplished
Body_Text:
As we all lined up to board the newly painted coaster bus (decked out in Panther blue and black with the bright orange Leaders of Tomorrow Children's Home logo painted on either side) everybody's excitement was apparent.
This was a real dream come true! I recalled the first day that the bus had been driven into the back space at United African Alliance Community Center (UAACC) campus looking a little dull with the drab original paint; the days following when the engine was being overhauled; the seats were being covered with bright blue Maasai shuka cloth; the banging hammers of the panel beaters and the strong scent of the metallic paint that would drift into the Red Onion as we would eat lunch. I recalled how my husband, UAACC founder Mzee Pete O'Neal , would take his chair and sit out in the banana grove watching the progress of the fundis as the dream got closer to being realized day by day.
And now the day had arrived! The day of the maiden voyage of The BUS!!
Mzee Pete called out, "George Bush said on the deck of that aircraft carrier that their mission was accomplished! Well, that wasn't true. But today, right now, we can say it and it is a fact! Come on Y'all! Get on board! Our MISSION IS ACCOMPLISHED!"
What an exciting day it was sure to be. The evening before I had been surprised by a party that was held in my honor and UAACC Operations Manager, Mzee Christopher Nnko ( "a real magician at making the impossible possible") for the work we had done for The BUS!!
"Get That BUS! Get that BUS!" still echoed in my mind! That chant had been my rallying cry for the four months that I was in the states for the Heal the Community Tour 2011, traveling from the west coast to the east coast and so many cities in between singing, performing my spoken word and speaking about our work at the UAACC; working almost nonstop, determined to raise funds to get a reconditioned coaster bus that would allow us to transport our 22 children living at Leaders of Tomorrow Children's Home at UAACC in comfort and safety!
It had been a dream of Mzee Pete to 'take the children to see the Indian Ocean one day' and as I braved the snow and sub zero temperatures during the first part of the tour and later the crazy heat and humidity that engulfed so many cities before I left to come back home in June, I kept that vision always in my head!
"Get That Bus! Get That Bus!" could be heard at the community events that I spoke at as people literally sometimes passed a hat collecting a dollar here; a hundred dollars there and sometimes checks in four figure amounts! It was like the old days in the 60s when community would come together as a force of love and make things happen despite all kinds of adversities like lack of money!
And now that I think of it, many of the people who helped organize the fund raisers and speaking engagement were actually 60s people; Panther comrades and community activists who despite advancing age still had that youthful vim and vigor and vision of nation building.
As we drove out the gate that morning, I marveled at how the engine purred like a satisfied cat. I held back tears of joy as the children excitedly sang song after song and chattered back and forth as we journeyed toward the Meserani Snake Park for an afternoon spent learning about some of the fascinating creatures there; stuffing ourselves with hot spicy samosas and riding the slow moving camels!
It had been a long day and hours later we were all tired and ready for the return journey home. I dozed off into a dream and that rallying cry "Get that Bus!" swirled around in my head. I woke up a little startled but saw the now dozing children and my eyes met my husbands. He smiled and I could read the words on his lips "Mission accomplished Mama!"

Original_Search_Title: Adoke - an AGF the Media Want to Crucify
Headline: Nigeria: Adoke - an AGF the Media Want to Crucify
Body_Text:
When the present Attorney General of the Federation and Minister of Justice, Mohammed Bello Adoke, SAN, assumed office for the first time on the 6th of April, 2010, he was quite aware he had begun that glorious and, at the same time, unenviable journey which most of his predecessors had undertaken. A journey along which banana peels would spring up at any spot and time.
The media set various agenda for public discourse on issues that often concern our national life and development. But irrespective of the issue at stake, there has always been the need to reckon with the sensibilities of the generality of the public.
This is borne out of the possible myriad of reactions ranging from getting deeply involved in the discussions to selectively perceiving the subjects; not to talk of outright suspension of beliefs and disbeliefs, depending on who and what are involved.
Recently, in contextualising the alleged inactions and perceived or obvious errors of Adoke, certain commentators have reeled out litanies of pitfalls and iniquities of his predecessors. In the process, some have even confirmed their earlier predictions of how such AGs had ended their tenures and are proffering a template for the current one.
In the case of the current Minister, the vexed issues include such allegations as taking over or withdrawal of some cases from the court, pardoning the Vaswani Brothers, the settlement (through payments) of corruption charges involving such multinational bodies as Julius Berger Plc, Halliburton and Siemens and non prosecution of top figures associated with them. Other allegations are the proposed merger of the two anti-graft agencies (EFCC and ICPC) and the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (Enforcement) Regulations, 2010.
The vehemence of the expletives and vituperation on the person and office of the Minister of Justice arising from the issue of the EFCC regulations in particular presupposed that not a few persons at first even believed or knew that the Attorney General of the Federation and Minister of Justice is empowered under section 43 of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (Establishment) Act 2004 to make regulations with respect to the exercise of any of the duties.
However, the AG has made it clear on various occasions that rather than muzzle the EFCC by unduly bringing its operations within the control of his office, a dispassionate look at the regulation's provision would reveal that the operational powers of the EFCC have been carefully preserved, while protection of public interest is ensured as it discharges it functions.
Curiously, the issue of merging the nation's two anti-graft agencies for operational efficiency, and the question of the raison d'être of the two bodies, is something Nigerians had been discussing before Adoke became the Attorney General. But since the klieg lights are perpetually focused on top government functionaries, especially the AG, in order to pick the slightest move in error, a section of the media and some civil rights groups have decidedly become visceral in their analysis of every move and utterance of the Attorney General.
The role of watchdogs, whistle blowers and rights groups in the realization of a vibrant democracy and good governance can never be in doubt. Nonetheless, resort to the use of vituperative outbursts and unfounded allegations by such groups against the person and office of the Attorney General and, indeed, any other top functionary of government can detract from their lofty objectives.
Every top government official would appreciate the fact that his position is like that of a passenger on a bus who has moved to the position of the driver. His or her view of the same road can never remain the same.
It is this déjà vu and the responsibility of their oath of office that impose that heavy moral duty on members of the present cabinet of President Goodluck Jonathan to discharge their duties with national and public interests as guiding principles.
In line with these principles, Adoke has decided not to waver but instead operate within the spirit and letter of the law. And this is where he draws the most flak. Examples. He had hardly assumed office as Minister of Justice in April 2010 when the Social Economic Rights and Accountability Project (SERAP) called for his head for not rushing to prosecute the past military Head of State, General Ibrahim Babangida, alleging that the Okigbo Panel report had indicted him in respect of the $12 billion windfall which accrued to Nigeria from the sale of excess crude oil during his regime. The fine points of the Okigbo Panel report on the issue are still a subject in court today.
In a similar vein, the Coalition Against Corrupt Leaders (CACOL) recently told reporters that the two anti-graft agencies had confirmed that they were instituting a probe of the AG in connection with a $26.5 million corruption charges involving some multinational bodies- a move which Adoke has said he heartily welcomes.
Mohammed Adoke's constant stand on issues that cannot be substantiated, like the one above, has been that he would rather quit as AG than pursue a crowd lead against any person no matter the status whose Fundamental Human Rights would be jeopardized in the process.
To anyone who knows the Attorney General well, the gimmick of attributing petty acts of corruption to him for the purpose of smearing his character is not likely to work. He had within a few months into his first tenure as AG submitted himself for investigation following an allegation of bribery leveled against him by a journalist working for one obscure magazine. But the young man on learning of the minister's move begged profusely and confessed that he was influenced. It will not serve any useful purpose either to bet on the probe which the Coalition Against Corrupt Leaders (CACOL) is touting in respect of the $26 million which they were alleging the AG to be connected with. And this is because the transparency of that bargain was too well ensured for such a venture to be worthwhile.
What is required at present, especially with the coming into being of the Freedom of Information (FOI) Bill, would be the deliberate application of due diligence and due process to finding the facts about passionate issues. This will reduce the tendency to make speculative and inflammatory statements which are capable of causing disaffection and heating the polity.
With the Attorney General's commitment to seeing that the Freedom of Information Bill works efficiently, the facility with which relevant information could be requested and given under its provisions should lead to the realization of a stable polity. One in which most of our energy should be channeled towards tackling the very challenging issues of the moment. In this regard, all Nigerians have a stake. So do the Civil Societies and Rights Groups in the country.
Ambrose Momoh is Chief Press Secretary, Federal Ministry of Justice, Abuja

Original_Search_Title: Cherop Leads Nation's Charge for Marathon Crown
Headline: Kenya: Cherop Leads Nation's Charge for Marathon Crown
Body_Text:
It is unique for an athlete to make the cut for Kenya's senior team at a tender age, owing to the talent glut and the stiff competition swirling around the national trials ahead of major championships.
Most athletes struggle to compete in the national team colours for many years, with some realising their ambitions when they are well over 30 years old. But one lithe athlete broke the jinx earlier on.
At 15 years, Sharon Jemutai Cherop beat all odds to pick the bragging rights for the Seventh All Africa Games in Johannesburg, South Africa, in 1999.
Before she took up athletics, the 27-year-old runner from the hilly Marakwet area braved the cattle-rustling area to shine in world long distance running.
She discovered her athletics skills while herding goats at her rural home located in the world's 3,000m steeplechase cradle of Marakwet.
This partly made her rise faster to global athletics fame and it is clear the challenges hardened her.
She is your typical Kenyan female athlete - who burns out at the senior level after shinning at the junior level.
Her parents did not take up athletics, probably because running only became serious business in Marakwet in the early '90s.
Confirmed her prowess
Cherop re-affirmed her prowess in distance running with a third-place at the 2011 Boston Marathon, where she got an automatic ticket to line up on Wednesday next week in the marathon race as the 13th IAAF World Championships in Athletics starts in Daegu, South Korea.
But Cherop, a second-born in a family of four, had all her siblings taking up athletics and needed little inspiration from outside to take up athletics.
She is a soldier at the Defence Forces and, like her elder sister Jennifer and younger sibling Beatrice all compete in 5,000m and 10,000m races.
Beatrice, who graduated in June at Recruits Training School (RTS) in Eldoret, and is attached to the Nakuru-based 81 Battalion alongside Sharon but trains in Nyahururu.
Jennifer stopped running after sitting for her Form Four examination at Sing'ore Girls Secondary School in Keiyo.
She trains in Iten with her husband, Mathew Kosgei, a marathoner, and has her task clearly cut out in Daegu next week.
Cherop will battle it out in Kenyan colours in a field comprising 2010 New York City Marathon winner Ednah Kiplagat, fourth-placed Caroline Rotich, Philes Ongori and Prisca Jeptoo in the hunt for 42-kilometre glory.
Quality field
She will take on a quality Ethiopian team, among them 2009 bronze medallist Aselefech Mergia and Bezunesh Bekele.
Dire Tune, the 2009 Boston Marathon runner-up, Atsede Baysa and Aberu Kebede as well as athletes from Japan and China will also spice up the women's field.
Kenya will be seeking to reclaim the title Catherine Ndereba won when she beat Chinese Zhou Chunxio and Reiko Tosa of Japan in Osaka in 2007.
Chinese Bai Xue, Japanese Yoshimi Ozaki and Ethiopia's Aselefech Mergia swept the podium in Berlin (2009) and Mergia could be keen for honours in Daegu.
World Marathon women's record holder Paula Radcliffe holds the championships record at 2:20.57, which she posted at the 2005 World Championships in Helsinki, Finland.
The mother of one is certain the Kenyan contingent is equal to the task.
"We will work as a team. The World Championship is similar to the Boston Marathon - no pace-setters - and we will run the way we did in Boston with Caroline Kilel and Caroline Rotich," said Cherop, who has come a long way to global fame.
She started running as a Class Six pupil at Tirap Primary School in Marakwet District, competing up to provincial level in the 10,000m race in 1997.
"I missed the 1998 action as the school had no funds for extra-curricular activities that year. I had a strong interest in athletics and I wanted to be like Catherine Ndereba," offered Cherop.
With a neighbour in former Chicago and London Marathon winner Evans Ruto and Ndereba in mind, Cherop soldiered on to her dream career in athletics.
"Ruto promised to get me a sponsor if I performed well. I then put in a lot of effort, especially when herding goats," said the soft-spoken athlete.
She used to walk over six-kilometres to Tirap Primary School in what would prepare her for her future career.
"Due to the long distance, we could not rush home for lunch but we carried food wrapped in banana leaves to school as our day's meal. We kept the food under trees till lunch hour," Cherop told Monday Sport after training session last Thursday in Iten.
Her experiences are similar to those of teammate Silas Kiplagat (1,500m), who hails from the area.
During weekends, Cherop herded goats deep in the bushes.
"I was not easy as I still recall an incident when I could not trace my packed food deep in the bushes and only found it two days later," she recalled.
While in Class Eight in 1999, Cherop finished eighth at the national cross-country trials, missing a ticket to the World Cross Country.
She took it in her stride and prepared well for the track season and made the team to the All Africa Games, where she finished fourth.
The following year, Cherop joined athletics-rich Sing'ore Girls and studied alongside and competed against Daegu Worlds teammates Violah Kibiwott (1,500m), Janeth Jepkosgei (800m), Vivian Cheruiyot (5,000m and 10,000m) and Sylvia Kibet in 5,000m.
World Junior Championships
At Sing'ore, Cherop made the World Junior Championships team to Santiago, Chile, where she won bronze in 5,000m behind Dorcas Inzikuru and Ethiopia's track assassin Meseret Defar.
"The training in Sing'ore Girls was good as coach Boniface Tiren conducted a holiday training programme in Kapcherop Athletics Club. While at school Byron Kipchumba (now a scholar in USA) guided me," said Cherop.
She said the intensive training helped her before she picked up a knee injury after the 2001 secondary schools provincial championships, which ruled her out of the season.
She returned in 2002, placing third at the Discovery Kenya Great Rift Cross Country in Eldoret behind Joan Jepkorir Ayabei, whom she competed against while in school.
"Since I was a candidate, I decided to concentrate on classwork in 2003. There was stiff competition in class and I could not spend more time in athletics," said Cherop.
She, however, burnt out in 2004 and returned the following year at the Kenya Police Cross-Country Championships, finishing third behind Olympic medallist Isabella Ochichi and cross-country medallist Jane Gakunyi.
In 2005, she underwent the mandatory nine-month military training and thereafter began to train with Evans Ruto and Lameck Aguta, a former Boston Marathon winner.
In 2006 she got married and gave birth to a baby girl Natalia Jepng'etich, named after a Russian friend who organised races for her husband Kosgei.
Cherop made her maiden marathon race in 2009 at the Nairobi Standard Chartered Marathon, again finishing third in 2:33.53.
In 2007, she entered the Hamburg Marathon and ran a solo effort to win the race and a posted a Personal Best of 2:28:38 - more than five minutes quicker than her previous time.
She improved on her Personal Best at the Toronto Waterfront Marathon in September last year, where she fell over mid-race but recovered to hold off Tirfi Tsegaye to win in 2:22:42 - the fastest time ever run in Canada, beating Lidia Simon's all-comers record set at the 2001 World Championships in Edmonton, Canada.
Her husband Kosgei expects her to perform well in Daegu.
"She is in great shape this time and if she keeps the high pace she had in Boston, she stands to win a medal," said Kosgei.

Original_Search_Title: Baganda Dancers Steal the Undercooked Show
Headline: East Africa: Baganda Dancers Steal the Undercooked Show
Body_Text:
Simba Saloon at the Carnivore is an icon of Nairobi's night life, the symbolic epicentre and spiritual home of party enthusiasts in the city.
"Carni" as it is known, has a quality that other up-and-coming clubs and night spots in the city find elusive -- staying power. It has managed to maintain its standards and stay relevant, an unusual feature in a city where clubs come and go like meteors -- shining brightly for a short time, only to dim and fade into oblivion, sometimes in just a few months.
But the Carnivore's winning strategy in recent years has been hosting cultural theme nights that are all the rage among Nairobi's urbanites; it seems that Nairobi's middle class are all posh city slickers outside but villagers inside, yearning for a sip of busaa or the strumming of a nyatiti.
It was in line with this that the Carnivore last week hosted the third edition of Uganda Night, where Ugandans living in Nairobi had the chance to come out and "Party Like a Ugandan, Banange!" -- the theme of the night. The Simba Saloon was decked out in black, yellow and red, Uganda's national colours, and the evening was skillfully hosted by Ugandan comedian Patrick "Salvador" Idringe, former contestant in the stand-up comedy reality competition Stand up Uganda.
Ugandans, unlike Nairobians, take night life seriously, so they made sure they were dressed up for the occasion. You could actually tell who the Kenyans in the crowd were -- if they were in jeans, or looked like they had just come straight from the office, chances were they were not from across the border.
And of course, matooke and groundnut sauce had to be on the menu, though a Ugandan colleague told me that the matooke was "badly cooked" -- merely boiled and mashed the Kenyan way, instead of being slowly steamed in banana leaves as Ugandan traditional cooking demands.
Hypnotising
But if the cuisine was a let-down, the evening was redeemed somewhat by the entertainment, especially the traditional Baganda dancing that Kenyans find so hypnotising. Although Proff from Kenya was nothing to write home about -- the singer of Data and Niambie sounds much better in studio than on stage, and has a tendency to go off-key, something I consider unforgivable if you call yourself a musician -- Msechu and Davis from Tusker Project Fame got the crowd energised and dancing, with their rendition of Papa Wemba's hit Show Me the Way nicely fused with popular Lugandan folk song Nsanji.
Juliana Kanyomozi was the star attraction of the night, the singer of hits such as Kanyimbe, Kibaluma and Nabikoowa, as well as Usiende Mbali which she recorded with Tanzanian Bushoke, and the current favourite for many, Haturudi Nyuma with Nairobi-based Burundian Kidum.
Juliana is best known across East Africa as one of the judges on the regionally televised music contest Tusker Project Fame. She was the antidote to Ian Mbugua's unforgiving, American Idol's Simon Cowell-esque style.
It's easy to see why she is so popular -- not only is she beautiful and has an amazing voice, she also has great stage presence and knows how to keep the crowd engaged without resorting to theatrics. It doesn't hurt, either, that she is a Toro princess -- she is a first cousin of King Oyo, the reigning Omukama of Toro. Perhaps her royal roots give her that grace and elegance so rare in an industry where women tend to interpret "sex appeal" as leaving nothing to the imagination.
If I were to grade the evening, I would give it a B-. The Ugandans I was with said they found it just about average; they were expecting a lot more energy, better prepared and more authentic cuisine, as well as a broader representation of what it is to be Ugandan -- "Uganda is not just about the Baganda!" according to one disgruntled reveller. But the Kenyans seemed to be completely at ease, perhaps because they didn't have as many expectations or prior hang-ups -- I suppose they were just happy to experience something from across the border, and by something I mostly mean "traditional Baganda dancers."

Original_Search_Title: When Style Overwhelms Substance
Headline: East Africa: When Style Overwhelms Substance
Body_Text:
An individual style is one thing, but when mannerisms threaten to obscure the picture, that is something else altogether.
I was reminded of this when visiting an exhibition of paintings by the Rwandan artist Jean Bosco Bakunzi, at the Banana Hill Art Centre outside Nairobi.
It is the latest show to confirm gallery owner Shine Tani's determination to bring us work from the entire East African region, and as such it is to be entirely welcomed.
Bakunzi is a young artist, only 25, and he relies heavily on what he sees around him -- village and market scenes plus a few portraits.Self taught, he was influenced, he told me, by the Rwandans Bill Ruterana and Collin Sekajungo, the constructivist who creates brightly coloured panels using slices of plastic containers and other found objects stitched together.
Bakunzi's 22 paintings show he has already developed a distinctive style, although I wondered, looking at his work, whether it might have been for the sake of standing out rather than because he had something pertinent to express.
Each of the pictures is superimposed by a grid of white painted lines creating panels which, Bakunzi explained, exemplified the fact that in life, different parts are joined to make the whole. It is his reaction to the genocide. And it is more than a passing nod to Sekajungo.
Within each panel, the paint is combed into series of parallel lines, some straight, some in semi-circles irrespective of the shape of the subject beneath. So vigorous is the combing that the effect is like a ploughed field.
This, Bakunzi said, was a reflection of the way women combed their hair to make themselves beautiful. So he combed his pictures to increase their appeal.
The problem here is that beneath the panelling and the combing -- laudable though the reasons might be -- the realisation of his subjects is sound enough not to need the embellishments.
Instead of enhancing the paintings they become distractions.
What would make a really stunning exhibition from Rwanda, I think, would be one of woven baskets; elegant, attenuated and wholly satisfactory both to use and to view.
I often moan about pictures that are so poor I feel they belong in curio shops rather than in galleries. These tall baskets, woven from grass or fibres and often containing secret messages and meanings -- like Kenya's kangas -- are exactly the opposite: Craft work raised to the level of fine art.
They are not alone, of course, in this ability to transcend the applied arts. One thinks of Magdalene Odundo, the latest in a long line of potters to bring artistry to their craft.
Exchange programme
Perhaps the National Museum of Kenya could arrange an exchange: Kenya gets a show of Rwandan baskets and maybe Rwanda would like to borrow something from Kenya's vast but usually unseen holdings of contemporary Kenyan art. Or maybe some kangas.
Paintings currently on show upstairs at the museum include a lovely, lyrical Beatrice Wanjiru, from 2005, called Simple Things and a strong Sane Wadu, with a typically heavy impasto.
Also there are a group of portraits of people from the different tribes of Kenya, painted by I. W. Katete. Originally completed as a commission by this newspaper group, the one of Mesong'a ole Masangwa Sambei with his rich, red toga and his heavily beaded wife, stopped me in my tracks. It used to hang in the office of the editorial director of the Nation Media Group and I often found myself staring at it as I sought to explain myself.
When it comes to Kenya the nation state, the museum has made an excellent job of its exhibition History of Kenya, curated by James Nyaga. It is almost a model of what should be done to bring the past to life and help make us aware of the country's intricately woven heritage.
There is something for everyone in this even-handed show: Home made guns and knives used by the freedom fighters, colonial uniforms and staffs of office, archive photos, and a video in which the exact nature of the Mau Mau oath is detailed -- pause to shudder, then pray for the possibility of overhearing a child ask: "Mummy, what does 'insert yourself' mean?"
There are costumes as well, including a Kuria elder's colubus skin cloak and the exotic red and gold robe given by Sir Sultan Mohamed Shah, Aga Khan III, to A.M. Jevanjee. There too are decorations for the deserving and even a mock up of the Lunatic Express - and mind how you go on the tracks.
I wrote "almost a model" but sense that I am carping about what is a superb permanent exhibition. It has been open now for the past six months and is one of those rare shows you really must visit.
It's just some of those labels... they need to be more detailed, clearer and when they refer to numbered objects let the numbers actually be there. Please.
Frank Whalley runs Lenga Juu, a fine arts and media consultancy based in Nairobi. Email: fwhalley@gmail.com

Original_Search_Title: Much TV May Deny the Nation Outliers
Headline: Tanzania: Much TV May Deny the Nation Outliers
Body_Text:
"MY nine year old son wakes up every day thinking about the cartoons, the soap operas and asks about the TV programmes the rest of the family watched the night he slept early. He storms the corridor and dashes to the sitting room to switch on the television.
At this moment, he has not brushed his teeth or washed his teeth. "He sits very contented that he has woken up to his love, the audio visual gadget, and he will spend all the morning hours there until he is called for breakfast, later to resume up to the lunch hour. It is just too much for me to control her behaviour.
I am caught up in a dilemma", says Susan Peter, a mother of 3, all schooling in a day English medium school. Susan's neighbour at Mwenge-Survey, Rehema Mosha and a mother of 4 has had a stream of 'wars' in her house in setting the minimum hours for her children watching television after school.
In turn, two of her four sons who are 11 and 13 respectively have turned rebellious and aggressive towards the mother's decision. "They no longer pay attention to what I say, especially when I switch it off for them to have quiet moments for their homework and the late night hours when I feel that they have to go and sleep," she says.
But for Alice Mwaposi, a mother of six and residing at Kunduchi beach, her efforts to control her children's television watching behaviour has not been that successful in the recent past. She subscribes to DSTV and has access to a variety of channels, that she has been locking up the channels that call for parental guidance for the case of children such that they do not access them.
"But the children get access to the passwords and get access to them in my absence," she says. In any case, Alice says that she has realized that locking the channels out is not a viable solution as this can not teach "my children to manage their behaviour and controlling themselves when it comes to choosing what to watch and when to watch it."
Instead, she now talks to the eldest 14 year old son and explains to her why she controls them from watching television for long hours and its implications on their studies and understanding if they eventually get addicted to television. Among the 40 children aged between 5 and 16 interviewed by 'Better Living' in Dar es Salaam recently, 28 said they spend 11 hours a day watching television when they return from school, 8 hang out with their friends, 6 read their books and the rest have a do at the chores at home under direction by their parents.
The revelation was that the children who have access to computer and video games spend less time playing or chatting away with their neighbouring friends and many do not engage in house chores. It should be noted that earlier research has shown that long hours on television affects a child's cognitive and intellectual development, sensory development, language ,creativity and imagination, social development, and perceptions of reality.
And John Mboya, a sociologist at Institute of Social Work, while commenting on parenting styles explains that television watching is a passive activity that confines them in one room, yet children are supposed to be playing, creating and recreating and make sense of the world around them. Children who are actively playing will have more opportunity to develop their senses than passively watching television.
But Doctor Masele Balla, from the department of literature at University of Dar es Salaam says that in Tanzania, in addition to conveying messages of violence and sex to children, children get addicted to this content. He adds that in rural areas where there is enough space and play materials like clay and banana fibres, children are more creative than their counterparts in urban Dar es Salaam.
He however commends the role played by television as a powerful education tool if moderately and appropriately used. He explains that its aspects of image, movement, sound and colours contain the necessary ingredients to stimulate and complete instruction making television learning attractive and meaningful. "There is a lot to learn on TV -- current affairs, nature, science and technology, geography, comedies only if parents could monitor and limit their children the time they spend in front of the screen." he says.
Masele adds that children learn passive communication skills on television but the intricate communication capacity is lost since they can not talk back to the actors they watch on television. Research has shown that heavy TV viewers put in less effort on school work, have poorer reading skills, socialize less, have fewer hobbies and act ivies and are more likely to be over weight. He adds.
And Ruhinda James, a sociologist at Open University, agrees to the above view, saying that long hours of children on television makes them socialize less with the outside world, thus they tend to be introverted, they try to act much of the violence they discern from the images and some of the grandeur superficialities that television conveys.
She adds that those who spend much of the time behind the screen as they munch junk foods are likely to get obese. Possi however adds that if the children are guided well in their television watching habits, it still stands as a strong educational tool. While viewing, the eyes are practically motionless and defocused in order to take in the whole screen. Constant movement is required for healthy eye development.
Visual exploration is a prerequisite of seeing and necessary for developing a sense of depth and perspective. The two dimensional screen does not facilitate such development. Excessive TV viewing, one of the most passive visual activities, can seriously impair a child's observation skills. Viewing affects not only eye mechanics but also the ability to focus and pay attention.

Original_Search_Title: Japanese Seek Uganda's Raw Materials
Headline: Africa: Japanese Seek Uganda's Raw Materials
Body_Text:
Uganda has been cited as one of the African countries with a potential to supply raw materials to industries in Japan.
Fumiko Uno of the Japan trade fair division department said the manufacturing industries in Japan were facing a shortage of raw materials to run, which is hoped that African countries and Uganda in particular can exploit.
Uno said the team was in the country to identify the raw materials. He was last week meeting with the top management of the Presidential Initiative on Banana Industrial Development at Nyaruzinga in Bushenyi at the banana flour factory.
The flour was last month exported to Oman in the Middle East. The meeting followed an exhibition of banana flour under the Tooke Flour brand was held in Japan in April. Uno said they would need 20 metric tonnes of tooke flour per month.
Rev. Dr. Florence Muranga, the director of the initiative, said the team wanted to see where the banana flour was made. She said the Government must invest heavily in research arguing that, "their standards are very high."
Hiroki Nagamine, the Japan external trade organisation director, said they were impressed with the Government's initiative to add value to products," Nagamine said after tasting the cakes and biscuits made from tooke flour.

Original_Search_Title: Environmental Regeneration Programme in Lagos
Headline: Nigeria: Environmental Regeneration Programme in Lagos
Body_Text:
Unarguably, one of the major pre-occupations of the Governor Babatunde Fashola administration in Lagos state is environmental renewal. The government is challenged to invest heavily in the sector as a littoral state which is greatly exposed to environmental abuse due to intense economic and social activities.
From landscape design and management, promotion of cleaner cities through greening and solid waste management, enforcement of pollution control for industries among others, except one is a blind bat, what has been done so far to redeem the environment and enhance its value are visible for all to see.
The efforts of the government can best be assessed and appreciated if we put into perspective how the state look like prior to 1999 and transformation that has taken place till date. We could all recall that before now, the state was full of mountain of refuse everywhere. Residential areas and major highways were turned into dumpsites to the extent that mischievous critics derisively turn the slogan of the state to 'state of aquatic' squalor'. One other critical point that needs to be understood before one could objectively appraise how well the government has fulfilled the promise of raising the city's standard is that close to 10,000 metric tons of refuse is being generated daily in Lagos, more than what Ghana as a country is generating.
Going to work with vigor and discipline implementation of its agenda on the environment, the BRF government in continuity of the good foundation laid by the Tinubu administration has been turning the above stated challenges into opportunity for job and wealth creation. The scourge of environmental malaise was arrested by strengthening and creating agencies to manage the process of waste collection, transportation and disposal in the state. Not only were agencies established for enforcing sanitation, integrated mass enlightenment and social mobilization on maintenance of sanitary environment has become on-going activity in state.
Introduction of Dino bins in over 2000 locations, contracting Highway managers for cleaning of roads, introduction and regulation of PSP operation are parts of the measures the state has used to achieve better environmental cleanliness. The state has rehabilitated dump sites at Olushosun, Solous and Abule Egba with establishment of additional mechanized land fill sites in Badagry, Mowe and Ikorodu. Additional transfer loading stations were constructed in addition to provision of over 100 new refuse compactor trucks. Every last Saturday of the month is made a Sanitation Day, when roads, public buildings and shops stay closed until noon, to allow Lagosians to clean and tidy their neighborhoods. To further improve the environment of markets across the state weekly market sanitation day was also introduced.
One project that manifests concrete improvement on the Lagos environment is the greening and beautification of open spaces, decrepit loops, verges and medians across the state. Whereas the governor has won the trust, respect and confidence of enlightened and appreciative citizens for redeeming the promise to bring back as much greenery as possible to Lagos, the cynics and mismatch political opponents are mischievously asking if it is the beautification that people will eat. One, the beautification has generated jobs for previously idle youths. Second, it has improved the aesthetic appeal and recreational value of the environment. Third, the greening project is done to mitigate the effects of global warming through carbon dioxide emission control. Last but not the least; it is done in line with the promise of creating a good and comfortable environment for all inhabitants in Lagos state. Today when the entire world is focusing attention on global
warming and its attendant effects on climatic changes it should be to the advantage of the current government that people will vote for continuity of this laudable project.
One can go on listing many other achievements in the area of flood control with creation of such agencies like Emergency Abatement Gang (EFAG), Drain duct, Kick against Indiscipline (KAI) and the concerted effort on climate change control through which major stakeholders are being carried along by the state government.
With the aforementioned work, one is not deluded that everything has been achieved. Still it appears that a lot still need to be done especially in terms of enforcement and enlightenment. This is due to the fact that behavioral change communication is a continuous thing. What is required to maintain sane and friendly environment is not just about what the government is doing but also about the people's attitude.
Many people seem not to understand that the quality of our lives as human beings is substantially a reflection of the quality of the environment which we inhabit. Many still seem not to comprehend that the environment which we inhabit, like kola in Igbo culture, is life. Or how else can we explain attitude of our people who persistently make their living abode unsanitary and inhospitable environment? One continue s to wonder if people really know that habit of cultivating environmental sins is to die prematurely by installment?
It is still common to see people throwing things out on the highways from their cars. Travelling in commercial busses even gives one more panoramic view of things. On our streets, stagnant gutters still occupy pride of place next to conveniently-located food hawkers - all the easier for garbage disposal. Even some of the fanciest areas are not exempt - "pure water" sachets (which surely rank very high on the list of the most environmentally-unfriendly products ever invented!), bottles and unofficial garbage dumps circle the gated walls of posh homes and well-manicured lawns.
This dirty habit is not justifiable in a state where the government is doing so much on environmental sanitation. It is detrimental to our common good. It is certain no one has ever died from walking around with a banana peel or pure water sachet in their pocket or car until it could conveniently be disposed of!
Despite efforts being put in place to sanitise the environment, some still prefer to continue to live in the past as they still get involved in actions capable of frustrating government's investment in the sector. For instance, many houses in areas like Ajegunle in Ikorodu (where there was cases of cholera forth night ago), Amukoko, Orile Iganmu, Ijora Badia, Ajegunle in Ajeromi and Idi Araba in Mushin are lacking toilet facilities. In some houses, septic are channeled directly into the drain! The question is, if cleanliness is next to Godliness as the saying goes, where does that leave us? Ultimately, the responsibility for making Lagos livable rests on the state government and the people.
Musbau is of Lagos State Ministry of Information and Strategy.

Original_Search_Title: Locally-Born Man Stands for French Presidency
Headline: Senegal: Black Candidate Dares France to Elect Him
Body_Text:
Dakar — A Senegalese-born French politician has offered himself as a candidate for France's Socialist Party in a bid to become the country's first black President.
Mr Yaya Lam, 46, a France-trained financial expert, officially announced his candidacy last week and remained optimistic that he could spring a big surprise that would set him firmly on the path to the 2012 presidential election.
His slogan is "Oser la France", literally translated to mean "Dare France".
The Socialist Party, on which former IMF head Dominique Strauss-Khan was expected to mount a strong challenge against President Nicolas Sarkozy, is considering Mr Lam's candidacy ahead of the October 9-16 primaries.
So far, only six candidates-- four men and two women including current party boss Martine Aubry--have registered. All the other candidates are white with Aubry and Francois Hollande seen as the outright frontrunners.
But in a public statement in Poitiers, France, Mr Lam said he was confident he would make his mark.
"The world has changed," he said, apparently referring to US President Barack Obama. "When he (Obama) fielded his candidacy, he practically had no one behind him," said Mr Lam.
'Regain glory'
"I have held reputable positions in Seine, le Val de Marne, in Montmorillon (where his adopted mother lives) and in Poitier - all French regions," he said.
Mr Lam insisted that with the help of the millions of original French people and the naturalised citizens who believe that France needs to regain its past glory he could edge ahead in the party primary.
In his statement, he said: "We're frequently being told that French people are losing jobs...but when I walk in the public space, I can only see Chinese behind the businesses and not French..."
"I am not against globalisation, but I can not integrate another person if I do not know who I am."
"We need to regain our values and our roots...we French people do have cultural values and have always led the others," he declared.
Analysts say the only banana skin to his candidacy was the number of years one should have been a member of the party.
The number of years should not be less than 10, but insiders say that point is subject to a candidate's potential.
Mr Lam has been member of the party for over half a decade.
Another Senegalese-born national, Ms Rama Yade, rose to the rank of a senior minister in Mr Sarkozy's government but did not nurture presidential ambitions.

Original_Search_Title: Tambuwal in a Fix Over Selection of Appropriation Committee Chairman
Headline: Nigeria: Tambuwal in a Fix Over Selection of Appropriation Committee Chairman
Body_Text:
Abuja — As the leadership of the House of Representatives makes moves at solving the knotty problem of appointing the House Majority Leader, a fresh crisis seems to be brewing as selections of Committee Chairmen are being concluded.
The House has been without a Majority Leader since the composition of the House body of Principal Officers as the parliament was evenly divided over the candidacy of the two gladiators angling for the position, Mulikat Akande-Adeola and Muraina Ajibola.
While Mulikat is the choice of the party leaders as well as the Presidency for the position, majority of the members prefer to have Muraina occupy the position.
However, recent events in the parliament clearly point to the fact that Mulikat is set to clinch the job with little impediments stalling the announcement of her name as the rightful occupier of the office.
While Speaker Aminu Tambuwal was busy making consultation over how best to resolve the House Majority Leader position crisis, another ugly challenge seem to be emerging over whom to appoint as the Chairman of House Committee on Appropriation.
The Committee, considered as number one among "Grade A" committees, is being hotly contested for by the duo of John Eno, the former Chairman of House Committee on Finance, and Isa Ashiru, the former Deputy Chairman of the Appropriation Committee in the last parliament.
Both, sources within the parliament told Sunday Independent, have been properly scrutinised and found worthy in terms of competence and acceptability to the members, leaving the Speaker and his think-tank with the difficult task of making a choice.
Tambuwal, in the course of lessening his burden of choice for committee membership, constituted a selection committee made up of a member from each state of the federation to look into the curriculum vitae of each member from their own state and suggest committees where such a member is best suited.
According to a source who was chosen as the representative of one of the South-South states in the committee, "the speaker insisted that no one should be allowed to get into a committee where he/she will become an onlooker when technical issues are being considered at public hearings or any other form of assignment the committee might be asked to undertake".
The Selection Committee, chaired by the Speaker himself, the source said, had concluded work on the committee composition and most members are already aware of which of the committees they would serve.
Having done this perfectly, he assured that the Speaker, upon resumption of members after the annual break, would make known names of the committee members, and also gave assurances that the announcement, unlike what obtained in the past, would not degenerate into any crisis.
According to the source, the leadership employed a broad-based system where members were nominated into their committees through their caucuses. He added that the leadership would have concluded work on the chairmanship of those committees before their resumption on September 12th.
The source disclosed that the Speaker avoided naming the Committee Chairmen before the House closed for recess so as not to create unwarranted tension which usually trailed such, especially now that experience on the field was taken into consideration in deciding committee membership.
He said, "The Speaker wanted to avoid the 'banana peel' that would accompany such announcement. He will surely announce the names of the Chairmen immediately we resume in September."
Investigations however revealed that the leadership might have resolved to applying the ranking rule that will ensure that majority of the active returning members are appointed to chair 'juicy' committees."
This is in accordance with the resolve of the leadership to play-up competence, qualifications and experience in the new parliament.
To this extent, experienced lawmakers who have made their marks in the preceding parliament have been penciled down for some challenging committees requiring experience and competence.
It was learnt that the Chairmanship of Marine Committee may be given to its former Chairman, Ifeanyi Ugwuanyi, in the spirit of competence and continuity, with the Public Accounts Committee, traditionally reserved for the opposition, may have been ceded to Abiodun Akinlade of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN). Akinlade was the immediate past Chairman, House Committee on Science and Technology.
The source however disclosed that the leadership seem to have met a brick wall in making a choice between Eno and Ashiru on the decision on who should head the Appropriation Committee.
"This is definitely a tough choice for the Speaker, but we are hoping that the issue would have been amicably concluded before we resume after the break. It will definitely do us no good if the issue stalls just like the appointment of House Leader did. It will not be good at all for our image, it will tell negative news about the leadership and managerial ability of the Speaker. I'm sure he's aware of this."
Sunday Independent gathered that definite moves are being made towards resolving the logjam over the choice between Mulikat and Muraina for the position of House leader.
Tambuwal, according to sources, recently embarked on the last round of consultations, first with the leadership of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) as well as aggrieved parliamentarians before announcing Rep Mulikat Akande-Adeola as the Majority Leader.
It was learnt that the Speaker would have announced Akande-Adeola as the Leader before the House went on a 32-day recess last week, but for the fact that some members who were strongly opposed to her emergence must have to be placated during the recess.
There are also indications that Tambuwal, who had earlier defeated Akande-Adeola in the contest for the Speakership of the House, may have realised the need to reconcile with the party and the Presidency, so as not to put the Seventh House in collision course with the executive.
The relationship between the Speaker and his Deputy, Emeka Ihedioha on one hand, and the party leadership combined with the Presidency on the other hand, had been frosty since the members of the parliament upstaged the PDP zoning formula to elect the duo as Speaker and Deputy Speaker respectively against the party's wishes.
However, following interventions by stakeholders, the rift was recently resolved. The resolution was confirmed at the last National Executive Council (NEC) meeting of the party where the Acting National Chairman of the party, Kawu Baraje, openly announced that the duo have been forgiven for the act.

Original_Search_Title: What, How to Eat During Fasting
Headline: Nigeria: What, How to Eat During Fasting
Body_Text:
The Holy month of Ramadan is here again. It is a period for purification of the body and the soul. Nonetheless, it is contended that many Muslim families see the period of Ramadan as a time to display their culinary skills. Most Muslim wives, it is believed, ignorantly indulge in food exuberance with a parade of delicacies which could be harmful to the body during this period of Ramadan.
Speaking to Weekly Trust on the appropriate diets for the Ramadan season, Dr. Maryam B. Alhassan, a dietician with Prenate Clinic and Maternity, Abuja says "Ramadan is one season when people do not take precaution and go about eating all and everything which defies the reason of the fast in the first place. If you notice, most homes consume more oily foods during this time and oil intake is one of the reasons for the rise in health cases in the hospitals"
"The basic rule is that our food during Ramadan should be as simple and easy as possible. Combining different foods will only cause discomfort and constipation which most people experience. There are precautions that people with existing health disorders have to keep in check so as not to risk the chance of having a crisis during this Ramadan."
