The regional security officers transferred are: Maj. Charles Tumusiime, who has been moved from West Nile to Entebbe Airport while Maj. Baguma Benda has been transferred from the airport to Defence Liaison Officer.
Others are Mr Charles Bukondo who was moved from Kampala to Central region that includes the districts of Mubende, Mityana, Luweero, Nakasekke. Several district internal security officers have also been transferred.

Original_Search_Title: Dont Be Lenient On Bars, MP Mututho Urges DC's
Headline: Kenya: Dont Be Lenient On Bars, MP Mututho Urges DC's
Body_Text:
Naivasha MP John Mututho has called on DCs to ensure bars meet the requirements of the new alcohol law before licensing them. The MP said the DCs must follow the rules to the letter. He was speaking during a visit to Banana Village where six people died after taking illicit brews last month.
Mututho said DCs should only license bars that conforms the Mututho law which came into effect last year. "The DCs should see to it that laws are not bended by the bar owners after they are awarded the licences," Mututho said. He said it is unfortunate that many people have died after consuming the brews adding that the new law will avoid such deaths in future.
Mututho said a new breed of alcohol hawkers are out to circumvent the law. "Brewers have now changed tack and are hawking brews using ciondos. Police should be very vigilant," Mututho said.
He called on all Kenyans to help in the fight against killer brews adding that the new law will only succeed through concerted effort. Kiambu DC Albert Kimathi vowed that only bars that conform to the law will be licensed. "There will be no favourism in giving out licences and this time everybody who wants to register his or her bar should follow the stipulated laws," he said. Kimathi called on the Church to help them fight the excessive use of drugs .

Original_Search_Title: My Speakership, Contracts, Future - Ochei
Headline: Nigeria: My Speakership, Contracts, Future - Ochei
Body_Text:
BEFORE his emergence as Speaker of the Delta State House of Assembly, Engr. Victor Ochei, was among the most vibrant and probing members of the House. He has now found himself in a position where as a presiding officer, he is bound to watch the vibrancy and listen to the grandiloquence of others without automatically imposing his own views.
Ochei's work as Speaker is not going to be easy given the unprecedented representation of the opposition in the legislature. Ochei in this interview with Vanguard explains how the unusual cordiality in the Delta House paved way for his election as Speaker, rolls out plans on how he would ensure the stability of the legislature and gives a peep into his political future.
How did you emerge as Speaker?
Let me not say I was taken by surprise, but I was overwhelmed by the total support that came from everybody. I had in my own little way expected that we were going to go into a contest, but you know the Delta State House of Assembly, we really don't contest with each other, we look at it and say let the person we think is good enough go for this or that, it is as simple as that and I must tell you, my party moved the motion and the DPP seconded the motion for my election. It was like this man was wanted by everybody and I don't have any reason to play funny game with my colleagues, but uphold their trust in me. I owe them a whole lot of gratitude for this.
What would be your mission?
My focus is basically to bring back the glory of the legislature as the first arm of government as enshrined in the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999 as amended. The legislature in Delta state today has done a lot, but there is lot of room for improvement, and that is what I want to put on a fast track by way of legislation with all the members cooperating to ensure that we succeed.
Participatory parliament
For me as a person, I want to run an inclusive participatory parliament, where everybody is seen by the constituency they represent, not by the party they represent, where every member is given his due regards and we are not divided along ethnic lines and you will not even notice the difference that we are a multi-party House.
In Delta, I am going to ensure that the legislature does the job it is supposed to do, we are going to serve as the eyes of the public, we are going to expose corruption, ensure the budget is done properly, in fact, everything the constitution empowers the legislature to do, we will do.
Relationship between PDP, DPP lawmakers
The deduction people hold from outside (is that) oh, because they are DPP, they have come to fight, No, DPP or PDP or whatever party you belong, you have come into the House to serve Deltans, to serve your constituency, so when you come in, your party's interest is locked behind the door as you enter the hallowed chambers to legislate because when you come there, the constitution tells you that you are here to make laws for peace, order and good governance of Delta state, not for peace, order and good governance of the party that you represent. At that time, the party that brought you to the legislature is only a vehicle that will take you to the venue and go to the parking lot to wait for you until you are done and come back. So, I can tell you, PDP, DPP, ACN, whatever party you have in the House today, as members, they have come with different vehicles but the moment they step into the chambers of the Delta State House of Assembly, they will do justice to peace, order and good governance of Delta state.
Partisan interest
It is no longer their partisan interests, the interest that come first is the interest of Delta state and the people. And I want to tell you that contrary to what a lot of people are thinking, that is not the situation. The members of the House, irrespective of their parties, are highly committed Deltans who are ready to serve the state and move the state forward. It doesn't matter who is the governor today, they have to ensure that there is cordiality, and that at the end of the day, there is peace, order and good governance in the state.
Since the DPP governorship candidate, Chief Great Ogboru is fighting Uduaghan, lawmakers in his party cannot but also fight Uduaghan. Why do you paint a different picture of DPP lawmakers?
None of the members of the DPP in the House brought any such mandate of fighting Uduaghan to the House and I have not seen it in their behavior that they came to fight anybody. They are here to legislate and represent their constituencies; I don't think anybody will come with the mindset to fight. May be Ogboru wants that, but until he becomes a member of the House, he cannot do that. Well, the PDP is a very accommodating party and that is why even at the national level, the party can run a government of national unity and inviting other parties to come together. It is probably only in Delta State that you have Commissioner for Intra-party relations, but we don't believe that because we are in a majority party, we should not think about others. No, it is live and let's live.
In fact, if you become the governor of the state or legislator, the number of your registered party members is so infinitesimal to the number of people you are representing and you owe allegiance to the majority that didn't even vote for you than those that voted for you. You have to decide, if those policies you want are for your party members only, be sure that your people may recall you.
You executed major contracts for the state government as a lawmaker; tongues are wagging over what will happen to the resources of the state now that you are Speaker?
I am an engineer by training and I did my electrical projects with the state government on grounds of competence, not on patronage. I have never been found wanting by my constituents who voted me overwhelmingly and I have not been found wanting in my private duty as an engineer. Sometimes, when people go all out, I know that there is an ulterior motive, but I can tell you today that the work of the Speaker of the House of Assembly is so overwhelming that I had resigned my interests completely in every company that I ever owned before now on moral grounds. With effect from June, this year, I ceased to be director in all my companies.
What is your take on jumbo pay for lawmakers?
Who is saying jumbo pay for lawmakers? I have only been reading it on the pages of newspapers, I think the National Revenue, Mobilization and Fiscal Commission, is an agency of government where public officers' remuneration are clearly stated and that is what we use as yardstick, my salary is known to the world, I earn less than N300,000.
Peoples mistakes
Take the executive for instance, as a minister or commissioner, you have imprest you use for this and that, and nobody is asking them questions, but when it comes to the issue of lawmakers, they shout oh, they have allowances to take care of their office and a lot of questions.
How do you intend to avoid the banana peel that has always been the nemesis of most Speakers?
Well, experience is the best teacher, you see, one learns from other people's mistakes because life is too short to make all of them yourself. So I have learnt from other people's mistakes, it may not be perfect, it may not be 100 per cent, but I can tell you that I have taken their mistakes as proper lessons and I will not fall into the same net, otherwise, Basically in the legislature, the major issue is how you are able to carry your members along, I am a team player, don't forget that I came from the minority and today, I am a big player in the majority, that simply tells you that I know how to marry both. Without equivocation, I will run a transparent and all -inclusive and participatory parliament and that way, I don't see any way that a person who is participating with you and you are transparent in your management, will want to fight against you.
You are from Delta North Senatorial District which is expected to produce the next governor of the state. How true is it that you have been penciled down for it?
First and foremost, I am glad you said "learnt" I don't know who you learnt from, but I can also tell you that the clamour for the Delta North to produce the next governor of the state is not in contention. However, this is 2011, we have just been elected, I have not even done one month as the time you are speaking to me and you are already talking 2015, that is the problem I have with our distinguished members of the fourth estate of the realm, they put you on the spot and expect you to make committal statements and then, they hold you to it. But I can tell you as a politician that 24 hours in politics is a long time, we are 47 months away from that time and it is too early in the day to be discussing things of that nature.
Let us first and foremost do the work for which our constituents voted us into office because Deltans are expectant. There is a lot out there and people are expecting, the elections are enough to show you the much people are expecting from those they voted into office. Expectations do not necessarily mean handouts to the people that elected you into office, expectations are that they want to see performance, they want to see a change from what it was before, so you owe them accountability and there is a lot that will occupy politicians this time around and it is not going to be business as usual.
And Nigeria is gradually going to where you will be judged on the account of what you have done before in office and so, if you fail them in office, be rest assured that you can't go back to ask for their votes again because voting is becoming much more credible than it used to be in times past.
So, first and foremost, what we have to do as politicians is to deliver on the promises we made to those that voted us into office and be accountable to them, and by the time, we draw near a new order, they will be the ones clamoring for those of them they want to represent them in higher offices or the present offices they occupy. So I think it is rather premature to be talking about it now. For those who say it, probably, it is their opinion and they feel I should be in contention, I don't have problems with that. When the time comes, it will start crystallizing and we will be able to say yes or no.

Original_Search_Title: Derbymania Explodes
Headline: Zimbabwe: Derbymania Explodes
Body_Text:
For Dynamos, the mission is simple win, pile the misery on their greatest rivals, keep the bragging rights and boost their credentials for a late surge in the race for the Castle Lager Premiership title.
For CAPS United, the mission is also simple - win, give the fans something to cheer their spirits after a poor run, repair some of the battered image and move back into top company.
For the match officials, the mission is simple - be fair to both teams, try as much as possible to be guided by Fifa's spirit of Fair Play and let the better team, and not the one favoured by the referee, be the winner.
For the Castle Lager Premiership, the expectations are simple - let the two teams turn on the show that will illuminate the stadium and be a credit to the flagship encounter in the league.
Yes, just in case you didn't know, the 68th edition of the Harare Derby is on at the National Sports Stadium tomorrow.
Dynamos are bracing themselves for a probable backlash from CAPS United when the two giants meet in the flagship fixture of the Castle Lager Premiership at the giant stadium.
DeMbare go into the match with their morale boosted by the win in the reverse fixture 1-0 last month but fans will be hoping for better value for their money after the first leg ended in a dull show.
This will be the only match in the capital as defending champions Motor Action travel to Mucheke for a date against Masvingo United.
But the first leg, which drew a good crowd, was almost a non-event despite the pre-match hype, save for the late strike by Tawanda Muparati, which separated the two teams after the encounter degenerated into a drab affair.
Dynamos' assistant coach Ralph Kawondera yesterday chose to be cautious and said they were expecting a tougher assignment tomorrow.
"CAPS are more dangerous than they were when we first played them because they have been going through a lot in recent weeks.
"This will make it a bit difficult for us but we are happy that our team has hit good form at the right time.
"We have been calling for consistency in the first half of the season, haven't we?
"Now it appears our players are stepping up their game, which is very encouraging. Nonetheless, we are not going to relax.
"We all know what is at stake against CAPS United. From the body language at training you can actually see that everyone is raring to go. Just the feeling of playing CAPS United is enough motivation for them and
we are looking forward to the game," said Kawondera.
He indicated that apart from striker Patrick Khumbula, DeMbare have their arsenal ready.
Midfielder Wonder Sithole returned from injury a few weeks ago but is a doubtful starter since he is still to gain match fitness.
Both sides have spoken against hooliganism, which reared its ugly head in the abandoned match between CAPS United and Gunners at Rufaro last week.
DeMbare are set to welcome back their striker Martin Vengesai who missed the back-to-back ties against Chicken Inn after he was red-carded in the first leg of the derby.
Vengesai is favourite to partner Roderick Mutuma in the attack as Khumbula is not available for selection due to injury.
Khumbula has not trained with the team since he suffered a knock during a practice session last week.
Dynamos have signed former Gunners midfielder Nathan Ziwini to boost their options ahead of the derby.
Ziwini signed a three-year deal with the Harare giants on Thursday after getting clearance from his previous employers on a permanent move.
Team manager Richard Chihoro yesterday confirmed that the Dynamos management was busy trying to formalise Ziwini's registration papers with the Premier Soccer League, which could make the player available for tomorrow's derby.
CAPS United goalkeeper Edmore Sibanda was all tears after conceding the last-gasp goal in the match and is expected to play a crucial role between the posts.
But changes are expected in the CAPS United defence where they need to cover up for centreback Edmore Chafungamoyo and David Sengu who were red-carded in their last match against Gunners. Sengu, who rejoined the Green Machine from Gunners last week, had come in as replacement for rightback Gilbert Mapemba who is away in South Africa for trials but got expelled in his first match.
Coach Maxwell Takaendesa Jongwe, however, has several other options, which include new signings Tapiwa Kumbuyani, Asani Nhongo and Tendai Samanja while Luckmore Simango and Douglas Walaza are also available to strengthen the defence.
"The team is not about two or three players. We have a pool of players registered with the PSL and in every game we can only field 11," said Jongwe.
Makepekepe are seventh on the log standings with 22 points and Jongwe said they were aiming for maximum points against DeMbare.
CAPS United have gone through a lean spell in the last month as they have neither won a match nor scored a goal in their last five outings, which include the 1-0 defeat at the hands of Highlanders in the BancABC quarterfinal.
"I usually don't want to work with predictions but every coach goes into the match expecting a to win. We remain positive," he said.
Gunners will resume their battle for survival when they host mid-table side Blue Ribbon at LaFarge this afternoon.
Gunners, now under new coach Moses Chunga, are second from bottom with 13 points, although they have three more points on the table from the abandoned match with CAPS United, which they led 2-0 before violence made it impossible to continue with the match in the 68th minute.
Chando Kupisa have changed since Chunga came on board and their passing game against CAPS United was a refreshing sight.
They come up against a Blue Ribbon side that began the season on a promising note but have since faded into an average team.
Misheck Chidzambwa will be looking for an improved performance from his charges who are coming from a pair of 3-3 draws against Shabanie Mine.
Leaders FC Platinum could extend their lead at the top to five points, should they beat a stubborn Kiglon at Gwanzura this afternoon in what is likely to be a banana skin for Rahman Gumbo and his charges. Kiglon are likely to be powered by a big contingent of Dynamos and CAPS United fans, united in their cause, against a rich team that has threatened to overwhelm the Premiership.
But whichever way you look east, west, south and north - it's the Harare Derby that matters this weekend.
Fixtures
Today
Shooting Stars v Zimbabwe Saints (Sakubva), Gunners v Blue Ribbon (Lafarge), Monomotapa v Chicken Inn (Rufaro), Kiglon v FC Platinum (Gwanzura)
Sunday
Shabanie Mine v Black Mambas (Maglas), Dynamos v CAPS United (National Sports Stadium), Masvingo v Motor Action (Mucheke), Hwange v Highlanders (Colliery).

Original_Search_Title: Art Changes Isingiro Women
Headline: Uganda: Art Changes Isingiro Women
Body_Text:
Art never realises its full potential until it touches hearts. The women of Ruhiira village in Isingiro district are a living example of a simple, yet powerful way in which art is making a difference in people's lives.
Using locally obtained materials such as banana fibre, plastic, beads and others, these women have woven their future around these simple crafts. And the results have been amazing.
The women proudly showed off their products to a team of visitors from the Millennium Villages project last weekend. The team was led by Prof. Jeffrey Sachs, special adviser to the United Nations Secretary General on the Millennium Development Goals.
He was accompanied by Walter Wang, the CEO of JM Eagle, the world's largest manufacturers of plastic pipes.
The visit was part of follow-up activities for the implementation of Millennium Villages project in Isingiro where it has been running since 2006.
The showcase comprised simple artifacts including baskets, necklaces, bracelets, purses, earrings and other functional materials.
The materials appear in a multitude of bright colours including green, red, blue, orange and yellow. They are also made in different designs and shapes.
From the round to the angular, the women try their best to put creativity into their work. I was most captivated by a purse made from banana fibre. The checkered pattern of contrasting dark and lighter shades of fibre is attractive to the eyes.
According to Millennium Villages project, the exhibit organisers, all the artifacts were produced after several months of training for the women, facilitated and conducted by the Millennium village staff.
The crafts are up for sale now and the proceeds will be used to buy more materials and also as a source of income for the participating women.
Jenina Kwarikunda, a widow has been a beneficiary. She said: "I have learnt a lot from this project. I can now use my skills to earn a living and support my children."

Original_Search_Title: Happy Beat Misses Gold
Headline: Namibia: Happy Beat Misses Gold
Body_Text:
Windhoek — Life can become difficult when one has recently received a Lifetime Achievement Award for one's involvement in the Performing Arts in Namibia over the past 30 years.
Life can become difficult when one has so many productions of a high standard under one's belt one has lost count of. Life can become difficult when one has recently put on a fantastic production such as 'The Creation' as part of the country's Independence celebrations. In fact, life can become really difficult when one is Sandy Rudd, one of the most prolific stage directors around - for the simple reason that by now, everybody expects whatever Rudd touches, to turn to gold!
And so it was with her latest offering, Happy Beat, which she wrote, directed and produced to celebrate the 40th anniversary of the College of the Arts (COTA) in Windhoek. Working with a cast and crew of more than 270 to pull this one off, was no mean feat. The hype surrounding the production was intensified with "Crisscross rhythms exploding with happiness!" the popular slogan of the West African/Caribbean jazz rock fusion band, Osibisa, which became world renowned in the early 70s.
So, did this latest Sandy Rudd production turn to gold? This time around it did not.
From the beginning, when a large crowd of players flooded the stage, waiting for Mr. Strauss from the Ministry of Youth, National Service, Sport and Culture to judge a drumming competition in the village, the sense of expectancy has built up to such extent that one was waiting for an explosion of happiness and exuberance on stage. But this was not to be.
The very flimsy plot revolves around Sebino who believes that he's a good drummer, but because he is deaf, the village will not allow him to participate in the drumming competition organised for Mr. Strauss. This makes Sebino very determined to prove himself. He leaves the village and goes into 'exile' - a journey on which he meets various characters who infuse in him a sense of self-worth, which makes him more determined to prove himself. He returns to the village, enters the competition and walks way with the laurels, proving that 'if you can dream it, you can do it!'
Although the play was well produced, I missed the promised "explosion of happiness", which one would come to expect from a 40th celebration!
And with all those young, eager and energetic souls at her disposal, Rudd could really have gone to town, so to speak! At first there was some promise of sparks flying when the stage became a bundle of joy with a variety of shades and hues joining in an Oshiwambo traditional dance, but this was short-lived.
Compliments to Nicky Marais, Kalapufye Nghidileko and John Pindalo for an amazing set which created the appropriate atmosphere from the word go. The beautiful silhouettes, complemented by some shadow dancing, lights and sound left no doubt as to where the play was set and created a pleasant ambiance in which to enjoy the action on stage.
The singing and dancing were pleasantly enhanced by the musical accompaniment of the orchestra directed by Jacus Krige, and the choir conducted by Fanie Dorlfling, although there were some blind spots, being the absolutely disappointing rendition of Vicky Sampson's 'African Dream' as well as the song sung by the Headman, Banana Shekupe. African Boy's narration kept the action on stage tightly together. His was a pleasant portrayal of a bouncy and unpredictable narrator who captured the audience's attention from beginning to end. This was a welcome first stage appearance for the popular singer, and by all accounts we will be seeing more of him, hopefully in more demanding roles.
The highlight of Happy Beat was undoubtedly the performance by Haymich Olivier who played Garab, the meerkat. Following //Gui, the Bushman, everywhere he went, Haymich interpreted the role effortlessly. Although his movements seemed rather simple, he packed so much feeling and interpretation into them that the attention of the audience was riveted on him throughout. His interpretation, in all its simplicity, was ample proof of why Olivier is regarded as the top choreographer in Namibia at present.
So if Happy Beat had all the ingredients for making it another 'golden' Rudd-production, why did it not go down as one of her more memorable productions? I think the main reason can be found in the flimsy plot which hampered the writer-producer-director from building substantially more around it.
Like with her previous production, The Creation, one was left with a yearning for more, but this time, the yearning was accompanied by an emptiness because the actual production did not live up to the expectation created by the marketing hype The happy beat and crisscross rhythms exploding with happiness just were not there!

Original_Search_Title: Champs Yanga to Enter Bunge
Headline: Tanzania: Champs Yanga to Enter Bunge
Body_Text:
MAINLAND Champions Yanga will take the Kagame Cup trophy that they won a fortnight ago, to the National Assembly in Dodoma on Friday.
While Yanga are heading to Dodoma their arch rivals Simba will leave on Friday for Zanzibar, where they will set up a camp to prepare for the forthcoming league season and Confederation Cup.
The coordinator of Yanga's four-day tour to Dodoma, Cellina Agustino, said the team will leave on Thursday for Dodoma and will enter the House on Friday to parade their silverware.
Yanga Chairman Lloyd Nchunga also confirmed of the team's tour to Dodoma.
Meanwhile, Simba Information Officer Ezekiel Kamwaga said all players with an exception of Zambian Felix Sunzu have started Gym training in Dar es Salaam, ready to leave for Zanzibar on Thursday.
He said Sunzu will join the rest on Friday.
In another development, Vodacom Tanzania is expected to join the Members of Parliament in a special dinner and dance party at the parliament's grounds in Dodoma.
The firm's Senior Marketing and Pubic Relations Official, Mwamvita Makamba, said the event is part of activities aimed at fulfilling social responsibility to the firm's customers that include the parliament and government, apart from appreciating the latter's contribution to the country's development.
"We have always been hosting this kind of parties during parliament sessions and this year's event will be one of a kind as compared to the previous parties," she said, adding that the event's motto will be "More Than Ever Before".
"Vodacom Tanzania will be going to the parliament in Dodoma with a completely new appearance in terms of colour and slogan which, in Swahili, is termed as "Kazi ni kwako", thus, we want to present our new appearance in everything that we are doing," she added.
Prime Minister, Mizengo Pinda, is expected to lead the MPs in the party, which will also include Speaker, Anne Makinda.
The leader of the opposition camp in the parliament, Freeman Mbowe, and other Members of Parliament including ministers and deputy ministers are also expected to be in attendance to join the Vodacom Tanzania team that will be led by Managing Director, Dietlof Mare.
Mwamvita said she was aware that the MPs are currently tackling a tough assignment of discussing the government budget, something her firm respects but the party still holds much importance to them as it will give them opportunity to meet outside their normal working environments.
"The event will be coloured by performances from vocalists, Banana Zorro, Nassib Abdul "Diamond" and Shakira, as a continuation of the firm's effort to promoting the existing better relations with a view to building a country that boasts of substantial achievement in mobile phone services", she added.

Original_Search_Title: Hawks 'Nsenene' in Juba,While Kenya Does Banks - Wait!
Headline: Uganda: Hawks 'Nsenene' in Juba,While Kenya Does Banks - Wait!
Body_Text:
Last week in The Observer, MP Ssemujju Ibrahim Nganda, in an article titled "Juba: Uganda vends bananas while Kenya opens bank branches" touched on one of the most shameful truths about "Uganda capitalism".
Writing from Juba, the capital of the new nation of South Sudan where he was for the independence ceremonies, Ssemujju said: "Juba is closer to Uganda, but it appears that Kenya has been quicker in spotting the market potential and is already outpacing Uganda...Uganda mostly exports fresh food, including fruits, vegetables and eggs, the Kenyan banking sector is already making inroads there. Kenya Commercial Bank and Equity Bank have already opened for business in Juba."
To mark South Sudan's independence, many newspapers in the region ran supplements. However, the supplements in the Kenyan papers revealed the extent to which banking, hotel, construction companies, and even universities from the country have entered South Sudan ahead of regional rivals.
The reason for this isn't because Ugandans are lazy, they are not. It isn't that Ugandan companies are unimaginative. It isn't even that we are a nation of small-minded fellows who can only sell grasshoppers (popularly known as nsenene), bananas, and jackfruit (fene) in regional markets. Rather, it is about what it takes to succeed in business in Uganda, and what kind of companies have arisen from the peculiarities of economic liberalisation in Uganda over the last 20 years.
During privatisation, quite a few companies were sold to fellows with political connections. That in itself is not a problem, because one's political allegiances has nothing to do with one's business acumen. However, privatisation happened concurrently with economic liberalisation, so while for example hotels that had been nationalised were sold off, licences were given for investors to build new hotels.
Rather than compete, the politically connected fellows who had bought off the State companies sought protection to allow them "stabilise" and recoup the money they had used to buy the assets. However, because the government was committed to a free market it refused (sensibly) to give them the protection they sought.
However, there were other ways the government could help. The easiest one was income tax exemptions. These exemptions enabled the State to pick and choose winners using political authority, while not interfering with the market.
As the economy opened up more and more, even people who had started businesses on their own without government help, felt the heat of competition. To survive, they too found a godfather who secured them exemptions.
Soon, even the income tax exemptions could not protect these businesses from nimble smaller operators who were doing business more efficiently. The next thing? They asked government to inject new money in their struggling businesses either through direct cash handouts from the Bank of Uganda (ordered by State House), or writing off their debts (as happened with nearly all the hotels that were privatised).
A local variety of crony capitalism had been born in Uganda; you succeeded in business if you were protected by a powerful figure in government or the military. Because in 1996 Uganda started having elections, even though they were still under a one-party system, there was still some level of competition. Often the winner was the one who spent the most money.
The politicians therefore started demanding that the companies they were protecting pay back by contributing to their campaigns, employing their campaign managers, and so forth. In turn, to cover these costs, the companies demanded more exemptions and inflated tenders from the government.
When these abuses were pointed out, the retort from the government was that the critics were "jealous", or that even if the system was corrupt, it was still good because it was creating wealth and jobs for Ugandans.
Now we are paying the price, because most Ugandan companies cannot compete outside the country because they cannot succeed in markets where they have to pay taxes, and where they cannot get subsidies and protection from government officials.
Uganda helped the Sudan People's Liberation Army and the Rwanda Patriotic Army in their liberation wars. But in both these countries, Kenyan firms have the big hotels, banks, construction companies, supermarket chains, while Ugandans sell bananas (matooke), are barbers and mechanics.
This is not to sneer at matooke and fruit sellers. On the contrary, it is to admire and say the bigger Ugandan companies should learn from them. The government does not help our matooke sellers. On the way to Rwanda and South Sudan, they have to bribe many traffic police officers. Then border officials shake them down. They pay commercial prices to transport their bananas, because their fuel is not subsidised.
Unlike Hassan Basajjabalaba, they don't get billions of taxpayer shillings to bail them out. They go through all these hurdles, and in South Sudan the police and army harass them, but they still do a sale, make a small profit, return, and go through the horrors all over.
The Uganda matooke sellers in Juba, like their counterparts in Kikuubo, represent the genius of Uganda enterprise. If Uganda is to ever become an export power in this region, it is their knowledge, not that of the Kampala Road companies, that will win the day for us.

Original_Search_Title: Tambuwal And the Challenges Ahead
Headline: Nigeria: Tambuwal And the Challenges Ahead
Body_Text:
Aminu Tambuwal became the fifth Speaker of the House of Representatives in the current democratic dispensation despite strong opposition from his party, Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) and the presidency. Given the experience of the past twelve years, his emergence took many observers of the political scene by surprise, largely because the PDP, being the majority party in the house has zoned the position to the Southwest and also because the peculiarity of our political environment gave a lot of powers and influence to the president whose favorite normally gets the job.
But things have changed. It indeed, fundamentally changed when President Goodluck Jonathan predictably came out to contest the presidency under the PDP with the spurious argument that every Nigerian has the right to contest for any office in the country. Some of the party leaders even went to town telling Nigerians that zoning has been discarded in the party many years ago, adding that the president's ambition is in tandem with the zoning arrangement introduced by the party in 1998 to sooth the nerves of the Southwest, a region that felt cheated after the annulment of June 12 presidential election and the eventual death of the acclaimed winner, Moshood Abiola.
Even so, some of these party leaders, whose motive was never altruistic, failed to bring to the table the fact that the North would not be satisfied that they were not allowed to rule for eight years. The party leaders were more concerned about their interest; pushing for the candidacy of Jonathan and by extension, that of their protégés. They also failed to reach out to aggrieved party leaders who were not comfortable with the arrangement. This thus, made some of the aggrieved leaders, of course mostly from the North, to come together and fight what they described as an injustice. The Northern Political Leaders Forum (NPLF) that was a product of this struggle could not however change the tide, the game was already won. They nonetheless made a strong point which is on record; that the PDP has circumvented its own rules by allowing Jonathan to contest. This bad precedent, they argued, would come back to hunt the party in the future.
For sure, not a few people think that the emergence of Tambuwal was a fall-out of the struggle for the soul of the PDP and the country. It was indeed, seen as a move to balance the power equation in the country. With the presidency going to South-south, the vice president to Northwest and the Senate President going to North-central, the core North would have been left with nothing. So, that was why Tambuwal had the temerity to come out and challenge the directive of the party. If anybody was in doubt that the leadership of the core north was behind him, you only need to look at his campaign and the funds at his disposal. Tambuwal left nobody in doubt about his readiness to fight the party and presidency to the speakership. He made effective use of his links in the media, the new members, most of who were amenable to his ambition and members of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN), who were readily available, because according to some of them, they would not want work with a stooge of the presidency.
So what are the challenges before Tambuwal more than a month after becoming the speaker and how far can he go in checkmating the excesses of a bloated executive? Would he succeed in leveraging on the popular will of the house members that brought him in or would he be subsumed by a party that believes governance should be a family affair? These are some pertinent questions that should be bothering the new speaker of a house that has largely not measured up to the expectations of Nigerians.
A cursory look at the affairs of the house in the past twelve years showed that most of its leadership has very strong link with the executive going by the domination of the PDP and more than casual interest shown by the executive in the affairs of the lower chamber, particularly during the General Olusegun Obasanjo's tenure. This has affected not only its oversight functions, which is vital for the entrenchment of democratic values and gains, it has also made it a moribund body that can only bark and not bite. There is nothing to show, in the past twelve years, that the house has been virile and vibrant in pursuing the interest of the Nigerian people. What we have seen on the contrary is a bunch of rapacious fellows who are more interested in their welfare rather than that of the people; a house that has not demonstrated enough capacity to face the challenges of the nation, which is the provision of good governance; and a house that has been insensitive to the yearnings and aspirations of Nigerians.
Just take a look at the way the house wasted tax payers' money to investigate corruption in the power sector only to come out with nothing. Nigerians were interested in the probe, thinking that the outcome would help in finding final solution to a sector that is so vital and so problematic. The revelation made by the former Speaker, Dimeji Bankole that more than 16 billion dollars was wasted on the sector with nothing to show for it during the Obasanjo years, highlighted the kind of country we are; where people who stole public funds are left to walk the streets without being punished. That is one of the tragedies of the Nigerian state.
Even then, it is yet too early to see how Tambuwal would surmount the many challenges before him given the nation's unpredictable political terrain and it increasing complexities. A presidency that is constitutionally so powerful and one that lost out in the speakership contest would look for ways to revenge. And for how long would the majority that catapulted him to power remain with him when the chips are down? This would largely depend on the mood of the nation and issues at stake. Which is the more reason why the Speaker and its team would have to be circumspect at all times to avoid the proverbial banana peal.
The nation is faced with a lot of problems at this critical juncture of its history and those in power have the responsibility of doing the right thing to enable the country climb out of this hole. I quite agree with Tambuwal that this is not the time for politics as usual; now is the time for the principal actors in the arena to roll up their sleeve and fix the country. Tambuwal has enough political capital, given his popularity in the house and country, to actively participate in this renewal project. Unemployment crisis caused by downturn in the economy needs to be tackled; a corollary is the power sector, where the cabal that has been holding the country hostage for decades needs to be faced; yet there is the big elephant in the room, corruption. This is a scourge that has defied successive governments; the Tambuwal house has a responsibility reduce it to the barest minimum. These and many others are the challenges before the current house leadership.
So, Tambuwal Nigerians are watching!

Original_Search_Title: Mandela an Inspiration to Humanity
Headline: South Africa: Mandela an Inspiration to Humanity
Body_Text:
Giyani — Limpopo MEC for Housing and Local Government, Soviet Lekganyane, described the man of the day, former President Nelson Mandela as a source of inspiration to humanity.
The iconic world leader, Madiba, who was born on 18 July 1918 in the former Transkei homeland, turns 93 today.
Lekganyane began Mandela Day celebrations by getting his hands dirty by finishing incomplete houses for residents at Homu village.
"We wish him many more days to live long so that he could see the good things which the government he loves so much is doing for its people, so that he could see that his efforts were not in vain.
"He is a source of inspiration to humanity, his good values and principles that he fought and stood for, would always remind us to do more for those of us who are less fortunate," he said.
The MEC officially handed over houses to Gabela Mphephu Mathebula, Piet Mkhatsane Shivambo, and the Mlanga and Lestinah Mbombi family.
Speaking to BuaNews, Mbombi who has been staying in a small dilapidated one-roomed house with her five children and unemployed husband, could not contain her joy.
"I will never forget this day in my lifetime. I never thought that I will ever get a decent house like this one. When it rains, we used to wake up and stand against the wall."
Earlier in the day, Limpopo Premier Cassel Mathale and Arts and Culture Minister Paul Mashatile donated 10 computers and books about Nelson Mandela's role in the liberation struggle to Mavalani High School.
Acting school principal Magezi Baloyi said: "We appreciate the donation from the leadership; it is a motivation for us as teachers and the learners.
"We believe that the books will help the kids to understand why as a nation we are celebrating Mandela's birthday."
Mandela Day is an annual international day adopted by the United Nations (UN) in 2009. It is more than a celebration of Mandela's life and legacy; it is a global movement to take his life's work into a new century and change the world for the better.
Mandela Day asks people across the world to embrace Madiba's values and honour his legacy through an act of kindness.
Mandela Day includes a series of events involving interactive art, entertainment, music, film, educational outreach and community service activities, with the hope of inspiring people around the globe to embrace their individual power to make an imprint and help change the world around them for the better.
This year, the main Mandela Day is celebrated at Giyani stadium in Limpopo. Giyani's economy is predominately rural, mealies, peanuts, tomatoes, potatoes, mangos, bananas and cattle ranching form the backbone of the economy.
With the country's economic boom, Giyani has now become a retail and entertainment centre for the local population. Modern shopping centres and a mini-mall have uplifted the local economy.

Original_Search_Title: Kirsty Coventry Gears for World Champs
Headline: Zimbabwe: Kirsty Coventry Gears for World Champs
Body_Text:
Six kilometres swimming, five minutes break, one banana, two poached eggs and turkey, two hours sleep, one bowl pasta and 6km swimming again.
This is Kirsty Coventry's typical day in numbers ahead of the World Swimming Championships in Shanghai, China next week.
Coventry said from her base in Johannesburg, South Africa, that "training is going well" and she would leave for World Championships tomorrow to start swimming on July 24.
Coventry said she is using the World Championships as part of her training for the 2012 Olympics to be held in London.
The World Championships are the real deal for Coventry.
She has been taking part in invitational tournaments this year on her return to competitive swimming after taking a break in 2010.
Coventry's management team said, "As an athlete she always strives to win but more importantly than winning is ensuring her times get faster than her recent European competitions. We are after faster times, a bonus if she gets on the podium."
Coventry, who had her bank account siphoned by fraudsters, has been refunded her money by First National Bank.
"Morning wake-up call. FNB (First National Bank) have sorted out the fraud issue and replaced the money in my account," Coventry twitted on Wednesday.
The 28-year-old swimmer sent a message through twitter on Tuesday saying that money had vanished from her South African bank account.
"Had money withdrawn by fraudsters out of my account last night," she tweeted. "Let's see how First National Bank handles this crisis!"
Coventry is going to the world swimming championships, which get underway next Saturday hoping to reignite her Olympic form as the countdown begins for London 2012.
She is expected to compete in three events at the purpose-built Oriental Sports Centre on the city's outskirts, a state-of-the-art facility that will host 16 days of swimming, diving and water polo from next Saturday.
Below is Coventry's swimming schedule for the World Championships being held in Shanghai released by her management team.
24 July
AM: 200 IM heats
PM: 200 IM semi finals
25 July
PM: 200 IM Final
29 July
AM: 200 backstroke heats
PM: 200 backstroke semi-finals
30 July
PM: 200 backstroke Finals
31 July
AM: 400IM heats
PM: 400IM Finals

Original_Search_Title: Husna Crowned Miss Temeke 2011
Headline: Tanzania: Husna Crowned Miss Temeke 2011
Body_Text:
HUSNA Twalib beat other contestants to the crown in the Redd's Miss Temeke 2011 pageant held at the TTC Chang'ombe venue in Dar es Salaam on Saturday.
The captivating event also witnessed favourite contestant, Cynthia Kimasha, who is also the reigning Miss Chang'ombe, grab the runner-up position while Mwajabu Juma came third.
Scores of spectators turned up at the venue to watch the pageant that was coloured by performance from B Band, which is owned by talented local vocalist, Banana Zoro.
The pageant, which officially signalled the beginning of several other contests in Dar es Salaam Zone, involved 16 contestants, who showcased their dancing skills to the tunes of various beats at the start of the event.
The beauties also paraded different attires for various occasions including evening attire, creative garments and beach wear.
A section of beauty pageantry stakeholders, who have been contributing immensely to the success of the Redd's Miss Temeke since its inception, were also awarded in the night.
The stakeholders include Redd's, Vodacom and domestic broadcasting media outlets, Times Radio and Clouds FM.
The event's co-ordinator, Benny Kisaka, said the awards aimed at appreciating the stakeholders' support.

Original_Search_Title: With Such Abounding Beauty, Why the Negative Africa Images?
Headline: Africa: With Such Abounding Beauty, Why the Negative Africa Images?
Body_Text:
Judging by long queues at European and American embassies, and the fact that the harder their visa requirements get, the unchanged the queues remain, one can assume that for whatever reason, it is indeed the dream of many to leave Africa and travel north!
Equally, there are many who dream to travel to Africa. For all the years I lived on two other continents, I never got the wow factor as I did when I mentioned I lived in Africa. Reactions varied between wonder at the level of courage it took to survive in Africa, to simple excitement of children who were awed by the fact that I could meet lions and giraffes every day as I left home!
For adults, Africa meant - most of time - South Africa, so I had an automatic corrector in my statements that there is also West, North and Central Africa, and that we lived in the Eastern part of this vast continent. The first question usually comes as: Is it safe to live there? The second is: it must be very hot there, right? And at the end of the conversation there is always the statement regarding a childhood dream to visit Africa.
Once things become a bit clearer and people realise which part of the map - Uganda for example - occupies, and they get over the hurdles of making it here, they are stunned by its beauty, and who can blame them, we have almost every gift nature could give a nation. Forests, mountains, water falls, the source of River Nile is here and so is one of the biggest lakes of the world.
No wonder then how many fabulous pictures will be taken out of Africa with breathtaking scenery, but the question is: what other pictures are people leaving with? I have seen pictures of children in villages with torn shirts, bare feet and running noses, some congested markets, overloaded trucks and potholed roads. Many years later one would expect that things will improve a bit, a little bit at least! Alas, that is not happening.
Speaking to a friend who sells art objects to tourists, I was informed that the most selling items are bicycles that carry a whole family on board, plus a goat and a banana bunch! Then wooden or clay taxis (Matatu) that are overloaded with passengers, heads squeezed, feet and hands hanging out - in other words, symbols of chaos - and who is making these pieces? Local craftsmen.
I once asked a Masai man in a market why are they making Masai warriors as door stoppers. I asked him: do you know that someone will be kicking the warrior's head with their feet to open and close the door? He looked unconcerned.
Like many other parts of the world, Africa has its ups and downs, but portraying poverty and confusion will only perpetuate this image and will undermine any efforts to get out of this vicious circle. So, without me sounding like a bowl that feels hotter than the soup itself, I am hoping someone will come up with more appropriate African souvenirs.
Ms Ebrahimi is a social commentator

Original_Search_Title: Ripples Over Al Qaeda Planned Base in Nation - Govt Debunks Claim - Experts Examine Alleged Bid
Headline: Nigeria: Ripples Over Al Qaeda Planned Base in Nation - Govt Debunks Claim - Experts Examine Alleged Bid
Body_Text:
Qaeda's alleged plan to set up a command and control base in Nigeria is creating diplomatic ripples as Nigeria debunked the claim, last week, insisting that country is not a safe haven for anarchist groups. Indications, however, emerged that the pro-jihadist group may have chosen to set up its operational base in Nigeria in its terrorist war with America and the Western countries.
Britain's intelligence MI5 had allegedly warned the prime minister, Mr. David Cameron, that Al-Qaeda wants to set up a command and control centre in Nigeria to gain access to the United Kingdom (UK) where there are dozens of flights from Lagos to London daily.
FG dismisses claim
Officials of Federal Ministry of Foreign Affairs in Abuja dismissed the claim in an interview with Sunday Vanguard, calling it a brilliant piece of fiction that suits the mood of the times. An official of the ministry, who pleaded confidentiality, said: "That report did not substantiate its claim but just preferred to cite the Boko Haram uprising to buttress it. It is a perfect conjecture of the author's imagination.
It is obvious that haters of Nigeria will capitalise on the Boko Haram incident to further dent the image of the country." The official will not elaborate on why the FG has not issued an official statement on the claim that was published on July 4, 2011, saying "government does not conduct its operations based on media reports."
Calculations
But diplomatic sources in Lagos cited several factors that may have favoured Al-Qaeda strategy in considering using Nigeria as a launch pad. First, according to the sources, is the issue of lack of consensus among the ruling elite on the acquisition and use of political power which is tearing them from the different parts of the country apart. The sources explained that the winner takes it all mentality and the attitude of 'let's destroy if it is not ours' has paved the way for a deep gulf that external forces may capitalise on politically and economically.
"The people who lead radical groups like Al-Qaeda are smart politically and will cash-in on this to their advantage. This is what the multinational oil companies are doing and a multinational terrorist group like Al-Qaeda is very smart", one of the sources told Sunday Vanguard. Another critical factor listed is the issue of Nigeria's international borders and weak internal security network. Said our source: "Our porous borders make it possible for narco-terrorism and gun running to thrive.
Narco-terrorism thrives in this kind of environment because it provides the anarchists easy access to funds to pursue their cause. With the global community targeting the source of funds used by terrorists, they would want a country where the banking regulations are weak and the internal security is lax. @With the drug war in Mexico being part of the global strategy to cut off the source of funds through narco-terrorism, it is obvious that the evil men would look for another safe haven, which is where Nigeria with it porous borders becomes attractive because it makes it easier for them to move in and out of the country without much hindrance. They could consider recruitment of attackers from neigbouring countries which have been identified as providing personnel to unleash mayhem in Nigeria".
Thirdly, the state institutions in Nigeria are considered to be weak, especially the law enforcement agencies and the judiciary which are believed to be reluctant to apprehend and punish suspected terrorists. "It is one thing to have the anti-terrorism legislation and another to have the political will to enforce it. Unfortunately, there is no political will to do that and, when you have a large army of unemployed young people who want easy access to wealth, rogue groups like Al- Qaeda will easily recruit people to do its jobs. For these deprived people, their love for money is high than the level of love for their country. In a country where a lot of its citizens are living below the poverty line national interest it is so easy to compromise," the diplomatic source said.
"We know that countries like Yemen, Somalia, Kenya were considered by Al-Qaeda as potential candidates for their operational base but Nigeria is strategic as an influential country in sub-Sahara Africa with the highest Muslim population and a high concentration of western economic interests with endemic corruption, high incidence of poverty and fervent fundamentalist fire in the northern part of the country. It is so easy to compromise national interest for material gains."
But a US based security consultant told Sunday Vanguard: "Since the killing of Osama Bin Laden, the feared Al-Qaeda leader, in May, relations between the US and Pakistan has been at the lowest ebb because of the violation of the sovereignty of the Asian country. The targeting of known Al-Qadea leaders worldwide has made those countries unsafe for them and there is no guarantee that Nigeria will be a secure base for Al-Qaeda. I have doubts that Nigeria, especially the north, will provide the expected safe haven for Al Qaeda."
Mrs. Uchenn C. Ekwueme, an expert in international relations, said the Federal Government must address the issues that make Nigeria a potential base for the Al-Qaeda instead of sweeping the claim under the carpet. According to her, there is social discontent at all the social strata and this poses serious threats to national security. "The threat from MASSOB, OPC, Niger Delta militants and now Boko Haram raises serious concerns on the ability of the state to enforce its laws", she stated.
"The possibility of Al-Qaeda setting up shop in Nigeria will have serious implication for Nigeria's economic relations with the industrialised nations who believe that their economic interests in Nigeria will be imperiled. Nigeria cannot afford to have its sovereignty violated as we have seen in Pakistan and Afganistan which have been turned into banana republics, but, above all Nigeria cannot afford to descend to the status of a rogue state."
International community rattled
Diplomatic sources claimed United States and the European Union (EU) have become increasingly concerned and watchful of the developments in Nigeria. "The revelation of the alleged Al-Qaeda plan to operate from Nigeria has caused a stir among the western countries, the US and other friendly countries who are lining up support for the Federal Government to tackle the scourge of terrorism rearing its head in Nigeria", one source pointed out.
The issue of terrorism featured prominently in last week's bilateral meeting and talks between German chancellor Angela Merkel and President Goodluck Jonathan in Abuja while some other European countries, notably Britain and France, are set to respond to the new security challenge facing Nigeria.
The issue of training of security personnel and counter intelligence equipment, sharing of intelligence are areas Nigeria expects external support to combat terrorism.
Mrs Ekwueme said the urban terrorism introduced by Boko Haram and the ferocity of their attacks has raised diplomatic stakes for Nigeria as the leadership will now communicate serious diplomatic issues rather than going to the world fora to ask for support to tackle malaria and typhoid.
"Now, we can engage the world diplomatically even though the Boko Haram is of negative value. We know that urban terrorism Boko Haram has introduced has caught the security agencies napping as they never expected people carrying and detonating explosive devices at random," she added.

Original_Search_Title: Driver, Two Others Remanded for Allegedly Committing Robbery
Headline: Ghana: Driver, Two Others Remanded for Allegedly Committing Robbery
Body_Text:
A driver and two others have been arraigned before an Accra circuit court for allegedly robbing a taxi driver of his taxi cab. The accused persons were accused of robbing the taxi, worth GH Â¢7,000.00 from the complainant after threatening him with a knife and a cutlass.
The accused persons, George Addai, driver, Joseph Idan, Alias Abeiku, auto mechanic and one K.K., currently at large, are facing two counts of conspiracy to commit crime and robbery.
The first and second accused persons, Addai and Idan respectively, who were present in court, pleaded not guilty on all the charges leveled against them before the court, presided over by Mr. Eric Kyei Baffour. Both accused persons have been remanded in custody to re-appear before the court on July 25, 2011.
The prosecuting officer, Assistant Superintendent of Police (ASP) Reynolds Agyemang, briefing the court on the facts of the case noted that on July 1, 2011 at about 3:15am, the complainant was working with his taxi and on reaching the traffic light intersection at Flamingo, a suburb of Accra Addai, the first accused approached him and asked him to convey his mother from Mataheko to Sowutuom, all suburbs of Accra.
He said Addai boarded the taxi and directed him to where he was to pick his mother. According to him after moving a short distance from the traffic intersection, he asked the complainant to stop which he obliged.
"Immediately he stopped, the first accused person pulled a knife and pointed it at the right ribs of the complainant, and ordered him to get out of the vehicle. The second accused person, who was armed with a cutlass and hiding behind a fence also rushed on the complainant and pulled him from behind the steering wheel, collected his weekly sales of GH Â¢185.00 and sped off with the vehicle", the prosecutor noted.
He indicated that the complainant made a report to the Kaneshie police, where a message on the robbery was re-laid to all police stations for the arrest of the suspects and the vehicle.
ASP Agyemang mentioned that on July 5, 2011, the police had a tip off that the accused persons has removed the original license plate of the vehicle and replaced it with Drivers Vehicle License Authority (DVLA) trail plate number DV 2560-10 and re-sprayed the yellow portions of the vehicle to white.
The prosecutor said the police therefore mounted surveillance at areas of Russia/Sukura a suburb of Accra, where the accused persons were seen driving the vehicle towards Banana Inn also a suburb of Accra.
"They were arrested and during interrogation they confessed committing the offence. In their caution statement, they stated that It was one K.K, now at large, who gave them the GH Â¢50.00 to re-spray the vehicle and the DVLA trial plate to fix on the vehicle", the prosecutor indicated.

Original_Search_Title: The Sooner We Reach Consensus On EPAs, the Better
Headline: Uganda: The Sooner We Reach Consensus On EPAs, the Better
Body_Text:
While I share many of the concerns expressed by my colleague and friend Ambassador Nathan Irumba in a statement he issued on behalf of several Civil Society Organisations and published by the East African recently, I would like to state that I concur with some of the views expressed by EU Ambassador Roberto Ridolfi in his article on the EPAs published in the Saturday Monitor of July 2. As one who represented Uganda at the EU- African, Caribbean and Pacific Group of States (ACP) negotiations from 2004 - 2008, I welcome the clarifications he made of the issues on which there is deadlock, misunderstanding, disinformation and even misrepresentation of facts. Much as I appreciate the support we have received from NGOs during the negotiations, I feel some of our friends in the NGO fraternity often act, to the dismay of members of the ACP, as if they were more Catholic than the Pope!
In addition to the offer the EU has made to the EAC to grant 100 per cent duty free and quota free access to the EU market, the EU made a similar offer in 2000 to all least developed countries (LDCs) at the 3rd United Nations Conference on LCDs held in Brussels. I was a member of the Ugandan delegation to that conference.
Since 2000, Uganda has not taken full advantage of that offer made by the EU, which is commonly known as EBA, ie duty free and quota free access of everything but arms, from LDC countries, including Uganda. For example, the embassy of Uganda in Brussels negotiated duty free and quota free access of Ugandan honey to the EU market in 2005, but as far as I know, Uganda has not exported even one tonne of honey to the EU market.
This is the crux of the matter. Uganda and most African countries simply lack the capacity to produce sufficient quantities of commodities and goods for sale on the world market. Our leaders have a tendency to talk big about our capacity to produce commodities and goods for international trade when in reality we produce very little for export on predictable and sustainable basis.
I would like to make an appeal to all Ugandan stakeholders to re-visit our rather rigid, and to some extent, unrealistic position on the EPAs, which is perhaps a result of a lack of in-depth understanding of the history and substance of the negotiations. In addition, during my tour of duty in Brussels, when the current EPAs negotiations were launched, then EU Trade Commissioner, Peter Mandelson's rather arrogant and abrasive manner did not help matters.
If his predecessor, Pascal Lamy, had been in charge of the EU's trade docket, I am sure a compromise would have been realised by now. As a relatively small trading bloc in world trade, ACP countries have more to gain than the EU, whose principal trading partners are the USA and Asian countries. Hence, the sooner we reach consensus on EPAs, the better for us.
By definition and by nature, all negotiations involve give and take. You win some and lose some. I sometimes get the impression that some of the stakeholders in the EPAs negotiations don't seem to appreciate this cardinal fact; they would like our side take, but give nothing in return which reminds me of a slogan of Northcote Hall of Makerere University of the 1960s: "We either win or you lose".
Part of our problem is that we lack skilled and experienced negotiators. Most African ambassadors accredited to the EU are political appointees, not career diplomats. In fact, most of them were politicians who had lost in general elections. So what else can one expect from these expired politicians who cannot even deliver in their banana Republics!
In the final analysis, the EU as well as the ACP Group must summon the necessary political will to resolve the impasse in the EPAs negotiations. There is need to strengthen the economic, political and historical links and relations which bind us for the mutual benefit of the peoples of Europe, Africa, the Caribbean and the Pacific. I sincerely hope that a new economic partnership agreement will soon be concluded and signed by the EU and ACP Countries.
Mr Acemah is a retired career diplomat and a former Ambassador of Uganda accredited to the EU in Brussels

Original_Search_Title: Rural HIV Affected Sell Domestic Animals to Access Art
Headline: Zimbabwe: Rural HIV Affected Sell Domestic Animals to Access Art
Body_Text:
Imagine an 80 year old grand mother selling her three and only chickens in order to get bus fare to take her five year old granddaughter to the nearest clinic 15 kilometers away for Anti retroviral Therapy. At the clinic the aged grandmother is ordered to fork out $3 for medical cards before she collects the ARVs for child who was orphaned by HIV and AIDS.
From the sale of the three chickens, the desperate grandparent earned $6, she needs $2 for transport to and from the clinic, $3 for getting the medical cards and administration fees. She is left with $1 which she then uses to buy some bananas for the hungry, frail sick child.
Such is the situation in the country's rural areas the HIV positive and AIDs affected communities are facing. Executive Director, Dr Vhumani Magezi Family AIDS Caring Trust (FACT) Zimbabwe carried out national research on the coping mechanisms of rural communities living with AIDS. The report revealed that the affected and infected, some of them old and most frail, all desperate, are resorting to selling their domestic animals and other property to access treatment.
Dr Magezi reported at an HIV conference recently that the situation out there is unbearable. He added that the young and energetic are the only ones who are able to get part time jobs to fund access to ARVs. These drugs are free, but service charges and transport costs are the greatest hindering factor. The old end up selling their only wealth, their chickens and their goats to get bus fare to the clinics which are more often than not at quite a distance.
If the government is serious about the universal access to ART surely there is urgent need to decentralize AIDS services. Health workers or care givers employed by the government should be made mobile to reach the rural communities so that the vulnerable and the disadvantaged get access to the life saving treatment.

Original_Search_Title: Art, Crafts Sales Drop As Dollar Rate Shoots Up
Headline: Uganda: Art, Crafts Sales Drop As Dollar Rate Shoots Up
Body_Text:
Kampala — Business owners dealing in African cultural wear and crafts have recorded a 30% drop in sales owing to the depreciating Shilling against the Dollar.
Mr. Jackson Tumwebaze, who owns a crafts shop at the National Theatre is worried that sales will drop further as the Dollar rate rises. The Dollar is currently trading at Ugshs2,700 from Shs2,200 at the beginning of the year. This has caused a rise in prices of many items especially fuel and food making it difficult for people to buy items such as crafts which are considered luxuries.
Tumwebaze who declined to pronounce his annual earnings from the business, says in June alone his sales have dropped by 30%. The target customers of such items are mostly tourists from the West who normally buy in dollars. With the fluctuating rates of the dollar, craft shops attendants have failed to establish a stable price for their commodities.
"The increase in the Dollar has made us fail to get appropriate prices for the various items and this has highly affected the sales since some of the foreigners have abandoned our products because they think we are cheating them by not setting a uniform price," said Mr. Charles Kunobwa a shop attendant at Uganda Small Scale Industries Association (USSIA) craft centre in Kampala.
Crafts vendors fear that this might affect their annual earnings since these fluctuations have occurred in June the time they normally record high sales.
"It's in June that we make most sales because more foreigners flood into the country for their summer holidays and buy these items," added Kunobwa.
"The unrests that have recently been affecting Uganda had scared away some of our customers. Tourists have just started coming in and this means that it's now that we have started making sales yet we are experiencing the Dollar increase to further reduce our earnings since some of our customers will be automatically laid off," complained Ms Irene Maliza, owns a craft shop in Kampala.
She said Ugandans are not the target for crafts because most of them find foreign products more attractive and also at times they know where to get these commodities other than buying from them.
They mainly deal in commodities such as 'kitenge' (African clothes), African shoes, bags, crafts, beads, curving, Vaseline mainly made out of ghee and milk, earrings, African chairs and mats, decorations and necklaces. These commodities are mainly bought to be used by people as gifts to friends, sitting room decorations and others.
Most of these products are got from local materials such as hides and skins, soak stone mainly got from karamoja region for craft carving, bamboo, sisal, elephant grass, banana fibers, and ghee among others.
In addition to the Dollar increase, high craft prices have been caused by the increase in the price of hides and skins since most of these commodities such as carpets, bags and shoes are made out of hides.
"Most of us have our own workshops where we make these products but now the price of raw materials such as hides is soon driving me out of business since people cannot afford buying at a very high price with the current economic crisis," explained Ms Halima Nabiryo, a vendor at Crafts Africa in Kampala.
Prices of African wear and crafts are most times higher than imported fabric mainly because these commodities because they are labour intensive and therefore command thorough attention during the process of production and they also need a lot of time to curve and make them attractive to the buyer.

Original_Search_Title: ICC Should Reject Muthaura's Lawyers, And This is Why
Headline: Kenya: ICC Should Reject Muthaura's Lawyers, And This is Why
Body_Text:
Nairobi — They say "one bad apple spoils the bunch". This is what may have happened to the defence team for Head of Civil Service Francis Muthaura.
Several weeks ago, QC Karim Khan, Mr Muthaura's lead counsel, hired two former ICC prosecutors to join his team.
The two -- Essa Faal and Shyamala Alagendra -- had only recently left the employ of ICC Prosecutor Luis Moreno-Ocampo.
Mr Faal is Gambian, while Ms Alagendra is Malaysian.
It's unheard of in any civilised legal jurisdiction for a lawyer to work for the prosecution, and then abscond and work for the defence in the same case.
This isn't what happened here, but the conduct of Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra is almost certainly unethical and unprofessional.
The legal question at stake is whether Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra -- while working as prosecutors for the ICC -- acquired any knowledge about Mr Moreno-Ocampo's thinking, strategies, and planning on Mr Muthaura's case.
If so, was such knowledge limited to Mr Muthaura's case, or did it involve the entire "PNU" case?
Inside knowledge
That is, did Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra also have "inside" knowledge about the cases against Deputy Prime Minister Uhuru Kenyatta and former Police Commissioner Hussein Ali?
Let's take this to its logical conclusion. Did the two lawyers acquire any "inside" information about the "ODM" case against Eldoret North MP William Ruto, Tinderet MP Henry Kosgey and radio journalist Joshua arap Sang? That would be nuclear -- really explosive
Why would any "information" or "knowledge" acquired by Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra while at the ICC on Mr Muthaura's case -- or the cases against any of the Ocampo Six -- be toxic?
The answer is simple. If true, then Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra would be guilty of professional misconduct. Their behaviour would clearly be unethical.
In the United States, for example, the idea of a lawyer working on both sides of a case is unthinkable. The guilty lawyer would be severely sanctioned, and possibly disbarred.
That's why former DPP nominee Kioko Kilukumi would have been in a legal quandary in that post because he was Mr Ruto's lawyer against whom the State had a case. Justice is about appearances.
I understand a lawyer's professional responsibilities. I know that every defendant and litigant, or party to a suit, deserves legal representation. Only lawyers can provide such representation.
That's why we shouldn't demonise lawyers who represent the mafia or any other unpopular defendants. It's our job as lawyers to represent our clients before the courts.
That's how justice is done, and it's what makes a legal system function, and gives it legitimacy in the eyes of the people.
Far be it from me to ridicule so-called "lawyers for the mob," the corrupt, or the murderous. I want to make it clear that I don't question the right of Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra to represent Mr Muthaura.
But I question whether they are eating from both sides of the same plate. I know the Office of the Prosecutor at the ICC quite well.
It's a small "law firm" with few prosecutors. Everybody knows everybody. Prosecutors are very collegial.
They "share" ideas and "bounce" strategies against each other. It's a very dedicated and professional group of lawyers.
It's not uncommon for one prosecutor to walk into the office of another to "pick their brain" on some legal question.
This is how "law firms" work. Is it possible that during any of these "collegial" chats, moments, or consultations Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra got inside information, or knowledge, about the Muthaura case, or of any of the Ocampo Six?
I believe the judges of the ICC have an obligation to determine whether Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra have behaved unethically, or unprofessionally -- or even unlawfully -- and whether they should be barred from representing Mr Muthaura.
But the matter of their misconduct, if established, wouldn't end there. I believe that the court would have to bar the entire Muthaura legal team from representing him.
Why? It's straightforward -- if Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra had shared any of the "illicit" information with the rest of Mr Muthaura's defence team, then the entire lot is tainted.
One apple spoils the whole bunch. The same goes for any of the lawyers for the other Ocampo Six -- they would have to go too.
I don't want to speculate why Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra are engaging in what seems like a high wire act.
Perhaps it's the money Mr Muthaura has thrown at them, or the chance to represent a high profile client to establish themselves as serious defence counsel.
Perhaps it's the adrenaline telling them to take on Mr Moreno-Ocampo and their old colleagues.
Moreno-Ocampo's thinking
But I know why Mr Muthaura has hired them. He believes that they are "insiders" who know the thinking of Mr Moreno-Ocampo, his prosecutors, and the ICC judges.
It's like having a spy from the other side on your team. It's the next best thing to breaking into your opponent's office and stealing his files.
But whether or not Mr Faal and Ms Alagendra have acted improperly, I believe that the ICC's reputation has already been injured.
This "revolving door" where prosecutors can go work for the defence in the blink of an eye stinks to high heaven.
It diminishes the legitimacy and moral standing of the court. It makes the ICC look like a court in a "banana republic".
That's why the judges must resolve this matter in a manner that safeguards its credibility. Otherwise, justice will be viewed as having been compromised.
Makau Mutua is Dean and SUNY Distinguished Professor at the State University of New York at Buffalo Law School and Chair of the KHRC.

Original_Search_Title: Wandegeya Through Sudanese Eyes
Headline: Uganda: Wandegeya Through Sudanese Eyes
Body_Text:
The early morning church bells and amplified voice of the Wandegeya mosque Mu'azzin at 5:45am broke Arwa Elrabie's fitful sleep, reminding her that it was time to chase her dreams and ambitions.
The previous day, freshly landed at Entebbe Airport from Sudan, Arwa had been driven straight to Akamwesi Hostel, right in the middle of Katanga, a Kampala suburb. Here, she would stay for two months as she improves her English at Makerere University. Little did she know what awaited her. "I will never forget the day I first jumped onto a boda boda.
I shouted a lot but felt ashamed because I am a big woman. I was scared by the youth riding it. I froze within my sit and hugged him tight, fearing I would fall," Arwa says.
For Arwa, this was a new experience, having never seen boda bodas in Sudan. The whirlpool of dusty air that engulfs the riders as they speed and cris-cross through backed-up cars shook her to the core. She has since given up on them, regardless of how late she would be. Instead, she hires a cab, whose driver, Eric is available whenever she calls him.
Then came the market ordeal.
"Auntie, muzungu, come and look at dis wannu also," the market vendors chorus as dirty black hands hold onto her, pulling her, left, right, up and down. To Arwa, the market is like a plague, one that can empty pockets within minutes.
"I saw many things in the market like bananas, pineapples and meat. Two girls, Olivia and Beatrice, even taught me how to bargain so I always tip them when I buy things cheaply," she says.
Once, some men wanted to cheat her but she had learnt the better part of bargaining. With this skill, shopping has become an everyday thing.
"I am proud because they call me a beautiful woman and they even greet me in Islam," Arwa adds.
In Khartoum, second-hand clothes are never heard of. To Arwa's surprise, Wandegeya is full of second-hand clothes preferably called mivumba.
