Shoes are her best pick because she can only equate them to those available in the Queen's land. She says this is good business because anyone can afford to purchase mivumba and look smart.
The weather, too, has been a surprise.
"It suddenly rains and then it's hot again. My face has darkened because of the weather; but these changes do not bother me because I respect my roots and want to remain like this," she shrugs.
Although Arwa was cautioned not to reside in Akamwesi Hostel, her instincts drove her there because that is where many of her friends had patched. But all is not a nightmare. Akamwesi becomes a discotheque at night and because it is holiday time, young people dance to different music, extending Arwa's sleeping hours. And she likes the experience.
But all these travails pale in contrast to what brought her here: communicating in English. See, Arwa is here to improve on her conversation, reading and writing skills at the School of Language, Literature and Communication at Makerere University. Learning a foreign language has its challenges, but perhaps her greatest challenge is that some English terms and phrases are hard to grasp and raising her hand to ask the lecturer is hard; she feels embarrassed given her age over 40.
But learning is a process and Arwa is encouraged by the different accents she hears in class. Studying has even raised her expectations; she's slowly catching up with one of her dreams.
"I hope to improve my English and be a better saleswoman. I also hope to bring my two children to study in Uganda because Ugandans are every welcoming people," Arwa notes.
Arwa is a lawyer and she deals with human rights cases, managing legal violence against women in northern Sudan. She also writes in a local newspaper, the Elsahafa and she researches in Sudanese music.
She also works in a local organization that deals with human rights. With a month left on her sojourn, Arwa is stuffing the best of experiences into her never-forget baggage. This she will narrate to her family back in Sudan.
For now, she is getting integrated into the Ugandan vuvuzela of cultures that only get better by the day.

Original_Search_Title: Police Grab Car Syndicate
Headline: Ghana: Police Grab Car Syndicate
Body_Text:
Two car snatchers have been arrested by the Accra Regional Police Command, and are currently in police custody.
The alleged suspects are Idun Joseph, 30, and George Marfo, alias Ageorgia, 27. They are currently in police custody for snatching a Kia Pride taxi cab from its driver, Eric Owusu Appiagyie.
Briefing The Chronicle in Accra, the Accra Regional Crime Officer, Superintendent Emmanuel Frank Adufati, said on July 1, 2011, at about 3:00 a.m., the two suspects hired the Eric Owusu Appiagyie, who drives a taxi cab with the registration number GR 8353-11, from Flamingo to Sowutuom, a suburb of Accra.
According to him, on reaching Sowutuom, the suspects attacked the complainant with a cutlass and took the car away. The driver later made a report to the Kaneshie police station, he added.
Superintendent Adufati said on July 5, 2011, at about 9:30 p.m., the Accra Regional Police Command had information that two men with a suspected stolen car were in Russia, a suburb of Accra, to sell it to an unsuspecting buyer.
The police, upon a tip off, immediately dispatched a team of detectives from the Regional Crime Investigative Department (CID) to Russia, who met the two suspects offering the KIA Pride then with a plate number DV 5260 V for sale.
On seeing the police, Superintendent Adufati said the suspects sped off, but were hotly pursued until they were arrested on the Mamprobi-Banana Inn road.
Superintendent Adufati said upon interrogation, the suspects, Idun and Marfo, admitted snatching the car from the complainant on the night of July 1, 2011 at the aforementioned place.
Superintendent Adufati said investigations revealed that the suspects changed the status of the car by spraying and replacing the original registration number plate "GR 8353-11" with a plate number DV 5260 V.
He said the police had intensified investigations to ascertain the veracity of the case.
The Crime Officer advised the public, especially, taxi drivers, to be cautious when operating at night.

Original_Search_Title: Coast Food Prices Skyrocket
Headline: Kenya: Coast Food Prices Skyrocket
Body_Text:
Mombasa residents continue to struggle to feed themselves as the cost of food items continues to soar. The growth in commodities prices is disturbing as parents struggle to feed their families.
According to Mwembe Tayari Market chairman Ishmael Abdulrahman, food commodities have shot through the ceiling because of the high transportation charges brought about by increase in fuel prices and hoarding of cereals.
Cereals prices have skyrocketed in the last three days. A bag of Maize which sold for Sh2,000 is now selling at Sh5,000; a bag of beans has jumped to Sh8,000 from Sh5,000. Fruits are also expensive and scarce now. A bunch of bananas is now selling at Sh80, up from 20. Tomatoes are in short supply. They are now selling at Sh2,000, up from Sh1,200 a crate.
A Mombasa resident Joel Akumu said that his family has now turned to millet which he said is a bit cheaper. "I cannot afford maize anymore and my children will die if I cannot buy something for them. We use millet flour," he said. Abdulrahman said that most goods are likely to be very expensive if the situation is not going to be controlled.

Original_Search_Title: Wages of War - My Experience in the Mogadishu Frontline
Headline: Kenya: Wages of War - My Experience in the Mogadishu Frontline
Body_Text:
Star reporter FRANCIS MUREITHI is just back from Mogadishu, Somalia, and narrates about his experience in this ghost town where gunshots are the norm
It is by all means and descriptions a ghost town, perhaps displaying magnificently the wages of war. The once beautiful capital city at the coast of the Indian Ocean near the Equator, Mogadishu, the capital of troubled Somalia, is phantom of what it was 21 years ago.
I depart for Mogadishu at the Jomo Kenyatta International Airport at exactly 7.45am via African Express Airways, a commercial airline, with my return ticket costing US$420 (Sh37,800).
Inside the plane, I notice a sizeable number of well-dressed Somali men and women, some with their children aged between one and 10 years. They are chatting in Somali language, which I can hardly comprehend. But their laughter and facial expression tells me that they are a happy lot. I assume they are Somali citizens, or were once Somali citizens who ran away from their country as a result of the armed conflict which begun in 1991 after the ouster of then President Siad Barre.
It is still not yet clear to me why anyone would want to return to a war-torn country, and making it worse with little children. I pose the question to three other Kenyan journalists whom I am traveling with, but no one has a tangible explanation.
At exactly 9am, we land at the Aden-Adde International Airport in Mogadishu. Constructed in the 1940s by the Italians, this facility was originally known as the Mogadishu International Airport.
However, when it was reopened after years of closure due to the war, the now ruling Transitional Federal Government (TFG) announced that the airport would be renamed to honour the first president of Somalia, Aden Abdullah Osman Daar, who died in Nairobi in June, 2007.
Temperatures are soaring, reminding me of Mombasa. The airport is far much small compared to JKIA. It is almost the size of the recently upgraded Kenya's airport in Wajir in North Eastern province. But at least both small and large planes, even some carrying over 100 passengers, can still land with much ease here in Mogadishu.
The Indian Ocean is a few metres from the airport's runway, making this country a perfect nation for tourists from all over the world. But due to the war and insecurity coupled with reports of pirates and radical groups such as Al Shabaab, the Al Qaeda linked militant group hijacking foreigners, tourism is just a pipedream - at least at the moment.
Some years back, this airport was being used by the Somali Air Force. The Air Force, which is now history as a result of war, had an airlift wing stationed here. The air force also maintained a military academy at this airport and was used by all members of Somali armed forces.
But today, the facility is in the firm grip of the heavily-armed Ugandan soldiers who are operating under the African Union Mission in Somalia (AMISOM).
As I walk towards the immigration offices, I notice dozens of AMISOM soldiers clutching to their guns. Far across, I spot a dozen of AMISOM military vehicles, mainly lorries and army tankers, all mounted with machine guns and rocket launchers, ready for action if need be. Some are facing the Indian Ocean.
I am, together with my three colleagues, received by our contact, a senior intelligence officer. She is a Somali lady in her early 20s who introduces herself as from the TFG government. She informs us that her special assignment is to ensure we stay safe in Mogadishu.
Before we pass through the immigration offices, I ask the intelligence officer, who introduces herself only as Halima, who the many men, women and children coming back to Somalia are. "They are Somalis who have been away. Some are coming back after over 10 years abroad. There is relative peace now especially here at the capital city. Some are just visiting since it is holiday season where they have been living," she explains.
I am surprised that she is mixing her perfect English with equally perfect Swahili. She later explains that though she was born in Mogadishu in the late 1980s, she grew up in Nairobi's Eastleigh after her parents escaped from Somalia due to the war.
For us to be cleared at the immigration desk, each one of us pays US$50 (4,500) for a one-week visa. We are told we will be required to pay another US$ 20 (Sh1, 800) when we leave the country. The money, we are told, is what the TFG government relies on to pay its workers, mainly soldiers who have been battling with Al Shabaab.
Outside, a team of six heavily-armed TFG soldiers are waiting for us. "This will be your security from today until you leave Mogadishu. They will always be ahead of your vehicle, and they will be staying at the entrance of your hotel whenever you are inside that hotel. And no one goes anywhere without me and them," the intelligence officer explains.
She also explains that orders from her seniors is that she keep a hawk eye on us and to sleep in the same hotel with us until the day we will leave Mogadishu. "Sometime back, some French guys came and we were providing security to them but at night we were leaving for our homes. One night, the Al Shabaab sneaked in and took them away. It is now over two years and they are still being held captive and the French government does not want to pay ransom to secure their release," explains Halima.
Due to the war and the influx of foreigners either on peace or humanitarian missions in Somalia, the economy has been 'dollarised' and life is expensive compared to Kenya. Halima explains that for accommodation alone, each one of us has to part with US100 (Sh9, 000) per night. We are also collectively required to pay each of the six soldiers US$20 (Sh1, 800) per day - bringing the total bill per day on security alone to US120 (Sh10,800).
In addition, we are required to pay US$ 200 (Sh18, 000) per day for the two vehicles we will be using. One vehicle, a pickup, is specifically for security men and the second one, a fully closed and tinted four-wheel, is ours.
This means that for you to survive in Mogadishu, you require at least Sh37,800 per day. And that budget is outside any other expense you may incur here, for example buying a coke soda to cool yourself, with a bottle retailing at US$ 2, which is equivalent of Somali Shillings 60,000 (Sh180). Back in Kenya, the bottle of Coke costs Sh50.
The sound of gunshots is the order of the day in Mogadishu. AMISOM trucks with heavily-armed soldiers are zooming across the battered streets of Mogadishu. TFG forces, in all sorts of military uniform, are also zooming in different vehicles or patrolling the streets with AK47 rifles in their hands.
Despite the war, the killings and destructions, there are a sizeable number of Somalis still in the capital, men and women and occasionally one can spot children playing with toy guns. Despite the occasional gunshots, there is a semblance of peace. No more guns are on sale in the streets as it used to be.
There are a dozen of matatus ferrying passengers from one area of Mogadishu to the other. Interestingly, no vehicle in Mogadishu has a license or a number plate or even insurance. Some are driven without windscreens while in others, having a door is a luxury.
There are, however, others in a perfect condition, all fighting for space along the potholed Mogadishu roads. A few shops are open. Smallscale businessmen and women are trying to trade in the streets.
From jerrycans of petrol, diesel and kerosene being sold in the streets to foodstuff such as fruits, mandazi and meat, the business is only concentrated in one area which is firmly under the control of AMISOM and TFG forces. "Business is slowly returning, but here anything can happen, from a shootout to a bomb. We have to be on the lookout always but at least TFG is trying to bring security," explains one vendor through one of our security guys who speaks English.
No visitor is allowed to stop at one point for long, and locals are also always on the move, or else a sniper, hidden in one of the many ruined buildings, gets into action. "There are many snipers, and you cannot know where one is hiding. Many people have died here after being shot at by snipers. You will just see someone falling, and you will never know where the bullet is coming from and why it was fired," explains the soldier with strained English. Shamo Hotel, where we are paying Sh9,000 per night, is in good shape and is of commendable standards considering this is Mogadishu.
All food cooked here are mainly imported, from Ethiopia and the Netherlands. We are, however, told that bananas and pawpaw are grown in some districts of Somalia which have been enjoying relative peace.
At night, gunshots are heard, some very close from the hotel. "That is normal for us here; we are used to. Maybe it is not even something serious, perhaps it is someone whose car has been blocked by matatus on the road and he wants to pass. So he fires," explains Halima, the intelligence officer.
On day two in Mogadishu, we tour the frontline. The frontline is regarded as the most dangerous zone, because this is the line separating the zone controlled by the TFG and AMISOM and the zone under Al Shabaab militants.
Some Ugandan soldiers are hesitant to allow us cross what they term as "safe line" and enter the battle zone. After a few phone calls, both in Nairobi and Uganda, we are allowed to cross over, but at our own risk. "We have destroyed all these buildings trying to chase Al Shabaab, sometimes we are inside this building and the building right next to it is under Al Shabaab and we have to battle until one gives up and leaves the ruined building, or is killed inside there," explains an Ugandan soldier who is in his early 20s. "We have managed to push them further away, but last week, they killed the commander of this base. So for now we have been told not to advance, but to wait for additional soldiers and weapons," explains the army man as we walk through rubbles which once used to be beautiful buildings of Mogadishu town. At one point, the gunshots increase, and one of the Kenyan journalists refuses to advance fearing for his life. We leave him behind under the watch of several Ugandan soldiers.
Occasionally, AMISOM with their heavy war weapons fire rockets towards the Al Shabaab zone, just to make the militant group aware that it is being watched.
Presently, neither the Al Shabaab nor the TFG and AMISOM is trying to advance in either side. The two groups are keenly monitoring the actions of the other, and vice versa.
Occasionally, gunshots are fired from the Al Shabaab side towards AMISOM and TFG controlled zone, and the government soldiers return fire, sometimes shooting with heavy artillery. "We are now controlling 60 to 75 per cent of Mogadishu, and we want to control the entire city in a few months," explains a senior Ugandan soldier who gives his name as Ben Ali.
Uganda soldiers, including women are controlling Mogadishu, while those from Burundi are controlling other zones outside what once used to be the Mogadishu central business district.
The two countries have contributed about 8,000 soldiers to assist the TFG. Burundi has contributed at least 3,500 troops to the AMISOM force while the remaining soldiers are from Uganda. The soldiers take us through the underground tunnels the Al Shabaab had been using to advance and launch attacks.
The frontline where we are at is about 2km from State House. "We recently uncovered a tunnel which they were digging towards State House, we captured their expert in digging these tunnels," says Halima, who says she has been in this frontline many times assisting the soldiers to advance.
In our second day in Mogadishu, we meet the head of Somalia National Security Agency, who alongside his heavy security escort, takes us to where TFG soldiers killed Fazul Abdullah Muhammed, a senior Al Qaeda operative in East Africa. It is about 15 minutes drive from Mogadishu central business district.
Day three in Mogadishu, we meet Somali President Sheikh Sharif Sheikh Ahmed at his State House in Mogadishu. Again, AMISOM is fully in control here, as far as security is concerned.
A dozen of AMISOM military tanks are parked here, just in case of any eventuality. President Sheikh Sharif, a humble-looking man, exudes confidence that his transitional government, which has been added one more year, will within that period crash Al Shabaab and take full control of not only Mogadishu but also the entire Somalia. "It is true that Mogadishu is now safer, we have been attacking Al Shabaab and Al Qaeda and now they have started retreating," explains the President in his well-furnished room befitting that of a Head of State.
While leaving State House, I hear gunshots, perhaps a few metres away. No one here seems bothered at all because of the gunshots, to them, this is an ordinary occurrence. It is the order of the day for someone to either open fire in Mogadishu.
As we drive back to the hotel, my eyes are kept busy by the ruins of Mogadishu; the once beautiful and tall buildings are currently rubbles. Some were once beautiful international hotels, but they are today used as hiding bases for either AMISOM and TFG soldiers or the Al Shabaab. All buildings have one thing in common, dozens of sandbags to protect fighters from the bullets of the enemy.

Original_Search_Title: Universal Primary Education Programme Still Grappling With Teacher Shortages, Big Numbers
Headline: Uganda: Universal Primary Education Programme Still Grappling With Teacher Shortages, Big Numbers
Body_Text:
Despite the government intervention to enable all pupils to access universal education, many schools in the rural areas are grappling with an ever-increasing enrollment.
In many schools, especially primary, the ratio of teachers to pupils is estimated at 1:70 compared to the required ratio of 1:40, depending on subjects taught.
A recent survey in a school in Mukono under Universal Primary Education (UPE) programme revealed that pupils in the boarding school are squeezed and they share a few beds. Brick holding platforms are used as beds and a single lantern is given to each dormitory.
St. Kizito, Ngombere, a Catholic Church-founded primary school in Mukono District with pupil population of about 890 has eight government-paid teachers and six others paid by parents, but the number is still below the required 16 teachers.
The pupils in the boarding section share banana leaf-thatched bathing places and a pressure lamp is used in the classroom at night.
But they school recently acquired a solar lighting system through Kamukamu Learning Foundation, a United States charity organisation as a boost to their learning environment, a move which pupils said would increase their hours of reading and with ease.
Teacher trouble
"Teachers had to leave early for long distances, leaving students with nothing to do in the dark. Now we have light, so studies will be easy and without interruptions," Joseph Kiguli, a primary seven pupil.
Kiguli says the school has no text books; "But I would like to see a well-built school with better facilities including a playground because my parents brought me here telling me it is the best school in the area."
Ms Teddy Nassande, a Primary Seven pupil commends the lighting of the school with solar power.
"We have been studying up to 7pm and unable to go beyond." She, however, says light at the school was just one impediment to their learning; "We are many in class and in the dormitory, we share beds yet this boarding section is helping me to have more time to concentrate on reading than walking the four miles to and from home."
Mr Fred Wanyu Nsubuga, the chairman of the school management committee says: "If we can acquire a library, our pupils will be able to carry out more academic activities.
Mr Stephen Ssemaala, the Chairman of Kamukamu Learning Foundation, says the idea of providing solar lighting to Ngombere Primary school was perceived through collaboration with the head teacher and other administrators.
Mr Henry Jacob Watuutu, the school head teacher says the school has been spending up to Shs15,000 worth of paraffin to light the classes and dormitories, "But the congestion problem is worse because the pupils sit squeezed," he emphasised.

Original_Search_Title: More Than Building Schools - Access to Affordable Sanitary Pads Can Help Keep Girls in School
Headline: Rwanda: More Than Building Schools - Access to Affordable Sanitary Pads Can Help Keep Girls in School
Publisher: United States Department of State (Washington, DC)
Body_Text:
How can countries encourage girls to attend school? Is the answer providing free textbooks or building schools closer to their homes? While these are important pieces of the puzzle, there is another issue that influences whether girls attend school: menstruation.
According to the United Nations Children's Fund, one in 10 African girls stays home during menses or drops out of school. In many cases, girls do not have access to affordable sanitary pads, and social taboos against discussing menstruation compound the problem.
American entrepreneur Elizabeth Scharpf founded Sustainable Health Enterprises (SHE) in 2007 to address this problem. SHE works in Rwanda with its she28 campaign to develop an affordable and eco-friendly pad made from banana stem fibers so that girls can attend school unimpeded by worries over their menses. In the 2011 pilot project, SHE expects to produce 1,200 pads an hour, or about 2,112,000 pads per year. The goal is to reduce the price of pads by 35 percent to 70 percent, SHE Vice President CeCe Camacho explains.
Not only is SHE working to help girls attend school during menses, but also the group is taking a market-based approach to boost local businesses in Rwanda. SHE plans to sell its more-affordable pads to local entrepreneurs, focusing on women sellers. "Donations don't work long-term. Market-based approaches do, so why leave them for just the business world? Let's apply them to some of our biggest social problems," says Camacho.
Taking a comprehensive approach to social challenges is an important part of SHE's mission. At first, it was thought that what the girls needed was just an affordable pad. However, after talking to local girls, the SHE staff realized that the girls wanted health and hygiene education as well. In response, SHE has trained more than 50 community health and hygiene education workers, reaching some 5,000 Rwandans, according to Camacho. In addition, SHE has partnered with the Forum for African Women Educationalists, the Rwanda Association of University Women, the Rwanda Ministry of Education, the nongovernmental group Population Services International and youth centers to develop a health and hygiene curriculum for girls ages 12 and above, as well as for women and men.
SHE and its partners are also working to get people talking about affordable pads as a policy issue. In 2010, "SHE led a grass-roots advocacy campaign with 10 other leading organizations in Rwanda called Breaking the Silence on Menstruation," says Camacho. Hundreds of Rwandans marched across the capital and "engaged in a public discussion about how to break down these barriers to girls' education." As a result, the Rwandan government approved "a $35,000 procurement of menstrual pads for the poorest girls in Rwanda."
SHE's work in Rwanda shows that a comprehensive approach is needed to expand women and girls' educational opportunities. "Women and girls are often left behind because of some of these silent issues," Camacho explains. "We need to approach women and girls' education in a holistic way." A holistic approach, Camacho elaborates, also means being inclusive and culturally sensitive. It's important "to listen to the girls and the women as well as the fathers and the sons. It takes everyone to address these issues."
More information is available on the SHE website.

Original_Search_Title: Confronting Godfatherism in Imo Politics
Headline: Nigeria: Confronting Godfatherism in Imo Politics
Body_Text:
Owerri — Given the flashes of actions by Imo State governor, Rochas Okorocha, there is the urge to conclude that he has come to perform where other governors before him had failed. His "Rescue Mission Agenda" is easily cited as an indication of the direction of his government. Incidentally, that was the punch line he employed against former Governor Ikedi Ohakim who chorused "Let's Do More" to the people during the campaigns.
Actually, the "Let's Do More" campaign strategy of Ohakim and his Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) would have worked if the ex-governor had controlled his temperament. This major flaw, it was said, cost the former Imo chief executive his job at the Dogulas House, Owerri - the seat of Imo government. While the Imo electorate grappled with the former governor's frequent outbursts before the 2011 elections, they were ironically treated with the alleged beating and stripping of a Catholic Reverend Father by his aides.
This was indeed the straw that broke the camel's back. The people could no longer tolerate that, and that was what gave Okorocha the edge over Ohakim at the polls.
It was not in doubts that Okorocha would have made a good showing in the election but he was in a way, assisted in the exercise by the ex-governor's actions, which resulted to his defeat. A section of the people have already raised him (Okorocha) high because of his free education programme before the elections while few others saw him as a philanthropist they could do business with.
But these achievements of this Ogboko, Ideato South LGA-born politician and former member of PDP would have at least given him a second position in the race if the then incumbent had played his card well. However, his free education philanthropy played a major role in his ascension to power as people, particularly parents and students, wanted him to come and make education free for them and their children as he had promised during the election. It will be recalled that Okorocha has made free education his campaign strategy, telling the people how their children and wards would go to school without paying a dime.
This actually stuck especially with the poor that of course are in the majority. True to what he had promised during the campaigns, Rochas less than two weeks after assuming office announced the offer of free education to primary and secondary levels of education in Imo State. For the high institutions in the state, he assured them that he would reduce their school fees by about 60percent.
Last actions of Ohakim
Immediately the governorship election result proclaiming Rochas winner of the 2011 governorship election was announced there were wild jubilations as people went to the streets destroying bill boards and other landmarks associated with Ohakim and his government. However, despite the name-calling against the ex-governor during and after the jubilations, Ohakim appealed passionately to the people to calm down, saying that he had accepted the defeat as a democrat.
He equally vowed not to go court or election petitions tribunal to challenge the outcome of the gubernatorial poll. This subsequently calmed his opponents and other interests who were also preparing to wrestle with him at the tribunal. Ohakim's action however disappointed members of his party, the PDP who felt that dropping their weapons of war without a single shot would demean the party in Imo. But the former governor stuck to his guns and rather embarked on the execution of some controversial actions and policies in the three weeks he stayed in office after the May 6, 2011 governorship election.
Some of the actions were the distribution of letters of employments to some youths in the state under the 10,000-job scheme of his government. As noble as the scheme is, Ohakim nearly destroyed the project as he issued at the last minute letters of employment to those who didn't merit it. The graduate/youth job scheme was done in phases and properly arranged as an employment firm executing the project was doing it to forestall ambiguity. The employment of the youths had started in November 2010 and continued in February 2011. However, shortly after his defeat at the poll and knowing that from May 29, 2011, he would cease to be governor, Ohakim reportedly issued employment letters to thousands of youths.
Besides, he gave re-organisation to 106 autonomous communities and conferred the same number of persons, traditional rulers of their communities. Some of these communities did not even apply to be carved out as a separate entity to their host communities. He also relocated the Imo State University to Uvuru-Aboh Mbaise and Ngor Okpalla communities. Even the Alvan Ikoku Federal College of Education, Owerri, was not spared as it was swiftly relocated to Ogbaku in Mbailoli LGA.
Similarly, he introduced free education, an issue he had opposed vehemently during the campaigns. He also promoted a number of people to the position of permanent secretaries and directors, an action the civil service considered out of order. He was said to have also swiftly inaugurated the Imo State Oil Producing Communities Development Agency among other controversial actions of his.
It will be recalled that the election of the local government councils last year was fraught with controversies. The opposition in the state had accused the former government of installing kangaroo council election at the expense of a real council poll that would have produced the people's genuine representatives. According to them, the August 2010 council poll was not a true reflection of the people's will because as they put it the immediate-past government disregarded the people's outcry and court injunctions over the election. They claimed that the fraudulent way the poll was conducted could be seen by the manner the election was conducted on Saturday, result announced on Sunday evening and the purported winners sworn in before 7a.m on Monday at the Dan Anyiam Stadium, Owerri, adding that even when the electoral commission had informed everybody while announcing the result that Sunday, that that of the councillors' were not ready, the former governor, after swearing in the chairmen the next day (Monday) directed them to go to their local government headquarters and swear in their councillors without delay.
The Revision
Immediately on assumption of office, Okorocha yielding to popular demand quickly nullified the entire employment process executed by his predecessor. He equally sacked the council of the traditional rulers whose chairman, Eze Cletus Ilomuanya, was allegedly playing politics under the canopy of the chairmanship of the Imo State Council of the Traditional Rulers. It is known that some of the traditional rulers at the apex of the council were card-carrying members of some political parties.
In fact, they did not hide their partisanship in politics but displayed it to the annoyance of everybody as they determined what happened then in the immediate past government. This is why the governor's sledgehammer fell on them. Apart from that, Okorocha reverted the name of Evan Enwerem University to its previous name, the Imo State University. Ohakim had changed the name to honour his former boss and third governor of the state, Chief Evan Enwerem.
The banana peels:
The controversial actions were actually made to serve as some banana peels to Okorocha administration. Apparently, it was taken to make the people that benefited from the exercise to fight back and in doing so, derail the government. This actually was about to happen when Okorocha immediately reversed himself on the 10,000 job offer and assured them that he would rather review the exercise with the aim of fishing out those that entered through the back door, particularly those that were issued employment letter that week Ohakim was leaving office.
But he stood his ground on other revisions. However, as commendable as these revisions are, it has been criticised by some people who insisted that some of the actions were backed by law and that they should have been repealed before any action is taken on them. Indeed, in apparent move to legalise some of these actions, the State House of Assembly became a partner in the process as it was used to legalise these actions under Ohakim. But at the beginning of the Okorocha administration, members of the outgoing Imo State House of Assembly, then few days to its dissolution carried out a revolution in the floor of the House where they sacked the Speaker, Hon. Goodluck Opiah, as well as the principal officers of the Assembly. The lawmakers vowed to repeal some of these "draconian laws" and establish a cordial working relationship with the new governor. Investigations revealed that things later gone awry between them and Okorocha as he reportedly refused to play ball with them. Some of the laws that would have been repealed were left untouched.
Disrobing the godfathers
Considering the way and manner Governor Rochas Okorocha was elected, it is safe to predict the death of godfatherism in Imo politics. Okorocha despite the opposition of elders like Chief Emmanuel Iwuanyawu, Chief Arthur Nzeribe, Chief Innocent Nwoga, Dr. Walter Ofonagoro, Professor Green Nwankwo and so on was able to defeat the incumbent whom they all supported.
However, what the governor will have to contend with is the influence of the former governor, Achike Udenwa. Interestingly, it was the quarrel between Udenwa and Ohakim that cost the former governor his second term. This is apparently why Okorocha is being careful, as he has accommodated people from all interests, groups and opinions in his cabinet just sworn in last week.
But the insinuation that he will soon dump APGA and re-join PDP could be a banana peel that could lead to his defeat at the polls in 2015, assuming he wants to re-contest. This is because renowned politicians like Senator Ifeanyi Araraume and Dr. Alex Obi of the ACN are said to have concluded moves to re-join the PDP after they had dumped the party to seek political fortune in ACN where Araraume contested the 2011 governorship poll. Similarly, it is also believed that Udenwa is also on his way to join the PDP. If they all join, the atmosphere may be too suffocating for Okorocha.
Subsequently, this could deny him the second term ticket that is if the Owerri senatorial zone whose turn it is to produce the governor of Imo State in 2015 goes to slumber in that year. But for now, whether Okorocha will make it a second term will depend on two factors: one, his performance in office, and two, his agreement with Owerri people. But for now, no one has started opposing him yet, apparently on account of how he got his victory, coming through popular mandate.

Original_Search_Title: Ministers - Senators Move Against 'Take a Bow' Syndrome
Headline: Nigeria: Ministers - Senators Move Against 'Take a Bow' Syndrome
Body_Text:
Abuja — Senators have summoned security chiefs to explain the Boko Haram nightmare and how to get rid of it, in the face of vacillation by Aso Rock, despite suicide bombings on its doorstep.
They have also taken a stand against 'bow and go', the syndrome whereby former lawmakers and old time Ministers are not questioned in the Senate during the screening of Ministerial nominees, and instead are asked to take a bow, leave the Chamber in smiles.
Senate President David Mark on Tuesday unveiled the list of 34 persons nominated by President Goodluck Jonathan on the Federal Executive Council (FEC).
Two former Senators - Bala Mohammed (Bauchi) and Idris Umar (Gombe) - made the list, along with 14 returnee Ministers and 18 freshers.
Senators, however, talked tough on the screening slated to begin today, as Daily Independent reported exclusively on Monday, which Senate Leader Victor Ndoma-Egba confirmed on Tuesday.
Ndoma-Egba reiterated that Chamber would no longer condone 'bow and go,' something lampooned by the public as cronyism.
"The tradition notwithstanding, the current mood (in the Chamber) is that every nominee would be subjected to the same level of screening as others," he told reporters after plenary on Tuesday.
He also disclosed that all the leadership positions in the Chamber have been filled except that of his Deputy, zoned to the North East.
Ali Ndume (former House of Representatives Minority Leader now Senator) from Borno was billed to occupy the post, but he did not take it up on Tuesday, even though he was present.
Abdul Ningi (Bauchi) also reportedly rejected it on the grounds that it is lower than his former position as House of Representatives Majority Leader.
Chris Ngige (Anambra), a member of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN), equally rejected George Akume as Minority Leader, because it violates the federal character principle.
One of the Ministerial nominees, Mohammed, as Senator, chaired the National Interest Group (NIG) which fought for the Doctrine of Necessity that enabled Jonathan to become Acting President on February 9 last year during the ill-health of the late President Umaru Yar'Adua - who eventually died on May 5.
Jonathan later rewarded Mohammed with the juicy Federal Capital Territory (FCT) Ministry.
Others on the list are Emeka Wogu (Abia); Diezani Alison-Madueke (Bayelsa); Godsday Orubebe (Delta), Onyebuchi Chukwu (Ebonyi), Caleb Olubolade (Ekiti), Ruqayyatu Rufai (Jigawa), Shamsudeen Usman (Kano), Mohammed Sada (Katsina), Mohammed Adoke (Kogi), and Olusegun Aganga (Lagos).
Labaran Maku (Nasarawa), Yisuf Suleiman (Sokoto), Onadiah Ando (Taraba), Zainab Maina (Adamawa), Ita Ewa (Akwa Ibom), Stella Oduah-Ogiemwonyi (Anambra), Abba Moro (Benue), Samuel Ortom (Benue), Bukar Tijani (Borno), Mike Onolememen (Edo), and Barth Nnaji (Enugu).
Idris Umar (Gombe), Viola Onwuliri (Imo), Bello Mohammed (Kebbi), Bolaji Abdullahi (Kwara), Olugbega Ashiru (Ogun), Akinwunmi Adesina (Ogun), Olusola Obada (Osun), Olajumoke Akinjide (Oyo), Tonye Cole (Rivers), Yerima Ngama (Yobe), Bashir Yuguda (Zamfara), and Zainab Kuchi (Niger).
Security agency heads who will appear before the Senate include Inspector General of Police (IG), Hafiz Ringim, the target on June 16 of the country's suicide attack, launched by Boko Haram, which has gone bananas ever since, in a guerrilla warfare the authorities simply stand by and watch.
Others are Chief of Defence Staff, Air Chief Marshal Oluseyi Petirin, and State Security Service (SSS) Director General, Ita Ekpeyong.
They will brief the Senate in camera, on a date to be fixed.
The Senate on Tuesday condemned the bombings by Boko Haram, which rejects Western education as sin and wants the implementation of Shariah law in 12 Northern states, among its demands.
Senators also commiserated with the families of those killed.
Ita Enang, who sponsored the motion, urged Chamber to discuss the June 16 explosion at police headquarters, which "calls for concern."
Deputy Senate President Ike Ekweremadu noted that the menace is "not only of national importance, it's a national emergency."
Former Vice Chairman of the Drugs, Narcotics and Financial Crimes Committee, Dahoru Kuta, said Ringim's statements on the sect led to the blast two weeks ago.
"Our security agencies must weigh their words before doing anything. It was wrong for (Ringim) to say he wanted to deal with them when he never knew they were planning against him," Kuta stressed.
Sola Adeyeye bemoaned insecurity in the country, where even the highly-placed are no longer safe.
In his view, "All patriots must be saddened by these events and they must wake up. What we witnessed in Abuja shows that even the IG cannot boast of his own security. If he cannot boast of his own security, then the Senate President cannot boast of his own security because he depends on the IG for his security.
"Very urgently, we must summon the security agencies to give us the details of what has transpired and what they want to do to contain the situation."
Abdullahi Adamu (former Nasarawa Governor now Senator) said the bombing is an alien culture in Nigeria and urged his colleagues to step up their oversight on the executive.
"This issue is not only alien to this country but it's a new phenomenon that's beginning to eat into the fabric of our nation ... which is the preservation of life and property.
"We should desist, as a Senate of the Federal Republic where 'elder statesmen' are, we should desist from addressing symptoms. As a Senate, we have a fundamental responsibility to oversight the executive, what they are doing or what they are about to do," Adamu added.
But Datti Baba-Ahmed saw no basis for Enang's motion, saying the Chamber often occupies itself with matters that do not concern it.
He added: "National security matters should be left to the experts. This matter is laden with sentiments. It's not a matter fort this House.
"Are we adding value by condemning? Are we adding value by setting up a committee that would not deliver anything?"
Ningi differed, however.
He urged Senators to invite the security chiefs for briefing, because "this isn't a political matter, neither is it a religious matter. I support the motion that the security agencies should give us details of how a small group was able to grow into what it is now."
Nkechi Nwaogu sounded a wake up alarm and called for vigilance.
"Issues are handled hastily. How did we allow ourselves to degenerate to the level of bombing ourselves? The issue of Boko Haram started as if it was a religious thing. Now, they are bombing markets. The nation needs to wake up," she stated.

Original_Search_Title: Banana Peels Tambuwal Should Watch Out for
Headline: Nigeria: Banana Peels Tambuwal Should Watch Out for
Body_Text:
Since the return of Nigeria to democratic rule in 1999, history has not been kind to past Speakers of the House of Representatives. If one cares to reminisce back in time, memory will surely reveal stories of falls from grace to grass, one after the other.
Like a soap opera written by the most mischievous of writers, there has always been a bitter ending for each of the former holders of that prestigious office. Each season has different twists and turns, but arguably the softest plots so far must be the stories of former Speakers Ghali Umar Na'Abba and Aminu Bello Masari, who successfully completed their full tenures without major scandals.
Speaker Na'Abba was much admired for his courageous defence of legislative independence, successfully checkmating the executive excesses of President Olusegun Obasanjo led government. However, former President Obasanjo had the last laugh as Na'Abba was strategically defeated in his re-election bid and eventually bullied out of the ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP). He has now returned to the PDP and will hope to bounce back sooner than later. Speaker Masari was more accommodating of the executive but could not save Obasanjo's third term aspirations. At the end of his tenure in 2007 he decided to swap the Hallowed Chambers in Abuja for the Government House in Katsina, but in a twist that surprised many, lost the PDP ticket to the Alhaji Ibrahim Shema and was thereafter completely isolated by the party.
Masari has struggled to re-establish himself since then, with his latest setback being the loss of the 2011 governorship election in Katsina, which he was expected to win easily, flying the flag of General Muhammadu Buhari's Congress for Progressive Change (CPC). The only female to occupy the hot seat so far was Mrs Patricia Olubunmi Etteh, who was sacked only a few months into her tenure as Speaker, following allegations of fraud and mismanagement of funds. However, she was officially cleared of wrongdoing by the House of Representatives just before the end of the last dispensation.
The immediate past Speaker Dimeji Bankole is currently under trial and has already spent a few nights in less comfortable environments than he was used to. While it is still possible that the courts may find him not guilty, his present predicament I am sure will leave a bitter taste in the mouth. The harshest fall however has to be that suffered by Speaker Salisu Buhari. The former Speaker, 29-years old at the time, could not have been in a better situation. While many of his age mates were still struggling to start a career for themselves, he was already at the peak of his own career. Unfortunately, the Toronto certificate and age scandal hit him badly. He was accused of forgery and perjury, and as they say, the rest is now history.
The tenure of Speaker Aminu Waziri Tambuwal started on a refreshing note. Millions of Nigerians were glued to their television sets watching and wondering if the presence of PDP's Mr. Fix It, Chief Tony Anenih, acting PDP Chairman Dr. Mohammed Halliru Bello, and other party top notches would intimidate the House members and force them to vote for their "Chosen Candidate". But to the joy of most Nigerians, common sense prevailed and the members democratically voted a Speaker for all Nigerians.
It is strange, almost unbelievable, that the PDP leadership tried very hard to publicly ridicule Nigerians in the eyes of the world by imposing a puppet legislature. They were clearly barking up the wrong tree. Now that the wheat has been separated from the chaff, the die is cast and the new Speakers name will be permanently engraved in Nigeria's history books. One wonders what storyline our soap opera writer has in mind this time. Surely it must be time for a fairy tale story with a happy ending.
Well in reality, history of "people in the public eye" is not a meaningless fable told by some storytellers but is often defined by a single profound event. What the new Speaker must do above all is to restore sanity and dignity to the office he holds at the moment, and also to the House of Representatives in general. His tenure starts at a time when the approval rating of the Federal lawmakers is at a particularly low point in the estimation of most Nigerians. This follows suspicions of self-enrichment and neglect of primary assignments. The ball is now deep in his court and, while much water has passed under the bridge, there is some optimism that he can hold his own. As the English would say "every cloud has a silver lining".
Tambuwal wrote from Usman Danfodio University, Sokoto

Original_Search_Title: Bankole - Offensive in Defence
Headline: Nigeria: Bankole - Offensive in Defence
Body_Text:
For sometime now, Dimeji Bankole, immediate past Speaker of the House of Representatives, was being hailed by some social commentators for having kept his cool since his arrest by Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), last June 5.
The anti-financial crime outfit had stormed his residence in Abuja, the country's capital, and taken him into custody. He was arraigned in two courts a Federal High Court and the Abuja High Court.
Incidentally and surprisingly to many observers of his trial at Abuja High Court last Thursday, June 23, Bankole lost his cool when he argued that no court in Nigeria has the jurisdiction to try him.
Speaking through his lawyers, led by Adegboyega Awomolo, Bankole maintained that no court in the country has the jurisdiction to try him, or subject him to jurisdiction in all the matter to which he was arraigned by EFCC. Among other reasons he adduced to drive his point home, Bankole said the offences of criminal breach of trust and theft, for which he was being tried, could not have been committed by him by obtaining a loan to augment the allowances and running cost of House members in violation of the Revised Financial Regulations of the Federal Government of Nigeria, 2009.
Continuing, the economic graduate of University of Reading in the United Kingdom (UK), sought an order directing Farida Waziri, Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), Chairman, to publicly apologise to him. Explaining the reason for the apology he sought, Bankole claimed it was for the denial of his personal liberty, unlawful detention, and discomfort to his person and family since June 5, when he was arrested.
Bankole felt this was necessary because he had been condemned, vilified, demonised, and branded a villain in the public domain as the charges against him were leaked to the press by EFCC before arraignment, and after the prosecuting counsel granted television interviews where he condemned him.
Bankole also claimed that he suffered deliberate harassment, intimidation, indignity, humiliation, and persecution instead of prosecution when the bail granted him by the Federal High Court on June 13 was rendered ineffective by the adoption of serial prosecution.
Many Nigerians were incensed with his statement, while others considered it as outrageous and an exhibition of excessive arrogance. Yet, others believe the reaction was appropriate because his case has since assumed the toga of persecution, which they see as the hallmark of the EFCC, in many cases.
Eze Eluchie, an Owerri, Imo State based legal practitioner was incensed by Bankole's statement that no court could try him, describing it as an insult to the whole Nigerians.
His words: "I think it is an insult to all Nigerians for him to make that statement. Nobody is above the law. Bankole is not a member of the House of Representatives, neither does he have any immunity at all...I think this is the time for Nigerian authorities to really do their job, at least to show that the battle against corruption is not a fluke."
Notwithstanding there are Nigerians who believed Bankole's troubles is a clear case of persecution, as was evident with a recent accusation made against EFCC Chairman by members of the House of Representatives. They had challenged her to "Mention a single Politically Exposed Person (PEP) that was prosecuted without the person having problem with the federal government since the establishment of the commission in 2003."
Many Bankole's admirers are however optimistic that he would come out of his present trials successfully. The reason for their optimism is not farfetched, for at several occasions in the past, he has proven himself to be solid in political brinkmanship, through which he has survived the famed banana peels at the National Assembly. Banana peels is a term used to describe the fall of key officers of the Assembly who had one time or the other been eased out of office in ignominious circumstances.
What intrigued most people about the former Speaker was that notwithstanding his relatively young age, he was able to whether the storm for over three years that he was in the saddle and exited successfully.
Sometime in June last year, there was an explosive rumble in the House when he fell out with a former Chairman of the House Committee on Information and National Orientation, Dino Melaye. Bankole was given an ultimatum to resign or be forced to quit, by a group headed by Melaye and his "progressive" group.
Melaye was also reported to have promised to make the House ungovernable if Bankole failed to resign voluntarily. Unfortunately for Melaye, his threat did not stand as Bankole survived the storm. Instead it was Melaye and his colleagues in the agitation that ended up being suspended from the House.
Part of the tricks Bankole was believed to have used to outsmart his accusers and survive the onslaught against him, was his playing the religion card. At that occasion he had claimed that Melaye and his friends (who had majority Christian membership) were on a mission of bequeathing to the country a lopsided leadership in favour of Christians.
Few weeks to the last general elections, Bankole once again proved that his staying power was not a fluke when he survived factionalisation of his party in his native Ogun State, and the removal of his name from the party's list of candidates submitted to the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC).
It was later restored as he was permitted to run for a second term. However, he eventually lost the election to Action Congress of Nigeria's (ACN) candidate Olusegun Williams.
Bankole was born in November 1969. He had his early education at Lara Day Nursery and Primary School. He then attended Baptist High School, Abeokuta. He holds a degree in Economics from the University of Reading, England and another in military Technique from Oxford University also in the United Kingdom, before proceeding to Harvard University in the United States of America for a course in Public Finance Managements. He was elected into the House in 2003, and was re-elected in 2007. He hails from Abeokuta in Ogun State.
The Ogun State-born former speaker was reportedly on the warpath last, when he argued his arrest and arraignment at the Abuja High Court.

Original_Search_Title: They Don't Want Their Children in the Spotlight
Headline: Tanzania: They Don't Want Their Children in the Spotlight
Body_Text:
The rise of a celebrity culture in Tanzania over the years has seen many people, young and old, religiously following the glamour and scandals that make the life of the famous all over the world. But if there is one thing Tanzanian celebrities have managed to deprive their prying followers, it is gossip about the private lives of their children.
When it comes to celebrity parenting, everyone agrees how great a challenge it is. The prying eyes of the press are everywhere, and journalists are always on stand by to pounce and cash in on the slightest indication of a scandal. It is a little wonder some celebrities, even in the crazy West, prefer to keep their children as far away from the limelight as possible.
Those who have decided to make their children a cradle of publicity often attract heavy criticism. For instance, there has been a backlash against Will and Jada Smith, for 'exposing' their child stars to merciless tabloids. But Jada came out fighting, defending their choice of lifestyle for their children.
Other Hollywood stars like Brad Pitt and Angelina Jolie, Jenifer Lopez, and Tom Cruise have their children all over the media. Although some try desperately to keep their children out of the limelight, hiding them away from the prying eyes of the press, has proven to be a Herculean task.
Back home, there is almost nothing to tell about our celebrities' children. We have seen a few of them in front of the camera, of course, like the son of African Star Band songbird Luiza Mbutu, Zahir Ali Zoro's children Banana and Maunda, and a very few others. Still, most Tanzanian celebs try to keep their offspring away from the media spotlight, while others even go the extra mile - refusing to admit that they have children.
So, when Sound Living recently caught up with some of Tanzania's celebrities, the question this reporter asked was: How and why have they chosen to keep the lives of their children a closely-guarded secret?
RITA PAULSEN:
Everyone knows Rita as one of Tanzania's top celebrities, and the brains behind Bongo Star Search. She has always been a media favourite. As such her lifestyle has been a subject of media content.
But with all the media attention she usually attracts, Rita, a mother of one, has managed to keep her daughter's life off the media radar. And it is not a big surprise.
Rita, who has played a leading role in building the careers of many young Tanzanian artistes, is very economical with words when the discussion shifts to her child and related private life.
"It is not a good thing to expose your children to celebrity life, especially when they are still young because they can't handle it," says the director of Benchmark Production.
"When you are a celebrity you don't except everyone to talk good about you. But I am a very strong person, who can handle any situation - good or bad. I am not so sure children can do that. This life cannot give them peace of mind."
A good number of Rita's fans may know that she has a daughter, who is already a university graduate. Save for a few special occasions, where the award-winning celebrity's daughter has been seen, not much is known about the daughter who is said to be working with mum at Benchmark Productions.
And her mother says: "I kept her out of my business and fame because I wanted her to work hard. This is what I encourage her to do. By being famous does not mean that all my family members have to be famous too."
In fact, so overly protective is Rita that her daughter, Evelyn, who is now old enough to decide on her own, cannot talk to the media without her mother's permission.
"I normally don't make comments to the media until I get permission from my mother. Let me talk to her then I will call you," says Evelyn, who is in her early 20s.
HOYCE TEMU:
The former Miss Tanzania, who is now communication analyst with the UN Tanzania Resident Coordinator's Office, is also a mother of one daughter, Rubby.
And like Rita, her wish is to keep her young baby away from the limelight, but she has no illusions about the challenges of being a celebrity parents.
"I must admit that this is one of the toughest challenges famous people have to face - to shield their children from the media," says Hoyce, who won the 2008 Miss Tanzania accolade.
She adds: "I will shield my daughter as much as possible, but I cannot deny the fact that she is a part of my life, and the media will also be interested to know what becomes of her."
So, is it possible for famous families to live their lives without constant interruption from the pressures of fame or the media? It is, Hoyce suggests.
"What I understand is that people who are interested in my lifestyle will not be satisfied until they also know about people around me, and that includes my daughter," she explains.
"The question maybe is how much is too much in trying to shield one's children from the media? Or, how much is too much in exposing them to the media?"
According to the beauty queen, there are limits celebrity parents have to know in raising their children because some form of exposure can be traumatic to their offspring.
"There are too many bad things going around, and you would want to protect your child just like any other parent. The media can put a lot of undue pressure on our children, and we would not want them to judge us for that when they grow up," she explains.
JOACHIM KIMARYO aka MASTER JAY:
Many call him Master Jay, but who knows that the producer and owner of the MJ Records is father of three girls?
Like most of his colleagues in celebrity circles, the usually talkative producer (you have seen him when he was a judge during the 2010 Bongo Search finals) has a few words when it comes to revealing finer details of his family life.
"I have learnt to differentiate and separate my role as a father and celeb, as you call it," says Master J.
So, why is that when celebs like Will and Jada Smith, Hollywood's most famous parents, are actually taking advantage of their fame to build their two children's future in music and film?
Says MJ: "We live with many people, and we create many friends as much as we create enemies, so you never know what everyone thinks of you, and you would not want your children to be caught up in this kind of life."
"You have heard about children of famous people being kidnapped every now and then, which is really disturbing. In fact, my eldest daughter wants to be a musician, but I always tell her to wait until she is 16 years. She is currently just 12 years old," he says.
No photos of my children in the newspaper, Master J tells this reporter. However, there are some things he feels free to divulge to prying journalists - like that his children attend school at an international school in Mikocheni.

Original_Search_Title: Banana - Disability is Not Inability
Headline: Kenya: Banana - Disability is Not Inability
Body_Text:
Gospel singer Banana Ofuyo has been to hell and back, her life story would move many. Banana was born with a short left leg that gives her a limp. Growing up in Malaba, the singer was lucky to have a father who believed in her, "I remember dad was told that I should be taken to the Physically challenged school and he told them, 'I want my daughter to learn like any other child.' I grew up knowing that I have nothing to fear about and that is what boosted my esteem."
Banana went on to work her way to the top, she paid for her diploma course in Law after her parents were unable to, she refused to fall back on the disability stereotype of begging. "I came to Nairobi to chase my father pension to pay for my fees. It was not easy. At one time I had nowhere to sleep because a friend I was staying with had locked her house and left. I had to sleep in the outdoor bathrooms. Later an aunt turned me into a house help with a promise of Sh750 salary, but I managed to get a job which did not pay well but much better."
Right now her biggest challenge is "finance without it you will go to your grave with your talent intact. Myles Munroe said that great wisdom/talent is found in grave yards." Banana has written over 1000 songs but only recorded two, Nipe Lote and Kiasi, the videos of which were recorded in Bujumbura, Burundi. The videos were sponsored by DNT Solar and Wind Energy company, a company that promotes the use of renewable energy.
Her most memorable moment was "my first performance in a fund raising for the Ivory Coast victims of the Post election violence in Bujumbura." The singer is now thinking of pursuing a degree in Law. You can get her singles online at Amazon, Tune Core and Bernsoft.

Original_Search_Title: The Mututho Laws' Have Failed Miserably
Headline: Kenya: The Mututho Laws' Have Failed Miserably
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Recent deaths in Banana, Kiambu County, due consumption of illicit liquor goes a long way towards exposing the ironies of the Alcoholic Drinks Control Act commonly referred to as Mututho Law.
The idea behind the law was noble. It was meant to address a very serious social problem of irresponsible drinking and illicit alcohol.It was meant to stop young people killing themselves with drinks laced with industrial chemicals to make them more potent.
Many such young people have gone to their early graves due to the chemicals used by distillers to make a quick shilling.
The import of the law was to ensure that those who brew or distil the stuff make it fit for human consumption.
It was meant to target illegal brewers. It was also supposed to target those who have been making millions by advertising alcohol-drinking as the best thing that could happen to impressionable youngsters.
But this did not happen. The Act seemed to target consumers of safe beverages sold by licensed brewers. This was done by reducing the hours during which legal brews can be sold in licensed outlets.
Indeed, it would seem that the Mututho Law sought to penalise responsible drinkers and those who run licensed outlets. Those who seek to abuse alcohol at any time of the day will always find illicit brews which are in abundance.
Indeed, consumption of these beverages has risen tremendously since the enactment of this law.
The country is paying through the nose due to loss of revenue, and the heavy health bill footed because of ailments caused by illicit liquor consumption.
What the government does not seem to realise is that those who abuse alcohol will not simply stop because drinking hours have been reduced. They will simply seek alternatives, like taking beer at home.
This means that the law which seeks to protect children from exposure to alcohol ends up creating a bigger social problem.
The law also implies that under-privileged Kenyans are village idiots who cannot make simple decisions concerning their social lives. It implies that only the rich and privileged are capable of deciding when they can drink, while the poor must be regulated despite taking legal beverages.
After all, the rich belong to private members' clubs or to the same Parliament that enacted that laws. These establishments for the affluent are not affected by it, and they can drink whenever they want.
What all this boils down to is that whereas the whole idea behind the law was well-meant, it will not have any impact on long-suffering Kenyans who are undergoing untold suffering from alcohol abuse and its repercussions on health and degradation of socio-economic status.
For it to have an impact, the law must seek to reduce illicit alcohol brewing, consumption and irresponsible drinking instead of escalating it by making it practically impossible for legal brewers, sellers and consumers to enjoy the brews at their own convenience.
Unless this is done, then the poor who cannot kick the habit will continue dying like flies, while the rich continue making useless politically-correct noises whenever such deaths occur.
Mr Njuguna is a secondary school teacher in Gatundu South

Original_Search_Title: Have We Lost Our Real Purpose of Life?
Headline: Ghana: Have We Lost Our Real Purpose of Life?
Body_Text:
For sometime now Ghana's media have been dominated by debate over the legality or otherwise of Gay (Homosexuality) and Lesbianism.
Few years ago, this issue was a taboo or an abominable topic which was never entertained even amongst friends or families let alone come up for discussion on our airwaves.
But thanks to some so-called Human Rights Activists (People who perhaps claim to be more catholic than the Pope), men and women who practice this sordid act can now boldly come out to defend their position.
The most basic defend put up by such people (I mean those who practice and those who claim to be human right activists) is that it is someone's sexual orientation and that everyone has the freedom and the fundamental human right to do what he or she feels.
I am very often awestruck by such arguments; anytime I hear such people speaking on radio, my adrenaline junks and feel like I am bursting out.
While I do not intend to go into the legal aspect of whether Gay or Lesbianism should be permitted in Ghana or not, I would like to take dear readers through some religious perspectives so that we call all reflect on certain aspect of lives and our very existence on this planet.
First of all, I would like to start by asking this question 'What is Our Real Purpose of Life', what the meaning is and what is the purpose of our existence is as human beings?.
What is the reason for which we are here on this earth, what are we supposed to be doing with ourselves.
This is not a question that a dog, a cat, an ant or a lion seems to have any problem about; the cat knows what is supposed to be doing and so are the ants and the dogs. These creatures know the reason for which they exist and they spend their existence fulfilling that creative purpose.
But this seems to be unique to we human beings that we can ask this question; what is the purpose of my life, as if we realize that we are something different from animals. There is something about human being that distinguishes us from other creatures and makes us a different type of creation. Though we eat, drink, sleep and procreate like the animals, there is this fundamental questions on our minds that our direction actions and make us accountable to our creator, the Omnipotent God.
Every religion, ideology and philosophy in one or another seems to be trying to make sense of this fundamental question of what is the purpose of our life as human beings.
By and large we live in a materialistic and secular world; Ghana professes to be a secular state and so are world democratic super powers like America, Britain and France.
Clearly, the Soviet Union, for instance claims to be an atheist state whose ideology is purely materialistic and whose driving force and underlying concept is totally materialistic.
A secular society recognizes religion but says that it should not have anything to do with normal day to day running of affairs of state. But whether the society claims, for example in Ghana, some politicians sometimes claim that this is a religious country and we run our society on religious principles but the fact of the matter is that the true religion and the basic ideology is a materialistic one.
Under materialistic society, we are told that the purpose of life is life and that the reason for your existence on this earth is to enjoy and to make the best of your physical existence right here on earth. That the way to be happy and to be fulfilled as human being is by acquiring material possession and when you acquire these material possessions, then you will find the happiness that you are looking for.
How you make it doesn't really matter. And just like every religion, philosophy or ideology, people have rational, scientific approach to things. Little attention is given to the spiritual implications of such acts.
So anyone at all can decide to be gay or a lesbian and that is entirely his or her own choice.
This nearly the theory of evolution which aims to root the human being very firmly in the material. This theory leads human beings to understand that we are just products of nature, random mutation of DNA, a product of natural selection; there is no hand of God. If anyone wants to believe in that, which would be entirely his or her own opinion because I believe strongly that a good number of readers know that there is someone up there, an Omnipotent God who sees every little of our action and records every minute of our existence on this planet.
Some scientists attempt to justify that we are descendants of an ape-like creature, that we have animalistic instincts. Therefore the meaning of human being is not much more than the animal.(Maybe we are just monkeys but a little bit sophisticated and little bit more advanced).
That the things that make animals happy are the things that make us happy. So assuming you have a monkey, how do you make it happy, you give it bananas and keep it safe from predators. You give a male monkey female monkey and you have a happy monkey.
Food, Shelter and Sex and that is. You have a complete happy mammal. This according to materialistic world, is no different from human being. Human beings are supposed to strive for food, shelter and a sex partner to satisfy our passion and animal instincts.
So all these open sexuality, nudity, fornication and homosexuality, we are being told, are all part of the satisfaction of natural instincts in man. That even homosexuality is a natural instinct because some people have homosexual genes and that they have sexual passions towards their fellow man or woman.
This is the underlying reason why the world is now confronted today with the reality of gay and homosexuality. Long time ago, this issue was inconceivable in a Ghanaian society. No one dared talk about this abominable and indecent practice.
But what do we see today; people are bold enough to declare their gay and homosexual status and are now calling for legislation to protect them.
Now our governments are very reluctant in boldly coming out to condemn and come out with legislation to criminalize such abominable act as if when they do so, they would lose power or incur the wrath of some influential people in the society.
Or is it to confirm rumours which made headlines sometime ago that some of our politicians in this country are homosexuals. One thing is however clear; that if Ghana decides to criminalize gay and lesbianism and go ahead to arrest people who indulge in such acts, they would incur wrath of our foreign donors. But should we fear our fellow human beings than God who created us.
I believe this one Ghana can call the bluff of the Americans, the British, France or any other country or international community.
We have our customs and traditions and these must be respected!
Let me at this point commend the MP for Shai Osu Doku, Hon.David Tetteh Assuming for boldly stating his position on the issue and even going ahead to warn worshippers of such act that they risk being lynched if they do not stop practicing their evil acts.Bravo Hon.Assuming for that single effort, I would have given you my total vote if I were to be in your constituency.
I therefore implore other honourable members, civil society groups and the media to launch serious campaign against the practice.The Muslim community has registered its position and so they must be commended.Lets stand up for once and fight for God.
The example of Sodom and Gomorrah is there for all of us to behold.Time to act is now!.

Original_Search_Title: Killer Brews-the Party Continues
Headline: Kenya: Killer Brews-the Party Continues
Body_Text:
Even as the victims of a deadly brew continued to be buried in Banana Hill and its environs, young men in Kiambu continue to consume illicit brews. Most with the abandon of the man who declared years back at a Mai Mahiu bar that, "we will continue to drink even if you put off the lights". The man's story was featured at a time when brewers used an almost pure form of methanol as an alcoholic drink. The man in question had lost his sight but in his drunkenness assumed that the lights had been turned off.
In the Banana Hill case and others before it including the infamous deaths and blindness cases in Kang'ari in Maragwa Districts, Mai Mahiu, Machakos, Nairobi slums and Laikipia Central the scare was short-lived. The brewers retreated for a few days as they kept watched of the situation whilst their customers cursed in thirst.
Thirty year old Nyaga Njeru who works as a casual labourer in Kikuyu, seeks to "unlock" himself with one of these drinks every morning before he goes to work and can do so with as little as Sh20. "At times I have only Ksh 15 left from the previous night but I borrow or beg Sh20 from prospective employers and with a colleague who may also have Sh35, we are able to buy one drink which we share among ourselves. Sometimes three of us would raise the money to buy one drink but the amount taken would not be enough to unlock", he shared.
These plastic bottled drinks used to retail between Sh50 and Sh60 but following the recent budget proposals, they have been bumped up to between Sh60 and Sh70. During the unofficial hours, one can only buy a full bottle and hence the need to cost share. In the evenings however, half measures are dispensed with bar attendants carefully ensuring the 'half-loaves' are equal.
So with the new rules that prohibit the consumption of liquor until late afternoon on weeks days how are Nyaga and his drinking mates able to "unlock" themselves before ten in the morning? "When the ladies come to clean the bars and wait for the beer trucks, they sell the drinks to us through the window bars or door cracks. The drinks are however strict take-aways and no one is allowed to drink near the selling premises. If one does this he risks being blacklisted". "The best thing about these drinks is that they get you high very fast, in fact almost immediately and when I am high, I am able to do any job fast and happily, the murk of its nature notwithstanding. That high feeling also keeps me away from evil thoughts when I do not have a job".
Nyaga seems unfazed by the fact that these drinks have sporadically killed dozens of their consumers. "I am very careful and always make sure I eat before I take the alcohol and also mix it with kitunguu saumu (garlic) to remove any impurities and assure reasonable intoxication".
The alternative to these strong drinks include spirits like Kane Extra or the cheap beer keg. Although the keg is sold at Sh 20 per plastic cup, it does not give the desired instant intoxication and due to its nature of dispensation, is not easy to come by outside the 'Mututho' hours.
Some bars visited in Kiambu admitted to selling these 'portable' drinks, all packed in plastic bottles during the unofficial drinking hours. Confirming their dubious nature; we witnessed one supplier offloading dozens boxes of this cheap liquor at a local pub in Kikuyu at around nine at night. "These are the real money makers. They sell like hot cake and a package containing two dozens of 200 ml bottles will make you Sh 480. Thus if you sell four boxes which is easily done in a day, you make close to Sh 2000," shared one of the supplier.
These dangerous drinks are popular with the unemployed, under-employed youth and some middle class men. These drinks go by exotic names such as Visa , Hardyman, Sampire, Kenya King, King Fisher (not the smooth ladies drink variety), Black and White, Charms, Chaps, Shangilia, Metropolitan, Discovery, Survivor, Country Walker (which was responsible for multiple deaths in Laikipia Central) and Flying Horse among many others with some that come without labels.
Unfortunately most of those that have labels bear fake stickers from the Kenya Bureau of Standards (KEBS). Nyaga admits that when one takes any of these drinks, "you feel as if your stomach is on fire but on an empty stomach, you literally feel it going through your entire digestive system". The drinks are said to shrivel the liver, kill the optic nerves and shred the intestines.
Two weeks ago, a good Samaritan bought Maina a well known consumer of the cheap liquor a beer at a bar in Muthure trading center in Kikuyu. When he finished the first beer he was asked by his benefactor to take another one but he insisted on taking a Visa. Half a glass later he was under the table totally blacked out.
When I met him at the same bar a week later, he bragged, "On any given day, I take up to three 200 ml bottles. We are lucky that at this center, they do not bring the poisonous drinks and so I am not afraid of dying or going blind." Many like Maina would love to drink less potent alcohol but because they are sold at high prices, it's beyond their means.
Following the Banana Hill tragedy, Kiambu DC Albert Kimathi is working towards forming bar owners associations in all towns and trading centers in Kiambu to enhance self-policing. Kimathi is also cracking the whip on bars suspected of selling the poisonous stuff. "Bar owners know the source of these lethal drinks and if they own the problem they will help to identify those selling fake and dangerous stuff. Despite the number of officers I have on the ground, I cannot be able to cover all the bars. However by involving the stakeholders, the problem can be tackled. Those found selling illicit brews will have their licenses canceled," said Kimathi.
