Contacted, the man behind the Mututho law, Naivasha MP John Muthutho reckoned the rules he had introduced in parliament were supposed to reduce access to alcohol by scaling down on cheap drinks and limiting opening hours.
Despite his good intent, the restricted opening hours only hit established bars selling regular beer and spirits. With the potent plastic bottled stuff still available both day and night.
In reaction to this Muthutho held, "Even the Ten Commandments are often broken and we will have people breaking the law even today. The beauty of it however is that we have put in place serious deterrent measures".
Mututho is of the opinion that Kenyans are drinking less and that there has been an upsurge of up to 300 per cent of labour. The capital to absorb this excess labour is however not available. The Naivasha MP believes his legislation is a success.
A bar owner we talked to at Muthure trading center however rubbished the Mututho law especially as it concerned the rule that bars must be sited at least 300 metres away from schools. "The Kikuyu MP Lewis Nguyai and the DC came here the other day to break the ground for a new market and told us that our days are numbered. But we open bars at five in the afternoon after schools have closed at four, so how sensible is this law?" he questioned.
He also noted that the proposed market will be much nearer to the school (the two will be separated by a ten meter road) and that it will be more of a nuisance to the school than the bars.
The easiest way to tackle the problem the bar owner felt, was to stop the production of the dangerous drinks at the factory level and "the government knows exactly where these are located," he claimed.
He also noted that in most small towns and trading centres in Kiambu, the only viable business are the bars and if blocked the pensioners who own the buildings would lose their only source of livelihood.

Original_Search_Title: NACADA Rewards Mututho for Alcohol Control Act
Headline: Kenya: NACADA Rewards Mututho for Alcohol Control Act
Body_Text:
Naivasha MP John Mututho who is behind the Alcoholics Drinks Control Act has been awarded for his efforts. The National Campaign Against Drug Abuse Authority recognised the MPs efforts in fighting alcohol and drug abuse. NACADA national coordinator Aggrey Busena said Mututho will be presented with the achievement award on Sunday at the AP Grounds in Naivasha.
The occasion will also be used to mark the International Day Against Drug Abuse and Trafficking, Busena said. In a letter to the MP, NACADA thanked the legislator for his passion and commitment to the fight against alcohol abuse in the country.
Popularly known as the 'Mututho law', the alcoholic control act has met both opposition and support from different quarters across the country. Speaking on phone, Mututho thanked NACADA for the award and said he will avail himself on Sunday to receive the award.
"I'm surprised and elated by the award which is meant to save our future generations from the second generation brews which are reducing them to cabbages," he said.
Mututho attributed the recent deaths of ten people in Banana in Kiambu after drinking illicit brews to failure by Internal Security minister George Saitoti. He said that since the alcoholic control act came into law, the minister had failed to sign regulations to control the quality of drinks.
"You cannot blame NACADA but the Minister who has failed to sign the regulations which were to come into force nine months after the act came into power," Mututho said.
He accused Saitoti of trying to woo some Mps to have the act amended adding that this would fail "The minister has been very lax on this alcohol issue and he should expect battle if he dares bring to parliament the act to be amended,"

Original_Search_Title: Kidnapped Foreigners Killed in  Presidential Palace
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Kidnapped Foreigners Killed in  Presidential Palace
Body_Text:
Four foreigners, including two French men, kidnapped during post-electoral violence in Côte d'Ivoire were killed by a police commissioner loyal to outgoing president Laurent Gbagbo in the presidential palace, officials announced Tuesday.
The four, who were grabbed at the Abidjan Novotel on 4 April as Gbagbo's supporters clashed with those of current President Alassane Ouattara, were tortured and beaten before being executed, according to Justice Minister Jeannot Kouadio Ahoussou.
Their bodies were then wrapped in black plastic bags "of the kind used to package bananas" and "thrown in the lagoon", he told a press conference.
Their remains were found near the naval base, officials say.
The police commissioner in question, four pro-Gbagbo militiamen plus an officer and two other members of the Republican Guard have been charged with involvement in the murder, the minister said.
The four kidnapped men were:
Novotel director Stéphane Frantz di Rippel, French citizen;
Yves Lambelin, chairman of Sifca, Côte d'Ivoire's largest private company, French citizen;
Raoul Adeossi, Lambelin's assistant, Beninois citizen ;
Chelliah Pandian, managing director of Sifca subsidiary, Sania, Malaysian citizen.
International Criminal Court prosecutor Luis Moreno-Ocampo is to visit Côte d'Ivoire from 27 June to 4 July with a view to opening an inquiry into the violence which followed last year's presidential election, Ahoussou announced.

Original_Search_Title: PC Warns Kiambu Civic Leaders Over Illicit Brews
Headline: Kenya: PC Warns Kiambu Civic Leaders Over Illicit Brews
Body_Text:
Central PC Jasper Rugut has warned Kiambu County administrators of legal action if they do not get rid of illicit brews which have claimed several lives over the past two years.
Rugut said the law stipulates the standards of alcohol with recommended regulations that the Provincial administration is supposed to adhere to. He said the administration has failed and warned that if the vice continues, DCs and their juniors will be answerable for the deaths.
The PC was speaking to the press after a closed door meeting with DCs, DOs, Chiefs, administration and regular police bosses from all eight districts in the region; Gatundu North and South, Kiambaa, Limuru, Kikuyu, Githunguri, Ruiru and Thika. "The alcohol law stated the standards of alcohol, brew requirements, and selling time and they must be followed to the letter. It's our duty to ensure all these laws are followed to avoid more deaths and if any officer will fail to implement them in his or her area, they will be dire consequences," Rugut said.
However, a chiefs from Limuru who declined to be named for fear of victimization, said DCs and police officers are frustrating their efforts by releasing suspects before charging them in court. of Chiefs and their subs.Last week, consumption of illicit brew in Banana village left 10 people dead while tens of others are in hospital.
The provincial administration has been blamed of laxity in implementing the alcohol laws, to curb illicit brews which. He said that most of the Administration police officers collude with the sellers of the illicit brews where they get bribes every month. He said that once they arrest one person to the police station, the police release them on cash bails even without going to court.
The Chief lamented that their efforts have also been frustrated by Officers in charge of Police divisions in the area who allow their juniors to release the suspects without being arranged in court. He said that they are pleading with the PC to help them to get a well defined rules on just forwarding their reports to the DCs and OCPDs who have the 'guns' and authority to arrest the suspects, "this way, we shall finish these illict brews," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Super Eagles, Argentina Return Leg Friendly Mired in Controversy
Headline: Nigeria: Super Eagles, Argentina Return Leg Friendly Mired in Controversy
Body_Text:
That Nigeria defeated Argentina national team in friendly encounter in Abuja is no longer news. The issue now is whether Nigeria should honour a planned return leg. In this piece, Pius Anakali asks if the money on offer is more important than what we are as a nation.
Which comes first: national pride or money? These are some of the questions discerning Nigerians, especially those conscious of the nation's image were asking when the news of Super Eagles and Argentina national team so-called second leg friendly match hit the media few days back.
To the Argentines, defeating them by whooping 4-goals to 1-goal is unheard of. No one, not even the Super Eagles players or even the visiting Argentines would have imagined that an African team or a country with a poorly organised and inefficient Football Association like ours will ever beat a two-times World Cup winner by such a margin.
Nobody ever believed that a country like Argentina will ever collapsed under the firing power of a hurriedly arranged team like the Super Eagles. But it happened and Nigerians are savouring the glory while Argentines are reeling in the pain of the after match.
It's a friendly match, many are wont to argue, but it is as crucial as well. It is that importance that made world soccer body, FIFA, to even think that there must have been some match fixing in the encounter.
The Argentines must have thought that beating them, even if it's far away from home, would have amounted to another wonders of the world.
With that in mind as the countdown to the Guinness Nigeria-sponsored competition gathers momentum, Argentina coach, Sergio Batista, scanned through its local league and fished out some players for the Guinness Cup dropping the likes of mesmerising Lionel Messi from the side.
While the Argentine coach was plotting his way through the encounter, our own Samson Siasia, was also hazarding a guess, tinkering with the idea of either fielding a second-rate team or the best legs for the match.
With Simon Kalika, the wizard around the Bayelsa gaffer, Siasia, unleashed the Obi brothers: Joel and Mikel, Ike Uche of Zaragoza of Spain, Fernabache defender, Joseph Yobo, Taiye Taiwo and others on the visitors. Not even the absence of enterprising striker, racy Osaze Odemwingie, could stop the Super Eagles resolve to rout and 'mow down' the visitors.
It was the sensational Uche who drew the first blood in the early minutes of the first half. With another in the second 45 minutes, Nigeria sealed a 4-1 victory over the visitors.
If the players were happy at the outcome, the coach, Siasia, was more elated.
In a chat with the media after the match, Siasia declared. "We are no longer Super Chickens, beating Argentina is no mean feat and I must congratulate the players for the achievement. The boys have done well even when we had little time to train. I was worried before the match because instead of concentrating we were busy arguing over the right tactics," Siasia explained.
That was not the first time Nigeria has beaten Argentina in football match. At the Atlanta Olympic in 1996, the story was the same.
Shortly after that Olympic euphoria, something gave way and the Nigerian side waned, becoming prey to the South American country. The once intensely competitive Super Eagles were nicknamed Super 'Chickens' after series of defeat from countries that included Argentina at every opportunity.
In 2005, Nigeria's U-20 lost to Argentina 1-2, the story was replicated at the Beijing Olympics when Nigeria lost to Argentina by 2-goals to 3-goals.
At the 2010 World Cup in South Africa, Nigeria was a mere participant losing its opening match to Argentina by a lone goal.
It was therefore no surprise that the Argentines believed that they could beat Nigeria any day and anywhere.
But the Abuja loss reverberated back home in Buenos Aires. The media was awash with the disgrace.
So, it is attempt to repair their already wounded image, the Argentines' national pride, that necessitated the call for a rematch. The question therefore is: should Nigeria honour that encounter?
Well, on this, opinion differs. While some Nigerians should be allowed to savour the victory over the highly rated Argentines, others felt Nigeria should honour the match.
Those who believe Nigeria should toe a line of caution say our the country's national image is much more than the dollars at stake in playing the match.
True, nations that play in the World Cup don't do so because of the financial gain involved but the honour of being identified as a nation, its culture and its people. Why do governments all over the world have ambassador in other lands? It is to serve the interest of the people.
What will Nigeria gain if it wins the World Cup for instance? It is simply honour, not money. So honour among men, among nations defines us a people.
But then, who are we, really? Simply put, we are 'black monkeys,' who can do anything for a piece of banana. So the best way to catch us is to throw the banana at us.
Already the Aminu Maigari-led Nigeria Football Federation has signed the deal just because of the money involved. If you know what $2 million means to those people, then you will know why they signed the deal before even looking at the contract papers. That is the way we are, hungry people who can do anything for bucks.
Yes, $2million is a big deal, but is it bigger than our corporate image?

Original_Search_Title: African Press Review 17 June 2011
Headline: African Press Review 17 June 2011
Body_Text:
Our review of the African press is dominated by reactions in the South African papers to the ANC youth congress which opened in Midrand on Thursday. Plus a bomb in Nigeria sows confusion among journalists and police.
The Mail and Guardian reports that it was a rare face-to-face meeting between President Jacob Zuma and his staunch critic ANC youth leader Julius Malema.
In an article titled "the romance is over", M & G reveals that Zuma waited three good hours for Malema to arrive. The paper comments that apart from swaying side by side to the popular Zulu war song Umshini wam, the rift between the two was laid bare in their speeches, Malema slamming the slow pace of land redistribution to blacks as state-backed racism, while Zuma pointed to the need for economic transformation and inclusive growth.
The battle for Libya
The Johannesburg newspaper stresses that the two also stand apart on foreign policy.
Zuma backs the Nato military operation against the Libyan regime, while Malema characterises Kadhafi as an innocent victim of "blood and oil-thirsty imperialist scavengers", according to the Mail and Guardian
Business Day also headlines on the ANC youth league congress pointing out, that notwithstanding the blunt speeches, Zuma emerged largely unscathed from the delicate meeting. The economic newspaper claims that Malema had been expected to propose a motion on leadership change in the ANC.
Business Day argues that he instead endorsed President Zuma for a second term as party leader, however calling only for the removal of ANC secretary general Gwede Mantashe at the ANC's elective conference, to be held in Mangaung next year. The Times however reports that union federation Cosatu's chief, Zwelinzima Vavi, had warned, in a leaked document, that SA "will become a banana republic" if the youth league is successful in removing Mantashe.
Cape Times runs an interesting angle to the Dominique Strauss-Kahn affair.
The newspaper publishes excerpts from court papers showing that he tried to claim diplomatic immunity, after he was detained at a New York airport on accusations he tried to rape a hotel maid.
The Nigerian press gives extensive coverage to a daring suicide attack at the police headquarters in Abuja. Louis Edet House, located within Abuja's strategic Asokoro vicinity was rattled on Thursday by a bomb explosion.
Police say two people died in the blast, that destroyed over 710 cars parked in front of the building, but The Champion quotes authorities at the nearby Asokoro General Hospital, where casualties were taken, claiming that victims rushed to the facility including no fewer than 10 bodies. The newspaper comments that police reluctance to come clean about the exact toll may be explained by the fact that the police headquarters lies just a few kilometres away from State House.
The Vanguard reports that the police hierarchy was engrossed in an overnight marathon meeting as fear anxiety and confusion enveloped the Nigerian capital, with business activities brought to a halt.
The paper traces the terror campaign to the Maitasine sect unleashed in the northern state of Kano in the late 1980s, killing over 4,000 people. According to The Vanguard, the ideology is opposed to most aspects of Western culture such as the possession of radio sets, wrist watches, cars, motorcycles and even bicycles.
This Day samples reactions to what is already being dubbed as the 6/16 attacks . Former military leader and presidential candidate Muhammadu Buhari called for a decisive action against terrorism. Buhari warns according to the paper that "episodic recurrences of bomb blasts are beginning to instil fears in Nigerians, which is very unhealthy for the nation".

Original_Search_Title: Recline - Could Trish Be Falling Hard?
Headline: Uganda: Recline - Could Trish Be Falling Hard?
Body_Text:
Trish, 25, told me she would be waiting at Soho with Stella, 35; Sheba, 40, and Joanna, 29.
As I walk in and pick her laptop and shopping bags from the floor...
Trish: I have to go, girls. My friend is giving me some cooking lessons today.
Sheba: Cooking lessons and Trish in one sentence? The world is coming to an end.
Stella: You mean you can't cook minus the lessons?
Trish: No. I've never cooked a thing. There was just no reason to.
Sheba: Okay, first wash the onions, boil the tomatoes and crush them without cutting them and...
Trish: ...no need to be cynical.
Sheba: Nobody is being cynical here, just that I do not understand why a grown-a** woman like you can say she has no idea how to peel bananas.
Trish: It is not all about the maids and being spoilt or feeling sweet. Girls, I just never got a chance to do it!
Joanna: So, when you want a boiled egg you go to the food court?
Trish: Look, some people can't swim, others can't dance and I can't cook. Is that so bad?
Sheba: Come on! Cooking just does not belong to that list!
Trish: Plus my father - I really must say this - was preparing me for 'higher' things.
Stella: 'Higher' things that don't involve eating?
Trish: What has cooking got to do with eating? Banange!
Joanna: Okay, so why the change of heart?
Trish: Sometimes you have to come down from this 'higher' place if you want a steady flow of good sex - what you people refer to as love.
Stella: Am I hearing things, or did you just agree that a woman can compromise her position on certain things for a man?
Joanna: Yeah, who are you and what have you done to the feminist we love to hate?
Sheba: I saw that coming. One of these days she will tell us, it is better to be in an exclusive relationship as opposed to an open one. Heeee!
Trish: Okay, so, I have found someone I want to fix spaghetti and minced meat for, and you girls are hearing wedding bells?
Joanna: Spaghetti and minced meat? Every bachelor can fix himself that! Go for a frittata. Or bake him an apple cake.
Trish: Aren't cakes supposed to be strictly bakery stuff? And what the heck is that other thing you said?
For a few minutes, Joanna and Trish discuss the recipes.
Sheba: This world!
Stella: Never say never.
Trish: That is why I did not ask any of you girls to teach me. I am sure you are not even that good. Could be the reason why some people have never popped the question...
Stella: Trust me, if your spags and minced ones are aimed at drawing the big question out of him, you have some years of cooking ahead of you! Just cook because you want to.
Joanna: Trust me, love, if cooking were the yardstick, I wouldn't be the lonely one here!
As Trish gets up for me to drive her to the cooking lesson, a big cookbook falls out of her bag and her friends burst into laughter.

Original_Search_Title: Nakuru Police Crack Down On Illegal Brewers
Headline: Kenya: Nakuru Police Crack Down On Illegal Brewers
Body_Text:
Police in Nakuru have intensified efforts to crack down on illicit brewing and consumption of illegal liquor within the area. So far 11 people have been arrested for drinking and being in possession of chang'aa.
Nakuru police chief Johnston Ipara said the crackdown will spread to all estates in Nakuru town and its environs. He said intensive police inspections will be done on all vehicles ferrying goods on long and short distance travels. "We want to wipe out all the illicit brews within our reach that are becoming detrimental to the lives of many drinkers and even revelers," said Ipara.
On Sunday seven people were arrested in Kambi ya Moto within Menengai area when they were caught drinking chang'aa. The police also recovered fermented flour believed to be used in making the brew.
Two women and two men aged between 24 and 30 were nabbed in Njoro market ferrying chang'aa from Nakuru in a Nissan matatu. "The four were in possession of 283 litres of chang'aa which they were taking to Molo town to sell in various entertainment joints," said Ipara. The police boss cautioned members of the public against drinking alcohol of which the contents are unknown. Over the weekend, over seven people lost their lives after consuming lethal brew liquor in Kiambu's Banana area.

Original_Search_Title: The Import of Tambuwal's Victory
Headline: Nigeria: The Import of Tambuwal's Victory
Body_Text:
Some praise at morning/what they blame at night/but always think the last opinion right. -Alexander Pope
The electoral victory of Honourable Aminu Tambuwal, as the new Speaker of the House of Representatives, with Emeka Ihedioha as the Deputy Speaker, in total defiance to the zoning principle as enshrined in the constitution of their party - the PDP - speaks volumes about the paradigm shift in Nigeria's political engineering. While proponents of zoning smart at the effrontery of the duo, to insist on pursuing their personal ambitions at the expense of the balancing lever for equity and justice in allocation of prime political offices analysts would think differently.
To them the PDP zoning principle died with the demise of former president Umar Yar'Adua. Had the North stood by President Jonathan early enough the bad blood generated would have been avoided.With Tambuwal hailing from the North - West geo political zone where the vice president also hails from and Ihedioha from the South - East this is a triumph of democracy over dictatorship. It is a symbolic victory of the wishes of a good majority of the members of the House of Representatives, over and above the usual foisting of favoured candidates by a powerful cabal. Thus, minority may have its say but majority has had its way.
However,the Ciromas, Babangidas, Atikus, Gusaus and Yakassais must not trivialise it to that low level of having scored a political point. Especially now that the plum political pedestals of the Vice Presidency, Senate Presidency and Speakership of the House of Representations are in the firm grip of the North, which many have seen as always greedy for holding on to the most powerful political positions. At the end of the day, such opportunities are used for self - aggrandizement instead of impacting positively on the quality of life of the average Northerner, still bedeviled with the Alimanjiri syndrome, mass illiteracy and poverty. Elements that willy - nilly become cannon fodder to stoke the fires of the Boko - haram religious sect and political violence.
Rather, the point must be made that Tambuwal got his victory on the platform of merit. In him, most returning members have seen a through - bred Nigerian lawmaker who would deepen the democratic process, by restoring the lost glory of the Green Chamber. And ensuring that there is a clear distinction of political power between the executive and the legislature that would be in tandem with the position of Charles De Montesquieu, the adequate of separation of power. He posted that "when the legislature and executive powers are united in the same person, or in the same body of magistrates, there can be no liberty."
We knew the demerits of such as anti - democratic scenario, when during the tenure of Chief Olusegun Obasanjo efforts were made to surreptitiously whip the legislative arm in line. Expectedly, poor Nigerians were at the receiving end of a legislature that would neither make people - friendly laws to advance the cause of democracy nor effectively check the excesses of the executive arm of government. The long - delayed passage of the FOI Bill into law is one of such. Legislators cooked up all kinds of excuses to protect their selfish interests.
Beyond these are the new challenges thrown up by Tambuwal/Ihediohian's victory. The South - West has been left holding the short end of the political stick. It must therefore be compensated with plum political posts. That is, as long as we continue to practise a unitary type of government skewed in favour of a centre that concentrates enormous political and economic advantages to itself. Good enough, President Goodluck Jonathan has made a promise to that effect.
As a lasting solution to unexpected changes to the zoning arrangement, as exemplified by Yar'Adua sudden death the seventh National Assembly must begin with a thorough and holistic amendment of the constitution to enthrone true fiscal federalism. With that, the ceaseless clamour for the sharing of political patronages on the basics of ethnicity would be drastically reduced.
Can Tambuwal/Ihediohian's make the desired change? The House of Representatives for instance, has had the notoriety for political scheming that leave its leaders in the lurch. The redolent ordeal of Salisu Buhari, Adolphus Wabara, Patricia Etteh and now that of Dimeji Bankole, all erstwhile Speakers of the Lower Chamber should make Tambuwal to tread cautiously. There would be hidden banana peels both from within the PDP and without.
Those who feel genuinely hurt, that they have been stabbed in the back by their victory would be waiting and watching, to identify his weak points and how to do him in. Others, who may not be comfortable with some steps the new Speaker would take to assert the authority of the House would be making their cold calculations. Now, Tambuwal remains a PDP member and must not forget that in a hurry.
Tambuwal must therefore, not be seen as overtly belligerent in posture to the executive, while leading the House to carry out its oversight functions. Criticism must be constructive, well thought - out and to the best interest of Nigeria and Nigerians.
What Nigerians want is a legislature driven by the patriotic fervour to serve the overall interests of Nigerians. We want laws that would create jobs for the teeming unemployed youth, ensure stable infrastructure (power, road, energy) to stimulate and strengthen entrepreneurship.
Indeed, we want laws that would enhance national security. Laws that would ban the importation of sundry items which we can produce locally. Laws that would increase the capacity of Local technology to produce intermediate products of international standard, to increase our foreign exchange earnings. Laws that would make the CBN focus on its core duty of enhancing monetary framework; that would make commercial banks start to lend again to the manufacturing sector to increase their capacity for massive job creation.
Tambuwal is therefore, enjoined not to be carried away by the deafening drumbeats of victory but settle down to the great tasks ahead. I wish him good luck.

Original_Search_Title: 18 Kiambu Bars Shut Down in Illegal Brews Crackdown
Headline: Kenya: 18 Kiambu Bars Shut Down in Illegal Brews Crackdown
Body_Text:
Some 18 bars were yesterday closed down in Kiambu and their owners arrested following the death of 10 people who drank a poisonous brew. Kiambu DC Albert Kimathi led the crackdown.
The DC said they were in an operation to rid Kiambu of illicit liquor that has proved to be deadly. "We are going to carry out the crackdown without sparing any one and we are asking members of public to volunteer information on where these brews are manufactured," he said. Prime Minister Raila visited the area on Sunday and ordered a crackdown on pubs. He directed that those selling illicit brews be arrested.
The Star has established that most of the killer brews- Miti ni dawa, Monalisa and Flying Horse - are manufactured in people's homes in Karia village. The fiesta brew is said to be manufactured in Thika. Kiambu women were yesterday set to hold special prayers for the victims at Banana shopping centre.
Kiambu County Women Forum chairlady Lilian Wakiiya said the brews have caused grief and shock to the residents of Kiambu. She accused the government of doing little to curb the menace. "We are going to join hands with the grieving families and other women from Banana to conduct a peaceful prayer. This is one of the biggest tragedies that has hit Kiambu county and we regret that our children are swimming in the dreaded brews," Wakiiya said. Kiambu public health officer Romano Kinyua said they have collected samples of nine types of brews.
He said they would be taken to Kenya Bureau of Standards and Government Chemist to determine their composition and what has caused the deaths. Kinyua said they are investigating the origin of the brews.
Meanwhile, Naivasha MP John Mututho has accused Internal Security minister George Saitoti of laxity in the fight against illegal brews. Speaking on Sunday when he visited the area, Mututho said he will move a censure motion against Saitoti in Parliament. "It is disheartening that the Ministry of Internal Security has been accused of laxity and the minister himself is not acting. I shall move this censure motion at the end of this week because we have lost a generation to this cheap liquor," said Mututho.

Original_Search_Title: Environmental Regeneration - the Lagos Example
Headline: Nigeria: Environmental Regeneration - the Lagos Example
Body_Text:
Unarguably, one of the major pre-occupations of the Governor Babatunde Fashola administration in Lagos state is environmental renewal.
The government is challenged to invest heavily in the sector as a littoral state which is greatly exposed to environmental abuse due to intense economic and social activities.
From landscape design and management, promotion of cleaner cities through greening and solid waste management, enforcement of pollution control for industries among others, except one is a blind bat, what has been done so far to redeem the environment and enhance its value are visible for all to see.
The efforts of the government can best be assessed and appreciated if we put into perspective how the state looked like prior to 1999 and the transformation that has taken place till date. We could all recall that before now, the state was full of mountains of refuse everywhere.
Residential areas and major highways were turned into dumpsites to the extent that mischievous critics derisively turned the slogan of the state to 'state of aquatic' squalor'.
One other critical point that needs to be understood before one could objectively appraise how well the government has fulfilled the promise of raising the city's standard is that close to 10,000 metric tons of refuse is being generated daily in Lagos , more than what Ghana as a country is generating.
Going to work with vigor and disciplined implementation of its agenda on the environment, the BRF government in continuation of the good foundation laid by the Bola Tinubu administration has been turning the above stated challenges into opportunity for job and wealth creation.
The scourge of environmental malaise was arrested by strengthening and creating agencies to manage the process of waste collection, transportation and disposal in the state. Not only were agencies established for enforcing sanitation, integrated mass enlightenment and social mobilization on maintenance of sanitary environment has become an on-going activity in the state.
Introduction of Dino bins in over 2000 locations, contracting Highway managers for cleaning of roads, introduction and regulation of PSP operations are some of the measures the state has used to achieve better environmental cleanliness. The state has rehabilitated dump sites at Olushosun, Solous and Abule Egba with establishment of additional mechanized land fill sites in Badagry, Mowe and Ikorodu. Additional transfer loading stations were constructed in addition to provision of over 100 new refuse compactor trucks.
Every last Saturday of the month is made a Sanitation Day, when roads, public buildings and shops stay closed until noon , to allow Lagosians to clean and tidy their neighborhoods. To further improve the environment of markets across the state, weekly market sanitation day was also introduced.
One project that manifests concrete improvement on the Lagos environment is the greening and beautification of open spaces, decrepit loops, verges and medians across the state. Whereas the governor has won the trust, respect and confidence of enlightened and appreciative citizens for redeeming the promise to bring back as much greenery as possible to Lagos , the cynics and mismatched political opponents are mischievously asking if it is the beautification that people will eat.
One, the beautification has generated jobs for previously idle youths.
Second, it has improved the aesthetic appeal and recreational value of the environment.
Third, the greening project is done to mitigate the effects of global warming through carbon dioxide emission control. Last but not the least; it is done in line with the promise of creating a good and comfortable environment for all inhabitants of Lagos state.
Today when the entire world is focusing attention on global warming and its attendant effects on climatic changes it should be to the advantage of the current government that people support continuity of this laudable project.
One can go on listing many other achievements in the area of flood control with creation of such agencies like Emergency Abatement Gang (EFAG), Drain ducks, Kick Against Indiscipline (KAI) and the concerted effort on climate change control through which major stakeholders are being carried along by the state government.
With the aforementioned work, one is not deluded that everything has been achieved. Still it appears that a lot still needs to be done especially in terms of enforcement and enlightenment. This is due to the fact that behavioral change communication is a continuous thing. What is required to maintain sane and friendly environment is not just about what the government is doing but also about the people's attitude.
Many people seem not to understand that the quality of our lives as human beings is substantially a reflection of the quality of the environment which we inhabit. Many still seem not to comprehend that the environment which we inhabit, like kola in Igbo culture, is life. Or how else can we explain the attitude of our people who persistently make their living abodes unsanitary and inhospitable environment? It is still common to see people throwing things out on the highways from their cars. Travelling in commercial buses even gives one a more panoramic view of things. On our streets, stagnant gutters still occupy pride of place next to conveniently-located food hawkers - all the easier for garbage disposal. Even some of the fanciest areas are not exempt - "pure water" sachets (which surely rank very high on the list of the most environmentally-unfriendly products ever invented!), bottles and unofficial garbage dumps circle the gated walls of posh homes and well-manicured lawns.
This dirty habit is not justifiable in a state where the government is doing so much on environmental sanitation. It is detrimental to our common good. It is certain no one has ever died from walking around with a banana peel or pure water sachet in their pocket or car until it could conveniently be disposed of!
Despite efforts being put in place to sanitise the environment, some still prefer to continue to live in the past as they still get involved in actions capable of frustrating government's investment in the sector. For instance, many houses in areas like Ajegunle in Ikorodu (where there were cases of cholera recently), Amukoko, Orile Iganmu, Ijora Badia, Ajegunle in Ajeromi and Idi Araba in Mushin are lacking toilet facilities. In some houses, septics are channeled directly into the drain! The question is, if cleanliness is next to Godliness as the saying goes, where does that leave us? Ultimately, the responsibility for making Lagos livable rests on the state government and the people.
Musbau is Lagos State Ministry of Information and Strategy

Original_Search_Title: Women's Fury as Brews Kill Their Men
Headline: Kenya: Women's Fury as Brews Kill Their Men
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Women in a Kiambu village are concerned that the number of males in the area is on the decline and they blame killer brews.
The women in Banana Village said men and youth have been seriously affected by the cheap brews manufactured in the area.
They voiced their concern two days after more than 10 people died after taking liquor known as Miti ni Dawa at the weekend. (READ: Relatives' last moments with killer brew victims)
"Enough is enough! Were are not going to sit down and watch our male children dying. The administration is doing very little. We will take the law in our hands," said Ms Joyce Wanjiku.
She said that apart from the recent reported deaths of 13 villagers, several other people had died after taking the brews, but their deaths were not reported.
"At least one person dies every week due to brew-related complications, but they are never reported. We have no men here; they have no strength to work any more and even never meet their marital responsibilities," Ms Wanjiku said.
Twenty seven-year-old Mwihaki Karanja said her husband died two years ago after consuming liquor, leaving her to take care of three children alone.
"I feel much pain that I was left by my husband at this very young age. It would be understandable had he died of an accident or of normal illness, but not brew related complications," Ms Karanja said.
In Banana, women say the Mututho Law is virtually unknown as men start drinking from as early as 7am.
"We have no Mututho Law. At sunrise, you find men drunk, yet the law states that bars should open from 5 to 11pm on weekdays," said Ms Joan Mukami.
And Ms Sarah Wambui, who lost her nephew in the recent tragedy, appealed to the government to enforce the laws and ensure alcohol is fit for human consumption.
"The government has the power to ban all cheap and harmful drinks; they are a danger to our generation."
She accused the administration of doing very little to maintain the Mututho law, adding that it was a shame that leaders took action after things had already gone wrong.
"We saw leaders ordering the bars to be closed, but should they always wait for people to die to take action?" she asked.
Area MP Stanley Githunguri agreed with Ms Wambui, and accused DOs, chiefs and the police of not doing enough to close down the dens.
And even as residents of Banana wait to bury their dead, three people from the nearby Riabai Village were admitted to Kiambu District Hospital after taking a brew branded Monalisa on Sunday night.
This prompted the closure of 18 bars and the arrest of their owners. On Tuesday, women will be holding prayers in Banana over the incident that has caused grief in the entire village.
Kiambu district public health officer Roman Kinyua said his staff were on alert, after it emerged that the brew affected villagers beyond Banana.
"We are going to take samples of all liquors being consumed here for examination because it seems there is a big problem," Mr Romano said.
Accusing Saitoti
Naivasha MP John Mututho, the man who drafted the Alcohol Bill is accusing the Internal Security minister, Prof George Saitoti, of failure to enforce laws.
"The ministry has been allocated Sh400 million to form structures in the licensing board to deal with quality, regulation and promotion, which are crucial," the MP said.
Last year, dozens of people died in Nairobi's Shauri Moyo, Thindigua in Kiambu and Nanyuki after taking illicit brews.

Original_Search_Title: Crack Down On 'Merchants of Death', Says Prime Minister
Headline: Kenya: Crack Down On 'Merchants of Death', Says Prime Minister
Body_Text:
Prime Minister Raila Odinga yesterday asked police and the provincial administration to decisively deal with selliers of lethal drinks. The PM said "merchants of death" are selling poison to the people in the name of alcohol.
He was speaking in Banana, Kiambu County, during the funeral of one of the ten people who died after consuming a local brew.
Several others are fighting for their lives in Kiambu district hospital.
The PM said police should apply laws dealing with sale of poison to rein in on those selling killer brews. He said alcoholism and drug abuse are becoming a national catastrophe and unless police swiftly and firmly, lives will continue to be lost. He said more women and youth are abusing drugs. Raila ordered police to close bars found to be dealing with lethal drinks. He urged residents to be cautious on the brews they take.
The PM said some people are selling poison using labels of licensed alcoholic. "Laws exist with severe penalties for those who supply dangerous substances. But the merchants of death are taking advantage of loopholes and corruption in our policing," he said. The PM was accompanied to by Kiambaa MP Stanley Githunguri and former Attorney General Charles Njonjo. Raila later visited Githiga village also in Kiambu, where a similar brew claimed three lives yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: Relatives' Last Moments With Killer Brew Victims
Headline: Kenya: Relatives' Last Moments With Killer Brew Victims
Body_Text:
Nairobi — On Thursday morning, Mr Martin Waweru, a matatu conductor on the Kiambu-Banana route woke up a healthy man and went to work.
In the evening, the father of three returned to his home in Kiambaa village feeling unwell.
His aunt, Ms Sarah Wambui, said Mr Waweru, 32, complained of abdominal disorders, joint aches and sight problems.
"He seemed drunk and complained of stomach and joint pains. He could also not see well," Ms Wambui said.
The aunt said they gave him water and a cup of milk and immediately begun vomiting.
At 3 am, his condition worsened and was taken to Kiambu District Hospital by his wife where he passed away.
Others had died
As time went by, Ms Wambui said she received calls from friends who informed her other people had died under similar circumstances.
About a kilometre away near Banana shopping centre, Mrs Margaret Waithera is mourning after her husband died after taking an illicit brew.
Ms Waithera said she had left her husband, Mr Samuel Kagethe at home on Friday morning to visit a friend.
"He was very fine when I left. While away, a relative called and told me my husband was sick and they wanted to hire a taxi to take him to hospital," she said.
On the way, she said, her husband complained of abdominal pains and fatigue and died on arrival at Karuri dispensary.
According to her, he loved taking alcohol and he may have consumed the killer brew on Thursday. Another victim is Mr Stephen Waweru, a matatu diver and a father of four from Karuri village.
On Saturday at 3 am, he complained of abdominal pains and could not stand upright. He died an hour later after being taken to hospital.
Ms Carol Njeri, his sister-in-law had spoken to him last, and said he was fine during the day.
"He later called me and asked for something to eat. I gave him githeri, and he had difficulty eating. Moments later he complained of stomach ache," she said.
Ms Njeri, said Mr Waweru loved taking the cheap brews and on Thursday had arrived home drunk, adding that it was a great loss especially to his widow who will have to look after the children alone.
Ms Mary Wangari, a casual labourer in Banana town, and a mother of four was not spared by the killer brew.
Ms Nancy Nyambura said she arrived home on Friday evening from Mombasa and had a chat with her aunt who she said was very jovial.
They slept in different rooms but when she woke up on Saturday morning, her aunt was lying dead on her bed.
"I thought it was a natural death, I later heard several people had died after taking illicit liquor and I know my aunt takes those," Ms Nyambura said.
The killer brew is sold at Sh20 per half litre. On Sunday, residents reported eight deaths and two others who had lost their sight.
Kiambu police boss Samuel Mukindia said they were only aware of three people whose bodies are in the mortuary.
"We have not established the number because no more reports have been made to us, but we have launched investigations into the matter," Mr Mukindia had said earlier.

Original_Search_Title: Mututho Says Alcohol Law Not Being Enforced
Headline: Kenya: Mututho Says Alcohol Law Not Being Enforced
Body_Text:
The government is to blame for the death of 11 people who died in Banana after consuming illicit brew. Naivasha MP John Mututho says the government has failed to enforce the alcoholic drinks control law.
It is only last week that Keroche Industries CEO Tabitha Karanja expressed fear that many Kenyans may start drinking illicit brews following a decision by the government to increase beer prices.

Original_Search_Title: 10 Die in Kiambu After Drinking Illicit Brew
Headline: Kenya: 10 Die in Kiambu After Drinking Illicit Brew
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ten people in Banana, Kiambu, were said to have died after consuming illicit brews.
Police said they did not have any reports about the deaths, but residents say the first person died on Friday morning.
Ms Sarah Wambui said her nephew, Martin Waweru came home drunk and began complaining he could not see and that his stomach was aching.
The father of three, 32, was rushed to Kiambu Hospital at around 3 a.m. after his condition worsened. He died a few minutes later.
Mrs Margaret Waithera from Banana town said her husband, Samuel Kang'ethe, 69, also complained of similar symptoms on Friday afternoon and died upon arrival at a nearby hospital.
Both men had consumed the brew, which is manufactured locally and sold at Sh20 per half litre.
Residents say 10 people have died so far, including two women, and all had consumed the deadly brew in two bars in Banana town.
They fear there could be more deaths. Two of the 10 died as journalists followed up on the story.
Stephen Waweru had spoken to journalists at around 3 p.m. on Saturday, saying he could not see and had abdominal pains.
He was taken to Karuri Dispensary but died an hour later.
"We have not been able to establish the number of those dead because no report has been made to us, but already we have launched investigations," Kiambu OCPD Samuel Mukindia said.
Angry locals stormed the two bars where the deceased are said to have taken the drinks known locally as Miti Ni Dawa and Fiesta.
"We are tired. The police are doing very little to crack down on illicit brews and if they will not do their work, we are going to take the law into our hands," said resident Raymond Kuria.

Original_Search_Title: Of Coronations, Royal Weddings And 'Mwizi Wa Baiskeli'...
Headline: Tanzania: Of Coronations, Royal Weddings And 'Mwizi Wa Baiskeli'...
Body_Text:
The royal wedding which was staged in London on April 29, 2011 took me generations down memory lane, reminding me of the coronation ceremony which saw the second edition of Queen Elizabeth (QE-II) succeed to the British throne on February 6, 1952, and was formally crowned on June 2, 1953.
The first edition -- Queen Elizabeth-I of the House of Tudor -- who died at 69 years, reigned for 44 years, from 1558 to 1603. She is described in Pears Cyclopaedia as "a fervid Protestant, lover of her country, a masterful and enlightened ruler who added distinction to a distinguished period..."
Perhaps no wonder that she orchestrated the "defeat of the Spanish Armada, the naval supremacy of England, extension of her colonies" --and William Shakespeare the Bard of Stratford-on-Avon!
Her namesake who ascended to the throne three-and-a-half centuries later could very well be said to have presided over dismemberment of the British Empire, seeing to the granting of independence (of sorts) to dozens of countries in the former British Empire.
But, at the ripe old age of 85 years, Queen Elizabeth-II is still going strong. Ample evidence of this was visible as Her Britannic Majesty presided at the wedding of her grandson, William Philip Arthur Louis, last April. William was born on June 21, 1982 to the Queen's first son, Prince Charles Philip Arthur George (born November 14, 1948) and the late Princess Diana.
On paper, Prince Charles is the heir apparent to the British Throne, and his newly-wedded son William is second in line to the throne. But, a colleague at the next workstation tells me that, the way things are going, he can see the father losing out to the son at the British majestic stakes.
This, he says, is partly because Prince Charles is a strong-headed fellow who 'failed' to toe the narrow and straight marital path, even after being shoehorned into marriage with one who was not his First Choice. I don't know.
But, what I know is that King Edward-VIII (also of the House of Windsor like QE-II) ascended to the British throne on January 20, 1936 -- and abdicated on December 11 the same year! Reason? The woman he loved, an American divorcee, was what British protocol frowned on... Sheesh!
Most flummoxing is, of course, why or when QE-Ii will give up the Throne--and to whom: son Charles or grandson William? She has already clocked 59 years on the Throne, nearly four years short of the 63 years of Hanoverian Queen Victoria's reign (1837-1901).
Does QE-II plan to beat Victoria's reign? She already has gone beyond Victoria's lifespan of 81 years, anyways...
Oh, the William's wedding reminded me of the QE-II coronation when the Northern Province Welfare Officer, one Mister Saddler, came to our place in Old Moshi and set up cinematic equipment among banana fronds for us to gawk at soundless films.
Those were also the days when we were able to look at films by a budding, talented actor then, the late Rashidi Kawawa, in his epic 'Mwizi wa Baiskeli,' Bicycle Thief! Most refreshing, I must say... Cheers!

Original_Search_Title: Bankole's Long Walk to Prosecution
Headline: Nigeria: Bankole's Long Walk to Prosecution
Body_Text:
Lagos — Nigeria's former number four citizen, Dimeji Bankole, is currently faced with charges of some questionable deals that took place during his tenure as the Speaker of the Federal House of Representatives. He was arrested on Sunday by the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), and was on Wednesday arraigned at a Federal High Court in Abuja on a16-count charge related to financial impropriety.
There was unconfirmed report that Bankole had planned to leave for the United Kingdom on a 7am flight to beat his arrest and maintain his freedom, but the EFCC investigators foiled that effort as they arrived at his official home at Apo Quarters in Abuja late on Sunday to arrest him.
In a written statement, Bankole's spokesman, Idowu Bakare, denied that he had planned to leave for the United Kingdom on the said day, claiming he had instead scheduled meetings in Abuja and Lagos for the weekend as part of his valedictory activities from the House.
Political watchers are in agreement that for about three years that he sat on the driving seat of the House of Representatives he had waded through oceans of controversy.
The various crises Bankole encountered while in office started coming just one week after his inauguration. Then his political opponents claimed he did not complete his one-year National Youth Service Corps (NYSC) scheme, which is mandatory for all Nigerian university graduates of 30 years of age and below.
Bankole scaled that hurdle and then another resurfaced when the story broke that an executive session he presided over approved the purchase of 380 units of Peugeot 407 cars; five for each of the 76 committees of the House.
Peugeot Automobile Nigeria, which supplied the vehicles, it was reported allegedly, supplied inferior vehicles at a much lesser cost than what was approved.
Although, Bankole might have survived the banana peels laid on his path, his present travail leaves sour taste in the mouth. His tenure, like many have observed, was a mixture of controversies based on financial underhand dealings and accolades in the art of lawmaking and oversight function.
It was not long that other sundry issues started unfolding.
For instance, a group of lawmakers in the Lower Chamber, operating under the guise of progressive, had accused Bankole of flouting the Public Procurement Act by influencing contracts to his cronies, friends and aides.
For instance, an allegation of N30million contract for the procurement of torchlight for members of the House of Representatives was sighted as one of the many fleecing acts of the former Speaker.
Late last year, June 22, a group in the House under the aegis of The Progressives, gave him a one-week ultimatum to resign from his position as speaker or face impeachment.
Spokesman of the group and archenemy of the former Speaker, Dino Melaye, never left anybody in doubt that Bankole was not a saint as being proclaimed.
Melaye told those that would care to listen that his group was in possession of damning evidence and documents to nail Bankole of corruption and abuse of office, accusing Bankole further of highhandedness.
The EFCC said it had been investigating him over fraud allegations, the first going back to 2008 when human rights lawyer, Festus Keyamo, petitioned against alleged irregularities in the House contract for the supply of cars.
Keyamo explained that the Clerk of the National Assembly (CNA) awarded the contract for the supply of the 380 units of 407 ST (Sports) Auto to Peugeot Automobile of Nigeria (PAN) at N2, 359,486,500 but what was supplied was the 407 Comfort Automatic at a total of N1, 570,800,000.
The second investigation kicked off last year when the anti-corruption agency set up a committee to probe Bankole and his principal officers over alleged misappropriation of over N9billion from the 2008/2009 House budget.
Another allegation surfaced this year that Bankole had approved a loan of N10billion without House resolution and this, to most Nigerians, led to his arrest on Sunday.
There are some critics who believe that Bankole's travail did not come in a jiffy. This is because since his election as speaker, he had been swimming from one stream of financial mess to the other.
Bankole, going by reports, also had it rough with former Governor of Ogun state, Gbenga Daniel. The story was that Bankole was interested in Daniel's office and was scheming to upstage the governor. There were series of fights between them, many of which they denied.
But on many occasions, Daniel had made some very derogatory remarks about the Speaker. For example, in July 2010, at the inauguration of the Ota Bridge, the governor said that Bankole had no visible achievements to show in the state.
He was quoted as saying, "Tell the Speaker to stop claiming responsibility for the construction. The former President, Olusegun Obasanjo, started the project and we want to thank him. Tell my younger brother, the Speaker, that he should do something before his tenure lapses. We all have less than few months to leave office."
Also at a public ceremony last year, Daniel lashed out at him saying that he was a curse to Ogun State.
The governor was not the only person Bankole fought. There was also the case of a member of the House of Representatives, Independence Ogunewe, over a simmering issue between the two, which started with a lousy joke and nearly ended in an exchange of blows. At the end, Ogunulewe was suspended.
While many political watchers say the recent travail of Bankole is politically motivated, some are of the opinion that it is a process of deepening the nation's democracy.
Okey Nwosu of Nigerian Democratic Party (NDP) is of the opinion that, "There in nothing as good as good stewardship. While the case is going on, which could prove that Bankole is either guilty or not, it is good to know that putting searchlight on public officers, whether serving or not, is good for any country. At least, that will bring out the best and brightest for the country. It is when such searchlights are brought to bear on all public officers that we will begin to have quality and selfless leadership in Nigeria."
Yinka Odumakin, Publicity Secretary of Afenifere Renewal Group (ARG) told Saturday Independent that the EFCC and other anti-corruption creations are weapons used to intimidate individuals that fall out of a political gang that controls power.
He said: "They are tools or weapons of gang fights. You know the Nigerian ruling elite have different gangs, when the member of a gang falls out, the other gang goes after that person and the people will be cheering without knowing the undercurrents of what is happening. What we have is selective war against different factions of the ruling elite. Before May 29, we heard the EFCC saying that some governors were indicted and will be arrested, how many of them have been arrested since then? Many of them have fled the country; did they leave without the consent of the President?"
The charge sheet alleges, among others, that: "Bankole and others now at large, on or about May 28, 2008, within the jurisdiction of the Federal High Court, being Body of Principal Officers of the House responsible for the approval of contracts, with intent to defraud, conspired among yourselves to inflate the cost of 400 units of 40-inch Samsung (LNS. 341) television sets by approving their purchase at the rate of N525, 000.00 per unit, instead of the prevailing market price of N295,000. The offence contravenes Section 58(4)(a) of the Public Procurement Act and punishable under Section 58(5) of the Act.
"They, on or about May 28, 2008, with intent to defraud, rigged the bid for the purchase of 100 units of Sharp digital copier 5316 by refusal to follow all the procedures prescribed for public procurements in Sections 17 to 56 of the Public Procurement Act, leading to a loss of value to the treasury.
"They, on or about May 28, 2008, with intent to defraud, rigged the bid for the purchase of three units of Mercedes Benz S-600 cars by refusal to follow all the procedures prescribed for public procurements in Sections 17 to 56 of the Public Procurement Act."
Of truth, opinion is still divided on the real reasons for the arrest of Bankole. This is because the N10billion loan allegation that came with so much noise from the anti-corruption body was downplayed in the charge sheet. This, to Bakare, is a pointer to mischief.
Bakare, in a statement made available to Saturday Independent noted that "After filling the request for the enforcement of his fundamental human rights, the EFCC promptly summoned Keyamo to dustup his old allegations against the Speaker bothering on purchase of cars and other items to form the basis for immediate charges against Bankole to preempt the outcome of his fundamental human right suit.
"It is for this reason that Mr. Keyamo immediately rushed to the same Federal High Court in Abuja to file a 16-count charge on June 7, a day after Bankole's suit. The charges filed by Keyamo contained nothing on the allegation of N10 billion loan, which formed the basis of the arrest and detention of Bankole. All items on the charge sheet were mainly from the old allegations of convoy cars and office equipment purchase."
Whether Bankole's ordeal is political or not is the duty of the court to decide.
However, one thing remains: Bankole has had his laugh in the past travails, perhaps now may be the turn of his opponents within and outside the Parliament to have their own laugh. Only the court will decide who laughs last.

Original_Search_Title: Zanu-PF in Dilemma
Headline: Zimbabwe: Zanu-PF in Dilemma
Body_Text:
ZANU PF lurched into a quandary over the hero status of the late nationalist Edgar Zivanayi Tekere who was revered by many within the party for his liberation war credentials but despised by others for his political intemperance that resulted in his defection after clashes with President Robert Mugabe.
"Two Boy" as Tekere was affectionately known, succumbed to cancer at Murambi Garden Clinic on Tuesday. ZANU-PF spokesperson, Rugare Gumbo, yesterday said that no position had been reached yet on Tekere's status.
"No province has yet approached the party with such a request (for hero status). (ZANU-PF) Manicaland (province) is meeting today; we want to hear from them because in our party a province where the death would have occurred first meets and makes an evaluation of that person's worthiness to be given (a particular) status. So I cannot say anything because the meeting is not yet over," said Gumbo yesterday.
ZANU-PF has come under fire for politicising the national shrine with some who made outstanding contributions to free Zimbabwe being relegated in death due to their differences with the party's leadership.
These include Ndabaningi Sithole, Sheiba Tavarwisa, Thenjiwe Lesabe, Henry Hamadziripi, Noel Mukono, Canaan Banana and James Chikerema. Others like Lookout Masuku were declared heroes posthumously after heavy political lobbying.
Tekere said before his death that he did not want to be buried at the national shrine, saying he did not want to be among "thieves and crooks".
Tekere went to St Augustines School in Penhalonga, and studied Economics with the London School of Economics. He was an Anglican.
Sources indicated that the death of the former ZANU-PF secretary general had plunged ZANU-PF into a dilemma, as some still idolised him for his war efforts, while others wanted to deny him the ultimate recognition for failing ZANU-PF's "consistency" test.
An early ally of President Robert Mugabe, Tekere later became critical of his former comrades, a development that saw him at one stage forming his own party, the Zimbabwe Unity Movement, credited for foiling ZANU-PF's attempts to turn Zimbabwe into a one party State.
ZANU-PF Manicaland province met yesterday to deliberate on his status, but details of the meeting were not available last night.

Original_Search_Title: Choice of Tambuwal as Speaker is Democracy at Work - Representatives
Headline: Nigeria: Choice of Tambuwal as Speaker is Democracy at Work - Representatives
Body_Text:
Abuja — A member of the House of Representatives, Zakary Mohammed, has observed that Monday's election of Aminu Tambuwal as the House Speaker against the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) zoning arrangement will not sink the ship of state, but rather strengthen democracy in the country.
Mohammed, a journalist and one time Commissioner for Sports in Kwara State, said though he was not in the position to speak for the party, what has happened is "democracy at play, it does not recognise that (zoning), but I am not in a position to speak on behalf of the party.
He spoke to newsmen at a retreat organised by the National Assembly in Abuja.
"We believe in the new Speaker because we know he would take us to the promised land, he has the experience as a third timer; I am quite optimistic he would perform, making good use of effective communication.
"As a former minority member, then subsequently becoming a majority member, he has tasted both sides and this is an added advantage to his pedigree in the House.
"As a person I do not have any problem with the leadership because if a people choose a leader, talk to themselves, route communications properly among themselves there is no way we can fail, we have seen the banana peels, so we must tread cautiously", he said.
Mohammed also said journalists, as members of the fourth realm of the estate, deserve better pay because without them, government cannot reach the governed.
According to him, the 4th press should be treated with dignity as it represents the fourth arm of government.
"The welfare of journalists will be uppermost in our deliberations as we settle down to legislate in the 7th Assembly, we must advocate minimum standards, good salary packages and minimum entry point for practitioners.
"A situation whereby some organisations just give a reporter an identity card and tell him 'you are on your own' does not tell well of the profession.
"The Nigerian Union of Journalists (NUJ) should look beyond the issue of triennial confabs and focus more on the welfare of pen pushers who play a very pivotal role in the act of information dissemination and nation building", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Testimonies - Woman, 21, Village By Blolequin, Western Ivory Coast
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Testimonies - Woman, 21, Village By Blolequin, Western Ivory Coast
Body_Text:
Woman, 21, village by Blolequin, western Ivory Coast:
Two months ago, we were all in our village when armed men entered. They started to shoot and kill people and burn houses. I fled into the bush with my baby. The attackers ran past me to where my parents were. I could hear my parents screaming and crying. The next day, I went back and found them both dead. Many people were dead and their bodies burned. I ran back to the bush and fled towards Toulepleu. My other young son was with my parents when the attack happened and I haven't found him since.
I stayed in the bush. We could not go to the village because we have no houses there. We slept under palm roofing and ate raw manioc. Some other people have gone to Liberia but they say there is nothing to eat there. One man came back recently and he says he will go back to call his family to join him here because there is not enough food there.
There are many people out there [in the bush]. And a lot of illness, with people who have stomach problems. We only come out of the bush to go to the clinic. We are too afraid to see dead bodies. Often I think a lot about what happened and my heart starts to pound.
Boy, 17, village by Pehé
At the end of February, my village was attacked. Twenty of us ran to our campement together. But the attackers came to find us in the bush in the middle of the night. They shot my mother in the chest, then shot and killed my father and my younger brother and sister. Four people, they killed.
Six other people were wounded. One was my aunt, who was shot in the shoulder. We tried to convince her to go to Danané hospital but she refused because she was too afraid. Up to today, she still has not seen a doctor, the wound is infected and the bullet remains inside her shoulder.
I escaped the campement to another house up on a hill. But the armed men came after me again and set fire to the house. My arm and my leg were burned. I fled the burning house. The men said they would kill. They hit me on the back of my head with a machete, and cut my arm as well. I lost so much blood.
There were two other campements near my village and one my was uncles. Twenty-six people were killed with machetes in those campements - men, women, children, babies. The attackers dug a hole and buried all these bodies so no one would see them.
From my village, I fled to a campement of my aunt and uncle near Pehé. I was there all alone, and didn't dare to wash for days. Later, who was left in my family came - just eight of us. Fourteen others had been killed.
We are now like refugees in our own country. We keep looking for food everywhere but from the start we couldn't find anything in the campements. Often the rice had been burned. We eat bananas and drink water from rivers and shallow wells we dig ourselves.
We never have enough to eat. If we eat in the morning, we have nothing at night. The rain falls on our heads through the roof of our shelter. People get sick out in the campements and go without treatment. One woman lost two children to malaria and the third one is now sick in the hospital in Danané.
We stay in the bush because we hope the fighting could not touch us there any more. If there is a food distribution I come to the village. But then I go back to the bush, because there is not peace in our village.
I knew all of those armed men. They were from my village, I even know their names. My village is now full of these people, how can I go back to live there? I think a lot about how all my family is gone. What is left behind is me and my memories .

Original_Search_Title: Anyim, Let the Tea Be Hot
Headline: Nigeria: Anyim, Let the Tea Be Hot
Body_Text:
During the Chief Olusegun Obasanjo years, the idea was mooted to appoint an Igboman as the Secretary to Government of the Federation (SGF). Our revered leader, Dim Chukwuemeka Odimegwu Ojukwu was so enraged over such an outlandish proposition that he derisively described the SGF position as a tea boy.
As a man given to grandiloquent hyperbole, Ojukwu's outburst must have ignited nationalistic indignation among the Igbo, such that the matter was eventually buried.
Now with the appointment of Senator Anyim Pius Anyim as the SGF, I don't know what would have been the reaction of Dim Gburugburu, were he not indisposed. However, the fact that President Goodluck Jonathan has received nothing but praises, since that appointment, suggests that it sits well with both Ndigbo and other Nigerians.
There still remains the issue of suitability or otherwise of Anyim, a former senate president for the position. In America, it is doubtful that a man, who has risen to the dizzying height of senate president, would condescend to be the Secretary of State. Somebody also raised the question of Anyim not having the requisite experience to execute the responsibility of his new office.
While I leave Americans to their politics and principles, taking into cognizance the fact that ours is a fledging democracy and that being in government means more to us than the so-called moral high horse, it is pertinent to state here that Anyim by character and bearing is more than qualified to occupy the position of SGF. As a man who had served this nation with empathy and passion; honesty and commitment, he is quite on a familiar turf. When we talk of experience, we should look at it holistically and not from the narrow prism of a particular job description. After all, what is the use of a tertiary education if not to equip one for any challenge?
Still on experience, in 1996, shortly after his enthronement as the Metropolitan See of Owerri Catholic Archdiocese, Most Rev Dr Anthony J.V. Obinna told a television panel which I was privileged to a member of that one does not know how to become an Archbishop until one starts the job. That was his response to the question of what experience he was bringing into his new job. Only recently, President George Bush Snr made it clear that you can never know how to be the president of United States of America until you get into the Oval office. The drift? Anyim needn't be a super civil servant in the mould of late Shehu Musa before he can discharge his duties efficiently and effectively.
Honestly, I think there are two major issues that will shape the success or failure of Anyim as the SGF. One is the level of confidence and trust Jonathan has on him with the concomitant responsibilities he will shoulder. The second is Anyim's competence vis-à-vis the politics and pressures associated with that office.
On the first score, I have seen a Secretary to a State Government that was more powerful than his principal, the governor. To the embarrassment of many, this powerful SSG was wont to overrule his boss on many issues. It was only when the governor's wife used bedroom tactics to bully the husband that the SSG lost his job. By then, seven years had gone by. I have also witnessed where an SSG was reduced to a glorified servant only fit to sign government press releases. His main schedule was taken away and handed over to the Chief of Staff in a government ran by family members. I think it is this scenario that sparked off Dim Ojukwu's disdain for that office.
In essence, Jonathan can decide to make Anyim relevant if he has respect for him as a person and the South East geo-political zone that he represents in the presidency. In the Gen Sani Abacha years, Aminu Saleh was quite a powerful man. One cannot say that about Ufot Ekaette during Obasanjo's days. We also know that the personality and character of the principal define how he relates with the SGF. Jonathan certainly does not have a complex. Educated and annoyingly humble, he is not likely to look down on Anyim. The fact that he appointed Anyim SSG who did not support him initially before the PDP primaries, points to a man who believes in merit and result.
Then how does Jonathan look at the South East where Anyim comes from? He chose Anyim based on the recommendation of Ohanaeze Ndigbo. That means he respects our leadership. That also means he appreciates the fact that the Igbo voted overwhelmingly for him. The far North believes that Jonathan is an Igboman. Also with Jonathan's ascendancy to the presidency, it will now take a minimum of 12 years for an Igboman to emerge Nigerian president. That alone should always be at the back of Jonathan's mind when he weighs the responsibilities of Anyim's office.
Then Anyim's competence. As politicians are fond of saying when they want to hoodwink voters, the man has been tried and tested. As senate president, Anyim was able to steer the senate away from the controversies and banana peels that led to the removal of his predecessors, Evan Enwerem and Chuba Okadigbo. He enjoyed the respect and support of his colleagues even when Obasanjo, aided by people like Governor Sam Egwu did everything to remove him from office. He was also investigated and found to be corrupt free. Above all he left when the ovation was loudest. That shows character, strength, integrity.
That naturally brings us to the germane issue of whether Anyim's tenure as SGF will benefit Ndigbo. No matter how we pretend, we are first of all Igbo before being Nigerians. I think Obafemi Awolowo propounded that theory. When in 2001 I met Anyim in company of my friend, Prof Chinedum Nwajiuba of the University of Ibadan , he spoke with passion and pain regarding the fate of Ndigbo in Nigeria .
He lamented our individualistic tendencies, our personal greed and our predilection towards group self destruct. He wished we had leaders who would think more of what will benefit the masses than personal enrichment and aggrandizement. Ten years on, I don't think we are nearer those ideals he dreamt of. But I am hopeful that it won't be long. That is why I salute Governor Peter Obi who said that he is more concerned with what President Jonathan will do for Ndigbo like the Second Niger Bridge, erosion control and upgrading of the Enugu Airport to an international status.
When Anyim was senate president, he influenced the award of the contract for the construction of Okigwe - Amasiri - Afikpo Road. Today that is the only visible and durable federal road in the entire South East. Millions of Ndigbo and other Nigerians, who knew what the road looked like before its reconstruction, do not forget Anyim in a hurry. That cannot be said of other Igbo leaders who have occupied federal positions and who also jostled for the SGF position especially from my axis in the old Bende of Abia State.
The Ohafia - Ihechiowa - Ututu - Arochukwu road was abandoned in 1998 when Gen Abacha died. Obasanjo cancelled the contract. Ike Nwachukwu had been our Senator. Mao Ohuabunwa was in the House of Representatives for eight years. Uche Chukwumerije has just resumed his seat as our Senator for a record third term. Yet that road has not been fixed. My prayer is that if it takes Anyim making a delicious hot tea for President Jonathan to fix the road, I will be bold to tell Dim Ojukwu that the position of SGF is more than just making tea for Mr President.
Kalu, a journalist, wrote in from Obiagwulu Ututu, Abia State

Original_Search_Title: Mass Defection Looms in Ondo PDP
Headline: Nigeria: Mass Defection Looms in Ondo PDP
Body_Text:
Akure — There are fears of defection in the troubled Peoples Democratic Party, PDP, members in Ondo State to Labour Party, LP, and Action Congress of Nigeria, ACN.
Vanguard gathered that two chieftains of the party, Col Tunde Awodeyi (Rtd) and the former finance commissioner, Chief Tayo Alasoadura and their supporters are billed to decamp soon.
The defection may not be unconnected with the lingering crisis and the woeful performance of the PDP in the state in recent times.
Plans have been concluded to receive Awodeyi and his supporters from Ose council areas of the state by Governor Olusegun Mimiko while the leaders of ACN are to receive Chief Alasoadura and his supporters.
Chief Alasoadura is to contest the 2012 governorship election under the ACN.
Also, some failed members of the state House of Assembly are equally billed to defect to the two parties. Some of them include Thompsom Atikase of the PDP who would be moving to ACN while Kunle Odidi of the ruling LP is moving also to the ACN.
Speaking on the planned defection of the PDP chieftains and their supporters, Prof Olu Agbi blamed it on the leadership style of former governor, Dr. Olusegun Agagu and Dr. Tayo Dairo.
In a statement, the Publicity secretary, Prince Mike Omogbehin, said the loss of five elections in a row was enough to discourage the members and supporters of the party.
"As you are already aware that the leaders and stakeholders of our party held a meeting where decisions were taken and these decisions include the suspension of Dr Agagu and Dairo with the dissolution of the State Executive." Committee, SEC, and replacing it with the interim of management committee".
In other places both Dairo and Agagu would have stepped aside in the interest of the party6 but both of them were both positioning themselves for higher offices. Also, under the Dairo led SEC the State Secretary, Barrister Sola Oludipe and the Women leader, Lady Folake Akinjoko resigned to contest elections and to the bewilderment of all members of PDP in the , they simply walked back to the Executive to assume their positions as if we were in a banana republic.

Original_Search_Title: The Sixth Senate - an Overview
Headline: Nigeria: The Sixth Senate - an Overview
Body_Text:
When the history of the Sixth Senate is written, the two important issues that would not escape any observer are the tranquility and stability that prevailed in the last four years and the seeming integrity that the leadership of Senator David Bonaventure Alechenu Mark conferred on the Senate. Quite unlike the Fourth and Fifth Senate when constant change in the leadership of the Senate cast a pall of negative perception among Nigerians about the Senate as an institution.
So when Mark the President of the Senate in an election that saw him pitched against his colleague from the same state, Senator George Akume, emerged the President of the Senate, not a few watchers of the legislature heaved a sigh of relief that for once, the Senate may have a new lease of life.
Four years after and considering what the Senate went through, those who invested their hopes in David Mark to stabilize the leadership of the Senate did not do so in vain.
But with his experience and especially antecedents in the Fourth and Fifth Senate, Mark's leadership could not have been otherwise.
