Headline: Nigeria: I Will Be a Transparent Speaker - Munikat
Body_Text:
The struggle for the position of Speaker of the House of Representatives has reached a crescendo as the nation awaits the inauguration of the new administration next week.
In this interview with senior journalists in Abuja, Hon. Munikat Akande-Adeola, one of the major contestants says that she has what it takes to lead the 360-member Green Chamber and would be transparent enough to write her name in gold when she would serve.
When did your interest in politics start?
I have always been interested in politics because when I was in school, I was always very active in campus politics. I and some of my friends always decided who is running for any position and who is going to get it so we were like king makers because we had a way with all the women on campus and even some of the men so if we are not supporting you, you are not going.
That was it. So I always know that I will like to be in politics one day. So at the end of 1998 when it became apparent that civilian rule was coming back that was in 1999, it started getting interested. I was living in Lagos then in Surulere. I found out about the PDP. I got to know where they were holding their meeting so I was attending. By year 2000 when I decided that okay, we are back to civilian rule and I will like to really go into active politics.
It takes a lot to control 360 members of the House of Reps. Do you think you have the energy and the capability?
Very much so. I have the capability. I am one person, like I told you I like to be around people. I'm very accommodating. So I know I have been a leader in so many spheres of my life and I know that for you to be a leader, you have to first of all believe that you are there by the grace of God. And that any other person could be that leader so if you are given that opportunity, you don't have to lord it over them. You have to be a good listener.
You have to have the right temperament which I think I have because of my age now may be if I was talking 20 years back, it would be a different ball game. You have to be able to lead by example first and foremost. I am one person that don't like someone to say you did this, why do you do that.
You know I like ideal things. As much as possible do the best at any given time and in a house like House of Representatives, you really need to be very open. If you are a very open person you will be able to carry your members along. You need to earn their confidence.
Let them know you that you are one of them and you won't have any problem. We have to learn how to manage people. When I was working in the bank, I attended so many courses on team spirit, leadership, management skills and all that. Aside you need to know people, if you want to be a leader, you need to study people. I have been there in the last 4 years unfortunately, the returning members are only 100, we are having 260 new members.
We have been meeting with so many of them and it is only when you listen that you have opportunity to hear their views that you know which direction this person is going, what kind of person is this, what he or she thinks about you and all that. I think I won't have any problem when it comes to control or carry the members along because what you aim at really is good governance and if you know the principles of good governance, openness, accountability, transparency, then you don't have any problem.
You will also learn how to manage the banana peels.
I have told you the cardinal principle about the banana peel, when you are corrupt then you will fear banana peel but when you are very open, everybody is aware of what you are doing, I don't think you will still have anything to do with banana peel. But if you sit down, assuming we are a group and all of us here agreed on something, definitely if you hear something about it outside, you will defend to beat your chest and say no.
That is not what it is. This is what it is because I'm aware and I don't think people just go about to set you up if there are no reasons to do that and that has to do with accountability. Once you make yourself accountable to them and to Nigeria, I don't think you have any problem it is when you try to hide things and it becomes suspicious. People build rumours around that line and when it escalates before you know it you cannot control it again.
There has been a female Speaker Hon Patricia Etteh won't that be a minus for your aspiration to be Speaker of the House?
First and foremost, I don't agree with you because the issue of vying for this position has nothing to do with gender. We are talking of capability here and whoever has that leadership quality or how the ability should be given an opportunity to do so. I said it many times before.
Some will say Etteh failed but I'm not here to rate her. I can not rate her and I don't even believe that she failed. And if we have to consider the number of men who have failed in this country to women, I'm sure the men shouldn't be given any further opportunity...general laughter
It's true because men run most of our every day live and they have issues all over the place and we still give you opportunity so why don't you just forget this issue of a thing and look at Mulikat.
The sit you are vying to occupy is a sit where you will meet with ideas, suggestions which will not necessary be ideal. Do you have the capacity to say no, when it becomes necessary?
Yes, sure. Dialogue is the solution to all these. Do you know how many times people go back to the conference table on one issue and get a solution at the end of the day? I am very much aware of the tasks that go with that office and I'm sure I have the capability. It's not that why can't you say no? You will be known for saying 'no' and if you say 'yes', you will be known for saying yes.
That is just it. All of you may not want to buy my idea but I can convince you and bring you along. At times, you may just be half convinced, and you okay let's leave her execute it, she has convinced us. You don't even win at all times. At times you loose.
There have been criticisms over what has come to stay as the bloated budgets of the National Assembly and the jumbo- pay syndrome among legislators.
Besides the committees have become so many that each members is either a Chairman or Deputy Chairman of committee. How do you intend to tackle these problems? There have been several allegations bordering on fraud in the House. Even right now there is a N10 billion issues on-going. How can you ensure that there is transparency in the system.
People often compare the number of the committees of our National Assembly to those of the United States of America. But we have to remember that the American democracy has lasted over many centuries and we are talking about a developed economy. Ours is different.
In a growing democracy, we have to do things that suit our circumstances, as much as possible. We will be narrowing them down. This is an issue on ground and people have been talking about.
How will you handle the chairmanship of House Committees, given that you have very few PDP members from your Zone without incurring the wrath of some interest group?
We are members of different political parties in the House. Whatever will make us move forward is what we will do. Don't forget that we are a House of Representatives of the Federal Republic of Nigeria and I believe that should be upper most in the hearts of all us. It is going to be a collective decision of the House.
It is not a matter of you are a Chairman or a Deputy Chairman. The efficiency of the committees is what is most important. We are going to do it in such a way that everybody will be happy. One may not even be a chairman or deputy chairman and yet do better.
That is my believe. Looking at the sharing of the committees, I am looking at a situation where we put square pegs in square holes so that we can have maximum benefits. Right now each member belongs to about four or five committees and there is no way you can be effective in all of them. One could be effective may be in some but certainly not in all of that number.
You are in the House on the platform of a political party, the PDP. As a party faithful how would you react if the party asks you to step down for your opponent in the speakership race?
I don't think my party would do that. The party is supreme, anyway, but I believe the party will give all members who are interested in the leadership position the go -ahead to test his or her popularity among the members. It is a democracy.
What is your political philosophy?
My philosophy in life, let me not say political, is to leave a legacy where so many people will be able to say that they are able to get here because of this person. This person has been the reason why I am able to achieve this or that. I want to help as many people as possible.
Our presidential system of government is based on capitalism so it is what you as a person want to achieve that is important. And whatever position God puts me I want to come out with a name written in gold. I will never be involved in these corruption that people are talking about.
In our society, a woman is considered more responsible if she is married.
We should be able to correct it because a woman may love to be married and her husband is a monster. What do you do to her?

Original_Search_Title: Senate Presidency - Mark Remains the Best -Gogwim
Headline: Nigeria: Senate Presidency - Mark Remains the Best -Gogwim
Body_Text:
Senators are divided over the leadership of the 7th Senate. What is your take on this?
The issue of the president of the Senate has to do with ranking, and I do not see how anybody would even think about dividing the Senate or the way senators think about the North Central geo-political zone having a shot at the Senate presidency again. Having given other areas the needed political positions, I think we should not lose focus to think about giving it to another geo-political zone apart from the North Central. Majority of the senators that are there have the mind that, yes they will want the present Senate President, Senator David Mark to maintain the status quo, that he should retain his seat in the next dispensation. Those of us who are not coming back, still feel that, that is the right thing to do.
What do you think are the qualities possessed by David Mark that you want him to continue as the president of the senate?
David Mark has the pedigree and experience. He has garnered these experiences over the years, he has been able to control the National Assembly. He also has the backing of the people. Apart from this, he has been able to control the senate from 2007 till date without having any problem. Senators never for once had any problem with him. He has a good leadership qualities and was able to conduct the affairs of the National Assembly without stepping on banana peel. To me, he is the longest serving senate president since the enthronement of democracy in 1999 and that is why we feel that he should be given another chance again to lead the National Assembly. I do not think anybody has the kind capacity that he has.
Furthermore, Senator Mark has been trying his best to avoid getting into controversy, that is why he is quite different from his predecessors who hardly spent two years in office.
What is your advice to the incoming and returning senators to the National Assembly?
The in coming senators lack legislative experience for now until they get into the system properly. They should make sure that the status quo remains. PDP is coming to form majority in the National Assembly and those who are coming should be able to look inward and find out what is going on in the house before they take a step that will cause any misgiving. For returning senators, they know what to do. They know the capacity of Senator Mark; they also know the ranking system in the senate. If you are not a ranking senator, there is no way you can agitate to become the senate president. Those who are coming in newly and want to become senate president are just daylight dreamers because there is no way anybody would want to upstage David Mark because he or she wants to become the senate president. Returning senators know the rules of the game. Besides Chief Mark has been able to control the National Assembly, like I said earlier he did not step on any banana peel so to say for him to even fall. In fact, he is a very smart guy indeed, so the returning senators know what to do. When they come back, they will definitely know who to vote for.
Governor Jonah Jang and President Goodluck Jonathan have won their elections for the second time, what kind of leadership would you want them to run?
God gives power to those he chooses to give. Governor Jonah Jang has been re-elected again because his policy and programme are people-oriented. Jang has done a lot under the circumstance that we find ourselves in Plateau State. We are under strict and disturbing situation. There are people out there who want to destroy the state who always call for state of emergency to be imposed on the state but unfortunately it did not work. They want to impose their own type of government on the Plateau. God has again given the opportunity to Jang to improve on what he has started. Actually, the governor has done a lot. As at the time we were in the National Assembly, we were solidly behind him. We know that ordinarily we should not leave Plateau State to suffer.
Any body who is thinking of state of emergency on the Plateau is a day dreamer because section 305 of 1999 Constitution does not give anybody the opportunity to impose state of emergency on any state at all. But rather the section provided for empowering the incumbent governor to be able to take security measures in his state. Those who were trying to fight the state and Jang are only wasting their time. They were only shedding innocent blood and would pay dearly for it. But sincerely the best we could do is to pray for Governor Jang to improve upon what he has done in the last four years. We know that where there is peace, there will be progress and development.
For President Goodluck Jonathan, he has done very well within the space of one year that he took over the mantle of leadership of this country. The best we can do is to pray for him to enable him bring the best brains who are active, those who actually want the programme of Mr. President being implemented at all level of government, people who will not come purposely to empower their pocket but the masses.

Original_Search_Title: At 76 Members, Namboole May Be Too Small for Mzee's Cabinet
Headline: Uganda: At 76 Members, Namboole May Be Too Small for Mzee's Cabinet
Body_Text:
State House logic: If the Cabinet is large, make it even larger. As always, the good MPs have done the President's bidding. The number of ministers now shoots to 76 from 70. Nice way to kick off the business of the Ninth Parliament, dear members.
Much as the Constitution provides for 21 substantive ministers and 21 other ministers (ministers of state), it also allows for those numbers to be varied only with the approval of Parliament. Clear parliamentary oversight was envisaged. Funny then that MPs have understood the word "approval" only one way - to mean routine endorsement of the presidential request.
Approval should mean that Parliament weighs the President's case and decides for or against or finds middle ground. But MPs, a vast majority of whom are ruling party members, always agree with the President hoping one more from their lot will be named honourable minister.
The MPs' selfishness (they are already discussing making their already juicy pay juicier!) coincides with the President's own expedient political calculations, which is a nice commentary on the ruling elite. Whenever critics decry the growth in public expenditure as a result of the ever-expanding Parliament and Cabinet, NRM apparatchiks shoot back saying that is necessary to maintain national stability. After 25 years in power, the NRM government is still busy balancing this and that interest - they call it accommodating the various national interests - for stability's sake. If there is no unity in diversity today, that should count as serious political failure. After all that preaching against sectarianism, including passing a law to boot, we still have to pander to ethnic, regional, gender, and religious interests.
That storyline, though, appears intended to hide the truth, which is that President Museveni is playing the politics of patronage to keep power. He is buying support by giving political elites from all over the place jobs. Only that this is at the expense of taxpayer shillings. Accommodating various national interests, the President's aides say, is expensive business that we must bear because the alternative is political chaos.
All right.
It just looks awkward this time around. A President faced with protests over rising fuel and food prices that he refuses to address say through some tax cuts or salary increments because that would amount to subsidising consumption while messing up macroeconomic stability is the same one subsidising consumption by growing the size of Cabinet. He could not hold off until when things are calmer. There appears to be little sensitivity toward public opinion. The mandate Mr Museveni got in February is not for wasting on patronage.
Effective supervision of government programmes is another reason given for increasing the number of ministers. As one commentator has pointed out, why do we then have the many, many LC5s, RDCs, MPs, chiefs, and councillors? What do they monitor? What do they supervise? UPE quality remains rubbish, health centres lack basics such as gloves forcing otherwise sane citizens to strip in protest, and expectant mothers are feeding on termites. Service delivery has virtually collapsed.
One of the new ministries coming is that of Teso. After all these years, Teso is deemed fit to have a ministry now. Maybe the area actually deserves a dedicated minister. Before one is named, it would be nice to know how successful the Presidential Commission for Teso was. In any case, it should no longer be necessary to have these "area ministries", which ministries never even solve the problems in the said regions because of incompetence, corruption, and underfunding.
On to the transformation of Uganda. President Museveni has periodically criticised his ministers for not being committed to Uganda's transformation. That is his way of saying they are not doing a good job. This is the same Museveni who has said all he asks for are MPs who can sleep all they want as long as they wake up in time to vote wisely in Parliament. Given that he picks a vast majority of his ministers from Parliament, he cannot expect better. The ministers have not disappointed.
Last year, the Red Pepper pulled off what will turn out to be one of the more enduring front pages of a Ugandan newspaper ever when it showed an array of ministers asleep as Mr Museveni delivered his State-of-the-Nation-Address. For its cheekiness and punch, that cover ranks alongside, even betters, 'Bad News - Akena Adoko is Back', a headline carried by Kakoza Mutale's newsmagazine The Economy in 1980.
As of this writing, the full Cabinet list is not yet out but I am ready to lose my nose if most of the slumbering ministers are not returned. Apparently, that is the way to transform a banana republic.
Mr President, we shall know you by your fruits.
Mr Tabaire is a media trainer and consultant with the African Centre for Media Excellence

Original_Search_Title: Mark-Ekweremadu - Not a Cat And Mouse Story
Headline: Nigeria: Mark-Ekweremadu - Not a Cat And Mouse Story
Body_Text:
The nearly universal acclamation of Senator David Mark's stable steering of the Senate in the last four years was a departure from the tale of anarchy that was characteristic of previous Senates.
Also remarkable in the last four years was the sense of unity in the top leadership between the two presiding officers, Senator Mark and his deputy, Senator Ike Ekweremadu.
Hitherto, other presiding officers of the Senate did not have the sense of unity that was shown by the pair of Mark and Ekweremadu.
The first presiding officer of the Senate was Senator Evan Enwerem who had the popular, wealthy and scheming deputy in the person of the late Senator Haruna Abubakar. While Enwerem sought to play the statesman, Abubakar played the politics and built a power base for himself. He saw out Enwerem and helped to usher in the late Senator Chuba Okadigbo as Senate President.
Okadigbo like Enwerem before him also sought to play a statesman. Abubakar who remained as deputy, however, continued with servicing his power base and was reportedly involved in the last minute political scheming that edged out Okadigbo. His decision to resign his office with the gentle agreement with the anti-Okadigbo elements to be reinstated was the final lever that pushed out Okadigbo.
Abubakar's bid to return was blocked by other schemers in the Senate, making way for the remarkable Senator Ibrahim Mantu to take over as deputy Senate President.
Mantu was a politician's politician in the Senate. He was elected deputy President Senate at the same time Anyim Pius Anyim was elected the President of the Senate.
Mantu was able to play a survival politics walking a tight rope between President Olusegun Obasanjo and his enemies in the Anyim Senate. He survived becoming the only presiding officer in either chamber to win re-election and return to his office after the 2003 elections.
He, however, had the former diplomat, Adolphus Wabara as Senate President. While Wabara played and some claimed enjoyed himself, Mantu built up his power base to become one of the most influential Senators in the chamber. His key was his access and willingness to play Obasanjo's game.
It was a game that was to devour him in future.
Mantu survived the turmoil against Wabara and saw him out of office and was around to receive Senator Ken Nnamani as his immediate boss as Senate President in April 2005.
Senator Nnamani, however, proved a tough one for Mantu. It was perhaps, Nnamani's hardened stance that stopped Mantu's reported schemes to push the third term amendment to the constitution that would have allowed President Obasanjo serve a third term in office.
For that failure, Mantu was reportedly punished with a denial of support in his bid to return to the Senate for a third term.
The absence of Mantu paved way for the emergence of Mark as Senate President. Had Mantu won his election in 2007 the challenge against Mark for the Senate presidency would have been stiffer.
Indeed there are insinuations that Mark and Mantu worked against one another in the general elections with the aim of stopping each other from returning to the Senate. The purpose was reportedly to ensure that whoever was successful in the scheme would have an easier task in the leadership election.
In June 2007 for the first time during a Senate leadership contest, two Senators entered the contest as a team: Mark for Senate President and Ekweremadu for Deputy Senate President.
In the thick of that contest which pitched Mark against George Akume for Senate President, there were reports of moves by the Akume group to winch Ekweremadu from the Mark camp with the assurance of preserving his position as a deputy under Akume. Ekweremadu was said to have flatly rejected the offer.
The Senate President leads other principal officers into the chambers.
Indeed on the eve of the 2007 leadership contest when a national leader of the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP from Edo State reportedly moved to decimate the Mark camp and hope for Mark was almost fading, Ekweremadu and Senator Iyiola Omisore were said to have rallied round using the then chairman of the PDP, Col. Ahmadu Ali (rtd.) to fence off the threat from the Akume camp that was masterminded by the leader.
One top Senate source affirmed that it was not for nothing that Omisore was appointed chairman of the powerful Senate committee on Appropriation.
"Omisore proved to be a loyal and committed follower of Mark and when many people were deserting, Omisore remained faithful," the source said in acknowledgment of the faithfulness of the outgoing chairman of the appropriation committee.
The enthronement of the Mark-Ekweremadu leadership has, however, paid off with the achievement of some legislative initiatives that were abandoned by preceding leaderships.
Foremost of the legislative attainments is the amendment of the 1999 constitution,
The far reaching amendments to the constitution articulated by the Ekweremadu led Senate Committee on Constitution Review, SCCR undoubtedly paved way for the relative successes achieved during the recent general elections.
Ekweremadu, unlike his predecessors was challenged in his efforts to review the constitution by the instigation of rebellion among members of the House of Representatives who were supposed to be partners in a joint exercise in the review of the constitution. The House members who in the past had quietly ceded leadership of the joint constitution committee to the Deputy President of the Senate, instigated a rebellion in the first outing of the committee in Minna in January 2008, when they sought for the co-chairmanship of the joint committee between Ekweremadu and the Deputy Speaker of the House of Representatives.
Senators peeved by the seeming intransigence rebuffed them leading to a walkout by the House members.
The whole episode was by some accounts a staged effort by some top presidency officials who were opposed to a review of the constitution at that time.
It took sheer guts from Ekweremadu and other Senators to sustain the effort in constitution review which was achieved after the demise of the former President, Alhaji Umar Yar'Adua.
The approach of the Senate committee in reviewing the constitution was equally a departure from the lump efforts by preceding constitution review committees. The Ekweremadu led committee's decision to adopt a piecemeal exercise undoubtedly helped in achieving the fundamental reviews concluded by the committee last year.
Among the highlights of the constitution review as achieved by the committee were electoral reforms, and the establishment of autonomy for the Independent National Electoral Commission and the National Assembly.
Unarguably, the successes of the Mark Senate were helped by the relative peace among Senators. That peace was primarily achieved by the absence of greed in the pair of Mark and Ekweremadu in the sharing of the common purse.
Most of the preceding tumults in the Senate leadership were instigated by the disproportionate sharing of money. Indeed, many of the insurrections were reportedly led by Mark, even if he may not have instigated the insurrections, he was reportedly recruited to lead the Senators in their crusades.
As one who knew where the banana peels were placed, Mark carefully avoided them.
Mark was also helped by the fact that he did not like many of his predecessors have to look back continually to watch the actions and plots of their deputies.

Original_Search_Title: Friendly Fire From Kampala
Headline: Nigeria: Friendly Fire From Kampala
Body_Text:
The commander of the assault on the Afghan presidential palace during the 1979 invasion of Afghanistan by the defunct Soviet Union left instruction with his troops that anybody emerging from the Afghan president's office should be shot. He personally led the assault into the president's office.
However, the Afghan presidential guards offered such a stiff resistance that the Soviet commander, the commandant of the KGB school of terrorism, decided to step outside to call for reinforcement. Because the Soviet invaders were dressed in Afghan combat fatigue, and the commander had left instructions that anyone emerging from the presidential palace should be shot, the Soviet soldiers opened fire on their own commander, killing him instantly.
That was friendly fire. It treats comrades and enemies alike. During the Gulf war of 1991, American casualties from friendly fire were so alarming that field commanders had to review their rules of engagement.
Nigeria had a taste of friendly fire on May 12, 2011. It came from a very friendly African country. The only advantage was that it happened in the public relations arena. If it was in the battle field we would be telling a different story now.
Public relations men all over the world have a tall order to execute. The brief is that no one wants to hear any bad news about their brand - be it corporate, human or inanimate product. The image makers would stop at nothing to ensure that no bad news smears the image of the object they are paid to project.
Ima Niboro, the special assistant to President Goodluck Jonathan on media, is one man who does his job with the zeal of a Pharisee. He does not want anything negative to be said about Jonathan.
On Thursday, May 12, Niboro ran into friendly fire in Kampala, the Ugandan capital. Fred Opolot, Uganda's equivalent of Niboro was the one who fired the shot. Apparently hoping to embarrass opposition leader Kizza Besigye over the unruly behaviour of his supporters, Opolot gave foreign journalists graphic details of how Besigye's supporters protesting the swearing-in of Uganda's sit-tight President Yoweri Kaguta Museveni, hurled stones at Jonathan's car.
Niboro found Opolot's account of the incident rather offensive and responded with a denial. He painted the picture of well-mannered Ugandans waving at, rather than stoning the car of, the Nigerian president.
I got Niboro's account of the incident at about 9.15 p.m. when I was rounding off work on my front page. Opolot's account was rather so convincing that I could not give Niboro any listening ear. I ran the Ugandan account of the incident since it was a friendly government side of the story. My decision was informed by the fact that I did not want to embarrass Niboro by presenting to the public what every reader would adjudge as a poorly crafted lie.
Instead, I added a paragraph to the Reuters story explaining that the protesters' venom was not directed at Jonathan. The Ugandans had no business hurling stones at Jonathan. In fact, unlike Zimbabweans, who persecuted Nigerian traders in their midst after we laboured so hard to secure majority rule for the country, Ugandans are said to be very friendly with Nigerians. Though some thought they attacked Jonathan's car for identifying with a cruel dictator who used the power of incumbency to perpetrate himself in office, detailed account of the incident clearly shows that Jonathan was not their target.
It turned out that my judgment was right. Niboro may not like what I did, but the truth is that if the media had ignored his account of the incident, we would have saved him and Nigeria a lot of trouble. One week after the incident, Aso Rock received yet another volley of friendly fire. This time it was from the Ugandan president himself.
Museveni apologised to Jonathan for an incident the president's image maker swore did not happen. The Ugandan president's apology has done incalculable damage to Niboro's trouble-shooting capabilities. By now, everyone can easily sift the weeds from the wheat in the Babel of voices over what happened to Jonathan's convoy on his way to the airport in Entebbe on May 12.
The Ugandan president apparently acted from detailed accounts of what happened that day. He decided on the apology after he was sure Jonathan's car was actually attacked.
At a time when the administration is battling the fall-out of the over-zealousness of some party officials in the south-east and south-south during the presidential election, the response to the friendly fire from Kampala is a public relations disaster.
That takes us to the debate on whether Jonathan should have been in Uganda in the first place. Museveni is entrenched in the club of Africa's notorious big men. In fact, there are four of them left in the continent.
The first is Muammar Gaddafi, the Libyan strongman who is slaughtering his kinsmen just to remain in power. By September this year, Gaddafi would have ruled Libya for 42 years. The next is Robert Mugabe. The president of Zimbabwe has been in power for 31 years. At the age of 87 he is still clinging to it. His calamitous attempt at strengthening the weak by weakening the strong has brought the economy of what used to be Africa's food basket to its knees. Mugabe is not worried.
Jose Eduardo dos Santos is another member of the notorious club of Africa's strongmen. The president of Angola has been in office for 31 years. He has no plans to cede power to anyone. The fourth in that hierarchy is Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo. Sometime in 1979, Mbasogo slit the throat of his uncle, Fransisco Macias Nguema Biyogo, and seized power in Equatorial Guinea - Africa's banana republic derisively tagged 'Panya' by some Nigerians. With Equatorial Guinea now an oil producing country, Mbasogo can only be expected to dig in. He has no plans to quit.
Many see Jonathan's presence at the inauguration of a man who manipulated his country's constitution for tenure elongation as a wrong message from the leader of the world's most populous black nation. The situation is even worsened by the fact that Jonathan is battling to shirk the shackles of fraudulent elections he inherited from his predecessors.
I personally hold the same view. However, in a world that even the rich democracies are courting dictators in the guise of oiling business relationships, Jonathan may have a strong point in his decision to attend Museveni's inauguration.
Last year, the British government released Abdelbaset al-Megrahi, a terrorist convicted by the International Criminal Court for blowing up Pan Am Flight 103 in December 1988, killing 270 innocent people. The British lied to the world that al-Megrahi had just three months to live. Today, the Libyan terrorist is even healthier than the prime minister of the UK. It has now been established that the decision was taken in the business interest of Britain.
Nigeria has a very warm business relationship with Uganda. Two Nigerian banks, UBA and BankPHB, have firm roots in Uganda. A third, Access Bank, has been licensed to commence operations. Besides, Global Trust Bank has commenced operations in the country with Nigerian equity holdings of 80 per cent. Arik Air has been authorised to operate direct flights to Entebe.
Jonathan's visit might have been informed by such business considerations. It is rather unfortunate that the public relations fall-out of the visit was grossly mismanaged. That is a sad reminder that friendly fire could be as dangerous as one from an enemy.

Original_Search_Title: Senate Presidency, Preserve of Ranking Senators - Gogwim
Headline: Nigeria: Senate Presidency, Preserve of Ranking Senators - Gogwim
Body_Text:
Jos — The outgoing senator representing Plateau central senatorial district in the National Assembly, senator Satty Davies Gogwim has disclosed that the position of the Senate president normally comes from ranking senators, even they were more experienced than the new comers. Senator Gogwim who spoke to LEADERSHIP in Jos, the plateau state capital on the leadership of the 7th National Assembly said the issue of the president of the Senate has to do with ranking, noting that he do not see how anybody who would even think about dividing the senate or the way senators think about the north central geo-political zone having a shot at the senate presidency again.
According to him, having given other areas the needed political positions, people should not lose focus to think about giving it out to another geo-political zone apart from the north central and stressed that majority of the senators that were there have the mind to let the present senate president, Senator David Mark to maintain the status quo.
He stressed that those of them who were not coming back to the senate still feel that, that is the right thing to do as the present president of the senate David Mark has the pedigree and experience garnered over the years as he was able to control the national assembly from 2007 till date without stepping on any banana peel.
According to him, David Mark was the longest serving senate president since the enthronement of Democracy in 1999 and that is why we feel that he should be given another chance to lead senators.
Senator Gogwim furthermore said the senate president has been trying his best to avoid getting into controversy, adding that, that is why he is quite different from his predecessors who hardly spend two years in office.
He also noted that the incoming senators lack legislative experience for now until they get into the system properly while advising them to make sure that the status quo still remains, adding that the PDP is coming to form the majority in the national assembly and those who are coming should be able to look inward and find out what is going on in the house before they take a step that will cause any misgivings.
The outgoing senator also said that the returning senators know what to do and the capacity of David Mark, while pointing out that the returning senators know the ranking system in the senate and would not jeopardize it.

Original_Search_Title: Judiciary As Nation's Last Hope
Headline: Nigeria: Judiciary As Nation's Last Hope
Body_Text:
"Never in the field of human conflict, was so much owed, by so many, to so few."
--Winston Churchill - 20 August 1940
In the summer of 1940, Britain faced its greatest challenge since the aborted invasion of the island by the French under Napoleon in 1805. Adolf Hitler, buoyed by the individual brilliance of Generals Heinze Guderian, Erwin Rommel and Walter Model - to name only a few - had completely re-invented warfare.
The devastating 'blitzkrieg' or lightning war they conceived, was spearheaded by tanks, and mechanized infantry. It also involved mobility and close air support from the German air force the Luftwaffe; all in sharp contrast to the static trench warfare of the First World War. In a matter of months, almost the entire Western Europe came under the suffocating weight of the Nazi Jack boot.
The British Expeditionary force dispatched to support France was subjected to a humilating defeat and forced to retreat back home, through Dunkirk. Occupied France was left to rue the monumental folly that was the Maginot line - a series of static fortifications across its borders inspired by the conventional principles of the First World War.
The task of containing the Nazi juggernaut fell on Britain which Hitler planned to invade next. But first, the British Royal Airforce (RAF) had to be dealth with to pave way for the ground invasion. Hermann Goering - the bombastic Reich Marshall of the German Luftwaffe, assured Hitler the task would be achieved in a matter of weeks.
The decisive resistance, and ultimate victory, of the officers and men of the RAF, against all odds, is now recalled in history as the "Battle of Britain". Its outcome was crucial it effectively changed the course of world history. The valiance of a single British institution - the RAF - deservedly earned Churchill's timeless commendation quoted above, and also set the theme for the remainder of this discourse.
After the American intelligence community predicted that our nation risked balkanization by 2015, several local commentators reacted with typical hypocrisy and short-sightedness. They failed to subject the American prognosis to balanced and intelligent examination, and settled for jaundiced postulations that punched even greater holes in our battered ship of state, wobbling dangerously in ocean of uncertainty. Among other things, they claimed Nigeria could not possibly disintegrate because we are not yet a banana republic.
They conveniently forgot that the old Yugoslavia and Czechoslovakia were not banana republics in the strict sense of the word either. If anything, the conduct of the last general elections, coupled with the violence and dishonest reactions it generated clearly suggests that the disintegration of the nation may occur even sooner than the Americans actually predicted.
When added to the criminal incompetence and uneven handling of some fractious ethno-religious conflicts by the current administration, in recent times undoubtedly for political gain; then only a fool will deny that the nation is being edged towards the precipice with a casual deliberateness that is both frightening and amusing.
Quite clearly, the violent reactions in the north with its historical antecedents in other parts of the country the authorities deliberately have chose to disemphasis in pursuit of scapegoats, is pregnant with dire implications; as much as the lack of sympathy for the anger of the people frustrated in the quest to elect leaders of their choice by constitutional means.
The deafening silence in the South-South and the South-East on the inconsistencies in voter turn-out and 'results' which were shamelessly advertised as evidence of electoral progress and sophistry defies belief; same as the under-hand arrangement in the South-West which provided those who stood to gain most from the deceit, an opportunity to lend credibility to what was, ultimately, a nationwide farce.
But we must not be deceived by the relative calm of the present. A steaming volcano, begging for a violent outlet, could rupture the current facade of normality. The deceptive indifference of the majority, so crudely disenfranchised by the wholesome rigging, and long embittered by social and economic exclusion, may actually be the prelude before a major storm. We have historical assurances of that.
Throughout history, the remote and immediate causes of crisis often combined to force once formidable empires into destructive ruin. In our peculiar case, the causal factors include endemic corruption, rampaging poverty, and social injustice; not to mention persistent electoral fraud, and a weak and corrupt judiciary.
I once wrote on these pages that the 'Nigerian factor' does not feed on order or organizational correctness. We seem to thrive on anarchy, and confusion, where camels could be literally fed through the eyes of the needle. For the nation to escape a tragic fate, the judiciary must be immune from the ravages of the Nigerian factor. It must avoid the ambush of those who seek to subject the appeal processes to realm of ethnic or religious bigotry. The individual identities of the litigants should matter less than the merits of each and every case. There should be no room for sentiments.
Now that electoral contests have shifted from INEC to the various tribunals, the judiciary must not dash the hopes of Nigerians who rightly believe the prospects of forensic evidencing have made their job much easier. The other advantage may be the careless and over-confident behaviour of the PDP in particular, caused by years of governance with arrogance and impunity.
In Abia state for instance, the PDP candidate T. A. Orji apparently was so overcome by the same air of impunity the issue of his membership of the party is now a subject of serious litigation. Previously elected on the platform of the PPA, he flirted briefly with APGA, before finally eloping with the PDP which contentiously removed the authentic state EXCO from office to smoothen the path for him.
Orji, obviously, was so intoxicated by the support he allegedly received from the presidency that he literally became a member of the Abia PDP coming through the roof, instead of being ushered in through the main door. In spite of that he was 'elected' governor. But it is not possible to become a General in the army without first going through the formal processes of conscription. Orji thought he had overcome all obstacles when he filed and obtained a collusive judgment within 48 hours that enabled him to run.
Those opposed to his anointment and subsequent imposition contend he is not a valid card-carrying member of the party because he brazenly failed to register in his ward! The same cases of abuse of due processes, and week internal democracy, abound even in the CPC, ACN and the ANPP. It was not just the Electoral Act that was serially assaulted, but the nation's constitution, which was subjected to the whimsical siege of corrupt politicians.
Consider also, the astonishing 'results' from the South-South and the Southeast. In Delta state, a village with only 5,000 registered voters produced over 50,000 votes! In the north, allegations of under-aged voting must be fully interrogated to dispel all doubts. If we are able to overcome the brazenness of our leaders, or mitigate the naked arrogance of those in the corridors of power, Nigeria will be a better place. It doesn't matter how long it takes.
Today, just as with the British RAF in the summer of 1940, the fate of our nation depends on the conduct of a single institution - the judiciary. Destiny has placed an enormous burden and responsibiltty at its doorsteps at this epoch in our history. We have little margin for error. How the judiciary responds to the election petitions nationwide, could prove or disprove the American and other previously held hypotheses on the nation's longevity.

Original_Search_Title: Politics of Our-Turn-to-Eat
Headline: Nigeria: Politics of Our-Turn-to-Eat
Body_Text:
On Thursday morning, as I left home for the office, I offered a ride to a young lady who had waved frantically as though Osama bin Laden's ghost was heading in her direction.
The manner she rushed into the car and slammed the door closed with surplus thanksgiving made me ask: "I hope all is well, or are you late for an appointment?" Obviously she was almost late for an appointment in the city. But the traffic was light. She introduced herself as a medical doctor. Lucky her; I just treated malaria a few days back. Midway, I pulled up to answer a call. I can't remember exactly what I discussed with the caller that prompted her to start a topic on political issues. As soon as I ended the call, she asked, "So is it true that David Mark is poised for re-election as Senate President?" I quickly pulled my thinking cap into place. Within a split second, I wondered within: why is she asking me this question; who is she; why should the Senate Presidency be of interest to a young, female medical doctor whose interest should be her patients? I stole a side-way glance at her; and I could see she was innocuously waiting for the answer. I replied without betraying my thoughts: "How did you know that?"
Her response was far more pedestrian than I expected. She asked, "Why should he be re-elected? He has been there for four years, he should give another person a chance to also go and eat. It should not be him alone. Why should one person be eating alone for eight years? And you can be sure he would not be impeached. He would not even resign. I don't like this kind of situation" The key word in her argument was that Mark should give somebody else a chance to also "eat." Her statement reminded me of a book by Michela Wrong. It is titled: It's Our Turn To Eat: The Story of A Kenyan Whistle Blower. Unarguably, that is what public office or public service in Nigeria has been reduced to. Appointment to serve is seen as an opportunity to amass illicit wealth.
Even where there is no evidence to prove that an occupant of that office has stolen, the general perception is that there is no way he would not still because that is more of the rule than exception. I encountered a chief executive officer of a federal government agency in 2002; and he told me an interesting story about making money from government service. He was about enlisting in politics in 2003; and was packing his personal belongings from his office. I casually reminded him that he would soon return to the same office to face a probe panel investigation his tenure. He asked me why. I replied that petitions would soon be flying against him. His response was: "look Sam, only a stupid person steals public funds while serving in this kind of office. There are a million ways to make clean, legitimate money without stealing. If anybody makes any effort to probe my tenure, it would be a mere waste of time. Nothing would be found; but that does not mean I did not make money."
He has since left the agency. No one has probed his activities. I asked him at another point how he made his money without stealing, he simply laughed before delivering a brief lecture on it. I thought about all this as I listened to the young medical doctor in my car. She was not bitter about Mark as a person. Her grievance was that if someone like Mark was not in the race, it would have been easier for someone from her area to contest, win the seat and occupy the office. It was all about self-interest and not necessarily public service. She told me that from what she had read in the papers, it was clear that Mark had conducted the affairs of the Senate pretty well and should therefore be re-elected. "But I believe that someone else can also do it, even better," she added. I simply smiled. I asked her what she preferred most: a stable Senate devoid of scandals or consistent change in leadership; or the swearing-in of five different Senate Presidents in eight years because of the legendary banana peels. She laughed and asked, "So you are supporting Mark?" I replied, "I support good, stable, scandal-free leadership whether at the Senate or anywhere."
Just before I dropped her off in town, I pondered over her comments and the reality. I told her in no uncertain terms that the final decision on who becomes the Senate President lies with the senators themselves. When all the senators and senators-elect are seated in the hallowed chamber, anybody could be nominated and possibly voted to the high office. I told her about the seniority arrangement which is globally accepted as an unquestionable norm. Sounding quite brilliant, she agreed that it would be ridiculous for a freshman senator to simply walk in and lay claim to the seat. She told me that most often, the problem we have in leadership has not always been due to lack of people with integrity to lead; but power is usually given to the highest bidder whose sole aim is selfish interest. She agreed that out-going state governors who have maneuvered themselves to the Senate must not be given any chance to rule the Senate the way they ruled their states. As she closed the door to leave, I nodded in appreciation of her political intellect and depth of thought.

Original_Search_Title: On Finding Myself
Headline: Nigeria: On Finding Myself
Body_Text:
...The first time I knew I wanted to be a writer, I was in primary school.
My classmate was the daughter of Flora Nwapa, the first African woman to be published in the United Kingdom.
Flora Nwapa published Efuru in 1962, four years after Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart. I did not know all of this then at that age but I was fascinated that she was a writer because whatever else that occurred to me that African women could be, writing was not one of them. We had had career days right from kindergarten where we were exposed to all the available career options that awaited us.
Writing was never one of those career options. And of all the books - all the children's books I had at home, none were written by an African woman. And there was probably none by an African man for that matter. If I am honest, I'd say that they were all books by Enid Blyton - the Magic Faraway Tree, the Enchanted Forest and so on. They were all books with white children who sat on wishing chairs and went to fairs and grandmothers with jolly faces baking cakes. It made me yearn for their lives.
I enjoyed reading these stories, but what it did to me was that it also made me feel rootless because in my imagination, I joined these children in adventures I could only approximate, never having lived in any way like them. This was not such a big problem until I began to write. Children begin by imitating their parents and so I imitated Blyton. So when I wrote, what little childish poetry and stories I wrote then, I had people "ice skating on a winter day." I had no desire to meet the people I wrote about because I feared that we would have nothing to talk about and that they would reveal me for the farce I was.
So when Flora bustled into our class one day with children's books she had written and published and distributed it to us to keep us occupied while she had a chat with our teacher - it was magical. The children in the book had adventures I could identify with. They had kinky hair and brown skin. Their grandmothers did not bake but told them stories.
The imaginary landscape I shared with them was more familiar, more intimate and therefore much more profound. She displaced Enid Blyton making her the foreign aunt I visited who I never wanted to imitate again. I fell in love with Flora Nwapa and wanted to be everything that she embodied. I also felt free for the first time. Free to write about children like me, who ate bananas and groundnut and skipped rope. She opened my eyes to those possibilities. For the first time, I was not afraid to meet my characters.
As I got older, I read her for instruction. I read her for herself but I also read her as an antithesis to the male Achebe. I was constantly comparing the two: their female characters, their storylines and their plots. I wanted to write like she did, creating strong women characters in village settings. My first stories as a young teenager had characters who lived in the sort of villages Nwapa placed her characters in. Once more, never having lived in the village, and as I was at an age when children still mimicked their mothers, my imagination was limited by my restricted knowledge of village life. Writing became tedious but I had no idea why.
At some point, I discovered Buchi Emecheta and fell in love again. Her characters were strong, independent women but more importantly, they also lived lives I could identify even more with. Their dilemmas were on the whole more modern. Being as fickle as any other writer I know, I allowed Emecheta to displace Nwapa and relegate Nwapa to the role of the nice step mother in the village who told tales by midnight.
I stayed with Emecheta for a very long time, finding relevance in her works for me as a would-be writer. When I read Emecheta again, years later, as a mother struggling to write in Europe, my writing morphed. It brought me to a home I was comfortable in. Emecheta became my literary mother because she also wrote across cultures. She lived in the UK and wrote about Nigerian immigrants struggling with retaining their Nigerian identity in a foreign land. She wrote about migration and the loneliness that goes hand in hand with migration in way that resonated with me- her daughter of the wind.
I read her and she gave voice to not just the way I felt, but also the stories I wanted to tell. It was only in being able to tell those stories that I could begin to make sense of my physical sense of belonging. The first novel I wrote, De Feniks, true to form was therapeutic. I took everything I felt for the Nigeria of my past and the Belgium of my present: anger, joy, helplessness and transferred them into my main character. This book gave me a sense of belonging. It freed me to tell other stories. It also helped me to root myself as a writer, the identity I valued above all else and the identity I had a conscious choice in choosing unlike my Nigerian identity (accident of birth) and Belgian (accident of chemistry).
By the time I started my second novel and several short stories later, I had found my voice and flown the nest. Along the way, I have met literary uncles and aunties, with whom I visit, from Leo Pleysier to Wale Okediran, and more relevantly, literary siblings with all the consequences of siblinghood. I borrow what I need from them like I would borrow my biological siblings' clothes and, of course, there is the issue of sibling rivalry, admiration and envy rolled into one. My literary siblings are from everywhere. But my voice is mine; I have found myself.
Chika Unigwe holds a PhD from the University of Leiden in the Netherlands. Her latest novel, On Black Sisters' Street was published in the UK by Jonathan Cape and will be published by Random House NY in 2011. She lives in Belgium

Original_Search_Title: Life in And Beyond the Studio
Headline: East Africa: Life in And Beyond the Studio
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Each year, Kenya's Department of Culture draws its collective breath, girds its governmental loins and goes forth in search of art.Thousands of paintings and sculptures are solicited at the district level throughout the country. Exhibitions are held and the best are selected for provincial exhibitions, from which the finest of the fine are chosen to go forward to Nairobi for the annual National Visual Arts Exhibition.
Selection is by panels of experts, defined in this case as high school and training college art teachers, who work to "guidelines" issued after "workshops." Pictures and sculptures are judged without reference to the artist's name (thus avoiding such politically dangerous areas as gender and ethnicity). But the selectors do take into account such matters as adherence to the theme -- this year it was "Life In and Beyond", whatever that means.
Crucially, I am told, the selectors put creative interpretation above such tricky areas as actual artistic ability.
The final cut of around 100 pictures is then taken to a place of lawful execution -- sorry, I mean a central exhibition site, this year being the Nairobi Gallery, which is the old PC's Office next to Nyayo House -- where they are hung askew on the wall, propped up on the floor against scrawled reference numbers (had they run out of nails?), or stood on unpainted and ugly wooden stands.
The problem is that old Civil Service bugbear, lack of funds. By the time the national exhibition is finally held in May, one month before the end of the financial year, the Department of Culture's budget is virtually all spent.
I understand that this year around Ksh200,000 ($2,360) was left for the show when even the organisers thought at least Ksh500,000 ($5,880) was needed. For an exhibition supposed to show off the best of the country's emerging talent, I would have thought at least Ksh2 million ($23,530) was nearer the mark, but there again I don't know what other demands stretched the budget.
As it is, some of the paintings are visibly bad, most are unexceptional to the point of merciful oblivion, although each year -- and this is the seventh -- a few gems somehow struggle through to make the wall. So here are a few from this year's show.
Split horizontally
The canvas on which James Nyakundi painted a gazelle calf suckling its mother was split horizontally into three sections, which were then wired back together; a device he introduced, he told me, because no-one else was doing it and it made his paintings unique.
Well, yes, but does it help an understanding of the picture and add value to its presentation? It is the quality of the painting that should create the hallmark he seeks.
Nyakundi actually paints quite well, with a confidence that suggests he really does not need to indulge himself -- and irritate viewers -- with this sort of frivolous distraction.
Another confident painter is Hassan Ahman, who showed several works, the finest of which, I think, was a view of Machon Market, which showed close observation and a love of architectural detail.
And there was detail galore in Evans Maina Ngure's coloured drawing Maasai Woman, her face like tree bark, lined with the wisdom of age, and her traditional beaded collars and earrings lovingly rendered. Yet for a subject so rich in colour, Ngure's palette was strangely weak. Nonetheless it was an honest piece of work and it shone like a beacon of technical excellence in this show.
A fine arts student at Kenyatta University, Nairobi, Ngure took precisely 13 hours and 11 minutes to complete the piece -- precise drawing, precisely timed. I know this because he wrote the timing on the paper beneath his signature (something often done in art schools but rarely seen outside them.) For him 13 hours and 11 minutes is quick. He told me he normally takes at least 24 hours to complete a picture.
How long it takes the soapstone sculptor Naftal Mageta Momanyi to complete his pieces, I shudder to think. There were around 10 of them on show and whether painstakingly literal (a woman grinding corn, for example) or as abstract as some of those writhing Makonde carvings seen in a curio shop near you, they were very well crafted and beautifully finished, even allowing for the soft compliance of his material.
Taking pride of place in the centre of the gallery beneath the cupola was Momanyi's Education for All; three Graces extolling the merits of knowledge represented by a mortar board, which provided the focal point atop the figures. It is a considerable piece of work, a judgement with which the artist presumably agrees... having stuck a price tag on it of Ksh850,000 ($10,000).
Fantasy Land by Godfrey Otieno Onyango also caught my eye... a confection of pink towers like gigantic termite mounds, beneath which people promenaded on aerial walkways where an artist -- Onyango himself, at a guess -- recorded the scene. The picture was somewhat eccentrically framed in a banana leaf binding.
It was so unique a vision that I think we shall see a lot more of Onyango and his private world. Certainly I hope so. For me, this was the find of the show -- and if the Department of Culture finds only one Onyango a year for us, then it is doing something useful, in spite of itself.
Frank Whalley runs Lenga Juu, a fine arts and media consultancy based in Nairobi

Original_Search_Title: A Treat for All at the Great Wall
Headline: Zimbabwe: A Treat for All at the Great Wall
Body_Text:
I must admit that when trying to judge a restaurant like the Great Wall, which specialises in Chinese food, I wished that I had actually visited China. I have always been fascinated by China and have a long cherished ambition to actually go there one day.
I tend to compare Chinese restaurants with the Westernised ones that I used to go to in England as a teenager, when they used to be all the rage for office and birthday parties. Naturally, they were full of Chinese themed knick knacks, billowy lanterns and red velveteen walls. They also used to help the Westerner by offering meals for groups of four or six, so that you would be able to order easily. However, although The Great Wall lacks a cohesive theme on the decorating front, I suspect that it might be closer to actual eating houses in China than my earlier experiences.
Also the fact that the place was completely packed speaks for itself. It is obviously very popular with big family groups and I wondered if this restaurant would not be better off in the Family Restaurant category. Throughout the evening many people came for takeaways, so the restaurant seems to run on a huge scale.
The menu offered a bewildering choice with some beautifully poetic dishes, such as shark fin soup, fried chicken with wood ears, angel hair noodles and drunken duck. However, my husband had been seduced by some of these very exotic choices before and he was determined to keep us on the straight and narrow. He rather pragmatically depended mostly on the advice of the waiter and past experience of the restaurant.
However, after reading Mao's Last Dancer I was on a quest to try the dumplings and I was delighted to see that they did have pork filled dumplings similar to the ones mentioned in the book. I was not alone in this mission, apparently after reading the book, one Canadian reader travelled all the way to the Qingdao district and pretended to be one of the author's friends in order to convince the author's mother to let him try her famous dumplings.
My husband tried to steer me away from them and grumpily scoffed that I had let my imagination run away with me but it was no good -- I had to try them! They were like delicious little purses, certainly the ultimate comfort food and practically a meal in themselves. My teenage son wolfed down as many as he could
Next time I think I would not bother with the starters, as we had to wait rather a long time for them, it seemed to make more sense just to order the main meal like everyone else. However, I had wanted to try the spring rolls. Their spring rolls were surprisingly good, light and not too oily, although I still would have loved to have some bean sprouts in them.
The most successful dishes we ordered were the sizzling beef with garlic and the sichuan chicken, and the meat in both dishes was tender and succulent. The teenagers enjoyed the crispy duck which they smothered with the sauce, but we found the taste a little bland.
The waiter suggested a plate of vegetables which my husband particularly enjoyed, in that the vegetables were perfectly cooked, crispy and a good combination of carrots, beans, broccoli and cauliflower. The spicy rice was a perfect combination to balance the meat dishes.
My husband was impressed that they offered most imported beer such as Hansas at the same price as the local beers. There is also filtered water available, which was very welcome. We were replete at the end of this feast but the teenagers were still ready to soldier on, so we just tried a mouthful of their desserts.
The poor waiter was looking a bit exhausted at this stage and said that the pan grilled bananas would just take too long, as my husband had been champing at the bit whenever he had to wait for anything.
The waiter was clearly overstretched that night because of the sheer number of patrons! The crunchy bow ties and ice-cream were a great hit with my daughter and my son enjoyed the lychees and ice-cream, which were a refreshing finish to the meal.
We noticed the swings and slides outside were very popular with the young ones. The Great Wall is an ideal place to take the whole family for a treat, and save for one major area of weakness, this restaurant would have pipped a 4 Plate rating; the toilets were awful! Restaurants neglect these at their peril, for little can spoil a great eating experience more, than unpleasant bathroom facilities.

Original_Search_Title: Mama Mwakilambo Worked On 'Sekiurete'
Headline: Tanzania: Mama Mwakilambo Worked On 'Sekiurete'
Body_Text:
The reason why Mama Mwakilambo deems it fit to call on my Chinese junk in the devil hours of the night, even when clamped in the arms of my one and only Bisho Ntongo is because I am a proud owner of a very thirsty Kalahari of throat and therefore subject to blackmail.
My throat must always be obeyed, so long as my sickly wallet is not singing its death dirge on its way to the financial ICU. I am convinced that one of the ways bar owners have learnt fleece me is by extending credit facilities, allowing me to spend my money before I have earned it!
Mama Mwakilambo is a bulky woman known for her savvy in Karate and Judo to those who refuse to pay for drinks. It puzzles many, including Bisho Ntongo why she has such respect for me to a paint of extending credit to me. I know that it has no respect for my innate self but what she respects is my wallet.
Last weekend, I woke up in the worst mood ever. What started as a prank directed to Bisho Ntongo turned into a serious quarrel. I had made an unfortunate joke that her legs reminded me of banana stumps in Katerero. She was not amused by all that she told me on my face that what she has always thought of me.
She clearly told me that she does not know why she married a brainless pauper in the first place when she had the option of marrying Magerantubus Kaijage or Rubiunus Rwezaura.
This must have been grass that breaks the camel back because my ego must have been terribly wounded.
I shot out of the house like a rocket undecided of where I wanted to be. I had three options - to visit Tatu the barmaid, to sip one at Mzee Shirima's joint or end up at Mama Mwakilambo's.
That is how I ended up at Mama Mwakilambo's joint. Something about this grand lady is that she is always happy to see me no matter how sickly my wallet is. Mama Mwakilambo is the motherly type despite of her obvious toughness.
As I poured my heart to her, she was genuinely concerned and offered me a couple of beers perhaps out of sympathy even though I had no money on me. I had been there for a couple of hours when some toughies appeared. They appeared to have been drinking elsewhere and obviously upset the peace that was.
One of them, smacked my face with a fist so hard that I saw Mama Mwakilambo visibly wincing.
The lout deemed that since he was from National Security as he bragged (whatever that is), he was right to discipline me for smoking in public.As he was trying to beat to pulp my terrified body, Mama Mwakilambo, like lightning emerged from the shadows and whacked the two guys like I have never seen before.
Each left with bloodied and bruised faces and missing teeth. I can't tell you how grateful I am to this grand lady. As soon as my wallet wakes up from financial ICU, I will go to pay Mama Mwakilambo.

Original_Search_Title: Common Problems With Condoms
Headline: South Africa: Common Problems With Condoms
Body_Text:
Male condoms are a widely used HIV preventative measure in South Africa. However, even though male condoms may seem relatively easy to use, they often present problems such as breaking and slipping off.
Condom manufacturers never mention problems associated with using male condoms in their information leaflets. They simply tell you to unroll it onto the erect penis and to pinch the teat at the end of the condom as you put it on.
A group of sexually active youth from Johannesburg shared their experiences about using male condoms.
"I've had one or two moments whereby you would find that, depending on the type of condoms that you buy, you get a condom burst. It's one of those unfortunate situations" says 31-year old Moses Mabala.
Olwethu Mkoko has also had an unfortunate incident. "In a few occasions where the condom breaks the best thing is to use the morning-after pill" she says.
"You find the condom breaking if it's of lower quality and running in the morning to get the morning-after pill. Sometimes, it would rub on your skin and you get an allergic reaction, and in that case you need to have some lubricant jelly around", adds Ruth Mokoena.
But even though these young people have experienced difficulties using the male condom, they believe it's the only protection they have from any potential risk.
"Well, I've got one partner and the both of us understand the importance of safe sex. And it's something that needs to be discussed and to be open about to one another as it results to in a lot of trust and respect for one another" says Moses Mabala.
Olwethu Mkoko reasons further. Hundred percent of the time I do use protection to prevent Aids and for pregnancy. And, currently, I don't have a stable boyfriend, so I don't have much choice but to use protection to be safe. It's my health, it's my life so, safe sex or no sex" she says.
Mkoko also added that she would prefer condoms that are quick to put on as she has noticed that some guys take a lot of time to put on a condom.
"Some guys take forever to open a condom and to put it on. Then you lose interest by the time they've put it on and you no longer in the mood" she says.
Asked whether they would stop using the male condom if they had a choice, interestingly, these young people insist that the male condom remains a better option than the female condom.
"I prefer the male condoms under the understanding that female condoms, from what I've read, are quite complicated. I'll be more comfortable using a male condom than a female condom. It's a Ppersonal choice!" says Moses Mabala.
"I found the female condoms very uncomfortable and very difficult to place properly. It used to get all messy, so I prefer male condoms because it's simple and easy to use" adds Ruth Mokoena.
Dr. Matsie Lebina from Zuzimpilo Clinic in Johannesburg weighed in on the subject.
"As a medical doctor, the commonest reason that people come to us is that the condom broke. But, we often wonder if the condom actually broke or it slipped off. And in that case they will be worried about their risk of acquiring HIV and they'll be coming for what we call post-exposure prophylaxis, which we provide here at Zuzimpilo Clinic" she says.
Dr. Lebina further explained what a post-exposure prophylaxis is.
"The post-exposure prophylaxis is basically HIV treatment or antiretroviral medication that needs to be taken for a month and it needs to be taken within 72 hours after exposure. So, if you had it last night and you're not sure about the HIV status of that person or you forgot to use a condom or the condom broke, then come and get the treatment that you need to take to prevent you from getting HIV".
She added that this raises questions as to whether people, more particularly men, know how to use condoms.
"People talk a lot about breaking of condoms. But one of the ways to prevent condom breakage is that people must lubricate on their private part before putting the condom. And, then, to also hold on to the condom when you withdraw, so that it does not slip off or fall off. If you know how to put it on properly, it shouldn't break. Get a banana and practise how to roll it down, so that when the moment comes you are the expert", concludes Dr. Lebina.

Original_Search_Title: Bringing a Traditional Touch Back to Clothes
Headline: Tanzania: Bringing a Traditional Touch Back to Clothes
Body_Text:
HILDA Tarabalunga Bandio gave birth to six children: Four boys and two girls. Sad to say one of each gender has passed away. This leaves her today with three adult sons and one daughter, between the ages of 40 and 32 years.
With her child rearing years behind her, she now has more time to concentrate on her traditional clothes line. It helps her to know that her husband, a former civil servant, who retired some years ago, is still around and willing to continue lending a helping hand wherever, whenever required.
He has been especially useful, she says, when it comes to writing proposals. This means she's well set to tackle the future with confidence.
Although both Mr and Mrs Bandio originated from Bukoba District in Kagera Region, the couple had met in Mwanza Town, while he was going through some training, as an officer in the fisheries section of the Ministry of Natural Resources, there.
At that time he had not finished his studies at Makerere University in Uganda so they made the best of their time together while he was in the country.
As part of his studies and training he had also to travel to the United States of America and Holland, which gave her, doubts that this man would still be interested in her when he finishes. So it should be easily understood why she was pleasantly surprised that before he took-up his first post, after completing his studies, they got married and moved to the Isamilo area in Mwanza Region.
Todate the couple have 42 years of marriage and Mrs Bandio says they still love each other very much. From that time up until eight years ago her main base was in Mwanza. She made frequent trips to Dar es Salaam, as it was required.
Then in 2003, things took an about turn and they rented their home in Mwanza and took up permanent residence in Temeke District here in the commercial capital Dar es Salaam. This then became their new main base and Mwanza remained a place she went now and then on business trips or just visiting friends.
To implant her base even firmer here, as from last year she has moved to Mbagala in the outskirts of the city in a house they bought. "It is this talent of mine that has brought me to Dar es Salaam," Mrs Bandio told the 'Daily News on Saturday' in an interview during the week while on a visit to her workshop in the Kinondoni section of the city.
"It is not wise for me to depend on people in the regions to have a love for such clothing. For example, there is no way you will find a person in Mwanza being prepared to part with 800,000/- for a piece of clothing. They do not know the value of such things or the time that has to be spent to produce a gown like this one here.
Now such clothing cannot be made just like that. If all the material is at hand, it cannot take less than six to seven days to make one," she explained as she showed me a readymade dress that goes for 800,000/-.
But please don't make the mistake of thinking that this lady only came into the clothing line since her coming to Dar es Salaam; for that would be most misleading. Actually, it is long before getting married that she has been making ladies and children clothes, although in the regular line of styles and materials.
It is in the area of women accessories that she has been using traditional materials, from her early days. These include things like bags, purses, mats, baskets and decorations on clothes. The seamstress maintains that this has all been towards helping women be more independent. A simple way in which this works is in a case when a section of a mother's clothing has started to show signs of wear and tear.
She would show them how they can cut this garment down themselves and make something for one of their children instead of throwing it away. Those are the kind of things she has been involved in Bukoba and Mwanza for over four decades. Mrs Bandio herself was taught by a German lady called Godron Lassen, who she stayed with between 1965 and 1968.
They were part of a group within the Lutheran Church in Bokoba. From there she went to stay with another German Madame called Analise Yarn who had since passed away. It was there she stayed until 1969 when she got married. Directly before this she had taken some training as an evangelist in Moshi District in Kilimanjaro Region.
This third of five children: Four girls and a boy, who had her formal schooling in Kagera Region and went to Mwanza after getting married, finished middle school in Bukoba and after Standard Eight. She then went to study theology for one year in Mwika, Moshi Rural District in Kilimanjaro Region. She had chosen this line after being advised by her father, who comes from a German and Bokoba-Tanzania union.
"Since finishing school, all through my working life, I have never worked for anyone but have been self-employed," she said with much pride. The reasoning behind how she got into making clothing with natural materials seem quiet logical, after hearing her explanation. From some time before she started this line of garments, she had noticed that there was not anyone making such things. Now when a former local singer asked her to make her clothes of this kind, it kindled an idea, which started churning something in her head.
It was only after taking on this challenge that she saw that there was much meaning from doing it. In fact, she has been shown much respect inside and outside of the country, for this line of work. Now in her cosy Kinondoni workshop she has two men, who are basically technicians and eight ladies, who sew and decorate the clothes, which are made right there.
Understandably, she is very proud of being the one who designs all the clothes they make and acknowledges that the ability comes from above.
"There are many reasons why I say this is a talent given by God. Just think of me being able to sit here and think of putting this here and that there and then someone else would come along and find the end product appealing.
