This skill can only come from God," she admits, while pointing to clothing made in regular tree bark, papyrus, bird feathers, coconut shells and banana tree barks. Her customers are mainly ladies, who require clothing of her kind on their graduations, send-offs and kitchen parties.
There is also a steady demand for bridal gowns for brides to be and even bridegrooms. Currently, she is striving to bring her business outside the country. This way she would be bringing more of the spotlight on local art and culture.
Talking of spotlights, this coming Friday 27, Mrs Bandio's clothing will be on full display at the Ubungo Plaza which is not too far from the up-country bus stand. In this fashion show, which is billed as, "Where young men and women will fascinate the people with original Tanzanian wear made with natural materials," she will be showing the work of the group, called the Tanzania Women Progressive Trust Fund (MMWATA).
"As a group we have agreed to do this show together to encourage other women to join us to do something that can be seen. The show has been organised by five women, of which I'm the designer. We have been sponsored by the Swiss government through the Swiss Corporation. The other women have the duties of making the accessories and finding the materials used to decorate the clothes," she explained.
Although she has participated in other exhibitions before, for her ordinary designs, this coming one has filled her with more excitement, she says because of it treading into new territories. Like her four colleagues, besides the obvious nervousness they are going forward with much optimism.

Original_Search_Title: Ford Foundation - Rewarding Excellence
Headline: Nigeria: Ford Foundation - Rewarding Excellence
Body_Text:
As Nigeria was celebrating its 50th anniversary last year, the Ford Foundation was also celebrating with us. It is one development partner with the stay on power. It has been around for the past 50 years working to promote development in Nigeria. Last year, foundation marked more than half a century of work in West Africa, a milestone worth celebrating particularly as it coincided with the country's memorable march to nationhood. To celebrate the occasion, the foundation announced a Jubilee Transparency Awards to recognize a time of great promise and transition in Nigeria which the post election period will usher in. The foundation has dedicated one million US dollars to the award as a mark of its commitment to strengthening democracy in Nigeria and West Africa.
Dr. Odaga, the Ford Foundation Representative for West Africa looks back with satisfaction at what the Ford Foundation has achieved in the work it has been doing across the sub region and in Nigeria in particular. 'Today with West Africa on the verge of a new era of peace and prosperity, we are working with visionary leaders in civil society and governments who are expanding participation in the democratic process and accelerating the pace of change'. Dr. Adhiambo Odaga, said that the foundation has a special relationship with Nigeria dating back to the country's independence. She said that the recent elections, as well as the spread of democratic values and accelerated social change across the sub-region, signaled the potential for a new era of democratic growth for West Africa.
"We envision a future in which West Africans and their governments leverage the region's extraordinary human and natural resources for the common good," Dr. Odaga said. "That future - in which all people have both the right and the opportunity to achieve their potential - begins with citizen involvement and good governance." She said the convergence of the Ford Foundation's golden jubilee in West Africa and Nigeria's 50th Independence anniversary gives the awards special relevance.
The foundation said the awards have two objectives: First, to recognize innovative projects and programs that promote transparent, effective and accountable governance at the community level in Nigeria; and second, to showcase the impact of these projects as models for deepening democracy,
To promote transparency in the award process, the foundation selected a Lagos based Non Governmental Organization, the Development Initiatives Network (DIN) to manage the application process for the foundation's $1 million Jubilee Transparency Awards. DIN is a development-support organization working on public interest issues in Nigeria. Ford Foundation appointed an eight-member National Awards Committee that will work with DIN and oversee the selection of up to 10 awardees. The committee will be chaired by Reverend Father Matthew Hassan Kukah; other members of the awards committee also include a retired Supreme Court Justice Adolphus Karibi-Whyte, T.Y. Bello, internationally acclaimed musician and photographer; Nigeria's youngest Media Merit Award winner, Chude Jideonwo; Nurudeen Lemu, Assistant General Secretary of the Islamic Education Trust; Mrs. Victoria Aiyeyemi, a community leader and school proprietor, and my humble self.
The awards will recognize and reward outstanding initiatives that promote transparency, good governance, and integrity at the community level in Nigeria. The foundation believes that strong democracies everywhere rely on transparency, citizen engagement, and good governance at all levels, and has long supported initiatives in Nigeria to promote these values. Looking at criteria for the award brought to mind the various activities the civil society organizations have undertaken to deepen democracy, civic education, election observation, gender mainstreaming in politics and public life, and human rights education. Some have focused on public finance analysis, accountability and transparency in the extractive industry, while others have worked as coalitions to using the issue based approach to promoting change. The demanding change and the drivers of change research are good examples of such initiatives. They are focused on promoting institutional change through partnership of coalitions from civil society, the private sector, the media and government. Together they are confronting institutional rules that reinforce patronage and corruption. They are tackling issues that constitute the deep-rooted constraints to achievement of the MDGs. They want to ensure public accountability around the use of debt relief and the repatriation of stolen assets so that these could be ploughed into economic empowerment of the people. Other areas are public safety, police corruption and the spread of small arms; and building a broader support base for the work of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission EFCC and other anti-corruption agencies.
On April 29 2011, the selection committee held its inaugural meeting at the Ford Foundation offices in Lagos, a sparkling ultra-modern complex on the famed Banana Island, an extension of Ikoyi. The Committee, deliberated on the structure, screening process and code of conduct for the Awards. In his opening remarks, Rev. Kukah commented on the significance of the launch of the awards, particularly coming on the heels of the recently concluded general elections in Nigeria. The Committee decided that nominations will be accepted electronically beginning on Wednesday May 4, 2011. Shortlisted nominees will be assessed and visited by an independent team of consultants led by noted legal scholar and development professional, Professor Bolaji Owasonoye. Rev. Kukah explained that "grants of at least $100,000 will be awarded by the Ford Foundation, in accordance with its global grant making policies, to up to 10 shortlisted nominees. Awardees will be required to use the prize money to enhance the scale and impact of their work to deepen democracy, good governance and anti-corruption across the country. Awardees will be required to use the prize money to enhance the scale and impact of their work.
DIN will act as secretariat for the awards process, managing applications and verification, and supporting the work of the National Awards Committee. The Committee will be solely responsible for selecting a shortlist of nominations from the total nominee pool, and for selecting the final group of awardees. There is also an Awards Verification Committee, headed by noted legal scholar Professor Owasanoye, which will carry out a process of verification to ensure the validity and integrity of each organization that makes it to the short-listed round of nominees.
Dr. Bola Fajemirokun, the Executive Director of the Development Initiatives Network, is the awards secretary. Her organization has received a grant from the Ford Foundation to manage the entire process in support of the National Awards Committee and the Awards Verification Committee. Mr. Kayode Samuel, a communication and public policy specialist is the consultant to the Awards Committee and will help the committee execute its duties. The Ford Foundation emphasized that the National Awards Committee will make the selections independently, and may select any number of awardees up to 10. Selected winners will receive the grants directly from the Ford Foundation, which will give final approval to the winners in accordance with its global grant making guidelines and standards. Nominations will be opened on Wednesday May 4. An online nomination form, including an overview of the nomination process, eligibility criteria, and selection criteria is available at www.fordfoundation.org/regions/west-africa.
For questions about the awards process, or to suggest nominees, inquiries should be made directly to DIN at

Original_Search_Title: Bold Woman Transforms Lives of Mathare Kids Through Education
Headline: Kenya: Bold Woman Transforms Lives of Mathare Kids Through Education
Body_Text:
As Magdaline Ogutu attended a church service one afternoon in 1998 at Mathare slum in Nairobi, she realised the low turnout of women and children and she decided to do a follow up on the same.
This was the beginning of a long journey which would later make her resign from her full time job as an accountant to follow what became her passion. Her bold step has in the years borne fruit and is today a success story as she has three primary schools, a secondary school and an orphanage to show for her effort. "The situation I found in Mathare when I went to do my research was wanting. Kids, who had reached school age, were just loitering around, most of them looking for food; that caught my eyes and moved my heart," she says.
Sitting in her office at Mathare Community Outreach Primary School, which is one of the schools to her credit, situated in the heart of the Mathare slum, Magdaline tells us how she began. "When I saw the bad situation deep in the slum, I went back to the church, Outreach Community Church, which is led by my husband Bishop Ogutu and explained the situation," she remembers.
It was at that point that she got a volunteer who joined her and together they started gathering children from the Mathare community at a common ground where they taught them songs and had bible lessons.
She says the turnout was so high that in a period of just one week, they had managed to get 150 children for the lessons. "I realised the open ground was not very healthy for the kids and from my pocket I decided to hire a room for Sh200 a month where they (children) would sit on the floor as we took the teachers' role," she remembers.
Magdaline knew the children needed food and because she could not afford to feed them she wrote a note to all kids requesting their parents to go to school for a meeting to discuss the issue. "We agreed that each parent would contribute Sh20 a month which would be used to buy flour and prepare porridge for the children."
She brought her sufurias and just because of the porridge, the number of kids attending the lessons doubled. "It was such a motivator but I still knew I needed to do much more," she says.
Magdaline started looking for funds and when persons from Compassion International visited the area they decided to take the role. "They gave us funds for complete balanced diet for the children, and we got staff on board on their budget."
One step led to the other and they added the rental rooms and started a baby class and a nursery school. "We advised the parents to take the kids to public primary schools after they leave our nursery class but we later realised that most of them were going to waste because not many could afford the little money they were asked to pay for admission," Magdaline says.
That was challenge enough to make them proceed on to starting a class one in 1993. Each year from then they went a class higher until their first class eight batch did the KCPE examination in 2001.
The Mathare Community Outreach Programme today has three primary schools, one secondary school and an orphanage. She estimates the number of students as 1,500 in all their schools and 114 kids in their orphanage at Kariobangi. To their credit are also more than 10 churches in Nairobi with a branch in Banana, Nyanza and Kisumu.
Magdaline declares that before they went to the community, people were dying of hunger but now the situation has gone down by 50%. "We started classes to educate women on how to get and prepare readily available foods to get a balanced diet."
Most of their former students who have cleared secondary education have returned back to the community to offer their support. "We have a number of our former students as staffs here, most of them waiting to join colleges or universities. They help in social work or teaching the lower classes," Magdaline says.
She gets her motivation from the lives she has transformed in Mathare. "Seeing kids who have gone through my hands get to university is the best thing I have seen," she says happily, adding that two of her former students are at the University of Nairobi.
To avoid dependency syndrome, parents are urged to partly contribute for their children's education. "We realised parents were relying too much on us and we decided they should also feel accountable for their children's learning," she says.
The programme is currently being funded by the World Food Programme with others chipping in to supplement their efforts. Magdalene says their next project is to put up a Discipleship Programme which is more of a spiritual learning institution where they will be putting form four leavers who wish to study theology.

Original_Search_Title: Over $8 Billion Flee Country in Two Decades
Headline: Ethiopia: Over $8 Billion Flee Country in Two Decades
Body_Text:
Ethiopia may be touted at international conferences on development agendas as one of the 10 fastest growing economies in the world, but it is also one of the top 10 out of the 45 least developed countries (LDCs) where foreign currency flows out in an illicit manner, worse than countries such as Sudan, Djibouti and Eritrea, a new study found.
Over 8.3 billion dollars left Ethiopia in 18 years after 1990, an amount comprising an average 3.6pc of its GDP, a damning and first of its kind study, conducted by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), revealed last week.
This is part of the one trillion dollars that is believed to have left LDCs over the years covered by the study, an amount estimated to be 10 times larger than what these countries receive from rich countries in the form of official development assistance (ODA).
Ethiopia's loss of over eight billion dollars in the past nearly two decades represents an average of 3.6pc of the amount it has received from its development partners during the same period, the study revealed.
The worst period was in 2006, when illicit funds representing 9.2pc of foreign assistance for the year, amounting to 1.4 billion Br, was believed to have left the country.
Bangladesh stands above any of these countries found to be victims of the illicit outflow of foreign currency; it has lost 34.7 billion dollars (equal to 3.4pc of its GDP) to illicit money transfers, followed by Angola, which lost 34 billion dollars, 10.9pc of its GDP.
"This money could be used for the countries' development efforts," Helen Clark, UNDP administrator, said at a panel discussion held in Istanbul, Turkey, on the side of the UN's fourth conference on LDCs.
The 63-page report by the UNDP was released on the afternoon of Wednesday, May 11, 2011, where Abdella Hamdok, an expert on the issue from the UN Economic Commission for Africa (ECA), was one of the panellists.
"It is an excellent report that has managed to capture the extent of the problem," she said.
Although there are 49 countries listed as LDCs, and 45 of them were covered under the study, 70pc of the illicit outflows of funds as "any money that is illegally gained, transferred, or received" originated from Africa, according to Hamdok.
Another finding of the report that raised eyebrows among panellists was the revelation that the usual suspects of African dictators and their cronies do not have as much part in the money laundering scheme as members of the private sector. A staggering 79pc of money laundering out of these countries was funnelled through what the studies described as the "mispricing of trade."
The mispricing of trade involves businesses under or over invoicing of their merchandise, according to Clark.
"When a ton of bananas is sold for a dollar, but the invoice says 50 dollar cents, the other half is slashed to be sent to an offshore account," said Geraldine Fraser-Moleketi, a former senior official from South Africa who now serves UNDP as director of Democratic Governance Group.
The source of illicit money is tax evasion and an attempt to launder the gains through the international financial system, according to the UNDP study.
"That there is a lack of adequate global tax monitoring and the absence of information sharing has contributed to the outflow," said Moleketi.
Macroeconomic problems such as high inflation, structural characteristics of an economy including non-inclusiveness of growth, and overall governance issues such as political instability were blamed by the UNDP experts as the reasons behind money laundering from poor countries.
Ethiopia, sharing all or some of these factors, lost an annual average of 491 million dollars over 18 years, the study disclosed. The highest amount to have fled the country, 2.1 billion dollars, was recorded in 2008.
It claims nearly eight per cent of the country's GDP registered that year, according to the UNDP study.
Ethiopia's loss of foreign currency to money laundering between 1990 and 2008 was 1.2 points below the 4.8pc average recorded for all the countries covered in the study, while 27 of these countries are above this average which bleeds their economy. Chad is the biggest prey with a loss of 15.4 billion dollars (27.3pc of its GDP).
Government representatives at the discussion were advised by the UNDP officials to modernise their customs systems and undertake public administration reforms to provide expertise to fight money laundering. No Ethiopian government delegate was present; while over 30 had arrived in Istanbul, they left the day before.
Apart from an Ethiopian working for the UN and stationed in New York, there were two other Ethiopians in the room where the discussion took place. Among them was a shareholder of a prominent importing company in Ethiopia, Garad Plc. They had also left before the recommendations of the UNDP were read out.

Original_Search_Title: Bankole in the Eye of the Storm
Headline: Nigeria: Bankole in the Eye of the Storm
Body_Text:
Lagos — Since the institution of the current civilian dispensation in 1999, Dimeji Bankole, Speaker of the House of Representatives, is among few principal officers of the National Assembly to have survived the so-called banana peel - a euphemism that often saw key officers of the federal legislature, being eased out of office in ignominious circumstances. In fact, despite his relative youthful age, he has been able to weather the storm in the lower house since over three years that he has been in the saddle. His survivalist instincts came handy last week when he swam through a plot that would have seen him suffering a last minute removal from his high office.
The latest controversy against Bankole arose on account of the allegation that he borrowed N10 billion from a bank without the knowledge of principal officers and the house.
Though, Bankole was not around during the Wednesday sitting of last week, angry members demanded for explanations on the allegations. It took serious lobbying and lots of persuasion for a motion calling for his immediate suspension to be stood down for discussion at Thursday's session.
Dino Melaye of the 11 members whose suspension was lifted recently, set the tune for the day when he said that investigations by the recalled members showed that the bank account of the House was in reds as a result of the said loan obtained by the Speaker.
Accusing the Speaker who was also absent in the previous sitting of mismanaging the resources of the House, Malaye told his colleagues that with the financial position of the House, it was certain that the payments of their quarterly and severance allowances were under serious threats. By Thursday however, Bankole had as in the past weathered the storm, successfully. But Nigerians who have been watching the regime of controversies in the House, were hardly amused.
The latest allegation against the Speaker adds to the concern of enlightened Nigerians that not much with positive impacts on the electorate had emanated from the lower house, in the last four years, other than one form of accusation or the other. Critics particularly argue that the trend among members had been to engage one another in occasional rowdy sessions without impacting much on the life of Nigerians.
While the allegation against the Speaker made the rounds, many had dismissed it as a hoax, insisting that it would have been difficult for him to have obtained a loan of such magnitude without recourse to the House. They consequently alleged that the 11 recalled lawmakers could be looking for a way of getting back at the Speaker for supposedly being the brain behind their plight.
"In the first place, it is hard to believe that the Speaker alone can negotiate with the banks and secure a loan of N10 billion. I am surprised to hear that. But if it is true, it only shows the level of depravity and irresponsibility of some of our elected officers in this country. Well, when Patricia Etteh, the former speaker was accused of corruption to the tune of about N628 million, nothing was done to her other than just impeachment. So, even if it is true, nothing will be done to him. The infraction by key political office holder in Nigeria is like a cycle," Maxi Okwu, National Chairman of Citizens Popular Party (CPP) remarked.
Though the Speaker has managed to swim his current travail, he is incidentally among the leading politicians whose dreams of consolidating their stay in the National Assembly have been put on hold.
Bankole, an Economist, lost his reelection bid to Segun Williams of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN).
The Speaker, who polled 23,103 votes against the 28,490 votes scored by the ACN candidate in his Abeokuta South Federal Constituency, had exhibited maturity in congratulating his opponent in the contest.
Bankole, who noted that in elections, there must be winners and losers, congratulated all the winners in the election and advised that those who lost should accept their losses in good faith.
He had equally condemned those bent on scuttling the electoral process through violence, particularly through bombings, saying no nation has ever made progress through such means in addressing the challenges of national development.
For his sportsmanly disposition, Bankole's admirers have described him as a great lover of democracy and true leader that should be commended for his action.
Born in Abeokuta on November 14, 1969, many feel that the squabble in the Ogun State PDP which led to the emergence of two factions in the build-up to the elections may have contributed to the defeat of Bankole and other candidates of the party.
Son of Alani Bankole, a businessman, former National Vice Chairman of the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) and Seriki Jagunmolu of Egbaland, the Speaker attended University of Oxford's Officer Training College, Oxford, England in 1991 and Harvard University, Cambridge, Massachusetts, US in 2005.
He was elected into the House of Representatives in 2003 on the ticket of PDP to represent the Abeokuta South Federal Constituency of Ogun State. He became the Speaker during his second term in the Green Chamber when Etteh was removed for allegedly spending N628 million on house renovation and automobiles.
Bankole was the Director of Freight Agencies Nigeria Limited from 1995 until 1998, Executive Director of Operations of West African Aluminium Products Limited from 1998 until 2003, and Director of ASAP Limited between 2000 and 2003.

Original_Search_Title: The Museveni, Besigye Duel
Headline: Uganda: The Museveni, Besigye Duel
Body_Text:
The shock that gripped the country following the violent arrest of FDC leader, Kiiza Besigye was in itself quite surprising. It was not the first time an opposition politician or even Besigye himself was being roughed up.
His arrest this time may have marked a particularly low moment in the history of the Museveni government, but there was nothing new there either. It is not the first time it engages in behavior few would associate with a government that is committed to deepening democracy, rule of law, and respect for human rights. There are several examples to illustrate this.
In 2001 blows and karate kicks rained on Kiiza Besigye and his associates, among them former MP, Okwir Rabwoni in Entebbe International Airport's VIP lounge. In full view of senior western diplomats, Rabwoni was thrown like a sack of banana peelings onto a waiting truck and driven to the headquarters of military intelligence for questioning. Soon thereafter, he sought political asylum in the United Kingdom.
The entire presidential campaign then went on to turn into the most violent in Uganda's history. A report commissioned by parliament to look into those campaigns, the "Report of the select committee on election violence and other related matters", is a sobering tale of what happens when an unaccountable and insecure government comes face to face with organised opposition it would rather do without.
From Museveni's controversial election victory in 2001 to the 2002 referendum on political systems and the lifting of term limits in 2005, opposition politicians were routinely beaten, arrested, and locked up for attempting to hold public meetings in open spaces.
The police required them to be inside buildings while President Museveni was at liberty to hold rallies outdoors. Many of the Democratic Party's Young Democrats who built their political careers on defying police orders to hold their meetings indoors as they campaigned for the restoration of multi-party politics, bear scars from repeated beatings. That some of them are among the architects of the Walk to Work campaign that's currently keeping the regime's inner circle awake at night exposes the limits of brute force as a strategy for stifling opposition.
In the 2005/2006 presidential elections, Besigye and his associates in the loose coalition supporting his candidacy, once again became targets of unspeakable acts of cruelty by the government. Recently we have seen stick-wielding gangs assaulting members of the public in full view of police officers who have always stood by and watched. Could it be that those who were shocked by recent acts of brutality by the same people who have been tormenting the opposition for years had somehow forgotten all this history?
The NRM regime has always been violent. Those who are shocked by the wave of violence sweeping across the country in reaction to demonstrations are probably those who in the past preferred to look the other way possibly because it was not they or their relatives or friends who were being beaten. Now that the beatings have been decentralised and spread out over several of Kampala's suburbs and upcountry towns, many of those who previously remained untouched have now understood that no one is safe.
My earliest glimpse into the regime's violent streak was during the early 1990s when, in reaction to non-destructive student protests at Makerere University where I was a student at the time, it sent in heavily armed security forces whose random firing of live bullets claimed the lives of some students.
It was at a time when even some of today's regime critics and opposition leaders and supporters were still optimistic about the future, and so could afford to disregard seemingly minor shortcomings. For many students, though, it made them realise that the tendency towards heavy-handedness had not been the preserve of previous governments. For some it marked the end of what until then had been a great romance with the new rulers.
For others the romance lasted until the mid-1990s when in 1996, following his departure from the government and nomination as the opposition candidate, DP's Paulo Ssemogerere and his campaign team and supporters were subjected to intimidation, harassment, violence, and obstruction by NRM-supporting hooligans, security operatives and Resident District Commissioners. Those with long memories will recall how some of today's opponents of the NRM used to justify otherwise indefensible misconduct by state agents who would disrupt opposition rallies and make it impossible for Ssemogerere to campaign in some places.
For many Ugandans all this violence and the reluctance by government officials to acknowledge wrong-doing and apologise to their victims negates what has all along been the highest selling point for both President Museveni and the NRM: their capacity to control the army, ensure discipline, and that soldiers do not threaten people's well-being and sense of security. At the time of his swearing in as president in 1986, the young, inspirational and by then hugely believable Museveni, made the most famous of his political pronouncements promising "fundamental change" in the country's politics.
His promise on personal safety and security was very specific: "our citizens shall not be beaten... and no citizen, not even a tramp on the streets, shall be killed unless he is so condemned by the courts". And then he sought to distinguish himself and the NRM from other leaders elsewhere on the continent: "In Africa... We have had one group getting rid of another one, only for it to turn out to be worse than the group it displaced. Please do not count us in that group of people".
Well, a quarter of a century later there are several reasons why those who want to attack Museveni's record especially on matters to do with handling the opposition invoke his "fundamental change" speech. In polite society where personal conduct is guided by values such as sincerity and honesty, one is not expected to castigate wrong doing and then turn around and indulge in it.
One of the more objectionable aspects of past governments was their intolerance for opposition and the seeming belief by leaders then that only they knew what was best for Uganda. Indeed, Museveni's war against the Milton Obote government and the suffering it inflicted on Ugandans was, we have always been told, intended to end all the abuses Ugandans suffered at the hands of unaccountable and power- hungry leaders.
Underlying the decision by Museveni and others to go to war was the belief that the best way to deal with a bully was not to run away, but to stand and fight back, however unbalanced the battle might turn out to be, and whatever the price one had to pay. It is hardly an exaggeration to say that in 1981 few people believed Museveni's handful of fighters and idealists stood a chance against Obote's Uganda National Liberation Army and their Tanzanian allies. Indeed, even on the eve of the collapse of the Tito Okello regime in 1985, it was hardly obvious that Museveni's National Resistance Army had the capacity to seize power, scatter or subdue other fighting groups, and impose order. Well, they did, and for a period led a largely grateful country.
In reaction to Besigye's announcement that the FDC would declare its own results of the Feb. 18 elections, Museveni was quoted to have boasted that in 2005 the government had tried to teach the colonel manners but that it seemed he had not learnt his lesson. President Museveni thrives on boasting. So whenever he embarks on boasting, it is hardly newsworthy. This time, though, his chest-thumping raised more than a few eyebrows.
Although the way the government handled Besigye during the 2005 presidential campaigns was presented to the public as a purely legal matter, few believed it was. There was a general feeling that here was a vengeful and nervous President Museveni doing everything to humiliate his opponent and minimize his chances of performing well at the elections. In referring to those events as intended to teach Besigye manners, the President finally confirmed what Besigye's acquittal had revealed: it was pure persecution.
Then, as today, President Museveni's behavior and that of his security operatives and the police raised the question whether strong-arm tactics were the best way to resolve the matters that Besigye raises. The recent wave of unrest across the country is evidence that the search for common ground calls for a different approach; that brute force will not work.
If anything can be learnt from history, it is that Museveni and associates went to the bush in response to escalating political intolerance and failure by those who at the time were drunk with power, to privilege dialogue over violence.
It is, however, doubtful that President Museveni will seek to engage with his opponents in any meaningful way. His record up to this point suggests that anyone expecting serious talks and treatment of the opposition as equals is being excessively optimistic.
In the same way as Obote who promised to follow by then rebel leader Museveni to the bush and leave him there, today President Museveni prefers to beat his opponents into submission if intimidation and humiliation will not shut them up. However, through his obsessive determination to humiliate Besigye, President Museveni has managed to turn what could have been merely a subject for mutually respectful discussion and collective search for solutions, into the source of a growing national crisis that has seen many killed and maimed, and turned whole towns into zones of military deployment. Although placing a ring of military hardware and soldiers around urban centres is intimidating to some, going by the repeated cycles of clashes between the security forces and protesters, its effect can only be temporary.
Left unaddressed, popular discontent easily pushes fear, even of military hardware, into the background. For that reason and the opposition's determination to carry on insisting on their right to voice discontent, including by walking to work, tensions can only continue to mount. Museveni may decide to cling to force and intimidation as his default positions. However, Uganda's history and that of the world in general shows, it is in reverting to violence each time they are challenged that powerful autocrats find their downfall, eventually.
Every episode of violence visited upon unarmed demonstrators and defenceless opposition politicians engaging in normal political activity entrenches the view of Museveni as an old-style African big-man politician who will do anything to cling on to power.
The man once feted as a bright spark on a continent not known for producing inspiring leaders has acquired the image of a man out of touch with the reality of life for the ordinary person. One angry citizen reacting to all the rioting wondered: "Is spending trillions of shillings on fighter jets the best way to thank people who gave him 68 per cent of the vote when they have nothing to eat?"
As for Besigye, for someone who seemed as if his star had waned only a few weeks ago, today he's looked upon, even by those who would ordinarily not have much time for him or his party, as a hero of sorts for daring to stand up to Museveni's bully-boy ways. Granted, there are some who blame him for his confrontational approach and apportion equal responsibility to him insisting on protesting because he has the right to do so when he knows the brutal consequences.
So where might all this end? There is no knowing what will happen to Besigye eventually. It seems as if the government has only two choices if they want to silence him: lock him up on some trumped-up charge, or kill him. The first is tricky. The second would literally open the gates of hell. The country would burn. Would the regime survive?
Dr Frederick Golooba-Mutebi1 is a political scientist & Senior Research Fellow, Institute of Social Research, Makerere University in Kampala.

Original_Search_Title: After 30 Years of Bananas, It's Time to Upgrade to Petrodollars
Headline: East Africa: After 30 Years of Bananas, It's Time to Upgrade to Petrodollars
Body_Text:
Nairobi — When he takes the oath of office on May 12, President Yoweri Museveni will have set forth on a journey at the end of which he will be a member of an exclusive club of only five or so big men who have ruled for three decades and beyond.
Beleaguered Libyan leader Muammar Gaddafi, whose grip on power caps 42 years if he survives to September, leads this club.
Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo of Equatorial Guinea and Angola's José Eduardo dos Santos both come second, having logged 32 years at the helm.
Close behind is 87-year old Robert Mugabe in Zimbabwe, who has ruled for 31 years.
Mugabe is a bit of the odd man out here -- Gaddafi, dos Santos and Nguema all preside over huge oil resources in their respective countries.
By coincidence, Museveni's last lap to three decades in charge comes just five years after striking two billion barrels of oil in the Albertine Graben.
For the Ugandan president, it's time to dump the matooke economy or his own favourite expression -- a milk economy.
Oil is the real deal. Political analysts have made much of Museveni's statements in this regard.
One of these is, "I have hunted and killed my game; I will not hand it to another man to eat on my behalf."
Museveni often says this of his bush war that brought him to power in 1986.
What can be bigger game than oil?
Trouble is, wherever oil has been found in Africa, misrule has been the inevitable consequence -- the jury is still out on Ghana.
So, with his February 18 victory, Museveni gets a fresh five-year mandate till 2016 when he crosses the 30-year milestone.
Whenever he is asked about his legacy after three decades in power, Museveni often gets irritated that his achievements are reduced to just his presidency.
"It's actually 45 years. How can you talk about 30 years? This struggle started long ago to liberate Africa. For us we don't talk about power. We talk about more capacity to do things. So, how much capacity do we have now...in tax collection?" he told journalists just before the February 18 election.
So, how will history remember this extraordinarily long presidency? "Another 30 years of bananas," might describe Museveni's rule.
In 1990, celebrated Ugandan satirist Alex Mukulu wrote 30 years of bananas, a drama that summed up the country's post independence history as political chaos and economic lunacy served up by the leadership.
Nothing changed
Mukulu capitalised on the tribal schemes of Edward Muteesa and Milton Obote in the 1960s, through Idi Amin's reign of terror and economic war in the 1970s to build the plot for his satire.
Then there was Yusuf Lule's 68-day presidency, Godfrey Binaisa's comic stay in State House and Paul Muwanga's rough edges, which gave way to Obote's second shot at power from 1980 to 1985, when he was shown the door in a coup led by Tito Okello who also dished out a few epithets at, the then guerrilla leader Museveni, calling him a "snake."
By the time the "snake" prevailed in 1986, Uganda had been through a rollercoaster of war, economic misery, chaos and degeneration of social services.
Twenty-one years after Mukulu's masterpiece, not much has changed; if anything, there is less matooke to talk about today.
Repression, tribal hegemony, patronage, state inspired violence, use of the army to control power, corruption and stolen elections are present today as they were in the years before Mukulu wrote his drama.
"We were talking about those regimes, but I don't know whether there would be any difference if we staged the play today. It is still appropriate," he recently told The EastAfrican.
"Our president back then was still doing exams; he was not in the museum, but now, the same mistakes the past leaders made, he has made."
In short, it has been another 30 years of bananas, and some. When it was written, the play was meant as a prophetic piece, drawing from lessons of the immediate past.
"There is always something new that we could add. We would have to include new developments under this regime like corruption, state repression, etc, but a large part of it is still relevant."
When he took power, Museveni was faced with a monumental task: A next to a non-existent economy, a volatile security situation, a demotivated civil service, a nation sharply divided between north and south, and devoid of any useful infrastructure to drive the economy.
He went ahead to promise a fundamental change. But the burdens of a long incumbency have caught up with the Ugandan leader and there are pointers that he has fallen into the same trap as Mukulu's characters.
His regime is already tainted by massive corruption, a questionable human rights record, a defective delivery of services and a sham electoral democracy -- twice the Supreme Court has said Museveni's "victory" is electoral theft.

Original_Search_Title: Author Jailed for Insult Released
Headline: Cameroon: Author Jailed for Insult Released
Publisher: International Freedom of Expression Exchange Clearing House (Toronto)
Body_Text:
The Writers in Prison Committee (WiPC) of PEN International welcomes the release of author Bertrand Teyou on 29 April 2011 after almost six months in prison for allegedly insulting the president's wife in a book he published last year. Teyou was released on payment of a US$4,371 fine.
Teyou was detained on 3 November 2010, accused of 'insulting' President Biya's wife in his 2010 book "La belle de la république bananière" : Chantal Biya, de la rue au palais (The Belle of the Banana Republic: Chantal Biya, From the Streets to the Palace), and of attempting to hold a public reading of the offending text. He was jailed after failing to pay 2,030,150 CFA (US$4,371) in fines and costs imposed on 19 November at the end of a summary trial, in which he had no legal representation.
Teyou was freed from New Bell prison in Douala on 19 April 2011 after a well-wisher paid the fine in full. His conviction for 'insult to character' and organising an 'illegal demonstration' has reportedly been lifted. He is now receiving medical treatment for health problems linked to a poor diet in prison, including severe bleeding from haemorrhoids.
Source:
Writers in Prison Committee, PEN International

Original_Search_Title: Senate Presidency - PDP Chieftain Backs Mark
Headline: Nigeria: Senate Presidency - PDP Chieftain Backs Mark
Body_Text:
A Peoples Democratic chieftain in Benue State, Mr. John Ochoga, has called on the Benue People and the people of the north-central zone to advocate and fight for Senator David Mark's return as senate president.
The PDP leader also urged the Tiv people to tolerate an Idoma governor in the state come 2015 in the spirit of brotherhood since, according to him, the country is now in an era of minority taking their rightful place.
Ochoga said this during a wedding thanksgiving service in Makurdi.
He stated that the fact that Mark has been there as senate president for four years without slipping on any banana peels attests to his capability, hence the need for him to continue.

Original_Search_Title: Protests Make Hand-to-Mouth Existence More Tenuous
Headline: Uganda: Protests Make Hand-to-Mouth Existence More Tenuous
Body_Text:
Rose woke up very early in the morning, even before the village rooster started crowing. She entered the little kitchen next to the hut where she was sleeping on a single bed. Her husband had died a few months ago, leaving her with three mouths to feed, and a heap of debts with the village grocery shop.
In her smoky kitchen, Rose put the firewood together boiling some water to make the morning porridge, the only breakfast the family could afford to have. She then went to the backyard garden to pick some bananas that she placed neatly in a round basket, taking extra care while stepping on the overgrown grass around the banana trees because she had been bitten by a snake some months ago, which had left her family in limbo till she recovered and could walk again.
Hearing the voices of other women approaching, Rose rushed to the banana basket placing it on the head while giving instructions to her ten- year-old daughter who had to grow up instantly and replace her mother to carry on with the day's chores.
The ladies, a group of banana sellers, started their morning walk towards the city; their aim was to catch the morning crowd walking to work or descending from commuter taxis, hoping that the little cash they get at the end of the day will help their children go to school in anticipation of a better future.
That morning the load on the ladies' heads was heavier than usual, each one of them had an extra bunch of banana that she was going to sell on behalf of their friend Mary who was hit by a strong case of malaria. They knew too well that one day missed meant little food on the table, and Rose remembered how everyone had helped her when she was bitten by a snake. They needed to stick together to survive!
The sun was now well positioned in the sky, their tired legs and aching necks yearned for a rest, but then the tall buildings of the city were now in full view, a few more blocks and the ladies would go in different directions hoping the bananas will fly from the baskets and turn to money in their pockets, but then that day this was not meant to be.
The ladies saw a wave of people surging towards them, screams, smoke and defining gun shots rocked the streets. Rose and her friends started running too, and in the mayhem her banana basket fell down and the bananas did fly but landed in pools of mud, crushed and squashed by the fleeing human wave.
When back in the village, she heard that those were demonstrations, protestors who wanted to walk to work to show their frustrations. Rose, who walked to work everyday of her life, was now confused. One thing was clear though, that night it was porridge again.
Rose may be an imaginary lady made in my head, but the walking banana sellers, garden snakes and the porridge meals are all real !
Ms Ebrahimi is a social critic

Original_Search_Title: Murugi's Words Often Dampen Society's Spirit
Headline: Kenya: Murugi's Words Often Dampen Society's Spirit
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Cabinet minister Esther Murugi's gaffes remind me of an e-mail conversation I recently had with a young Kenyan.
Like most people, he did not know that the tongue, not the brain, is what is peculiar to human intelligence -- that language, not thought, is the human thing par excellence.
Thought is at least incipient in all intelligent species. How thoughtful your dog is.
But the bonobo is the deepest non-human thinker. A bonobo who espies a bunch of bananas atop a tree will summon up enough wit to grab the twig lying by to fetch down some fruit.
Communal use
But it will never occur to him to pass to other chimps that vital information so that they can all benefit from the discovery, either immediately or by keeping the twig -- and even trying to reshape it -- for a more efficient communal future use.
The difference, then, is that all sub-human thinking is individual, not social. Thinking can become human only after -- by going upright -- an anthropoid ape frees and turns her forelimbs into hands for grabbing and manipulating things.
Work -- as we now call it -- was what stimulated the brain into its present size.
But, though size -- i.e., a certain number of neurons -- was a necessary condition for human intelligence, it did not suffice.
A certain pitch of intricacy of atoms, molecules and cells was necessary to transform the anthropoid's brain into the human mind to catalyse the work and loosen the vocal organs so that members could compare notes at work.
Thought, then, is nothing but work transferred from the hands to the brain, where the work is idealised so that the ideas can be ploughed back to the hands to improve the work; and language is but thought transferred from the brain to the tongue -- where the ideas are transmuted into words -- so that all members can share them.
Species like the gorilla, the thrush and the dolphin have a remarkable ability to think and even to work.
What they lack is the ability for intra-specific sharing of an individual's experiences in such details as size, colour, number and kind -- something which is possible only through oral language.
It is possible only because of our ability to abstract things from one another and to give conventional names to those abstractions.
Names -- i.e., words -- are the quantum elements of all specific human communication. I say "specific" as opposed to such other communication techniques as gestures and grimaces, which many other species enjoy.
Words, then -- oral communicability -- are what make human thinking unique. That is why it is vital for members to use words in such a way that the message is accurate and immediate and conduces to communal welfare.
In a species in which individuals are intelligent enough to learn to take an unfair advantage of others, falsities, prevarications and what Mark Twain called "silent-assertion lies" will abound.
For a brain whose vocation and volition limit it to learning only an infinitesimal fraction of what it is capable of, there will also be a deadly deal of honest misuse of words.
Communication bottleneck
Both cases can occasion a communication bottleneck of the kind now leading our nation into the abyss.
Thus, although Esther Murugi is correct to remind us that she is entitled to her thoughts, we must remind her that we are entitled to ours that may be contrary to hers.
If language, thought and work evolved together to promote society's survival interests, they must work in harmony. Where words regularly create widespread misunderstanding, they are likely to injure society's work spirit very adversely.
As I said here once, Ms Murugi probably means well. But good will is not good enough. Good intentions can be useful to society only if expressed in words (and deeds) that invigorate society's work spirit.
Ms Murugi's words often dangerously dampen that spirit.

Original_Search_Title: Minister's Wife Goes to Jail for Drug Dealing
Headline: South Africa: Security Minister's Wife Goes to Jail
Body_Text:
There's growing pressure on South Africa's intelligence minister to quit or be fired after his wife and a Nigerian accomplice were jailed for drug trafficking this week.
Opposition politicians say its inconceivable that the country's top spy did not know what his wife was up to.
Sheryl Cwele, the wife of South Africa's Minister of State Security, was sentenced to 12 years in prison.
She's been convicted of hiring young women as mules to smuggle cocaine into the country.
Her Nigerian coaccused, Frank Nabolisa, received a similar sentence at the Pietermaritzburg high court in the eastern province of Kwazulu-Natal.
Both have moved to appeal.
Minister Siyabonga Cwele is refusing to comment on the matter until after the appeal.
His wife's lawyers argue she's been manipulated by Nabolisa. Opposition parties intensified calls for the minister to resign.
African Christian Democratic party leader Kenneth Meshoe says Cwele has failed the state by not uncovering drug trafficking happening under his nose.
The head of the country's largest trade union federation, Zwelinzima Vavi, says this is the kind of thing that can only happen in a country that is close to becoming a banana republic.
Hennie Van Vuuren of the Institute for Security Studies says Cwele's wife has been implicated in transnational organised crime that took place at the time of his appointment.
If the intelligence services did not know about this, why not? And, if they did and informed President Jacob Zuma, what did he do about it?

Original_Search_Title: Intrigues And Power Play in the Senate President Contest
Headline: Nigeria: Intrigues And Power Play in the Senate President Contest
Body_Text:
PERHAPS for the first time in the about four years he assumed office as Senate President, Senator David Mark these days has compelling reason to forgo his regular afternoon teeing at the Abuja Golf Club. Unlike his many predecessors whose stay in office was characterized by tension and many plots, Mark has sat out almost four years in office without much hiccup. While many of his predecessors used their afternoons to plot survival strategies, Mark to the surprise of many spent his own time in his lifelong passion - golfing.
Critics say that he could only have afforded the leisure of playing golf in the afternoons because as ringleader of the many insurrections that removed his predecessors from office, that he knew the locations of the banana peels that caused them to slip off and as such carefully avoided them.
His carefulness has paid off leading to the most stable Senate of the fourth republic. His expertise has helped Senator Mark break the survival record achieved by Senator Anyim Pius Anyim between July 2000 and June 2003.
After achieving that record and also another record of being the first sitting Senate President to win re-election to the Senate, Senator Mark is looking forward to the unprecedented history of becoming the first Senate President to lead two different Senates.
At midweek, the challenges were as expected stiff. Like the last Senate leadership contest in June 2007, the most formidable opposition against Mark is coming from the class of outgoing Governors coming to the Senate.
The office of Senate President is presently zoned to the North-Central geopolitical zone from which has the outgoing Governor of Kwara State, Dr. Abubakar Saraki as a Senator-Elect. Senator-Elect Abdullahi Adamu, the immediate past Governor of Nasarawa State is also being regarded as a possible contender in the race for the Senate presidency.
The aspirations of the duo would, however, have to be circumscribed within whatever zoning configuration that the ruling Peoples Democratic Party, PDP allocates the office to. The PDP has the highest number of Senators in the Senate and the House of Representatives and if members follow party discipline the party would be in good position to even dictate the emergence of the new leadership.
The office was zoned to the North-Central in the twilight of the Olusegun Obasanjo administration ostensibly as a pay-off to Senator Mark who at that time had spent his eight years in the Senate as an abiding crooner in the Obasanjo Amen Corner in the Senate.
Mark was to shake off a last minute insurrection mounted by George Akume to assume office as Senate President in early June 2007. Since then he has led the most stable Senate of the fourth republic without any of the mutterings and rumpus that characterized the fourth and fifth Senates.
Mark according to information has already launched a bold bid for the office. His effort indeed went back to even before the National Assembly elections according to Vanguard sources.
The Senate President it was learnt made contacts and sometimes congratulated winners of the PDP senate primary tickets in most of the country. He is reported to have written letters of congratulation and in some cases to even to opposition party candidates he believed had good chances of defeating PDP candidates.
"He wrote me a letter of congratulation when I obtained the primary ticket," one fresh Senator-Elect belonging to one of the opposition parties told Vanguard.
Besides the contenders from within the North-central geopolitical zone, the Senate President is equally faced with the serious challenge from other geopolitical zones scheming for a review of the present zoning arrangement. In this regard he is contending with the clamour from the Northeast and the Southeast geopolitical zones which are seeking for higher offices in the polity.
Presently, the highest elected political office holder from the Northeast is the Deputy Speaker of the House of Representatives, while the Southeast has the Deputy Senate President as its highest office holder in an elective office.
Stakeholders from both groups have in recent meetings been advocating a review for the purpose of giving them higher stakes in the polity.
The Northeast geopolitical zone which presently has 14 Senators-Elect on the platform of the PDP it was learnt is already agitating for the office. Remarkably, only one PDP Senator from the region, that is, Senator Alkali Ahmed won re-election to the Senate.
Ahmed who is from Gombe State is ironically not interested in the contest, a fact that stems from the rumoured interest of the outgoing Governor of Gombe State, Alhaji Danjuma Goje who is to represent the Gombe Central Senatorial district in the next Senate.
The argument of the North-East geopolitical zone is that the core North should in the spirit of national reconciliation be pacified with the office of Senate President. They are equally arguing for religious balance in the top positions in the National Assembly," an argument that presumes that all 14 Senators-Elect from the Northeast are Muslims.
However, that argument is being shot down by advocates of Mark. Under the present arrangement, the Senate President and his deputy are both Christians while the reverse is the case in the House of Representatives where the Speaker and his deputy are both Muslims. Canvassers for Mark are hoping to retain the balance.
While advocates for Mark are hinging on a retention of the present zoning policy of the PDP, there is equally some drumbeat that the presidency would be much more comfortable with Mark sustaining the ongoing collaboration between the two arms of government.
The advocates point at Mark's supportive role for Jonathan during the national crisis triggered by the ill-health of former President Umaru Yar'adua last year.
However, the contention from the Southeast is that after delivering more than 85% of their votes to the PDP during the last round of elections that the zone should not be put to lag behind the Southwest PDP which could barely survive the Tsunami of the Action Congress of Nigeria, ACN in the region.
Indeed, in canvassing lifting the zone in the next dispensation, the Southeast caucus of the House of Representatives at a meeting on Tuesday agreed that religion should not be factored in the election of the new leadership.
"In 2007, the President of the Senate, Senator David Mark and the Speaker of the House of Representatives, Rt. Hon Patricia Etteh were Christians. In fact, in the current legislature, both the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker of the House of Representatives are Muslims. Religion has never been an issue. How has it now crept in surreptitiously?"
"We insist on either the offices of the President of the Senate or Speaker of the House of Representatives. We will reject other offices such as Secretary to the Government of the Federation, Deputy President of the Senate or any such office that may be offered to undermine the attainment of the two offices so mentioned," they asserted in a communiqué released on Wednesday.
The position of the Southeast House of Representatives caucus is bound to raise apprehension in the camp of Senator Mark considering the pivotal role of Senator Ike Ekweremadu, the deputy President of the Senate who is from the Southeast.
Would Ekweremadu jettison the collective demand of Ndigbo for his own desire to remain a deputy to Mark? It is a sensitive question which associates of Ekweremadu, however, wave aside on the platform of religious sensitivity.
"We already have a winning team and changing it would bring the difficulty of having a Senate, Deputy, and Speaker who are all Christians," an associate of Ekweremadu involved in the joint campaign with Mark told Vanguard.

Original_Search_Title: Museveni, Peers Can't Stop Western Intervention in Africa
Headline: Africa: Museveni, Peers Can't Stop Western Intervention in Africa
Body_Text:
Gen. Yoweri Museveni loves the label of game changer. First, he brushed off as simple-minded Western recognition of Alassane Ouattara over poll loser Laurent Gbagbo. Then he penned a love-hate remembrance of good old brother Gadaffi and damned UN Security Council for imposing a no-fly-zone over Libya and knocked down their actions as 'double standards'.
And Museveni was not alone in his grief. Turkish President Abdullah Gul also fell in and mourned that the blitzkrieg were driven by 'opportunism'. Both picked apart the West as zealots who turned a blind eye to worse situations in Bahrain, Somalia, and Côte d'Ivoire. Museveni then drew a line in the African sands and warned US and European troops not to cross.
"Foreign troops' coming into Africa without the permission of the AU is not acceptable. We're going to deal with it..." (Daily Monitor, April 18). But can't US and European troops intervene in Africa anymore? "No! They will not! Because we will not allow it and if they want another Vietnam, they will get it!"
Crushing knockout
Museveni has reason to be touchy. But the UPDF supremo hazards boxing above his weight. Museveni's call up to banana republics to scale up arms race to counter Western might is pathetic. They risk suffering a crushing knockout punch. No African leadership is about to match the military might of USA and NATO. Africa's vampires can only suck up to their neo-colonial masters for arms to bomb citizens into submission, and not stick up against Western intervention in Africa.
Pan-African interests remain irrelevant in Western interventions. That is why the West brushed aside the ego-massaging 'deal-democracy' overtures for Gadaffi and Gbagbo. The West unilaterally executed UN Resolution 1973 and wiped off Gadaffi's airpower. Predictably, the London conference on Libya shut out the ne'er-do-well AU dawdlers. The West too bombed out Laurent Gbagbo as AU sat around comatose.
Africa's interests
And interests of Africans are not about to matter in such interventions, even as Museveni scrawls: "Why? Are there no human beings in Somalia similar to the ones in Benghazi? Or is it because Somalia does not have oil which is not fully controlled by the Western oil companies...?" And for Museveni, the Lake Albert Graben oil-curse will soon beckon the unimpeachable US and UK might to prop or order him quit. And Museveni and weak-kneed peers will only whine.
Western powers execute only interest-predicated interventions, whether unilateral or multilateral. The interventions are executed to fulfil national policy objectives, but couched in diction of saving people's lives, guaranteeing democratic aspirations and ending oppression. And this motif was dead on target for US' bombardment of Gadaffi - to forestall loss of civilian lives, and so was it for Gbagbo and soon will it be for Uganda. Then Museveni will, as has Sudan's President Omar al-Bashir, remember no Western intervention is virtuous.
And no African leadership is about to stymie this Western imperial arrogance. The West has built key instruments of international relations executed through unrivalled framework of shrewd political alliances, coercive diplomacy, and economic sanctions. These are secured by military force and peace enforcement. All channelled by Western global informational and perception management tools such as BBC, CNN, Sky News, and France 24.
Even the UN only intervenes in complex humanitarian crises with prompts from the US, UK and France. Sierra Leone was only pacified after the intervention of elite British ground, sea, and air forces in Operation Palliser. Nigeria-led UN troops had dismally failed (2000). Only US-led air power was able to hold back a slaughter in Libya and France-led air power able to stave off massacre in Côte d'Ivoire as AU fiddled.
And timing of humanitarian interventions by the West based on goe-strategic security and regional security overrides motifs for protecting civilian lives. Consider Syria, Bahrain, Yemen, and Libya. All had bloody repression of protests, but only Libya speedily pulled in Western intervention.
Yet Syria's Bashar al Assad the ruling al-Khalifahs of Bahrain and Yemeni President Ali Abdullah Saleh were similarly savaging anti-government protesters. Darfur, a latter conflict; also speedily forced intervention while the withering LRA crisis did not. Western media fostered the articulation of cultural clash between Arabs and Africans. But more significant was Western quest to vilify and cut down the Islamic 'pariah state' in Khartoum.
But the US and UK as chief sponsors of the Darfur motion in the UN Security Council looked away when the more horrific LRA crisis report was tabled before the UN. They similarly averted their gaze when the US State Department's Country Report on Human Rights Practices and UN Human Rights Mapping Report on Dr Congo, accused Uganda of human rights violations in northern Uganda and in DR Congo, covering grave violations of human rights and international humanitarian law between March 1993 and June 2003.
Double standards
Paradoxically, the initial confidential UN rights report on Darfur crisis, which documented massacres, rapes, torture, and one million being forced to flee the crisis failed to force intervention. This was because Western powers, led by the US, wanted the Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) between Khartoum-SPLA peace sealed.
Interventions premised on humanitarian intervention by the big powers remain a hollow rhetoric and slave to willful policy of indifference and obstructionism to satisfy Western policy objectives. Centring the spotlight on Darfur, Liberia and DR Congo (1999 - 2001) conflicts over northern Uganda was selective and pegged to security, trade, investments and national policy objectives. Liberia is culturally proximate to USA - a country founded by freed African-American slaves. Mineral-rich DR Congo is of immense economic interest to the West. But sweeping under the carpet the LRA war was strategic for rearguard action in Sudan.
Humanitarian intervention
US' first pure humanitarian intervention in Africa was meant to placate criticisms of its wars of expediency in Operation Desert Storm in Iraq (1991), and Kosovo (1999). But US-led operation Restore Hope in Somalia (1992-3), mandated to protect humanitarian assistance and peace-enforcement operations, ended in a slaughter. So, in 1994, appeals by UNAMIR Force Commander Lt. Gen. Romeo Dallaire on an impending genocide in Rwandan were ignored.
Understandably, the US had just suffered tragic loss of lives in Somalia, dramatised in Academy Awards winning war film, Black Hawk Down (2002). The US dreaded another failure in Rwanda! Yet the UN readily adopted latter motions by the US on both Rwanda (1994) and Darfur (2004)! Again, Museveni and Gul are not candid on rubbishing Western 'double standards' and 'opportunism'. Both have selectively blanked out their own favours with 'double standards' of Western powers.
Geo-strategic allies
As geo-strategic allies, both countries benefited from refusal of the West to intervene in their internal conflicts. Gul forgot to say President Obama blocked his ears to Kurds cries of self-determination from Turkey. Obama also sweet-talked US Congress to pass over old Turkey (Ottoman Empire) denial of the 1915 Armenian Genocide and not pass a resolution damning the atrocities (2010) for fear of provoking a diplomatic row.
George Bush did the same for Uganda. Museveni did not think back that calls for intervention in the LRA conflict (2004), including from the UN Security Council, failed to force intervention because the selfsame UN movers - the UK and US - stood firmly on his side. They looked away as 12,000 children were abducted, and hundreds of civilians massacred in cycle of atrocities in Atiak, Lamwo, and Barlonyo.
In the end, both Museveni, who wobbled over a home-proposed anti-homosexuality legislation when powers in Washington, London and Ottawa telephoned; and Gul, culpable of repression against his people, are as guilty of turncoat politics as their Western benefactors.
Mr Lucima is the Revise Editor at Monitor Publications

Original_Search_Title: Kist, German Company to Construct Low Cost Houses
Headline: Rwanda: Kist, German Company to Construct Low Cost Houses
Body_Text:
A German firm, in collaboration with Kigali Institute of Science and Technology (KIST), plan to construct up low-cost housing estimated to cost approximately Rwf 4.5 million per unit.
BAM Federal Institute for Materials Research and Testing, a German construction company, will implement the project, in close collaboration with Germany African Business Association (GABA).
Each unit will consist of two bedrooms a bathroom and living room and will be built using locally available materials.
Speaking to The New Times yesterday, Helmut R. Schrader, the Senior Advisor at GABA, noted that the houses would be affordable.
Schrader pointed out that the company has developed technology to construct low cost housing, adding that it had already built light weight houses in Germany, South Africa, Ghana and Morocco.
"The houses are storm and fire resistant and before this year ends, we shall have built a sample house at KIST. This project is not limited to a few countries; we are going step by step to launch it in other African countries," he explained.
G. Senthil Kumaran, the Head of Department Civil Engineering and Environmental Technology at KIST, said that project is vital for a section of the Rwandan population, who cannot afford high quality housing units.
"In order for people to afford the cost of shelter, we are importing the new technology that can be easily used by every Rwandan. We are going to use locally available materials to construct the houses," he noted.
Among local materials to be used are volcanic cinders, banana and sisal fibre, among others.

Original_Search_Title: Ford Foundation Announces Awards Nomination Process
Headline: Nigeria: Ford Foundation Announces Awards Nomination Process
Body_Text:
The Ford Foundation has announced the selection of Development Initiative Network, DIN, to manage the application process for the foundation's $1 million jubilee Transparency Awards.
It also named Rev. Fr. Matthew Kukah to chair an eight-member National Awards Committee that will work with DIN to oversee the selection of 10 awardees.
At press briefing in Lagos, held that the Foundation's office at Banana Island in Ikoyi, Ford Foundation Representative for West Africa, Dr Adhiambo Odaga said that the award is aimed at achieving two objectives.
According to her, the awards will recognize innovative projects and programmes that promote transparent, effective and accountable governance at the community level in Nigeria.
In addition, Odaga explained that the foundation equally aims to showcase the impact of the projects embarked upon by the recipients, as models for deepening democracy, good governance and anti-corruption across the country.
She said: "We envision a future in which West Africans and their governments leverage the region's extraordinary human and natural resources for the common good. That future, in which all people have both the right and opportunity to achieve their future, begins with citizens' involvement and good governance."
She further explained that the convergence of the Ford Foundation's golden jubilee in West Africa and Nigeria's 50th independence anniversary gives the awards special relevance adding that "the one-time programme, will conclude at the end of the jubilees year in September 2011 and the selection of the awardees will be supervised by a National Awards Committee chaired by Rev Kukah."
On his part, Kukah commended the foundation for the innovation just as he called on other foundations to take a cue from Ford Foundation.
He said "The award is a source of encouragement and also an effort to encourage the winners to do more. This should also encourage other foundations to toe the same path that Ford Foundation has taken."

Original_Search_Title: Githae's Plea Over Metropolis' Roads Cash
Headline: Kenya: Githae's Plea Over Metropolis' Roads Cash
Body_Text:
Nairobi Metropolitan Development minister Njeru Githae wants funds for the metropolis' roads and health services channelled through his ministry.Githae said his ministry should manage the funds for it to be relevant adding that this is the only way development in the metropolis can be realised. "Any money concerning roads and health should be directed to the Metropolitan ministry as it reaches many people in the metropolis area and I appeal to the Ministry of Finance to consider this issue so that utilities and facilities can be brought closer to the people," Githae said.
Githae said Vision 2030 can be achieved if the Nairobi Metropolitan ministry can control the money for roads and health. This, he said, will enable the ministry to provide good roads within the metropolis as well as rehabilitate health centres and hospitals. "Unless we build good sewers, good roads and health facilities in the metropolis, we cannot achieve Vision 2030," Githae said. Githae said this yesterday at Banana town in Kiambu during the commissioning of the rehabilitation of Banana-Ndenderu road.

Original_Search_Title: 'South East Bereft of Central Leader Like Mbakwe'
Headline: Nigeria: 'South East Bereft of Central Leader Like Mbakwe'
Body_Text:
Ndukwe Ikoh is a community leader and among leaders of thought in Abia State. In this interview with journalists in Lagos, including SENIOR CORRESPONDENT Austin Oboh, he speaks on the spate of political violence that featured across the country in the general elections. He also discusses why he feels the South East is on the fringes of power and what shape the future might hold for Ndigbo. Excerpts:
It was said that South East has always been in the opposition and that is why it has not gotten its due from the centre. Now that the region is almost PDP-dominated, what do we expect?
There has never been a time that the South East performed better by aligning itself with the centre. On the contrary, most of the good governance we have had has been from governments in opposition to the government at the centre. Take the old Imo State governor, Chief Sam Mbakwe (1979-1983) who was in the opposition in the Nigeria Peoples Party (NPP) when the National Party of Nigeria (NPN) controlled the centre. Up till date nobody has equaled his records in the South East in terms of good governance. Even Governor T. A. Orji of Abia - people thought he was up to some good work in his first two years, but he started derailing immediately the idea of moving to the centre was sold to him.
Also, Imo people said Governor Ikedi Ohakim was on course until he joined the centre. Every state whether in party A or B has statutory allocation, but its management determines the difference. Ebonyi and Enugu states since 1999 have been with the centre; what have they gotten from the centre that should attract other states there?
In the PDP, South East has not reclaimed the national chairmanship of the party, what is your view?
There is very serious lack of political leadership at the moment in the South East. The governors of the zone with the political leaders are not performing their due roles as leaders, like you have in the South West or in the North. The East is bereft of a central political leader - somebody who will sit and talk to the governors and politicians and tell them that this is the direction we should follow, for our collective benefits. As it is today, it will be difficult for anyone to emerge as PDP National Chairman from the South East without him stepping on the proverbial banana peel. The governors are like people of the same ilk; no one thinks higher than the other. This is what translated in the zone's adoption of Jonathan without any concrete promise coming from Jonathan but the bandwagon effect. This lack of political leadership in the South East is affecting us.
What are Jonathan's chances of winning the South East?
His chances are 100 percent, not that he has said anything convincing that this is what he is going to do for the Easterners. I don't think anybody should hold him accountable for anything even if he doesn't do anything for the East. The bandwagon effect - just because people are shouting his name - is enormous, and his chances of picking all the votes from the zone are so bright. The other presidential aspirants are not helping matters. They behave as if they have surrendered that region to him and none of them is coming around to tell the people the other side of the story, otherwise a candidate like Buhari has a lot of goodwill in the East, but he has not capitalised on that goodwill to come down to the East and go into proper negotiation with the leadership in the East to translate this goodwill to voting advantage. In the East, as far as I'm concerned, there are only two presidential aspirants, Jonathan and Buhari. They know Buhari by his antecedents and from various perspectives, but, unfortunately, he is not on the ground in the South East and that is why Jonathan is having an easy ride at the moment.
What is the shape of things to come at the national level?
It is dicey and so close to call. The same bandwagon effect you may imagine that Jonathan have in the South East and South South is the same for Buhari in the North West and North East. The South West is open to anybody, especially the ACN candidate. There could be a run off as speculated, but the power of incumbency is mighty.
The rate of violence across the country, in Akwa Ibom over 800 vehicles razed, bomb blast, etc - don't you think this trend can mar the election?
There is heavy apprehension everywhere because people want change and a lot of people are resisting this change. In various states opposition parties think that there is going to be rigging; they believe that the sitting government has a way of circumventing the process, and they are planning to counter this. We in community leadership, traditional rulers and opinion moulders, should send the right message of non- violence. Politics is a beautiful game if play it in a beautiful way. Preach the good gospel so that followers would not be involved in activities that may mar the whole process.
What should we expect in Lagos State?
ACN will still be in power in Lagos because the governor has done wonderfully well. In the South West, ACN is like a moving train and the take off point of the train is Lagos. ACN will win Lagos no matter the force against it. It is the only way the citizens will pay back the governor for his good deeds.

Original_Search_Title: Where Theme is the Message
Headline: East Africa: Where Theme is the Message
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Themed exhibitions can be a bit tricky. Unless tightly controlled, adherence to the topic often overcomes the quality of the art.
