Despite having led the winners' list last year, and being nominated at the Mama awards in Lagos, young afro pop musician Diamond led the list of former winners who walked home empty-handed.
He was joined on the list by Belle9, Fid Q, Mrisho Mpoto, Ali kiba, AY and Banana Zorro, who would rather wish to forget the awards very fast.

Original_Search_Title: Nairobi Students Protest Colleague's Killing
Headline: Kenya: Nairobi Students Protest Colleague's Killing
Body_Text:
University of Nairobi students yesterday went on the rampage disrupting traffic in the city. The students were protesting alleged killing of their colleague at a restaurant in Westlands.
The students paralysed business in Westlands. The police just watched as the students smashed car windscreens and destroyed everything on their path. Their colleague Denis Omondi, a second-year engineering student, was beaten fatally by guards outside the Psys restaurant last weekend. Three Dragon Security guards were charged with his murder in court on Tuesday.
The student also snatched valuables from motorists and commuters along Uhuru and Chiromo road. The students looted a pickup ferrying bananas.
They were stopped by police from entering the Psys bar in Unga House in Westlands. "We want to see the manager to tell us what exactly happened," the students said.
The students smashed the pub's windows, lit bonfires and stoned motorists around the pub. "How can a guard kill an engineer? Is it because they want to bring the Chinese to do our jobs?" posed one of the students.
For close to two hours, operation on Uhuru and Chiromo roads were paralysed. Police kept vehicles out of the roads. Gigiri police chief Josek Nasio pleaded with the students to go back to campus.
Yesterday Arthur Mwai, the owner of Psys bar, said it was not correct to say the pub's guards had beaten the student. He said the Unga House guards had beaten the student on the third floor while the Psys club, which has separate bouncers, is on the ground floor.

Original_Search_Title: Top Artists to Perform At Kili Music Awards
Headline: Tanzania: Top Artists to Perform At Kili Music Awards
Body_Text:
A HOST of celebrated local musicians have been lined up to perform at Kili Music Awards that will take place at Diamond Jubilee Hall in Dar es Salaam this Saturday.
Music lovers will have the opportunity of seeing and partying with the country's top performers such as Ali Kiba, THT, Banana Zoro, Linah, Diamond, Joe Makini, C Pwaa, 20%, Mzee Yusuf, Mapacha Watatu and Stara Thomas.
"We have agreed to use local artists as we believe that they are more than capable of producing breathtaking performances that will appeal to the audience," the Marketing Director of Tanzania Breweries Limited (TBL), sponsors of the glamorous music event, David Minja told reporters in Dar es Salaam on Tuesday.
He said Kili Music Awards is a celebration of Tanzanian inspired talent and aims to expose the incredible richness of local music and culture, showcasing the spectacular performances from the country's renowned musicians.
We want to take Tanzanian music to greater heights and to add on to this year we agreed that it is better that our local artists entertain us and that is a way for them to show their talents in musical art."
With live performances from previous winners of the Kili Music Awards, the night promises to be spectacular. Minja affirmed that this year's event will be filled with entertainment different from that of last year and will have a modern outlook than experienced in previous years.
"We are looking to take the awards to the next level this year. We have been sponsoring these awards for a few years now and each year we have been showing different styles in terms of improving the awards.
We have improved the Awards even more and we are positive that those who will attend the award ceremony will be witnesses."
Minja said the nominated artists and those who have been invited will be welcomed to the venue in a very special way and will be interviewed by the media. "We have agreed to use local artists and believe that what they will do is entertain us," Said Minja.
According to Minja, tickets for the show have already started to go on sale at different outlets in the city.
VIP tickets are sold at 25,000/- and ordinary tickets at 15,000/-. The awards that are being competed for the year 2010/2011 is Best Female Artist, Best Male Artist, Best Female Singer, Best Male Singer, Best Taarab Music, Best Song of the Year.
Others are Best Swahili Song (Band), Best Song in R&B, Best Song in Hip Hop, Best Song of Ragga, Best Rapper of the Year (Band) , Best Artist of Hip Hop, Best Song of East Africa, Best Producer, Best Video of the Year and Upcoming Artist, Best Collaboration Song, Best Cultural Song and Best Rhumba Song.

Original_Search_Title: The Nation Crush Kenya
Headline: Zimbabwe: The Nation Crush Kenya
Body_Text:
Kolkata — ZIMBABWE finally got the all-round team performance they had been looking for with a crushing 161-run victory over Kenya, in their swansong 2011 ICC Cricket World Cup tie at Eden Gardens yesterday, to put a glossy closing chapter to a campaign that didn't entirely go to plan.
A World Cup, in which they came short in their challenge to the established nations, could certainly have turned into a horror show if they had slumped to defeat against a Kenyan side that, save for its courageous batting display against Australia, had looked out of depth at this tournament.
But the Zimbabweans avoided that banana skin, and the tones of criticism that would inevitably have followed such a defeat, and turned the screws with a strong batting performance, as they made 308-6, and a good show with the ball in which they bowled out Kenya for 147 with 84 balls to spare.
Shem Ngoche was the last wicket to fall, his attempt to fire Graeme Cremer over deep mid wicket, only finding Chris Mpofu who completed the catch to give Zimbabwe a victory their superiority and, crucially, their all-round efforts deserved.
Half centuries from the consistent Craig Ervine (66), who was named man-of-the-match, Vusi Sibanda (61) and the reliable Tatenda Taibu (53) powered Zimbabwe, who won the toss and elected to bat, past the 300-run mark for only the first, and unfortunately the last, time at this World Cup.
Seamer Mpofu got the breakthrough, in what was always going to be a tough run chase for the Kenyans, when he trapped David Obuya for a duck and when dangerman Collins Obuya went shortly afterwards, run out for just one, the Kenyans never recovered and Zimbabwe coasted to their 25th win in 32 ODI matches against the East Africans.
There were two wickets each for Ray Price, Greg Lamb and leg-spinner Crèmer while Prosper Utseya and Mpofu got one each.
Nehemiah Odhiambo was the only one to offer real resistance and was left stranded on 44 at the end.
"I'm very happy that the guys played well, especially with the bat, which is what we have been asking for throughout the tournament," said Zimbabwe captain Elton Chigumbura.
Kenya veteran Steve Tikolo, made his team's captain in honour of his farewell international game for his nation, was given a touching send-off by the Zimbabwean fielders when he was dismissed, for the last time in the colours of his country, for 10 after being trapped by Price.
Tikolo called for a review but television replays clearly showed that the ball was going onto his leg stump and, since most of the ball was on target and with the initial decision from the umpire being out, he had to walk.
The crowd immediately realised what was happening, as the Zimbabweans saluted him, and soon Eden Gardens cheered the veteran who responded by raising his bat.
"We are disappointed because this is our last game in the tournament and we wanted to put on a good performance," said Tikolo.
"We didn't do that well on the field and the young guys have to take the positives and the negatives and move forward."
After Chigumbura had again won a crucial toss, Zimbabwe did not hesitate to bat and, although the Kenyan bowlers enjoyed some success with the new ball with Regis Chakabva falling early for nine and Taylor being taken at mid-on for 26, Taibu and Sibanda repaired the innings with a 110-run partnership for the third wicket. Playing with both caution and assurance, Sibanda worked his way to his half century, justifying the decision to call him after injuries ravaged the original squad, reaching his 50 from 54 balls in which he hit five fours and a six.
The six brought his fifty but, after reaching 61, he fell after a horrible mix-up with Taibu, which left him stranded on the other end, and he was run out to end an innings in which, as he grew in confidence, had appeared well set for his century.
Moments later umpire Asoka da Silva also gave Taibu out, to a ball that crashed into him but that was clearly missing leg stump, and the diminutive batsman/wicket-keeper called for a review. Not for the first time in this tournament, the Sri Lankan umpire had got it wrong and Taibu survived to complete his half century, punching the ball with authority to third man for the four that brought his fifty, to take his ODI tally to 3001 runs.
Taibu was on 54 when he was trapped by Ngoche, as an attempted reverse sweep went terribly wrong, and again he asked for a review but there was no reprieve this time and he was gone.Ervine has been Zimbabwe's best batsman at this World Cup and once he was set, there was no stopping him and, having been joined by skipper Chigumbura, they went about their business with both control and purpose.
There was a scare for the skipper when he was dropped off Tikolo, before he had scored, after offering a leading edge but he made good use of the life and he was the fifth Zimbabwe wicket to fall, after he had scored 38, as his men tried to push the target,Ervine was going well, the way he has done all tournament, and was on 66 when he was bowled by Elijah Otieno as he attempted another big hit but he had clearly made his mark and his huge six, hit while he was on one knee, which flew over mid-wicket, was probably the shot of the innings.
There were good contributions from Lamb, unbeaten on 17, and Utseya on 19, and Zimbabwe had done a good job for their big score.
The Kenyans never got going, from the moment David Obuya fell in the first over, and when Collins Obuya, who scored 98 against the Aussies, was soon run out for a duck, they were on the backfoot.
The Zimbabweans combined their good bowling spells with some fine fielding and Chigumbura's direct throw, from the covers to run out Otieno for five, showed their brilliance in the field.

Original_Search_Title: I Was Betrayed By Plateau PDP - Gogwim
Headline: Nigeria: I Was Betrayed By Plateau PDP - Gogwim
Body_Text:
Jos — Major Satty Davies Gogwim (Rtd.) is the senator representing plateau central senatorial zone at the national assembly. He spoke with journalists in Jos on the achievements of the senate under the leadership of David Mark; , his defeat at the PDP primary and other national issues. Achor Abimaje was there for LEADERSHIP SUNDAY.
With a little more than two months to go, what is your assessment of the Senate in terms of contribution to the development of the country?
We have done a lot; if you look at the amendment of the constitution, the amendment of the electoral act and all the other acts that we have put in place for the coming general election, we have done our best and if you see the number of bills we have rolled out within the past four years, we have surpassed the efforts of our predeccessors. The present senate is composed of egg-heads, very intelligent and educated people, who are experienced in various fields. Some of them came from various state houses of assembly, some moved from the House of
Representatives to the Senate while others like me came direct. To the best of my knowledge we have done our best within the past four years.
Ordinarily, there is no way you can be hundred percent perfect, people are bound to criticise you; there is no way you can satisfy everybody.
Do you think the PDP can still form majority in the national assembly with the gale of defection by most members to other political parties after losing the primaries?
People have the constitutional right to speak and to move from one party to another if they are not satisfied with the way they are treated by their party.
You can't stop them from moving. Sincerely, the PDP is the best political party in the country and moving from one political party to another came up as a result of internal democracy that was not strictly observed in some states. Some members of the senate who moved from the PDP to other parties were disenfranchised, they were not given the opportunity they felt they should have got.
Some of the states are being controlled by the chief executives, who would want to choose who becomes senators in their states. When it comes to that, there would be a fight between the governor and the serving senator, but with all these I still believe that the PDP can form the majority in the house if they play the right type of politics.
Did you get a raw deal during the primary that led to your defeat and despite that, are you still a party faithful?
If you say what happened during the primary was a raw deal, that would be an understatement. It was a high-wire conspiracy and betrayal, as a lot of people whom I had thought we were working together turned around within 48 hours to stab me in the back. I say this without any fear of contradiction; those who betrayed me know themselves, I know them and they do know that I know them, as well as what they did.
Consider the person who; he has never campaigned and has no structure on ground; he never for once went out to campaign, but they brought him to scuttle my ambition for 2011. They have their reasons they have their reasons and I know their reason: my rising profile is becoming a problem to a lot of them, so the best they could do was to scuttle my ambition. To answer the other part of your question, I am still hundred percent PDP, I cannot become a political harlot because I was betrayed. I will remain to ensure survival of my party, governor Jonah Jang and president Goodluck Jonathan.
Are you saying these 'people who betrayed' you are not trying to bring change into the state polity by involving younger men?
Off course, you wouldn't call the man a young man because he is older than I am. That is one. They were acting on a script and only God knows their reason for that. My closeness to the governor is making them uncomfortable and jealous. Anybody who comes out to contradict me will be regarded as one of those who betrayed me. Those who betrayed me are executives of the party, people whom we fought for, to ensure they were not dissolved. When you fight for a group of people and the same group decide to come after you, what do you call that? That is total betrayal. I am saying this because we knew what we did to sustain the present executives who eventually fought back against me.
Do you think the candidate who defeated you have the political strength to withstand former governor Joshua Dariye, one of thwe contestants, who is now on the cards of the Labour Party?
I will leave that to the candidate of the PDP, it is left for him to go out and convince people to vote for him and the people who pushed me out have to come out and work for him to ensure that he wins.
Looking at the pattern of voting at the primary, don't you think the conspiracy goes beyond the party?
Indeed, the conspiracy goes beyond the party. The party members were used by some people to scuttle my return to the senate. Going back to the senate after four years is an experience and given the idea that the Bokkos people were able to present one candidate twice, Mangu had one candidate twice and when it got to the turn of the Kanke they decided to remove me and brought in another person. What beats my imagination is, if these two local government created before us offered the same people on two occasions, why is it that when it got to my turn they worked against me, ensuring that I did not return. They have their agenda.
But some of your party members alleged that it was an attempt by the governor to impose you on the people that led to your defeat at the primary . . .
That is a big lie. Jang never imposed anybody on the party or electorates. If they are talking of Jang imposing me on people then the reason is not tenable. First, when did my opponent begin to campaign? Let me tell you something: I have not been fully integrated by the PDP because I came in 2009 and to them I probably have become an impostor but I have come to help build the PDP. I came with thousands of people to build the PDP. We decamped from the AC to ensure that we build the PDP. If they are saying Jang is trying to impose me on them that is unfortunate. I and my colleagues in the national assembly fought to ensure that those who betrayed me are left intact on their seats. Well the whole thing is an irony.
What is your view of the Jang/Longjan gubernatorial ticket?
As far as I am concerned,that is a good combination. Jang has actually done well for Plateau State; we have never had it so good during any administration. You can see the developmental program- mes being executed and those being completed. If given another four-year opportunity, there definitely will be lots of changes. For Mr. President you cannot tell Nigerians that he has not performed. Given another opportunity to serve for another four years would be a good omen for the country.
What is your perception of the leadership of the senate?
The leadership (qualities) of the Senate president cannot be questioned; he has the pedigree to lead the senate for the second term. In Benue state, especially in Idoma land, they know their son is number three in the hierarchy of Nigeria's ruling class today; there is no reason for them to bring forth another person who would not be given the opportunity to become Senate president. We are also saying that the people should give him the opportunity so that he can complete what he started; we know that he has the capacity to do it.
Nigerians consider Senator David Mark a lucky senate president, one who has successfully steered the affairs of the Senate without much hitches; what sets him apart from his predecessors?
David Mark has the pedigree and experience garnered over the years and has been able to control the national assembly. There are no bananas peels in his way to make him fall, because he has been doing all in his power to avoid getting into controversy. That is why he is quite different from his predecessors who hardly spent two years in office.

Original_Search_Title: Man Who Lost Family in Blaze Seeks Help
Headline: Kenya: Man Who Lost Family in Blaze Seeks Help
Body_Text:
Four days after he lost his family of five to a fire, Geoffrey Gichuki yesterday visited the scene of the tragedy for the first time since the Tuesday tragedy.
He walked like a man in his own world, wearing the same shoes he had on Tuesday at Kikuyu police station waiting for the news of his family at 'the hospital'. But yesterday he had decided to confront the ghost of his dead family by seeing the spot his wife Ann Mumbi and their four children perished in a mysterious blaze. The police are yet to establish how the fire started.
Gichuki went straight to the room that was their home, bent and picked a book. He gives a page a blank stare."This was my daughters book, you can see her handwriting," he says as he shakes his head reliving memories too grave to bear."Yesterday night I heard Eliza (his six-year-old daughter who perished) asking me to bring her bread," he recounts some of his now recurring nightmare. He still cannot believe his family is gone forever. He picks a burnt bunch of banana and recalls how he had done his monthly shopping of 20kg of beans, 8kg rice, two bars of soaps and 2kgs of sugar.
Suddenly he starts scratching the the spot he last saw the family bed. His fingers hurt. He abandons the quest. Instead of life, only burnt and charred napkins come forth. They were for his 21-day old son Ndung'u. He moves around in a trance. At the back of his mind plays out the final painful moments.
Gichuki tells of how his landlord Ben had a violent row with his wife on Monday evening and later burnt her clothes. A neighbour said Ben could not stomach his wife in a trouser.
Even after losing his family Gichuki has not been counselled. The trauma is tearing him apart.But despite the impact of the fire the area MP Lewis Nguyai has not bothered to show up: "I feel all alone in all this nobody has even come to help me," says Gichuki. Efforts to reach the MP was fruitless after his aide said he was in class the whole day.
Funeral committee chairman David Gachuruba says they have organized a fund raising meeting on Sunday at 3pm at Holy Eucharist Catholic Church in Kingeero."Already some well wishers like Paul Matagi promised to foot part of the coffin money coffins and we hope other people will come in," said Gachuruba.
Former Kabete MP Paul Muite made a public appeal for assistance so Gichuki can start life afresh and give a decent burial to his loved ones."That fire was started deliberately and we want a thorough investigation into this matter," said Muite.
Meanwhile the committee says they burial may be between Wednesday and Friday next week if they raise Sh100,000.

Original_Search_Title: Mother Throws Children Into River Nile
Headline: Uganda: Mother Throws Children Into River Nile
Body_Text:
Kampala — Shock and grief gripped residents of Kalagala village in Kayunga District in central Uganda when a 26-year-old woman allegedly drowned her three children in River Nile.
The woman, who lives a stone-throw away from River Nile, reportedly drowned the children aged six, four and three-month-old. The suspect's mother said her daughter left with the children to an unknown place on Monday at 5:30pm, East Africa Time.
She said the suspect separated with her husband a month ago. "After peeling matooke (bananas) for supper with her, she picked her three children and went with them to an unknown place. She didn't tell me where she was going, but I thought she was going to the neighbourhood because she looked happy," she said.
She added that after 45 minutes, an area resident came running to her home and informed her that her daughter had thrown the children into River Nile.
"After receiving the news, we rushed to the river where we found my daughter with her clothes drenched in water. She also confirmed to us that she had thrown the children into the river."
Charged with murder
Furious residents later arrested the suspect and handed her over to Kangulumira Police Station. Fishermen tried to search for the bodies, but only managed to retrieve one which was taken to Kayunga Hospital for a post-mortem.
The officer in-charge of Kangulumira Police Post, Mr Francis Jotono, said the suspect, who was later transferred to Kayunga Police Station, told police that she was forced to drown her children after her husband refused to provide food, medical care and clothing.
At Kayunga Police Station, the suspect, who appeared composed, said after drowning her children, she also jumped into the river with an intention of committing suicide, but said she was not aware of how she came out of the river alive.
"I was angry because my husband refused to provide all necessities to the children. The children had no food and were crying so I thought dying with them was the best option," she said.
The district CID, Mr John Dhabangi, said a search for bodies of the two children was still ongoing, as investigations continue. He said the suspect would be taken for medical check up to ascertain her mental state before being charged with murder.

Original_Search_Title: Why I Can't be Sexually Harassed - Odogwu Nonso
Headline: Nigeria: Why I Can't be Sexually Harassed - Odogwu Nonso
Body_Text:
Odogwu Michael Nonso is one of the rising acts in Nollywood. Reputed for his fatherly role in Opa Williams-produced 'Just the two of Us', he has carved a niche for himself in soap opera. Nonso, a graduate of Mass communication from Enugu State University of Science and Technology, speaks with Correspondent Emeka Umejei, on his accidental entry into acting and others issues in his life. Excerpts:
You are gradually creeping into the consciousness of most movie watchers as an actor. When did you begin to believe that you would one day become a household name?
Well, I think for me it started when I was a little boy but my first break into movies started when I was still in school in Enugu in 1996. I represented Enugu State in a drama competition, where I played the lead role of Emeka in the said drama.
How did it all start?
I was in class when a coursemate by name Lionel-Potts Johnson told me to accompany him to NTA Enugu and I inquired from him what the visit was for but he refused to say more. So I just went along with him and when we got there, I discovered that people were rehearsing for something serious. So I just sat and watched my friend go in to take a role, but as God would do his thing, the guy that was given the lead role was not getting his lines well so the director was not impressed with what the lead role actor was giving him. So he turned around and told me to come and try something out for him and I got up with a little fright in my legs and when I read the first time, I got it. I did it again and the third time and he now told me to act for him, but I told him that I could not act but he told me to forget whatever was making me to lose confidence in myself. When I started acting, I got my lines well that we did the drama and the rest is history, as people would say. Later I was told that the drama came second in a national competition.
So how much were you paid for this drama?
I got N1,000 considered a big money in those days .
One would say you had a humble beginning. How did your journey into the real world of acting begin?
After that I was told I would be signed for another drama and later movie and till now, I have not heard anything from the director. So later on, I was at home one day when a programme The Fresh Prince of Bel Air came on the network. Somehow after watching the programme on television, I started believing that I would want to be like Will Smith, the great American actor. Then I watched another movie by Denzel Washington another great actor. From then, I just concluded that acting was the thing for me. Back then acting wasn't so lucrative when you compare it to what is obtainable today.
So after school I went job hunting and bumped into a friend, Steve Onu, known more as Yaw in Wazobia 95.1 Fm. He told me that I could do much in Nollywood and promised to connect me to someone who would help me. He did and sent me to Pat Oghre also an actress and movie producer. That was how I got my first role professionally in the industry. That was in Everyday People, a soap opera in 2006.
How has it been with you ever since?
It has been okay and nice, especially when you compare yourself to the years when you were struggling to break into the fold of known actors. I thank God that I have been climbing up the ladder. After that, I did something in another soap The Maze. I had my first movie, Felicima directed by Henry Omereye. It's still not out. They are still in the studio doing some work. Finally, there was the big one that has kept me in the eye of the public; Just the two of us, an Opa Williams production.
How would you rate your rise and status in the industry?
I wouldn't know what my status is in the industry. That is left for my fan base to decide. I would say I thank God for using the people he used to take me to where I am now. It was not my making that I am where I am today. So I just thank God that he has answered my prayers. My growth in the industry is something that is in the hands of God.
What would you say were the sacrifices you made to get to the position you are today?
Bumps, difficulties were surely on the road through which I passed and finally made my big break. I also had issues with making out time for auditions and being promised a lead role and at the end of the day, you wouldn't get it. There were cases where you would go for auditions and would be told to pay for the roles before it was given to you and when some desperate ones eventually paid, they wouldn't get to feature in such movies.
So I thank God that he finally saw me through, and I am here to say but for him, where would I have been in the industry?
You are the first child in your family. Was there pressure from your parents that you were wasting your time jumping from one audition to another instead of looking for a well paid job in another profession?
Yes, there were times when such situations came up, but I thank God for the kind of family I have who saw reasons with me on why I needed to remain focused. In the beginning, my people thought I was wasting my time jumping from one audition to another but I told them that life is all about time and God saying this is your time. That, I believe has come to pass because I never had doubt that I would make it in the industry.
People say you have good physique. With all these attributes of a supermodel and sex symbol, do you see yourself pushing off the likes of Jim Iyke, Ramsey Nouah, and Emeka Ike from the scene so quickly because in Nollywood once a new face emerges they begin to seek out such personalities?
Well I don't know anything about being a hunk, sex symbol and all those superlatives you just used to describe me. What I know is that I thank God whenever I look at myself in the mirror I know he created me well and am okay with the looks he has given me. I know not many actors, who had made it big in the industry had all the combination that I have but I am not the type of person who goes flaunting it to make trouble. What I am after is to make a name for myself in the industry
As regards pushing the likes of Jim Iyke, Emeka and Ramsey Nouah aside to take their place, all that I leave in the hands of God. So, I am seriously praying for God to lift me up like them.
What are the big movies you have done in Nollywood ?
I just finished from a set. The name of the movie is the Wedding. I played the role of a guy called Tony. I also acted alongside the likes of Justice Esiri, and I just hope that it turns out to be a big movie when it hits the market.
Was your role in the movie a minor one or was it something close to what you are known for in Just The Two Of Us?
It is something close to my character in the soap, Just The Two of Us It's not a minor role.
Would you settle for a minor role if called up to act in a new movie?
Professionalism demands that any role you are given as an actor should be addressed and done well. It is wrong for someone to say I am a big boy why should I be given the role of a houseboy in a movie? When you do all this, people are in the right position to say this is the kind of actor who fits the role of a superstar.
Would you take up such roles?
Yes I will take up such role if it is challenging, and will add something to my knowledge of acting.
How have you been managing fame and the perks that go with it?
I try my best but it hasn't been easy because they think you are the same rich man they see on television. You are always trying to explain to people why you are not the same person they see everyday on television.
Has there been pressure from the women folks; with fame comes women?
Yes they are there, they always bother my head off, calling me asking long questions and trying to be very friendly and all that.
How did they get your number in the first place?
I don't know. They will say a friend of mine gave them my number and all that and you can't stop them from calling, but a line is drawn because I can't start dating my fans so as not to get a bad name.
Would you now say you have been harassed sexually by a woman?
No, I don't think so. Can't you see how big I am?
What is the craziest thing a woman has told you?
I cannot remember but I want to believe maybe a girl asking me if I could make love to more than three girls at a time.
And what was your reply?
I asked her if she had gone bananas.
How do you start your day, what do you do to make your day complete?
I say my prayers in the morning; eat very light food, then I relax, read a book, watch a movie and when my girlfriend is at my crib we just stay together doing our thing.
You are so confident mentioning your woman. Would you want to bring her into the big picture?
I don't think she needs that kind of publicity right now, but when the time comes, the right people will get to meet her in a big way.
Who is the real Michael Odogwu Nonso?
The real person in me is humble, down to earth easygoing guy, although I could really lose it because everyone has his own flaws.
Have you ever been heartbroken?
Yes I have.
Does that mean you are the emotional type?
Somehow but I won't say am too emotional.
Have you broken hearts?
No I don't think I have broken any female hearts.
Why are you an exception, because it is taken that men are fond of breaking female hearts?
I don't know; maybe unconsciously but I can't remember breaking any hearts.

Original_Search_Title: Statistics Bureau Confiscates Illegal Brew
Headline: Rwanda: Statistics Bureau Confiscates Illegal Brew
Body_Text:
Kigali — Rwanda Bureau of Standards (RBS), yesterday, in a surprise inspection exercise, confiscated illicit alcohol in Gatsata Sector, a Kigali City suburb.
This was after the agency was informed that the area was awash with substandard food, beverages and other commodities.
RBS officials carried a surprise operation in which one, Catherine Mukantabana, was found to have been supplying substandard alcohol in various suburbs of Kigali which she branded 'Agashinguracumu'.
The local brew has been on the market for over three years, the officials were told.
According to Dr. Mark Cyubahiro Bagabe, the Director General of RBS, the manufacturer did not seek approval from the body.
"First of all, the product lacks manufacturing and expiry dates, and they work under poor hygienic conditions. We can't determine the nature of the brew," said Cyubahiro, who led the inspection exercise.
"We are doing this to protect consumers and we will not compromise with the required minimum standards. Our responsibility is to help you upgrade the business as we protect the population from the dangers that may arise because of this."
The brew is sold in another brewer's bottles, which is contrary to the regulations of RBS. "They were using Heineken bottles, which is illegal," Cyubahiro said.
Mukantabana said that she was in the process of upgrading her brewing standards and to abide with the rules and regulations of RBS.
"We were going to ensure that our alcohol meets RBS standards," she said, adding that her brew is manufactured from bananas and millet, and its alcoholic content is 7.5, which, she said, was not very dangerous.
However, according to some residents, the brew had already killed two people.
"We know people who died from stomach complications after taking this alcohol; personally I develop funny feelings whenever I drink it," said Theodore Niyirora.
Mukantabana has been producing over 300 crates of beer per week, with each bottle going for Rwf200.

Original_Search_Title: Capone is in Charge of the Bank, But We Won't Revolt
Headline: Uganda: Capone is in Charge of the Bank, But We Won't Revolt
Body_Text:
Will the protests we are seeing in Kampala, and lately, Jinja over the alleged theft of votes by Team Museveni in the February elections lead to a full-blown national uprising?
I think that, long-term, President Yoweri Museveni's government will get in serious trouble - but not over election rigging. Now you could say that in 1981 Museveni went to the bush to start a rebellion after the December 1980 elections were allegedly rigged in favour of Milton Obote's Uganda Peoples Congress.
To which the answer is that Museveni's success against the Obote, and later short-lived regime of the Okello generals, would not have resulted in victory without something else - an economy in a terrible state.
The effect of the economic collapse of the Idi Amin years, were made worse by the Tanzanian-led war that ousted the Field Marshall in 1979. Obote II's biggest mistake was that the UPC government wasted 1981 and 1982 trying to control prices, and beating up market vendors and smugglers who were the only source of goods. In a situation of scarcity, UPC activists were burning markets, which made economic conditions worse!
When Obote started reforms, it was mid-1983, and they were timid and muddle-headed. The short of it is that inflation continued its flight into orbit, speculation was rife, and honest producers and workers were devastated further. The hope that the post-Amin period would be better was dashed.
If Museveni had found the peasants in Luweero making millions out of their coffee, bananas, and investing in new homes, his rebellion would have failed. With the rural economy in ruins, Museveni was able to offer them a hope of a better tomorrow.
Ugandans are not united by election thefts, and even torture and killings ala Amin regime. This is because these events are particularised. If your neighbour is abducted by security goons and murdered, and your family is safe, you will commiserate with the bereaved, but not risk your own to fight the people who wrecked their lives. That is one reason not too many people will rush to rescue a wailing neighbour if he is attacked by robbers in the night.
Economic problems, on the other hand, affect everyone - including the people who support the vote-cheating president who is mismanaging the country.
In this election, the Museveni government broke into the national Treasury to win it--spending nearly a record $600m to buy victory. Now, as even those who are not Nobel Prize-winning economists would have predicted, prices have run out of control. The people who were bribed to vote for the system, find that the "kitu kidogo" they got is not enough to buy goods at present prices. In other words, all the money the Museveni campaign splashed on the election, in the end, might bring more economic hardship for both the NRM and pro-opposition man and woman in the street.
It will take some time, but it is when economic hardship combines with political discontent, that Kaguta's son will be in trouble. For me, the President's win-at-all-costs strategy belies something more troubling. One reason Uganda's economy has been performing reasonably well and maintained growth, is that there has been a group of smart mandarins at the Ministry of Finance, and shrewd bureaucrats at the Bank of Uganda who have been a counter-balance to the excess of the politicians, especially the spendthrift State House.
Some of these fellows are controversial, and many of them will not be your choice for pen pal, but they have done this country well; helping push for policies that kept inflation and exchange rates stable; holding the government back from squandering the future; and doing old-fashionably stupid things like printing money.
What this election told us is that these mandarins and central bank bureaucrats have lost to State House. In many ways Al Capone is all but in-charge of the bank in Uganda today.
There is also another constant in Ugandan politics. No regime has ever granted concessions (as the colonialists did in the face of various protests including the boycott of non-African shops of the 1950s), or fallen without the active support of Buganda. Buganda support was crucial for Amin's coup in January 1971; and for his fall in April 1979. And it was critical to the defeat of Obote II and the Okello military junta.
As it happens, most of the rest of Uganda is suspicious of Buganda's activism, fearing that the kingdom wants to go it alone in the end. I sense that, finally, a time has come when a Buganda that is jaded with anti-Ganda sentiment will no longer help topple regimes in Kampala, without assurance that it will get respect and a decisive seat at the table in the political order that follows.
Until that time comes, Buganda will not abandon and turn against Museveni in numbers that are large enough to help effect regime change. If it takes generalised political grievance, a ruined economy, and Buganda's unconditional support to overthrow a regime in Uganda, you can say Museveni's risk factor is at about 35 per cent--there is another 65 per cent to go.

Original_Search_Title: INEC, Injunctions And National Assembly Unfinished Business
Headline: Nigeria: INEC, Injunctions And National Assembly Unfinished Business
Body_Text:
Cudjoe KPOR, economic analyst with Independent Newspapers Limited looks at the hidden banana peels which could mitigate against next month's elections...
The late Justice Chukwudi Pats Acholonu of Court of Appeal, about a year after the annulled June 12, 1993 elections, looked critically at its chaotic aftermath and refused to exculpate his constituency: Judges in Lagos and Abuja were competing among themselves for who would issue the most absurd injunctions, he admitted bluntly. Nearly, two decades later, it is obvious, the political class - no, the classless politicians - have learnt nothing from the anarchy. As a result, some of them are hiding behind the judiciary again to cause electioneering chaos.
The National Assembly's widely applauded constitutional and Electoral Act amendments have taken care of most of the problems which hamper holding free, fair and credible elections in the country: INEC is now on first line charge, so it cannot be tied to the apron strings of a manipulative Presidency. Nobody can sue INEC to stop conducting any election, among others, recommended by the Justice Mohammed Uwais Panel.
Obviously, the eminent Uwais Panel believed, wrongly in retrospect that once they put all the checks on the illiterate godfathers who pretend to "own" the political parties because they fund them heavily as investments recouped after victory, internal democracy work was done. Perhaps the national lawmakers would, themselves, have seen the loophole which illiterate politicians, incapable of learning from past mistakes, engendered for the June 12, '93 debacle.
But the lawmakers were too busy scheming to write themselves by the backdoor into the National Executive Councils (NEC) of the parties. So, not one out of 469 national legislators saw the loophole which can still mar the elections, despite their widely-applauded amendment. Of course they nearly vitiated it all with their "legislative rascality," as the Governors' Forum branded their fiasco of NEC member amendment.
Barely two weeks to the start of the general elections, as many as 200 court cases, 70 of them ex-parte injunctions by the politicians, have tied up INEC: Last minute substitution of candidates' names are flying with conflicting court orders with the same jurisdictions. The cause of litigation by any aggrieved candidate may be traced to the established parties like Congress for Progressive Change (CPC) led by General "Sai" Buhari who appears to have shot in the lead; or from PDP which is bursting at its seams. The spoilers such as ANPP, APGA and ACN are on the sidelines. Then the mushroom parties with no more than 100 members also have their strife.
The serious parties have met the name-submission deadlines prescribed by Electoral Law and INEC Guidelines. The snag is, the slowest court is the rate-determining step of the entire INEC preparations for the elections. In that INEC is obliged to print the pictures and names of the candidates in every constituency on the ballot papers... If any party is not decided on which candidate is selected for the election only two weeks to the polls, how would INEC get the ballot papers ready? Maurice Iwu's INEC got the preparations thoroughly messed up because it waited till the Supreme Court awarded victory to former Vice President Atiku Abubakar a week to the polls!
Needless to say, the illiterate, incompetent, undemocratic and unenlightened party leaders and their clueless politicians are about to spoil the free and fair elections conducted by a well-prepared INEC for all - once again. Frankly, the judiciary has no constitutional role to play in the election process. Afterwards, the judges swear in the winners. However, its role as the only civilized mechanism of settling all disputes in society leaves it prone to the weak contestants to drag the judiciary into the mainstream political arena to mediate in disputes which competent, enlightened and democratic party leaders and members resolve rationally because they are worth their onions. Without the latter, the entire process may be heading full speed into chaos.
NASS has the unfinished business to immune the electoral umpire from all election-related disputes through the courts. In other words, no court ex-parte orders should infringe on INEC's independence or tie it up from performing its duty. All parties must resolve their internal disputes intelligently and democratically to submit their names by the stipulated deadline - or the party forfeits participation in that election. NASS must take the one more step to forbid all politicians from dragging the judiciary into the political arena - till the post-election tribunals. There is now loose talk of stopping the judiciary from overturning victory to curtail judicial ambush; they can only order re-run elections if there were gross violations in the winner's victory.
The suggested amendment has the effect of compelling every contestant to head for the soap box to campaign for votes, rather than lay "judicial ambush" for the victorious candidate who might have campaigned in all the relevant constituencies only to lose because of infringements.
A legal expert says the number of court injunctions is not important. What should be worrisome is the speed with which the courts vacate the ex-parte orders. Ordinarily, that should not take more than 48 hours. What are INEC's options as the election date draws near? Unilateral, illegal fiat? Or the Attorney General's mass nollo prosequi? Or... another postponement of the elections?

Original_Search_Title: Kakooza's Jokes Are Serious
Headline: Uganda: Kakooza's Jokes Are Serious
Body_Text:
The shrewd and scheming proposer of a 7-year term for elected officials has a knack for successfully pushing unsavoury agendas.
James Kakooza, the junior Minister of Health and Kabula County MP has the face of someone about to crack a joke; with narrow-slit eyes on an elongated face dominated by a strong jaw, thick moustache, and wide mouth with thick lips that open often to reveal a gap between his incisors.
In reality, however, although most of the topics Kakooza raises initially sound comic, they rarely end up being a laughing matter. Rubanda West MP Henry Banyenzaki knows this by now. He dismissed Kakooza as a "comedian" in 2006 for suggesting that Museveni should run for a fourth term. But just as Kakooza proposed, Museveni won a fourth term on Feb. 20.
Even before the storm settles on that one, however, Kakooza has launched his next project; a proposal to raise the term of parliament, the presidency, and elected local government councils from the current five years to seven.
Like Banyenzaki and others before, when Kizza Besigye, the leader of Uganda's biggest opposition party; the Forum for Democratic Change, was told about Kakooza's proposal he laughed heartily. He said Kakooza is a joke and merely NRM Secretary General and Security Minister Amama Mbabazi's ' errand boy'.
According to Besigye, Kakooza's idea is meant to divert people's attention from the just concluded "flawed electoral process and other challenges that Ugandans face".
But sitting in his simple but neat office on the 4th floor of the Ministry of Health Headquaters, 47-year-old Kakooza argues passionately for his proposal saying it will enable elected leaders deliver better on their manifestos.
Kakooza is a relative newcomer into politics. He was a freight forwarder, clearing agent, accountant, and managing director before joining politics as an MP for Kabula County in today's Lyantonde district in 2001.
Since then, he has built his political career around attracting the President's attention. He literally worships at Museveni's feet. If he had an opportunity to bend and tie Museveni's loose shoelace like the late Brig. Noble Mayombo did, Kakooza would do it without a second thought.
Kakooza is a politician with an instinct for survival regardless of the public opinion.
When the debate raged in parliament over whether MPs should be given Shs 60 million car loans (equivalent to a government medical doctor's salary for five years) and the public accused the MPs of fleecing the tax payers Kakooza was unbothered.
"I joined politics knowing that such things would happen," he told journalists, "I am like a fishmonger. A fishmonger does not fear flies, so I am unbothered about the public's outburst referring to us as greedy hyenas."
He is, therefore, not averse to controversy.
In 2003, he was instrumental in drumming up support for a "third" presidential term for Yoweri Museveni that led to amendment of the constitution to lift the term limits.
When he began the third term campaign for President Museveni, many dismissed Kakooza as a joker. He hit headlines when he wrapped himself in dry banana leaves, esanja and started asking other members to join him in rooting for a "third term" for President Yoweri Museveni, which was dubbed ekisanja. The campaign led to the lifting of the presidential term limits in the first major amendment to the 1995 Constitution. It effectively ushered in a life presidency in the country.
After the 2005 referendum on switching to multiparty democracy, the same Kakooza suggested that the term for the 6th parliament be extended to prepare for transition from the Movement system to multiparty politics. His proposal was thrown out. But he was not about to stop floating weird ideas favouring the ruling NRM party.
He became controversial when he co-authored a minority report to exonerate Security Minister Amama Mbabazi who was accused of dubiously selling land to the national pension scheme, the NSSF, in the so-called Temangalo land saga. He was appointed minister of State for Health soon after.
Now, even before the tempers from the 2011 presidential polls calm down, Kakooza is already toying with another controversial scheme of extending the constitutionally recognised five-year term office for the president, parliament, and local government leaders to seven years.
Some describe him as a schemer, others as a shrewd politician who takes advantage of opportunities as time presents them. But he describes himself as a "consistent and foresighted politician". He always floats his weird thoughts soon after elections. His third term for Museveni proposal in 2003 was just after the 2001 elections.
Soon after Museveni was elected for his third term in 2006, Kakooza's pursuit to please the regime again went on rampage; he started collecting signatures of NRM legislators in support of Museveni's fourth term bid. He started wearing yellow t-shirts with imprinting agitating for a fourth term for President Museveni. Many MPs were shocked. But it's just a few days after Museveni was elected for a 'fourth' term in office. Kakooza has now hatched a seven year term of office.
Critics say seven years of a bad leader can be a nightmare for citizens. But proponents say it will enable politicians concentrate more on delivery of services and promises in manifestos which are constrained by time.
Some circles think Kakooza is a front for the NRM and an attention seeker. But whatever one may think of Kakooza, most of his schemes have been embraced. Given that record, therefore, Kakooza might get his seven years.

Original_Search_Title: Not Ready to Hang Up My Soccer Shoes
Headline: Uganda: Not Ready to Hang Up My Soccer Shoes
Body_Text:
Dear Tingasiga: The nimble dance and speed with which the late [David] Otti, son of Ogaba treated soccer always suggested that his skeleton was made of lighter material than the usual bone matrix that carries the carcasses of lesser beings.
Lord, the way the man danced with the ball so blithely as though his bones were made of titanium or, aluminum! Or was it carbon fibre from Acholi? News of Otti's death 10 days ago brought memories of the golden age of Ugandan soccer, when men like him and Obua, Ouma and Oundo, brought us national pride and a "yes we can" attitude to life.
The 1960s were the years of my own forgettable contributions to Ugandan soccer. I had taken up the sport on the manicured compounds of Mparo Health Centre in Kigezi, where I spent most of my early childhood. My older brother Byaruhanga and our beloved friend James Nyindombi never let a single day pass without us chasing the balls. For the most part we would play with balls we had fashioned out of dry banana leaves. Occasionally, a visiting teenager would bring a rubber one we called tenesi (tennis) enabling us to enjoy the faster speed of such balls which were resistant to rain and dew.
I played soccer as a pupil at Kihanga Boys' Primary School, though not a single triumph has survived my fading memory. For some reason I have a vivid recall of my disastrous efforts, details of which we must spare you.
It was at Kihanga that I learnt the skill of placing a safety pin in my team's goalpost in order to kugisiba (to lock it), a sure method of stopping the opponents from scoring. I also learnt what strategically placed herbs and shrubs could do to the opponents' goal post and its minder. What still puzzles me is how it was supposed to work given that both sides employed the same magical precautions against each other.
What is clear is that such rigging on the soccer fields, which was encouraged by our teachers, prepared us for the democratic elections that lay ahead. Why engage in free and fair competition when you can sneak to victory using illegal means like safety pins and deadly herbs?
Whenever the magical efforts did not yield the desired results, we would fall back to plan B, unleashing kibokos (whips) on our victorious opponents, under the direction of our teachers. We did not know it at the time, but those post-soccer match battles were preparing us to manage Kiboko Squads 40 years down the road. The motto was Northcotian: Either we win or they lose.
When we moved to Kabale in 1963, I discovered a world of soccer that I had never seen before. Where soccer in the village had been a haphazard affair, with players kicking the ball towards the goal posts and hoping for the best, the Kabale footballers played with style and seeming calculation.
Nobody was as gifted as Shem Bageine, a student at Kigezi College, Butobere, with the nickname of 'Twist' because of the way he danced with the ball as if with a damsel in a Kabale pub. Twist was the Kadenge of Kigezi, the Pele of the Highlands, dancing and floating with the ball as he coasted towards the hapless goalkeeper of the opposing team. The only time Twist's efforts would be occasionally thwarted would be when the man tending the goal was Kabuliya, a Kabale Kid who was rumoured to possess hands made of magnets against a leather ball.
Kabuliya, like Bill Matama, the premier goalkeeper of Kigezi College, was born of an Asian father and an African woman. The two dominated goal-keeping so much that it was believed that there was something unique about the food their mothers fed them on which enabled them to excel in the role. What pretence I had had as a budding soccer player vanished after I watched Twist and Bill and Kabuliya and Kabashekye and a host of other talented Kabale boys play the game with magical ease.
It was at King's College Budo that my love for soccer came to its zenith. King Edison Arantes do Nascimento, better known as Pele of Brazil, was ruling the game. Then there was the venerable Joe Kadenge of Kenya, who flew like a rocket as he sprinted towards the goal posts.
Police and ambulance vehicles were christened Kadenge, because they moved as fast as he did. Could I, with practice and patience, master the game the way these men had? None of my contemporaries will recall any of my exploits on the football field, but that is because their memories, just like mine, are failing.
I am here to tell you, Tingasiga, that I was God's gift to Ugandan soccer. Whenever the ball came hurtling towards me, I would close my eyes, aim my foot in its general direction, and kick the thing with supreme elephantine strength.
That most of the balls would roll on past me is neither here nor there. I vividly recall a great moment when our opponents' defence had scattered, leaving the way clear for me. My foot collided with the ball and, for a split second, I felt that my Pele moment had arrived. I gave it my best, aiming the ball directly between the goal-posts. Someone must have put a safety pin in that goal-post. How else to explain my directly aimed ball being retrieved from the bushy field way, way to the left of the football pitch?
That that was the end of my soccer career. I became an accomplished spectator, occasionally kicking fellow soccer lovers as I got carried away by the action on the field, especially with boys like Henry Kyeyune and Frederick Nkonge displaying a wizardry that still brings a smile 40 years later. But I am not ready to hang up my shoes. I owe it to Nyindombi and Otti and Obua and Kabuliya and Bill Matama and all my soccer heroes who are now dead, to play one more game and redeem my reputation. Yes I owe it to my father who was a soccer wizard during his days at Kigezi High School in the 1940s.
Next time I am in Uganda, I will invite Shem Bageine, who now presides over a highly successful and respected property management empire, to meet me in Kabale Stadium. Before our kinsmen and before the eyes of the international soccer fraternity, I shall claim the title of Twist from him.
Dr Mulera is a Consultant pediatrician and neonatologist

Original_Search_Title: The Perfect Church
Headline: Nigeria: The Perfect Church
Body_Text:
Author: Ebi Akpeti
Reviewer: Yemi Adebisi
Publisher: AuthorHouse
No of pages: 67
Ebi Akpeti, author of Growing Pains and Castrated, collection of short stories that have defeated its rank and file in content, narrative and potency, has written another book entitled, The Perfect Church. The new book reflects holistically, the rate at which the so-called 'saints' in our churches have deceitfully and cleverly 'killed' the scriptures to satisfy their flesh and physical needs. The author uses her prowess to present to her world the story of deceit, repentance and forgiveness. The front cover design and the potable size of the book is the first attraction. It relieves the tension of boredom. Secondly, the choice of the title, The Perfect Church, ignites a thought that tempt a reader into the ocean of reality. Thirdly, the soft language of the author, coupled with the lexical structure on each page, concludes a reader's friendly network.
As an expert in narrative stories, Akpeti voluminously adapted suspense and sanctimony to drive home the network of moral laxity that has narrowly found its way into the pulpits of the end-time churches in Nigeria. If you have never read any of her collections, this might end up to be an 'unput-down-able' publication, going by the strength of each story and its soothing effect on the crime rate in the house of God. The book has been adapted for screen play by Wale Adenuga Productions.
Most pastors like Pastor Benson in this book will need to read Akpeti's report. Pastor Benson, pastor of The Perfect Church, is ever delighted each time he sees the notice placed in front of his church: 'This is a church where only perfect people worship.' The church claims to be holy and perfect but unfortunately, most of the arrowhead of the congregation, including workers and pastor, are busy wallowing in sin. The pastor is loved by all and each family takes its turn to bless the 'holy servant of God' with 'riches and gold.' He lacks almost nothing.
The story of Mr. and Mrs. Ojo will probably settle certain scores in homes where it seems issues bordering on fidelity and deceptiveness exist. Every member of the church, including the singles and the married pray to be like them but unfortunately, they are living in bondage. Their relationship starts out of passion. When Mrs. Ojo meets Mr. Ojo, she is married and so is he. Her former husband, Baba Michael, a personal assistant to an expatriate loses his job and she becomes the bread-winner. Something leads to another that the man becomes a thorn in her flesh, spending his wife's money on women and girlfriends to the extent of having affairs with these lovers on their matrimonial bed. Mr. Ojo, the newly found lover, eats in Mrs. Ojo's shop and is privileged to hear the sad story of her dying home. He is moved by compassion and becomes an emergency advisor to her to cushion her social pains. Along the line, he impregnates Mrs. Ojo and the rest is story.
Sister Angela, the choir leader and head of single fellowship is found to be highly committed in the church. She is one of the 'holier than thou' workers who may find it difficult even to sit beside a man in the church for fear of impending 'contamination.' Unfortunately, her secret love affair with Valentine Banana, which nearly leads to her death, is a lesson for today's singles. Angela leaves the church to meet Banana in a hotel; though she never allows him to sleep with her, but his freedom to kiss her earns her a gift of many wads of dollar she rolls into her purse.
Brother Tony, head usher, is a very clever and committed worker. The church holds him in high esteem because he appears to be an honest man and a cheerful giver. Unfortunately, he is the perfect usher that converts church fund into personal account, but nobody is aware of his evil agenda. For ten years, he steals half of every collection the church raises.
The perfect pastor, Pastor Benson, is not married because women do not appeal to him. He knows he can never marry a woman. He has special anointing for healing and deliverance and this makes him to command special respect among his congregation and other pastors. The strange relationship of the pastor and a male member of the church, Leo, started after a counselling session. The sin of homosexualism arrests the anointing and fame of the pastor. He cannot do without having Leo around. Nevertheless, in return, he pays Leo's school fees. Anytime he wants Leo, he is ever available since he knows that nothing goes for nothing in life. "After that day, he offers to take up Leo's school fees on the condition that he keeps quiet about their affair. They have this affair for over three years during which he spends lavishly on Leo to protect their secret affair." (p. 29)
At the height of these compounded evils, Bishop Williams, a highly respected servant of God visits The Perfect Church and God reveals to him that about five people in the church are chronic sinners. "There is Grace for five People." (p. 35)
It is Brother Tony and Mrs. Ojo that honour the altar call. Sister Angela imagines what will happen to choir if she openly confesses her sin. She prefers to shed tears secretly and asks God for forgiveness. Pastor Benson cannot surrender to the altar call, thinking about what people will say. The last straw that breakes the camel's back happens when Leo comes to expose the secret dealings of the pastor at one of the church's services after Bishop Williams has gone.
What happens to Sister Angela? Are the Ojos able to reconcile their marriage? What is the end of the homosexual pastor and his church? Check these and other fascinating stories from the book.
There are several grammatical and typographical errors in the book. The commonest error is wrong use of punctuation marks especially coma, semi colon and full stop. Page 3 should have been "...so we must really honour him," sister Angela said. But we have" "...so we must really honour him. Sister Angela said (no full stop). Mr. and Mrs. Ojo is frequently written as Mr and Mrs Ojo. Page 14 reads "...for them and sent then" as against "...for them and sent them." In page 24, "She had a baby boy" should have replaced, "She had the baby which was a boy." In page 28, it should have been "why have you not taken a wife?" and not "why have you have not taken..." In page 31, "it was the third time she was treated like that" should have replaced "it was the third time she was treating like that." Then, "there is an ocean of mercy for three men and two women..." should have replaced, "there is an ocean of mercy for three men and two women" in page 35. Page 36 is supposed to have been "...with the other choir members" and not "...with the other chorus members." In page 41, it would have been more appropriate to say "no one special yet but I am still searching sir" as against "no one special yet but I am looking sir."
Apart from these, the book is one of the best story books in recent times that face reality in practical terms on good moral, discipline, godliness and noodles of end-time church.
It is recommended for all families, churches, mosques, ministers of God, workers and all lovers of good stories.

Original_Search_Title: 'Don't Accept Parallel Vote Count in Zambia'
Headline: Zambia: 'Don't Accept Parallel Vote Count in Zambia'
Body_Text:
Former chairperson of the Electoral Reform Technical Committee Mwangala Zaloumis has said the parallel vote tabulation being proposed should not be accepted in Zambia because it will bring conflict in the management of elections and is a recipe for chaos.
Ms Zaloumis has also asked the civil society organisations agitating for a parallel vote tabulation to immediately stop the plan because it amounts to usurping the functions of the executive.
Ms Zaloumis, who was spokesperson of the dissolved National Constitutional Conference (NCC) and is a prominent lawyer, said non-governmental organisations (NGOs) were either in support of the Government or the opposition and coming up with PVT pits such groups on opposite sides ready to fight, depending on the election outcome.
Ms Zaloumis advised that governance should not be left to speculation because the same civil society organisations would come back with another agenda of wanting to have their own executive, Parliament and oversight institutions if they were allowed to go ahead with such a dangerous idea.
She said all functions of the executive should be left to the institutions responsible for discharging them such as the Electoral Commission of Zambia (ECZ).
Ms Zaloumis said the civil society organisations calling for PVT were bent on sparking a revolt against the election results because they would cause anger in any group that would lose.
The Anti-Voter Apathy Project (AVAP), the Southern African Centre for the Constructive Resolution of Disputes (SACCORD) and Caritas Zambia, among others, have lately led agitation for PVT but stakeholders have objected to their intentions because of failure to provide convincing reasons for the system.
Ms Zaloumis said it would be wrong to accept anything that would attract dispute in the management of the elections instead of leaving such a role to ECZ.
"We are not a banana Republic where anyone wants to do what they want.
Tomorrow they will come and say we are setting up our own parliaments, our own MPs and eventually government. Does that happen even overseas?" Ms Zaloumis said.
She explained that Zambians must rely on the functions of ECZ as long as Government was able to involve all political parties and stakeholders in the management of the elections.
Ms Zaloumis wondered in whose benefit the PVT would be if the civil society organisations would exert their support to a parallel structure outside ECZ and the Government.
She said the role of the civil society everywhere was to render support through monitoring, civic education and getting involved in the programmes that Government was implementing and this must be done within the law and in the interest of the nation.
She said NGOs did not have any authority to manage the elections because the law recognises ECZ as the only institution mandated to lead the elections in the country.
"What exactly do they want? Do they want to declare candidates? Does that happen in other countries? Who will they report to? Do they intend to start their own process? That is recipe for chaos," Ms Zaloumis said.
She said the civil society organisations should not just promote such an agenda for the sake of gaining mileage and challenged them to state which countries had ever accepted a system where more than one institution were announcing winners in an election.
Former Mineworkers Union of Zambia (MUZ) president Andrew Mwanza said it was a waste of time and resources to have a PVT and that the citizenry should have more faith in the ECZ to discharge its responsibilities.
Mr Mwanza said Zambia had been independent for more than 40 years and that people should instead call for the reinforcement of existing institutions to promote effective elections.
"Let us discuss other important developmental issues and not matters that may just cause confusion in the country," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Renewed Igbo Quest for PDP Chairmanship
Headline: Nigeria: Renewed Igbo Quest for PDP Chairmanship
Body_Text:
Some Nigerians believe the Igbo are the most disorganised people in the country, so much so that any run-of-the-mill political commentator will not consider his work done until he has lectured them on how best to remain relevant in Nigeria's murky political waters.
If you are in doubt, you will be told how badly they fared when the Senate Presidency, the third most important office in the country, was zoned to the South-east. Because five Igbo Senators occupied the position in eight years, they would, most disingenuously, argue that no further evidence is needed without bringing to bear on their hollow analysis the role President Olusegun Obasanjo's inordinate third term ambition played in reducing the office to the axiomatic game of musical chairs.
Another half truth being bandied as evidence that the Igbo are incapable of holding high political office is that in less than four years, they produced two national chairmen of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) - Prince Vincent Ogbulafor and Dr. Okwesilieze Nwodo.
While that is true, the Igbo-bashers conveniently forget that the North Central zone produced four chairmen - Chief Solomon Lar, Engineer Barnabas Gemade, Chief Audu Ogbe and Dr Ahmadu Ali - in the eight years Obasanjo dominated the land.
They fail to appreciate how Obasanjo's self-succession gambit and President Goodluck Jonathan's single-minded determination to undermine the PDP constitution by running for the Presidency in April are one and the same banana peel (apologies to Dr Chuba Okadigbo). Ogbulafor was kicked out because he insisted that the rules of the party should not be sacrificed on the altar of political expediency. Nwodo, who was drafted in by Jonathan to ensure that he got the PDP ticket was also shoved aside when his fidelity to the project was doubted.
Now, that is not to say that the Igbo don't have their problems. Achieving consensus on any issue remains a daunting task. But the fact remains that all other groups competing for power in Nigeria have their soft-belly as well. For instance, it only took a determined Obasanjo to prove that the idea of a monolithic North is a mirage, a point Jonathan is forcefully re-echoing.
Obasanjo even succeeded in demystifying the Yoruba, who pride themselves as being the most politically sophisticated group in the country. He not only orchestrated the demise of Afenifere, the most potent group in the region, he also created a counter force, the Yoruba Council of Elders.
So, the Igbo are no exception. They are as susceptible to manipulation by a mischievous leader as any other group. Truth be told, majority of Nigerian political elite are jobless men and women, who survive on government patronage. With a presidency as powerful as Nigeria's, anyone who is in power can easily manipulate the system to promote his selfish interest.
So, while it is true that the Igbo have their problems, that should be no reason to push them out of the country's power loop.
Unfortunately, that seems to be the game being played by the rest of the country that still see the Igbo as a people conquered in a civil war and who must not only be kept away from the power loop but also ensure that the Southeast is made a waste land for decades as agreed in a meeting held in London between Northern and Southwest leaders with officials of the British Government shortly after the civil war in 1970.
This explains the intrigues surrounding the office of the National Chairman of the PDP, a position which was zoned to the Southeast in the party's subsisting zoning arrangement. The resignation of Nwodo on January 18, 2011, suddenly created a vacuum and left the Southeast as the only geopolitical zone without a first-tier position in power-sharing, rotation of key political offices in Nigeria in line with power sharing agreement of the party.
There has been discordant tunes since the former Deputy National Chairman (North), Dr Bello Haliru Mohammed, stepped in as the Acting National Chairman as to whether the post should revert to the Southeast immediately or after the April elections. There have also been some discordant tunes on whether Ndigbo, particularly the Governors, want the position or not and if they do, whether immediately or after the polls.
Ndigbo, no doubt, want the position back, not after the elections in April, but immediately. The Governors, except perhaps Martins Elechi of Ebonyi State, are all agreed on this. It is because of this that the Eastern Leaders Forum, in consultation with the Southeast PDP Committee on selection of the party's new National Chairman, the President General of Ohanaeze Ndigbo and other Igbo professional associations and interest groups set up a five man screening committee with Professor Anya O. Anya as chairman, and Dr. Chukwuemeka Ezeife, Senator Sylvanus Ngele, Comrade Peace Obiajulu, Chief Eddy Onuoha and Dr. Ifedi Okwenna as members.
The committee has gone ahead to screen eight out of the 11 aspirants that indicated interest in the job on a ten-point screening agenda that included aspirant's membership profile in PDP; familiarity with current party leadership structure; participation in party activities/contribution to primaries; commitment to success of 2011 elections; respect for, belief and participation in Igbo affairs; experience in party organisation/administration; integrity, principled behaviour and intellectual ability; ideas to ensure permanence of one Nigeria; personality/charisma/poise; and past public offices held and performance.
During the two-day screening late February, Dr Chinwoke Mbadinuju, former PDP Governor of Anambra State, Barrister Okey Muo Aroh, founding member of the party and the man who had the singular privilege of announcing the name of the party on a public address system to the teeming conveners seconds after the party was born in 1998, Dr Ezekiel Izuogu, former chairman of the Social Democratic Party, the biggest of the three parties that formed PDP, Professor Ihechukwu Madubuike, former Minister of Education, and Health, Chief Agunwa Anaekwe, former Speaker of the House of Representatives, Chief Chukwuemeka Kanu, Chief Rex Onyeabo, and Chief Dan Ikechukwu Ulasi, were interviewed.
Though Chief Ojo Maduekwe, former Transport Minister and Secretary of the party, showed interest in the job, he did not come for the screening, having been appointed Deputy Director General of the PDP Presidential Campaign Organisation. Former Senate President, Anyim Pius Anyim, the man who has been in the minds of most Igbo people since the position became vacant, did not present himself for screening, fearing that his showing interest in the position might jeopardise the position for the Southeast.
Anaekwe came tops having scored 71 points with Ulasi following closely on 70 points. The names of the two candidates have been forwarded to Senator Ike Ekweremadu, Deputy Senate President and highest ranking Igbo political office holder, who in turn has given same to President Jonathan.
For too long, the Igbo have had their leaders chosen for them from outside. Since he who plays the piper dictates the tune, such Igbo leaders have paid more allegiance to their masters rather than promote Igbo cause. This is what promotes the disorganisation which has become the swan song of those who claim to know the Igbo better than they know themselves.
For too long, the impression has been created consciously, but falsely, that the Igbo cannot come together, not to talk of agreeing on one position when the issue is political. This project undertaken by Prof Anya and the authentic Igbo leadership is not only a renewed quest to create a new dawn in the politics of Ndigbo, but also to dispel such utterly misleading insinuation.
Having done their job, it is now left for Jonathan, if indeed he believes in equity and justice, to ensure that another Igbo man is appointed Chairman of the PDP. And the time to do so is now, not after the elections.

Original_Search_Title: Elections Are Part of Country's Culture
Headline: Rwanda: Elections Are Part of Country's Culture
Body_Text:
Although genuine democracy requires more than just elections, elections and democracy complement each other and thus elections are central to promotion of democracy.
Peoples, Nation and State or even a Family setup must have an organized system for the smooth running of its affairs and such a system is established through a direct or indirect representation at various levels.
It is difficult or almost impossible for an entire people within a Nation, State or Country to engage in governing the affairs of their state.
Thus resorting to govern through representatives selected or elected by the people in accordance with rules and standard laid down by the law or other due process accepted by societies.
The due process of law must be adhered to in order to avoid irregularities and chaos. The systematic processes of election or selection will guard against unnecessary tensions and conflicts which could lead to undesirable consequences, hence the concept of free and fair elections.
A free and fair election can be said to be a direct dividend of democracy and vice-versa, because, there can only be free and fair election where there is democracy, and there can never be democracy when there is no free and fair election. In his book titled 'The Spirit of Laws' Montesquieu states that; "in the case of elections in either a republic or a democracy, voters alternate between being the rulers of the country and being the subjects of the government.
By the act of voting, the people operate in a sovereign (or ruling) capacity, acting as "masters" to select their government's "servants". Also, by the same act, citizens are exercising one of their fundamental human rights.
Elections are also important because they endorse legitimacy of government. Without regular, free and fair elections, governments may not be able to claim legitimacy either to govern the citizens or to enter into international obligations on behalf of the people.
"The liberty of man in society is to be under no other legislative power but that established by consent," says the English scholar, John Locke.
The history of elections goes back to the old and ancient Greek and Roman States.
