Elections also were conducted in ancient Athens, in Rome, and in the selection of popes and Holy Roman emperors, as well as the selection of Caliphate in the ancient Islamic history after the death of Prophet Muhammad. In the contemporary world, elections can be traced back to the emergence of representative government in Europe and North America in the 17th century.
Although, across Western Europe and North America, adult male suffrage was ensured almost everywhere by 1920, the woman suffrage was not established until 1928 in Britain, 1944 in France, 1949 in Belgium, and 1971 in Switzerland, to mention but a few. In the USA, African Americans were not allowed to vote until the passage of Voting Right Act in 1965.
The aim of the US Voting Right Act of 1965 was to give broader election and voting rights to Americans, including women and African Americans.
The history of elections in Rwanda is one associated with manipulations, violence and destruction. The first attempt to conduct elections in Rwanda was in the 1950s when the colonial administration was under the UN pressure to prepare Rwandans for self rule like was happening all over colonies.
According to research done by the Rwandan Senate, the introduction of universal suffrage in 1956, limited to able-bodied adult males at sub-chieftaincy level, but at the higher level, the status quo established by the Belgians remained intact.
The next elections were parliamentary, held concurrently with the referendum on whether to retain the monarchy or change to a republic in 1961. These were however held in an atmosphere of fear, characterized by manipulations, violence and destruction.
Tens of thousands had been and were being killed at the time while hundreds of thousands went into exile under the pretext of the so called social revolution of 1959.
The sham elections of 1961 brought to power MDR-PARMEHUTU with Kayibanda as head of state, replacing the interim one, Mr. Mbonyumutwa Dominic, with UNAR's few legislators as the opposition. When the Tutsi refugees attempted an armed incursion into Rwanda in 1963, all the UNAR legislators were killed by Kayibanda and thereafter Rwanda became a one party repressive state up to early 1990s.
When Habyarimana captured power in 1973, he presided over a one party rule system in which he was always the sole presidential candidate, always getting 100% of the vote. In the 80s, Habyarimana introduced legislative elections but based on one party rule.
The parliamentarians were exclusively Hutu males. Such is the situation that the post genocide government inherited.
The above scenario, coupled with the 1994 Tutsi genocide explains why the post genocide government approached the issue of elections cautiously; deciding to start with grassroots elections in 1999, election of leaders at district level in 2001, referendum on the new constitution in 2003, followed by parliamentary and presidential elections later that year.
2003 is a turning point in the history of Rwanda because it marked the beginning of regular elections both at local and national level, with a new constitution and clear guidelines set by the National Electoral Commission.
Even then however, political parties could only compete for elective posts at the national and not at local level. The situation changed a couple of years ago and all political parties are now free to compete in the local elections. When one looks at the timeline of the elections that were conducted from 1999 to date, the picture of how far Rwanda has come in terms of elections becomes vivid.
March 1999, election of local councilors and representatives In Late 1999, a Commission on Legal and Constitutional Affairs was established to consult the population and propose a new constitutional that would put Rwanda on a democratic journey;March 2001, the then communes were transformed into districts and mayors and councilors were elected;
August 2003, Rwanda held the first democratic presidential elections ever in her history. October 2003, Rwanda held multiparty parliamentary elections;
August 2008, 2nd Parliamentary elections since the adoption of the new constitution in 2003;August 2010, 2nd presidential elections since the adoption of a new constitution.
February 2011, local leaders' elections from the village, cell, sector, district to Kigali city level;Late this year 2011, Rwanda will hold the second senatorial elections.
The positive aspect of the conduct of elections in Rwanda is better present by international elections observer teams. For example, Mr. Cashman of the EU observer mission noted a positive conduct of elections in Rwanda after the 2008 parliamentary elections using the following words; "The process of democratisation in Rwanda since the end of the Genocide is remarkable. The elections were conducted in a peaceful environment, a fact that deserves to be commended.
Also the massive election of women to the chamber of deputies is a world wide record that Rwanda can highly be proud of".The 2008 legislative elections saw Rwanda breaking the record of female representation in parliament at 56 percent and subsequently brought the first woman Speaker in the Chamber of deputies in the region.
No wonder then that the EU observation mission concluded that the 2008 elections signified all elements required to meet international election standards, and best practices.
Fred Cowell of Consultancy Africa correctly sums it up after the 2008 parliamentary elections that "the peaceful management of the election and high turnout indicate that a voting culture has been instilled into Rwanda and that support for the participatory institutions."
The Commonwealth Observer Team had this to say about the 2010 presidential elections; "Overall Commonwealth Observer Teams were very positive regarding the conduct of the opening and voting processes in 2010 presidential elections. Voters turned out in extremely large numbers (reflecting the NECs stated turnout rate of 97.5%) and conducted themselves in an extremely calm and orderly manner. On the two days before the election women and men from villages were observed cleaning and decorating designated Polling Centres.
There was evidence of a strong level of commitment, with the men mainly responsible for outside decorations using material easily available (such as banana trees). Women washed classrooms and decorated the Polling Centre with local fabrics, flowers and grasses, reminiscent of a wedding or community festival.
Polling Stations were well prepared for their task, in that they were largely ready to open on time, had all relevant materials and were well organised.
The Voter Lists seemed to be of a high quality, as most people found themselves on the list, providing for both universal suffrage and the right to vote. Polling officials were checking ID in most cases and following voting voters had a finger inked and their Voter Card was stamped."
Rwandans appear to have zealously embraced the practice of holding regular elections clearly convinced that it is not only an indispensable part of the democratization process but also a fundamental right.
Evidently, even among modern democracies the concept of a free and fair election or even universal suffrage took centuries to evolve and become part of their values and culture. It is the same process that is taking place in Rwanda but at a faster and focused pace and as Fred Cowell puts it, "the peaceful management of the election and high turnout indicate that a voting culture has been instilled into Rwandans and their participatory institutions."

Original_Search_Title: Civil Servants And the National Anthem
Headline: Nigeria: Civil Servants And the National Anthem
Body_Text:
When the news filtered into the Senate Press Centre last Wednesday that an ambassadorial nominee had failed a simple test of reciting the national anthem, most of us simply laughed it off.
The reason why we couldn't believe the news was because the ambassadorial screening by the Senate Foreign Affairs Committee was deemed a mere routine exercise.
Why, you ask? Very simple: The 25 ambassadorial nominees are all career officers and so, it was inconceivable that a civil servant could fail to recite the national anthem, more less the national pledge.
But that was exactly what happened at the Senate Hearing Room 1 in the White House Complex last week.
Ijioma Bristol, an ambassadorial nominee and the wife of a serving Ambassador failed to recite both the national anthem and the national pledge before the Senate Committee on Foreign affairs.
She appeared before the Jibril Aminu-led Foreign Affairs Committee for the routine screening as one of the 25 career ambassadorial nominees of President Goodluck Jonathan.
As part of the routine questions panel members ask any nominee who appear before them, Ijioma was asked to recite the first stanza of the national anthem.
Originally from Anambra State, Ijioma was asked three times to recite the first stanza of the national anthem. She failed on all occasions.
To proffer soft-landing for the ambassadorial nominee, the committee asked her to name the capital of Jigawa State. Again, Mrs Bristol failed the task.
Committee Chairman Jibril Aminu in reaction to her failure recommended that she be sent to do a crash-programme on national anthem.
Apparently angered by Ijioma's responses to the questions posed to her by members of the committee, Aminu said: "Madam, this is not a joke. This is a problem and you know the media people are here. It is not a joke at all that you cannot recite the national anthem and the pledge."
A member of the committee, Anthony Manzo, who is himself a former Ambassador and Vice Chairman of the Information and Media Committee asked Mrs Bristol to name different ways of diplomatic communication and articulate the Vienna convention.
Again, Ijioma failed. She replied Manso: "I cannot quote the content of the Vienna convention" but she was able to respond to questions on diplomatic communication by mentioning memoranda, minutes and note verbal as the types of diplomatic communication.
Some members of the Committee attributed her lukewarm performance to the tension posed by the prospect of appearing before a Senate panel for screening, while others believed that the woman lacked the requisite knowledge of diplomatic relations.
As I said earlier, it's inconceivable that a civil servant who was primed to represent her country at the very highest level would not even know the simplest of the
Aba Aji recants on 2015 Igbo Presidency
In a dramatic u-turn on Thursday, embattled Presidential Adviser on National Assembly Matters, Mohammed Aba Aji on Thursday gave the all-clear to agitations for Igbo Presidency in 2015 just as he clarified opposition to Presidential assent to the Freedom of Information (FOI) Bill.
Aji had earlier in the week foreclosed the possibility of an Igbo Presidency in 2015 with an insistence that Vice President Namadi Sambo would naturally succeed President Goodluck Jonathan should he win the ballot in April.
Uproar from Nigerians forced Aji to beat a fast retreat. In fact, in refuting his earlier comments, the Presidential aide noted that his support for a Sambo Presidency in 2015 was strictly his personal opinion.
To press home the fact that his comments were simply his own, he even addressed a press conference where he reiterated that the Igbo have every right to aspire to the Nigerian Presidency as enshrined in the country's Constitution.
His words: "I strongly believe that my Igbo brothers and sisters have equal rights with all members of other ethnic groups of our nation to seek any public office including the exalted office of the President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria.
"The statement as it relates to Vice President Namadi Sambo is entirely my personal view, which he neither desired nor solicited. I, therefore, hereby withdraw it and regret any misunderstanding that it may have generated."
Not done yet, Aji had earlier in the week ran himself into trouble when , in an interview published in a national newspaper vowed to ensure that the President does not sign the Freedom of Information Bill into law just as he expressed disappointment in the House of Representatives for passing the Bill.
The House had penultimate week approved the FOI Bill after being in the cooler for eight long years. Aji added that should the Senate concur with the House in passing the Bill, he would be left with no option but to advise the president not to assent to the bill.
A penitent Aji, however, explained to Senate Correspondents why he toed the extreme position.
"On the issue of the Freedom of Information (FOI) Bill, what I did was to draw attention to the conflict that in my view, it is likely to have with the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria as it relates to the Oath of Office and the Official Secrecy Act, which is yet to be repealed.
"I also tried to make the distinction between the FOI Bill, which seeks to compel public officers to reveal official matters and the Freedom of Press which is already enshrined as Section 39 of the Constitution."
Aji's recant, notwithstanding, the public is still baying for his blood; with calls for his sack as strident as ever.
Mark: Race for Senate Presidency Begins
Last Saturday, the race for the Senate Presidency commenced in far away Otukpo, Benue State. It was a day the incumbent David Mark kicked off his 2011 senatorial campaign for Benue South.
Mark has been in the Senate since 1999 and in 2007 beat his former governor and kinsman George Akume to emerge as the sixth Senate President of the Fourth Republic.
Many had given Mark just some months to last in office. Some attributed the skepticism to his demeanor in his past public life.
To the many skeptics who kept turning their noses at him, Mark has not only surpassed the timeline given his Presidency, he has made history in the Fourth Republic as the only Senate President to stabilize in office and serve out a four-year tenure.
More than his predecessors, Mark made sure he banished the proverbial banana peel. The race this time around would be keen because in four years, the political class now knows what it means to occupy the office the Senate President.
Whether Mark would have it easy as he did on June 5, 2007 would be known in just three months.

Original_Search_Title: Freight Forwarders Bemoan Govt Interference in CRFFN
Headline: Nigeria: Freight Forwarders Bemoan Govt Interference in CRFFN
Body_Text:
Members of the National Association of Government Approved Freight Forwarders (NAGAFF) have expressed disappointment over the interference of the Ministry of Transport in the affairs of the Council for the Regulation of Freight Forwarding in Nigeria (CRFFN).
Founder of the association, Dr. Boniface Aniebonam, in a press statement, accused the Ministry of hijacking the CRFFN to the extent that the employees of the council now take orders from the Ministry. He also said that following this, some employees of the council see themselves as civil servants under the employment of a government agency.
NAGAFF had gone to court to seek an order restraining the Ministry of Transport from interfering in the affairs of the Council. Aniebonam expressed dismay that both Ministry of Transport and CRFFN have ignored advice from well meaning Nigerians, including the Nigerian Shippers Council (NSC) and maritime lawyer, Mrs Mfon Usoro, on how the council should be run.
"It is very sad that despite the ongoing judiciary enquiry into the improper composition of CRFFN, the Council leadership has continued to act as if Nigeria is a banana Republic", he said. He called on the Minister of Transport, Alhaji Yusuf Suleiman, to call officials of his Ministry and the Council to order, so that they will stop interfering with a private sector initiative.
He said that a situation where officials of the Council see themselves as government officials, apparently because of the involvement of the Transport Ministry, negates the objectives of the body.
He added, " that our council is established by law does not in any way rank it as a government agency in the industry, because it is made up of private sector practitioners voted into the council by their colleagues with some nominations from government agencies" Aniebonam recalled that the council has been receiving grant from the Ministry, adding that this was not the best, since it will create an opportunity for the Ministry to interfere in its affairs.
He threatened that the Economic and Financial Crime Commission (EFCC) will soon be invited on the issue, maintaining that the association was not different from other professional bodies like NBA, ICAN, NMA and others.
"For some of these gentlemen and lady to parade themselves as public servants and using Nigeria's coat of arm in secret and in their previous complimentary cards and letter heads are not only criminal but cheap and lacking in knowledge", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Dolphins See Home Defeat for JUTH
Headline: Nigeria: Dolphins See Home Defeat for JUTH
Body_Text:
Dolphins manager Stanley Eguma has vowed to compound the woes of Nigeria Premier League (NPL) bottom club, Juth when both sides clash at the Rwang Pam Stadium this weekend.
The Healers are in dire need of points as they currently sit bottom of the log with 11 points from 17 matches.
"We want to win in Jos," Eguma told SuperSport.com.
He, however, noted that the game could be a banana skin for his side and urged his players to prevent themselves from being lured into a false sense of security.
"When you play against a relegation-threatened side, you have to be on your toes and I am expecting us to play with our usual intensity and aggressiveness. If we do that, we will win" he said.
A 1-1 draw with 3SC on Sunday in Abeokuta saw Dolphins stretch their lead at the top of the NPL to five points over the chasing pack though Sunshine Stars, in second place, have a game at hand.
With just seven goals conceded in 17 games this season, the Port Harcourt based-side have by far the best back line in the land.
However, the spirit in the camp is low as players of the runaway leaders of the NPL are muttering over financial obligations yet to be met by their sponsors.
There are revelations that players and officials are owed salaries, sign-on fees and match bonuses for the 2010/2011 season.
And with the situation threatening to derail the club's storming start to the season, a passionate SOS has been sent to the sponsors.
"We want them to help us. We have families and most of us are the bread winners.
"If we get at least, some of the money owed us, it will go a long way in alleviating our plight," a player of the club who pleaded anonymity said.
Eguma, who confirmed the situation, said he had been reliably informed by sports commissioner, Boma Iyaye, that the Rivers State Government would soon offset the outstanding debts.
"I have spoken to the commissioner and we have been assured that the players would soon smile.
"It is important that we remain motivated because things like this can affect morale and bring down the performance of the boys. The players don't want that," he stressed.
Dolphins lead the NPL standings with 37 points from 17 matches and travel to the Rwang Pam Stadium to face Juth for their next game this weekend.

Original_Search_Title: Rogue-Politicians Free to Contest April Poll - EFCC
Headline: Nigeria: Rogue-Politicians Free to Contest April Poll - EFCC
Body_Text:
Abuja — Chairman of the Economic and Financial Crime Commission (EFCC) Farida Waziri yesterday denied reports that it was planning on publishing list of corrupt politicians
Waziri who spoke with State House Correspondents, at the Presidential Villa yesterday however noted that those in need of the list of corrupt politicians can get them on request from the ant- corruption agency.
She said: "Some persons are under investigations and any government institution like the Independent Electoral Commission (INEC) or political party are at liberty to requests for the list".
The EFCC boss also disclosed that no party has so far come forward to requested for the list.
She said: "I never said EFCC will publish list of corrupt politicians what I said was that we are investigating certain persons and if, and when, anyone requests - these are public documents, they are in court, the people appearing in court are in the public domain. So if anybody asks for it - Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), INEC -we will give them, even though its on our internet".
Mrs. Waziri however refused to comment on the lavish reception accorded ex-convict, Bode George after his release from Kirikiri prison recently, "I don't think I should answer that, there is no comment on that. You have seen comments from other Nigerians, its not for me. So I won't answer that".
On the recently passed anti-money laundering bill, the EFFC boss expressed happiness that it has finally been passed as Nigeria was already under threat of being blacklisted.
She said: "Lack of that law was almost getting Nigeria blacklisted, so we are very happy about that. With the passing, when it is gazzetted, and Mr. President signs it into law, we will go back to the drawing board, together with banks and other relevant agencies, to map out new strategies.
"It is very useful for us and even the international agencies would take us seriously because you cannot do this without the relevant laws to back it up.
"We are not a banana republic; we are a democracy and we work within the rule of law".
The Senate had Monday, passed the amended Anti Money Laundering Bill to replace the extant Money Laundering Act 2004, recommending a maximum of 10years and minimum of five years for offenders, in addition to fines that may be applicable. The bill as passed by the Senate is slightly different from the version forwarded to the National Assembly from the Presidency .
The Senate version has raised the bar for cash transaction that can be made outside a financial institution from the N500,000.00 and N2 million to N5 million and N10 million for individuals and corporate bodies respectively.
The Senate also raised the amount of international transfers that ought to be reported to government agencies from $2,000 to $10, 000 for individuals. It also places a duty on bankers and other financial institutions to report international transfers of funds exceeding $10,000 to the Central Bank of Nigeria (CBN) from where the records can be accessed by security operatives.
Transportation of cash or negotiable instruments in excess of $10,000 or its equivalent by individuals in or out of the country shall be declared to the Nigerian Customs Service, section 3 of the act prescribed.
Defaulters of this section "shall be liable upon conviction to forfeit not less than 25 per cent of the undeclared funds or negotiable instrument or to imprisonment of not less than two years or both.

Original_Search_Title: EFCC - No Request for Corrupt Politicians' List
Headline: Nigeria: EFCC - No Request for Corrupt Politicians' List
Body_Text:
Chairman of the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC), Mrs. Farida Waziri has said there has not been any request to the Commission to supply names of corrupt politicians.
She also stated that at no time did she say she will publish list of corrupt politicians
"I never said that; I said we are investigating certain persons and if, and when, anyone requests - these are public documents, they are in court, the people appearing in court are in the public domain. So if anybody asks for it - PDP, INEC - we will give them, even though its on our internet", she clarified.
Waziri who came to the Presidential villa in Abuja in Wednesday and fielded questions from correspondents also refused to comment on the reception organised by the Peoples Democratic Party for Chief Olabode George just released from prison for financial crimes.
She replied on her reaction to the reception "I don't think I should answer that, there is no comment on that. You have seen comments from other Nigerians, its not for me. So I won't answer that".
On the passage into law of anti money laundering, she lauded it as very useful as the international community will now take the country seriously in her efforts to check money laundering.
She said relevant agencies would come together once the law is harmonised and assented to by President Goodluck Jonathan to fashion ways of ensuring its strict implementation,
Waziri explained that "Lack of that law was almost getting Nigeria blacklisted, so we are very happy about that. With the passing, when it is gazetted and Mr. President signs it into law, we will go back to the drawing board, together with banks and other relevant agencies, to map out new strategies.
"It is very useful for us and even the international agencies would take us seriously because you cannot do this without the relevant laws to back it up. We are not a banana republic; we are a democracy and we work within the rule of law".

Original_Search_Title: Simba Eye Top Spot
Headline: Tanzania: Simba Eye Top Spot
Body_Text:
AFTER dropping two crucial points at Toto African last weekend, reigning champions, Simba, will attempt to return to the top of the Premier League with a victory over Mtibwa Sugar when the two giants face off at the Uhuru Stadium in Dar es Salaam on Sunday.
A win for third-placed Simba, who have so far garnered 30 points from 15 outings, would see them open a two-point lead at the top of the Vodacom Premier League.
Patrick Phiri's men currently lie one point behind first and second-placed, Azam FC and Young Africans respectively, and face a banana-skin test against in-form Mtibwa Sugar, who are fourth on the table with 30 points from 17 outings and striving to reduce the gap with top three teams.
A win will also be a perfect morale boost for the Msimbazi Street outfit ahead of their coming epic battle with archrivals Yanga due next Saturday.
Simba have suffered a torrid week. The title holders saw themselves slip from top to third slot following a 2-2 draw against Toto African in their previous league encounter, coupled by victories to closest title challengers, Azam and Yanga.
Mtibwa fought for the necessary three points in their midweek clash, ironically against Toto African, beating the rocky city boys 2-1, thanks to Salum Machaku's 79th minute winner.
The victory saw them remain in touch with the sides at the top of the table. First round's fixture was an thrilling encounter with Simba prevailing 1-0.
Rashid Gumbo's 50th minute spot-kick decided the tense affair at the Jamhuri Stadium on September 29, last year, despite an excellent attacking performance by Mtibwa Sugar.
Determined to retain the crown they clinched last year in a memorable unbeaten campaign, Simba need to hit a better form and be more clinical especially with their work in the final third against Mtibwa, who would be looking to maintain their impressive away form.
Encouraged by a hard fought win against Toto, Mtibwa Sugar coach Tom Olaba will be hoping to invoke the same fearless attitude that earlier this month saw his side claim the scalp of Simba's archrivals Young Africans at the Uhuru Stadium.
But the Morogoro based outfit, will have to do it without their first choice goalkeeper Shaaban Kado who saw a red card against Toto African for allegedly insulting referee Judith Gamba.
With just four rounds to go, Azam FC are surprisingly in control of the 12-team league tied on 35 points with Young Africans, but the later occupy the driver's seat courtesy of goal-difference superiority.
Azam FC coach Stewart Hall is living to his billing and has seen his formidable and expensively assembled side challenging the usual top two-Simba and Yanga.
However, Simba have an advantage as they have two matches in hand, including this evening's tie. Should they win both they will open a six points lead. But that remains on papers as the Msimbazi street side would be required to translate such an advantage into results for them to stand a chance of defending their title.
The league is set to continue on Monday with Ruvu Shooting hosting Young Africans at the Jamhuri Stadium in Morogoro.

Original_Search_Title: Snack Time With Abundant Sambusas
Headline: Ethiopia: Snack Time With Abundant Sambusas
Body_Text:
Sellers of sambusas, the deep-fried dough triangles filled with, most commonly, lentils, have expanded their services to such an extent over the past year that the snack has become available at a wide variety of different outlets across the capital city.
While it used to be sold mostly by pedlars from plastic covered trays or bamboo containers, going about town, many stationary selling points have sprung up lately. While some of these take the form of mini cafés, serving tea and other baked goods, like biscuits and ambasha bread, the newest form of sambusa sellers is rudimentary takeaway counters on the street.
Abeje Negash opened his food stall about a year ago. The shop appears to be constructed from corrugated steel sheets from which a saleswoman donning a cap and apron serves customers across a glass case displaying the food. Inside are deep fryers in which she cooks the raw sambusas, which have been previously prepared.
Located on Bole Road, in front of Asay Primary and Secondary School in Olympia, it is unsurprising that half of the shop's customers are students.
"We are hungry after school," Hananiya Teferi, 19, a final-year student at the school, told Fortune. "This is better than other places, it is tastier, and especially the fish filled ones."
The fish is popular, and at around 4:45pm on Thursday, February 17, 2011, the shop had sold them out, with mountains of vegetable, meat, and rice filled sambusas left over, forcing Hananiya and his friend, Mikias Endale, 18, to settle for enjoying vegetable filled ones, instead of their favourite.
There are two kinds, or forms, of sambusas on offer on the capital's streets. Small outdoor cafés, as well as normal cafés and even big hotels, tend to sell lentil filled triangles of which all the dough is compacted around the filling. However, pedlars and smaller, arguably less hygienic, street shops, as well as outlets like Abeje's, stock smallish triangles that are dwarfed by a large, crunchy dough appendix attached to the bit containing the filling, which sometimes include other fillings, despite lentils being the most common.
"Customers seem to like the crunchy bit," Abeje told Fortune about the two shapes and his reason for preferring to sell the one.
Degefa Yazhe, 27, a father of two, has been peddling sambusas around the Stadium area for about two months. He sells about 50 of the same shape as Abeje's, filled only with lentils, in a workday that lasts from 3:00pm to 8:00pm, at two Birr apiece.
Wearing a clean white cap and button-up shirt, his sambusas are likewise presented in a neat manner, on a clean plate covered with clean plastic to protect the food from the dust and exhaust fumes that particularly characterise the minibus stop that is his most frequent place of transacting.
By the look of things, people in want of a snack can hardly have any concerns of hygiene to prevent them from buying and eating his sambusas. As he constantly moves about, his coming to the customer is an added convenience.
Degefa Yazhe is a travelling salesman, selling about 50 sambusas daily by walking around the Stadium area.
Convenience serves Hananiya, the student, who buys and eats sambusas with his friends about three times per week. They usually buy from Abeje because his shop is located suitably close to their school and to the minibus taxi stop they use to go home.
A rice or vegetable sambusa costs three Birr, while one with meat is four Birr. They are surprisingly filling with all that dough, and the oil gives off on the paper with which it is served for customers to hold onto. Alternatively, to take them away, one can carry them in small plastic bags.
"Our quality is good and we use good oil," Abeje claimed to explain the price for the about 100 sambusas he sells, on average, daily. "The dough is also prepared by machine, instead of by hand as some small sellers do. These are still good even after three days."
His prices are significantly higher than that of other sellers, who tend to stick to filling their sambusas with lentils, a less expensive filling than meat or fish, and generally charge around 1.50 Br apiece.
The whole structure of Abeje's shop seems sturdy and the equipment clean.
"The quality is good," confirmed Hananiya. "I have never been sick from it."
The way sambusas were sold in the past was not inviting, Abeje, an accountant, explained. While the pedlars are still in operation, the newer, cleaner, stationary service points have attracted many new customers to the snack, he claimed. It was this possibility that persuaded him to set up shop.
Like Abeje's, Genet Nesro's sambusa shop is also located around a major transport hub, at Bole Dildey. She has been selling sambusas, along with other baked goods and tea, for about a year.
Aside from daily labourers, her customers consist mostly of taxi drivers and assistants, a.k.a. woyalas, around Bole Dildey, as well as street children and homeless people, around Bole Medhanialem, according to Genet.
On Tuesday, February 15, 2011, business was slow, as it was a public holiday, Maulid, and many people stayed at home. However, for some customers it was business as usual.
"My brother and I are regular customers of Genet because we do not take long lunch breaks," Bruk Anbesse, 21, a woyala who works with his brother, a taxi driver, told Fortune. "The sambusas are also good and make for a cheap meal."
As the snacks are prepared in advance, it is ready to be eaten instantly, and customers do not have to wait at all. One would be hard-pressed to find another filling lunch or breakfast for such a low price. Even a piece of bread and a banana cost two Birr, and the price does not include a hot drink.
Genet charges 1.65 Br per sambusa, and 80 cents for a cup of hot sweet tea. The sambusas, which she bakes herself, are served out of plastic trays, covered with clean plastic. Unlike those sold by Abeje, the dough of Genet's firmly encloses the lentils inside.
Prior to opening her little street café, she used to work as a clothes washer, but, as a single parent, needed more flexible work hours to take care of her two boys, aged six and eight.
The money she earns now is not great as she only makes a 20-cent profit on both the sambusas and tea, she claimed.
"Daily, I earn an average ranging between 10 Br and 16 Br daily," she told Fortune.
As sambusas have had to undergo rebranding to make it more attractive to customers who used to shun the snack in the past, another reason for Abeje making it a little more expensive than the average 1.50 Br or two Birr most street vendors charge, is the public's perception.
"People are suspicious of cheap things," Abeje claimed.
To illustrate his point, he related a story of a customer who used to buy sambusas for his family from Abeje, but expected them to object to the "low" prices and told them he bought it from New York Café, also in Olympia. They happily accepted this, until one day when his father happened to be passing by at the same time as his son making the purchase. "So, this is New York then, yes?" he asked.
Abeje's shop, also located at a busy transport hub, is at its busiest from around 4:30pm, when schools close and civil servants finish work; they make up a substantial number of his customers, having a snack on their way home, he claimed.
The zero preparation time arguably makes sambusas Addis Abeba's fastest food. Given the fillings, and putting the oil from being deep-fried aside, it is not entirely unhealthy. Aside from this, and the comparatively low prices, the availability of the snack has become so widespread that it is real convenience, and not beyond serving as a meal. Being such a substantial bite, it is certainly filling enough for this purpose.
Elias Gebreselassie, Fortune staff writer, contributed to this story.

Original_Search_Title: '20 Percent' Grabs Seven Kili Music Awards Nominations
Headline: Tanzania: '20 Percent' Grabs Seven Kili Music Awards Nominations
Body_Text:
A FAST-RISING 'Bongo flava' star, Abbas Hamis best known as '20 Percent' will lead the pack at the upcoming Kilimanjaro Music Awards after landing seven nominations for the 2010 awards.
'20 Percent' will compete for Best Male Artist, Best Male Singer, Best Song Writer of the Year, while his two hit tracks 'Ya Nini Malumbano' and 'Tamaa Mbaya' are up for Best Song of the Year and Best Afro Pop Song. Upcoming singer Linah also dominates the nominations with five nods, including Best Female Artist of the Year, Best Female Singer and Best Upcoming Artist, whilst her 'Atatamani' track is up for Best R&B song, and her 'Bora Nikimbie' competing for Best Zouk/Rhumba Single of the year.
Nasib Abdul Juma, famously known as Diamond, winner of three awards during the 2009 edition held at the Diamond Jubilee last year, 'Upcoming', "Best R&B Single" and "Best Song of the Year" awards is still in for more recognitions and scored four nominations, including Best Male Artist, Best Male Singer of the Year, while his 'Mbagala' hit will be among the tunes vying for Best Afro Pop Single.
Diamond is also nominated in the Most Popular Video of the Year category. Songbird Lady Jaydee and gentle singer Barnabas landed four nominations each for the fan-voted gala set for March 26 in Dar es Salaam.
Hit track Mama Ntilie by Gelly wa Ryme feat AT & Ray C also stood out after bagging four nominations, including Best Song of the Year, Best Collaboration Single and Most Popular Video of the Year.
And this year's nominees for Best Female Artist of the Year include Lady Jay Dee, Mwasiti, Shaa and Khadija Kopa. Male Artist of the Year category has attracted the likes of 20 Percent, Ali Kiba, Barnaba, Diamond, AY, and Belle 9. Best Male Singer: 20 Percent, Barnaba, Ali Kiba, Banana, Belle 9, Diamond.
Best Female Singer: Lady Jaydee, Linah, Mwasiti, Khadija Kopa, Shaa. Song of the Year: Mama Ntilie- Gelly wa Ryme feat AT & Ray C, Sina Raha-Sam wa Ukweli, Ya Nini Malumbano, Tamaa Mbaya- 20 Percent, Mkono Mmoja- Chege & Temba feat Wahu.
Taarab Song of the Year: My Valentine- Jahazi, Top in Town- Khadija Kopa, Acheni Kuniandama- Isha Ramadhani, Langu Rohoni- Jahazi, Mama Nipe Radhi- Isha Ramadhani.
R&B Song of the Year: We ni Wangu- Belle 9, Nikikupata- Ben Poul, Hello- Hussein Machozi ft Maunda Zorro, Atatamani- Linah, Kisiwa cha Malavidavi- Z-Anton.
Hip-hop Song of the Year: Propaganda- Fid Q, Karibu Tena, Joh Makini, Ukisikia Paah- JCB ft Fid Q& Chidi Benz, Usije Mjini- AY& Mwana FA.
Best Swahili Song of the Year: Shika Ushikapo- Mapacha Watatu ft Mzee Yusuph, Laptop- Extra Bongo, Kauli- Twanga Pepeta, Pongezi kwa Wanandoa- Akudo.
Best Reggae Single: Sauti ya rasta- Ras Rwanda Magere, Misingi ya Rasta- Warriors from the East, Sayuni- Jhiko Man, Ujio Mpya- Hardmad ft Enika & BNV.
Best Ragga/ Dancehall Song: Benjamin wa Mambo Jambo ft AT, Action- CPWAA, Dully, Nwear, Ms Trinity, Far Away- Big Jah Man ft Richard, My Friend- Benjamin, Kiuno Weka Busy- Jet Man. Best Band Rapper of the Year: Fergsuon, Khalid Chokaraa, Toto ze Bingwa, Kitokololo, Msafiri Diouf.
Best Hip-hop Artist: Fid Q, Joh Makini, Ngwear, Chidi Benz, Godzilla.
Best East African Single: Nitafanya- Kidumu & Lady Jaydee, Kare- P-Unit, Songambele- Alpha ft AY, Vuvuzela- Goodlife, Kasepiki- Bebe Cool.

Original_Search_Title: Businesses Count Losses in Dar Suburb After Blasts
Headline: Tanzania: Businesses Count Losses in Dar Suburb After Blasts
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — As life was returning to normal in the weekend at the Gongo la Mboto and neighbouring suburbs in Dar es Salaam, businesses were counting their losses following the bomb blasts, which wreacked havoc and disrupted the lives of residents with thousands fleeing their homes.
The Dar es Salaam suburb was rocked by bomb blasts on Wednesday evening at the military barracks in the area, which forced the closure of the Julius Nyerere International Airport for some hours.
The terror unleashed by the blasts and the following disruption of lives and businesses also added salt to the wound already soured by the ongoing extensive power load shedding that has brought most businesses to a virtual standstill.
A survey conducted by The Citizen in the weekend showed that most traders who operate shops, pharmacies, hair salons and vegetables had returned and resumed their businesses. But some shop owners were still reluctant to open their premises saying that the situation was not all that conducive for them to re-open their businesses. "I am waiting for people to settle down and resume their daily lives.
It is too early for them to start buying new clothes now," Dustan Nungamali, a boutique owner at the Gongo la mboto bus stop told The Citizen.
He said many traders were still assessing the losses that they had incurred while they awaited the condition to return to normal. Some have had their premises vandalised as the bombs were exploding on the night of terror. It was not on the massive scale though and it was still too early to establish the real loss.
"I had only finished cooking, and when the bombs started exploding I ran and left everything there. And when I returned the following day I didn't find even a single spoon left in my café," said Hamisa Mihambo, an eating place owner at the Gongo la Mboto busy bus stop.
She said she will now have to start from scratch but the main problem will be servicing a loan she took from a women group.
Mr Damian Lukas (34) sales fresh fish at the nearby market at the Banana area. He lamented that the bomb explosions have seen him counting a loss amounting to Sh300,000 since all the fish he was selling got rotten for the two days that he was out of business.

Original_Search_Title: If the Mountain Won't Come to Raila
Headline: Kenya: If the Mountain Won't Come to Raila
Body_Text:
The PM's recent foray into Meru and other regions has made the outgoing powers that be extremely nervous and suspicious. And it's all part of his unique 2012 presidential strategy.
There is one constant about Raila Amolo Odinga in the multiparty era. Twist and turn as he may, changing parties and alliances every which way, he never employs the same General Election strategy twice.
The ninth and 10th General Elections of 2002 and 2007 respectively illustrate this powerfully. In the 2002 election, when the country was faced for the first time with the choice only of two candidates from Kikuyuland, Raila, who was actually secretary-general of then ruling party Kanu, soon threw his weight behind the then Leader of the Official Opposition in Parliament, Mwai Kibaki, in a move that destroyed Daniel arap Moi's preferred successor Uhuru Kenyatta's chances.
Raila's ringing endorsement of Kibaki in the single well-timed, well-aimed, resonant, rhetorical and suitably colloquial question "Si Mzee Kibaki tosha? (Kibaki fits the bill, doesn't he?)" dashed not only Kanu's and Uhuru's hopes in October 2002 but those of many others, however unrealistic they may have been. Subsequently forever remembered and rendered as "Kibaki tosha!", no longer a question but an eruptive affirmation, a done deal, a fact of life, this declaration was made at Uhuru Park, Nairobi, before a crowd of hundreds of thousands.
2002: A choice of two Mt Kenyans
Ford-People leader Simeon Nyachae was the first to oppose Raila's move, bitterly complaining that the massed opposition leadership had agreed on no such move and insisting on a full-blown nominations exercise.
Also firmly opposed, although he did not make his views known until years later, was Stephen Kalonzo Musyoka (now Vice President and 2012 hopeful), who, like Nyachae, fancied himself as being in with a chance if the matter of the single unified candidate had been put to a nominations vote.
No doubt both Nyachae and Kalonzo were, deep down, of the view that a country of 40-plus diverse ethnic communities ought not to be given such a narrow choice of candidates from one community and the same power elite-the older man a protege of the late Jomo Kenyatta and the younger his son.
But no one was more keenly aware of the narrowness of the choice on offer in Kenya's first Presidential transition General Election, or of its true implications for future elections, than Raila himself. He also knew two things for sure-that election was all about timing and, of course, overwhelming numbers. What's more, given the sheer persistence of the Kikuyu factor at the top in Kenyan politics (for instance, Moi appointed only Kikuyus as Vice President until the last 90 days of his marathon 24 years at State House), Raila was also organising closure, or a hiatus, on the matter of Kikuyu Presidential contestants. He was quietly reckoning that surely no Kikuyu would present himself or herself for a State House run after the country had entertained the spectacle of a choice between two Mt Kenyan contenders.
Looking ahead to 2007 in late 2002, even before Kibaki trounced Uhuru 3.6 million votes to 1.8 million, Raila saw no prospect of either a second Kibaki term or a substantive Kikuyu rival. Few people can blame Raila for having misread Kibaki's chances of seeking a second term; the man had, after all, entered State House in a wheelchair, with a neck brace and one leg and foot in plaster. By February 2003, he had suffered a series of medical emergencies, including, reportedly, a mild stroke.
2007: The 'Southern Strategy'
When Kibaki not only recovered but actually evinced every sign of gunning for a second term, Raila changed tactics and strategies completely. Proceeding on a basis of Kibaki-anything-but-tosha, Raila borrowed heavily from an American strategy that even his most intellectual PNU adversaries had never either heard of or found inconceivable as being applicable to Kenya. This is the so-called 'Southern Strategy' in American politics, a reference to a Republican Party strategy in the final decades of the 20th century of exploiting southern White fears and antipathies about Blacks to win elections against the Democratic Party, a formation that has overwhelming Black support in the same South. Raila's 2007 strategy of 41-against-1, or the rest of Kenya against the Mt Kenyans, applied much the same methods of building on existing fears and prejudices and winning the hearts and minds of those who harbour these antipathies.
In the 21st century, the Republican Party has both apologised for these racist tactics and appointed its first African American chairman, Mr Michael Steele. For his part, Raila entered the National Peace and Reconciliation Accord with Kibaki and joined the grand coalition government as Prime Minister.
For the 2007 contest, Raila first organised a Cabinet rebellion in which even Kalonzo and Uhuru heartily participated and then the 'dry run' against Kibaki when he led the 'No' side of Kenya's first ever national referendum-the plebiscite on the then proposed new constitution in November 2005, which he won handily, humiliating the government in the process. The Banana ('Yes') and Orange ('No') referendum was promptly characterised as a rehearsal for the 2007 General Election, at which Raila, after a convoluted nominations process, became the unified opposition candidate.
Raila entered that election with his mind focused on one salient yet subtle fact-two Presidential administrations had lost two national electoral events in a row, the Moi government at the 2002 General Election and the Kibaki government at the 2005 national referendum. He could smell blood in the political water; three-in-a-row defeats of the Government of Kenya were finally a distinct possibility, which would mean both a hat trick for himself (having been instrumental in the first two) and the ultimate prize, the crown of the Presidency itself.
Kenyan Presidential administrations had become so used to 'winning' in the years of the Kanu single-party supremacy that two defeats in a row fronted by Raila made Kibaki and his team extremely nervous about the prospects of a Government of Kenya being seen to suffer loss number three.
The dispute over the 2007 results showed that Raila truly believed he had carried off the third-in-a-row defeat of Kenyan Old School officialdom, long his own (and his father's before him) sworn enemy.
2012: Into the Mountain
Now on the road to 2012 and only the second General Election in which the incumbent of State House is not a contender, the Mountain has politically hived off the Rift Valley from ODM, but not at the ballot box... yet. What has actually happened is that the Mt Kenyan power elite have co-opted the Rift power elite away from Raila in smoke-filled back-room deals.
But there is still something that the Mt Kenyans, deep down, truly dread about Raila-they do not trust him campaigning in Central Kenya and they do not trust their own younger generations, particularly the slum dwellers and the peasants, as rapt Raila audiences.
Raila's visit to Meru a week ago, an area considered the fiefdom of Head of Civil Service and Secretary to the Cabinet Francis Muthaura and Energy Minister Kiraitu Murungi, was in many ways deeply upsetting to the Central high command, particularly in view of the narrative being rolled out at the grassroots to the effect that he is behind the plight of the Ocampo Six. The people of Chuka actually closed their shops and suspended all other activities and filled the streets to overflowing, cheering every dig Raila made at the idea of the Kikuyu, Kalenjin, Kamba (KKK) alliance. The loudest cheer came when the Prime Minister declared that there was "no need for K this, K that and K something else. As far as Kenyans are concerned, there's only one K-for Kenya!"
Reiterating at every stop that he made that he was PM and therefore could tour any and every part of Kenya, including nooks and crannies, Raila told the people of Meru that he would be back often in the coming months.
He drove into town in a convoy of high-end SUVs, with the car that he was in showing all the body mass of armour plating and bullet-resistant windows, escorted by officers and men of the PM's Escort and local police and administrators. This was clearly no longer Raila, rebel without a pause; this was Raila co-principal of the grand coalition government in the lame duck phase of an outgoing President, as well as Muthaura's and Kiraitu's senior and supervisor. But no local MP or other luminary was either in attendance or in sight. They had all ducked and would not resurface until after Raila left the area.
Towards the end of the week, Raila returned to North Eastern province, visting Wajir for the commemoration of the victims and survivors of the 1984 Wagalla Massacre. It was at that emotive and volatile place that Raila declared a situation akin to the post-election violence still lies in the near future (read Election '12) if the Ocampo Six will not have been whisked away to The Hague for full prosecutions and trial before then.
Back at the Office of the President at Harambee House in Nairobi, Muthaura's sanctum sanatorium, it was quietly noted that Raila was re-visiting the Wagalla Massacre at a time when a local TV station is running a highly controversial documentary series on the slaughter in which it is claimed that two otherwise distinguished sons of Meru-Kibaki confidant and former Finance Minister Daudi Mwiraria and one-time PC Samwel Kobia-were among the abattoir bureaucrats of the murder and mayhem in Wajir 27 years ago.
Given the grassroots narrative accusing Raila of having 'betrayed' the Ocampo Six, Mwiraria, former Nairobi PC Joseph Kaguthi and former Chief of the General Staff Joseph Kibwana must be experiencing a collective and unfamiliar deep chill.
Raila's 2012 election strategy is slowly coming into view, and it is as radically different from his 2007 game plan as that was from 2002's exultant endorsement of Kibaki. Like the Prophet Mohammed, he is coming to the Mountain, not the other way round. And in his forays into the Mountain, as elsewhere in the country, he is carefully lighting a fire under selected individuals in a very special category (not under the community as an ethnic bloc). And these individuals are all heading straight for near-future status as the outgoing Kibaki's political orphans.
That the Prime Minister's targets are both past and present Government of Kenya and, or individual high-ranking or formerly senior but still living Mt Kenyans' acts of malfeasance and misfeasance in office is not in doubt, as evidenced by four other tours of recent weeks. At the Coast he touched on the increasingly volatile issue of port privatisation; in Taita and Mau Narok, he addressed the IDP resettlement problem; in Webuye he held forth on Panpaper. And each time he made the outgoing powers-that-be extremely nervous and suspicious.
The PM's entourage has been considerably trimmed by the Rift Valley defections and the practice of Cabinet ministers on the other side of the Coalition making it a point lately of skipping his functions. But he has pressed on nonetheless, with a lean team that's big on issues of national interest that have resonated with his far-flung audiences, including, at a lower but quite significant frequency, inside the Mountain itself. Lands Minister James Orengo is often with the PM and also addresses these gatherings. He is a man whose utterances are carefully taken note of in the Mountain, where land hunger continues to be a major issue. In Wajir, Gitobu Imanyara also accompanied the PM and spoke eloquently after him from the podium, words that were of great interest to both the Meru elite and rank and file.
Is the Mountain ripe for the Odinga message? This is a very good question after a decade of Kikuyu stewardship at State House, and in view of Raila's high profile and stature in the coalition regime. Without Kibaki in the running, Raila is undoubtedly the most substantial of all those who are seeking to replace the President. All measures and weights, including the opinion polls, still show him to be tipping the balance.
The greatest fear now gripping those in the Mt Kenya power elite who are absolutely opposed to the Raila brand-or who suffer from what the late Vice President Michael Kijana Wamalwa called "Raila-phobia"-is that he can traverse the Mountain and both make an impression as well as an impact while Luo Nyanza remains hermetically sealed, politically speaking, to any other influence but his. In other words, the Luo are impervious to any other political message or formula but Raila's.
However, many parts of Central are not impervious to Raila's take on what ought to happen in post-Kibaki Kenya. And, as the old-fashioned phrase has it, there's the rub.

Original_Search_Title: The Arab Political Inferno - Lessons in Participatory Decision Making for Africans
Headline: Ghana: The Arab Political Inferno - Lessons in Participatory Decision Making for Africans
Body_Text:
The inferno of political uprisings that are rapidly spreading in the Arab world has re-ignited international debate over the role of the amorphous body called the "international community", in ensuring effective citizen participation in decision making.
Not since the end of the Cold War have there been so much citizen uprisings against regimes that were hitherto thought to be invincible. Watching television these days leaves no one in doubt about the wave of anger and resentment that are only now being fearlessly expressed on the streets of Tunis, Cairo, Amman and Algiers.
A seemingly harmless self-immolation of a young Tunisian, frustrated at his inability to get a job and his subsequent death, exploded into a popular uprising, leading to the overthrow of President Ben Ali, who has been in power since 1987. The repercussions of the Tunisian popular uprising have been phenomenal. In nearby Egypt, three people set themselves ablaze, in an apparent warning to the government of impending danger.
At this stage, most international commentators doubted the potency of this action and its potential to cause a regime change in Egypt. They cite the strong influence of the army and the support of the United States for the regime of President Hosni Mubarak, who has been in power since 1981.
Desirous of change
But later events have proven that no matter how strong or well equipped an army is, they cannot withstand the force of a group of determined people desirous of change. Interestingly, the Arab world was transfixed on events in Egypt, with trepidation to gauge the direction of these popular uprisings.
Once President Mubarak addressed the nation and appointed a Vice-President for the first time since 1981, and went ahead to promise political reforms, the stage was then set for a massive shake-up within the ruling political elite in the Arab world.
Jordan's King Abdullah bowed to popular pressure and appointed a new Prime Minister. In Yemen, President Ali Abdullah Saleh, in power for the past 34 years, gave in to protesters' demands and went ahead to announce that he would not seek re-election after 2013. He also appointed a new Prime Minister. Though unsatisfactory to protestors who are calling for nothing less than his resignation, they mark a significant shift in the response of the regime to popular demands.
The international community
The response of the international community to these waves of protests has 0 been interesting. French and American leaders called for 'restraint' in Tunisia, and urged the government to "respect the rights of the Tunisian people".
President Obama, in a telephone call to his Egyptian counterpart, called for a "smooth transition". A careful analysis of all the countries currently undergoing these revolutions indicates a commonality that cannot be ignored.
Let us start from President Zine al-Abidine Ben Ali. He came to power in 1987 on the back of popular agitations against then President Habib Bourguiba, who was accused of being mentally unsound to rule.
For the next 23 years ruled Tunisia with an iron fist, clamping down on popular protests and imprisoning political opponents. Basic human rights like freedom of speech and assembly were denied the ordinary Tunisian.
Whiles all these human rights abuses were going on, the West looked on quietly, because of President Ben Ali's clampdown on Islamist extremists. After the September 11 attacks, Ben Ali became a pawn of the West, receiving massive assistance to flush out suspected Islamist militants.
So while the ordinary Tunisian was being suffocated under a repressive regime, the US, France, UK and UN all kept quiet, loudly sending a message that the fight against terrorist was more paramount than the rights and freedoms of the individual.
In the case of Egypt, President Hosni Mubarak came to power in 1981, following the assassination of President Anwar Sadat. Sadat had become the first Arab leader to sign a peace agreement with Israel, under the aegis of the United States. Whiles the West applauded this agreement as being progressive, for President Sadat, it became a banana peel, eventually leading to his assassination.
Vice-President Hosni Mubarak was then sworn in as President, vowing to deal with Islamist militants and improving relations with the West. True to his word, political groups like the Muslim Brotherhood were banned for almost his entire Presidency. Political opposition was stifled, press freedoms were curtailed and the country was ruled like a military dictatorship.
All these happened under the watchful eyes of the United States, which continued to provide military assistance to this repressive regime. With time, Egypt became the third largest recipient of American foreign aid and largest in Africa. It became apparent that the American government kept propping up this regime.
Yemen became significant to the international community after the September 11 terrorist attacks. Once reviled for its oppressive regime, the Bush administration provided military assistance to the country due to concerns that militants that had been flushed out in nearby Saudi Arabia had found refuge in that country.
For the fight against terrorism to be successful, President Ali Abdullah Saleh needed to be supported at all cost, even it meant the Yemeni leader used the foreign assistance he received to suppress his own people.
Clearly, the West cannot deny complicity in the gross misrule that has become characteristic of regimes in the Arab world and parts of Africa. In pursuit of very parochial political agenda, despotic regimes have been imposed on Africans.
On the back of halting the spread of Communism, Prime Minister Patrice Lumuba was brutally murdered by President Mobutu Sese Seko at the instigation of Belgium.
For more than two decades, this dictator impoverished his people whiles becoming a darling boy of the West. President John F. Kennedy referred to this man as "a great friend of the American people". In Belgium, President Mobutu was a regular guest of King Leopold.
In Ivory Coast President Houphouet Boigny made himself life President and imposed a one party state on the country. Throughout his life, he was a friend of the French and Americans.
Interestingly enough, next door, the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) overthrew Kwame Nkrumah for making himself life President and creating a one party state. Down south in Angola, the American CIA provided support for rebels fighting the regime of Eduardo dos Santos, because he was deemed to be communist. That support led to one of Africa's most intractable civil wars, ending only with the death of rebel leader Jonas Savimbi.
Why were the Bush and Obama administrations prepared to cope with the political and religious intolerance in countries like Saudi Arabia, Egypt and Tunisia but were quick to impose sanctions on Zimbabwe for the same "offences"? Why has the Obama administration been quick to condemn the attack on gays and lesbians in Uganda and Zimbabwe but continue to remain silent over the repression of Christians, gays and lesbians in Saudi Arabia?
Unfortunately, Africa has never learnt its lessons as evidenced by the approach that ECOWAS and AU have adopted to deal with the Ivorian crisis.
The need for an African response is an absolute imperative. Africans do not need France, the US and Britain, under the guise of international assistance, to dictate any response planned by the African Union to deal with the Ivorian crisis.
Presidential delegation
It is disappointing that already a Presidential delegation has gone to Europe to "confer" with the "international community". The obvious question is: what interest does the West have in the Ivorian crisis?
The inferno in the Arab world is a clear statement to the west to back off Arabian affairs. The people are clearly making a case for participatory decision-making in national affairs. They have shown that they are more than capable of punishing regimes that ignore their interests in favour of Western powers.
The next destination of this wave of revolution is unknown. But what is certain is that by now despotic regimes in Africa are watching and listening with baited breath.
The author is a freelance Journalist and Development Practitioner.

Original_Search_Title: Bankole on Freedom of Information Bill
Headline: Nigeria: Bankole on Freedom of Information Bill
Body_Text:
Lagos — Dimeji Bankole, Speaker of the House of Representatives made news recently, by his sudden favourable disposition towards passing the Freedom of Information (FOI) Bill into law. FOI Bill is the longest in Nigerian legislative history which the current National Assembly has ignored for the past three and half years.
The Speaker has incidentally pledged that the House in the current legislative session would replicate what was done at the 2003 - 2007 session when both chambers of the National Assembly passed the Bill with flourish at the end of their tenure. However, the then President, Olusegun Obasanjo, vetoed the bill, making all the efforts of the National Assembly fruitless.
Bankole who was speaking as guest of honour at a lecture of Muslim Media Practitioners of Nigeria (MMPN) at the National Mosque in Abuja, stressed that in the present circumstance, the House would pressure the President, Goodluck Jonathan, to sign the Bill into law.
"I have always said the FOI Bill will enhance the work of the legislature in asking and getting answers from the executive on their activities and enhance the role of the media in performance of their constitutional role...I am happy the Minister of Information, Labaran Maku, was in the House last week to solicit its passage. We will remind the executive of this solicitation when we pass the bill for their assent," he remarked.
In reaction, however, critics, especially journalists were hardly swayed by the Speaker's pledge. Some even insist that the pledge coming at this time, could be a ploy by the Speaker to win in the April election. Those who hold this view do so on the backdrop that it was the same Bankole's House of Representatives which on Thursday, January 2, last year, rejected deliberations on the Bill with excuses that were hardly convincing to Nigerians. Remarkably, many members of the House then had, on the flour of the House, demanded that the Bill be thrown out in its entirety.
Lanre Idowu, publisher and trustee of Diamond Awards for Media Excellence (DAME), sees Bankole's promise as a welcome development. According to him, Nigerians should not disturb themselves in trying to know whether Bankole and his members would be able to achieve the feat, stressing that the end of the administration was at hand, and Nigeria would soon discover whether he was telling the truth.
He added that Nigerians should not rejoice yet about the statement until they see what version of the bill they would pass.
"There are different versions of freedom of information law, varying from one country to the other. So at the end of the day, we will know what version they are giving to Nigerians, and it is only then we will know whether we are to rejoice or not," he noted.
Notwithstanding, analysts have interpreted the inability of the National Assembly to pass the bill into law since 2007, in different ways.
For instance, there were those who alleged that the House of Representatives, indeed the whole of the National Assembly, were reluctant on passing the bill into law because they feared it would work against them. Though relatively young in age, Bankole has, at different occasions, proven to be solid in political brinkmanship with his survival of the proverbial banana peels of the National Assembly for more than three years that he has been on the saddle.
Sometime in June last year, for instance, there was an explosive rumble in the House when a former Chairman of the House Committee on Information and National Orientation, Dino Melaye, gave the Speaker an ultimatum to resign or be forced to quit. Melaye was also said to have promised to make the House ungovernable if Bankole failed to resign voluntarily. But Bankole had survived the storm, even as Melaye and his colleagues in the agitation ended up being suspended from the House.
Few weeks ago, he once again proved that his staying power was no fluke by surviving the factionalisation of his party, and the removal of his name from the party's list of candidates submitted to the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC). It was later restored as he was permitted to run for a second term.
Bankole was born in November 1969. He had his early education at Lara Day Nursery and Primary School. He then attended Baptist High School, Abeokuta. He holds a degree in Economics from the University of Reading, England and another in military Technique from Oxford University also in the United Kingdom, before proceeding to Harvard University in the United States of America for a course in Public Finance Managements. He was elected into the House in 2003, and was re-elected in 2007. He hails from Abeokuta in Ogun State.

Original_Search_Title: AMA, Check Operations of Food Vendors
Headline: Ghana: AMA, Check Operations of Food Vendors
Body_Text:
The Accra Metropolitan Assembly (AMA) must check the operations of food venders upon a severe warning from the Ghana Health Service (GHS) on the outbreak of cholera in some parts of the country, especially, in the Greater Accra Metropolis, as food sellers continue to operate under unhygienic conditions.
A tour by the Accra File of some principal corners of the city, noted that food venders were not adhering to the caution by the GHS.
Those at risk in the city are patrons of both cooked and uncooked food from vendors on street corners, with the most vulnerable being pupils and students.
According to experts, the disease is caused by the bacterium Vibrio Cholerae. Ingestion of this bacterium causes acute diarrhea, and transmission occurs through direct fecal-oral contamination, or through the consumption of contaminated water or food.
In its most severe form, cholera manifests as a sudden onset of acute watery diarrhoea that can prove fatal, as it causes severe dehydration and kidney failure.
While touring some parts of the city, especially at Agbogbloshie, the File noted that fishmongers leave the fish open for flies and dust to settle on them, meanwhile, buyers are encouraged to test the fish before buying, although it has not been washed.
The situation is no different from the butchers and cold store operators, whereby the refrigerators used to store meat and fish are outmoded, and full of rusted parts, which contaminate the product, putting consumers at risk.
The worst part of the situation is the mill where these food vendors blend their ingredients, and the kind of ingredients they use in preparing the food.
The file noted that only a small amount of water is used in washing all the vegetables, meanwhile, some of the vegetables are overripe with worms in them, while the machine used in blending the vegetables is not also kept clean, with flies hovering and settling on it.
According to the Health Service report, areas where the disease is often reported include Chorkor, Agbogbloshie, Korle-Gonno, Mamprobi, Agege, Maamobi, Nima, Banana Inn, Zambrama Line, Sukura, Russia and Mallam, all suburbs of Accra.
The annual outbreak of cholera in these suburbs of the capital city, and in some parts of the country, can be attributed to general insanitary conditions.
Some preventive measures include eating cooked food while it is still hot, and washing of the hands with soap before eating.
It is equally difficult to understand why the AMA Public Health Department and the Accra Directorate of the Ghana Health Service are unable to plan strategically to, at least, minimise or stop the disease from breaking out.
What worries the File is that the Accra Metropolitan Assembly has established a sanitation and motor court in some sub metros of the metropolis to check and arrest individuals who do not keep the environment clean.
The File wonders why some of these vendors leave their food uncovered, while the environmental sanitation inspectors of the AMA Public Health Department and those at the various sub-metro offices appear to look on unconcerned.

Original_Search_Title: Salami VS Chief Justice - NJC's Intervention Has Not Resolved Crisis - Falana
Headline: Nigeria: Salami VS Chief Justice - NJC's Intervention Has Not Resolved Crisis - Falana
Body_Text:
These are not the best of times for the nation's judiciary. Last week, the news broke that the Court of Appeal President, Justice Ayo Salami, had rejected his promotion to the Supreme Court by the Chief Justice of Nigeria (CJN), Justice Aloysius Katsina-Alu.
Salami followed up his action with a law suit to stall the promotion while alleging that Katsina-Alu tried to influence judgment in the Sokoto State gubernatorial election case presided over by a panel of the Court of Appeal. The National Judicial Council (NJC) intervened, asking Salami to stay on in the Appeal Court. In this interview, Lagos lawyer and human rights activist, Mr. Femi Falana, says the NJC's intervention has not resolved the crisis between Salami and Katsina-Alu, explaining why he and a colleague instituted a suit on the matter.
What do you make out of the on-going crisis in the judiciary over the promotion of the Court of Appeal President to the Supreme Court which he rejected?
I was flabbergasted for the President of the Court of Appeal to be promoted against his wish and contrary to the provision of the Federal Character Commission Act, which stipulates the procedures for the appointment and removal of the President of the Court of Appeal, as well as the appointment of the justices of the Supreme Court to reflect the federal character of the country. That is why myself and a colleague, Mr. Bamidele Aturu, decided to file an action in court so that we could get a definite pronouncement on the matter.
The case is still in court, and we intend to pursue it to a logical end. Every aspect of our life is being governed by the law, and you will notice on a daily basis the culture of impunity being displayed by those in authority.
We are challenging this illegal aspect of our governance. When there is problem in the universities or other public institutions, they will ask you to go to court when they know that the matter will not be resolved quickly. All these are not good for the system.
What is the import of the suit you have instituted?
As an outsider to the crisis, I felt worried that the dramatis personae did not realise the enormity of damage being done to the judicial system. Even in a banana republic, all these things don't happen. You wonder why we should have a situation whereby a senator is assisted by some senior lawyers to destroy the judiciary.
When you have that kind of situation, you ask yourself whether these people are aware that they are destroying their own system, and, if we allow them to go on unchallenged, all of us will be affected and we will also be guilty.
When a problem like this happens, whose responsibility is it to resolve it? The NBA, the AGF?
It is the responsibility of everybody. The NJC saw the dangerous scenario, they called for a truce among the warriors. But, their intervention has not solved the problem, which has almost eroded the integrity of the judiciary. We cannot afford to sweep the allegations raised under the carpet. Something must be done and quickly too.
Some of us supported Justice Kayode Eso when he advocated that there should be a separate body to discipline erring judicial officers and not by the judges involved, for instance, in the present case. I think if we want to move for the independence of the judiciary, we need an independent body to look into all these. But, if any allegation is made against the CJN as the head of the body, he will have to step aside, because he cannot be a judge in his own case.
What is your view on the NJC absolving the CJN?
Frankly speaking, I don't want to join issue with the body now, because we have a case in court and, definitely, we will take his response and statements backing him to court. The writer of the statement knows that there are matters pending before the court, and he is making definite pronouncement, approving the decision of the CJN on the same matter. In my own case, I am asking for the legal validity of the decision of the Federal Judicial Service Commission to recommend the President of the Court of Appeal to the Supreme Court.
If that matter is still pending in court, and you proceed to absolve the person involved, there is much to it. We will definitely do something about that in court. What he has done is prejudicing the matter before the court. However, our efforts is to curb impunity in our judicial system.
What happens to the case affecting an ordinary citizen, whose matter cannot attract publicity in the media, like that of Justice Salami? When we went to court to challenge the illegality, we are not out to embarrass anybody, but we are trying to protect the integrity of the judiciary. We shall pursue our case to its logical conclusion.
What is the implication of the crisis on the nation's image?
I think Nigerians have developed an unbelievable resistance to this type of thing and are no more embarrassed by this type of development. However, this crisis has far-reaching effect on the judiciary in this country, especially on our political development. It has further eroded the confidence built over the years. It is a crucial moment in this nation. Otherwise, we may have anarchy.
I am not quite sure that the governing party, the ruling class , appreciates the enormity of what this could have done to the nation. I am expecting the President to have called the members of his party fuelling the crisis; he could have called the CJN and the President of the Court of Appeal to order, not to make the country a laughing stock in the comity of nations.
Any country, where there is this type of crisis, there is no doubt that it will likely lose its integrity. I am so much concerned about the implication of what will happen in our country over this development.

Original_Search_Title: UPDF Will Ensure Polls Are Peaceful
Headline: Uganda: UPDF Will Ensure Polls Are Peaceful
Body_Text:
Interview with Lt. Gen Aronda Nyakairima
Some politicians insist that UPDF is a partisan force.
That is cheap politics. Some remarks are designed to cause disharmony in the army. The UPDF has a national character. Our recruitment and training policy make us so. We recruit from all corners of the country using a quota system. That system largely qualifies us an army with a national character.
Our training ensures that our soldiers are being trained from entry to exit into a culture of professionalism. The dominant characteristic of our professionalism is that we are pro-people.
That training is enough to inculcate nationalistic qualities so there's no way one can say that we are biased. Our patriotism, professionalism and modernity cannot put us anywhere near being partisan.
Is the UPDF prepared for a transition in case an opposition candidate wins?
The army is part of this county. It is part of the population. When we made a contribution to the Constitution we said that we will be with the people when they decide on who leads them. We have outgrown the time when every regime came and went with its army. So no one can disband the army.
Does it occur to you that the opposition has reason to fear since UPDF came with the NRM?
More than three quarters of the army have been recruited from the population. We are more than 52,000 so you can't say that all of them came from the bush. Therefore, it's a misplaced fear and sometimes a deliberate one, to gain some cheap political capital. These are nationalistic soldiers. For the first time our country has a national army. Of course, the bush army was imbalanced because of circumstances but since 1986, every part of the country is represented in the UPDF.
But there have been accusations that the army is 'quietly' behind President Museveni because he's its Commander-in-Chief?
He is the Commander-in-Chief, President and founder of the UPDF so you can't say we are quietly behind him.
Not at all. We are quietly disarming the Karimojong warriors, we are quietly hunting down Kony in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), we are training but not quietly. Those are our patriotic duties and it's not about being quietly behind the President.
Col. Kizza Besigye referred to you as a latecomer in the UPDF. Does that undermine your authority?
