It's not about latecoming. It's about what you do. And certainly he knows when he joined and when I joined. As I said, that's cheap politics. All of us serve to the best of our abilities. And we have been serving and we don't know when he left.
Some Ugandans are worried that the UPDF may get involved in the coming elections.
The key stakeholders, who are the people, expect us to secure them as they go to choose their leaders. They have done their part - they have worked hard, they have paid taxes, their country has recovered and it is developing.
So they are asking us, can you do your part and secure us when it comes to February 18 and we elect our leaders? Can you? And they insist and say: we have given you all that it takes. They have trained us, they have equipped us and they have done that adequately. And therefore it's our turn when it comes to February 18 to do our part.
Won't your other operations be affected during the elections?
We are going to continue our ongoing operations. Internally, we will continue to pick the remaining guns in Karamoja so we will not stop the disarmament because elections are going on. Externally, we are not withdrawing our soldiers from Somalia because we are going to elect. No. Our operations against Al-Shabaab will continue very seriously.
Operations against the Lord's Resistance Army will continue so we are not going to stop hunting for the rumunants of the LRA in the DRC because of elections. The alertness of our forces against the ADF in the Rwenzori Mountains will also continue.
What exactly will be your role during the elections?
We are going to do our part to secure the country as stakeholder number one goes about the electoral process to elect their leaders. Stakeholder number two is the Electoral Commission. The EC chairman and his team are also telling us: you people, we've done our part. We've fulfilled all our mandate, are you ready to secure the people when they do what I have prepared them for? We say yes we are.
Should EC chairman require a helicopter to take ballot papers in the event of rain, a broken bridge or time sensitivity, it is my work to provide a helicopter. Should any disaster strike, like the EC team has had a fatal accident, then it's my work to provide a helicopter.
And who is stakeholder number three?
Security. During these elections, UPDF is going to secure the country from one border to another. We will secure the country, its borders, the people and their property to ensure that their ongoing operations are not disrupted.
They will be having enough forces to support the Uganda Police Force during the elections to secure the electoral process. Should the Police require an urgent reinforcement in a distant place, I am required to get a helicopter to the Inspector General to lift a police force quickly from one area to go and reinforce them.
Where does the UPDF get the mandate to provide security during the elections?
Everything is embedded in the Constitution, the electoral laws, the Presidential and Parliamentary Elections Act and other laws. We are the operative role in all those laws. Our constitutional mandate is to secure territorial integrity and sovereignty of the country with or without elections.
Thus far, the Police has been acting alone - maybe with the intelligence - but should there be a situation which goes beyond the capacity of the police during, before and after voting, the Constitution mandates the Police to call upon UPDF to reinforce them and handle the situation that could endanger the lives and property of our people. That's what would take us to the electoral process.
If there's no problem and we hope everybody is a democrat and law abiding, we will only go to cast our votes and go about our other missions. But if the Police calls upon us, we will come with full force and support them.
Why do you think the Police is likely to call upon you?
One July 11 last year, we suffered terrorism. Innocent people were targeted by Al-Shabaab so we have to keep vigilant. We will not halt elections because terrorists have attacked.
Two, politically motivated violence. All of us are in this country and we are hearing what people are talking about. You hear there are brigades - Red Brigade, Bamboo Brigade, Kikankane Brigade - where are these brigades coming from? Our business is anyone claiming to be having a brigade.
Will you come in to fight crime and insurgency?
Crime intelligence is the third element. If there's a threat, the President as the Commander-in-Chief will call upon reserves to help the Police. Insurgency is the fourth element. If the Allied Democratic Forces come into the country in the middle of elections, that will be treated as an insurgency.
If someone has been talking about a brigade and he loses and a brigade turns into an insurgency force, then the UPDF shall be there to use its might to deal with it.
Are these brigade threats real? Should Ugandans worry?
Are they empty threats? Intelligence will prove. A brigade is a military formation. There's no one else who can have a brigade. Even a police force cannot have a brigade. It's authorised under a military organisation. So who is this one with a brigade? Where are its barracks? Who is its commander? Where are its logistics? Where are its arms?
This far we will say these are empty threats. But should they be serious, should they be real, we will not be found wanting in securing our people as they go from their homes to vote. Even if they are there, are they such a small threat that the Police can handle? Time will tell. This far, it's a matter of intelligence and police. If they disrupt elections and the Police deems it necessary that we come to their support, then we will not be found wanting.
How serious is the Al-Shabaab threat during election time?
Since we have not defeated Al-Shabab, there's no one who can say 'well, there's no problem, people go back and sleep." We cannot do that. That's why we are continually saying that in the event of a terrorist attack we will be there to support the Police as they secure the electoral process.
Who, between the Police and UPDF, will be giving the orders?
Police commanders are in charge and the UPDF commanders are in subordinate and support roles. Some of those police commanders may not necessarily be senior to the UPDF commanders but we did this because we want to comply with the electoral laws.
Ordinarily, that would not happen - a District Police Commander cannot command a brigade commander of three battalions but for the sake of complying with the electoral laws, a battalion commander of a major rank will be called upon for support and he will oblige.
Has UPDF played any roles in previous elections?
This is not the first time we are holding national elections. I think one can go as far back as 1989 when the National Resistance Council was expanded. We did participate and provided security. Then we had elections in 1996 when most parts of the country were still suffering insurgencies - LRA in the north, Karimojong warriors in parts of the northeast and ADF rebels in the west.
Even in the centre here, we were just recovering from Itongwa's terrorism. We played a leading role in ensuring peaceful elections because the Police at that time was still very thin.
How were you involved in subsequent elections?
In 2001, the security situation was a little worse because the LRA's numbers were about 5,000 with 3,000 under arms. Abductions and mutilations were at their peak and so the entire north was insecure. We played a leading role by working with the EC, because the Police could not manage to protect people in an area that was experiencing insurgency.
In 2006, the situation was worse in terms of the activities of LRA because 1.8 million people were in camps. But we did play a leading role then and still had an election because the President was very clear that the country should be secured. Parts of Adjumani and Oyam were still disturbed by LRA but we worked with the EC and a few Police personnel to ensure that no one failed to reach the polling centre because of the LRA.
How do you rate your track record in ensuring peaceful elections over the years?
If anyone wants to check our track record of involvement in electoral process, go back to 1996. The records are on record, our performances are there and (they prove that) we secured the country (because) no one went away without voting because of insecurity. This far, the country has always had peaceful elections.
What were your most important roles during the elections in the north?
We secured our people and we are very proud of that. We provided an environment to ensure that all our people vote and our record speaks for itself. We even know some MPs who were voted for by the LRA fighters but we will not mention names. So these people who are shouting now that the UPDF is involved, the UPDF is doing this; where were they at that time? We know they were around. Maybe at that time they were not talking because they knew they would not campaign anywhere because LRA would be with them.
Does the UPDF need representation in Parliament?
This is a people-based matter. Since the people still want us in Parliament, we will be there. When Idi Amin was around, Uganda was a laughing stock. When governments lasted months and years and hours, we were a laughing stock.
Who had made us a laughing stock? A failed state. Banana republics. Killing fields. Two elements of state - politicians and soldiers - are the ones who failed the state.
When the Movement put in place a Constitution written by the people, they said 'you army be here and we legislate with you' for the purposes of learning together the past, the present and where the country is. Our role has been very positive and enriching to the country and I have no problem with it. Only 10 members being there until people say we no longer want you (is okay).
After all is said and done, is there cause to worry about the coming elections?
What is the excitement about? What is the running around about? What is it when we have the best situation this country has ever had? This is a very good environment which we should be celebrating. I think that's why people are excited because they are not used to campaigns in an environment of peace.
What is the excitement about? When we were over-stretched at that time from 1996 to 2006 with the LRA, we managed to secure the country. This time the electoral laws are absolutely clear and we are not ignorant about them. What is clear today is that we are going to have elections come February 18, 2011.

Original_Search_Title: PSVS Told to Instal Litter Bins
Headline: Kenya: PSVS Told to Instal Litter Bins
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Refuse bins are set to occupy space in public transport vehicles, following new directives issued by the National Environmental Management Authority.
Commuters will be required to dispose any kind of refuse into the bins.
Those who fail to comply risk being jailed for two years, or paying a fine of Sh500,000.
While issuing the directives, Nema acting director general, Dr Ayub Macharia, said refuse from public service vehicles had contributed to littering of roads and streets country wide.
"It is common to see passengers throwing waste such as plastic soda bottles, biscuit paper wrappers and banana peels through the vehicle windows. This has to stop," he said.
The regulatory agency has also joined hands with the Transport Licensing Board, The Traffic Department and Motor Vehicle Inspection Unit, with the aim of tightening the noose around violators of the Noise and Excessive Vibrations Pollution Control regulations of 2009.
Consequently, the agencies have outlawed enhanced sound equipment in matatus.
The vehicles are only allowed to operate with the sound systems installed by the manufacturer or assembler.
Dr Macharia said operators have been exposing commuters to adverse health effects associated with noise, including impaired hearing, heart attacks, miscarriages, high blood pressure, stomach upsets and ulcers among others.
"Some matatu operators have installed speakers occupying a whole row of seats, which are placed beneath the seats. They have simply turned matatus into mobile discos," said Dr Macharia.
"A matatu is not a place for entertainment. We only want background music, which is not possible with the enhancers," he added.
"Who do you want to install the systems for? It is illogical for you to install equipment worth close to Sh300,000," said TLB, chairman, Mr Hassan Ole Kamwaro.
Operators have also been directed to remove all musical horns and multi-tone horns popularly known as 'pararilas' or 'twitters'.
The director complained that noise from matatus has already affected many touts and drivers causing them to turn rogue.
According to scientific research, exposure to noise of higher that 90 decibels for eight hours can lead to deafness.

Original_Search_Title: Segun Aganga - Ten Months After
Headline: Nigeria: Segun Aganga - Ten Months After
Body_Text:
Finance Minister Segun Aganga is a very brilliant man. You do not become a managing director of Goldman Sachs, a leading investment banker in the world, for being stupid. And for a Blackman to have become so in the United Kingdom - an essentially White world - meant there must have been one or two things going for him besides his obvious academic brilliance. When he was appointed Finance Minister, some Diaspora Nigerians proudly appropriated him as representing their 'constituency' in the government. But has Dr Aganga, managing director in hedge funds at Goldman Sachs since 2003 until he was appointed Finance Minister, really delivered?
Several observations could be made:
One, in our type of countries, where the institutions are weak and the wishes of the man of power are virtually law, it will be unfair to completely blame any Finance Minister for the failings of the economy. For Dr Aganga who was appointed Finance Minister on the eve of an election year in which the President is also a candidate, assessing his performance becomes even more complicated since political considerations are often the dominant considerations in policy-making and execution in an election year. My personal opinion is that the legacy of Dr Aganga, an expert in wealth management, may well be tied to the fate of the National Sovereign Wealth Fund, which has his finger prints all over it.
Two, while Dr Aganga can be excused for any non-performance of the economy, it is possible to assess the extent to which he has brought the 'x-factor' to governance - positive intangibles that reflect his long years of sojourn in Europe and from being a director of a major investment bank. My strong feeling here is that he takes criticisms of the economy by top politicians and public figures as a direct attack on his competence and consequently reacts inappropriately. A case in point was when former Vice President repeatedly expressed concerns about the direction of the economy, in particular over the regime's accumulation of huge loans and depletion of both foreign reserves and the Excess Crude Account. There is no doubt that Atiku's concerns about the economy were, as should be expected of any presidential candidate, tainted with politics. Unusual for a Finance Minister, Dr Aganga jumped into the fray and warned Atiku, a politician, not to 'play politics' with the economy. In our political tradition, a Minister is considered too junior to take a direct shot at a presidential candidate, especially a credible one who had been a former Vice President of the country. Dr Aganga further told Atiku that the 'economy is too technical for some people to understand' (ThisDay, November 25, 2010). In countries like the USA, this statement would have opened him up to charges of 'elitism' - of talking down to people.
Three, ironically while Dr Aganga believes that the 'economy is too technical for some people to understand', some economists hold a similar 'disdainful' view of him on grounds that he is not a macroeconomist, has not published any paper on Nigerian economy and has questionable understanding of how Nigeria works, having been living abroad for too long. I have several times found myself playing the role of Dr Agang's unofficial spokesman, especially among economists - not because he is a fellow Diasporan but more because I have tremendous respect for what he has accomplished at a personal level and also believe that with his exposure he will be able to attract competencies that will complement his own skills. Dr Aganga graduated in 1980 with a First Class Honours in Financial Accounting from the University of Ibadan and subsequently did post graduate studies at Oxford University, UK.
Four, with the lull in political activities following the end of the primaries, it may perhaps be germane to have honest discussion about the direction of the economy. Besides the undeniable depletion in foreign reserves and excess crude accounts, there are other worrying statistics: The Vanguard of July 20 2010 reported that the federal government raised alarm over the increasing rate of unemployment in the country which official figures said was at 19.47% (some say the real figure is likely to be around 40-50%). On November 5, 2010, the Nigerian Tribune reported that the "World Economic Forum (WEF) has slammed Nigeria over alleged poor governance and inadequate infrastructure financing" and downgraded the country to 127 out of 139 countries. In the 2010 Human Development Report compiled by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), Nigeria was grouped among the 41 countries considered to have the "least human development". According to the Report, Nigeria's wealth -as defined by gross domestic product per head-has slipped. The GDP per head in Nigeria was given as a mere 1,224 dollars, compared to 9,812 dollars in South Africa, 1,628 dollars in Kenya and 2,197 dollars in Cameroon. On life expectancy, the Report said that in 2010 Nigeria's life expectancy was 48.4 years on average, below that of Ghana (57.1.), Cameroon (51.7), Benin Republic (62.3), and Uganda (54.1). Meanwhile a list released by Transparency International on October 26, 2010 showed that Nigeria has fallen four steps (from 121 position in 2008 to 130 in 2009 to 134 in 2010). Again in October 2010, it was reported that Nigeria slipped from 35th to 40th position in the Mo Ibrahim good governance ranking of 53 countries in Africa. Similarly a survey conducted as part of its membership operational audit in January 2010 by the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria, showed that 839 manufacturing companies closed shop in 2009 as a result of harsh operating environment. . With these statistics - and politics aside - it is difficult for any patriotic Nigerian not to be concerned.
Five, with due respect, I disagree with the Finance Minister that expressing what I will regard as robust concerns about the direction of the economy amounts to deliberately talking down the economy. I am uncomfortable with some of the language used by the Minister in replying to critics, including his use of innuendo to suggest that Professor Chukwuma Soludo, former Governor of the Central Bank, should be in jail- apparently for his rather ill-advised harsh criticisms of the direction of the economy in the media. In the 'marketplace of ideas' theory of democracy, it is generally believed that "the best of truth is the power of the thought to get itself accepted in the competition of the market". And this implies looking beyond the messenger - even if his ideas offend shock or infuriate - to interrogate his message and accommodate those that are useful. It is precisely this capacity to look beyond a repugnant messenger and to accommodate uncomfortable criticisms that separates mature democracies from banana republics.

Original_Search_Title: Locals Earning Big From Trade With Southern Sudan
Headline: Uganda: Locals Earning Big From Trade With Southern Sudan
Body_Text:
IN the gritty dusty hot afternoon, truck loaded with bananas travel to Konyo Konyo market in the heart of Juba for between 12 and 13 hours from Uganda.
A bunch of bananas that goes for sh7,000 in Uganda sells for about sh30,000 in Juba.
According to Southern Sudan minister of Information and Broadcasting, Dr. Barnabas Marial, Uganda earns about $200million (about sh460b) annually from trade with Southern Sudan. Dr. Marial says Uganda is the biggest trading partner to Sudan.
There are over 150,000 traders from Uganda in Southern Sudan. Uganda earns about $900 million (about sh2trillion)through trade with Southern Sudan.
Other trading partners are Kenya, Ethiopia, DR Congo, Eritrea, Tanzania, Zimbabwe, South Africa, China, US and Europe.
He says Uganda will play a vital role in the new nation because Southern Sudan does not have technical people or materials for reconstruction.
They do not have processing and manufacturing industries.Construction materials ranging from cement to bricks are imported from Uganda. The Ugandans are also providing manpower in the country industry.
Available statistics from the ministry of regional cooperation indicate that over 1,500 Ugandans are employed in the construction industry of Southern Sudan.
Uganda will tap in the huge oil potential in Southern Sudan and this will cut down on the price of gas in Uganda.
Southern Sudan is also an attractive destination for Ugandan professionals. Statistics from the ministry of regional cooperation indicate that over 1,200 Ugandan professionals are working in Non-governmental organiations, ministries and agencies.
With the nation gaining self rule - Dr. Riek Machar says there will be peace across the region and trade between Ugandan and Southern Sudan will increase to unprecedented levels.
He says Uganda is also an attractive destination for Southern Sudan students.
Over 100,000 Southern Sudanese Students are studying in Uganda.
Dr. Riek says the cordial relationship between Uganda and Southern Sudan will continue.
Fred Okello, a Ugandan trader says Ugandan traders are being allowed to operate their businesses in any part of Sudan.
Okello explains that the biggest challenge facing them though is the poor road infrastructure especially from Gulu to Juba.
Ugandans also face a challenge of language barrier. Southern Sudan people speak Juba Arabic, while many Ugandans speak English. Dr. Marial explains that as Southern Sudan plans to join the East African Community.

Original_Search_Title: Chief Justice of Nigeria Vs President, Court of Appeal - Lawyers Differ
Headline: Nigeria: Chief Justice of Nigeria Vs President, Court of Appeal - Lawyers Differ
Body_Text:
Abuja — Lawyers, yesterday, differed on the constitutional powers of the Chief Justice of Nigeria (CJN) to propose the appointment of the President of Court of Appeal,(PCA) Justice Issa Ayo Salami as Justice of the Supreme Court.
While Chief Mike Ahamba (SAN) argued that the proposed appointment was not illegal, Chief Femi Falana contended that the appointment was illegal and unconstitutional.
According to Ahamba: "There is nothing illegal for the Chief Justice of Nigeria to propose the appointment of the president of Court of Appeal to the position of Justice of the Supreme Court. It is not unconstitutional. It has no precedence but it does not mean it cannot happen. The offer can of course be rejected."
He added that the CJN could not have acted on his own, the National Judicial Council (NJC) decided on the proposed appointment, he only did their bidding.
On his part, Falana said he was concerned about the tussle between the CJN and the president of the Appeal Court but asserted that "the proposed appointment is illegal and unconstitutional".
"It is pertinent to make the point abundantly clear that the PCA was approved by the president of the Federal Republic of Nigeria and confirmed by the Senate. The appointment shall lie until retirement age unless there is a proven case of misconduct or insanity which will make it impossible for him to discharge the functions of that office."
According to Chapter 7 Section 231 (2) of the 1999 Constitution "The appointment of a person to the office of a Justice of the Supreme Court shall be made by the President on the recommendation of the National Judicial Council subject to confirmation of such appointment by the Senate."
Are there precedents that perhaps guided the CJN? According to Mr. Oluwarotimi O. Akeredolu (SAN), former president of Nigeria Bar Association (NBA), we have had Justices moving from the Supreme Court to the Court of Appeal or even to the High Court. Hon. Justics Mamman Nasir, Buba Ardo and Dan Ibekwe being notable examples.
Insiders believe that the growing animosity between the CJN and the president, Court of Appeal stems from the powers of the latter as enshrined in section 240 of the 1999 constitution which states: "Subject to the provisions of this constitution, the court of Appeal shall have jurisdiction to the exclusion of any other court of law in Nigeria, to hear and determine appeals from other tribunals as may be prescribed by an Act of the National Assembly."
Notably, the 1999 constitution states that "The decisions of the court of Apeal in respect of appeals arising from election petitions shall be final."
It envisaged that Salami's disposition so far had endeared him to powers that be, as it feared litigations arising from the April polls may cause upsets.
A lawyer who wants to remain anonymous said the ruling government might not be comfortable with Salami remaining as the president of the Court of Appeal since under his watch two Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) governors, Olusegun Oni and Olagunsoye Oyinlola of Ekiti and Osun states respectively were removed by the court.
The governor of Sokoto State, Alhaji Aliyu Wamakko could have gone the same way had the Supreme Court remitted the case to the Court of Appeal to adjudicate upon as required by the Nigerian constitution.
Legal luminaries who spoke to LEADERSHIP said the feud between the duo began from that Sokoto governorship tussle.
Meanwhile, the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN) has urged President Goodluck Jonathan to quickly put an end to the ongoing frontal attack against the judiciary by his party, the PDP, just because it was not happy with the ruling of the Appeal Court in some election petition cases.
In a statement issued in Ilorin yesterday by its national publicity secretary, Alhaji Lai Mohammed, the party warned that there would be far reaching-consequences for all if the PDP, was allowed to use its clout to destroy the judiciary just to serve its own selfish ends.
In the same light, the Conference of Nigerian Political Parties (CNPP) said it "condemns the unsolicited appointment of Justice Ayo Salami to Supreme Court by the CJN, Justice Katsina-Alu and accordingly stands by Justice Salami's instant rejection of the unholy offer."
In a press statement issued by CNPP yesterday, it also stated that they viewed the "trumped-up appointment as orchestra of rigging and prelude to appointment of a pliable president of the Appeal Court, who will be a rubber stamp to the CJN".
The CNPP however hailed Justice Salami, saying that it stood by his outright rejection of the offer, not only because of the unethical manner of the appointment, as he was neither consulted nor the matter listed in the agenda of the Federal Judicial Service Commission, but on the believe that it was clearly politically motivated and simulated by President Jonathan and his journey men who view Salami as an obstacle in the April 2011 post electoral petitions.
The ACN further expressed grave concern at the contradictions that have emerged over the issue of Justice Salami's 'promotion', saying that while the PDP took a full-page newspaper advertorial to pillory Justice Salami with the worst and crudest of language, the CJN was pushing hard for the 'elevation' of the same man to the Supreme Court on the basis of his competence and fearlessness. So the same man being savaged by the PDP was the same one being considered for elevation to the Supreme Court because he is capable?
"What is happening? Who is fooling who? Have these people, in their hurry to remove Justice Salami from the Court of Appeal, forgotten to tie the loose ends, thus exposing themselves for all to see? Do they think Nigeria is a banana republic where people can be hoodwinked by such unimaginative propositions?" ACN queried.
"For clarity, we are not asking President Jonathan to intervene in the workings of the judicial arm of government, since that will negate the separation of powers principle entrenched in the nation's Constitution. What we are saying is that the President should call his party to order, to desist from attacking the judiciary and seeking to influence its workings, as can be seen by the push to force Justice Salami out of the Court of Appeal," the party said.
It said such a move, and the manner it was being executed, was unprecedented in the history of the judiciary, seems to be in bad faith.
"We do not know how much of these developments are known to President Jonathan. But we do know that if he allows the judiciary to be destroyed under his watch, posterity will not forgive him, since the buck stops at his desk," ACN said.

Original_Search_Title: Supreme Court - CNPP, ACN, Back Salami's Refusal of Elevation
Headline: Nigeria: Supreme Court - CNPP, ACN, Back Salami's Refusal of Elevation
Body_Text:
Abuja — Both the Conference of Nigerian Political Parties (CNPP) and the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN) have praised the courage of Justice Ayo Salami for turning down his 'elevation' from being President of the Court of Appeal to become a Justice of the Supreme Court.
Salami rejected the offer, disclosing in a letter he wrote to Chief Justice (CJ) Aloysius Katsina-Alu last Friday that the CJ had sought to 'promote' him as a ploy to ease him out of the Appeal Court which he has led to deliver landmark judgements in election petition cases.
Salami was also piqued that Katsina-Alu did not even "deem it fit and proper to discuss the proposal with me."
He stated in the letter that Katsina-Alu "Characteristically informed members of (Federal Judicial Service Commission, FJSC) of his proposal to strengthen the Supreme Court by moving me to that court and presumably to replace me with his minion or stooge" on the Court of Appeal.
A statement issued in Abuja on Sunday by CNPP National Publicity Secretary, Osita Okechukwu, viewed "the trumped up appointment as an orchestra of rigging and prelude to appointment of a pliable President of Appeal Court who will be a rubber stamp to (Katsina-Alu).
"We salute (Salami) and stand by his outright rejection of the offer, not only because of the unethical manner of the appointment, as he was neither consulted nor the matter listed in the agenda of the (FJSC); but on the truism that it is clearly politically motivated and simulated by President Goodluck Jonathan and his journeymen who view Salami as an obstacle in the April 2011 post-election petitions.
"We recall how (Katsina-Alu) ignobly descended into the arena and unceremoniously halted the Sokoto State Governorship election petition judgment ready for delivery at the Appeal Court.
"On the issue of judgments delivered by the Appeal Court being against the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), we wish to state that out of the over 100 cases, including Presidential election petitions, only five Governorship and few parliamentary seats were won in the Appeal Court by the APGA (All Progressives Grand Alliance), Labour Party, and (ACN), while the PDP won the remaining cases since the 2003 elections."
The CNPP agreed with Salami that the environment is already over heated and in a state of flux; so, Nigerians will frown at anyone or group of persons who, out of personal interest, manipulates the judiciary to achieve predetermined results.
It urged the National Judicial Council (NJC) to cancel the appointment and call Katsina-Alu to order "before he plunges Nigeria into a constitutional crisis."
Another statement issued by ACN National Publicity Secretary, Lai Mohammed, asked Jonathan to quickly end the attack against the judiciary by the PDP, just because it is not happy with the ruling of the Court of Appeal in some election petition cases.
The ACN warned of far-reaching consequences for the entire country if the PDP, because it is the ruling party, is allowed to use its clout to destroy the judiciary for its own selfish ends.
"It is not unusual for any individual or political party to disagree with a court ruling. Such disagreement can be vented through appeal process, where possible, or other accepted civilised means.
"But to resort to downright abuse of Judges and the courts, as the PDP and Senator Iyiola Omisore have been doing in the case of the Court of Appeal, is a dangerous trend that can only destroy an arm of government that is widely believed to have done more than any other to save our democracy," the ACN maintained.
Mohammed said it is "a clear example of the unbridled onslaught against the judiciary" that Katsina-Alu, using the FJSC, to spearhead the 'promotion' of Salami to the Supreme Court so as to replace him with someone who is believed to be pliable.
"The report said while Salami is ostensibly being 'promoted' because he is needed to help shore up the apex court, in view of his erudition and courage, the real reason is that the PDP has become uncomfortable with the recent rulings of the Court of Appeal under his watch, especially the loss of Osun and Ekiti States to the opposition, and has thus arrived at a final solution to ease him out. This is very sad for our country.
"It is sad because, according to the published report, which has yet to be refuted, Salami was neither consulted nor did he ask for the promotion - as is the tradition. It is sad because Jonathan's name is being dragged into the whole issue, as he has reportedly agreed to increase the number of the Justices on the Supreme Court just to accommodate Salami.
"It is sad because the PDP believes that it must always receive favourable rulings from the courts, just because it is the ruling party.
"Worse still, (Katsina-Alu) is said to have brought forward (since the publication of the report), from March to February, the next meeting of the NJC at which the decision to promote Salami, against his wish and in deviation from the established tradition in the judiciary, is to be endorsed."
Mohammed expressed concern over the contradictions that have emerged over Salami's 'promotion', saying while the PDP took a full page newspaper advertorial to pillory Salami with the worst and crudest of language, Katsina-Alu is pushing hard for his 'elevation' to the Supreme Court on the basis of his competence and fearlessness.
"So the same man being savaged by the PDP is the same one being considered for elevation to the Supreme Court because he is capable?
"What is happening? Who is fooling who? Have these people, in their hurry to remove Salami from the Court of Appeal, forgotten to tie the loose ends, thus exposing themselves for all to see? Do they think Nigeria is a banana republic where people can be hoodwinked by such unimaginative propositions?
"For clarity, we are not asking Jonathan to intervene in the workings of the judicial arm of government, since that will negate the separation of powers principle entrenched in the Constitution.
"What we are saying is that (he) should call his party to order, to desist from attacking the judiciary and seeking to influence its workings, as can be seen by the push to force Salami out of the Court of Appeal."
The ACN said the manner in which matter is being executed is unprecedented in the history of the judiciary, and seems to be in bad faith as well as a clear violation of established practice of the judicial system.
"In the first instance, the issue was not even on the agenda of the (FJSC) which met in January. It was reportedly smuggled into the agenda by (Katsina-Alu). Secondly, Salami's opinion was not sought, in line with tradition, before (Katsina-Alu) muscled through the proposition to 'elevate' him.
"Thirdly, Salami had rejected an offer to move to the Supreme Court in 2008, and is not known to have changed his mind since then.
"When these facts are placed side by side with the fact that Omisore has been relentlessly attacking and impugning on the integrity of Salami and other Court of Appeal Justices, without any word of caution from the government or any remedial action by the Attorney-General of the Federation and Minister of Justice, (Mohammed Adoke), it is very clear where the so-called promotion to the Supreme Court is coming from.
"We do not know how much of these developments are known to Jonathan. But we do know that if he allows the judiciary to be destroyed under his watch, posterity will not forgive him, since the buck stops at his desk."

Original_Search_Title: Mourners for Hire
Headline: Tanzania: Mourners for Hire
Body_Text:
Dar es Salaam — Would you cry at the funeral of somebody you don't even know, a person you never met during his or her lifetime? How would anybody work himself into a sombre mood at the funeral of a total stranger and break into sobs, shedding lots of tears? Well, faced with economic hardships and lack of jobs, some young people in Dar es Salaam are doing something that would never have been imagined only a few years ago. In fact, the traditionalists would have been shamed to death in the face of such a scenario.
But the young people are going to extremes to earn some money, and in some cases doing what to many would consider rather weird, such as offering to cry at funerals for a fee.
And this is not a scene from a stage play about death and funerals. It is the real thing - a new profession, mourning for hire, is taking root in Dar es Salaam.
While death has always been a source of income for funeral homes in the urban centres, coffin makers, transporters, grave diggers, and providers of other services, including catering, to give the dead a grand send-off, the idea of "professional mourners" is a brand new one.
But the rather morbid business of professional mourners is gaining popularity, with many families hiring strangers to cry at their relatives' funerals.
The Umoja wa Waombolezaji Misibani (UWAMI), a group of 'professional mourners' in Dar es Salaam is already gaining ground by offering what is increasingly becoming a major part of funerals in the city. They charge Sh150,000 for a day's service, which ends when the person is buried.
The business is fast catching on as the greater society opens up to adopt the traditional rites of communities or things that happen in other countries.
Some traditions demand the holding of grand funerals, where loud crying by close relatives, acquaintances, friends or associates, is meant to demonstrate deep love for the deceased.
The gift of acting (crying) among the UWAMI group members has been nurtured by their daily activities of making and selling coffins in the Manzese suburb of the city.
Comprising six members, the group was established in October last year to provide "crying services" at funerals for a fee.
Speaking to The Citizen of Saturday, UWAMI chairperson Frank Anthony said the members had been inspired by the search for a livelihood amid hardships due to lack of steady jobs to find an alternative source of income.
He said the idea was born, when, as they sold coffins, they would ask some of their customers to consider hiring some "readymade" mourners, as well.
A customer, who told them that he had heard on radio that such groups were already operating in Kenya, got them to turn their idea into reality.
They started out as a group of three, but as more city residents heard about the service and demand increased, more members joined.
In less than six months, the group has provided mourners for hire at three funerals, but is looking forward to a business boom as more people get to know about the service.
"We hope to provide well organised funeral crying services based on the needs of our customers from different ethnic and cultural backgrounds," said Mr Anthony.
But the chairman conceded that the group would have to work hard to cope with the different requests for their services.
He said that at some funerals, relatives asked them not to cry, but to "joyously sing along with the family members". He quipped: "It is serious business ... crying."
To prepare for a job, they need information on the age, sex and occupation of the dead person. They should also know about where he or she used to work, relations with the neighbours, workmates and family members.
The group must find out what exactly the family expects them to do. "In some communities, they do not cry all the time, but just sit waiting for relatives who come in crying, and they join in."
UWAMI members cry along with funeral music.
The professional mourners are served with ugali and meat and provided with alcoholic drinks to stimulate their crying.
For a session, a crate of beer, konyagi and banana, a local alcoholic drink, are sufficient.
Group member Faida Yassin said that judging from the income earned in the short time they have been offering the services, the future is promising.
Mr Yassin, a father of three children, said he was able to pay school fees for his son in secondary school with his earnings.
"This service will really change our lives if the society responds positively," he said.
However, he conceded that it had not been easy at the beginning, as they learned how to cry for pay.
A lecturer in psychology at the University of Dar es Salaam, Prof Issa Omari, said the phenomenon was part of society's development, and should be viewed positively.
"It is not a new thing, as it started in Kenya almost a decade ago. It is just a money making enterprise for jobless youth," he said. "There is nothing strange in a money-dominated society about allowing people to buy anything they want."
But Dar es Salaam residents have received the news of the "professional mourners" with mixed reactions.
Some of those interviewed said the emergence of such groups "simply shows the levels of frustration in the society due to lack of job openings".
Mr Stephen Maina, a 70-year-old resident of Mabibo, said it "is not part of Tanzanian culture".
He added: "Crying in a funeral is an act that close relatives or friends of the deceased can do. Having some hired people crying is shameful and unethical."
He said that in some traditions, having a stranger crying at a funeral was seen as an act that could result in a bad omen in the bereaved family.
"In Germany, France, China and England, where I've lived, the practice is quite different. They hire people to perform at funerals but not to cry."
He added: "I think even those who hire mourners must have some problems."
Mr Rashid Juma, a businessman at Kariakoo Market, said: "Tanzanians should not just copy anything, including services that add no value to our norms."
The vice-chairman of the Christian Council of Tanzania, Bishop Peter Kitala, dismissed UWAMI's services as ungodly, adding that crying at a funeral was a way of sharing the grief of the bereaved and should not be for financial gain.
"I urge the public to shun the services of the professional mourners. I find it really sorrowful that Tanzanians can go to that level of pretending just to get money," he said.
The clergyman said it was sinful to pretend to be in a certain state for personal gain.

Original_Search_Title: Increasing Commodity Prices Scare Teso Buyers
Headline: Uganda: Increasing Commodity Prices Scare Teso Buyers
Body_Text:
Teso — Prices for various essential commodities across the Teso sub-region have shot up in the last one week, raising fears of escalating cost of living during election time.
Traders running retail businesses in the region say they had been compelled to increase the prices following a corresponding rise in wholesale prices. In Bukedea town, where most retailers from Teso purchase their merchandise, a bag of cement now sells at Shs30,000 up from Shs24,000 a week ago.
Ms Joyce Akello, a shopkeeper in Bukedea town, on Wednesday said some consumers are hoarding essential items such as sugar and salt in anticipation of more price increases. "We don't know where the problem is coming from. The government should help prevent the scarcity from worsening," Ms Akello said.
A mini survey done by this newspaper in the towns of Ngora, Serere, Amuria and Katakwi indicated that the price for a bar of laundry soap has risen from Shs2,200 to Shs3, 200 while the cost of sugar has increased from Shs2,200 to Shs2,600.
The Chairman Chamber of Commerce eastern region, Mr Charles Elasu, blamed the rise in prices on traders who are hoarding essential items to attract bigger profits during the election season.
In Soroti, residents in the municipality are also feeling the pinch of rising prices of Irish potatoes, beans and bananas, which usually come from the Bugisu sub-region.

Original_Search_Title: Ulinzi Call On Fans - Come Cheer Team
Headline: Kenya: Ulinzi Call On Fans - Come Cheer Team
Body_Text:
Nairobi — Ulinzi Stars have made an impassioned plea to Kenyan football fans to turn out in large numbers on Saturday and cheer them to victory in their Caf Champions League preliminary round, first leg match against Zamalek of Egypt.
"We are begging the fans to come and support us. We are a Kenyan team. Having a big partisan crowd will give the boys a lot of morale," coach Benjamin Nyangweso said.
The possibilities of a poor turnout are very real for Ulinzi Stars, underlined by the slow sale of the advance match tickets this week.
"Ticket sales have not been very good. I think people are waiting for the last minute. We are waiting for tomorrow [Friday] to see how the sales go," an official of Ticket Master Outlets, the official ticket agents for the match, said on Thursday.
Tickets are retailing at Sh200 (terraces) and Sh500 (centre stand) and can be obtained at Nyayo National Stadium, City Stadium, Kenya Cinema and 20th Century. No tickets will be sold at the match venue on Saturday.
Elsewhere, Enyimba of Nigeria, Mouloudia Alger (Algeria), Club Africain (Tunisia) and Vita Club of Democratic Republic of Congo are other former champions who are away in the first leg.
Saint Michel Ouenze of Congo Republic could be a 'banana skin' for off-form Enyimba and Mouloudia will not relish facing Real Bangui in the Central African Republic where 2010 World Cup qualifiers Algeria were humbled last October.
Club Africain have made little impact since conquering Africa 20 years ago and must be wary of Rwandan military club APR while Vita should find the going easier against continental debutants Ocean View on the island of Zanzibar.

Original_Search_Title: Segun Aganga, Ten Months After
Headline: Nigeria: Segun Aganga, Ten Months After
Body_Text:
Finance Minister Segun Aganga is a very brilliant man. You do not become a managing director of Goldman Sachs, a leading investment banker in the world, for being stupid. And for a black man to have become so in the United Kingdom - an essentially White world - meant there must have been one or two things going for him besides his obvious academic brilliance.
When he was appointed Finance Minister, some Diaspora Nigerians proudly appropriated him as representing their 'constituency' in the government. But has Dr Aganga, managing director in hedge funds at Goldman Sachs since 2003 until he was appointed Finance Minister, really delivered?
Several observations could be made:
One, in our type of countries, where the institutions are weak and the wishes of the man of power are virtually law, it will be unfair to completely blame any Finance Minister for the failings of the economy.
For Dr Aganga who was appointed Finance Minister on the eve of an election year in which the President is also a candidate, assessing his performance becomes even more complicated since political considerations are often the dominant considerations in policy-making and execution in an election year.
My personal opinion is that the legacy of Dr Aganga, an expert in wealth management, may well be tied to the fate of the National Sovereign Wealth Fund, which has his finger prints all over it.
Two, while Dr Aganga can be excused for any non-performance of the economy, it is possible to assess the extent to which he has brought the 'x-factor' to governance - positive intangibles that reflect his long years of sojourn in Europe and from being a director of a major investment bank.
My strong feeling here is that he takes criticisms of the economy by top politicians and public figures as a direct attack on his competence and consequently reacts inappropriately. A case in point was when former Vice President repeatedly expressed concerns about the direction of the economy, in particular over the regime's accumulation of huge loans and depletion of both foreign reserves and the Excess Crude Account.
There is no doubt that Atiku's concerns about the economy were, as should be expected of any presidential candidate, tainted with politics. Unusual for a finance minister, Dr Aganga jumped into the fray and warned Atiku, a politician, not to 'play politics' with the economy.
In our political tradition, a minister is considered too junior to take a direct shot at a presidential candidate, especially a credible one who had been a former Vice President of the country. Dr Aganga further told Atiku that the 'economy is too technical for some people to understand' (ThisDay, November 25, 2010). In countries like the USA, this statement would have opened him up to charges of 'elitism' - of talking down to people.
Three, ironically while Dr Aganga believes that the 'economy is too technical for some people to understand', some economists hold a similar 'disdainful' view of him on grounds that he is not a macroeconomist, has not published any paper on Nigerian economy and has questionable understanding of how Nigeria works, having been living abroad for too long.
I have several times found myself playing the role of Dr Aganga's unofficial spokesman, especially among economists - not because he is a fellow Diasporan but more because I have tremendous respect for what he has accomplished at a personal level and also believe that with his exposure he will be able to attract competencies that will complement his own skills. Dr Aganga graduated in 1980 with a First Class Honours in Financial Accounting from the University of Ibadan and subsequently did post graduate studies at Oxford University, UK.
Four, with the lull in political activities following the end of the primaries, it may perhaps be germane to have honest discussion about the direction of the economy. Besides the undeniable depletion in foreign reserves and excess crude accounts, there are other worrying statistics: The Vanguard of July 20 2010 reported that the federal government raised alarm over the increasing rate of unemployment in the country which official figures said was at 19.47% (some say the real figure is likely to be around 40-50%). On November 5, 2010, the Nigerian Tribune reported that the "World Economic Forum (WEF) has slammed Nigeria over alleged poor governance and inadequate infrastructure financing" and downgraded the country to 127 out of 139 countries.
In the 2010 Human Development Report compiled by the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), Nigeria was grouped among the 41 countries considered to have the "least human development". According to the Report, Nigeria's wealth --as defined by gross domestic product per head--has slipped.
The GDP per head in Nigeria was given as a mere 1,224 dollars, compared to 9,812 dollars in South Africa, 1,628 dollars in Kenya and 2,197 dollars in Cameroon. On life expectancy, the Report said that in 2010 Nigeria's life expectancy was 48.4 years on average, below that of Ghana (57.1.), Cameroon (51.7), Benin Republic (62.3), and Uganda (54.1). Meanwhile a list released by Transparency International on October 26, 2010 showed that Nigeria has fallen four steps (from 121st position in 2008 to 130th in 2009 to 134th in 2010).
Again in October 2010, it was reported that Nigeria slipped from 35th to 40th position in the Mo Ibrahim good governance ranking of 53 countries in Africa. Similarly a survey conducted as part of its membership operational audit in January 2010 by the Manufacturers Association of Nigeria, showed that 839 manufacturing companies closed shop in 2009 as a result of harsh operating environment. . With these statistics - and politics aside - it is difficult for any patriotic Nigerian not to be concerned.
Five, with due respect, I disagree with the Finance Minister that expressing what I will regard as robust concerns about the direction of the economy amounts to deliberately talking down the economy. I am uncomfortable with some of the language used by the Minister in replying to critics, including his use of innuendo to suggest that Professor Chukwuma Soludo, former Governor of the Central Bank, should be in jail- apparently for his rather ill-advised harsh criticisms of the direction of the economy in the media.
In the 'marketplace of ideas' theory of democracy, it is generally believed that "the best of truth is the power of the thought to get itself accepted in the competition of the market". And this implies looking beyond the messenger - even if his ideas offend shock or infuriate - to interrogate his message and accommodate those that are useful.
It is precisely this capacity to look beyond a repugnant messenger and to accommodate uncomfortable criticisms that separates mature democracies from banana republics.

Original_Search_Title: The Casualties of Nwodo's Fall
Headline: Nigeria: The Casualties of Nwodo's Fall
Body_Text:
Political analysts and commentators are of the view that the exit of Dr. Okwesilieze Nwodo as the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) National Chairman was a disaster foretold. So much has been said and written about Dr. Nwodo and how he came from what many termed a political sabbatical owing to his loss of his position as the National Secretary of the party. He had been accused along with some other people in the National Identity Card financial scam.
All the commentators seem to share the same opinion that his fall started soon after he was made chairman through waivers arranged by the Enugu state Governor, Sullivan Chime who later was at the receiving end of Nwodo's alleged overbearing influence.
I would not bother myself with how Nwodo's fall started or the intrigues that preceded his fall as many would hold from his Enugu State, the South East, the National Working Committee (NWC) of the PDP, all the flash points of PDP chapters that started boiling soon after he became PDP chairman etc.
I would not equally bother myself about how his 'reform agenda' was received, the e-registration that died on arrival or his 'deepening' of democracy programme. All those are matters for another day.
The first group of people that created a huge crisis of confidence for Nwodo was the hydra-headed members of the PDP 'Reform Group' that high jacked him soon after he became the party chairman, set an agenda for him and blackmailed him into being seen as their man Friday.
The insincerity of the 'Reform Group' in the whole gamble became obvious when subsequent actions they took gave them away as individuals who wanted to destroy PDP before abandoning it. That sent the first warning signal and perhaps prepared the ground for those who saw everything jejune about Nwodo's chairmanship to start laying the infamous banana pills.
The grapevine had it that Baba OBJ, for example, did advise against a President Jonathan's interest in his Chairmanship, which he considered a political hemlock. What I may not say for sure is whether Baba grinned, frowned or laughed (keh-keh) when Nwodo mounted the saddle but definitely all feelers pointed to the fact that the Board of Trustees under former President Obasanjo did not see in Nwodo the chairman that the party deserved.
Perhaps for reasons based on the zeal to perform or overwhelmed by the challenges of the office, he started opening so many 'war fronts' the most disturbing of which was the face-off between him and the Governor of his state, Sullivan Chime, who against all odds, played a major role in projecting him and the National Working Committee of the party that sold the impression that he was running a 'one man show'.
Whether the roughshod he had with some PDP governors, NWC members, party leadership in some states and some other party chieftains at the national level should naturally earn him the boot is not an issue. The greatest worry remains the numerous casualties of his short-lived outing.
The effects of this fall will resonate for months and years to come and would definitely outlive the current actors on the scene. Ironically, it appears a grand way of completing the cycle that has been projecting Ndigbo as politically naïve and crassly incapable of handling high political offices.
In the last republic, it was seemingly a political musical chair when the Igbo were expected by the PDP to provide leadership at the upper chamber of the National Assembly. Within a period of eight short years, literarily all Senators from the zone either had a taste of the Senate President's chair or had the conviction or worked towards ensuring that they get to sit on it. The political treachery was benumbing.
This time around, the exit of Prince Vincent Ogbulafor, the former PDP National Chairman and now Dr. Okwesilieze Nwodo, presents an ugly scenario and many are beginning to joke that between now and the next five months that the Ogbulafor tenure will be served out, Igbo, if allowed may have five more PDP chairmen to go round the five South Eastern states at an average of one each month.
Honestly, given a thorough political diagnosis, neither a President Jonathan nor an Atiku Abubakar is a direct casualty of the fall of a Nwodo, who prides himself as a former party secretary. It is now doubtful whether experience is the best teacher in this instance.
The PDP with its high turnover of chairmen since the party's inception in 1999 has proved the late Dr. Chuba Okadigbo right because he maintained that the political system in Nigeria has not given birth to solid political parties because of lack of people with similar political ideology. He referred to the different political parties as mere rallies peopled by people who had little or nothing in common. There seem to be too many big men in the PDP and that undoubtedly has engendered lots of internal crises.
The party itself has seen exit of some members who ordinarily might not have left the party. The Nwodo- Chime incident might prove a big political metaphor of one biting the finger that fed him. At the recent PDP national convention, there was a subdued rumour that the former chairman's action raises doubt about trust in the political equation in the country.
Again, the preceding events that led to the fall of Nwodo points the searchlight to what has been like an intractable political irony of the average Igbo politician at the Abuja political angle. It does appear that instead of being a plus to the economic and political life of the zone those who go there either through elections or appointments suffer some chronic messianic complex that set them against their home states.
We are still smarting from the Ekwueme-Jim, Anyim-Egwu, Orji Kalu-Ojo Maduekwe-Adolphus Wabara, Nnamani-Chime, and Chris Ngige-Ubas imbroglios of the past. The supremacy complex that 'Abuja' conjures in the average Igbo political big man has been a minus for the people.
Now that the chairmanship of the biggest party in the land has slipped out from Ndigbo, it is wryly interesting to consume the rumour that the South East governors are urging the party to forget appointing a new chairman till after the April elections. Who would really blame them? Self preservation even for politicians is not a crime. Who wants to empower a Frankenstein?
However, a bigger lesson from the Nwodo fall should be instructive to all the casualties, the man, the party, the governors, Ndigbo in general, the political class and the rest of the Nigerian citizens. In our quest to imbibe the best tenets of democracy, there would be natural, systemic and human problems that only the love of the larger community can solve. Selflessness in service attracts and retains followership more than the urge to build fiefdoms and this goes from the lowest level to the highest. That alone is the route to true greatness.

Original_Search_Title: Will Africa's Despots Want South to Secede?
Headline: Sudan: Will Africa's Despots Want South to Secede?
Body_Text:
A 'dinosaur' named Laurent Gbagbo has made sure that his obstinacy is at least as strong as the will of all the people determined to tear his claws from the reins of the Ivorian state.
The sustained tension in Cote d'Ivoire and the rapidly moving events in Tunisia have robbed some attention from the referendum on the secession of Southern Sudan. But by the time this article appears in print, it will have probably been confirmed that the South has overwhelmingly chosen to exist as a separate state. So, welcome to the club of banana republics that have oil.
Western government officials (generally referred to as The International Community) find comfort in deluding themselves that Africa has been steadily democratising, that the bad apples are in a minority. Why; because such a picture vindicates the West.
Otherwise, the packages of aid, resource exploitation and timid approach to Africa's political perverseness, which together roughly define the West's engagement with Africa, would be deemed a failure. Movement towards Western-styled democracy, and respect for human rights have been major demands in that engagement. However, in practice, once the West has determined who its allies on the continent are, especially in the context of security and big business, the values of political morality associated with Western civilisation receive little more than lip service.
The only condition seems to be that the ally must devise a system that produces (even if by barely concealed deception) numbers that on paper look like expressions of democracy and socio-economic development. The numbers that come from general elections and parliamentary votes, the numbers of privately owned newspapers and radio stations, the numbers that reflect reductions in one-dollar poverty... etc.
Even when you have a parliament whose majority is totally indifferent whether it is perceived as a host of prostitutes in the short term or a bunch of traitors in the long term, the numbers backing its conspiracies and its decisions will satisfy a friendly West, a pinch of salt notwithstanding.
The Ivorian dinosaur is in trouble, not because he is more of a rogue than, say, Ethiopia's Zenawi, but because he failed to cook the right numbers. South Sudan, whose independence I fully support, is unfortunately probably doomed. The foreign sharks swimming in its waters have only their self interest to play for.
The neighbouring states will play for advantage in trade and regional influence. The moralising of the West will disappear in its contest with China for fossil resources, and the barrier perceived as holding off ideological Islam will breed new brutes and accelerate the growth of the Southern Sudanese military. The Cold War ended so long ago that most of Africa's reigning despots were partly projects of the West, or at least were subsequently supported by the West. They are cynical, but not stupid; they make calculations.
They cannot fail to see that if Southern Sudan is a successful example of democracy and social justice, their record, by contrast, will suffer. If they drag down the new country as they share in the spoils of its "liberation", they would grin, shake hands and sit more comfortably at African Union summits. Gen. Bashir in Khartoum might even get new friends from among his bitter enemies of yesterday. And if Southern Sudan masters the art of producing the right numbers, the West will live with it.
Ultimately, a failed Southern Sudan would help Africa's bad apples to hold the old boundaries as sacrosanct, arguing that even in this very special case, it had been demonstrated that there was no benefit in dismantling or reviewing the African states in their present form. So maybe it is just as well that Cote d'Ivoire and Tunisia stole some of the attention from Southern Sudan, linking a possible future to the darkness of the present time.
Mr Tacca is a novelist, socio-political commentator and artist

Original_Search_Title: OT Africa Adds Fifth Ship to Europe-West Africa Service
Headline: Nigeria: OT Africa Adds Fifth Ship to Europe-West Africa Service
Body_Text:
OT Africa Line restructured its roll-on, roll-off service from Europe to Africa by adding a fifth ship to the service line, the company said Tuesday, in an effort to offer more frequent sailings departing from Europe every 10 days.
This rotation connects the ports of Rouen and Antwerp to Pointe Noire, Congo; Port Gentil, Gabon; Libreville, Gabon; Douala, Cameroon and Abidjan, Cote d'Ivoire.
OT Africa, which is part of the CMA CGM Group, said the service offers a dedicated ro-ro service catering for industries such as oil, gas and mining as well as transporting industrial projects as well as vehicles and containerized cargo. It connects with transit services from Dublin, Rotterdam, Amsterdam, Hamburg and Tilbury.
Onward connections to all main ports in Angola and DRC are offered via Pointe Noire. These destinations include Soyo, Boma, Matadi, Banana, Cabinda, Lobito and Namibe.
CMSA CGM acquired OT Africa Line in 2005. Established in 1975, the company operates a fleet of multi-purpose ro-ro and container vessels that serves routes to, from and within West Africa.

Original_Search_Title: Artists Told to Step Up Campaigns Against Malaria
Headline: Tanzania: Artists Told to Step Up Campaigns Against Malaria
Body_Text:
THE Minister for Health and Social Welfare Haji Mponda has told local artists who have come together for 'Malaria No More' campaign, to step up their efforts and raise awareness about malaria and the urgent need to eradicate the disease.
He told them when they paid him a visit in Dar es Salaam on Wednesday that there was still a long way to got to eradicate the disease because he said, some members of the public were still ignorant especially on how to treat the disease.
"You need to continue working tirelessly to educate the public through your artistic work on how malaria is transmitted and the means of overcoming it. Some people just ignore to treat themselves, especially youths," he said.
The minister further said that musicians can reach the wider public and the musicians' positive messages will help the public take malaria seriously.
He also paid tribute to the founders of the 'Malaria No More' project, saying their efforts to fight against malaria in the country will succeed.
Over a dozen of Tanzania's brightest stars have come together for the Zinduka! Malaria Haikubaliki campaign to raise awareness about malaria and the urgent need to eradicate the disease.
The campaign's title stands for "Wake Up! Malaria Is Unacceptable." Organized by the global nonprofit Malaria No More and Tanzanian President Jakaya Kikwete, the message empowers Tanzanians to take responsibility for their future by working to ensure that every single person has access to a mosquito net, to ward off the insects that carry malaria.
Speaking on behalf of other 'Malaria No More' Ambassadors, local hip hop star Joseph Haule alias Professor Jay called for the ministry's support, insisting that they to some extent have succeeded to transform the public to take effective precautions against the killer disease.
The ten ambassadors who visited the ministers included Profesa Jay, Lady Jaydee, Fid Q, Barnaba, Marlow, Mwasiti, Diamond, Amini, Banana Zoro and B Twelve.

Original_Search_Title: Kisakaate Nurtures Leaders
Headline: Uganda: Kisakaate Nurtures Leaders
Body_Text:
Gayaza — Flags of Uganda, Buganda and Kisakaate flew high up at the closing ceremony of the fifth Kisakaate that was held at Gayaza Junior School on Monday.
The theme, "Culture Nurtures Good Leadership", was clearly displayed as the basakaate (children that attended the function) were dressed in cream T-shirts that bore the words of the theme. It was chosen purposely to enable the young people get acquainted with leadership skills and knowledge as the country prepares for next month's general elections.
In Luganda, Kisakaate is a word derived to mean an enclosure. The concept dates as far as ancient Buganda. In the traditional setting, the Kabaka had chiefs who made it a point to fence their homesteads with long reeds. Within the homesteads, their children as well as those of their colleagues were groomed in cultural ideals. It is from this lot that the next Kabaka would choose another generation of chiefs, palace officials or even wives.
To emphasise how cultural this year's ceremony was, a drum was placed high up on a table clothed in white. For those who were in doubt of the relevance of the kisakaate, the children's narration throughout the event kept spelling it out. "We have learnt how to make baskets, bagiya, 'daddies', and black-cloth bags," they chanted.
Speaking at the closing ceremony of the two-weeks training camp, Nnaabagereka Sylvia Nagginda called on the country's leadership to embrace culture in the struggle to serve Ugandans wholly, as expected. "If we take on culture seriously, we will rest assured of good leadership for our country. This is the main basis for our kisakaate this year, to mould young leaders who attach importance to culture," the Nnaabagereka said at the function.
In the training, which attracted over 600 children ranging from six to 18 years, children were taught how to cook, bake, peel, learnt art and crafts, sewing, self defence and co-curricular activities that included dancing, playing instruments and singing among others.
It was Naswifah Nambikiire,18, however, who surprised many people. With her halting Luganda, she let the crowds know that tying matooke (bananas) was not as simple as many may think, but a rather mind boggling process if the names of the things used are anything to go by. "When peeling matooke, the first thing that is cut off, is called the kajanja," she said, leaving the audience astounded.
She went on to state that pairs of banana leaves and fibres are used because most parts of our bodies are paired, stating also that, the kawuwo (the most peripheral on the banana leaf) can be male or female. The male is one that makes the final cover because a man's role is to guard and protect the home.
Prizes for best
There were prizes that were given to the best and 14-year-old Trevor Mugisha could not contain his joy as he emerged the best musakaate, walking away with a bicycle. Mr Andrew Mukiibi, the head of trainers in the 2011 kisakaate, urged parents to continuously support this yearly activity, which is spearheaded by the Office of the Nnaabagereka and that moulds children into young leaders by enabling them to love their culture and enhance their talents.
"Our children have been brought up with less knowledge about culture and morals because parents have less time for them today. The kisaakate has helped play this role and indeed you will all appreciate that a wonderful job has been done," he said. The final stretch of the ceremony attracted dignitaries from the government of Uganda and other dignitaries from Buganda kingdom.

Original_Search_Title: Take a Walk on the West Side, It's Wild
Headline: East Africa: Take a Walk on the West Side, It's Wild
Body_Text:
Nairobi — There is a whole new West Side Story emerging in Nairobi.
It is rapidly becoming part of the city that art lovers are making their own.
A drive through Parklands to Limuru Road and on ever westwards is where the interest is growing.
First stop, 2nd Parklands Avenue to pay our respects to the RaMoMA in the hope it might survive not only as the children's art school, which is to remain open, but also as a group of galleries.
The trustees met earlier this month to consider the future of the place and it was decided the operating arm of the business, RaMoMA Ltd, would be put in mothballs.
But that still leaves the RaMoMA Trust, which owns among other things the permanent collection.
No mention was made of selling the land and buildings - surely a temptation in an area where land prices are increasing exponentially.
However one of the trustees, Lyn Fuss, was later at pains to point out that should a sale eventually become inevitable, all the proceeds would go into other charitable projects, mainly involving the arts, in Kenya.
I understand that while it is true that RaMoMA is more or less at a standstill, the possibility remains of reopening the galleries, perhaps for occasional shows in aid of worthwhile causes, or even for hire.
Ever westward and next stop is the Village Market with its two large exhibition halls, which frequently feature strong shows by local artists.
I remember with pleasure those of sculpture by Irene Wanjiru and, more recently, large canvases by Patrick Mukabi.
A little further along the road is Rosslyn, home to the One-Off gallery run by Carol Lees, former manager of the RaMoMA.
One-Off is the base for some of Kenya's major artists, many with international reputations.
At any one time you can expect to see excellent examples by Timothy Brook, Peter Elungat, Peterson Kamwathi, Richard Kimathi, Justus Kyalo, Jesse N'gang'a, Emily Odongo and Beatrice Wanjiku, among others.
A relatively new gallery is next on the visiting list, at Ruaka.
Just before the turn off to Banana Hill and opposite the Delta petrol station is the Kilele Africa (sub-titled Contemporary Art Studio) run by a group of four artists: Cosmos Musyoka, John Ndung'u, Patricia Njeri and Alex Wainaina.
Wainaina is the one who first catches your eye. His sculptures, bent and welded from scrap metal, parade on the gallery forecourt ... birds, a buffalo, a hippo's head, a warthog or two and a giraffe.
Lifelike
Scrap metal sculpture is becoming very popular (as a glance at Ngong Road near the racecourse will confirm), but whereas the racecourse sculptures tend to be as lifelike as their creators can make them, Wainaina goes for caricature. "I try to make them more interpretive," he says.
The Kilele Africa opened only last year and houses the artists' studios as well as a small sales area, which also contains pieces by other painters and sculptors.
These include some small birds made out of driftwood - breathtaking little things that absolutely capture the essence of their subjects.
They are so good they transcend the normal run of craft shop works to become pieces of fine art in their own right.
One example is a seated ibis that reaches forward to stab the air with its bill, the ruffled feathers of its plump body indicated to perfection by the natural ridges of the untouched knotted wood.
The difficult thing with this type of work (with most types of work, come to think of it) is knowing when to stop. This sculptor is in complete control. Simply brilliant.
Unfortunately, the Kilele owners do not have his name, recalling only that he wandered in one day with work to sell. I hope he wanders in again, this time with his calling card.
Turning right to Banana Hill leads you up to the studio centre run by the painters Shine Tani and his wife Rahab.
I reviewed their current show, by Ash Uman and Wainaina, last week.
You can go west too, if you like to see artists at work.
The colourist Mary Collis and figure painter Fitsum Berhe both work on the west side of the city (at Kiberage and Kitisuru respectively) while Banana Hill and Ngeche villages are home to more artists than you can wave a wallet at.
They include painters Cartoon Joseph, Wanyu and Eunice Brush, Chain Muhandi, Allan Githuku, Tabitha wa Thuku and James Mbuthia and the sculptors Evanson Kang'ethe and Morris Foit, and many more besides.
So if you want art, go west. It's wild.
Frank Whalley runs Lenga Juu, a fine arts and media consultancy based in Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: Horse Racing Up
Headline: Uganda: Horse Racing Up
Body_Text:
UGANDA has produced another sporting star in a rather unfamiliar sport -- horse racing. Katie Bowser, a Ugandan youngster rode to victory winning 15 trophies in the five-day Kenya 'Horse of the year show' held in Nairobi recently.
The 13-year-old's haul of trophies came from the Dressage, Combined training, Showing and Show Jumping racing categories at the annual event.
Crowning moment
Her crowning moment came when she was awarded the Grand Championship trophy and Sash of Honor. She became the first youngster to win this accolade.
Bowser, who trains at Flametree Stables club in Kampala, started riding horses at the age of two and has never looked back.
At the Nairobi event, she won two successive children finals after piloting Pendragon her horse in the Childrens Victor Ludorum race.
Liam Walker, 17, came home with the reserve Victor Ludorum on his Ugandan horse Cochise while his sister Kayleigh won some categories on her pony Flying fox. Nina Morley on her pony Pipit was also well placed in all her races.
Other riders at the event were Geoff Morley, Siyan Hudda, Tariq Hudda and Alexia Demetriov.
Bowser mooted idea
Horses were introduced in Uganda by businessman Sudhir Ruparelia and a horse riding enthusiast Miranda Bowser in 1996.
They picked Speke Resort-Munyonyo to launch the sport. Visitors at the resort who had interest in the horses did rounds of riding along with their children in an enclosure the size of half a football field.
But what started as Miranda's hobby has now transformed into an horse riding school, the first in Uganda.
"We started in January last year. I had always kept horses as a hobby but when people came to me with their children for a ride, the hobby grew to what we have today," Miranda said.
"It's a massive investment, a dream come true for me. We have so far invested $150,000 minus the money that was used to buy the land," Miranda who owns 15 of the 21 horses at the site adds.
Flametree stableLocated on a 20-acres piece of land, a 30 minute drive from Kampala at Kijabijo village along Gayaza road, Flametree as the first riding stable in Kampala provides an opportunity for kids from four years and adults to learn how to ride Ponies.
It offers private and group lessons, show jumping and an exciting cross country course to learners with no riding experience.
Miranda travels with ten horses, up to 5 times a year to compete alongside Kenyan riders.
Ugandan junior riders have always come top against their kenyan rivals. Miranda also competes with the senior riders.
Miranda and Ugandan horse manager/instructor Robin Kasumba train their customers in Dressage, Combined training, Showy and Show Jumping and some cross country races over a stretch of 3-5kms at Flametree.
Costs of the races vary with a 20- minute leading rein going for sh25, 000 while one would need sh60, 000 for a 45-minute private outing.
Other group lessons fall within the cost bracket above.
The Lake Victoria Swimming-Hack which requires a minimum of four riders can also be provided depending on number of riders available.
The Kenya factor
Majority of the horses come from Kenya while a couple are frown from South Africa with the help of Ruparelia. A horse costs beteeen $500-5000.
Each one of the horses live in its own stable inscribed with fancy names, nick names or names of owners.
