Names in clude Pipit aka pips, Jana (Jana Banana), Dr. Doolittle (Dr.D), Boadicea (Boda boda), Mr. Brumby (Bamps), Purdey (Purds), Winnie the Pooh (Winnie), Pendragon (Fupi), Geronimo (Jelly), Cochise (Cheesy), Flying fox (Foxy) among others.
Various breeds
"The majority of horse breeds come from Kenya, Ethiopia and Sudan. We have the warm blood, Bush Ponys that we cross with Thourough bred, Pony Blood lines, American saddle bred and Irish draught. We keep on cross mating them. We have lots of different mixes," Miranda said "
Unlike in Europe where horse racing is associated with betting, Miranda said theirs hasn't reached that stage.
But a few individuals can bet on their horses winning particular races on race tracks.
"We do more technical things and teach skills like jumping over huddles," Miranda added.
Training regime
"We train our horses to a high level to eliminate accidents and we have a safe track record.
"We have not encountered any major accidents though some kids do fall off."
She said that their equipment has safety approval and the horses are child friendly.
Kasumba, who has 10 years experience as an instructor, does not only help in training people but also runs programmes to keep the horses fit so that they follow orders properly when engaged by different riders.
Instructor's job
"My job involves schooling and exercising the horses. Like a footballer they have to keep fit in order to jump over the hurdles".
Just like riding a bike, Kasumba says it's the riders balance and technique that keeps the animal moving without the rider falling off.
"We always do group lessons with a maximum of three people and are always very busy at the weekend.
During the weekend, we sometime handle a maximum of 60 riders," Kasumba adds.
The official said that transport to the site can be arranged on request for those wishing to have the experience of riding Ponies.
Kasumba and his group are keen on their work and are determineds to take their programmme to another level.

Original_Search_Title: Issues as Mark Seeks Fourth Term
Headline: Nigeria: Issues as Mark Seeks Fourth Term
Body_Text:
It was not surprising that David Mark emerged as the PDP flag bearer for Benue South Senatorial District. The sheer quality and quantity of crowd from all works of life that stormed Idoma land for his declaration for a 4th tenure at the Senate was an obvious prelude. You see, when a flying bird jacks and dips in air, you can guess on which tree it would land.
Way back in April 2009, I had seized the opportunity the Senate President's birthday to do an opinion piece entitled "David Mark: Amalinze the Cat at 61". It was a sort of mid-term assessment of his Senate Presidency. Likening him to the legendary wrestling champion, Amanlinze the cat whose back remained a stranger to ground throughout his career even though he wrestled malevolent men and ventured into the land of the spirits, I said Mark's strength lies in his humble dispositions often misjudged by rivals. And that is the strength of all members of the cat's family- lion, leopard, tiger, pussycat, etc. They move as though they are afraid of even the ground they walk on, yet the spirits beneath quake at their thuds and the animal kingdom live in their awe. Indeed, Mark is humble, but he is loaded. He reminds one that it is not the size of the dog, but the fight in the dog that matters. The manner he fought off the vicious gang-up against his third voyage in 2007 shows he no boardroom General. Mark may be soft spoken, but his actions roar like a lion. And why not? After all, looking through his training and career which spans virtually the whole continents, it is correct to say he has seen it all.
These notwithstanding, however, only a few gave Mark and his Deputy, Ike Ekeremadu any chances of lasting in office, let alone leading a successful Senate. Bayed at by hot litigations against his mandate, Mark remained focused. Good still, in Ekweremadu, Mark has a loyal lieutenant and companion like the Amalinze's flutist who spoke the language he understood, warned him where danger lurked, and urged him on whenever the going got tough. Incidentally, both men are very unassuming. They may not have the charisma and flamboyance of Chuba Okadigbo, the massive stature of Anyim Pius, the oratory of Adolphus Nwabara or the braggadocio of some others, but they have quietly taken the Senate to the next level and repositioned it in outlook and efficiency.
It is a fact, for instance, that Mark inherited a Senate renown for instability occasioned by incessant power struggle. Add to it the heavy baggage of credibility challenge inflicted by the infamous "banana peels". But Mark has so far run an open-door policy and promoted accountability. In an unprecedented move in 2008, the Senate under him returned N7 billion unspent allocation to public coffers. Again, despite the stiff challenges he faced in the race for the Senate Presidency, Mark leads a witch-hunt free Senate. He has used respect and fairness as a magic wand to rally the Upper Chamber in solidarity, unity of purpose and efficiency. Mark's leadership has a listening ear and have in many instances deferred to public voice as in the recent move to conscript NASS members into party NECs. Today, the Mark-Ekweremadu leadership is not just the longest serving Senate Presidency in this dispensation, but also the most stable and efficient.
History will also remember Mark's Chairmanship of the current National Assembly for the amendment of the 1999 Constitution. Mark will pride himself as the man who, together with his Deputy, provided the leadership that resulted in the first real constitution amendment since 1960 against all odds and scepticism. The only one before it was the minor alteration to give Nigeria the status of a republic and the creation of Wid-West Region about 47 years ago!
Mark's will further be remembered for charting a new trajectory for our democracy through extensive and strategic reforms of the electoral system vides the amendment of the Constitution and the Electoral Act. Among others, INEC is now both administratively and financially independent. Internal party democracy, staggered elections, and accelerated and fixed timeframe for election litigations are now all entrenched in our electoral legal frameworks.
Yet despite the challenges at the centre, Mark has effectively touched the lives of his constituents in both his official and personal capacities through projects that build the human and infrastructural capital of the State. For instance, he personally built, equipped and donated a multimillion naira Study Centre to the National Open University of Nigeria in Otukpo. There are so many water, road, educational, and health projects to his name. He runs a scholarship scheme that handed over N20 Million in scholarships to 1,768 students of Benue origin in 2009 and increased it in 2010.
But there must be a correlation between Mark's performance and his experience as a public administrator and ranking Senator. Unlike an executive, the career of a lawmaker gets better with age like wine. The US democracy succeeds partly on the longevity of service of lawmakers. As at June 2010, 25 US Senators had served between 33 - 51 years. They include Robert Byrd (Democrat, West Virginia) who died in Office in June after serving for about 51 years and 5 months and Edward Kennedy who also died in Office after 46 years, 9 months and 19 days in the Senate. Daniel K. Inouye, Strom Thurmond, Joe Bidden (now US Vice President), etc have all put in between 47 to 36 years in the Senate.
However, as in the developed democracies and in Mark's case, lawmakers should earn re-election by a dint of hard work, commitment to common good, and in credible electoral processes. Incidentally, Mark is the first Senate President to seek re-election since 1999 and the Amalinze's has just scaled the primary hurdle because it is founded on public good. The likes of Mark and Ekweremadu deserve another berth at the Senate, for one good turn deserves another.
Uche Anichukwu, Public Affairs Analyst, Abuja

Original_Search_Title: Out of Africa, the Fine Art of Cubism
Headline: East Africa: Out of Africa, the Fine Art of Cubism
Body_Text:
Nairobi — One of the great innovations of painting took place between 1907 and 1914 in a series of dingy Parisian studios.... the creation of Cubism.
Its inventors were Pablo Picasso and Georges Braque. They worked out the technique of deconstructing and reassembling their subjects with such commonplace themes as still life (often using the guitar and the violin as metaphors for the female body) the human figure and landscape.
First they fragmented their subject matter and then put the pieces back together in an order that enabled people to see the back and sides (and sometimes the insides, too) at the same time as the front.
It increased understanding and gave an added reality, they said.
Immediately they attracted followers including Juan Gris and Ferdinand Leger who became equally obsessed by this new way of seeing.
Before long Cubism ceased to be a revolution on canvas and became part of the canon of European art.
Picasso and Braque were both collectors of tribal art - masks, ancestor figures and such like -- and were in fact not so much inventing a new form of art but providing a European framework for something that African sculptors already knew well, and had demonstrated in many works.
For instance, some figures of the Shankadi Luba were carved with their flexed knees seemingly shooting forward into the dimension they would occupy should the figure run, indicating dynamic movement and force.
The Fang of Gabon foreshortened the arms and legs of their reliquary guardian figures to indicate simultaneously infancy and adulthood; the radical compression of time through space.And the Songye carved their masks and figures almost literally as a series of cubes.
"Out of Africa, always something new..."
Which brings us neatly, if somewhat bizarrely, from Aristotle and Pliny the Elder - both of whom quoted that ancient Greek proverb -- to the Banana Hill Art Studio just a little way Out of Nairobi.
For there the New Year has kicked off with the first of the promised short and snappy exhibitions (a strict limit of two weeks each) by artists from the region with a show of some 35 paintings by Ash Uman. It opened this weekend.
The point about Uman -- aged 46 and a painter for some 25 years - is that he is as overtly influenced by early Cubism as any artist I have seen in East Africa, including Francis Kahuri who is frequently billed as the Kenyan Picasso.
The good thing about Uman is that he uses the technique to explore his interest in relationships and the fragility of possessions rather than as an end in itself.
"You can have $1 million in the bank but it won't save you if you are drowning in the river," he told me.
The brittle self-consciousness of the style - he dips in and out of Cubism at will, often in the same picture - is an ideal reflection of his subject matter.
Perhaps it is his experience as a map-maker that enables Uman to use space so well. He seems to have plenty of it to spare even in the most densely textured of his paintings.
One example is his Priest in the Bedroom, a Rabelaisian allusion to the difficulties of maintaining celibacy.
The temptress wears a cat on her head. It appears ready to take the bird perched protectively on her breast while the priest looks seemingly askance, yet already has one leg stretched across hers.
He wears a virginal white vestment which contrasts pleasingly with the woman's blue gown; an echo of the Madonna, in colour if not in pose.
A Ugandan from Katwe, Kampala, who has made his home outside Nairobi, Uman shows us Lust again in the person of The Envoy... paunchy with wealth and closing in on a desperate looking woman against a background that can only be Mombasa.
Here the figures are more spatially reconfigured, particularly in the lower foreground. And there are 33 other pictures to get your teeth into.
In another painting, this time untitled, a man clutches a guitar. A closer look shows it has no strings. At first sight an important possession, yet ultimately one seen to have no value.
This is a fascinating exhibition, well worth a visit for the way the artist uses his well developed technique to explore and elaborate on his subject.
Alas, for the most part, that subject seems to be a cynical view of all our human failings.
Showing with Uman is Alex Wainaina who makes animals and birds out of scrap metal.
They are great fun...whether a perky little starling fashioned from bent wire and an old can or an ostrich with a hexagonal nut for its head.
His buffalo looks duly cantankerous and his hippo - again a lifesize head - has that misleading touch of humour that the real things seem to possess, until (we are told) you get in their way.
Wainaina's winning creatures are influenced too... not by Braque and Picasso (whose bull's head made from a bicycle saddle and handlebars remains an icon for all sculptors who use found objects) but by inspiration nearer home - Nairobi's Kioko Mwitiki, who was the first and, in my opinion, remains the best of the lot.
Frank Whalley runs Lenga Juu, a fine arts and media consultancy based in Nairobi.

Original_Search_Title: Is Reggae Music Losing Its Appeal?
Headline: Tanzania: Is Reggae Music Losing Its Appeal?
Body_Text:
The Makumbusho Village was the venue for the Explosive Zion show in Dar es Salaam a few weeks ago. The event was a cultural blend as people from different walks of life came to celebrate reggae music in Dar es Salaam.
The multi cultural gathering told a story of the genre with performances from the celebrated Jhikoman, Fred Saganda and Hardmad among other artistes who kept the audience on their feet for the rest of the night.
The Makumbusho gig had taken over a ground that is usually reserved for the popular Congolese bands like Akudo Impact and FM Academia.
Nevertheless, the attendance was a spectacle to behold as a mammoth audience turned up for such a rare occasion in Tanzania's music.
As fans danced to reggae tunes from Roots Culture to Lovers' Rock, one rude and cold reality that reggae fans have to contend with is the little attention that the genre is given.
As the soul search for reasons for low key shows continues, there are pundits who put the blame squarely on the radio stations who barely have time for reggae with the exception of a few.
According to Simon Joseph from Sababisha Empire their group has written several reggae songs which they are yet to record for the fear that they might invest their money in something that won't be given enough airtime.
Fred Saganda, another reggae artiste corroborate Joseph Simon's view, however, he points out that something has to be done to make sure that reggae music is given airplay.
"Some of these presenters and DJs are the same people responsible for the organisation of different concerts. Now if they can't play the songs on radio this means it will equally be difficult for them to hire reggae artistes to perform at such concerts," he says.
But as the artistes complain of lack of airplay, there are some fans who have taken the rod to the artistes whom they think lack the passion for the music despite singing.
According to Mnanda a reggae fan, the genre is not just about music it is a passionate lifestyle that be a musician needs to live.
"Reggae music is not music for those in a hurry to make a quick buck, for one to do it you must have the passion and the desire to pass the message. Most of the artistes with the exception of a few are those that take the Tanzanian fans for granted and as a result the get ignored," he says.
Michael Baruti a radio presenter with East Africa Radio thinks it is much more to blame on the trend in the industry where radio presenters prefer playing hot and trendy music.
"I think the problem is not about the genre it just about what is hot at the moment, I don't see why a presenter will neglect a hot reggae song to go for a dull RnB hit?" he says.
According to Joachim Kimario, Tanzanian artistes unlike their Kenyan, Nigerian, South African and Uganda counterparts lack sharp business acumen.
"Don't blame the producers, the presenters and Dj for your poor work ask, just do yourself a favour and yourself what should you do to change your music so," he explains.
And due to this lack of a business niche and direction some few artistes such as Lady Jaydee, Mwana FA, AY and Banana Zorro continue to dominate the industry.
"Even if you can't be business minded look for a manager to manage you," he says.

Original_Search_Title: ISO Boss Elevated to Colonel
Headline: Uganda: ISO Boss Elevated to Colonel
Body_Text:
Kampala — President Museveni has promoted officers and men of UPDF, including ISO director general, Ronnie Balya who was promoted from the rank of lieutenant to colonel.
The promotion of Lt. Balya means that he has jumped three ranks of captain, major and lieutenant colonel. According to military sources, the President as the commander in chief of armed forces, announced last evening the changes that saw all persons holding the post of directorship at ISO promoted from lieutenant to major.
And these include, Capt. Charles Oluka, Lt. Herbert Maramagi, Lt. Charles Tumuhairwe, Lt. Frank Banana, Don Mugimba, Lt. David Rusa, Lt. Fred Kyakulaga, Lt. Hannington Kakura, Lt. Kibrai Kambao, Lt. Melkideki Were, Lt. Fred Lubega, among others.
Most of those promoted are non-commissioned officers serving in these sister institutions charged with gathering intelligence both internally and externally.
All ISO deputy directors including Lt. Joseph Aliganyira, Francis Atiku, Joel Agaba among others, ave been promoted to the rank of captain. "All officers that participated in Operation Lightning Thunder from both UPDF and ESO are promoted to various ranks," said a source who did not want to be disclosed because of the sensitivity of the matter.
However, the promotions do not capture the commander of Special Forces Brigade, Lt. Col. Kenarugaba Muhoozi, who oversaw the DR Congo operation that was launched against the Lord Resistance Army.

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo - Story of My Collapse Concocted to Sell Papers
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo - Story of My Collapse Concocted to Sell Papers
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Former President Olusegun Obasanjo has described last week's reports of his collapse at a church service in Igbotako, Ondo State, as a marketing strategy by the Nigerian media to sell their newspapers, saying that he never at any time slumped at the event.
Obasanjo who gave a vivid picture of all that transpired at the event during a chat with newsmen in Abeokuta Friday said he was shocked after 72 hours of the incident to read his purported collapse.
Obasanjo was reported to have slumped midway into the church service to mark retired Major General Olu Bajowa's 70th birthday at St. Stephens Anglican Church, Igbotako, Ondo State. But responding to questions on the incident, Obasanjo said,
"I left here (Abeokuta) about 7.45am, but I got to Igbotako by 10.00am. The programme was supposed to start by 11.00am. Gen Olu Bajowa received me. He even gave me a room. It was about 10.45am, we all went to the church in a convoy. That church is still under construction. But, by my own assessment of that church, it was meant to be air-conditioned because there were scanty windows and there were no many fans.
"Where I sat, there was only one fan. A number of traditional rulers were sitting on my right hand side. I sat between Gen. Adeyinka Adebayo and Gov. Olusegun Mimiko. I started feeling a little bit uncomfortable. By then, Bishop Gbonigi had started giving sermon. And I was feeling like vomiting and Gen. Adebayo said the place was stuffy. One of the traditional rulers also complained that the ventilation was not okay. And suddenly, I started sweating profusely and I said to Gen. Adebayo that I don't think I could cope and he asked me if I could manage for Gbonigi to end his sermon and I said no, that I needed fresh air and I worked out."
"Unaided?" asked the reporter.
"Yes, unaided to my car," Obasanjo turned to Daily Trust, with his eyes glued to that direction, adding that, he subsequently walked to his air-conditioned car, and felt okay again.
"I was even tempted to go back about 2-3 minutes after and I said No, let me go and take my breakfast, since I had not eaten since morning. I got to Gen. Bajowa's house and I said get me something to eat and I was offered a bunch of bananas. I ate two and I was okay and I went back to the thanksgiving service and after the service, I went to the reception where I commanded the cutting of the cake and danced to Ayefele's band and we came back home only to read two days later that I slumped," he said.
"I think it was a gimmick by you journalists. May be they wanted to use that to sell their newspapers. It was a marketing strategy. Some of the former President's aides who spoke with Daily Trust also expressed shock over the story. One of them wondered why if the story was true the journalists in the state failed to file the story on the same day,

Original_Search_Title: Obasanjo - I Can't Die Before My Time
Headline: Nigeria: Obasanjo - I Can't Die Before My Time
Body_Text:
Abeokuta — Former President Oluse-gun Obasanjo yesterday declared that he would not die before his due time, saying that the incident in Ekiti State on Wednesday in which he was speculated to have fainted and subsequently died yesterday was the seventh time his death would be rumoured.
Obasanjo was rumoured to have fainted during the 70th Birthday thanksgiving service of the former Managing Director of the defunct Nigeria Airways, Olu Bajowa in Ekiti State.
"My words to Nigerians are that wishes are not horses; if people wish and God does not make it to happen, there is nothing that can be done to make it happen by any mortals. I think people take delight in speculation and rumours," said Obasanjo yesterday.
The former President said it was a friend of his that broke the news of his death to him yesterday morning.Obasanjo who spoke to newsmen at his Presidential Hilltop in Abeokuta yesterday while narrating how he was choked during the birthday ceremony, said lack of enough ventilation arising from lack of air-conditioners and fans, forced him to retire to his car to feel some breeze of air-conditioners.
He said after staying there for about three minutes, he felt okay and went to his host's house for breakfast. "I was given two pieces of banana which I ate and I returned to the church. I was shocked three days after to hear reports of Obasanjo fainting. It is part of the gimmick of selling newspapers."
"A friend who called me this morning wanted to confirm whether the news he heard that I was dead was true. I have counted seven times that I have been rumoured dead, since the eve of my inauguration in 1999 as a democratically elected President of Nigeria till today.
"Those who indulge in this will not give up; and it is left to those who have superstitious believe that when there are rumours and speculation like this, it means longevity. Whether longevity or not, what I know is that until God who has created me decides to say yes, I have reached my take-off point to return to him, I will still remain Kampe, despite peoples speculation and rumour."
He therefore urged all Nigerians to pray for the success of the elections, wishing the "young and old, male and female Nigerians every new fortunes the year will offer."

Original_Search_Title: Five Million Crates of Beer for Festive Season
Headline: Mozambique: Five Million Crates of Beer for Festive Season
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican governments General Inspector of Economic Activities, Jose Rodolfo, has insisted that there is no reason for any shop or other business to hoard beer over this year's festive season, since there will be no shortages.
Rodolfo was speaking on Friday at a press conference held by the brewing company Cervejas de Mocambique (CDM - Beers of Mozambique), the local subsidiary of SAB-Miller, in order to brief the media on the availability of CDM products.
Cited by the Maputo daily "Noticias", CDM director Jose Moreira announced that the company will put 5.17 million crates of beer on the market, which is a 15 per cent increase on the 2010 festive season
"In southern Mozambique - that is Maputo city and provinces, and Gaza and Inhambane - 2.94 million crates will be on sale, which is over 14 per cent more than last year".
He added that the Impala brand of beer, brewed from cassava, which is brewed in the northern city of Nampula, has proved very popular since its launch on 14 November. "It is in great demand", said Moreira, "and we have already produced 4,900 hectolitres".
The National Director of Trade, Gabriel Muianga, said that, based on last year's experience, the government had "full confidence" in CDM, "and so we believe that beer supplies are guaranteed".

Original_Search_Title: Unreported Horrors - Male Rape
Headline: Congo-Kinshasa: Unreported Horrors - Male Rape
Body_Text:
Kampala — They are men who have lost all pride and self-confidence and who have been left severely traumatised by their experience. At the medical centre in Uganda where they are being treated, they talked candidly about the crimes carried out against them.
"In the past, I thought that it was only females who were raped but not men. I cannot understand myself today. I feel pain all the time in my anus and bladder. I feel like my bladder is full of water. I do not feel like a man. I do not know whether I will ever have children," said John (not his real name), a 27-year-old refugee from the Democratic Republic of Congo who is just one of possibly thousands of victims of male rape as civil wars and tribal conflicts continue unabated across Africa.
On Jan. 14, 2009, rebels loyal to the former renegade Congolese general Laurent Nkunda attacked Jomba village in the DRC's North Kivu province. There, the militia abducted ten people including six boys and forced them to carry out looting before taking them to a jungle base in Virunga National Park. John was among those captured.
"We were held for nine days. The leader of the group asked to have sex with me. I did not understand what he meant. He ordered that I be tied up and then he raped me. The other nine came after him. I passed out. My bottom was covered with blood. All nine days in the bush were like that. It was like that for the others. One of the boys died," John said.
Nearly two years on from his ordeal, he is one of dozens of male and female rape victims being treated at a trauma counselling centre called the Refugee Law Project (RLP) in the Ugandan capital, Kampala. The survivors come from a number of African nations ridden with conflict including - among others - DRC, Sudan, Somalia, Ethiopia, Eritrea and Burundi.
The RLP was founded ten years ago and is an outreach of the law faculty at Uganda's main university of Makerere where staff help rape victims recover from their mental and physical wounds. The project is unique in that it is largely unknown by the Ugandan community and it is situated on a hill in the northern part of the city called Old Kampala where it is housed in a colonial-style residential building.
Salome Atim, the RLP gender official responsible for aiding male rape victims, said that since the beginning of the year they have received about 30 cases of male rape, mostly refugees who have escaped from conflict zones. "These are the ones who are open. The others do not speak, and that means there could be very many (victims)," she said.
Many boys and men fear speaking out because they are often branded homosexuals, even by doctors and medical workers trying to help them. Rape victims from Islamic states such as Somalia, for example, often refuse to talk because revealing what happened can result in them being labelled criminals by wider society.
Atim added that the question of homosexuality - which is taboo in many African societies - compounds the dilemma. But she said some people do feel able to talk, although only after much counselling
"People are not used to homosexuality and they do not listen to these people. They think anal sex is consensual where they think the victim agreed. They (victims) get further stigmatised because the moment they go to a health centre, they are asked, 'so you are homosexuals?' So they do not speak. We help them to open up slowly," she said.
One of Atim's patients, Pierre (not his real name), was a student at a school in Congo's city of Bukavu but in 2004 gangs from one of the numerous militia groups trying to exert control of the area attacked his family home and gang raped him, his father and brother.
"Men in uniform bloke into our home. They tied up my father's legs and arms. They then undressed my brother and told me to have sex with him. I refused," Pierre said before breaking down in tears.
After composing himself with the help of Atim, he continued. "They undressed me, held my penis between sticks and repeatedly banged it. One of them held my leg and another held the second leg and they pulled them apart. The rest of the gang then raped me one by one."
According to Atim, Pierre was suicidal when he arrived at RLP and has since been treated at a psychotherapy clinic.
Male rape has been prevalent as a weapon of war in many conflict zones and also in prison cells. But since these crimes are mostly unreported, also because the focus is on female victims, the extent of the problem is unknown. What is known is that male victims face horrendous problems in recovering.
John, who covered his face with a cap during the interview, said he now struggles to walk any distance due to unhealed wounds. "When I walk a long distance, blood flows out of my anus. When I eat hard food like cassava I have problems going to the toilet because my rectum gets out when I pass out faeces," he said.
Published under an agreement with Street News Service.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Set to Meet Inflation Target for 2011
Headline: Mozambique: Govt Set to Meet Inflation Target for 2011
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican government is set to meet its one-digit inflation target for the current year.
Data released on Thursday by the National Statistics Institute (INE) show that Mozambique's inflation rate, as measured by the Consumer Price Index, was 4.06 per cent for the period January to September 2011.
This is mainly due to the appreciation of the metical against the US dollar and South African rand.
The government aimed in its Economic and Social Plan for 2011 (PES 2011) for an average annual inflation rate of not higher than 8.0 per cent.
Last year the government failed to meet its target, with inflation skyrocketing to 16.62 per cent, according to figures released in January by the Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Mozambique.
Cirilo Tembe, director of integration, coordination and external relations at the INE, explained that food and non-alcoholic beverages contributed to the overall inflation with 1.37 percentage points.
Tembe pointed out that fish, charcoal, brown sugar, petrol, higher education tuition fees, and cassava are the items which had a major impact on overall inflation.
Tembe pointed out that Eduardo Mondlane University (UEM), the oldest institution of higher education in Mozambique, increased its tuition fees by over 400 per cent. In addition, at the beginning of July the government increased petrol prices from 44 meticais to 47.52 meticais a litre.
The INE official also pointed out that in September inflation fell by 0.08 per cent.
The Consumer Price Index is based on data on the prices of 1,200 items sold at 852 shops and markets, of which 444 were in Maputo, 152 in Beira and 152 in Nampula.

Original_Search_Title: Liberian Refugees Face Xenophobic Attacks
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Xenophobia or Reprisal Attacks
Body_Text:
The Ivorian post-election mayhem may have just ended; the nation may be said to be picking up the pieces and making amends; diplomatic shuttles may be ongoing and peace may be supposed to be taking roots in the Mano River enclave. However, beneath it all, Liberian refugees in Ivory Coast are still living the hell of what seems hatred for Liberians or reprisal attacks. The Analyst reports from an SOS letter it received yesterday.
Unconfirmed reports from Ivory Coast say armed government soldiers, Sunday, August 28, 2011, stormed a UNHCR refugee camp situated in the west of the country and attacked dozens of Liberians leaving several almost dead, brutalized and maimed, or without food, water, and shelter.
The reports said many more, who are without water, food, and shelters as the result of the attack, are currently hiding in the bushes or sleeping in the open in cassava patches at the goodwill of sympathetic residents.
The report, which was filed along with video clips secretly filmed during and after the attack by the alleged soldiers, said the victims were recently relocated to refugee camp located in the San Pedro area in Ivory Coast.
“The victims include nursing mothers, kids, the elderly, and anyone that they can lay their hands on. Many refugees were badly beaten and wondered. Some sustained broken teeth, and at present, some are still unconscious,” said the SOS dispatch, which was filed by Chei M. Bargeboe, a Liberian resident in Canada.
The SOS dispatch said prior to the Sunday attack, which followed several other attacks by Ivorian soldiers and civilians on Liberian referees in the camp, camp authorities had denied the victims food for three weeks, beginning August 11, 2011.
Bargeboe narrated further: “The assistant chairman of the refugees, Mr. David Dennis, said that prior to the time of  this unprovoked attack on them, the local Ivorian refugee agency, the Helpdesk and Assistance to Refugee and Stateless Person(SAAR), had  deprive them their daily food rations since Sunday, August 21.
“Presently the entire refugee population of the camp, which include 16 babies, 176 children, 96 women and 84 men, are without food and drinking water. They are driven from the camp and they have been sleeping in the opened field and under cassava bushes. It rains and shines on them.”
It is not clear whether Bargeboe is an eyewitness, but she warned that unless the Liberian and Ivorian authorities took swift rescue actions, dangerous men operating under guise of Ivorian security forces would wipe the refugees out.
She did not say what prompted the attacks months after the official end of violence in Ivory Coast and following massive refugee welfare collaborations between the two countries in recent months.
However, she suggested that it might be a combination of xenophobia and reprisal for the roles, some Ivoirians believe, Liberian mercenaries had played during the post-election violence that left thousands of Ivoirians and foreign residents killed, maimed, or dispossessed.
“They continue to be threatened with massacre by some of the soldiers and local residents who had previously not welcomed the refugees' stay in their midst. These poor and innocent refugees are at the mercy of their predators and at the brink of another Rwanda. The International Community needs to know of their plights before it is too late,” she said in the dispatch.
The dispatch contained the cell phone numbers of three victims of the attacks, who Bargeboe said, could be contacted for further information.
The Analyst is withholding the names for the security of those involved.
The reported attack on Liberians in the San Pedro refugee camp is one of many attempts by some Ivoirians to punish Liberians for their political, and some say, religious woes.
For instance on December 18, 2010 Liberian refugees sought refuge at the UNHCR Building in Abidjan after supporters of current president, Alhassan Ouattara, accused them of supporting the then incumbent president, Laurent Gbagbo.
At the time, the refugees stayed at the UNHCR Building for nine months, to no avail, in their feeble efforts to declare their innocence to their Ivorian brothers and sisters. How of them perished when the war finally swallowed the capital and sent the incumbent president scurrying for cover, remains unclear to date.
“One sorrowful day during the  heat of the crisis, a car hit a 2-year-old baby and the driver ran off. The women were so angry and scared that they broke into the UNHCR compound with their children and husbands. Even while in the UNHCR compound, the agency did not cater for the needs of the refugees. Instead, the UN agency collaborated with the government to accuse the refugees of being violent, rude, wild, and burglars. They were threatened that they would be harmed if they did not immediately leave the UNHCR compound,” the reports said at the time.
The reports said that, as with the San Pedro situation, members of the FRCI would walk into the UNHCR Compound in Abidjan to issue threats to the Liberian refugees who had thought that they were  under the protection of the UN refugee agency.
According to the reports, it was the Liberian refugees, who the agency offered US $100.00 to relocate to San Pedro, that were now being used for target practice by ruthless Ivorian security details.
The report said what now appears to be the problem between and the agency, and perhaps upon which the Ivorian security are basing their harassment, is that UNHCR intended the San Pedro camp as a temporary transit point, something the refugee least expected.
The agency told the refugees that it would offer them an additional US $100 to relocate to Liberia or face forced eviction by the Ivorian security.
Whether last Sunday’s brutal attack on the Liberians is part of that eviction, remains unclear as this paper went to bed late last night.
Investigation continues.

Original_Search_Title: Issues - NDA Gives Partial Report On Market Survey
Headline: Sierra Leone: Issues - NDA Gives Partial Report On Market Survey
Body_Text:
Freetown — The National Democratic Alliance (NDA) & African Socialist Movement (ASM) wish to express very serious concerns over the continuous rise in commodity prices despite claims by government that commodity prices have decreased since the announcement by President Ernest Bai Koroma on May 26, 2011.
Due to a recent survey of the market conducted by the NDA & ASM, we have discovered that prices of basic commodities have increased between 5% (five percent) to 15% (fifteen percent) since May 26, contrary to claims by government officials that commodity prices have subsided.
The National Democratic Alliance & African Socialist Movement also wish to state that although prices of other commodities like sugar, flour, fish, meat and tea have remained constant since May 26, the 10% (ten percent) deduction on import tax on rice has only resulted to a marginal 3.5% decrease in the overall sale cost of a 50kg bag of rice.
The NDA & ASM therefore underline the fact that the president's statement on May 26, 2011 has not realistically solved the rise in commodity prices, which resulted from the 30% (thirty percent) increase in the prices of petroleum products created by government's withdrawal of fuel subsidy.
We also want to point out that while the 30% (thirty percent) increase in transport fares in the Western Area has decreased, in the rest of the country transport fares are still up by 20% (twenty percent).
Besides, the NDA & ASM wish to reveal that the current cost of a 50kg bag of rice in the market supersedes the price of Le130, 000 announced by Presidential and Public Affairs Minister, Alpha Kanu as the official market cost.
Consequently, the NDA & ASM absolutely believe that this terrible economic situation is connected to government's refusal to return fuel subsides.
We want government to understand that commodity prices cannot be reversed to their pre-May 1 cost when there is still a 20% (twenty percent) increase on the pre-May 1 price of petroleum products.
Therefore, the NDA & ASM wish to recommend the following:
The immediate removal of the 20% (twenty percent) increases in the price of petroleum products and the return of fuel subsidy.
Urgent increase by 400% (four hundred percent) the salaries of all workers especially teachers.
Possibility of an additional 15% (fifteen percent) increase on government revenue from multinational corporations especially mining companies to balance the cost of fuel subsidy.
The establishment of an Independent Commission tasked with the responsibility of studying and regulating the economy.

Original_Search_Title: Hundreds of Thousands Will Be at Risk for Months
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Hundreds of Thousands Will Be at Risk for Months Without Urgent Humanitarian International Effort, Warn Aid Agencies
Publisher: International Coalition of Civil Society Organizations
Body_Text:
The situation in Ivory Coast and Liberia has descended into a huge humanitarian disaster which requires urgent international funding, a coalition of 13 aid agencies has warned today.
Despite recent political developments, the humanitarian emergency remains and the needs of people affected by extreme fighting continue to escalate.   The crisis is far from over in Ivory Coast, agencies say, and will require international attention for months to come. Funding is desperately needed to provide life-saving aid and to rebuild shattered communities, but the humanitarian aid pledged so far has fallen dangerously short.
The conflict in Ivory Coast has already displaced more than one million people from their homes and forced around 135,000 refugees to flee to Liberia[1]. People made homeless by violence are now in desperate need of food, water, shelter, sanitation and protection. The majority of the displaced are children, many of whom are at risk from disease and hunger.
Guy Cave, Save the Children's Country Director for Ivory Coast said: "According to our staff on the ground, displaced children and their families are living in terrible conditions in Duekoué. There are no proper toilets and a huge lack of water, so people can't keep clean. There is massive overcrowding and people have nowhere to sleep. The risk of disease spreading is acute. This is a catastrophic situation and children need urgent help."
Displaced people and refugees are mostly living with relatives or in strangers' homes, causing a major stress on local food supplies. In Liberia, aid agencies are reporting a near- exhaustion of food stocks, families reducing their food consumption to one meal per day, and major increases in the price of rice and cassava in local markets. Scattered in remote and dense forest border areas between Ivory Coast and Liberia, a huge number of refugees remain incredibly vulnerable and out of reach of life-saving relief.
Tariq Riebl, Oxfam's Humanitarian Manager in Liberia said: "Many refugees we are working with are too afraid to return home any time soon. The crisis has also severely affected the incomes of families. This is a humanitarian crisis that is likely to continue for months to come. We need to urgently expand our response to meet the escalating needs in Ivory Coast and Liberia but without the funds tens of thousands of people will go without help."
The coalition is calling for funding to increase rapidly to meet the growing humanitarian needs on the ground both in Ivory Coast and Liberia, which will continue for months to come. So far funding has been inadequate. The $146.5 million UN humanitarian appeal for Liberia has received just 29% of the funds requested[2], while a new $160 million appeal for Ivory Coast has received only 18%[3]. Unless funding is dramatically scaled up, the needs of tens of thousands of people will remain unmet[4].
Vincent Taillandier, Desk Officer for Action Contre la Faim (ACF) just back from Ivory Coast said: "In addition to a huge displacement of the population, there is a major problem with a lack of critical supplies. Health centers, for example, are without any drugs or therapeutic food to treat malnutrition. Medical staff are in place, but are no longer receiving their wages and feel powerless without the medical equipment they need to treat their patients. It is essential to get health centers they provisions they need."
The coalition also urges donors to ensure aid is delivered to those most in need. Concrete and coordinated measures must be taken to preserve neutral, impartial and independent humanitarian action, and further aid pledges must be delivered according to proper needs assessments on the ground.
"On the Ivory Coast-Liberia border, people seeking refuge are saying they are exhausted and in desperate need of assistance. Most vulnerable are isolated women, children, the elderly and disabled persons who require specific support, such as psychosocial and medical services. The international community must ensure that humanitarian aid is impartial and reaches all who need it regardless of location or political affiliation," said Laurent Davy, Programme Manager for Ivory Coast and Liberia at Handicap International.
The coalition calls upon the humanitarian donor community to:
Urgently increase their funding to enable aid agencies to meet the needs of displaced people in Ivory Coast and refugees in Liberia, as well as affected neighboring countries;
Ensure funding allocations are made transparently and equitably to ensure that needs are met across the country.
The coalition calls upon the UN and the international humanitarian system to:
Ensure military and humanitarian response are distinguished to preserve neutral, impartial and independent humanitarian action;
Take adequate measures to ensure humanitarian access to all affected populations, regardless of ethnicity, religion or perceived political alignment.
Signatory organizations:

Original_Search_Title: Govt Set to Meet Inflation Target for 2011
Headline: Mozambique: Govt Set to Meet Inflation Target for 2011
Body_Text:
Maputo — The Mozambican government is set to meet its one-digit inflation target for the current year.
Data released on Thursday by the National Statistics Institute (INE) show that Mozambique's inflation rate, as measured by the Consumer Price Index, was 4.06 per cent for the period January to September 2011.
This is mainly due to the appreciation of the metical against the US dollar and South African rand.
The government aimed in its Economic and Social Plan for 2011 (PES 2011) for an average annual inflation rate of not higher than 8.0 per cent.
Last year the government failed to meet its target, with inflation skyrocketing to 16.62 per cent, according to figures released in January by the Monetary Policy Committee of the Bank of Mozambique.
Cirilo Tembe, director of integration, coordination and external relations at the INE, explained that food and non-alcoholic beverages contributed to the overall inflation with 1.37 percentage points.
Tembe pointed out that fish, charcoal, brown sugar, petrol, higher education tuition fees, and cassava are the items which had a major impact on overall inflation.
Tembe pointed out that Eduardo Mondlane University (UEM), the oldest institution of higher education in Mozambique, increased its tuition fees by over 400 per cent. In addition, at the beginning of July the government increased petrol prices from 44 meticais to 47.52 meticais a litre.
The INE official also pointed out that in September inflation fell by 0.08 per cent.
The Consumer Price Index is based on data on the prices of 1,200 items sold at 852 shops and markets, of which 444 were in Maputo, 152 in Beira and 152 in Nampula.

Original_Search_Title: Liberian Refugees Face Xenophobic Attacks
Headline: Cote d'Ivoire: Xenophobia or Reprisal Attacks
Body_Text:
The Ivorian post-election mayhem may have just ended; the nation may be said to be picking up the pieces and making amends; diplomatic shuttles may be ongoing and peace may be supposed to be taking roots in the Mano River enclave. However, beneath it all, Liberian refugees in Ivory Coast are still living the hell of what seems hatred for Liberians or reprisal attacks. The Analyst reports from an SOS letter it received yesterday.
Unconfirmed reports from Ivory Coast say armed government soldiers, Sunday, August 28, 2011, stormed a UNHCR refugee camp situated in the west of the country and attacked dozens of Liberians leaving several almost dead, brutalized and maimed, or without food, water, and shelter.
The reports said many more, who are without water, food, and shelters as the result of the attack, are currently hiding in the bushes or sleeping in the open in cassava patches at the goodwill of sympathetic residents.
The report, which was filed along with video clips secretly filmed during and after the attack by the alleged soldiers, said the victims were recently relocated to refugee camp located in the San Pedro area in Ivory Coast.
“The victims include nursing mothers, kids, the elderly, and anyone that they can lay their hands on. Many refugees were badly beaten and wondered. Some sustained broken teeth, and at present, some are still unconscious,” said the SOS dispatch, which was filed by Chei M. Bargeboe, a Liberian resident in Canada.
The SOS dispatch said prior to the Sunday attack, which followed several other attacks by Ivorian soldiers and civilians on Liberian referees in the camp, camp authorities had denied the victims food for three weeks, beginning August 11, 2011.
Bargeboe narrated further: “The assistant chairman of the refugees, Mr. David Dennis, said that prior to the time of  this unprovoked attack on them, the local Ivorian refugee agency, the Helpdesk and Assistance to Refugee and Stateless Person(SAAR), had  deprive them their daily food rations since Sunday, August 21.
“Presently the entire refugee population of the camp, which include 16 babies, 176 children, 96 women and 84 men, are without food and drinking water. They are driven from the camp and they have been sleeping in the opened field and under cassava bushes. It rains and shines on them.”
It is not clear whether Bargeboe is an eyewitness, but she warned that unless the Liberian and Ivorian authorities took swift rescue actions, dangerous men operating under guise of Ivorian security forces would wipe the refugees out.
She did not say what prompted the attacks months after the official end of violence in Ivory Coast and following massive refugee welfare collaborations between the two countries in recent months.
However, she suggested that it might be a combination of xenophobia and reprisal for the roles, some Ivoirians believe, Liberian mercenaries had played during the post-election violence that left thousands of Ivoirians and foreign residents killed, maimed, or dispossessed.
“They continue to be threatened with massacre by some of the soldiers and local residents who had previously not welcomed the refugees' stay in their midst. These poor and innocent refugees are at the mercy of their predators and at the brink of another Rwanda. The International Community needs to know of their plights before it is too late,” she said in the dispatch.
The dispatch contained the cell phone numbers of three victims of the attacks, who Bargeboe said, could be contacted for further information.
The Analyst is withholding the names for the security of those involved.
The reported attack on Liberians in the San Pedro refugee camp is one of many attempts by some Ivoirians to punish Liberians for their political, and some say, religious woes.
For instance on December 18, 2010 Liberian refugees sought refuge at the UNHCR Building in Abidjan after supporters of current president, Alhassan Ouattara, accused them of supporting the then incumbent president, Laurent Gbagbo.
At the time, the refugees stayed at the UNHCR Building for nine months, to no avail, in their feeble efforts to declare their innocence to their Ivorian brothers and sisters. How of them perished when the war finally swallowed the capital and sent the incumbent president scurrying for cover, remains unclear to date.
“One sorrowful day during the  heat of the crisis, a car hit a 2-year-old baby and the driver ran off. The women were so angry and scared that they broke into the UNHCR compound with their children and husbands. Even while in the UNHCR compound, the agency did not cater for the needs of the refugees. Instead, the UN agency collaborated with the government to accuse the refugees of being violent, rude, wild, and burglars. They were threatened that they would be harmed if they did not immediately leave the UNHCR compound,” the reports said at the time.
The reports said that, as with the San Pedro situation, members of the FRCI would walk into the UNHCR Compound in Abidjan to issue threats to the Liberian refugees who had thought that they were  under the protection of the UN refugee agency.
According to the reports, it was the Liberian refugees, who the agency offered US $100.00 to relocate to San Pedro, that were now being used for target practice by ruthless Ivorian security details.
The report said what now appears to be the problem between and the agency, and perhaps upon which the Ivorian security are basing their harassment, is that UNHCR intended the San Pedro camp as a temporary transit point, something the refugee least expected.
The agency told the refugees that it would offer them an additional US $100 to relocate to Liberia or face forced eviction by the Ivorian security.
Whether last Sunday’s brutal attack on the Liberians is part of that eviction, remains unclear as this paper went to bed late last night.
Investigation continues.

Original_Search_Title: Issues - NDA Gives Partial Report On Market Survey
Headline: Sierra Leone: Issues - NDA Gives Partial Report On Market Survey
Body_Text:
Freetown — The National Democratic Alliance (NDA) & African Socialist Movement (ASM) wish to express very serious concerns over the continuous rise in commodity prices despite claims by government that commodity prices have decreased since the announcement by President Ernest Bai Koroma on May 26, 2011.
Due to a recent survey of the market conducted by the NDA & ASM, we have discovered that prices of basic commodities have increased between 5% (five percent) to 15% (fifteen percent) since May 26, contrary to claims by government officials that commodity prices have subsided.
The National Democratic Alliance & African Socialist Movement also wish to state that although prices of other commodities like sugar, flour, fish, meat and tea have remained constant since May 26, the 10% (ten percent) deduction on import tax on rice has only resulted to a marginal 3.5% decrease in the overall sale cost of a 50kg bag of rice.
The NDA & ASM therefore underline the fact that the president's statement on May 26, 2011 has not realistically solved the rise in commodity prices, which resulted from the 30% (thirty percent) increase in the prices of petroleum products created by government's withdrawal of fuel subsidy.
We also want to point out that while the 30% (thirty percent) increase in transport fares in the Western Area has decreased, in the rest of the country transport fares are still up by 20% (twenty percent).
Besides, the NDA & ASM wish to reveal that the current cost of a 50kg bag of rice in the market supersedes the price of Le130, 000 announced by Presidential and Public Affairs Minister, Alpha Kanu as the official market cost.
Consequently, the NDA & ASM absolutely believe that this terrible economic situation is connected to government's refusal to return fuel subsides.
We want government to understand that commodity prices cannot be reversed to their pre-May 1 cost when there is still a 20% (twenty percent) increase on the pre-May 1 price of petroleum products.
Therefore, the NDA & ASM wish to recommend the following:
The immediate removal of the 20% (twenty percent) increases in the price of petroleum products and the return of fuel subsidy.
Urgent increase by 400% (four hundred percent) the salaries of all workers especially teachers.
Possibility of an additional 15% (fifteen percent) increase on government revenue from multinational corporations especially mining companies to balance the cost of fuel subsidy.
The establishment of an Independent Commission tasked with the responsibility of studying and regulating the economy.

Original_Search_Title: This Week in the Market
Headline: Sierra Leone: This Week in the Market
Body_Text:
Freetown — This week in the market is a column aimed at gauging the market trend in Freetown. Concord Times' reporter Victoria Saffa was at the Salad Ground Market in the central part of Freetown to seek information on the in and outflow of food commodities and to gauge the prices of food items.
Speaking to one of the market women, Kadiatu Sesay, prices of commodities have increased due to several factors and this has affected their sales. Ms Sesay said this has made their level of business to drop as most of their customers who used to buy large amount of food items now buy lesser or no foodstuff from them.
The reporter also spoke with Mr Chernoh Barrie, a shop owner in the Salad Ground Market who gave a brief insight into the trend for which sales of foodstuff is taking. Mr. Barrie explained that the high cost of living in the country which is getting higher everyday should be blamed for the drop in sales lately and pleaded with the government to act on the rising prices of food items.

Original_Search_Title: U.S. Embassy Burglar Jailed
Headline: Sierra Leone: U.S. Embassy Burglar Jailed
Body_Text:
Freetown — "After hearing the testimonies of PW1 and PW3 as to the theft of the banana and the damage of the perimeter wall given by the other witnesses from the prosecution, I have no doubt that the prosecution has proved the case against both accused persons beyond reasonable doubt," says Magistrate
Steven Conteh yesterday at court No1 while delivering his judgment in a case that was before him for larceny and malicious damage.
" I have carefully considered the testimony of the second accused aged 15 years. I believe in his testimony and held that he was innocent of this offense. I therefore find the second accused not guilty. The first accused is guilty of all three counts and his fine is Le1.6 million or two-month's imprisonment."
The accused, Mohamed Kamara and Michael Sesay were in the docked for three counts in which they pleaded not guilty on all counts. Kamara was discharged while Sesay was returned to the Pademba Road prisons as he did not pay the stipulated fine.
It would be recalled that on Tuesday October 11 this year, the accused unlawfully broke and entered into the premises of the United States Embassy, being the residence of the US envoy to Sierra Leone at Signal Hill and carted away three banana stumps.

Original_Search_Title: Fifa Office-Holder Condemns Racist Soccer Players
Headline: South Africa: Blatter Comments Unfortunate - Sexwale
Body_Text:
Johannesburg — There is no excuse for racial insults on the football pitch, politician and businessman Tokyo Sexwale said on Friday.
He was referring to recent comments by Fifa president Sepp Blatter.
"Blatter is a great man; nevertheless this is very unfortunate. It should never have happened," the human settlements minister said in his capacity as a member of Fifa's anti-racism committee.
It was unacceptable to use match tension as an excuse for racial insults, Sexwale told journalists in Johannesburg.
Blatter apologised on Friday for his comments.
Sexwale was drawn into the fray on Wednesday when Fifa posted a file photograph of himself and Blatter embracing to accompany a statement saying Blatter acknowledged racism and was against it.
In an interview with Al Jazeera, Blatter suggested there was no racism problem on the pitch.
He said that during a match "sometimes things are done which are wrong.
"But, normally, at the end of the match, you apologise to your opponent if you had a confrontation during the match.
"You shake hands, and when the game is over, it is over. Anyone who has played a football match, or a match in any sport, knows that this is the case."
Sexwale rejected this, saying it was well known there was racism on the pitch and he was unhappy that offenders were getting off with a slap on the wrist in spite of Fifa's anti-racism lobbying.
As a member of Fifa's anti-racism committee, with Blatter, he felt that there should be harsher consequences for on-pitch racism.
For example, black players had bananas or peanuts thrown at them while supporters made monkey sounds.
As a former player himself he knew all about the insults.
They were usually about a person's nose, thighs, or mother, he said.
"It breaks your legs, it breaks your heart, and no handshaking can repair this thing. It doesn't go away with the exchange of a jersey."
He spoke highly of Blatter's determination to bring the World Cup to Africa and South Africa last year and said Blatter could not be called a racist.
Blatter had acknowledged his "inadvertent mistake" and in doing so had helped raise the issue of racism "high in the skies".
On calls for Blatter to resign, Sexwale said his future in Fifa should be determined by Fifa.
After reading Blatter's apology for the "unfortunate situation" his statement had created, Sexwale said: "I want to believe he is genuine."
Blatter's remarks were made in the midst of a debate over racism in football after England Football Association (EFA) charged Liverpool striker Luis Suarez with racially abusing Manchester United's Patrice Evra.
EFA was investigating a similar claim against England captain John Terry who is alleged to have insulted Queens Park Rangers' Anton Ferdinand.
Sexwale could not understand why Terry was still allowed to wear the captain's armband, especially since people like him were expected to behave as role models.
Racism was also not confined to black and white.
"The England players say funny things against the Irish players," he said.
Sexwale said he thought the Fifa picture was a file photo and did not want to judge them on their choice of that particular picture.
Blatter has said he will not resign.

Original_Search_Title: CAF Unveils Nominees for 2011 Player of the Year and Player of the Year (Based in Africa)
Headline: Africa: CAF Unveils Nominees for 2011 Player of the Year and Player of the Year (Based in Africa)
Body_Text:
The Confederation of African Football(C AF) on Wednesday in Accra unveiled the nominees for the 2011 Player of the Year and the Player of the Year (Based in Africa).
The nominees were revealed by Kwesi Nyantakyi, CAF Executive Committee member and president of the Ghana Football Association.
Ten nominees each were named for the two categories based on the performance and contribution of the players to their clubs and national teams during the year under review. For the first time, there are 10 nominees for the African Player of the Year (Based in Africa), contrary to the five for last year.
The lists was compiled through a vigorous exercise by the Technical, Football and Media Committees of CAF, taking into account the performance of the player for his national team and club from January to November, 2011.
The nominees are (in alphabetical order):
2011 African Player of the Year (Based in Africa)
Ayoub Elkhiqi (Morocco and WAC)
Yaya Banana (Cameroon and EST)
Edward Sadomba (Zimbabwe and Al Hilal)
Idrissa Laouali (Niger and ASFA)
Jerome Ramatlhakwane (Botswana and Vasco da Gama)
Kalu Uche (Nigeria and Enyimba)
Oussama Darragi (Tunisia and EST)
Samir Aboud (Libya and Al-Etihad)
Youssef Msakni (Tunisia and EST)
Zouhir Dhaouadi (Tunisia and Club Africain)
2011 African Player of the Year
Adel Taarabt (Morocco and Queens Park Rangers)
Andre Ayew (Ghana and Olympique de Marseille)
Asamoah Gyan (Ghana and Al-Ain)
Didier Drogba (Cote d’ Ivoire and Chelsea)
Kevin-Prince Boateng (Ghana and AC Milan)
Moussa Sow (Senegal and Lille)
Samuel Eto’o (Cameroon and Anzhi)
Seydou Keita (Mali and Barcelona)
Yao Kouassi Gervais “Gervinho” (Cote d’Ivoire and Arsenal)
Yaya Toure (Cote d’Ivoire and Manchester City)
The winner will be decided by votes from the Head Coaches or the Technical Directors of the National Associations affiliated to CAF.
The Awards Gala will be held on on Thursday, December 22 in Accra, Ghana. It will be the third time the Ghanaian capital will be hosting the annual event after 2006 and 2009.

Original_Search_Title: Two Remanded for Breaking Into U.S. Ambassador's Residence
Headline: Sierra Leone: Two Remanded for Breaking Into U.S. Ambassador's Residence
Body_Text:
Michael Sesay and Mohamed Kamara were yesterday remanded by Magistrate Steven Conteh at the Freetown Court No.1 for allegedly breaking into the perimeter wall of the residence of the United States Ambassador to Sierra Leone.
Both accused were charged with two counts of malicious damage and larceny which, according to the presiding magistrate, are contrary to section 51 of the Malicious Damage Act 1861.
The particulars of offence indicated that on Tuesday October 11, 2011, the accused persons maliciously damaged the perimeter wall of the Ambassador's residence situated at Signal Hill in Freetown.
The perimeter wall, according to the complainant, is valued at about Le 880,000 property of the US Embassy.
On the second count of larceny, the accused were indicted for allegedly stealing three stems of banana valued at Le274, 000 being property of the US Embassy.
Inspector Agatha Heals who prosecuted the matter, appealed to the magistrate not to grant the accused bail for the fact that they have not had evidence from the prosecuting witness. The matter was adjourned to 31st October this year.
